en.abbauen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~buwen~, from OHG ~buan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~buan~, from Proto-Germanic *~buanan~, from PIE *~bhuH-~ ("to be, to grow, to dwell"). Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different branch), Dutch ~bouwen~, and, more surprisingly, English ~be~ (from the same PIE root *~bhuH-~, whose basic meaning was "to exist, to grow").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epo~ ("off, away"). This prefix adds a sense of "down, off, away," often implying dismantling or reduction.

The semantic development of ~bauen~ from "to dwell" to "to build" is a natural progression: one builds where one intends to live. The related noun ~Bauer~ ("farmer") preserves the older sense of "dweller, cultivator." The compound ~abbauen~ reverses the constructive sense of ~bauen~: rather than building up, one takes down. It is used in mining ("to extract ore"), in employment ("to lay off workers"), in biology ("to break down substances"), and in general for any process of reduction or dismantling. This versatility makes it one of the most semantically productive ~ab-~ compounds in German.
en.abbilden:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~bilden~:

- ~bilden~: From MHG ~bilden~, ~bileden~, a merger of OHG ~biliden~ (from Proto-West Germanic *~biliأ¾ijan~) and OHG ~bilidإچn~ (from Proto-West Germanic *~biliأ¾إچn~), both derived from *~biliأ¾ؤ«~ ("image, likeness"), whence modern ~Bild~ ("picture, image"). The deeper origin is Proto-West Germanic *~bil~ ("equal, alike").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). In this compound, the prefix conveys the sense of reproducing or transferring an image from one medium to another.

The MHG predecessor ~abebilden~, ~abebileden~ is attested, confirming the compound's medieval origin. ~Abbilden~ means "to depict, to portray, to map" â€” to create an image (~Bild~) of something, transferring it "off" (~ab~) the original onto a new surface. The noun ~Abbildung~ ("illustration, figure, mapping") is standard in textbooks, scientific papers, and mathematical discourse. In mathematics, ~Abbildung~ is the formal term for a mapping or function â€” a precise abstraction from the concrete sense of depicting. Cognate with Dutch ~afbeelden~ ("to depict, to portray"), which uses the same prefix-root combination. The deeper connection to *~bil~ ("equal, alike") is revealing: to depict is to create something equal to the original.
en.abbrechen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~brechen~:

- ~brechen~: From MHG ~brأ«chen~, from OHG ~brأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~brekanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°reg-~ ("to break"). A class IV strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~brechen~, ~brach~, ~gebrochen~. Cognate with English ~break~, Dutch ~breken~, Old Saxon ~brekan~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys separation, termination, or removal.

The compound means "to break off, to abort, to discontinue" â€” ~ein Gesprأ¤ch abbrechen~ ("to break off a conversation"), ~ein Studium abbrechen~ ("to drop out of one's studies"), ~das Programm abbrechen~ ("to abort the program"). Both transitive and intransitive: a branch ~bricht ab~ ("breaks off") and a person ~bricht etwas ab~ ("breaks something off"). The English cognate ~break~ is one of the most transparent Germanic word pairs: the High German consonant shift turned Proto-Germanic *~k~ into the OHG affricate written ~hh~ (later ~ch~), so ~break~ and ~brechen~ differ only by this regular sound change. Someone who ~bricht das Studium ab~ is a ~Studienabbrecher~ ("university dropout"), a compound noun that carries considerably less stigma in German-speaking countries than its English equivalent, partly because the German apprenticeship system (~Ausbildung~) provides respected alternative career paths.
en.abdecken:
From Middle High German ~abedecken~, equivalent to ~ab-~ + ~decken~. Cognate with Dutch ~afdekken~ ("to cover"). Compound of ~ab-~ + ~decken~:

- ~decken~: From MHG ~decken~, from OHG ~decchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾akkjan~. Equivalent to ~Dach~ ("roof") + ~-en~. Cognate with Frisian ~dekke~, Dutch ~dekken~, English ~thatch~, Danish ~taekke~, and Swedish ~tأ¤cka~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). This prefix conveys removal or separation.

The verb ~abdecken~ presents a fascinating paradox: it can mean both "to uncover, to remove a covering" (taking the ~ab-~ prefix literally as "away") and "to cover, to protect" (where ~ab-~ implies shielding from above). Context determines which sense applies. English ~thatch~, the cognate of ~decken~, reveals the original Proto-Germanic root *~أ¾ak-~ ("roof, covering"), which underwent the High German consonant shift from ~أ¾~ to ~d~. The same shift turned ~أ¾ing~ into ~Ding~, ~أ¾orn~ into ~Dorn~, and ~أ¾ank~ into ~Dank~. The PIE root *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover") also produced Latin ~tegere~ ("to cover"), yielding English ~protect~, ~detect~, and ~toga~ (a garment that covers the body).
en.abgeben:
~ab-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~ab-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). The prefix adds the sense of transfer, delivery, or separation from the self.

~Abgeben~ means "to hand in, submit, deliver; to give off (heat, sound); to make (a statement); to play (a role)." The prefix ~ab-~ transforms a generic act of giving into an act of handing something away from oneself â€” a completed transfer. The reflexive ~sich abgeben mit~ ("to busy oneself with, to associate with") adds another dimension: the self is handed over to a task or person. The range of uses is striking: one ~gibt eine Erklأ¤rung ab~ ("makes a statement"), one ~gibt Wأ¤rme ab~ ("gives off heat"), one ~gibt ein Paket ab~ ("drops off a package") â€” all unified by the image of something departing from the agent's sphere. Cognate with Dutch ~afgeven~.
en.abhalten:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~. The PIE origin is uncertain â€” possibly from *~ل¸±el-~ ("to hide, conceal") or *~kel-~ ("to drive"). Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~, Danish ~holde~, Icelandic ~halda~, Swedish ~hأ¥lla~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). The prefix adds a sense of keeping at a distance or conducting away.

The literal sense is "to hold off" â€” to keep something at a distance or to conduct an event. ~Abhalten~ has two distinct modern meanings: "to keep from, to prevent" (~jemanden von etwas abhalten~, "to keep someone from something") and "to hold, to conduct" (a meeting, an exam, a ceremony). These senses connect through the idea of holding in a controlled manner â€” either holding something back or holding something in place while it runs its course. The English cognate ~hold~ preserves both senses too: "hold back" (prevent) and "hold a meeting" (conduct). The relationship between German ~halten~ and English ~hold~ is direct and unbroken from the Proto-Germanic period.
en.abholen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~holen~:

- ~holen~: From MHG ~holen~, ~holn~, from OHG ~holإچn~, ~holؤ“n~ ("to fetch, get, draw"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~halإچnؤ…~, *~hulإچnؤ…~ ("to call, fetch, summon"), from PIE *~kelhâ‚پ-~ ("to call, cry, summon").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). In this compound it carries a sense of removal or retrieval.

The literal sense is "to fetch away," meaning to pick someone or something up from a location. Unlike simple ~holen~ ("to fetch"), ~abholen~ emphasizes going to a specific place to collect something or someone -- you ~holen~ a glass of water from the kitchen, but you ~holen ab~ a friend from the airport. English ~haul~ is a distant cousin: it entered English from Old French ~haler~, which itself was borrowed from Frankish *~halإچn~, the same Proto-Germanic root as ~holen~. So ~holen~ and ~haul~ are etymological siblings reunited through a detour via medieval France. The PIE root *~kelhâ‚پ-~ ("to call, summon") also appears in Latin ~calؤپre~ ("to call, proclaim"), whence English ~calendar~ (originally a day for calling out debts) and ~claim~.
en.ablaufen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~laufen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupanؤ…~ ("to run, leap"), from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, stumble"). A class VII strong verb. Cognate with English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Dutch ~lopen~, Danish ~lأ¸be~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys completion, departure, or expiration.

The compound means "to run off, to expire, to elapse" â€” time, a contract, or a process ~lأ¤uft ab~ ("runs off, expires"). The image is of something running its course until it departs. The range of uses is characteristically broad: a passport ~lأ¤uft ab~ ("expires"), water ~lأ¤uft ab~ ("drains off"), an event ~lأ¤uft ab~ ("proceeds, unfolds"), and shoes ~laufen sich ab~ ("wear down from running"). The root verb ~laufen~ shows an interesting phonological history: OHG ~hloufan~ still had the initial ~hl-~ cluster (like English ~leap~ preserves the ~l-~), which was simplified to ~l-~ in Middle High German. The English cognates ~leap~ and ~lope~ seem semantically distant from "to run," but the original Proto-Germanic sense encompassed any rapid forward movement. The related English ~elope~ ("to run away secretly to marry") entered English from Anglo-French but traces back to the same Germanic root.
en.ablegen:
From Middle High German ~abelegen~, from Old High German *~abaleggen~, from Proto-Germanic *~abalagjanؤ…~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~, a causative formation from *~legت°-~ ("to lie"). The verb is thus the causative partner of ~liegen~ ("to lie"): to ~legen~ is to "cause to lie," i.e., to lay. Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~, Danish ~lأ¦gge~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). Cognate with English ~of~ and ~off~, Latin ~ab~ ("from").

The combination of "away" + "to lay" yields the core sense "to lay aside, to put down." From this spatial meaning, ~ablegen~ has developed a rich array of figurative uses: ~eine Prأ¼fung ablegen~ ("to take an exam," literally "to lay down a test"), ~einen Eid ablegen~ ("to take an oath"), ~Rechenschaft ablegen~ ("to give an account"), and ~Gewohnheiten ablegen~ ("to shed habits"). The English causative pair ~lie~/~lay~ mirrors the German ~liegen~/~legen~ perfectly, both descending from the same PIE causative derivation -- a grammatical mechanism over five thousand years old that is still productive in both languages.
en.ablehnen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~lehnen~:

- ~lehnen~: From a merger of two related Middle High German verbs: (1) ~linen~ / ~lenen~ (intransitive "to lean"), from OHG ~hlinؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlinؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlinؤپnؤ…~; and (2) ~leinen~ (transitive "to lean something"), from OHG ~hleinen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlainijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlainijanؤ…~, a causative of the former. The merger happened in Central and Low German dialects where the vowels fell together.
- ~ab-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). This separable prefix adds the sense of turning away, removing, or rejecting.

~Ablehnen~ means "to decline, to reject, to refuse" â€” literally to lean away from something. The physical gesture of leaning back from an unwanted offer is frozen in the word. In formal contexts it carries official weight: ~einen Antrag ablehnen~ ("to reject an application"), ~eine Einladung ablehnen~ ("to decline an invitation"). The root verb ~lehnen~ is cognate with English ~lean~ (from the same Germanic source) and Dutch ~leunen~. An unrelated homograph ~lehnen~ ("to lend"), from OHG ~lؤ“hanإچn~, gave rise to Dutch ~lenen~ and English ~lend~ â€” a false friend lurking within the same German dictionary entry.
en.ableiten:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~leiten~:

- ~leiten~: From MHG ~leiten~, from OHG ~leiten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laidijanؤ…~ ("to lead, guide"), a causative of *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to pass, to go by"). Cognate with English ~lead~, Old Saxon ~lؤ“dian~, Old Norse ~leiأ°a~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). The prefix adds the sense of derivation or leading away from a source.

The literal sense is "to lead away from" â€” to derive, to divert, to deduce. ~Ableiten~ covers a remarkable semantic range: in linguistics, it means "to derive" (a word from a root); in mathematics, "to differentiate" (a function); in engineering, "to divert" (water, electricity); in logic, "to deduce" (a conclusion from premises). The noun ~Ableitung~ serves as the standard term for "derivative" in both calculus and linguistics. The connection between "leading" and "learning" runs deep in this word family: Proto-Germanic *~laidijanؤ…~ ("to lead") is the causative of *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to go"), so to lead is literally "to make someone go." English ~lead~ is a direct cognate, and the semantic parallel â€” to lead someone through a derivation â€” is perfectly preserved.
en.abnehmen:
From Middle High German ~abenemen~, from Old High German ~abaneman~, from Proto-Germanic *~abanemanؤ…~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due, to allot, to assign"). Cognate with English ~nim~ (archaic "to take, filch"), Dutch ~nemen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp"), Swedish ~fأ¶rnimma~ ("to perceive").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). Conveys removal or decrease.

~abnehmen~ means "to take off/away, to decrease, to lose weight, to accept (a delivery), to answer (the phone)." The weight-loss sense â€” where pounds are "taken off" â€” is the most common in everyday speech. The PIE root *~nem-~ is fascinating for its dual nature: it meant both "to give" and "to take" one's allotted portion, reflecting an ancient concept where distribution and reception were two sides of the same act. This root also gave Greek ~nأ©mein~ ("to distribute"), the source of ~Nemesis~ â€” the goddess who deals out what is due. The phone sense (~den Hأ¶rer abnehmen~, "to lift the receiver") preserves the most literal meaning: physically taking the handset ~off~ its cradle. Cognate with Dutch ~afnemen~.
en.abrufen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~/~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrإچpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrإچpanؤ…~. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~ ("to call"). The origin of *~hrإچpanؤ…~ is uncertain; one theory connects it to *~hrإچأ¾iz~ ("glory, fame"), the first element of names like Rudolf.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). The prefix adds a sense of retrieval or completion.

~Abrufen~ means "to call off, to recall" and, in modern usage, "to retrieve (data)." The computational sense -- "to call away from storage" -- has made this once-ordinary compound a staple of the digital age. German speakers ~rufen Daten ab~ ("retrieve data") and ~rufen E-Mails ab~ ("check email"). The journey from a Proto-Germanic shout (*~hrإچpanؤ…~) to silently polling a server is a striking example of semantic adaptation.
en.absagen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~sagen~:

- ~sagen~: From MHG ~sagen~, from OHG ~sagؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~saggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sagjanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to say"). Cognate with English ~say~, Dutch ~zeggen~, Danish ~sige~, Swedish ~sأ¤ga~.
- ~ab-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). As a separable prefix, it means "off, away, down."

~Absagen~ means "to cancel, to call off, to decline" â€” literally "to say off," speaking an event or invitation away from existence. The construction works in two directions: one can ~eine Veranstaltung absagen~ ("cancel an event") or ~jemandem absagen~ ("decline someone's invitation"). The pair ~zusagen~/~absagen~ ("to accept"/"to decline") forms a neat antonym based on directional prefixes: ~zu~ ("toward") versus ~ab~ ("away").
en.abschlieأںen:
From Middle High German ~abeslieب¥en~, equivalent to ~ab-~ + ~schlieأںen~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~schlieأںen~:

- ~schlieأںen~: From MHG ~slieب¥en~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slإ«tanؤ…~, *~sleutanؤ…~ ("to lock, shut"). A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schlieأںen~, ~schloss~, ~geschlossen~.
- ~ab-~: From OHG ~ab-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). As a separable prefix, ~ab-~ adds a sense of removal, completion, or finality â€” "locking away" or "closing off."

The compound covers a remarkable semantic range: "to lock" (a door), "to conclude" (a contract), "to complete" (a project), and "to infer" (a logical conclusion) â€” all unified by the metaphor of bringing something to a close or fastening it shut. The last sense (~daraus schlieأںen~, "to conclude from that") shows how physical bolt-sliding became abstract reasoning. Cognate with Dutch ~afsluiten~.
en.abschneiden:
From Middle High German ~abesnؤ«den~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~schneiden~:

- ~schneiden~: From MHG ~snؤ«den~, from OHG ~snؤ«dan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~snؤ«أ¾an~ ("to cut"), from Proto-Germanic *~snؤ«أ¾anؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~sneyt-~ ("to cut"). Cognate with English dialectal ~snithe~ ("to cut"), Swedish ~snida~ ("to carve"), Icelandic ~snأ­أ°a~ ("to trim").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). This prefix adds a sense of removal or separation to the base verb.

The root is related to English ~snide~, which originally meant "cutting" in a literal sense before acquiring its modern figurative meaning of "derisively critical." Dutch ~snijden~ ("to cut, carve, intersect") is a close cognate. The compound ~abschneiden~ literally means "to cut off" but has developed the idiomatic sense of "to perform" or "to do" (as in ~gut abschneiden~, "to do well"), a semantic leap from the concrete act of cutting to an abstract assessment of results.
en.absehen:
~ab-~ + ~sehen~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~ ("to see"), from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice").
- ~ab-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). The prefix adds the sense of looking away, looking off (from something), or discerning from a distance.

~Absehen~ means "to foresee, to look away from, to refrain from" â€” a verb whose meanings seem to pull in different directions but share the logic of directed visual attention. ~Es ist abzusehen~ ("it is foreseeable") uses the prefix in its "seeing from a distance" sense. ~Von etwas absehen~ ("to refrain from, to disregard") uses it in the "looking away from" sense â€” one averts one's gaze and therefore one's intention. The derived noun ~Absicht~ ("intention, purpose") crystallizes the "aiming one's gaze" metaphor: an ~Absicht~ is literally where you direct your sight, hence your aim, your plan. The participial adjective ~absehbar~ ("foreseeable") is a mainstay of German political and economic discourse: ~in absehbarer Zeit~ ("in the foreseeable future"). Cognate with Dutch ~afzien~ ("to refrain from; to be able to see from a distance"), which preserves the same dual-meaning structure.
en.absetzen:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, to place"), the causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). As a separable prefix, it conveys removal, departure, or setting down.

~Absetzen~ means "to set down, to remove, to depose, to deduct" â€” an extraordinarily versatile verb. One can ~die Brille absetzen~ ("to take off one's glasses"), ~einen Kأ¶nig absetzen~ ("to depose a king"), ~Kosten absetzen~ ("to deduct expenses" from taxes), ~ein Medikament absetzen~ ("to discontinue a medication"), or ~ein Theaterstأ¼ck absetzen~ ("to cancel a play from the repertoire"). The PIE root *~sed-~ ("to sit") is one of the most prolific in Indo-European, producing Latin ~sedؤ“re~ ("to sit"), whence English ~sediment~, ~session~, ~president~ ("one who sits before"), ~reside~, and ~siege~ (via Old French, from Vulgar Latin *~sedicؤپre~). The causative relationship between ~sitzen~ ("to sit") and ~setzen~ ("to set, to cause to sit") perfectly mirrors that between English ~sit~ and ~set~.
en.absichern:
From ~ab-~ + ~sichern~. Compound of a Germanic prefix and a Latin-derived verb:

- ~sichern~: Derived from the adjective ~sicher~, from MHG ~siher~, from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~ ("secure, sure"), borrowed from Latin ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ ("free from care, safe"), itself from ~sؤ“-~ ("without") + ~cإ«ra~ ("care, concern").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). Here the prefix conveys completeness or thoroughness rather than removal.

The word ~sicher~ is one of German's earliest Latin loanwords, entering Germanic speech during the Roman period when Germanic tribes had extensive contact with the Roman Empire along the ~limes~. Latin ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ is also the source of English ~secure~ and ~sure~ (the latter via Old French). The prefix ~ab-~ in ~absichern~ intensifies the meaning beyond simple ~sichern~ ("to secure") to suggest a more thorough safeguarding or hedging against risk.
en.absolvieren:
From Latin ~absolvؤ“re~ ("to set free, to complete, to finish"), composed of ~ab-~ ("from, away from"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"), + ~solvإچ~ ("to release, loosen, dissolve"), which is itself from ~sؤ“-~ ("apart, aside, away"), from PIE *~s(w)ؤ“d~ (ablative of *~swأ©~, "self"), + ~luإچ~ ("to untie, set free"), from PIE *~lewhâ‚ƒ-~ ("to wash, to loosen"). The German suffix ~-ieren~ marks the standard adaptation of Latin verbs.

The Latin original meant "to loosen from, to release, to acquit" â€” hence its theological use: a priest ~absolviert~ a penitent from sin (gives ~Absolution~). But German has developed a second, thoroughly secular meaning: "to complete, to pass" (~eine Prأ¼fung absolvieren~, "to pass an exam"; ~sein Studium absolvieren~, "to finish one's degree"; ~ein Training absolvieren~, "to complete a training course"). This semantic shift from "releasing from obligation" to "completing an obligation" is almost paradoxical â€” yet both senses share the notion of bringing a process to its end. The derived nouns ~Absolvent~ (male graduate) and ~Absolventin~ (female graduate) are specific to the completionist meaning.
en.abstellen:
From Middle High German ~abestellen~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). Here the prefix conveys cessation or deactivation.

The PIE root *~stel-~ is extraordinarily productive: it yielded not only German ~stellen~ but also English ~stall~, ~still~, ~install~, and Greek ~stؤ“lؤ“~ (an upright stone slab). The compound ~abstellen~ has developed two distinct semantic branches: a concrete sense of "to put down, to park" (as in parking a vehicle) and an abstract sense of "to turn off, to stop" (as in stopping an engine or eliminating an abuse). Both senses derive logically from the idea of placing something away from its active state.
en.abstimmen:
From ~ab-~ + ~stimmen~. Compound of:

- ~stimmen~: From MHG ~stimmen~, from OHG *~stimmen~ (attested in the compound ~gistimmen~), a denominative verb from ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). ~Stimme~ descends from MHG ~stimme~, from OHG ~stimma~ or ~stimna~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnإچ~, from PIE *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle"). Cognate with English ~steven~ (archaic "voice"), Old English ~stefn~, and Ancient Greek ~stأ³ma~ ("mouth").
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)-~, from OHG ~ab(a)-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ab~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). As a verbal prefix, it often conveys removal, completion, or adjustment.

The two principal senses of ~abstimmen~ â€” "to vote" and "to coordinate, tune" â€” both arise from the root meaning of ~Stimme~. The voting sense is literal: casting one's ~voice~ in a poll. The coordination sense derives from the musical meaning of ~stimmen~ ("to tune an instrument") â€” bringing multiple voices or elements into harmony. The prefix ~ab-~ here suggests finality or settlement, as in reaching a settled result. The connection between ~Stimme~ ("voice") and Ancient Greek ~stأ³ma~ ("mouth") reveals an ancient conceptual link between the organ that produces sound and the sound itself. Modern German ~Abstimmung~ can mean either "vote" or "coordination" depending on context â€” a homonymy that occasionally produces amusing ambiguities.
en.abwarten:
Compound of ~ab-~ + ~warten~:

- ~warten~: From MHG ~warten~, from OHG ~wartؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wardؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~wardؤپnؤ…~ ("to guard, protect, watch over"), derived from *~warduz~ ("a guard"), ultimately from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard"). Cognate with English ~ward~, Swedish ~vأ¥rda~.
- ~ab-~: From MHG ~ab(e)~, from OHG ~ab(a)~, from Proto-Germanic *~ab~ ("off, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). Here it adds a sense of completion or patient observation from a distance.

~Abwarten~ means "to wait and see, to bide one's time." Where simple ~warten~ is neutral waiting, ~abwarten~ implies patience with an eye on the outcome: watching from above (~ab-~ "down from") until events unfold. The famous German proverb ~Abwarten und Tee trinken~ ("Wait and drink tea") counsels patience -- a calmer counterpart to the English "wait and see." The root ~warten~ itself preserves the original sentinel sense: one who ~abwartet~ is, etymologically, a guard watching from a vantage point, waiting for the situation to resolve itself.
en.abziehen:
From MHG ~abeziehen~, from OHG ~abaziohan~, from Proto-Germanic *~abateuhanؤ…~. Cognate with Gothic ~aftiuhan~. Compound of ~ab-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with English ~tug~, Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead").
- ~ab-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~ ("off, away"). This separable prefix conveys removal, withdrawal, or subtraction.

~Abziehen~ means "to pull off, to withdraw, to subtract, to depart" -- always with the core image of pulling something away. Its semantic range is astonishing: "to skin" an animal (~einem Hasen das Fell abziehen~), "to subtract" in arithmetic (~drei von fأ¼nf abziehen~), "to withdraw" troops (~Truppen abziehen~), and colloquially "to leave, to take off." The Gothic cognate ~aftiuhan~ confirms that this compound existed already in the earliest recorded Germanic, making it one of the oldest ~ab-~ compounds in the language family.
en.achten: From Middle High German ~ahten~ ("to consider, heed, esteem"), from Old High German ~ahtإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ahtإچn~, a denominative verb derived from *~ahtu~ ("consideration, attention"). The noun *~ahtu~ is of uncertain pre-Germanic origin; it may be related to Proto-Germanic *~ahjanؤ…~ ("to think"), which has been compared to PIE *~hâ‚ƒekت·-~ ("to see") â€” cognate with the Gothic verb ~ahjan~ ("to think") â€” though this connection remains debated among scholars. The verb's core sense of "weighing, attending to" is reflected in its modern meanings: to respect (~achten~), to heed (~darauf achten~), and, with the negative prefix, to disregard (~missachten~). Cognate with Dutch ~achten~, Low German ~achten~, English ~aught~ (from the related noun).
en.agieren:
From Latin ~agere~ ("to do, to drive, to act"), with the German suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin verb descends from Proto-Italic *~agإچ~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ ("to drive"), one of the most prolific roots in all of Indo-European. Cognate with Greek ~agإچ~ ("I lead"), Sanskrit ~ajati~ ("to drive, propel"), and Old Norse ~aka~ ("to drive").

The PIE root *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ has produced an extraordinary family of English words through Latin: ~act~, ~agent~, ~agile~, ~agenda~ ("things to be done"), ~navigate~ ("to drive a ship"), ~ambiguous~ ("driven both ways"), ~prodigal~ ("driving forth"), ~examine~ (from ~exigere~, "to drive out, to weigh"), and ~essay~ (from ~exagium~, "a weighing"). Through Greek it gives ~pedagogue~ ("child-driver"), ~demagogue~ ("people-driver"), and ~synagogue~ ("led together"). German ~agieren~ entered the language in the 16th century as part of the massive influx of Latin vocabulary during the Renaissance and Reformation. Unlike native German verbs, ~agieren~ always takes the regular weak ~-ieren~ conjugation pattern, a reliable marker of its foreign origin. In modern usage, it often has a theatrical connotation ("to perform on stage") alongside its general meaning of "to act."
en.ahnen:
From Middle High German ~anen~, from Old High German *~anإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anإچn~, derived from the preposition *~ana~ ("on"), from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on"). Cognate with Middle Low German ~ؤپnen~, dialectal Dutch ~anen~ ("to conjecture"), and Saterland Frisian ~oanje~ ("to suspect, guess").

The semantic development from a preposition meaning "on" to a verb meaning "to suspect, to have a presentiment" likely passed through an intermediate sense of "to be on the track of" or "to be onto something." Despite the phonetic similarity, ~ahnen~ ("to suspect") and ~Ahn~ ("ancestor," from Proto-Germanic *~anأ´~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en-~ "ancestor") are etymologically unrelated -- their resemblance is a coincidence that has nonetheless influenced German speakers' perception of both words. The noun ~Ahnung~ ("inkling, presentiment") is derived from ~ahnen~ and appears in the common expression ~keine Ahnung~ ("no idea"), one of the first phrases many German learners acquire.
en.aktivieren:
From ~aktiv~ + ~-ieren~. ~Aktiv~ is borrowed from Latin ~ؤپctؤ«vus~ ("active, practical"), derived from the verb ~agإچ~ ("to act, to do, to drive"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ ("to drive, to lead") â€” the same root that gives Greek ~أ،gإچ~ ("I lead"), Sanskrit ~أ،jati~ ("drives"), and English ~act~, ~agent~, ~agile~, ~agenda~, and ~essay~ (via French ~essai~ from Latin ~exigere~). The suffix ~-ieren~ marks the standard German pattern for creating verbs from adjectives of Latin or French origin.

~Aktivieren~ means "to activate, to enable, to put into operation," covering a wide range from the physical (activating a machine) to the chemical (converting a substance into a reactive form) to the digital (enabling a software feature). The prefix-free simplicity of ~aktivieren~ masks a complex semantic history: PIE *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ originally meant "to drive cattle" â€” from this pastoral root grew the entire vocabulary of human agency and action. The antonym ~deaktivieren~ ("to deactivate") and the noun ~Aktivierung~ ("activation") follow standard German derivation patterns.
en.aktualisieren:
Formed from ~aktuell~ + ~-isieren~ (the German adaptation of the productive suffix ~-ize~). The adjective ~aktuell~ was borrowed from French ~actuel~ ("current, present"), which in turn comes from Late Latin ~actuؤپlis~ ("active, practical"), from Latin ~ؤپctus~ ("a doing"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ ("to drive, to do").

A notable false friend: German ~aktuell~ means "current, up-to-date," not "actual" (which is German ~tatsأ¤chlich~). Accordingly, ~aktualisieren~ means "to update" or "to refresh," not "to actualize." This semantic divergence between German and English is a common source of confusion for learners. The ~-isieren~ suffix is the standard German mechanism for creating verbs from adjectives of Latin or French origin, parallel to English ~-ize~ and French ~-iser~.
en.akzeptieren:
Adapted from Latin ~acceptؤپre~, the frequentative form of ~accipiإچ~ ("to receive, accept, take"). ~Accipiإچ~ is itself a compound of ~ad-~ ("towards") + ~capiإچ~ ("to take"), from PIE *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to grasp, seize") â€” the same root that gives English ~capture~, ~capable~, ~heave~, and German ~heben~ ("to lift").

A frequentative verb in Latin was formed by adding ~-tؤپre~ to a past participle stem, expressing repeated or habitual action: ~acceptؤپre~ thus originally meant "to take repeatedly" or "to receive willingly." The German adaptation ~akzeptieren~ adds the standard ~-ieren~ suffix. The Latin past participle ~acceptus~ ("accepted, welcome") also survives directly in the German adjective ~akzeptabel~ and the noun ~Akzeptanz~. The spelling with ~kz~ (rather than ~cc~ as in Latin) reflects a German phonological convention: Latin double ~cc~ before a front vowel, pronounced [ktح،s], is rendered ~kz~.
en.analysieren: Borrowed from French ~analyser~ with the addition of the German verbal suffix ~-ieren~. French ~analyser~ derives from the noun ~analyse~, from Medieval Latin ~analysis~, from Ancient Greek ~analusis~ ("a dissolving, a breaking up"), from ~analuein~ ("to unloose, undo"), composed of ~ana-~ ("up, back, throughout") + ~luein~ ("to loosen, untie"), from PIE *~lew-~ ("to cut, loosen, divide"). The suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard German mechanism for absorbing foreign verbs, borrowed itself from Old French ~-ier~ (from Latin ~-ؤپre~ verb endings filtered through French phonology). The etymological image behind ~analysieren~ is thus one of untying or loosening â€” taking something complex apart into its component threads, the opposite of synthesis (~sunthesis~, "a putting together"). Greek ~luein~ also gave English ~lysis~, ~catalysis~, ~dialysis~, and ~paralysis~ â€” all words about loosening, dissolving, or undoing. German could have coined a native equivalent (perhaps *~zergliedern~, which does exist as a near-synonym meaning "to dissect"), but the Greek-Latin internationalism won out, as it did in nearly every European language.
en.anbieten:
From Middle High German ~anebieten~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~bieten~:

- ~bieten~: From MHG ~bieten~, from OHG ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ewdت°-~ ("to be awake, aware"). A class II strong verb whose PIE root conveys notions of awareness and offering.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix adds a sense of direction toward or presentation to.

The directional ~an-~ prefix focuses ~bieten~ ("to offer, bid") toward a specific recipient: ~anbieten~ means to offer something directly to someone. The verb's PIE ancestry in "awareness" is semantically apt â€” to offer is to make another aware of what is available. Cognate with Dutch ~aanbieden~.
en.anbringen:
From ~an-~ + ~bringen~. Compound of:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, from pre-Germanic *~bت°renل¸±-~, likely from PIE *~bت°er-~ ("to carry") + *~hâ‚پneل¸±-~ ("to bring, reach"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys attachment, arrival, or application.

~anbringen~ means "to attach, install, mount" (a shelf, a sign), "to apply" (paint, criticism), and "to manage to place" (a joke, a remark into a conversation). All senses flow from the core image of bringing something ~onto~ a surface or into a context. The idiomatic sense of slipping a remark into conversation (~einen Witz anbringen~) reveals a playful metaphorical extension: one ~attaches~ a quip to the flow of talk. The past participle ~angebracht~ has developed an independent adjectival life meaning "appropriate, fitting" (~das ist nicht angebracht~ = "that's not appropriate") â€” what has been properly placed is deemed suitable.
en.anerkennen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~erkennen~ (itself ~er-~ + ~kennen~):

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijanؤ…~, a causative form of *~kunnanؤ…~ ("to be able, to know"), formed with the suffix *~-janؤ…~. From PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds the sense of directing recognition toward something, affirming or acknowledging it.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This inseparable prefix conveys achievement or completion of the base action.

~Erkennen~ (from OHG ~irkennen~, Proto-West Germanic *~uت€kannijan~) means "to recognize, to perceive," and ~anerkennen~ builds on this: "to acknowledge, to recognize officially, to appreciate." The double-prefix structure (~an-~ + ~er-~ + ~kennen~) is comparatively rare in German and reflects layered derivation: first, ~er-~ transforms ~kennen~ ("to know") into ~erkennen~ ("to come to know, to recognize"), then ~an-~ directs that recognition outward as a formal act of acknowledgment. The PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ is spectacularly productive across the Indo-European family: it yields English ~know~, ~can~, and ~ken~ (Scottish "to know"), Latin ~cognإچscere~ (whence English ~cognition~ and ~recognize~), Greek ~خ³خ½ل؟¶دƒخ¹د‚~ (~gnosis~), and Sanskrit ~jؤپnؤپti~ ("to know"). Cognate with Bavarian ~kennan~, Dutch ~erkennen~.
en.anfangen:
From Middle High German ~anevؤپhen~, from Old High German ~anafؤپhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anafؤ…ج„han~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~fangen~:

- ~fangen~: From MHG ~vؤپhen~, from OHG ~fؤپhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fؤ…ج„han~, from Proto-Germanic *~fanhanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to secure, attach, fasten"). A Class VII strong verb. The modern present stem ~fang-~ was borrowed from the past forms in Middle Low German ~vangen~ and then spread into Central German in the early modern period â€” a process also seen in ~hأ¤ngen~. The archaic English ~fang~ ("fang of a tooth; to seize") descends from the same root.
- ~an-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix expresses direction toward or attachment.

The semantic shift from "to catch, seize" to "to begin" â€” preserved in ~anfangen~ â€” is striking. The underlying image is one of grasping a thing and setting it in motion, a sense also alive in the noun ~Anfang~ ("beginning, start"). Cognate with Dutch ~aanvangen~, Low German ~anfangen~.
en.anfertigen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~fertigen~:

- ~fertigen~: Derived from the adjective ~fertig~ ("finished, ready"), from MHG ~fertec~, from OHG ~fartؤ«g~, originally meaning "ready for a journey," from the noun ~fart~ ("journey"). Cognate with Dutch ~vaardig~ ("ready, swift") and Old English ~fierd~ ("expedition, campaign").
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). In this compound, the prefix adds a sense of directed production or purposeful creation.

The semantic journey of ~fertig~ is remarkable: it began as "ready for travel" (from ~Fahrt~, "journey"), then broadened to "prepared" in general, and finally settled on "finished, completed" -- essentially reversing its temporal orientation from looking forward to a journey to looking back at a completed task. The compound ~anfertigen~ thus literally meant something like "to bring to a state of readiness" and now means "to produce, to manufacture." English ~ready~ shows a parallel development from Old English ~rا£de~ ("prepared for riding").
en.anfأ¼hren:
From Middle High German ~anevأ¼eren~, from Old High German ~anafuoren~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~fأ¼hren~:

- ~fأ¼hren~: From MHG ~vأ¼eren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, the causative of ~fahren~ ("to travel"), from Proto-Germanic *~fإچrijanؤ…~ ("to cause to go"), from *~faranؤ…~ ("to go, travel"), from Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ ("going, passage"). Cognate with English ~fare~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix here conveys direction and guidance.

The causative relationship between ~fahren~ ("to go") and ~fأ¼hren~ ("to lead, cause to go") is a textbook example of the Proto-Germanic causative formation with *~-janؤ…~: ~fahren~ means "to travel oneself," while ~fأ¼hren~ means "to make someone travel" -- hence "to lead." The compound ~anfأ¼hren~ has developed two strikingly different meanings: "to lead, to head" (neutral) and "to dupe, to deceive" (pejorative). The second sense likely arose from the image of leading someone astray, a semantic path also visible in English ~to lead someone on~.
en.angeben: From Middle High German ~anegeben~, equivalent to prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~, "on, onto") and ~geben~, from MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Victor Klemperer notes in his ~Lingua Tertii Imperii~ (1947) that the sense "to brag, to boast" â€” the meaning a learner encounters when someone is called an ~Angeber~ â€” entered German from Yiddish. This is a striking example of semantic drift: a humble "give" verb acquiring social arrogance through a specific community's usage.
en.angehen:
From Middle High German ~angؤ“n~, ~angؤپn~, from Old High German ~anagؤپn~. Equivalent to ~an-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤ“n~, ~gؤپn~, from OHG ~gؤ“n~, ~gؤپn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to go").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of approaching, beginning, or concerning.

~Angehen~ is one of German's most versatile prefix verbs, with meanings that span "to approach" (~jemanden angehen~), "to concern" (~das geht mich nichts an~ â€” "that doesn't concern me"), "to begin" (~die Arbeit geht an~), and "to tackle" (~ein Problem angehen~). The "concerning" sense is particularly idiomatic: it derives from the physical image of something walking up to you â€” what approaches you is what concerns you. The past participle ~angegangen~ and the separable prefix behavior (~es geht mich an~) mark this as a separable-prefix compound. Cognate with Dutch ~aangaan~.
en.angehأ¶ren:
From Middle High German ~anegehإ“ren~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~gehأ¶ren~:

- ~gehأ¶ren~: From MHG ~gehإ“ren~ ("to listen to, obey"), from OHG ~gihإچrian~ ("to hearken to"), from Proto-West Germanic *~gahauت€ijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gahauzijanؤ…~. The prefix ~ge-~ here is the old perfective/collective prefix.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a sense of attachment or belonging-to.

The semantic evolution of ~gehأ¶ren~ is fascinating: it began as "to listen to, to obey" (related to ~hأ¶ren~, "to hear") and shifted to "to belong to." The logic is that what one hearkens to is what one is beholden to, and by extension, what one belongs to. Old English ~ؤ،ehؤ«eran~ and Gothic ~gahausjan~ are cognates that retained the original hearing sense. The further compound ~angehأ¶ren~ ("to be a member of") intensifies the belonging sense with ~an-~ ("to, at"), emphasizing attachment to a group. English ~hear~ is the root cognate, from PIE *~hâ‚‚eل¸±-~ ("sharp") + *~hâ‚‚ows-~ ("ear"), literally "to be sharp-eared."
en.angreifen:
From Middle High German ~anegrؤ«fen~ ("to grab, seize upon, assault, attack"), from Old High German ~anagrؤ«fan~ ("to touch, grab, seize, attack"), from Proto-West Germanic *~anagrؤ«pan~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grؤ«fen~, from OHG ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~, Lithuanian ~griebti~ ("to seize").
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, at"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). As a verbal prefix, ~an-~ conveys contact, approach, or the beginning of an action.

The semantic development from "to seize upon" to "to attack" is straightforward: an attack is an aggressive grasping at the enemy. In Austrian German, ~angreifen~ retains the older, gentler meaning "to touch, to handle," synonymous with ~anfassen~ or ~berأ¼hren~. The related noun ~Angriff~ ("attack") and the sibling compound ~begreifen~ ("to comprehend," literally "to grasp at") both preserve different metaphorical extensions of the same root. Cognate with Dutch ~aangrijpen~ ("to seize, to grip"), Low German ~angriepen~.
en.anhalten:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~. Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~, Icelandic ~halda~, Swedish ~hأ¥lla~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, onto"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). As a separable prefix, it adds a sense of continuation or initiation.

~Anhalten~ has two principal senses: "to stop" (transitive and intransitive) and "to persist, to continue" (~die Hitze hأ¤lt an~ -- "the heat persists"). These seem contradictory: how can one word mean both "to stop" and "to continue"? The answer lies in the prefix: ~an-~ adds "on, onto," so ~anhalten~ is literally "to hold on." Holding on can mean braking to a standstill (holding on to stop forward motion) or enduring (holding on through time). The derived noun ~Anhalter~ means "hitchhiker" -- someone who ~hأ¤lt Autos an~ ("stops cars"). ~Per Anhalter fahren~ ("to travel by hitchhiking") is the phrase rendered in the German title of Douglas Adams's famous novel: ~Per Anhalter durch die Galaxis~ (~The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy~).
en.anhأ¶ren:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~hأ¶ren~:

- ~hأ¶ren~: From MHG ~hإ“ren~, from OHG ~hإچren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauت€ijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijanؤ…~ ("to hear"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚ل¸±hâ‚‚owsyأ©ti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), a compound of *~hâ‚‚eل¸±-~ ("sharp") + *~hâ‚‚ows-~ ("ear") + *~-yأ©ti~ (denominative suffix). Cognate with English ~hear~, Dutch ~horen~, Danish ~hأ¸re~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a sense of directed, attentive listening.

The PIE etymology of "to hear" is delightfully literal: *~hâ‚‚ل¸±hâ‚‚owsyأ©ti~ means "to be sharp-eared," combining the root for "sharp" (also the source of Latin ~acer~ and English ~acrid~) with the root for "ear" (source of Latin ~auris~ and English ~ear~). While ~hأ¶ren~ means simply "to hear," ~anhأ¶ren~ implies deliberate, sustained listening -- "to listen to" rather than merely "to hear." The reflexive ~sich etwas anhأ¶ren~ adds the further nuance of enduring something one must listen to, as in ~sich eine Predigt anhأ¶ren~ ("to sit through a sermon").
en.ankommen:
From Middle High German ~anekomen~, from Old High German ~anaqueman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anakweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~anakwemanؤ…~ ("to come to, come at, arrive"). Compound of ~an-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~quemen~, ~komen~, from OHG ~queman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت·em-~ ("to step"). Cognate with English ~come~, Latin ~venؤ«re~.
- ~an-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix indicates arrival at a destination.

Gothic preserves the direct ancestor of the compound in ~anaqiman~, showing how old the formation is. The modern idiom ~es kommt drauf an~ ("it depends") â€” literally "it arrives on it" â€” retains the spatial logic of the prefix. Cognate with Dutch ~aankomen~.
en.ankأ¼ndigen:
~an-~ + ~kأ¼ndigen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~kأ¼ndigen~:

- ~kأ¼ndigen~: From MHG ~kأ¼ndigen~ ("to make known"), derived from the adjective ~kأ¼ndic~ (modern ~kundig~, "knowledgeable, informed"). ~Kundig~ descends from OHG ~kundؤ«g~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunأ¾ؤ«g~, from Proto-Germanic *~kunأ¾ؤ«gaz~, from *~kunأ¾az~ ("known"), originally the past participle of *~kunnanؤ…~ ("to know, to be able"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The separable prefix adds a sense of direction toward an audience.

~Ankأ¼ndigen~ means "to announce, to herald, to give advance notice of." The prefix ~an-~ directs the announcement ~toward~ an audience: to ~ankأ¼ndigen~ is to make something known ~at~ or ~to~ someone. The PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know") is one of the most productive in all Indo-European languages: it gave Latin ~gnإچscإچ~ / ~nإچscإچ~ ("to get to know") â€” whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~, ~noble~ (via ~nإچbilis~, "well-known"), ~note~, and ~notion~ â€” as well as Greek ~gignل¹“skإچ~ ("to perceive") â€” whence ~gnosis~, ~diagnosis~, and ~prognosis~. German ~kأ¶nnen~ ("to be able") descends from the same root, reflecting the ancient idea that knowing and being able are the same thing. English ~know~ likewise descends from *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ through the Germanic branch, via Old English ~cnؤپwan~. Interestingly, ~kأ¼ndigen~ alone means "to terminate, to give notice" (as in quitting a job), while ~ankأ¼ndigen~ means simply "to announce" â€” the prefix reshapes the word entirely. Cognate with Dutch ~aankondigen~.
en.anlegen: Formed from prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~, "on, onto") and ~legen~, from MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~. The compound traces to Proto-Germanic *~analagjanؤ…~. The causative root *~lagjanؤ…~ â€” "to lay, to cause to lie" â€” is itself from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~, the causative of *~legh-~ ("to lie"). English ~lay~ is a direct cognate. ~Anlegen~ is semantically rich: to lay something on (dock a boat), to put on (a garment), to invest (money), or to create (a garden). The single prefix transforms one root into a dozen contextual meanings.
en.anmelden: Formed from prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~, "on, onto") and ~melden~, from MHG ~mأ«lden~, from OHG ~mأ«ldإچn~, ~mأ«ldؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~melأ¾إچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~melأ¾إچnؤ…~, from PIE *~meldت°-~ ("to ask, pray, speak"). The compound means "to register, to log in, to announce one's arrival." The prefix ~an-~ contributes a sense of directed address: one reports oneself ~an~ (to) an authority or a system. In the digital age, ~anmelden~ has become the standard German verb for "to log in," while ~abmelden~ serves as its antonym "to log out" â€” a mirror-image pair that modern users encounter constantly.
en.annehmen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb.
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). As a separable prefix, ~an-~ adds a sense of acceptance, approach, or adoption â€” "taking something onto oneself."

The compound has developed a rich semantic range: "to accept" (an offer), "to adopt" (a child), "to assume" (a hypothesis), and "to pass" (a law). The reflexive ~sich annehmen~ ("to take care of, attend to") adds yet another shade. The PIE root *~nem-~ is the same root underlying Greek ~خ½خ­خ¼د‰~ (~nemإچ~, "to distribute, manage") and German ~Nomade~ (via Latin ~nomas~, "one who wanders in search of pasture") â€” at bottom, ~annehmen~ means taking what is due to you. Cognate with Dutch ~aannemen~.
en.anordnen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~ordnen~:

- ~ordnen~: From MHG ~ordenen~, from OHG ~ordinإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ordinإچn~ ("to order, put in order"), borrowed from Latin ~إچrdinؤپre~ ("to arrange, set in order"), from ~إچrdإچ~ ("row, rank, order"). A doublet of ~ordinieren~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, onto"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys "on, at, to" or initiation of an action.

~Anordnen~ means "to order, to arrange, to decree." The Latin source ~إچrdإچ~ originally meant "a row of threads in a loom" before generalizing to any ordered arrangement. German borrowed the verb early (in the OHG period) but gave it a native prefix: ~an-~ adds the sense of directing an order toward someone. The doublet is instructive: ~ordinieren~ entered later via ecclesiastical Latin (meaning "to ordain a priest"), while ~ordnen~ and ~anordnen~ came earlier and stayed secular. English ~order~ and ~ordain~ mirror this split.
en.anpassen:
Equivalent to ~an-~ + ~passen~:

- ~passen~: From MHG ~pazzen~, borrowed from Italian ~passare~ or French ~passer~, from Vulgar Latin *~passؤپre~, from Latin ~passus~ ("step, pace").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of fitting something onto or adjusting to something.

~Anpassen~ means "to adapt, to adjust, to fit" â€” one ~passt~ clothing ~an~ (adjusts the fit), ~passt sich an~ a new environment (adapts oneself), or ~passt~ a plan ~an~ new circumstances (adjusts it). The reflexive ~sich anpassen~ ("to adapt oneself") has become central in biological (~Anpassung an die Umwelt~, "adaptation to the environment") and social (~sich der Gesellschaft anpassen~, "to conform to society") discourse. The derived noun ~Anpassung~ ("adaptation") is the standard German translation of Darwin's concept of adaptation â€” a Latin-rooted verb with an Arabic-influenced French intermediary, now wearing a Germanic prefix and suffix to discuss evolutionary biology. Cognate with Dutch ~aanpassen~.
en.anregen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~regen~:

- ~regen~: From MHG ~regen~ ("to make protrude, to erect, to excite, to move"), from Proto-Germanic *~hragjanؤ…~ ("to make stand, to wake up, to incite"), a causative formation from *~hreganؤ…~ ("to jut out"), from PIE *~krek-~ ("to project, to protrude").
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a sense of initiating or stimulating action.

The verb ~anregen~ means "to stimulate, to inspire, to suggest," carrying a sense of setting something into motion intellectually or physically. The causative origin is visible: Proto-Germanic *~hragjanؤ…~ meant "to cause to stand up or project," and this evolved through Middle High German into the more abstract sense of stirring someone to action or thought. The noun ~Anregung~ ("suggestion, stimulus, inspiration") is widely used in both casual and academic German. The related verb ~sich regen~ ("to stir, to move") preserves the older physical sense. In modern German, ~anregend~ ("stimulating") can describe a conversation, a cup of coffee, or an idea equally well, while the homograph ~Regen~ ("rain") is an entirely unrelated word from a different Proto-Germanic root (*~regnؤ…~).
en.anrufen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~ (also ~hruofan~), from Proto-West Germanic *~hropan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hropanan~. The Proto-Germanic origin is unclear; it may be distantly related to *~hrothiz~ ("glory, fame"), but the exact connection is debated. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~, English dialectal ~roop~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, at"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds a directional quality, turning a general shout into a call directed at someone.

The most common modern use of ~anrufen~ is "to telephone," a meaning that emerged only in the 20th century with the spread of telephone technology. Before that, ~anrufen~ meant simply "to call out to someone" or "to invoke" (as in a legal or religious context: ~das Gericht anrufen~, "to appeal to the court"). The initial ~h-~ in OHG ~hruofan~ (reflecting Proto-Germanic *~hr-~) was lost during the transition to Middle High German, as happened with many words beginning with ~hr-~, ~hl-~, ~hn-~. Interestingly, ~rufen~ historically wavered between strong and weak conjugation; weak forms persisted until the 19th century, and the lack of umlaut in the present tense (~du rufst~, not *~du rأ¼fst~) is a relic of this weak-verb past.
en.ansagen:
From Middle High German ~anesagen~, from Old High German ~anasagؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anasaggjan~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~sagen~:

- ~sagen~: From MHG ~sagen~, from OHG ~sagؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~saggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sagjanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~sekت·-~ ("to say"). Cognate with English ~say~, Dutch ~zeggen~, Danish ~sige~, Swedish ~sأ¤ga~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix conveys direction -- saying something ~to~ someone, making an announcement.

The PIE root *~sekت·-~ also produced Latin ~inquit~ ("says") and Old Irish ~insce~ ("speech"). In modern German, ~ansagen~ has found new life beyond its traditional meaning of "to announce": it is widely used in the colloquial expression ~angesagt sein~ ("to be trendy, in vogue"), a usage that emerged in the late 20th century. The word ~Ansager~ ("announcer") was the standard term for a television presenter before the English loanword ~Moderator~ largely replaced it.
en.anschauen: Formed from prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~, "on, onto") and ~schauen~, from MHG ~schouwen~, from OHG ~scouwإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~skawwإچnؤ…~ ("to look, see, watch"), from PIE *~(s)kewhâ‚پ-~ ("to heed, look, feel, take note of"). The prefix transforms "to look" into "to look at" with directed, attentive focus: to gaze upon something. A near-synonym ~ansehen~ (~sehen~ from MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice")) can be interchangeable, but ~anschauen~ tends to carry a slightly more contemplative or visual-aesthetic sense â€” one ~schaut~ a painting or a landscape ~an~. Note the German idiom ~Schau an!~ ("Well, look at that!") â€” a trace of the older sense of directed, surprised gaze.
en.anschlieأںen:
From Middle High German ~aneslieب¥en~, equivalent to ~an-~ + ~schlieأںen~:

- ~schlieأںen~: From MHG ~slieب¥en~, from OHG ~slioب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sleutanؤ…~, from PIE *~klew-~ ("to close, shut").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of attachment or connection.

The semantic range of ~anschlieأںen~ runs from the physical ("to connect, to plug in" â€” ~den Computer anschlieأںen~) to the social ("to join" â€” ~sich einer Gruppe anschlieأںen~, "to join a group") to the temporal ("to follow" â€” ~anschlieأںend~, "subsequently"). All these meanings share the image of closing something onto something else â€” locking one thing to another. The reflexive ~sich anschlieأںen~ is particularly productive: one can attach oneself to a group, an opinion, or a movement. The derived adjective ~anschlieأںend~ ("subsequent, following") has become one of German's most common temporal connectors, its etymological sense of "locking on after" faded to a mere "and then." Cognate with Dutch ~aansluiten~.
en.ansehen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with Dutch ~zien~, English ~see~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix directs the base verb's action toward a goal or surface.

The ~an-~ prefix gives ~sehen~ a focused, directed quality: where ~sehen~ is "to see" in general, ~ansehen~ is "to look at" something specifically. The reflexive ~sich ansehen~ ("to watch, to look at for oneself") extends this to viewing as an activity â€” watching a film, inspecting a site. The noun ~Ansehen~ ("reputation, standing") developed from the sense of "how one is looked upon" â€” social regard crystallized from the directed gaze. Cognate with Dutch ~aanzien~ ("to regard; reputation"), which preserves exactly the same semantic range.
en.ansetzen:
From Middle High German ~anesetzen~, from Old High German ~anasezzen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, to place"), a causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~, from the root *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~, Yiddish ~×–×¢×¦×ں~.
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~ ("on, onto"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds a sense of attachment, initiation, or application.

The relationship between ~setzen~ and ~sitzen~ mirrors English ~set~ and ~sit~ exactly: both pairs reflect the Proto-Germanic causative pattern where *~-jan-~ turns "to sit" into "to make sit, to place." ~Ansetzen~ is remarkably polysemous in modern German: it can mean "to put on" (a pot), "to schedule" (a meeting), "to begin" (an approach), "to form" (rust), or "to prepare" (a mixture). Compare English ~onset~, which shares the same etymological structure (~on~ + ~set~) and originally meant "an attack" before broadening to "beginning." The PIE root *~sed-~ is one of the most well-attested roots in historical linguistics, producing Latin ~sedؤ“re~ ("to sit"), English ~sit~, ~seat~, ~saddle~, and ~sedan~.
en.ansiedeln:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~siedeln~:

- ~siedeln~: From MHG ~sidelen~, from OHG ~sidalen~, derived from ~sidila~ ("seat"), cognate with Old English ~setl~ ("seat, settle"). Related to ~sitzen~ ("to sit") and hence ultimately to English ~settle~, though not a direct cognate of it.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a sense of establishing or fixing in place.

The connection between sitting and settling is ancient and deeply embedded across the Indo-European family: to settle somewhere is, at its most basic, to sit down and stay. OHG ~sidila~ ("seat") developed into ~Siedlung~ ("settlement"), and the verb ~siedeln~ preserves this link between physical rest and permanent habitation. English ~settle~ followed a parallel path from Old English ~setl~ ("seat, place to sit") to the verb ~setlan~ ("to place, establish"). The compound ~ansiedeln~ emphasizes the purposeful act of establishing a settlement, often used reflexively (~sich ansiedeln~, "to settle down").
en.ansprechen:
~an-~ + ~sprechen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprأ«chen~, from OHG ~sprأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanؤ…~, probably from PIE *~spregت°-~ ("to crackle, make noise").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of direction toward someone or something, initiating contact.

~Ansprechen~ means "to address, to speak to; to appeal to; to respond to (treatment)." The prefix ~an-~ directs the act of speaking toward a target â€” one ~spricht jemanden an~ by approaching and initiating speech. The secondary sense "to appeal to" (~das spricht mich an~, "that appeals to me") developed from the idea of speech landing on and engaging a listener. The medical sense â€” ~auf ein Medikament ansprechen~ ("to respond to a medication") â€” is a 20th-century extension of the same logic: the body "responds" when the treatment "speaks to" it. The PIE root *~spregت°-~ is onomatopoeic â€” crackling into speech â€” making ~ansprechen~ ultimately an echo of the very sound of language itself. Cognate with Dutch ~aanspreken~.
en.anstellen:
From MHG ~anestellen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix adds the sense of placing at, turning on, or setting to work.

~Anstellen~ is one of German's most versatile verbs: "to employ" (~jemanden anstellen~), "to turn on" (~das Wasser anstellen~), "to queue up" (~sich anstellen~), and "to behave, to make a fuss" (~sich anstellen~ in its colloquial sense: ~Stell dich nicht so an!~, "Don't make such a fuss!"). The employment sense is the source of ~Angestellte(r)~ ("employee"), one of the most common nouns in German labor law. The PIE root *~stel-~ is enormously productive: it gave Latin ~stؤپre~ ("to stand"), whence English ~state~, ~station~, ~statue~, and ~install~.
en.anstreben:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~streben~:

- ~streben~: From MHG ~strأ«ben~, from OHG ~strأ«bؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stribؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~stribؤپnؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)ter(hâ‚پ)-~ ("to be stiff, to exert"), the same root that produced Proto-Germanic *~staraz~ ("stiff"). Cognate with Dutch ~streven~ ("to strive"), English ~strive~ (via Old French ~estriver~, itself borrowed from a Germanic source).
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix directs the striving toward a specific goal.

English ~strive~ took a scenic route back to its Germanic origins: it entered English from Old French ~estriver~ ("to quarrel, contend"), which had been borrowed from a Frankish Germanic source related to OHG ~strأ«bؤ“n~. So ~anstreben~ and ~strive~ are cousins who went on very different journeys before ending up with similar meanings. The PIE root *~(s)ter-~ ("to be stiff, rigid") also produced English ~stare~, ~stark~, ~stern~, and ~stereo~ (via Greek ~stereأ³s~, "solid") -- all sharing the core notion of rigidity or firmness.
en.antreten:
~an-~ + ~treten~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trأ«ten~, from OHG ~trأ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudanؤ…~ ("to tread, to step on"). Cognate with English ~tread~.
- ~an-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of stepping toward, stepping up to, or stepping into position.

~Antreten~ covers a wide semantic range unified by the image of stepping forward. In its military sense, it means "to fall in, to line up for muster" â€” the sergeant's bark ~antreten!~ commands soldiers into formation. In sports and politics, it means "to compete, to run" (~gegen einen Gegner antreten~, "to compete against an opponent"; ~zur Wahl antreten~, "to run for election"). With a direct object, it means "to begin" a duty or journey (~eine Reise antreten~, "to set out on a journey"; ~ein Amt antreten~, "to take office"; ~ein Erbe antreten~, "to accept an inheritance"). And in its most physical sense, it means "to kick-start" a motorcycle by stepping on the starter. The derived noun ~Antritt~ ("commencement, start") appears in formal compounds: ~Antrittsrede~ ("inaugural address"), ~Antrittsbesuch~ ("introductory visit"). The metaphor of stepping forward to begin something, to face a challenge, or to take one's place runs through every usage.
en.antworten: From Middle High German ~antwأ¼rten~, from Old High German ~antwurten~, from Proto-Germanic *~andawurdijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~andawurdijؤ…~ ("answer"), itself from *~anda-~ ("against, in return") + *~wurdijؤ…~ ("speech"), a derivative of *~wurdؤ…~ ("word"). The archaic prefix *~anda-~ ("against, in return") is the same element that appears in Gothic ~anda-~ and Old English ~and-~. By surface analysis, the modern verb looks like a denominative of ~Antwort~ ("answer") + ~-en~, which is how most contemporary speakers parse it. The fascinating parallel with English ~answer~ is not accidental: English ~answer~ comes from Old English ~andswaru~ ("a sworn statement against [a charge]"), from ~and-~ ("against") + ~swaru~ ("affirmation, swearing"). Both ~antworten~ and ~answer~ contain the same ancient prefix meaning "against, in return," and both refer to speech made in response to speech â€” though German built on *~wurdؤ…~ ("word") while English built on *~swarإچnؤ…~ ("to swear"). The Proto-Germanic *~anda-~ is cognate with Greek ~ل¼€خ½د„خ¯~ (~antأ­~, "against, opposite"), which gives English ~anti-~. All three descend from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, front, opposite"), whose comparative form *~hâ‚‚أ©nteros~ ("the one facing, the other of two") produced Proto-Germanic *~anأ¾eraz~ â€” the ancestor of both German ~ander~ ("other") and English ~other~. In English, the nasal was lost before the fricative by the Ingvaeonic nasal spirant law (*~anأ¾eraz~ â†’ *~ؤپأ¾eraz~ â†’ OE ~إچأ¾er~ â†’ ~other~), while German preserved it (~ander~). So ~antworten~, Greek ~anti-~, German ~ander~, and English ~other~ are all siblings from the same PIE root â€” the one about facing, fronting, and opposing. Cognate with Dutch ~antwoorden~, Afrikaans ~antwoord~.
en.anweisen:
From Middle High German ~anewؤ«sen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«sen~ (weak verb until the seventeenth or eighteenth century), a West and North Germanic derivation from the adjective ~weise~ ("wise"), from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«saz~, from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know"). Cognate with English ~wise~, Dutch ~wijs~, and related to English ~wit~, ~wisdom~, Latin ~vidؤ“re~ ("to see"), and Sanskrit ~veda~ ("knowledge").
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a directional or purposive sense.

The verb ~anweisen~ means "to instruct, to direct, to assign, to transfer (money)." The semantic chain runs from "seeing" (PIE) through "knowing" (Germanic) to "showing the way" (~weisen~) to "directing someone toward a task" (~anweisen~). The noun ~Anweisung~ ("instruction, directive") is ubiquitous in bureaucratic and technical German. The PIE root *~weyd-~ is the source of an astonishing range of descendants: Latin ~vidؤ“re~ ("to see") gave English ~video~, ~vision~, and ~evidence~; Sanskrit ~Veda~ ("sacred knowledge") named the oldest Hindu scriptures; and Germanic *~wؤ«saz~ produced ~wise~, ~wisdom~, and the German ~-weise~ suffix meaning "in the manner of." The financial sense of ~anweisen~ ("to transfer money") derives from directing funds toward a recipient.
en.anwenden: Formed from prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~, "on, onto") and ~wenden~, from MHG ~wenden~, from OHG ~wenten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wandijanؤ…~ ("to turn"), from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, wind, braid"). Compare Middle Low German ~anwenden~. The prefix adds the sense of directing or turning something toward a purpose: to ~wenden~ (turn) something ~an~ (onto) a task. The English verb ~apply~ (from Latin ~applicare~, "to fold toward") performs exactly the same semantic work by different means.
en.anzeigen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~zeigen~:

- ~zeigen~: From MHG ~zeigen~, from OHG ~zeigإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taigإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikإچnؤ…~, from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to point out, show"). Cognate with Dutch ~tonen~, Latin ~dؤ«cإچ~ ("to say, show"), Greek ~deiknأ؛إچ~ ("to show").
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix directs the base verb's action toward a recipient or surface.

The ~an-~ prefix sharpens the sense of ~zeigen~ from "to show" into "to indicate, point at, display." In legal German, ~anzeigen~ specifically means "to report to authorities" â€” to point someone out to the state â€” giving the word a charged legal register absent from the neutral ~zeigen~. The noun ~Anzeige~ covers both "advertisement" (a directed display) and "police report" (a directed accusation), a semantic duality that reflects the verb's core gesture of pointing something out to a recipient. Cognate with Dutch ~aangeven~.
en.anziehen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanan~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, to lead"). Cognate with English ~tow~, ~tug~, and ~duke~ (the last via Latin ~dux~, "leader," from the same root). Also related to obsolete English ~tee~ ("to draw, lead").
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, at"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds the sense of pulling something onto oneself.

The semantic range of ~anziehen~ is remarkably broad: "to put on (clothing)," "to attract," "to tighten (a screw)," and even "to accelerate." All these meanings flow logically from the core image of "pulling onto": you pull clothes onto your body, a magnet pulls iron toward itself, you pull a screw tighter. The Dutch expression ~zich iets aantrekken van~ ("to take something to heart") mirrors the western German idiom ~sich etwas anziehen von~, suggesting a shared metaphorical tradition. English ~attract~ is a Latin calque of the same concept (~ad-~ + ~trahere~ = "to pull toward").
en.arbeiten: From Old High German ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijanؤ…~, ultimately from PIE *~hâ‚ƒأ³rbت°os~ ("orphan"). The semantic evolution from "orphan" through "hardship, toil" (the lot of the fatherless) to "work" reflects a shift from social condition to the labor it entailed. Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, Old Saxon ~arvedian~, Gothic ~arbaidjan~, and â€” from the same PIE root â€” English ~orphan~ and Greek ~orphanos~.
en.atmen:
From Middle High German ~ؤپtemen~, from Old High German ~ؤپtamإچn~, derived from Proto-Germanic *~ؤ“أ¾maz~ ("breath"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚پehâ‚پtmأ©n-~ ("breath"). Equivalent to ~Atem~ ("breath") + ~-en~. Cognate with Dutch ~ademen~ ("to breathe").

Latin ~anima~ ("breath, soul") and Greek ~psychؤ“~ ("breath, soul") show the same pattern. The archaic German doublet ~Odem~ ("breath"), now largely poetic, also descends from *~ؤ“أ¾maz~ via a different phonological path.
en.aufbauen:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~bإ«wen~, from OHG ~bإ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bإ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~bإ«anؤ…~ ("to dwell, reside"), from PIE *~bت°uH-~ ("to be, become, grow"). The same PIE root yields English ~be~, Latin ~fuؤ«~ ("I was"), and Greek ~phأ؛إچ~ ("to grow").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-Germanic *~up-~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). This separable prefix adds an upward or constructive directionality to the base verb.

The compound is the standard word for constructing something systematically â€” a building, a business, an argument (~ein Argument aufbauen~). The figurative extensions are productive: ~aufgebaut sein aus~ ("to be composed of"), ~auf etwas aufbauen~ ("to build on something"). Cognate with Dutch ~opbouwen~, Low German ~upbuuen~.
en.aufbewahren:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~bewahren~:

- ~bewahren~: From MHG ~bewarn~, from OHG ~biwarإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~biwarإچnؤ…~, compound of *~bi-~ ("about, around") + *~warإچnؤ…~ ("to guard, protect"), from Proto-Indo-European *~wer-~ ("to become aware of, to heed"). Cognate with English ~beware~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix here conveys preservation and safekeeping, with an underlying spatial metaphor of storing something upward or away.

The result means "to store, to keep safe, to preserve" -- a word that sounds bureaucratic but carries millennia of watchful vigilance in its roots.
en.aufbringen:
From Middle High German ~إ«fbringen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, from a Pre-Germanic compound *~bت°renل¸±-~, blending Proto-Indo-European *~bت°er-~ ("to carry") with *~hâ‚پneل¸±-~ ("to bring, reach"). - ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds an upward, outward, or provocative force.

Cognates include Dutch ~opbrengen~ ("to yield, produce") and the archaic English ~upbring~ ("to bring up, to raise").
en.auffallen:
From Middle High German ~إ«fvallen~, ~إ«ffallen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallanؤ…~, from PIE *~pehâ‚ƒl-~ ("to fall").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The separable prefix adds a sense of upward motion or sudden appearance.

The literal image is striking: something that ~falls upward~ â€” that rises against the expected direction â€” naturally catches the eye. The cognate Low German form ~upvallen~ confirms the compound's age. In modern usage, the verb is impersonal: ~es fأ¤llt mir auf~ ("it strikes me, I notice it"), and the derived adjective ~auffأ¤llig~ ("conspicuous, striking") is ubiquitous. The verb captures a deep insight about perception: we notice not what falls as expected, but what ~falls up~ â€” what violates our expectations. Cognate with Dutch ~opvallen~ ("to stand out"), which follows the same metaphor exactly.
en.auffordern:

- ~fordern~: From MHG ~vodern~, ~vordern~, from OHG ~fordarإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~forأ¾(a)rإچn~ ("to move forward, to bring to the front"), from *~forأ¾ar~ ("further"), ultimately from PIE *~per-~ ("to go forth"). Cognate with English ~further~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic stem). Related to German ~fأ¶rdern~ ("to promote, support"), which is the same word with umlaut.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp~ ("up"), from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). As a prefix, it adds urgency or directedness.

The literal sense is "to demand upward" â€” to call upon someone to do something. ~Auffordern~ means "to request, to call upon, to challenge" and carries more urgency than ~bitten~ ("to ask") but less than ~befehlen~ ("to command").
