The relationship between ~fordern~ ("to demand") and ~fأ¶rdern~ ("to promote") â€” distinguished only by umlaut â€” illustrates how a single root meaning "to bring forward" could split into "to demand something be brought forth" and "to push something forward."
en.auffأ¼hren:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~fأ¼hren~:

- ~fأ¼hren~: From MHG ~vأ¼eren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچrijanؤ…~, originally the causative of OHG ~faran~ ("to proceed, travel"). - ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below").

The reflexive ~sich auffأ¼hren~ ("to behave, conduct oneself") adds yet another dimension, sometimes used disapprovingly: ~Fأ¼hr dich nicht so auf!~ ("Don't carry on like that!").
en.aufgeben:
From Middle High German ~إ«fgeben~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-Germanic *~up-~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds a sense of upward transfer or relinquishment.

The compound's meaning of "to give up, to surrender" derives from the image of giving something upward â€” releasing it from one's grasp to the sky, as it were. But ~aufgeben~ also means "to post (a letter)" (giving it up to the postal system), "to assign (homework)" (giving a task upward to students from teacher), and historically "to serve food" (giving it up onto the table). These senses all preserve the core gesture of handing something over and away from oneself. The derived noun ~Aufgabe~ ("task, assignment, mission") reframes the act of giving up as a duty received â€” what is given up by one party becomes the task of another. The phrase ~sich aufgeben~ ("to give up on oneself") is among the most psychologically loaded expressions in German. Cognate with English ~upgive~ (archaic), Dutch ~opgeven~.
en.aufgreifen:
From Middle High German ~إ«fgrؤ«fen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grؤ«fen~, from OHG ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~gripe~ and ~grip~, Dutch ~grijpen~, Swedish ~gripa~. Also related to Lithuanian ~griل؛½bti~ ("to seize").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds the sense of picking up or seizing upon.

The PIE root *~gت°reyb-~ has produced a cluster of English words all revolving around grasping: ~grip~, ~gripe~, ~grope~, and ~grab~ (the last via a secondary formation). The compound ~aufgreifen~ means both "to pick up" in a physical sense and "to take up" in an abstract one -- as in taking up an idea or a suggestion. In legal and police contexts, ~aufgreifen~ means "to apprehend, to pick up" a suspect, preserving the original forceful sense of seizing. The Gothic cognate ~greipan~ appears in Wulfila's Bible translation, showing that this verb was already well established across the Germanic world by the 4th century.
en.aufhalten:
From Middle High German ~إ«fhalten~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~ ("to hold, keep"). The PIE origin is uncertain; one proposal connects it to *~ل¸±el-~ ("to hide, conceal"), while others suggest *~kelH-~ ("to rise") or *~kel-~ ("to drive"). Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~, Swedish ~hأ¥lla~, Icelandic ~halda~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below").

Cognate with Dutch ~ophouden~ ("to stop, to cease") and English ~uphold~ ("to maintain, support"), though the three languages have sent the same compound in different semantic directions. German ~aufhalten~ primarily means "to halt, to stop (someone)" and reflexively "to stay, to sojourn" (~sich aufhalten~). The reflexive use with ~mit~ in the negative -- ~sich nicht mit etwas aufhalten~ ("not to bother with something") -- adds the nuance of refusing to be delayed by trivial matters.
en.aufheben:
~auf-~ + ~heben~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~heben~:

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-Germanic *~hafjanؤ…~, from PIE *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to grasp, to seize").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The separable prefix adds a sense of upward motion or removal.

~Aufheben~ is one of German's most philosophically famous verbs, carrying three seemingly contradictory meanings: "to pick up," "to preserve, to keep," and "to cancel, to abolish." Hegel seized upon this triple meaning to create his concept of ~Aufhebung~ ("sublation"): the dialectical process in which a thesis and antithesis are simultaneously ~negated~, ~preserved~, and ~elevated~ into a synthesis. It is rare for a single word to contain its own opposite (such words are called ~auto-antonyms~ or ~contronyms~), and rarer still for a philosopher to build an entire system on the ambiguity. The three senses are etymologically unified: to lift something ~up~ (~aufheben~) removes it from where it was (cancellation), takes it into one's hands (preservation), and raises it to a higher position (elevation). The colloquial phrase ~gut aufgehoben sein~ ("to be in good hands") uses the preservation sense. Cognate with Dutch ~opheffen~ ("to abolish, to lift up").
en.aufhأ¶ren:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~hأ¶ren~:

- ~hأ¶ren~: From MHG ~hإ“ren~, from OHG ~hإچren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hإچrian~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijanؤ…~ ("to hear, to listen"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ل¸±hâ‚‚owsyأ©ti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), a compound of *~hâ‚‚eل¸±-~ ("sharp") + *~hâ‚‚ows-~ ("ear") + *~-yأ©ti~ (denominative suffix). Cognate with English ~hear~, Dutch ~horen~, Danish ~hأ¸re~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp~ ("up"), from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys upward motion, opening, or cessation.

The compound means "to stop, to cease, to quit" â€” ~Hأ¶r auf!~ ("Stop it!"). The connection between "listening" and "stopping" is not immediately obvious, but it likely arose from the idea of heeding a command: when you "listen up" (~aufhأ¶ren~), you stop what you are doing and pay attention. The PIE etymology of ~hأ¶ren~ is itself remarkable: *~hâ‚‚ل¸±hâ‚‚owsyأ©ti~ literally means "to have sharp ears," a compound that reveals the Proto-Indo-European speakers understood hearing as a sharpening of the ear's attention. The PIE root *~hâ‚‚ows-~ ("ear") survives in Latin ~auris~ ("ear"), whence English ~aural~ and ~auricular~. The expression ~Da hأ¶rt sich alles auf!~ ("That's the limit!" literally "Everything stops hearing itself there!") is a characteristically German way of expressing indignation at something beyond the pale.
en.aufklأ¤ren:
From ~auf-~ + ~klأ¤ren~, a calque of Middle Low German ~upklؤپren~. Compound of a Germanic prefix and a Latin-derived verb:

- ~klأ¤ren~: From MHG ~klأ¦ren~, from the adjective ~klar~, which entered German from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch ~claer~, reinforced by Old French ~cler~, all ultimately from Latin ~clؤپrus~ ("bright, clear, famous").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix conveys clearing up, illumination, or resolution.

The word ~aufklأ¤ren~ is historically inseparable from the ~Aufklأ¤rung~ -- the German Enlightenment of the 18th century. Immanuel Kant's famous 1784 essay "Was ist Aufklأ¤rung?" defined it as humanity's emergence from self-imposed immaturity. The word itself mirrors this intellectual program: ~auf-~ ("up") + ~klأ¤ren~ ("to clear") = "to clear up, to enlighten." Compare Dutch ~opklaren~ ("to clear up," especially of weather). The meteorological sense of clearing up likely preceded the philosophical one, and ~aufklأ¤ren~ still retains both: the weather clears up (~Das Wetter klأ¤rt auf~) and a detective clears up a crime (~einen Fall aufklأ¤ren~). Latin ~clؤپrus~ also gave English ~clear~, ~clarify~, and ~declare~.
en.aufkommen:
From Middle High German ~إ«fkomen~, from Old High German ~إ«fqueman~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~komen~/~quأ«men~, from OHG ~quأ«man~ (rarer ~cuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanؤ…~, from the aorist subjunctive of Proto-Indo-European *~gت·em-~ ("to step, to arrive"). Cognate with English ~come~, Dutch ~komen~, Danish ~komme~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix conveys emergence or arising.

The compound ~aufkommen~ has branched into several meanings: "to arise, to emerge" (of trends or ideas), "to bear the cost" (~fأ¼r etwas aufkommen~, "to pay for something"), and "to recover" (from an illness). The PIE root *~gت·em-~ ("to step") also produced Latin ~venؤ«re~ ("to come"), Greek ~baأ­nein~ ("to go"), and Sanskrit ~gأ،mati~ ("goes") -- making ~kommen~ a cousin of English ~advent~, ~venue~, and ~acrobat~.
en.auflأ¶sen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~lأ¶sen~. Compare Dutch ~oplossen~:

- ~lأ¶sen~: From MHG ~lإ“sen~, from OHG ~lإچsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lausijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lausijanؤ…~ ("to make loose, release, set free"), a factitive derivation from *~lausaz~ ("loose, free"), from PIE *~lewhâ‚پ-~ ("to loosen, cut apart"). Cognate with English ~loose~ (via Old Norse ~lauss~), obsolete English ~leese~ (from Old English ~lؤ«esan~), Danish ~lأ¸se~, Swedish ~lأ¶sa~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below").

The Dutch cognate ~oplossen~ has specialized further than its German cousin: it is the standard word for "to solve" (a problem), "to dissolve" (a substance), and even "to resolve" (a conflict). German ~auflأ¶sen~ covers similar ground but tends more toward "to dissolve, to disband, to break up." The expression ~sich in Luft auflأ¶sen~ ("to dissolve into thin air") captures the most dramatic end of this semantic spectrum.
en.aufnehmen:
From Middle High German ~إ«fnemen~, from Old High German *~إ«fneman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uppneman~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp-~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). This separable prefix adds a sense of upward motion, opening, or reception.

The compound's range of meanings â€” "to pick up," "to record," "to admit (a member)," "to photograph," "to receive (a signal)," "to absorb (a shock)" â€” illustrates how the directional ~auf-~ prefix transforms the basic act of taking into a rich array of receptive and upward-oriented actions. Dutch ~opnemen~ and Old English ~upniman~ are parallel formations. Cognate with Dutch ~opnemen~.
en.aufpassen:
From ~auf-~ + ~passen~, a calque of Dutch ~oppassen~. Compound of a Germanic prefix and a Romance-derived verb:

- ~passen~: Borrowed from French ~passer~ ("to pass"), ultimately from Latin ~passus~ ("step, pace"). Most closely related to German ~passieren~ ("to happen, to pass").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds a sense of alertness and upward attention.

The sense of "to be attentive, to pay attention" in ~aufpassen~ is not inherited from the French source but is a native Germanic semantic development within Dutch, from which it was calqued into German. French ~passer~ means simply "to pass," but Dutch ~oppassen~ and its German calque ~aufpassen~ developed the meaning "to watch out, to be careful" -- perhaps from the idea of stepping up to alertness. The phrase ~pass auf!~ ("watch out!") is one of German's most common warnings. Curiously, ~passen~ in German also means "to fit" (as in clothing), a sense that developed independently from the idea of a step or measure being correct.
en.aufrufen:
~auf-~ + ~rufen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~, ~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrإچpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrإچpanؤ…~ ("to cry out, call, shout"). The Proto-Germanic etymology is uncertain; a traditional connection to *~hrإچأ¾iz~ ("glory, fame") is phonetically problematic. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~.
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds the sense of calling upward or outward â€” summoning to action.

~Aufrufen~ means "to call (someone's name), to call upon, to summon, to invoke." In modern German it has gained a strong technical sense: ~eine Webseite aufrufen~ ("to call up a webpage"), ~eine Funktion aufrufen~ ("to invoke a function"). The older sense of publicly summoning someone by name persists in legal and administrative contexts (~die Zeugen aufrufen~, "to call the witnesses"). The modern umlautless present-tense conjugations (~du rufst~, ~er ruft~, not the obsolete ~du rأ¼fst~, ~er rأ¼ft~) are a relic of this weak-verb influence, as lack of umlaut on rounded diphthongs before labials is regular in Upper German but has only become standard in ~rufen~, not in ~laufen~ or ~saufen~.
en.aufstellen:
Equivalent to ~auf-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stallijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stallijanؤ…~, from PIE *~stelhâ‚‚-~ ("to put, to stand").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds the sense of placing something upright or establishing something.

~Aufstellen~ means "to set up, to erect, to establish" â€” covering everything from physical placement (~ein Zelt aufstellen~, "to pitch a tent") to abstract establishment (~eine Theorie aufstellen~, "to put forward a theory," ~einen Rekord aufstellen~, "to set a record") to military formation (~Truppen aufstellen~, "to deploy troops"). The prefix ~auf-~ lifts the act of placing into an upright, visible, public position. The derived noun ~Aufstellung~ means "lineup, formation, arrangement" â€” the starting eleven in a football match is the ~Aufstellung~. Cognate with Dutch ~opstellen~.
en.aufsuchen:

- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds purposefulness to the seeking.

The PIE root *~sehâ‚‚g-~ also produced Latin ~sؤپgؤ«re~ ("to perceive keenly") and Old Irish ~saigim~ ("to seek, pursue"), suggesting that the original sense was more about keen perception than mere searching. The related noun ~Suche~ ("search") and compound ~Versuch~ ("attempt, experiment") both stem from the same root, showing how "seeking" branches into "trying" and "testing."
en.auftauchen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~tauchen~:

- ~tauchen~: From MHG ~tإ«chen~, from OHG ~-tإ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dإ«kan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dإ«kanؤ…~ ("to duck, dive under"). The ultimate PIE origin is unclear; one theory connects it to *~dت°ugت°nأ©hâ‚‚-~, from a root meaning "to fall," which might also be related to *~duganؤ…~ ("to be fit"). Cognate with Dutch ~duiken~, Swedish ~dyka~. A derivative with Low German consonantism is ~ducken~, cognate with and synonymous with English ~duck~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f~, from OHG ~إ«f~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below").

The word ~tauchen~ had become obsolete in Upper German dialects but was reintroduced from Central German ~dإ«chen~/~tإ«chen~ and Middle Low German ~dإ«ken~. In this process, the originally strong verb became weak. The compound ~auftauchen~ literally means "to dive up," describing a submarine, a whale, or a diver surfacing. Its figurative use -- "to turn up, to appear" -- captures the sudden, often unexpected quality of something emerging from hidden depths.
en.aufteilen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~ ("to divide, part, deal"), from Proto-Germanic *~dailijanؤ…~, from PIE *~dت°oyl-~ ("part, watershed"). Cognate with English ~deal~ and Dutch ~delen~.
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). As a separable prefix, it means "up, open, on."

~Aufteilen~ means "to divide up, to partition, to distribute" â€” literally "to part open," splitting something into portions. The ~auf-~ prefix adds a sense of opening or distributing that plain ~teilen~ ("to share, to divide") lacks: ~teilen~ can mean sharing (without separating), but ~aufteilen~ always implies breaking something into distinct portions. The PIE root produced English ~deal~ (exact cognate), ~dole~ (a distributed share), and ~ordeal~ (originally "a judgment," from the act of distributing or meting out justice).
en.auftragen:
From Middle High German ~إ«ftragen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag"), from Proto-Indo-European *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, draw"). Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~ ("to carry"), Portuguese ~trazer~ ("to bring").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix conveys applying something onto a surface or assigning a task upward.

The semantic shift from "to pull, drag" to "to carry, bear" happened within Germanic: what was originally dragged along the ground came to be carried. The compound ~auftragen~ has developed several distinct meanings that seem unrelated on the surface: "to apply" (paint, cream), "to assign, commission" (a task), and "to serve" (food). The connecting thread is the idea of placing something onto or upon: paint onto a canvas, a duty onto a person, food onto a table. English ~draw~ and ~drag~ are doublets from the same Proto-Germanic root, split by dialect: ~drag~ entered English from Old Norse ~draga~, while ~draw~ continued the native Old English ~dragan~.
en.auftreten:
From Middle High German ~إ«ftreten~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trأ«ten~, from OHG ~trأ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudanؤ…~. A class V strong verb cognate with English ~tread~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-Germanic *~up-~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). As a separable prefix, ~auf-~ adds upward directionality or the sense of opening, beginning, or public emergence.

The compound's primary senses â€” "to appear (on stage)," "to occur," "to kick" â€” all derive from the image of stepping upward into visibility. An actor who ~auftritt~ steps forward onto the boards; a problem that ~auftritt~ steps up into one's awareness; an aggressor who ~auftritt~ puts a foot forward in force. The ~Auftritt~ ("appearance, scene, entrance") and the theatrical term ~Auftreten~ ("manner of conducting oneself") preserve the original footstep metaphor. Notably, the ~treten~-based family accounts for nearly every German word for social or institutional entry: ~eintreten~ ("to enter, to join"), ~betreten~ ("to step on"), ~vertreten~ ("to represent"), and ~auftreten~ itself â€” German social life, from stage to parliament, is expressed through the vocabulary of footsteps. Cognate with Dutch ~optreden~, English ~tread~.
en.aufwachsen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~wachsen~:

- ~wachsen~: From MHG ~wahsen~, from OHG ~wahsan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wahsan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wahsijanؤ…~ ("to grow"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚weg-~ ("to grow, increase"). Cognate with English ~wax~ (as in "to wax and wane"), Dutch ~wassen~ ("to grow"), Swedish ~vأ¤xa~, Gothic ~wahsjan~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). The prefix emphasizes the upward trajectory of growth.

The PIE root *~hâ‚‚weg-~ ("to grow, increase") also produced Latin ~auxilium~ ("help," originally "increase, reinforcement"), Lithuanian ~أ،ukإ،tas~ ("high"), and Greek ~auxأ،nein~ ("to increase") -- the source of the modern biological term ~auxin~ (a plant growth hormone). English ~wax~ in the sense of "to grow" (as the moon waxes) preserves the old meaning, though it has been largely displaced by ~grow~ in everyday speech. The compound ~aufwachsen~ means specifically "to grow up" in the sense of childhood and maturation, reinforcing the upward metaphor with ~auf-~. The unrelated homophone ~wachsen~ meaning "to wax, to polish" (from ~Wachs~, "wax") is an entirely different word with a different Proto-Germanic root.
en.aufweisen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~, from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). This separable prefix adds a sense of displaying, presenting, or bringing upward to view.

~Aufweisen~ means "to show, to exhibit, to feature" â€” literally to point something upward so it can be seen. It is used in formal or academic registers: ~Das Gebأ¤ude weist Schأ¤den auf~ ("The building exhibits damage"). The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know") connects ~weisen~ to a vast family of cognates: English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~vision~ (via Latin ~vidؤ“re~), Sanskrit ~vأ©da~ ("knowledge," as in the Rigveda), and Greek ~خµل¼¶خ´خ؟د‚~ ("form, appearance," whence English ~idea~). The journey from "seeing" to "knowing" to "showing" is preserved in ~aufweisen~: what you point out is what you have seen, and what you have seen is what you know.
en.aufzeigen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~zeigen~:

- ~zeigen~: From MHG ~zeigen~, from OHG ~zeigإچn~, an intensive form of ~zؤ«han~ (whence modern ~zeihen~ "to blame, accuse"), ultimately from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show, point"). Cognate with English ~teach~, Latin ~dؤ«cere~ ("to say"), Greek ~deiknunai~ ("to show").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~إ«f-~, from OHG ~إ«f-~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp-~, from PIE *~upأ³~ ("up, from below"). As a prefix, it conveys "up, upward, open."

The PIE root *~deyل¸±-~ produced a remarkable family: Latin ~dؤ«cere~ ("to say"), ~digitus~ ("finger" -- the pointer), ~index~ ("indicator"), and ~iإ«dex~ ("judge" -- one who shows the law). In Germanic, the root went in a different direction: English ~teach~ (originally "to show") and German ~zeigen~ ("to show"). Also related is ~Zeichen~ ("sign, mark"). In Modern German, ~zeigen~ absorbed the synonymous but entirely unrelated MHG ~zأ¶ugen~ (cognate with Gothic ~ataugjan~), which is why variant spellings like ~zأ¤ugen~ once appeared. The prefix ~auf-~ adds the sense of raising something to visibility: to point out, demonstrate, highlight. In Austria and far-western Germany, ~aufzeigen~ also means "to raise one's hand" in school -- literally showing upward.
en.ausbauen:
~aus-~ + ~bauen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~bإ«wen~, from OHG ~bإ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bإ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~bإ«anؤ…~ ("to dwell, reside"), from PIE *~bت°uH-~ ("to be, become, grow").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of expansion or development outward.

~Ausbauen~ means "to expand, to develop, to extend" â€” literally "to build outward." Where ~bauen~ alone means "to build, to construct," the prefix ~aus-~ directs the building process outward from an existing structure: one ~baut~ a road network ~aus~ (expands it), ~baut~ a lead ~aus~ (extends it in competition), or ~baut~ an attic ~aus~ (finishes it for habitation). The verb thus straddles the literal and figurative: from physically enlarging a building to abstractly developing a strategy, a relationship, or a competitive advantage. The derived noun ~Ausbau~ ("expansion, development, extension") appears frequently in planning and policy contexts. Cognate with Dutch ~uitbouwen~.
en.ausbilden:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~bilden~:

- ~bilden~: From MHG ~bilden~, ~bileden~, a merger of OHG ~biliden~ (from Proto-West Germanic *~biliأ¾ijan~) and OHG ~bilidإچn~ (from Proto-West Germanic *~biliأ¾إچn~), both derived from *~biliأ¾ؤ«~ ("image, likeness"), whence modern ~Bild~ ("picture, image"). The deeper origin is Proto-West Germanic *~bil~ ("equal, alike").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds a sense of full development or completion.

The literal sense is "to form out" â€” to shape someone or something into full development. ~Ausbilden~ means "to train, to educate" (especially in vocational or professional contexts), and the noun ~Ausbildung~ ("training, education") is a cornerstone of the German dual-education system, in which apprentices alternate between workplace training and classroom instruction. The MHG predecessor ~إ«zbilden~ is already attested. The semantic journey from "image" (~Bild~) to "to form" (~bilden~) to "to train" (~ausbilden~) follows a coherent metaphorical chain: to train someone is to shape them according to an ideal image. Cognate with Dutch ~afbeelden~ ("to depict") and Swedish ~bilda~ ("to form, educate").
en.ausdrأ¼cken:
From Middle High German ~إ«zdrأ¼cken~. In the sense "to express," a calque of Latin ~exprimere~ ("to press out"). Compound of ~aus-~ + ~drأ¼cken~:

- ~drأ¼cken~: From MHG ~drأ¼cken~, from OHG ~drucchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rukkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾rukkijanؤ…~ ("to press on, crowd"), from PIE *~trewk-~ ("to press on and break"). Cognate with Welsh ~trychu~ ("to cut"), Lithuanian ~trإ«جپkti~ ("to tear into pieces"). Doublet of ~drucken~ ("to print"), which was originally a southern German variant that later specialized in meaning.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t-~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out").

The physical meaning "to squeeze out" (juice from a lemon, a cigarette stub) and the abstract meaning "to express" (a thought, an emotion) coexist in the same verb -- the metaphor of pressing inner content outward into the world. Compare Dutch ~uitdrukken~, which underwent the same semantic split. The Latin model ~exprimere~ (literally "to press out") that inspired the abstract sense also gave English ~express~, making ~ausdrأ¼cken~ and ~express~ etymological mirror images -- one Germanic, one Romance, both built on the same metaphor. The split between ~drأ¼cken~ ("to press") and ~drucken~ ("to print") is a rare case of a single verb differentiating into two standard forms, each with its own semantic domain.
en.auseinandersetzen:
From ~auseinander-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~, from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~, the causative of *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Thus ~setzen~ is literally "to cause to sit," i.e., "to set, to place." Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~, Yiddish ~zetsn~.
- ~auseinander-~: From ~aus~ ("out") + ~einander~ ("one another"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("out") + *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in") + *~hâ‚‚enteros~ ("other"). The adverb means "apart, asunder."

The reflexive ~sich auseinandersetzen mit~ ("to engage with, to come to grips with, to examine critically") is the most common modern usage, literally meaning "to set oneself apart with" a topic, implying thorough, often difficult engagement. The non-reflexive ~auseinandersetzen~ means "to explain in detail" or "to take apart." This is one of German's celebrated long compound verbs, combining four etymological layers into a single separable verb. The causative relationship between ~sitzen~ ("to sit") and ~setzen~ ("to set, place") mirrors exactly the English pair ~sit~/~set~ -- both pairs descend from the same PIE causative formation *~sodأ©yeti~ ("to cause to sit"), making this a grammatical pattern that has survived intact for over five millennia. English ~asunder~ is a cognate of ~auseinander~, sharing the same Germanic root for "apart."
en.ausfallen:
From Middle High German ~إ«zvallen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallanؤ…~, from PIE *~pإچl-~, possibly a compound of *~hâ‚‚epo~ ("off, away") + *~hâ‚ƒelhâ‚پ-~ ("to fall"). Cognate with English ~fall~, Dutch ~vallen~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~إ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it often conveys motion outward or cessation.

The compound ~ausfallen~ has developed a remarkable range of meanings: "to fall out" (hair, teeth), "to be cancelled" (a class, an event), "to fail" (a machine), and "to turn out" (a result). The cancellation sense â€” where something ~falls out~ of the schedule â€” is perhaps the most distinctive, since English uses the unrelated "cancel" (from Latin ~cancellؤپre~). The PIE etymology of ~fallen~ is debated: the compound-root theory connecting it to Latin ~aboleإچ~ ("to destroy") and Lithuanian ~pأ¹lti~ ("to fall") is attractive but uncertain. Cognate with Dutch ~uitvallen~.
en.ausfأ¼hren: From Middle High German ~إ«zvأ¼eren~, Old High German ~إ«zfuoren~. By surface analysis, ~aus-~ (from PIE *~أ؛d~, "upwards, out") and ~fأ¼hren~, from MHG ~vأ¼eren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچrijanؤ…~, from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). Cognate with Dutch ~uitvoeren~. The range of meanings is characteristic of Germanic prefix-verb productivity: to carry out (execute a plan), to lead out (escort someone), to export (carry goods out), or to elaborate (develop an argument out to its conclusion). Dutch ~uitvoeren~ covers the same semantic territory, showing how deeply the compound is shared across West Germanic.
en.ausgeben:
From ~aus-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of giving outward, distributing, or expending.

~Ausgeben~ means "to spend (money)" â€” ~Geld ausgeben~ â€” but also "to dispense" (medication), "to issue" (banknotes, stamps, orders), "to output" (data, in computing), and, colloquially, "to buy someone a drink" (~eine Runde ausgeben~, "to buy a round"). The reflexive ~sich als etwas ausgeben~ ("to pose as, to impersonate") is a fascinating extension: one "gives oneself out" as something one is not, distributing a false identity to the world. The derived adjective ~ausgiebig~ ("extensive, thorough, generous") preserves the sense of giving freely and fully â€” an ~ausgiebiges Frأ¼hstأ¼ck~ is a lavish breakfast where nothing is held back. The pair ~ausgeben~ (to give out, to spend) and ~eingeben~ (to input, to enter) form a natural antonym in computing terminology. Cognate with English ~outgive~ (archaic "to give out, to distribute"), Dutch ~uitgeven~ ("to spend, to publish" â€” the Dutch sense of "to publish" shows another direction of "giving out": releasing a book into the world).
en.ausgehen: From Middle High German ~إ«zgؤ“n~, ~إ«zgؤپn~, itself from Old High German ~إ«zgangan~, ~إ«zgؤپn~, ~إ«zgؤ“n~. Equivalent to prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~أ؛d~, "upwards, out") and ~gehen~, from MHG ~gan~, ~gen~, from OHG ~gan~, ~gen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with Dutch ~uitgaan~, Low German ~utgahn~, English ~outgo~, and Swedish and Danish ~utgأ¥~. The compound captures a nuance that German preserves through prefix: motion that takes one out of a bounded space. The same PIE root that gives "go" in English gives ~gehen~ in German, and the ~aus-~ prefix turns an abstract motion into a departure from enclosure â€” going out to socialize, going out (of a flame), or running out (of stock).
en.ausgleichen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~gleichen~:

- ~gleichen~: From MHG ~gelؤ«chen~, from OHG ~gilؤ«hhon~, from Proto-Germanic *~galؤ«kإچnؤ…~ ("to make alike, to compare"). The adjective ~gleich~ ("equal, same") descends from OHG ~gilؤ«h~, a compound of ~ga-~ ("together," from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~) + ~lؤ«k-~ ("body, form"), related to ~Leiche~ ("corpse," originally "body").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of completion, balancing out, or evening.

The original meaning of ~gleich~ was "having the same body/form," a compound that literally said two things looked alike because they shared the same shape. The element ~-lؤ«k-~ also produced the English suffix ~-ly~ (originally "having the body/form of") and the word ~like~ (same root). English ~like~, German ~gleich~, and the suffix ~-lich~/~-ly~ are all siblings from the same Germanic root. The compound ~ausgleichen~ means "to equalize, compensate, balance out," carrying the sense of bringing two unequal things into the same form. The sports term ~Ausgleich~ ("equalizer") captures this precisely: the goal that makes the score equal again. The noun ~Gleichgewicht~ ("balance, equilibrium") and the adjective ~gleichgأ¼ltig~ ("indifferent," literally "equally valid") are further compounds showing the productivity of this root. The morbid origin in ~Leiche~ ("corpse") is long forgotten, but it reminds us that the concept of likeness began with the physical human body.
en.auslegen:
From Middle High German ~إ«ت’legen~ ("to display, set out; interpret; lend money"). Compound of ~aus-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~ ("to lay, place"), the causative of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~, causative of *~legت°-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«ت’~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-West Germanic *~إ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out").

The MHG verb already had a remarkably wide semantic range: display, interpret, and lend money. All three senses survive in modern German. ~Auslegen~ means "to lay out, display" (goods in a shop), "to interpret, construe" (a text, a law, a remark), and "to advance money" (paying on someone's behalf). Cognate with Dutch ~uitleggen~ ("to explain") and Yiddish ~×گ×•×™×،×œ×™×™×’×ں~ ("to spell out; interpret; lend money"), both preserving a similar range.
en.ausliefern:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~liefern~:

- ~liefern~: From MHG ~lieveren~ (attested before 1350), probably borrowed via Middle Dutch and Middle Low German ~lؤ“veren~ from Old French ~livrer~ ("to deliver"), from Latin ~lؤ«berؤپre~ ("to set free, hand over"), from ~lؤ«ber~ ("free"). The vocalism ~-ie-~ (instead of the expected ~-ؤ“-~) is attributed to hypercorrection. Low German definitely contributed to the spread of the word, which was part of the technical vocabulary of the Hanseatic League. Cognate with Dutch ~leveren~, Swedish ~leverera~, and French ~livrer~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys movement outward, completion, or distribution.

The compound ~ausliefern~ means "to deliver, hand over, extradite" â€” to send something ~aus~ ("out") by ~liefern~ ("delivering") it. The root ~liefern~ is one of the few German verbs with a Romance etymology that entered the language not through courtly culture but through trade: the Hanseatic League's commercial network spread the word from Low German ports across the German-speaking world. The Latin ancestor ~lؤ«berؤپre~ ("to set free") â€” which also gives English ~liberate~ and ~liberty~ â€” underwent a remarkable semantic narrowing in its passage through Old French into Germanic: from "freeing" to "handing over goods." English ~deliver~ traveled the same Old French path but kept a wider semantic range.
en.auslأ¶sen:
~aus-~ + ~lأ¶sen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~lأ¶sen~:

- ~lأ¶sen~: From MHG ~lإ“sen~, ~lإچsen~, from OHG ~lإچsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lausijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lausijanؤ…~, a factitive verb derived from *~lausaz~ ("loose, free"), from PIE *~lewhâ‚پ-~ ("to loosen, cut apart").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds a sense of releasing outward, completing a process of liberation.

~Auslأ¶sen~ means "to trigger, to initiate, to cause; to ransom" â€” two senses united by the image of releasing something from constraint. The triggering sense (~eine Reaktion auslأ¶sen~, "to trigger a reaction"; ~den nأ¤chsten Bأ¶rsencrash auslأ¶sen~, "to trigger the next stock-market crash") envisions a mechanism held in check that is suddenly set free. The ransoming sense (~einen Gefangenen auslأ¶sen~, "to ransom a prisoner") is the older meaning: to pay the price that frees someone from captivity. The derived noun ~Auslأ¶ser~ means both "trigger" (on a camera or a gun) and, figuratively, "the thing that set events in motion." The English cognate ~loose~ descends from Old Norse ~lauss~ (from the same Proto-Germanic *~lausaz~), while ~lose~ comes from a different path through Old English ~losian~. German ~lأ¶sen~ and English ~loose~ are thus cousins, not siblings â€” arriving at similar sounds from slightly different routes through the same Proto-Germanic family.
en.ausmachen:
From ~aus-~ + ~machen~. Compound of:

- ~machen~: From MHG ~machen~, from OHG ~mahhإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~makإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~makإچn~, from *~mak~ ("fit, suitable") + *~-إچn~, ultimately from PIE *~mag-~ ("to knead, mix, make"). Cognate with English ~make~, Dutch ~maken~, Low German ~maken~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~إ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it can convey completion, extinction, or determination.

~ausmachen~ is a lexical chameleon with at least five major senses: "to turn off" (a light), "to make up, constitute" (a sum), "to matter" (~es macht nichts aus~ = "it doesn't matter"), "to agree upon, arrange" (a meeting), and "to make out, discern" (something in the distance). This polysemy makes it one of the trickier German verbs for learners. The "turn off" sense â€” extinguishing something ~out~ â€” parallels English "put out" (a fire), while the "constitute" sense reflects the idea of ~making out~ a total. The "to matter" sense is perhaps the most idiomatic: ~macht dir das etwas aus?~ ("does that bother you?") literally asks whether something "makes something out" of the situation for you. The PIE root *~mag-~ ("to knead") points to the most physical origin of all: shaping dough with one's hands.
en.ausprobieren:
From ~aus-~ + ~probieren~. ~Probieren~ derives from Latin ~probؤپre~ ("to test, to inspect, to approve, to prove"), a denominal verb from ~probus~ ("good, fit, virtuous"), from PIE *~pro-bت°uH-s~ ("being in front"), from *~pro-~ ("forward") + *~bت°uH-~ ("to be"). ~Probieren~ is a doublet of both ~proben~ ("to rehearse") and ~prأ¼fen~ ("to examine, to test"), all from the same Latin root but via different routes.

- ~probieren~: Directly from Latin ~probؤپre~. The sense in German is "to try, to taste, to sample" â€” not "to probe" in the clinical English sense. This is a subtle false friend: English ~probe~ (also from ~probؤپre~) has narrowed to mean investigation or intrusion, while German ~probieren~ kept the lighter sense of tasting or trying.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«ب¥~, OHG ~إ«ب¥~, from Proto-West Germanic *~إ«t~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Cognate with English ~out~. The prefix here adds the sense of thoroughness or completion â€” testing something all the way through.
en.ausreichen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From MHG ~reichen~, from OHG ~reihhen~, ~reichen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijanؤ…~ ("to reach, stretch out, extend"). Cognate with English ~reach~ and Dutch ~reiken~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys sufficiency or completion.

The compound literally means "to reach out sufficiently" and has come to mean simply "to suffice, to be enough": ~Das Geld reicht aus~ ("The money is enough"). The adjective ~ausreichend~ ("sufficient, adequate") is also used as a school grade in Germany â€” equivalent to a D, the lowest passing mark, which tells students their performance ~reicht gerade aus~ ("just barely suffices"). The root verb ~reichen~ preserves both the physical sense ("to hand, to pass") and an older sense of sufficiency (~Das reicht!~ "That's enough!"). English ~reach~ underwent the opposite semantic narrowing, losing the sufficiency meaning and retaining only the physical extension. The PIE ancestor *~raikijanؤ…~ meant "to stretch out the hand," an image that survives whenever a German speaker says ~Reichen Sie mir bitte das Salz~ ("Please pass me the salt").
en.ausrichten:
From Middle High German ~إ«zrihten~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to right, to direct").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The separable prefix adds a sense of outward direction or completion.

~Ausrichten~ carries a remarkable range of meanings: "to align, to orient, to deliver (a message), to organize (an event), to accomplish." The root ~richten~ means "to straighten, to direct, to judge" â€” cognate with English ~right~ â€” and the prefix ~aus-~ pushes this straightening outward, toward completion. When you ~eine Nachricht ausrichten~ ("deliver a message"), you are directing words outward to their recipient. When you ~ein Fest ausrichten~ ("host a festival"), you bring an event into proper arrangement. The MHG compound ~إ«zrihten~ already carried the sense of equipping or furnishing, showing that the "arranging outward" metaphor is centuries old. Cognate with Dutch ~uitrichten~.
en.ausschalten:
From Middle High German ~إ«zschalten~, equivalent to ~aus-~ + ~schalten~:

- ~schalten~: From MHG ~schalten~, from OHG ~scaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaldan~ ("to push, shove"). Further etymology is obscure; according to Pokorny, possibly from PIE *~(s)kelH-~ ("to split, separate"), in which case related to English ~shell~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~skaldan~ ("to push").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix conveys removal or deactivation.

The literal sense is "to switch out" â€” to push something out of its active state. ~Ausschalten~ means "to switch off, to turn off, to disable; to eliminate, neutralize." The second, figurative sense ("to eliminate a rival, to neutralize an opponent") reveals a darker dimension: one "switches off" a person as though they were a machine. The root ~schalten~ originally meant "to push" or "to shove" in Old High German, and its modern meaning "to switch" preserves the physical gesture of pushing a lever or mechanism. The compound ~Schalter~ ("switch; counter, window at a service desk") unites both the electrical and bureaucratic senses through the same image of a mechanism that controls access. Dutch ~uitschakelen~ is the exact cognate.
en.ausschlieأںen: Formed from prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~أ؛d~, "upwards, out") and ~schlieأںen~, from MHG ~slieب¥en~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slإ«tanؤ…~, *~sleutanؤ…~ ("to lock, shut"). The root ~schlieأںen~ descends from Middle High German ~slieب¥en~, Old High German ~sliozan~, Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, and Proto-Germanic *~slإ«tanؤ…~ / *~sleutanؤ…~, meaning "to lock, shut." Cognate with Dutch ~sluiten~, and â€” strikingly â€” English ~slot~. To "slot out" is a false friend here: English ~slot~ kept the narrow physical sense (a groove or opening), while German ~schlieأںen~ and ~ausschlieأںen~ developed the abstract sense of closing off and excluding.
en.aussehen:
From Middle High German ~إ«zsehen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with Dutch ~zien~, English ~see~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut-~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This separable prefix adds an outward or emergent sense to the base verb.

The compound's literal sense is "to look out" â€” as through a window â€” and from this developed the dominant modern meaning "to appear, to look (a certain way)": the impression projected outward toward the viewer. The parallel Yiddish ~×گ×•×™×،×–×¢×ں~ (~oyszen~) and Dutch ~uitzien~ preserve identical structure and meaning, while Scandinavian equivalents (~se ut~ in Swedish and Norwegian, ~se ud~ in Danish) express the same idea with postverbal ~ut/ud~. A false friend for English speakers: "he looks good" is ~er sieht gut aus~, not *~er schaut gut~.
en.aussetzen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~, a causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of outward placement, exposure, or cessation.

~Aussetzen~ unfolds into a striking range of meanings, all derived from "setting something out": to expose (someone to danger â€” setting them out in the open), to abandon (a child or animal â€” setting them out and leaving), to release (a captive animal back into the wild â€” setting it out to freedom), to suspend (an activity â€” setting it outside the flow of events), to stop (of a machine or heartbeat â€” ceasing to operate), and to miss a turn (in a game â€” being set outside the round). The abandonment sense is perhaps the most emotionally charged: ~ein Kind aussetzen~ evokes the ancient practice of infant exposure, known in German legal history as ~Kindesaussetzung~. The word ~ausgesetzt~ ("exposed") functions as both past participle and adjective, describing vulnerability itself. Cognate with Dutch ~uitzetten~, Luxembourgish ~aussetzen~.
en.aussprechen:
From Middle High German ~إ«zsprأ«chen~, from Old High German ~إ«zsprehhan~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprأ«chen~, from OHG ~sprأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanؤ…~ ("to speak"), probably from PIE *~spregت°-~ ("to crackle"). Cognate with English ~speak~, Dutch ~spreken~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t-~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a prefix, it conveys the sense of "out, outward, completely."

The connection between "cracking/crackling" and "speaking" in the PIE root *~spregت°-~ suggests that the earliest Germanic conception of speech was of sounds bursting forth -- noises that crack the silence. The ~aus-~ prefix in ~aussprechen~ reinforces this outward motion: to "speak out" a word is to pronounce it fully, to articulate it. The verb also carries the sense of "to utter" something otherwise kept silent, and reflexively (~sich aussprechen~) means "to pour one's heart out" or "to declare oneself in favour of something." In its intransitive use (~Lass ihn aussprechen!~), it means "to finish speaking" -- to let someone speak completely out to the end.
en.ausstatten: Formed from prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~أ؛d~, "upwards, out") and the now-obsolete verb ~statten~. The core verb ~statten~, from Middle High German ~staten~ ("to help do something; to add"), appears to be a denominal formation from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~ ("place, location"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stأ©hâ‚‚tis~ ("standing, position"). The related noun ~Statt~ (now chiefly surviving in compounds like ~Statthalter~ and the preposition ~statt~ "instead of") is a doublet of ~Stadt~ ("city") and cognate with English ~stead~ â€” as in ~homestead~ and ~instead~. To "outfit" someone is thus, at root, to "place out" a full complement of equipment or furnishings.
en.ausstellen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, to position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, to put, to post, to stand"). Cognate with English ~stall~ and ~still~.
- ~aus-~: From OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t-~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix conveys the sense of putting forth or outward.

The verb ~ausstellen~ has two principal meanings that developed from the same image of "placing outward": first, "to exhibit, to display" (as in a museum exhibition, ~Ausstellung~), and second, "to issue" (a document, certificate, or receipt). In both cases, something is being put out into the world for others to see or use. The PIE root *~stel-~ is remarkably productive, giving rise to German ~Stelle~ ("place, position, job"), ~Gestalt~ ("form, figure"), English ~install~, ~stall~, and Greek ~stؤ“lؤ“~ ("upright stone slab"). The noun ~Ausstellung~ ("exhibition") is one of the most common German cultural terms, used for everything from art shows to trade fairs. The double meaning of ~ausstellen~ occasionally creates ambiguity: ~einen Pass ausstellen~ means "to issue a passport," not "to exhibit" one.
en.aussuchen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~suchen~:

- ~suchen~: From MHG ~suochen~, from OHG ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sإچkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sإچkijanؤ…~, from PIE *~sehâ‚‚g-~ ("to seek out"). Originally an Upper German form without umlaut on the long rounded vowel before a velar consonant, reinforced against the umlauted Central German form (compare Luxembourgish ~sichen~) by the umlautless past forms. Cognate with English ~seek~, Dutch ~zoeken~, Low German ~sأ¶ken~, Danish ~sأ¸ge~, and Gothic ~sokjan~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys selection or extraction from a group.

The compound literally means "to seek out," i.e., to pick or choose from available options. While ~suchen~ means simply "to search," ~aussuchen~ implies a completed selection: ~Such dir etwas aus!~ ("Pick something out for yourself!"). The reflexive ~sich etwas aussuchen~ is the everyday German equivalent of English "to choose." The English cognate ~seek~ shares the same PIE root *~sehâ‚‚g-~, which also produced Latin ~sagؤ«re~ ("to perceive keenly"), the ancestor of English ~sagacious~ and ~presage~. A seeker, in the Indo-European mind, was someone with keen perception.
en.austauschen:
~aus-~ + ~tauschen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~tauschen~:

- ~tauschen~: From MHG ~tiuschen~, derived from ~tإ«schen~, related to ~tأ¤uschen~ ("to deceive"). The semantic shift ran from "to deceive" to "to trick someone into buying one's goods" to "to trade, to exchange" â€” a revealing commentary on medieval market ethics. The further origin of ~tإ«schen~ is uncertain.
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of completeness or thoroughness â€” a full exchange, in and out.

~Austauschen~ means "to exchange, to replace, to substitute" â€” a verb of symmetric transfer: what goes out is matched by what comes in. One ~tauscht~ ideas ~aus~ (exchanges them in conversation), ~tauscht~ a defective part ~aus~ (replaces it), or ~tauscht~ prisoners ~aus~ (swaps them between sides). The derived noun ~Austausch~ ("exchange") appears in contexts from student exchanges (~Schأ¼leraustausch~) to information sharing (~Informationsaustausch~). The kinship between ~tauschen~ ("to swap") and ~tأ¤uschen~ ("to deceive") â€” both from the same Middle High German root â€” is one of German etymology's most entertaining revelations: to trade and to trick were once, at some level, the same act.
en.austragen:
From Middle High German ~إ«ztragen~, from Old High German ~إ«ztragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~إ«tdragan~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). Cognate with Dutch ~dragen~, English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Danish ~drage~, Icelandic ~draga~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix conveys outward movement or completion.

The compound means "to carry out, to deliver (mail); to bear (a child to term); to settle (a dispute); to hold (a competition)." Each sense extends the image of carrying something to its destination or conclusion. The mail carrier (~Brieftrأ¤ger~) ~trأ¤gt die Post aus~ ("carries out the mail"), while a pregnant woman ~trأ¤gt ein Kind aus~ ("carries a child to term"). The connection between German ~tragen~ ("to carry") and English ~draw~ / ~drag~ is not immediately obvious but perfectly regular: the semantic shift from "to drag" to "to carry" happened within Germanic, while English preserved the older "pulling" sense. Portuguese ~trazer~ ("to bring") is also cognate, having inherited the same root through Latin.
en.ausweisen:
From Middle High German ~إ«zweizen~, from Old High German ~إ«zwؤ«sen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~ ("to show, direct"), a causative derived from *~wؤ«saz~ ("wise, knowing"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know"). The adjective ~weise~ ("wise") is cognate with English ~wise~, Dutch ~wijs~, and Latin ~vidؤ“re~ ("to see") â€” all from the same root, which links knowledge to vision.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z~, from OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix conveys outward direction or proof.

The compound means "to expel, to deport; to identify, prove" and reflexively ~sich ausweisen~ means "to identify oneself." The semantic bridge between "showing the way out" (expulsion) and "showing who one is" (identification) lies in the root ~weisen~ ("to show, to point"). The ~Ausweis~ ("identity card") is literally "that which shows outward" â€” an external proof of who one is. The connection between seeing, knowing, and wisdom runs deep in Indo-European languages: Latin ~vidؤ“re~ ("to see") shares the same PIE root *~weyd-~ as German ~wissen~ ("to know") and ~weise~ ("wise"). To the Indo-Europeans, knowledge was something seen.
en.auswerten:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~werten~:

- ~werten~: A verbal derivation from the adjective ~wert~ ("worth, valuable"), from Proto-Germanic *~werأ¾az~ ("worthy, valuable"), from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn"). Compare English ~worth~.
- ~aus-~: From OHG ~إ«z~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~ ("out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix means "out" in the sense of extracting or deriving results.

~Auswerten~ means "to evaluate, to analyze, to interpret (data)" â€” literally "to value out," extracting meaning or value from raw material. It is the standard German verb for data analysis: ~Daten auswerten~, ~Ergebnisse auswerten~, ~Statistiken auswerten~. The PIE root *~wert-~ ("to turn") connects value to turning: the original sense was "to turn toward," hence "to regard, to esteem," hence "worth." The same root produced Latin ~vertere~ ("to turn," whence English ~convert~, ~reverse~, ~versus~). The noun ~Auswertung~ ("evaluation, analysis") is indispensable in scientific and statistical German.
en.auswirken:
From Middle High German ~uzwurken~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~wirken~:

- ~wirken~: From MHG ~wirken~, from OHG ~wirken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wirkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wirkijanan~, a denominative verb from *~werkan~ ("work"), from PIE *~werg-~ ("to do, to work"). Cognate with English ~work~, Dutch ~werken~, and Greek ~ergon~ ("work," as in ~energy~).
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~uz-~, from OHG ~uz-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ut-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut~ ("out"), from PIE *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of "out" or "thoroughly" to the base verb.

In MHG there were actually two competing forms: ~wirken~ (with ~i~, prevailing in ~Central German~) and ~wurken~ (with ~u~, prevailing in ~Upper German~). The ~i~-form won out in the written standard by the 18th century. Today ~sich auswirken~ is almost always used reflexively, meaning "to have an effect," while the base verb ~wirken~ retains meanings like "to work, to have an effect, to knit." The English cognate ~work~ preserves the broader semantic scope that ~wirken~ has gradually narrowed.
en.auswأ¤hlen: Formed from prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~أ؛d~, "upwards, out") and ~wأ¤hlen~, from MHG ~weln~, from ~welen~, ~wellen~, from OHG ~wellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~walljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~waljanؤ…~, from PIE *~welhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, choose"). The semantic logic is identical to English ~pick out~: to single out from among alternatives. The prefix ~aus-~ here has a selective, extractive sense â€” lifting one item out of a set â€” rather than the directional or exhaustive sense it carries in other compounds.
en.auszeichnen:
Equivalent to ~aus-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, symbol"), from MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taiknؤ…~, from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show, point").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of singling out or distinguishing.

~Auszeichnen~ means "to distinguish, to honor, to award" and also "to mark, to price" (in retail: ~Waren auszeichnen~, "to price goods"). The reflexive ~sich auszeichnen~ means "to distinguish oneself, to excel." The derived noun ~Auszeichnung~ covers both senses: an award or honor, and a price tag. The semantic link is illuminating: to ~auszeichnen~ is to "mark out" â€” whether marking a person for distinction or marking a product with its price, the underlying gesture is the same: making something visible by putting a sign (~Zeichen~) on it. The PIE root *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show, point") that underlies ~Zeichen~ also gives Latin ~dؤ«cere~ ("to say") and ~index~ ("pointer"), making ~auszeichnen~ and ~indicate~ distant PIE cousins. Cognate with Dutch ~uitblinken~ (a different compound for the distinction sense).
en.ausأ¼ben:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~أ¼ben~:

- ~أ¼ben~: From MHG ~أ¼eben~, ~uoben~, from OHG ~uoben~, from Proto-Germanic *~إچbijanؤ…~ ("to do, practice"), the causative-iterative formation to a lost strong verb *~afanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒep-~ ("to labor, work").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~إ«z-~, from OHG ~إ«z-~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t-~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness or the outward-directed performance of an action.

The base verb ~أ¼ben~ means "to practice, drill" -- to perform something repeatedly for improvement. The prefix ~aus-~ transforms this into "to exercise, exert, practice" in the sense of carrying out an activity in the real world: ~einen Beruf ausأ¼ben~ ("to practice a profession"), ~Druck ausأ¼ben~ ("to exert pressure"), ~Macht ausأ¼ben~ ("to wield power"). The shift from repetitive practice to active performance is the key semantic contribution of ~aus-~. The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒep-~ ("to labor, work") also gave Latin ~opus~ ("work") and ~opera~, making ~أ¼ben~ and ~opera~ distant cousins -- both tracing back to the fundamental human concept of purposeful labor.
en.backen:
From Middle High German ~backen~, from Old High German ~backan~, an originally weak verb and geminated variant of an older strong verb: MHG ~bachen~, from OHG ~bahhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bakan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bakanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ehâ‚ƒg-~ ("to roast, bake"). The two verbs -- strong ~bachen~ and weak ~backen~ -- blended early, each developing both weak and strong forms. The strong form ~bachen~ was originally predominant in Upper German dialects, but ~backen~ prevailed in the modern standard because it was common in Central German and aligned with Middle Low German ~backen~.

Cognate with English ~bake~, Dutch ~bakken~, Danish ~bage~, Swedish ~baka~, and also distantly with Greek ~phإچgإچ~ ("to roast"). The verb retains optional strong conjugation in modern German: alongside the regular weak past tense ~backte~, the strong form ~buk~ (and even ~bأ¼ke~ in the subjunctive) survives, particularly in literary and regional usage. This dual conjugation pattern is a living fossil of the historical merger of the two verbs. The English cognate ~bake~ has lost all trace of its strong-verb heritage and conjugates entirely regularly. The PIE root *~bت°ehâ‚ƒg-~ points to a deep antiquity for this culinary technology -- the Indo-Europeans were already baking over five thousand years ago, and the word for it has survived relatively intact across millennia.
en.basieren:
Borrowed from French ~baser~ ("to base, to have as a foundation"), from ~base~ ("base, foundation"), from Old French ~base~, from Latin ~basis~, from Greek ~bأ،sis~ ("step, pedestal, base"), from PIE *~gت·mج¥-ti-~ ("a stepping"), from *~gت·em-~ ("to come, to go, to step"). The same PIE root gives English ~come~, German ~kommen~, Latin ~veniإچ~ ("I come"), and Greek ~baأ­nإچ~ ("I walk, I step").

~Basieren~ is used both transitively ("to base something on") and intransitively ("to be based on"): ~der Film basiert auf wahren Begebenheiten~ ("the film is based on true events"). The word entered German through the prestige of French intellectual and scientific discourse in the 17th and 18th centuries, when French was the language of European courts and academies. The ~-ieren~ suffix marks it as a Romance adaptation. The underlying metaphor â€” a building resting on its base â€” runs from Greek architecture through Latin engineering to modern German argumentation: one's argument, like one's building, must ~auf etwas basieren~.
en.basteln:
An Upper German verb, from earlier ~besteln~, an iterative (frequentative) of MHG ~besten~ ("to tie, to bind, to lace"), from Proto-West Germanic *~bastijan~ ("to plait, to sew, to weave"). The vowel ~-a-~ may reflect dialectal influence or readaptation to the underlying noun ~Bast~ ("bast, inner bark"), from MHG and OHG ~bast~ ("inner bark of trees").

~Basteln~ means "to do handicrafts, to tinker, to do DIY" â€” to make things by hand in an amateur, creative fashion. In medieval usage, the word carried a slightly pejorative connotation: it referred to the work of craftsmen without guild membership â€” unsanctioned handwork. The modern sense has shed this stigma entirely; ~basteln~ now evokes cheerful creativity, especially children's arts and crafts (~Bastelnachmittag~, "crafting afternoon") or hobby electronics (~an einem Radio basteln~, "to tinker with a radio"). The noun ~Bastler~ ("hobbyist, tinkerer, DIY enthusiast") is affectionate. The connection to ~Bast~ ("bast fiber") is fitting: bast was one of the earliest materials humans worked with their hands â€” binding, weaving, and plaiting the fibrous inner bark of linden and willow trees into rope, mats, and baskets.
en.bauen: From Middle High German ~bإ«wen~, from Old High German ~bإ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bإ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~bإ«anؤ…~ ("to dwell, reside"), from PIE *~bت°uH-~ ("to be, become, grow"). The semantic journey from "to be, exist" to "to dwell" to "to build" reflects how habitation and construction are inseparable: one builds where one lives. The same PIE root *~bت°uH-~ is extraordinarily productive across Indo-European, yielding English ~be~ (via PGmc *~beunؤ…~), Latin ~fuؤ«~ ("I was"), Greek ~phأ؛إچ~ ("to grow, bring forth"), and Sanskrit ~bhavati~ ("to be, become"). In the Germanic branch, the original sense of "dwelling" survives in ~Bauer~ ("farmer," literally "one who dwells on the land"), ~Nachbar~ ("neighbor," literally "one who dwells nearby"), and ~Bau~ ("structure, building"). Cognate with Dutch ~bouwen~, English ~bower~ ("dwelling"), ~neighbor~ (from Old English ~nؤ“ahgebإ«r~, "near-dweller"), Danish ~bygge~.
en.beachten:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~achten~:

- ~achten~: From MHG ~ahten~, from OHG ~ahtإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ahtإچn~, a denominative verb from *~ahtu~ ("consideration, attention"), of uncertain pre-Germanic origin.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, here applying attention directly to an object.

The ~be-~ prefix shifts ~achten~ ("to consider, heed generally") into the specifically transitive ~beachten~ ("to pay attention to, to observe, to comply with"). The distinction is visible in legal and instructional registers: ~beachten Sie~, not *~achten Sie~, is the standard formula in German instruction manuals and official notices. The negative ~unbeachtet~ ("unheeded, unnoticed") collapses back toward ~achten~'s older sense of ignoring. Cognate with Dutch ~beachten~ (archaic), Dutch ~in acht nemen~ (the functional equivalent).
en.beantragen:
From ~be-~ + ~Antrag~ + ~-en~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~antragen~, which is ~an-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~Antrag~: From MHG ~antrac~, a verbal noun derived from ~antragen~ ("to carry toward, to propose"), itself composed of ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~, "on, onto") + ~tragen~ (from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~, from PIE *~dت°regت°-~, "to pull, draw").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive: to direct a formal request at someone.

~Beantragen~ means "to apply for, to formally request, to propose" â€” exclusively in bureaucratic and legal contexts. It is the verb of official paperwork: one ~beantragt~ a visa, a building permit, a divorce. The word perfectly encodes the German administrative concept that formal proposals must be ~carried toward~ (~antragen~) an authority and ~directed at~ (~be-~) the relevant office. The derived noun ~Antrag~ itself has drifted from its literal meaning of "carrying toward" to "application, motion, proposal" â€” and in dated usage, even "marriage proposal" (~einen Antrag machen~). Cognate formations exist in Dutch (~aanvragen~, using a different root but the same structure).
en.beantworten: ~beantworten~ is not simply ~be-~ + ~antworten~ in the usual prefixation sense but a denominal verb formed directly from ~Antwort~ ("answer") with ~be-~ and the infinitive suffix ~-en~. ~Antwort~ comes from Middle High German ~antwأ¼rte~, from Old High German ~antwurti~, from Proto-Germanic *~andawurdijؤ…~ â€” itself from *~anda-~ ("against") and *~wurdijؤ…~ ("speech, conversation"), a derivative of *~wurdؤ…~ ("word, speech"). The noun's gender shifted from neuter to feminine through semantic association with ~Frage~ ("question"). A grammatically significant contrast: ~antworten~ ("to answer") governs the dative â€” ~jemandem antworten~ ("to answer someone") â€” while ~beantworten~ governs the accusative â€” ~eine Frage beantworten~ ("to answer a question"). The prefix ~be-~ here adds transitivity, shifting the object from the person answered to the thing answered. Cognates include Dutch ~antwoord~ and Afrikaans ~antwoord~.
en.bearbeiten: From ~be-~ + ~arbeiten~ ("to work"). The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") makes the base verb transitive. ~Arbeiten~ derives from Old High German ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijanؤ…~, ultimately from PIE *~hâ‚ƒأ³rbت°os~ ("orphan") â€” semantic evolution from "orphan" through "hardship, toil" (the lot of the fatherless) to "work." ~Bearbeiten~ intensifies and directs that labor: to process, to work on, to treat a material or topic. The prefix strips away intransitivity and focuses the action on an object â€” ~er arbeitet~ ("he works") becomes ~er bearbeitet den Text~ ("he processes the text"). Cognate with Dutch ~bewerken~.
en.beauftragen:
Derived from ~be-~ + ~Auftrag~ + ~-en~. Notably, this verb is not derived from the strong verb ~auftragen~ ("to apply, to serve food") but directly from the noun ~Auftrag~ ("commission, assignment, order"):

- ~Auftrag~: A deverbal noun from ~auftragen~ ("to carry upon, to apply"), from MHG ~إ«ftragen~, composed of ~auf-~ ("up, upon," from PIE *~upأ³~, "up from below") + ~tragen~ ("to carry," from MHG ~tragen~, OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~, "to draw, drag"). Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~.
- ~be-~: From Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). Turns the noun into a transitive verb.

The derivation from the noun rather than the verb explains why ~beauftragen~ conjugates weakly (~beauftragte~, ~beauftragt~) rather than with the strong ablaut pattern of ~tragen~ (~trug~, ~getragen~). This is a common German pattern: when a verb is derived from a noun (even if that noun itself came from a strong verb), the result is always a weak verb. The semantic chain runs from "to carry up" (~auftragen~) to "the thing carried up / laid upon someone" (~Auftrag~, a task or commission) to "to give someone a commission" (~beauftragen~). English ~draw~ is the cognate of ~tragen~, both from PIE *~dت°regت°-~, with the semantic split happening early: the Germanic branch kept "to carry," while the English branch specialized toward "to drag, to draw."
en.bedanken:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~danken~. Apparently not attested before Modern German (thus after 1400):

- ~danken~: From MHG ~danken~, from OHG ~dankإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ankإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankإچnؤ…~ ("to thank"), a denominative from *~أ¾ankaz~ ("thought, gratitude").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb reflexive-transitive: while ~danken~ takes a dative ("to thank someone"), ~sich bedanken~ emphasizes the act from the thanker's perspective.

~Sich bedanken~ means "to express one's thanks, to say thank you" and is slightly more emphatic or formal than simple ~danken~. The ~be-~ prefix transforms the dative construction (~jemandem danken~) into an accusative-reflexive one (~sich bei jemandem bedanken~), shifting the grammatical focus to the grateful person's active expression. The root noun *~أ¾ankaz~ ("thought") reveals that Germanic gratitude is literally "thinking of" someone â€” a semantic parallel to English ~thank~ (from ~think~). The connection between thought and gratitude is preserved in the German phrase ~Ich denke an dich~ ("I think of you"), where ~denken~ and ~danken~ share the same Proto-Germanic ancestor. Cognate with Dutch ~bedanken~, English ~thank~, Danish ~takke~.
en.bedenken:
From Middle High German ~bedenken~, from Old High German ~bidenken~, ~pidenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biأ¾ankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~biأ¾ankijanؤ…~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~denken~:

- ~denken~: From MHG ~denken~, from OHG ~denken~, ~thenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankijanؤ…~, from PIE *~tong-أ©ye-ti~, causative of *~teng-~ ("to think, feel, perceive, know").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive.

~Bedenken~ means "to consider, to reflect upon, to have reservations about." The prefix ~be-~ takes the intransitive act of thinking and gives it a direct object: one does not merely ~denken~ (think) but ~bedenkt~ (thinks over, considers) something specific. As a noun, ~Bedenken~ means "misgivings, reservations" â€” one's considered doubts. Cognate with English ~bethink~ (archaic, "to think about, to consider"), Dutch ~bedenken~ ("to think up, to consider"), and more distantly with Latin ~tongeإچ~ ("to know"). The English cognate ~think~ underwent a curious phonetic shift: Proto-Germanic *~أ¾~ became English ~th~ but German ~d~ (via the High German consonant shift), making ~denken~ and ~think~ less obviously related than they truly are.
en.bedeuten:
From Middle High German ~bediuten~, ~betiuten~, from Old High German ~bidiuten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~deuten~:

- ~deuten~: From MHG ~diuten~, from OHG ~diuten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾iudijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾iudijanؤ…~, derived from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾eudإچ~ ("people, nation"), the same root as Old English ~أ¾ؤ“od~ ("people").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The original sense of ~deuten~ was "to explain to the people" â€” to interpret, make intelligible. The surprising etymological kinship between ~bedeuten~ ("to mean") and ~Deutsch~ ("German") reflects their shared root: both trace to the Proto-Germanic word for "people," *~أ¾eudإچ~. Cognate with Dutch ~beduiden~, Icelandic ~أ¾أ½أ°a~ ("to translate"), Swedish ~tyda~, Danish ~tyde~.
en.bedienen: From ~be-~ + ~dienen~ ("to serve"). The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") makes the base verb transitive. ~Dienen~ derives from Old High German ~dionإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ewanإچnؤ…~ ("to serve"), a denominative of *~أ¾ewaz~ ("servant, slave"). The compound ~bedienen~ adds the transitivity that ~dienen~ (which takes the dative) lacks: ~jemandem dienen~ ("to serve someone") vs. ~jemanden bedienen~ ("to attend to someone, operate a machine"). This shift reflects the modern range of the verb: waiting tables, operating machinery, serving a customer. The Proto-Germanic noun *~أ¾ewaz~ also underlies Old English ~أ¾egn~, which elevated from "servant" to "warrior" to the Scottish title ~thane~, immortalized in Shakespeare's ~Macbeth~ â€” a social ascent that ~bedienen~ has not shared. Cognate with Dutch ~bedienen~.
en.bedrohen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~drohen~:

- ~drohen~: From MHG ~drإچn~, from OHG ~drouwen~, related to ~drأ¤uen~ ("to threaten"), from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rauwjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾rawjanؤ…~ ("to desire, suffer, threaten"), from the noun *~أ¾rawإچ~ ("suffering, longing"), from PIE *~trewhâ‚پ-~ ("to endure, suffer"). Cognate with Old English ~أ¾rؤ“agan~ ("to threaten, rebuke") and Old Norse ~أ¾reyja~ ("to long for").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the action toward a specific object.

The prefix ~be-~ converts intransitive ~drohen~ ("to threaten," with a dative) into transitive ~bedrohen~ ("to threaten someone/something," with an accusative). The semantic path from PIE "to endure suffering" to "to threaten" is instructive: the one who causes suffering came to be understood as the one who threatens, an agent-patient reversal that occurred within the Germanic branch. The noun ~Bedrohung~ ("threat, menace") is the standard term in both everyday and legal German. English ~threat~ is not from the same root â€” it descends from Old English ~أ¾rؤ“at~ ("crowd, pressure"), from a different PIE stem.
en.bedأ¼rfen:
From MHG ~bedأ¼rfen~, ~bedurfen~, from OHG ~bidurfan~ ("need, require"). A compound of ~be-~ + ~dأ¼rfen~.

- ~dأ¼rfen~: From MHG ~durfen~, OHG ~durfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾urban~. Cognate with archaic English ~tharf~ ("to need").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix transforms the base verb and shifts its argument structure â€” ~bedأ¼rfen~ is one of the rare modern German verbs that governs the genitive case (~Wir bedأ¼rfen eines starken Mannes~), a construction now otherwise largely extinct.

The modern meanings of ~dأ¼rfen~ ("to be allowed to, may") and ~bedأ¼rfen~ ("to require, to need") have diverged sharply from each other. In older German, ~dأ¼rfen~ itself could mean "to need" (the genitive construction seen in Luther's Bible translation). ~Bedأ¼rfen~ preserved that older sense by adding the transitivizing ~be-~ prefix, while bare ~dأ¼rfen~ shifted to expressing permission. ~Bedأ¼rfnis~ ("need, desire") keeps the older meaning alive.
en.beeindrucken:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~eindrأ¼cken~ ("to press in, to impress"):

- ~drأ¼cken~: From MHG ~drأ¼cken~, from OHG ~drucchen~, ~drukken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rukkijan~ ("to press"). A doublet of ~drucken~ ("to print"), which has Upper German lack of umlaut before a velar geminate â€” the first German printers worked in southern Germany.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the impressing action onto a person.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). Conveys inward direction.

~Beeindrucken~ means "to impress" â€” literally to press an image into someone's mind. The noun ~Eindruck~ ("impression") is itself a calque of Latin ~impressiإچ~ (from ~in-~ + ~premere~, "to press into"). The parallel is strikingly exact: both the Latin and Germanic compounds use "in" + "press" to describe how experiences stamp themselves upon consciousness. The doublet ~drucken~/~drأ¼cken~ is a fascinating case of semantic specialization through dialect: Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rukkijan~ meant simply "to press," but when southern German printers adopted the technology in the 15th century, their local dialect form ~drucken~ (without umlaut) became the technical term for printing, while the umlauted ~drأ¼cken~ retained the general sense of pressing and pushing.
en.beeinflussen: ~beeinflussen~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Einfluss~ ("influence") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to influence, to have an effect on." ~Einfluss~ itself is a calque of Latin ~influentia~ (from ~influere~, "to flow in"), rendered as ~ein-~ ("in-") + ~Fluss~ ("flow, river"), from Middle High German ~vluz~, from Old High German ~fluz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fluti~, from Proto-Germanic *~flutiz~, from PIE *~plewd-~ ("to flow, run"). Note that German ~Fluss~ and Latin ~fluvius~ are not cognates â€” Latin took the branch *~bت°lewH-~ while Germanic took *~plewd-~. No simplex *~einflussen~ exists in German; ~beeinflussen~ is formed directly from the noun ~Einfluss~, making it a beautiful example of double prefixation: ~be-~ + ~ein-~ + ~Fluss~ + ~-en~. The concept of influence as a "flowing in" â€” originally astrological, describing stellar emanations flowing into human affairs â€” passed through Latin, was calqued into German, and then verbalized with the ~be-~ prefix that adds the transitive sense of directing that flow upon something.
en.beeintrأ¤chtigen:
A denominative verb formed from ~Eintracht~ ("concord, harmony, unity") with the prefix ~be-~ and verbal suffix ~-igen~, yielding the sense "to act against someone's harmony." The core noun ~Eintracht~ derives from Middle High German ~eintraht~ ("agreement, accord"), which developed from the MHG phrase ~أ¼ber ein tragen~ ("to come to agreement, to agree"), literally "to carry into one." The MHG noun is a borrowing and phonetic adaptation from Middle Low German ~ؤ“ndracht~, analyzable as ~ein~ ("one") + ~Tracht~ ("bearing, carrying"). ~Tracht~ itself comes from MHG ~traht~, from OHG ~trahta~, from Proto-Germanic *~drahtuz~, from the verb *~draganؤ…~ ("to drag, to carry"), whence modern ~tragen~. Cognate with Dutch ~dracht~ and English ~draught~.
