The prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") makes the verb transitive, and the overall meaning of ~beeintrأ¤chtigen~ is "to impair, to detract from, to prejudice" -- literally, to act upon someone's concord. This is a beautiful example of German's talent for building complex concepts from transparent components. The related ~Eintracht~ lives on as the name of several German football clubs, most famously ~Eintracht Frankfurt~, where it retains its original sense of unity. Note that despite superficial similarity, ~Eintracht~ is historically unrelated to ~trachten~ ("to strive"), which comes from Latin ~tractؤپre~ via OHG ~trahtإچn~, though modern speakers often associate the two.
en.beenden: ~beenden~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~enden~ ("to end"), from Middle High German ~enden~, from Old High German ~entإچn~. ~Enden~ is itself a denominal verb formed from ~Ende~ ("end"), which comes from Old High German ~enti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~andؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~andijaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚entأ­os~ ("front, forehead, tip") â€” the same root that gave Latin ~ante~ ("before"). The PIE sense of "forehead" developed into "boundary, edge, end" as the front of something became conceptually the limit of it. The prefix ~be-~ adds a sense of completion and transitivity: while ~enden~ can be intransitive ("the road ends"), ~beenden~ always takes a direct object ("to finish, conclude something"). A subtle semantic note: ~beenden~ implies a deliberate, willed conclusion, while ~enden~ is neutral â€” a process can ~enden~ on its own, but a person ~beendet~ it.
en.befassen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~ ("to fetch, to hold, to grasp"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb reflexive-transitive â€” directing the action of grasping upon a subject.

~Sich befassen mit~ means "to deal with, to occupy oneself with, to engage with" â€” ~sich mit einem Problem befassen~ ("to deal with a problem"). The physical root is still visible: ~fassen~ means "to grasp, to seize," and ~befassen~ extends this into the intellectual domain â€” grasping a subject with the mind. The cognacy between German ~fassen~ and English ~fetch~ is a surprising one: both derive from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~, but the semantics diverged â€” German kept the "grasp, hold" sense while English developed "go and get." The ~be-~ prefix performs its classic function of turning an intransitive into a transitive-reflexive construction: one does not merely grasp (~fassen~), one actively engages oneself with a topic (~sich befassen~). The word is characteristic of administrative and academic German: committees ~befassen sich mit Antrأ¤gen~ ("occupy themselves with applications"), scholars ~befassen sich mit Forschungsfragen~ ("engage with research questions").
en.befestigen:
From Middle High German ~bevestigen~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~festigen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~festigen~:

- ~festigen~: From ~fest~ + ~-igen~, where ~fest~ descends from MHG ~vest~, from OHG ~festi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fast~, from Proto-Germanic *~fastuz~ ("firm, solid"). Cognate with English ~fast~ (originally meaning "firm, fixed," as in ~hold fast~ or ~steadfast~, before acquiring its speed sense through the idea of running firmly or decisively).
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive: "to fasten something onto."

The semantic range of ~befestigen~ spans from the physical ("to attach, fasten, secure") to the military ("to fortify"), preserving the original Germanic sense of making something firm and immovable. A ~Befestigung~ can be both a wall bracket and a fortress. English ~fast~ and German ~fest~ are doublets that drifted apart dramatically: English ~fast~ primarily means "quick" today, while German ~fest~ still means "firm, solid, fixed." The PIE root also yielded Old Norse ~fastr~ and Gothic ~fastan~ ("to hold firm"), the latter giving rise to the religious practice of ~fasting~, originally meaning "to hold firmly to a discipline."
en.befinden:
From Middle High German ~bevinden~, from Old High German ~bifindan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bifinأ¾an~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~finأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The semantic development from "to find (transitively)" to "to be located; to judge, deem" reflects the ~be-~ prefix's tendency to shift meaning toward a state or condition. The reflexive ~sich befinden~ ("to be located, to feel") developed in Early New High German.
en.befragen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~fragen~:

- ~fragen~: From MHG ~vrؤپgen~, from OHG ~frؤپgؤ“n~, ~frahؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frؤپgؤ“n~, a denominative verb from *~frؤپgu~ ("question"). Cognate with Saterland Frisian ~frأ¤igje~, Dutch ~vragen~, Low German ~fragen~, English ~frain~ (archaic "to ask, inquire"), Swedish ~frأ¥ga~, and Icelandic ~fregna~ ("to hear of, learn about").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the asking at a specific person or topic.

Where simple ~fragen~ means "to ask" in general, ~befragen~ means "to question, to interrogate, to consult" -- the ~be-~ prefix directs the act at a particular person and implies a more thorough, structured inquiry. One ~fragt~ a friend casually, but one ~befragt~ a witness, a database, or an expert. The derived noun ~Befragung~ ("survey, interrogation") is standard in social-science and legal contexts.
en.befreien:
~be-~ + ~freien~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~freien~/~frei~:

- ~frei~/~freien~: From MHG ~vrؤ«~, from OHG ~frؤ«~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~frijaz~ ("free, beloved"), from PIE *~preyH-~ ("to love, to please"). The denominative verb ~freien~ ("to woo, to court") is from Proto-Germanic *~frijإچnؤ…~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the adjective-derived verb transitive â€” one ~befreit~ someone or something.

~Befreien~ means "to liberate, to free, to release." The etymology of ~frei~ conceals one of the most remarkable semantic shifts in Indo-European: PIE *~preyH-~ meant "to love, to please," and *~priyأ³s~ meant "dear, beloved" (compare Sanskrit ~priyأ،-~ "dear, favorite"). In the Germanic and Celtic branches alone, this word for "beloved" acquired the meaning "free" â€” the idea being that the beloved, the members of one's own tribe and family, were the free people, as opposed to slaves and outsiders. Proto-Germanic *~frijaz~ thus meant both "beloved" and "free," and the noun *~frijإچndz~ ("lover, friend") became English ~friend~ and German ~Freund~. The goddess ~Freyja~ / ~Frigg~ bears a name from the same root. ~Befreien~ thus carries within it an ancient equation: to liberate someone is to restore them to the status of a loved one, a member of the community.
en.befأ¶rdern:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~fأ¶rdern~. The verb is either a ~be-~ prefixed form of ~fأ¶rdern~ ("to promote, to advance") or a derivation from ~vorder~ ("front, fore"):

- ~fأ¶rdern~: From MHG ~vأ¼rdern~, from OHG ~furdiren~, a derivation from ~vorder~/~furdir~ ("further, forward"), from Proto-Germanic *~furأ¾er-~ ("further"), from PIE *~per-~ ("forward, through"). Cognate with Old English ~fyrأ°ran~ ("to further"), English ~further~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

Before the 16th century, ~befأ¶rdern~ was used like its root ~fأ¶rdern~ in the general sense of "to advance." From the 16th century onward, it developed the specific meanings "to transport" and "to support." In the 18th or 19th century â€” sources disagree on the exact date â€” it acquired the meaning "to promote in rank" as a German replacement for the French loanword ~avancieren~ ("to advance"). It also came to mean "to ship, to forward goods" as a replacement for ~spedieren~. The root ~fأ¶rdern~ itself is related to English ~further~: both derive from the same Proto-Germanic comparative form meaning "more forward." The original nebenforms ~bevordern~ and ~befأ¼rdern~ show the vowel variation typical of Middle High German dialects.
en.befأ¼rchten:
From Middle High German ~befأ¼rhten~, ~bevأ¼rhten~, from Old High German ~biforhten~, ~bifurihtإچn~, ~biforhtإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bifurhtijan~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~fأ¼rchten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~fأ¼rchten~:

- ~fأ¼rchten~: From MHG ~vأ¼rhten~, from OHG ~furihtan~, synchronically analyzable as ~Furcht~ + ~-en~. The noun ~Furcht~ descends from MHG ~vorht~, OHG ~forhta~, from Proto-Germanic *~furhtإچ~ ("fear"), from PIE *~prج¥k-~ ("to fear"). The modern vowel ~-أ¼-~ is due to umlaut influence from the verb.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, allowing a direct object: one ~befأ¼rchtet~ a specific outcome.

Cognate with Middle Low German ~bevrأ¼chten~ and the obsolete English ~befright~ ("to terrify"). The PIE root *~prج¥k-~ also produced English ~fright~ (though via a different Germanic stem, *~furhtؤ¯ج„~). The semantic distinction between ~fأ¼rchten~ and ~befأ¼rchten~ is subtle but important: ~fأ¼rchten~ expresses a general emotional state of fear, while ~befأ¼rchten~ implies a rational apprehension about a specific, anticipated negative outcome. One ~fأ¼rchtet~ the dark but ~befأ¼rchtet~ that it will rain. This distinction between visceral fear and reasoned worry has no neat parallel in English, which uses ~fear~ for both.
en.begeben:
~be-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~ ("to give"), from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give, move"). Cognate with English ~give~ (from the Old Norse descendant rather than the native English reflex).
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Begeben~ is a formal, almost literary verb with several distinct meanings. The reflexive ~sich begeben~ ("to betake oneself, to go, to proceed") carries an air of deliberate, purposeful movement: ~er begab sich auf den Heimweg~ ("he made his way home"). The impersonal construction ~es begab sich~ ("it came to pass") is the opening formula of biblical narrative in Martin Luther's translation â€” ~und es begab sich zu jener Zeit~ â€” and sounds archly archaic in any other context. A third meaning, "to issue" (a financial instrument), belongs to the world of banking: ~eine Anleihe begeben~ ("to issue a bond"). And the reflexive ~sich eines Rechts begeben~ ("to relinquish a right") adds yet another layer. This semantic diversity â€” going, happening, issuing, renouncing â€” all radiates from the core image of ~geben~: to give oneself over to movement, to give forth a story, to give up a claim. Cognate with the now-archaic English ~begive~.
en.begegnen:
From Middle High German ~begegenen~, ~begagenen~, from Old High German ~bigaganan~ ("to meet, to encounter"), with prefix from Proto-West Germanic *~gaginijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gaginjanؤ…~. Equivalent to ~be-~ + ~gegnen~ ("to encounter"). Cognate with Dutch ~bejegenen~.

Despite the surface resemblance to a standard ~be-~ compound, ~begegnen~ has a unique history. The root ~gegnen~ (now archaic in isolation) descends from the Proto-Germanic adverb/preposition *~gagin~ ("against, toward"), cognate with English ~again~ and ~against~ (from Old English ~ongؤ“an~, "toward, against"). To meet someone was, etymologically, to go toward them â€” to come face to face. The verb takes the dative case (~ich begegne dir~, not ~*ich begegne dich~), a relic of its original directional sense: one encounters someone by moving toward them. The formal ~begegnen~ and the informal ~treffen~ ("to meet," from "to hit, to strike") offer an illuminating contrast: ~begegnen~ emphasizes the chance or directional quality of an encounter, while ~treffen~ emphasizes the impact â€” you cross paths versus you collide. Cognate with Dutch ~bejegenen~ ("to treat, to deal with"), which has shifted to a purely interpersonal meaning.
en.begehen:
From Middle High German ~begؤ“n~, ~begؤپn~, from Old High German ~bigؤ“n~, ~bigؤپn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bigؤپn~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with English ~go~, Dutch ~gaan~, Swedish and Danish ~gأ¥~.
- ~be-~: A prefix that typically makes an intransitive verb transitive, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near").

The ~be-~ prefix transforms the intransitive "to go" into a transitive verb meaning "to walk upon" or "to commit (a crime, a celebration)." Cognate with Dutch ~begaan~ and the archaic English ~bego~. The dual meaning is striking: ~eine Straأںe begehen~ means "to walk along a street," while ~ein Verbrechen begehen~ means "to commit a crime" and ~ein Jubilأ¤um begehen~ means "to celebrate an anniversary." All three senses share the underlying idea of "going through" something, whether a path, an act, or an occasion. The spelling with silent ~-h-~ in ~gehen~ was introduced by analogy with ~sehen~, in which the ~h~ had become mute but was preserved in writing.
en.begeistern:
From ~be-~ + ~Geist~ + ~-ern~, a denominal verb meaning literally "to fill with spirit, to inspire":

- ~Geist~: From MHG ~geist~, from OHG ~geist~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gaist~, from Proto-Germanic *~gaistaz~, from PIE *~اµت°أ³ysdos~ ("anger, agitation"). Cognate with Dutch ~geest~, West Frisian ~geast~, English ~ghost~, Swedish ~gast~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the denominal verb transitive â€” it directs the filling of spirit onto an object.

The etymological link between ~Geist~ ("spirit, mind") and English ~ghost~ is one of Germanic's most dramatic semantic divergences. Both descend from PGmc *~gaistaz~, but where German elevated ~Geist~ to mean "intellect, spirit, mind" (as in ~Zeitgeist~, "spirit of the age," or ~Geisteswissenschaften~, "humanities"), English narrowed ~ghost~ to mean a spectral apparition. The PIE root *~اµت°أ³ysdos~ originally meant "agitation, fury" â€” the ~Geist~ was a state of being seized by something beyond oneself. ~Begeistern~ preserves this ancient sense beautifully: to be ~begeistert~ is to be filled with spirit, seized by enthusiasm, possessed by excitement. The suffix ~-ern~ (a verbal derivation pattern also seen in ~klettern~, ~wandern~) creates the denominal verb from the noun ~Geist~, and the ~be-~ prefix makes it transitive. The derived noun ~Begeisterung~ ("enthusiasm, excitement") is among the most expressive words in the German emotional vocabulary.
en.beginnen: From Middle High German ~beginnen~, from Old High German ~biginnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biginnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~biginnanؤ…~. The etymology of the simplex root *~ginnanؤ…~ is debated: some scholars connect it to PIE *~gت°en-~ ("to gape, yawn, open"), suggesting an original sense of "to open" and hence "to begin." Others consider it a substrate word with no secure Indo-European etymology. A class III strong verb with the nasal-infix ablaut pattern (~beginnen~, ~begann~, ~begonnen~). Cognate with Low German ~beginnen~, Dutch ~beginnen~, West Frisian ~begjinne~, English ~begin~.
en.begleiten:
From Middle High German ~begleiten~ (14th century), from an underlying *~begeleiten~, a confluence of ~beleiten~ and ~geleiten~, both prefixed forms of ~leiten~ ("to lead"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~Geleit~ ("escort, convoy") + ~-en~:

- ~Geleit~ (root): A nominalization of ~geleiten~ ("to escort, conduct"), itself from MHG ~geleite~, a compound of ~ge-~ + ~leiten~. ~Leiten~ ("to lead") descends from OHG ~leitan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, a causative verb formed from PGmc *~laidijanؤ…~, from *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to travel, go") + causative suffix *~-janؤ…~. The root *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ stems from PIE *~leyt-~ ("to go forth, depart"), the same root that underlies Gothic terms for dying and departing.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The critical warning for learners: ~begleiten~ does NOT descend from ~gleiten~ ("to glide"), despite looking like it should. The ~gl-~ sequence is a coincidence â€” the ~g~ belongs to the old collective prefix ~ge-~ and the ~l~ begins ~leiten~ ("to lead"). The underlying idea is "to provide someone with a Geleit (escort)," not "to glide alongside." Cognate with Dutch ~begeleiden~.
en.begreifen:
From Middle High German ~begrifen~, from Old High German ~bigrؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bigrؤ«pan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grؤ«fen~, from OHG ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("by, at, around"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). As a prefix, ~be-~ makes intransitive verbs transitive or adds a thoroughgoing sense.

The semantic development from "to grasp physically" to "to comprehend mentally" is one of the most universal metaphors in language. English ~comprehend~ follows the exact same pattern: Latin ~comprehendere~ means "to seize, grasp together." Similarly, English ~grasp~ itself moved from physical to mental. German preserves both layers: ~greifen~ remains physical, while ~begreifen~ is almost entirely intellectual. Cognate with Dutch ~begrijpen~ ("to understand") and Low German ~begriepen~. The related noun ~Begriff~ ("concept, notion") literally means "that which has been grasped."
en.begrenzen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~grenzen~, from the noun ~Grenze~:

- ~Grenze~: From MHG ~grenize~, borrowed from Old Polish ~granica~ ("border, frontier"). This Slavic loanword spread westward through German in the fifteenth century and was reinforced by Luther's Bible translation. It replaced older Germanic terms for "border." Doublet of ~Granne~ ("awn, bristle").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near").

~Grenze~ is one of the most important Slavic loanwords in German, reflecting centuries of contact along the eastern frontier. Before its arrival, German used words like ~Mark~ (as in ~Dأ¤nemark~, ~Ostmark~) and ~Rain~ for borders. The borrowed word conquered the entire semantic territory. ~Begrenzen~ adds the ~be-~ prefix to make the noun into a transitive verb: "to limit, to restrict, to mark the boundary of."
en.begrأ¼nden:
Equivalent to ~be-~ + ~grأ¼nden~:

- ~grأ¼nden~: A denominal verb from ~Grund~ ("ground, reason, foundation"), from MHG ~grunt~, from OHG ~grunt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grundu~, from Proto-Germanic *~grunduz~, from PIE *~gت°rendت°-~ ("to grind").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the act of grounding onto an argument or institution.

~Begrأ¼nden~ means "to found, to establish" and "to justify, to give reasons for" â€” a dual meaning rooted in the polysemy of ~Grund~ itself, which means both "ground" (physical foundation) and "reason" (logical foundation). One ~begrأ¼ndet~ a company (founds it on solid ground) or an argument (gives it reasons). The derived noun ~Begrأ¼ndung~ ("justification, reasoning") is standard in legal and academic German. The PIE root *~gت°rendت°-~ ("to grind") connects to the physical sense of ground â€” what has been ground down, the bottom layer. English ~ground~ is a direct cognate of ~Grund~, and both preserve the same literal-to-figurative extension: from the earth beneath your feet to the basis of your argument. Cognate with Dutch ~begronden~.
en.begrأ¼أںen: From Middle High German ~begrأ¼ezen~, from Old High German ~bigruozen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bigrإچtijan~; equivalent to ~be-~ + ~grأ¼أںen~. The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") intensifies and makes the greeting act outwardly directed. ~Grأ¼أںen~ itself derives from Middle High German ~grأ¼ezen~, from Old High German ~gruozzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grإچtijan~. The noun ~Gruأں~ ("greeting") is a deverbal form from Middle High German ~gruoب¥~. Cognate with German Low German ~begrأ¶ten~, Dutch ~begroeten~, West Frisian ~begroetsje~, and English ~greet~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic root, without the ~be-~ prefix). The English cognate ~begreet~ exists but is archaic. A greeting, etymologically, is an act of touching or addressing â€” the root sense in Proto-West Germanic was likely physical approach or hailing.
en.behalten: ~behalten~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~halten~ ("to hold, keep"), from Middle High German ~halten~, from Old High German ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~, of uncertain PIE origin â€” possibly from *~ل¸±el-~ ("to hide, conceal"). ~Behalten~ appears in Old High German as ~bihaltan~ and in Middle High German as ~behalten~. Its cognates across Germanic are striking: Dutch ~behouden~ ("to keep, preserve"), English ~behold~ â€” which preserves the older sense of "to hold in view, to grasp with the eyes" â€” and the Scandinavian forms Danish and Norwegian ~beholde~, Swedish ~behأ¥lla~. The English ~behold~ and German ~behalten~ are thus direct cognates, though ~behold~ has narrowed to visual perception while ~behalten~ means "to keep, retain." The prefix ~be-~ intensifies the holding, giving it the sense of retaining something in one's possession.
en.behandeln:
From Middle High German ~behandeln~ ("to extend one's hand to; to deal with"), from Old High German ~bihantalإچn~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~handeln~:

- ~handeln~: From MHG ~handeln~, from OHG ~hantalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~handulإچn~, a denominative verb from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~ ("hand") with the frequentative suffix *~-lإچnؤ…~. Cognate with Dutch ~handelen~, English ~handle~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically extends the action of the base verb onto an explicit object.

The verb's original MHG meaning â€” "to extend one's hand to someone" â€” is strikingly physical: the medical sense of "to treat a patient" preserves exactly this original gesture, the physician's hand laid upon the sufferer. From the same handshake root comes "to deal with a subject" (~ein Thema behandeln~) and "to treat someone a certain way" (~jemanden respektvoll behandeln~). The word ~Behandlung~ thus covers medical treatment, thematic coverage, and interpersonal conduct â€” all radiating from the extended hand. Cognate with Dutch ~behandelen~.
en.behaupten: ~behaupten~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Haupt~ ("head, chief") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to assert, claim, maintain." The original sense was "to maintain as the main point, to hold as chief." ~Haupt~ comes from Middle High German ~houbet~, from Old High German ~houbit~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haubud~, from Proto-Germanic *~haubudؤ…~, from PIE *~kأ،put~ ("head") â€” the same root that produced Latin ~caput~ ("head"), English ~head~, and the anatomical term ~capital~. The ~-au-~ in ~Haupt~ reflects Upper German dialectal development where ~-ou-~ did not undergo umlaut before labials; the Central German umlaut form survives in ~Hأ¤uptling~ ("chieftain") and the phrase ~zu Hأ¤upten~ ("at the head of"). The semantic journey from "head" to "to assert" â€” through the intermediate step of "to hold the chief position, to maintain as one's main claim" â€” is a vivid example of how body-part metaphors generate abstract intellectual vocabulary.
en.beheben:
From Middle High German ~beheben~ ("to take away"), equivalent to ~be-~ + ~heben~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~heben~:

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, ~heven~ (rarer ~heffen~), from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~. Cognate with Dutch ~heffen~, English ~heave~, and Danish ~haeve~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, allowing a direct object like a problem or defect.

The semantic journey of ~beheben~ is striking: it originally meant "to take away, remove" in Middle High German, and this sense of removal persisted into the modern meaning "to fix, repair, remedy." Fixing a problem is, after all, removing it. The ~-b-~ in ~heben~ is itself a product of regularization; in Middle High German, the paradigm alternated between fricative ~-v-~ and stop ~-b-~, and the ~-b-~ won out. The original fricative survives in the related noun ~Hefe~ ("yeast," literally "that which heaves or rises"). The original past forms ~hub~ and ~gehaben~ were retained into Early Modern German before being replaced. English ~heave~ is a cognate from the same source. In Austrian German, ~beheben~ has an additional meaning not found in Germany: "to withdraw" (money from an account), preserving the older "take away" sense in a financial context.
en.beherrschen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~herrschen~:

- ~herrschen~: From MHG ~hؤ“rschen~/~hؤ“rsen~, from OHG ~hؤ“rrisإچn~/~hؤ“risإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hairisإچn~ ("to be strong, dominate"), from the root of ~hehr~ ("sublime, awe-inspiring"), from Proto-Germanic *~hairaz~ ("grey, hoary"), from PIE *~(s)kج‚ehâ‚ƒi-~, an extension of *~(s)kج‚ehâ‚ƒ-~ ("grey"). The connection between "grey-haired" and "lordly" reflects a culture that equated age with authority. The first element was later remodeled after ~Herr~ ("lord, gentleman") during the Middle High German period. Cognate with English ~hoar~ ("grey").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("by, at, around"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). As a prefix, ~be-~ typically makes intransitive verbs transitive or adds an exhaustive sense.

The ~be-~ prefix makes the intransitive ~herrschen~ ("to rule over") into a transitive verb: ~beherrschen~ means "to master" or "to dominate" something directly. The reflexive ~sich beherrschen~ means "to restrain oneself" -- literally to rule over oneself. Cognate with Dutch ~beheersen~ and Yiddish ~×‘×گض·×”×¢×¨×©×ں~.
en.beinhalten: ~beinhalten~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Inhalt~ ("content, contents") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to contain, to entail." ~Inhalt~ breaks down by surface analysis as ~in-~ + ~Halt~ (a nominalized form of ~halten~, "to hold"), giving the sense of "that which is held within." ~Halten~ derives from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~, of uncertain PIE origin. The prefix ~be-~ here does not add its usual transitivity â€” ~beinhalten~ is already transitive â€” but instead adds a formal or slightly elevated register: while ~enthalten~ ("to contain") is the everyday word, ~beinhalten~ appears in legal, academic, and official prose. A common learner's error is to misparse the word as ~bei-~ + ~nhalten~, but ~bei-~ is not a verbal prefix in modern German in this function; the correct segmentation is ~be-~ + ~Inhalt~ + ~-en~. No simplex *~inhalten~ exists as a standard verb â€” ~beinhalten~ is fully lexicalized as a whole.
en.beitragen:
Compound of ~bei-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). A class VI strong verb. Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~.
- ~bei-~: From MHG ~bؤ«-~, from OHG ~bؤ«-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). As a separable prefix, ~bei-~ conveys accompaniment, proximity, and the idea of adding something alongside something else.

The compound's meaning â€” "to contribute, to lend to a cause" â€” is a clean metaphor: one carries (~tragen~) something alongside (~bei-~) the efforts of others. The noun ~Beitrag~ ("contribution, fee, article, premium") covers everything from a financial subscription to an essay in a journal to an insurance payment, always carrying the sense of something carried alongside a collective effort. That ~tragen~ is cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~ is not immediately obvious â€” the High German consonant shift converted the initial ~d~ to ~t~, obscuring the relationship. Cognate with Dutch ~bijdragen~.
en.bekennen:
~be-~ + ~kennen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~kennen~:

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannjan~, a causative of *~kunnan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijanؤ…~ ("to make known"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix here makes the verb transitive and intensifies the action â€” from mere knowing to active declaration.

~Bekennen~ means "to confess, to profess, to avow" â€” to make one's knowledge or belief public. The prefix ~be-~ transforms interior knowing (~kennen~) into outward declaration. The compound appears already in Old High German as ~bikennen~ ("to recognize, acknowledge"). The derived noun ~Bekenntnis~ ("confession, creed") carries deep religious weight in German: the ~Augsburger Bekenntnis~ (Augsburg Confession, 1530) was the foundational document of Lutheranism. The reflexive ~sich bekennen zu~ ("to profess, to declare allegiance to") remains one of the most forceful verbs of commitment in German. Cognate with Dutch ~bekennen~ ("to confess").
en.bekommen:
From Middle High German ~bekomen~, from Old High German ~biqueman~, ~bikweman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bikweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~bikwemanؤ…~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~quemen~, ~komen~, ~kumen~, from OHG ~queman~ (rarer ~cuman~, ~chuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanؤ…~, from the aorist subjunctive of PIE *~gت·emt~ ("to take a step, arrive"), from *~gت·em-~ ("to step").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

Despite the compound's formal resemblance to English ~become~, the two have diverged sharply in meaning: German ~bekommen~ means "to receive, get," while English ~become~ means "to come to be." This false-friend pair is a famous pitfall for learners in both directions. Cognate with Dutch ~bekomen~.
en.bekأ¤mpfen:
From Middle High German ~bekempfen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~kampfen~:

- ~kampfen~: From MHG ~kempfen~, from OHG *~kempfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kampijan~, a denominative verb from *~kamp~ ("field, battlefield"), itself borrowed from Latin ~campus~ ("field"). The semantic journey from "open field" to "battlefield" to "fight" reflects how combat in the ancient world took place on open terrain. Cognate with English ~camp~ (which retained the spatial sense) and ~champion~ (via Old French from Latin ~campio~, "fighter on the field").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("by, at, around"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, turning the intransitive "to fight" into "to fight against something."

The word ~Kampf~ is one of several Latin military loans that entered Germanic languages during the centuries of Roman-Germanic contact along the ~limes~, the empire's northern frontier. Dutch ~kampen~ ("to fight, to struggle") is a close cognate, while English ~combat~ arrived later from French, covering similar semantic ground through an entirely different route.
en.belasten:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Last~ + ~-en~:

- ~Last~: From MHG ~last~, from OHG ~last~, ~hlast~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlasti~ ("load, burden"), from PIE *~klehâ‚‚-~ ("to put, lay out"). The initial ~hl-~ cluster, still visible in OHG, was simplified to ~l-~ in later German. Cognate with English ~last~ (in the sense of a unit of weight/cargo) and ~laden~ ("to load"); English ~ballast~ is a compound containing the same root.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near").

The semantic range of ~belasten~ maps neatly onto different kinds of burden: physical ("to load, to stress"), psychological ("to burden, to weigh on"), legal ("to incriminate"), and financial ("to debit, to charge an account"). The related ~belأ¤stigen~ ("to harass, to bother") looks similar but derives from the adjective ~lأ¤stig~ ("annoying") and carries a distinctly different meaning -- an annoyance rather than a load.
en.belaufen:
From Middle High German ~beloufen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~laufen~. Cognate with Dutch ~belopen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupanؤ…~ ("to jump forward, to leap"), from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, to hop"). Cognate with Dutch ~lopen~, English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Danish ~lأ¸be~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The reflexive ~sich belaufen auf~ means "to amount to, to total" â€” an oddly physical metaphor for a numerical concept, as though a sum "runs itself" to a certain point. The root verb ~laufen~ underwent a dramatic semantic shift: Proto-Germanic *~hlaupanؤ…~ meant "to leap, to jump," and this sense is preserved in English ~leap~, while German ~laufen~ gradually broadened from "leaping" to "running" to simply "going on foot." The initial ~hl-~ cluster, still visible in Old High German ~hloufan~, was simplified to ~l-~ during the Middle High German period â€” a common fate for initial clusters throughout Germanic. Lithuanian ~klأ¹pti~ ("to stumble") is a distant Baltic cousin from the same PIE root.
en.belegen: ~belegen~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~legen~ ("to lay, place"), from Middle High German ~legen~, from Old High German ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~ â€” a causative form of the root *~legh-~ ("to lie, lay down"). The prefix ~be-~ shifts the meaning from the intransitive placement to covering or occupying a space entirely. Cognates of ~legen~ include English ~lay~, Danish ~lأ¦gge~, and the English prefix verb ~belay~ â€” the nautical term for securing a rope, which is the direct English cognate of ~belegen~. A delightful false friend: ~belegen~ can mean "to book" (a seat, table, or room) as well as "to document" or "to coat" â€” contexts where laying something upon a surface shifts into reservation or proof.
en.beleuchten:
From Middle High German ~beliuhten~, from Old High German ~biliuhten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~leuchten~:

- ~leuchten~: From OHG ~liuhten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liuhtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~liuhtijanؤ…~, a denominative or factitive verb from *~leuhtؤ…~ ("light") or *~leuhtaz~ ("light, bright"), ultimately from PIE *~lewk-~ ("to shine"). Cognate with English ~light~, Dutch ~lichten~, and Latin ~lux~ ("light").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("at, by, near"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, turning "to shine" into "to illuminate (something)."

The PIE root *~lewk-~ is one of the most productive roots in Indo-European languages, giving rise not only to ~leuchten~ and ~light~ but also to Latin ~lإ«cؤ“re~ ("to shine"), whence ~lucid~ and ~Lucifer~ (literally "light-bearer"). The ~be-~ prefix transforms intransitive ~leuchten~ ("to shine, glow") into transitive ~beleuchten~ ("to illuminate, to shed light on"), a pattern found across dozens of German ~be-~ verbs. In figurative usage, ~beleuchten~ also means "to examine" or "to shed light on a topic," mirroring English "to illuminate a subject."
en.belohnen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Lohn~ + ~-en~:

- ~Lohn~: From MHG ~lأ´n~, from OHG ~lإچn~ ("reward, wages"), from Proto-West Germanic *~laun~, from Proto-Germanic *~launؤ…~, from PIE *~lehâ‚‚w-~ ("to capture, enjoy, profit"). Cognate with Old English ~lؤ“an~ ("reward"), Gothic ~laun~, and -- surprisingly -- Latin ~lucrum~ ("profit," whence English ~lucre~ and ~lucrative~) and Greek ~leأ­a~ ("booty, spoils").
- ~be-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). Here it functions as a transitivizer, turning the noun ~Lohn~ into a transitive verb meaning "to give a reward to."

~Belohnen~ means "to reward." The deep etymology reveals that ~Lohn~ ("wages, reward") and ~lucre~ ("monetary gain") share a common ancestor in the PIE root *~lehâ‚‚w-~, whose original sense was "to seize, to enjoy the fruits of seizure." The semantic path from "captured spoils" to "earned wages" to "reward" reflects the evolution from raiding economies to settled labor. The related Old English word ~lؤ“an~ ("reward") died out in English, replaced by the French borrowing ~reward~ (from Old Norman French ~rewarder~, "to regard, to repay"). German preserved the native Germanic term, making ~belohnen~ a linguistic survivor.
en.bemerken:
From Middle High German ~bemerken~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~merken~:

- ~merken~: From MHG ~merken~, from OHG ~merken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~markإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~markإچnؤ…~ ("to mark, to note"), from *~markإچ~ ("boundary, sign"), from PIE *~merاµ-~ ("boundary, edge").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the act of noting onto a specific object.

~Bemerken~ means "to notice, to remark, to observe" â€” one ~bemerkt~ something that catches one's attention. The derived noun ~Bemerkung~ ("remark, comment, observation") straddles the boundary between perception and speech: a ~Bemerkung~ can be something you merely notice or something you say aloud. The PIE root *~merاµ-~ ("boundary, edge") connects ~bemerken~ to an unexpectedly concrete origin: to notice something is to mark its boundary, to distinguish it from its surroundings. The same root gives English ~mark~, ~margin~, ~marquee~, and ~march~ (as in "the Welsh Marches" â€” border regions). Cognate with Dutch ~bemerken~, ~opmerken~.
en.bemأ¼hen:
From ~be-~ + ~Mأ¼he~ + ~-en~. Compound of ~be-~ + the denominal verb derived from ~Mأ¼he~:

- ~Mأ¼he~: From MHG ~mأ¼eje~, ~mأ¼e~, from OHG ~muohؤ«~, from the verb ~mأ¼hen~ ("to toil"), from Proto-West Germanic *~mإچjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mإچjanؤ…~ ("to tire"), from PIE *~mehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to cause toil, be a burden"). Cognate with Latin ~mإچlؤ“s~ ("burden, mass"), Greek ~mإچlos~ ("turmoil"), and Slavic *~majati~ ("to detain, tire"). Compare Dutch ~bemoeien~ ("to interfere, to meddle").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive and reflexive: to direct effort at something or someone.

~Bemأ¼hen~ means "to make an effort, to take trouble" (reflexive ~sich bemأ¼hen~) and, less commonly, "to trouble, to bother" (transitive: ~darf ich Sie bemأ¼hen?~, "may I trouble you?"). The polite imperative ~bemأ¼hen Sie sich nicht!~ ("don't trouble yourself!") is a standard courtesy formula. The derived noun ~Bemأ¼hung~ ("effort, endeavor") appears frequently in formal correspondence. Dutch ~bemoeien~ is a cognate but has shifted toward negative meddling ("to interfere"), while German ~bemأ¼hen~ retains the more neutral sense of earnest effort â€” the two words illustrate how the same compound can drift in opposite evaluative directions across closely related languages.
en.benennen:
From Middle High German ~benennen~, from Old High German *~binemnen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~nennen~:

- ~nennen~: From MHG ~nennen~, ~nemmen~, ~nemnen~, from OHG ~nemnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~namnijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~namnijanؤ…~ ("to name"), a denominative from *~namأ´~ ("name"), from PIE *~hâ‚پnأ³mnج¥~ ("name").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive with the sense of bestowing a name upon.

~Benennen~ means "to name, to designate, to label" â€” to bestow a name ~upon~ something or someone. Where ~nennen~ simply means "to call" or "to name," the prefix ~be-~ adds the sense of formal designation or bestowal. The PIE root *~hâ‚پnأ³mnج¥~ ("name") is one of the most stable words in all Indo-European languages, barely changing across thousands of years: Latin ~nإچmen~, Greek ~أ³noma~ (with metathesis), Sanskrit ~nؤپجپman~, Old Irish ~ainm~, and English ~name~ all descend from it. The Old Saxon cognate ~binemnian~ and Old English ~benemnan~ show the same ~be-~ + ~name~ compound across the West Germanic languages. The archaic English ~bename~ ("to name, to designate") is a direct cognate, though it fell out of use centuries ago. Cognate with Dutch ~benoemen~, Swedish ~benأ¤mna~, Old English ~benemnan~.
en.benutzen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~nutzen~:

- ~nutzen~: From MHG ~nutzen~, from OHG ~nuzzإچn~, a denominative verb from the noun ~Nutz~ ("use, benefit"), from Proto-West Germanic *~nuti~, from PGmc *~nutjؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ewd-~ ("to enjoy, benefit"). The double spelling ~nutzen~/~nأ¼tzen~ in modern German reflects a North-South dialect split, with ~nutzen~ (without umlaut) prevailing in the North and ~nأ¼tzen~ in the South.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The ~be-~ prefix adds a transitive, directed quality: while ~nutzen~ can be used intransitively ("es nutzt nichts" â€” "it is of no use"), ~benutzen~ is always transitive and implies actively putting something to use. Cognate with English ~need~ (via the same PGmc root), Dutch ~benutten~.
en.benأ¶tigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~nأ¶tigen~:

- ~nأ¶tigen~: From MHG ~nإچtigen~, from OHG ~nإچtigإچn~, a denominative verb ("to compel, necessitate") derived from the adjective ~nأ¶tig~ ("necessary"), itself from the noun ~Not~ ("distress, need, compulsion"), from OHG ~nإچt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~naudi~, from Proto-Germanic *~naudiz~, from PIE *~nehâ‚‚w-tأ­-s~ â€” a *~ti-~-stem related to the root of *~nawiz~ ("corpse"). The connection between "need" and "corpse" reflects a grim ancient logic: the PIE root evokes the state of the dead â€” inertness, absence of vital force â€” and the Germanic word shifted this toward the desperate need or compulsion that threatens to reduce the living to that state.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive or adjective-derived verb into a transitive one.

The ~be-~ prefix shifts ~nأ¶tigen~ (intransitive "to compel") toward the transitive "to need, require." The semantic distinction in German is subtle: ~brauchen~ and ~benأ¶tigen~ both mean "to need," but ~benأ¶tigen~ carries a more formal register. Cognate with Dutch ~benodigden~, English ~need~ (from the same PIE root via *~naudiz~).
en.beobachten:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Obacht~ + ~-en~:

- ~Obacht~ (root): From MHG ~ob-~ + ~Acht~. The element ~ob-~ is a preposition meaning "over, upon, above" (cognate with Latin ~ob-~). ~Acht~ here is from the noun of "attention, heed" (OHG ~ahta~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ahtu~) â€” not from ~acht~ ("eight"). The compound ~Obacht~ thus means "watching over, heedful attention from above" â€” a watchful, supervisory gaze. The related survival ~Obacht geben~ ("to pay attention") still appears in Southern German and Austrian speech.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The layering is vivid: ~beobachten~ = be- + ob- + Acht + -en = "to apply upward-directed attention to something," a verb built from three ancient strata of meaning. The English near-parallel is ~observe~ (Latin ~ob-~ + ~servؤپre~, "to watch over"), which uses the same Latin prefix ~ob-~ in the same spatial sense of oversight. Cognate with Dutch ~observeren~ (a later Latin borrowing for the same concept).
en.beraten: ~beraten~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~raten~ ("to advise, counsel; to guess"), from Middle High German ~rؤپten~, from Old High German ~rؤپtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rؤپdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ“danؤ…~, from PIE *~Hrehâ‚پdت°-~ ("to think, arrange"). The same Germanic root produced Old English ~rا£dan~, which became modern English ~read~ â€” a remarkable semantic journey from "to interpret, counsel" to "to interpret written text." The cognate English ~rede~ (archaic: "counsel, advice") preserves the older meaning. Beraten appears in Old High German as ~birؤپtan~ and in Middle High German as ~berؤپten~, with the obsolete English ~berede~ and ~beread~ as direct cognates. The noun ~Rat~ ("council, advice") and the city-council chamber ~Rathaus~ descend from the same root. ~Beraten~ adds the transitive force of ~be-~: one ~berأ¤t~ a person (gives them counsel), but also ~berأ¤t eine Frage~ (deliberates on a question).
en.berechnen:
Equivalent to ~be-~ + ~rechnen~:

- ~rechnen~: From MHG ~rechenen~, from OHG ~rehhanإچn~, related to Proto-Germanic *~rekإچnؤ…~ ("to count, to explain, to reckon").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive and adds the sense of applying calculation to something.

~Berechnen~ means "to calculate, to compute, to charge" â€” the ~be-~ prefix transforms ~rechnen~ ("to do arithmetic") into a transitive verb that applies calculation to a specific object: one ~berechnet~ a cost, a trajectory, a probability, or a fee. The additional sense "to charge" (~Wir berechnen Ihnen 50 Euro~, "We'll charge you 50 euros") shows the natural extension from counting to billing â€” what you calculate, you charge. The derived adjective ~berechnend~ ("calculating, scheming") carries a pejorative sense: a ~berechnender Mensch~ is someone who computes every social interaction for personal advantage. English ~reckon~ is a direct cognate of ~rechnen~, and both the German and English words retain the dual sense of mathematical computation and informal estimation (~I reckon~ â‰ˆ ~ich rechne damit~). Cognate with Dutch ~berekenen~.
en.berechtigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~recht~ + ~-igen~, a denominal derivation from the noun ~Recht~ ("right, law"):

- ~Recht~: From MHG ~rأ«ht~, from OHG ~rأ«ht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~reht~ ("right, straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rehtaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to straighten, to direct"). Cognate with English ~right~, Dutch ~recht~, Danish ~ret~, Icelandic ~rأ©ttur~, Latin ~rؤ“ctus~ ("straight"), and Sanskrit ~ل¹›jأ؛~ ("upright").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive: to confer a right upon someone.

The verb means "to entitle, to authorize" â€” literally "to make right for someone." The ~be-~ prefix provides the crucial transitive frame: ~recht~ alone is an adjective ("right, correct"), but ~berechtigen~ acts upon a person, conferring ~Recht~ upon them. The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to straighten, to make right") produced an astonishingly wide family: Latin ~rؤ“x~ ("king," originally "the one who directs"), ~rؤ“gula~ ("rule"), and ~regiإچ~ ("direction, region"); Sanskrit ~rؤپjan~ ("king"); Irish ~rأ­~ ("king"); and Germanic ~recht~/~right~. The Indo-European concept of justice was thus inseparable from straightness â€” what is ~recht~ is what is straight, not crooked.
en.bereichern:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~reicher~ + ~-n~, a deadjectival derivation from ~reich~ ("rich"):

- ~reich~: From MHG ~rؤ«ch~, ~rؤ«che~, from OHG ~rؤ«hhi~ ("noble, mighty, of high birth"), a ~ja~-stem adjective from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ«kijaz~, derived from the noun *~rؤ«k-~ ("ruler, prince"). This noun is widely regarded as an early Celtic loanword into Germanic, cognate with Latin ~rؤ“x~ ("king"), both from PIE *~hâ‚ƒrؤ“اµs~ ("king, ruler"). Cognate with Dutch ~rijk~, English ~rich~, Old Norse ~rأ­kr~, and Old English ~rؤ«ce~ (whence the ~-ric(k)~ in names like ~Fredrick~ and ~bishopric~).
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix creates a transitive verb meaning "to make rich."

The verb means "to enrich" â€” literally "to make rich upon." The reflexive ~sich bereichern~ ("to enrich oneself") often carries a negative connotation of illicit gain. The adjective ~reich~ preserves a remarkable layer of Celtic-Germanic contact: the Germanic peoples borrowed the word *~rؤ«k-~ from their Celtic neighbors, probably during the La Tene period (roughly 5th to 1st centuries BC). The original meaning was "ruler, chief," not "wealthy," and the semantic shift from "powerful" to "rich" happened gradually within Germanic. The same Celtic root gave English ~rich~ via Old French ~riche~, which itself was a Frankish (Germanic) re-borrowing â€” making ~rich~ a word that bounced from Celtic to Germanic to Romance and back to Germanic again.
en.bereiten:
From Middle High German ~bereiten~, from Old High German ~bireitan~. By surface analysis, ~bereit~ + ~-en~; historically a denominal verb from the Proto-Germanic adjective *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged"), with the prefix ~bi-~ (ancestral form of modern ~be-~). The adjective *~raidaz~ derives from PIE *~rؤ“y-~ ("to arrange, prepare"). The derived verb *~raidijanؤ…~ meant "to make ready, to equip."

The word ~bereiten~ is not to be confused with ~reiten~ ("to ride"), despite the similar appearance. While ~reiten~ descends from PGmc *~rؤ«danؤ…~ (PIE *~reydت°-~, "to ride"), ~bereiten~ descends from PGmc *~raidaz~ (PIE *~rؤ“y-~, "to arrange"). The two roots are entirely unrelated. Modern German preserves ~bereiten~ primarily in elevated or fixed expressions â€” ~Freude bereiten~ ("to give joy"), ~Schwierigkeiten bereiten~ ("to cause difficulties") â€” while the simplex ~reiten~ in the readying sense has vanished, surviving only in the adjective ~bereit~ ("ready") and the compound ~vorbereiten~ ("to prepare"). English ~ready~ is a direct cognate of ~bereit~, both descending from PGmc *~raidaz~: to be ready is etymologically to be "arranged." Cognate with Dutch ~bereiden~, English ~ready~.
en.bereitstellen:
Compound of ~bereit~ ("ready") + ~stellen~ ("to put, place"):

- ~bereit~: From MHG ~bereite~, from OHG ~bireiti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raidؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged, prepared"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚roy-dت°hâ‚پ-~ ("arranged"), from *~hâ‚‚rey-~ ("to count, order"). Cognate with English ~ready~.
- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").

The compound literally means "to place in a state of readiness" -- that is, "to provide, supply, make available." It is a separable-prefix verb (~er stellt bereit~) where the first element is not a traditional prefix but the adjective ~bereit~. The kinship between German ~bereit~ and English ~ready~ is not immediately obvious because the English word lost the ~be-~ prefix through phonological erosion, while German preserved it. Both ultimately trace back to a PIE root meaning "to put in order" -- readiness as the state of being arranged. In modern usage, ~bereitstellen~ is especially common in technical and administrative contexts: ~Mittel bereitstellen~ ("to provide funds"), ~Daten bereitstellen~ ("to make data available").
en.bergen:
From Middle High German ~bأ«rgen~, from Old High German ~bergan~ ("to hide, protect, salvage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~bergan~, from Proto-Germanic *~berganؤ…~ ("to hide, protect"), from PIE *~bت°ergت°-~ ("to take care of, protect, preserve"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with English ~bury~ (originally "to shelter, protect" â€” the modern sense of interring the dead is a semantic narrowing), Dutch ~bergen~, Old Norse ~bjarga~ ("to save, help"), Gothic ~bairgan~ ("to hide"), and Old Irish ~commairge~ ("protection").

The semantic range of ~bergen~ is remarkable: it means "to rescue" (from a shipwreck or avalanche), "to salvage" (a sunken ship), "to recover" (a body), and "to contain, hold" (~in sich bergen~, "to hold within itself"). The reflexive ~sich verbergen~ ("to hide oneself") preserves the original "hide" sense more transparently. The connection between ~bergen~ and English ~bury~ is a striking example of semantic divergence from a shared root: German kept the "rescue/protect" sense while English narrowed to "put underground." The PIE root *~bت°ergت°-~ also produced the Proto-Slavic *~borgرٹ~ that became Russian ~berech'~ ("to guard, protect"), showing remarkable semantic stability across the Indo-European family. The place name ~Salzburg~ ("salt fortress") uses the related noun ~Burg~ ("castle, fortress") â€” a fortified place where one is protected.
en.berichten:
From Middle High German ~berihten~ ("to put in order; to instruct, to lecture"), from Old High German ~birihten~ ("to make correct"), from Proto-West Germanic *~birinhtijan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to straighten, set straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijanؤ…~, from *~rehtaz~ ("straight"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreاµtأ³s~ ("right, straightened"), from *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to stretch out, straighten, direct"). Cognate with English ~right~, Dutch ~richten~, Latin ~regإچ~ ("I rule, direct").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The prefix ~be-~ shifted the sense of "to straighten" toward "to set right, put in order, instruct" â€” and from there, "to report" (as in giving a straight account of events). The sense of "to execute" (as in a judicial sentence, still heard in ~hinrichten~) also derives from "to put right according to the law." Cognate with Dutch ~berichten~, English ~beright~ (archaic).
en.berufen:
From Middle High German ~beruofen~, from Old High German ~biruofan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~, ~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrإچpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrإچpanؤ…~. The origin is uncertain; it may be related to *~hrإچأ¾iz~ ("glory, fame"), with the ~-p-~ arising from analogical association with the similar-sounding *~wإچpijanؤ…~ ("to cry out"). Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~.
- ~be-~: From OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). Makes the verb transitive and adds a sense of direction toward.

The ~be-~ prefix transforms "to call" into "to call upon, to appoint, to summon to an office." In modern German, ~berufen~ carries the sense of formal appointment or vocation: ~jemanden auf einen Lehrstuhl berufen~ ("to appoint someone to a professorial chair"). The related noun ~Beruf~ ("profession, vocation") is literally a "calling," a calque of Latin ~vocؤپtiإچ~ -- Martin Luther coined this usage to argue that every honest trade, not just the priesthood, constituted a divine calling. The adjective ~berufen~ can also mean "qualified" or "authorized," but superstitiously, ~Ich will es nicht berufen~ means "I don't want to jinx it" -- the idea being that calling attention to good fortune might summon bad luck. This superstitious sense preserves an ancient belief in the magical power of spoken words. The modern present tense notably lacks umlaut (~du berufst~, not *~du berأ¼fst~), because the historical umlaut on the rounded diphthong ~uo~ never established itself here, unlike in most other strong verbs.
en.beruhen:
~be-~ + ~ruhen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~ruhen~:

- ~ruhen~: From MHG ~ruo(we)n~, from OHG ~ruowؤ“n~, derived from the noun ~ruowa~ ("rest, peace"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rإچu~, from Proto-Germanic *~rإچإچ~ ("calm, rest"), from PIE *~hâ‚پrehâ‚پ-~ ("quiet, calm"). Cognate with the Sanskrit ~rؤپtrؤ«~ ("night").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, but here it shifts ~ruhen~ ("to rest") toward a more abstract, relational sense: "to rest upon" becomes "to be based on."

~Beruhen~ means "to be based on, to rest upon, to depend on" â€” exclusively in the figurative sense. One says ~die Anklage beruht auf Indizien~ ("the charge rests on circumstantial evidence") or uses the fixed phrase ~etwas auf sich beruhen lassen~ ("to let something rest, to leave a matter be"). Unlike ~ruhen~ itself, which retains strong physical connotations of sleeping, resting, and lying buried, ~beruhen~ exists entirely in the abstract domain. The ~be-~ prefix has lifted the verb off the bed, so to speak, and onto the plane of logic and causation.
en.beruhigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~ruhig~ + ~-en~:

- ~ruhig~: From MHG ~ruowec~ ("quiet"), from ~Ruhe~ ("rest, quiet, peace"). ~Ruhe~ descends from OHG ~ruowa~ ("peace, quiet, rest"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rإچu~ ("calm, rest"), from Proto-Germanic *~rإچإچ~, from PIE *~hâ‚پrehâ‚پ-~ ("quiet, calm"). Cognate with Sanskrit ~rؤپtri~ ("night"), whence Hindi ~rؤپt~ ("night").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the calming action at a specific person or situation.

~Beruhigen~ means "to calm, to reassure, to soothe" -- the ~be-~ prefix transforms the adjective ~ruhig~ ("calm, quiet") into a transitive verb. ~Beruhige dich!~ ("Calm down!") is one of the most frequently heard imperatives in German. The reflexive ~sich beruhigen~ ("to calm down") and the noun ~Beruhigung~ ("reassurance, calming") complete the word family. A ~Beruhigungsmittel~ ("sedative," literally "calming-agent") is the pharmaceutical extension. The connection between PIE *~hâ‚پrehâ‚پ-~ ("quiet") and Sanskrit ~rؤپtri~ ("night") is poetic: night as the time of quiet, of rest.
en.berأ¼cksichtigen:
A triple derivation chain: ~Rأ¼ck-~ ("back") + ~Sicht~ ("sight, view") â†’ ~Rأ¼cksicht~ ("consideration, regard") â†’ be- + ~rأ¼cksichtigen~:

- ~rأ¼cksichtigen~ (intermediate verb): A now-rare denominative verb "to have consideration for," formed from ~Rأ¼cksicht~ + the verbalizing suffix ~-igen~. In modern use it survives almost exclusively in the prefixed form ~berأ¼cksichtigen~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix transforms the denominative verb ~rأ¼cksichtigen~ into a fully transitive form meaning "to take into account, to consider."
- ~Rأ¼cksicht~ (root noun): An 18th-century calque of Latin ~respectus~ ("a looking back; regard, consideration"), from ~re-~ ("back") + ~spectus~ (from ~specere~, "to look"). German translates each Latin element precisely: ~Rأ¼ck-~ ("back, return") for ~re-~, and ~Sicht~ ("sight, view") for ~spectus~. ~Sicht~ derives from MHG ~siht~, from OHG ~siht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sihti~, formed from the root of ~sehen~ ("to see"), from PGmc *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with English ~sight~.

The full semantic chain runs: PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see") â†’ Germanic "sight" â†’ German "a looking back" â†’ "regard, respect" â†’ "to take into regard." The derivation mirrors Latin ~respectus~ precisely: ~Rأ¼cksicht nehmen~ corresponds to ~respectum habere~, and ~berأ¼cksichtigen~ provides a single-verb equivalent of the Latin compound.
en.berأ¼hren:
~be-~ + ~rأ¼hren~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~rأ¼hren~:

- ~rأ¼hren~: From MHG ~rأ¼eren~, from OHG ~ruoren~, ~hruoren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrإچzijanؤ…~ ("to stir"), a causative formation from *~hrإچzaz~ ("active, stirring, moving"), possibly from PIE *~kroHs-~ (a root connected to stirring and shock). Cognate with Dutch ~roeren~ ("to stir"), and the now-rare English ~rear~ ("to move, to stir").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix directs the action of stirring or touching toward a specific object, making the verb transitive: to touch something.

~Berأ¼hren~ means "to touch" in both the physical and emotional sense: ~berأ¼hr das nicht!~ ("don't touch that!") and ~ihre Geschichte hat mich tief berأ¼hrt~ ("her story deeply touched me"). In geometry, ~berأ¼hren~ means "to be tangent to" â€” a circle ~berأ¼hrt~ a line at exactly one point. The derived noun ~Berأ¼hrung~ ("touch, contact") appears in the elegant compound ~Berأ¼hrungsangst~ ("fear of contact, inhibition about engaging with something") â€” a word that captures reluctance to engage with unfamiliar ideas, people, or technologies. The underlying verb ~rأ¼hren~ itself means "to stir" (as in cooking: ~die Soأںe rأ¼hren~) and "to move emotionally" (~zu Trأ¤nen gerأ¼hrt~, "moved to tears"), preserving both the physical and affective senses from which ~berأ¼hren~ drew its dual nature. The military command ~rأ¼hrt euch!~ ("at ease!," literally "stir yourselves!") uses ~rأ¼hren~ in yet another sense â€” permission to move after standing at attention.
en.beschleunigen:
From the circumfix ~be-~ + ~-en~ around the adjective ~schleunig~ ("hasty, swift"):

- ~be-...-en~: The ~be-~ prefix (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") combined with the verbal ending ~-en~ creates a verb from an adjective, meaning roughly "to make [adjective]."
- ~schleunig~: From MHG ~sliunec~ ("hastily"), from OHG ~sliumo~, ~sniumo~ ("immediately," literally "moving quickly"), from or related to Proto-West Germanic *~sniumijan~ ("to hurry"), from Proto-Germanic *~sniumijanؤ…~ ("to expedite, hurry"). The ~-l-~/~-n-~ replacement appears to be assimilation influenced by the suffix ~-n-~. The Proto-Germanic form is related to *~snewanؤ…~ ("to rush"), from PIE *~snewH-~ ("to be fleet, flow, run").

The literal sense is "to make hasty" â€” to accelerate, to speed up. ~Beschleunigen~ is the standard German word for acceleration in both everyday and scientific contexts: ~Beschleunigung~ is the physics term for acceleration. The phonological history is remarkable: the original Proto-Germanic *~sn-~ cluster at the beginning of the word shifted to ~sl-~ through assimilation during the Old High German period, then the ~s~ was lost entirely in Standard German, leaving ~schleunig~ as a distant descendant of a root meaning "to rush." The related English word ~snell~ (dialectal: "quick, clever") preserves the original *~sn-~ cluster that German transformed beyond recognition.
en.beschlieأںen:
From Middle High German ~besliezen~, from Old High German ~bisliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisleutan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~schlieأںen~:

- ~schlieأںen~: From MHG ~slieب¥en~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slإ«tanؤ…~, *~sleutanؤ…~ ("to lock, shut"). A class II strong verb.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one or intensifies the action.

While ~abschlieأںen~ emphasizes physical closure or contractual completion, ~beschlieأںen~ has specialized almost entirely into the domain of deliberate decision-making: "to decide, resolve, pass (a resolution)." The underlying metaphor is the same â€” a bolt sliding home â€” but ~be-~ gives the action a transitive, encompassing quality: the will locks around its object. The Bundestag ~beschlieأںt~ laws; a committee ~beschlieأںt~ a motion. Cognate with Dutch ~besluiten~.
en.beschreiben:
From ~be-~ + ~schreiben~. Compound of:

- ~schreiben~: From MHG ~schrؤ«ben~, from OHG ~scrؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrؤ«banؤ…~, an early borrowing from Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~ ("I write"), from PIE *~(s)kreybت°-~ ("to scratch, cut, incise"). The word entered Germanic through contact with the Roman world along the Rhine frontier.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The ~be-~ prefix in ~beschreiben~ does double duty: it creates a transitive verb from the originally intransitive ~schreiben~ and adds the sense of covering or filling a surface (ein Blatt Papier ~beschreiben~ â€” to fill a sheet of paper with writing). The sense of "to describe" then develops naturally from the image of tracing something in writing. Cognate with Dutch ~beschrijven~.
en.beschrأ¤nken:
~be-~ + ~schrأ¤nken~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~schrأ¤nken~:

- ~schrأ¤nken~: From MHG ~schrenken~ ("to place crosswise, to obstruct"), from OHG ~screnken~ ("to spread out, to lay across, to trip someone up"), from Proto-Germanic *~skrankijanؤ…~, related to *~skrankaz~ ("barrier, grid"), probably from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to bend, turn").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes an intransitive verb transitive or intensifies the action.

~Beschrأ¤nken~ means "to limit, to restrict, to confine." The related noun ~Schranke~ ("barrier, gate, railing") preserves the physical origin: a ~Schranke~ is a crossbar or gate that blocks passage, from the MHG sense of "that which is placed crosswise." A ~Schrank~ ("cupboard, wardrobe") is an enclosed space â€” originally the railing or barrier around it, then the enclosed furniture itself. The verb ~beschrأ¤nken~ abstracts this physical barrier into any kind of limitation: one ~beschrأ¤nkt~ freedoms, budgets, or access. The reflexive ~sich beschrأ¤nken auf~ ("to limit oneself to, to confine oneself to") is particularly common. The derived adjective ~beschrأ¤nkt~ can mean either "limited" (neutral) or "narrow-minded" (pejorative) â€” a semantic split where physical confinement becomes intellectual confinement. Cognate with Dutch ~beperken~ (which uses a different root) and ~verschranken~ ("to cross, to interlock"). The related English word ~shrink~ may share the same PIE root *~(s)ker-~, though this connection is debated.
en.beschأ¤digen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~schأ¤digen~:

- ~schأ¤digen~: Derived from ~Schaden~ ("damage, harm"), from MHG ~schade~, from OHG ~scado~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaأ¾إچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaأ¾أ´~ ("damage, harm"). Cognate with English ~scathe~, Dutch ~schade~, Danish ~skade~, and Swedish ~skada~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, at, about"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). As an inseparable verbal prefix, it typically makes an intransitive verb transitive or intensifies the action.

The verb ~beschأ¤digen~ means "to damage, to cause harm to" â€” ~Das Auto wurde beschأ¤digt~ ("The car was damaged"). The ~be-~ prefix makes the verb fully transitive, focusing the action on a specific object. Without the prefix, ~schأ¤digen~ also means "to damage" but is more often used in abstract or legal contexts (~die Gesundheit schأ¤digen~ "to damage one's health"). The English cognate ~scathe~ survives mainly in the negative ~unscathed~ ("undamaged") and the derived adjective ~scathing~ ("harshly critical"). The Old Norse cognate ~skaأ°a~ ("to harm") gave English its borrowed word ~scathe~ via the Danelaw period. The Proto-Germanic root *~skaأ¾-~ may be connected to the idea of splitting or severing, making ~Schaden~ an ancient cousin of the concept behind English ~shatter~, though the precise PIE etymology remains debated among historical linguists.
en.beschأ¤ftigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + an adjectival root ~schأ¤ftig~, from Middle High German ~scheftec~ ("active, busy"), from ~Schaft~ ("shaft; handle; business"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~schأ¤ftigen~:

- ~schأ¤ftig~ (root): From MHG ~scheftec~, possibly influenced by Low German ~bescheftich~ ("bustling, industrious"). The underlying noun ~Schaft~ derives from MHG ~schaft~, from OHG ~skaft~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaft~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaftaz~, from PIE *~skehâ‚‚p-~ ("rod, shaft, staff; instrument for striking"). The original sense was a physical shaft or handle, but in OHG ~schaft~ already denoted "condition, state, business" (as in the suffix ~-schaft~, cognate with English ~-ship~ in ~friendship~, ~lordship~). This abstract sense â€” "being engaged in an activity" â€” gives ~schأ¤ftig~ its meaning.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, and is used to form denominative and deadjectival verbs.

The reflex ~-schaft~ in words like ~Wirtschaft~ ("economy"), ~Gesellschaft~ ("society"), and ~Wissenschaft~ ("science") is the same root. Cognate with Dutch ~bezighouden~ (the functional equivalent).
en.beseitigen:
Formed from ~be-~ + ~Seite~ + ~-igen~, literally "to put to the side, to set aside":

- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near").
- ~Seite~: From MHG ~sؤ«te~, from OHG ~sؤ«ta~, from Proto-Germanic *~sؤ«dا­~ ("side"), derived from *~sؤ«daz~ ("long"), with a sense development from "long (surface)" to "side." Cognate with English ~side~, Dutch ~zijde~, Swedish ~sida~.
- ~-igen~: A verb-forming suffix from adjectives and nouns.

The literal image -- moving something to the side -- has darkened considerably over time. While ~beseitigen~ can innocently mean "to remove, to dispose of" (obstacles, problems, waste), it also serves as a chilling euphemism for "to eliminate" in the sense of killing. This sinister secondary meaning makes ~beseitigen~ a prime example of how spatial metaphors can acquire lethal force.
en.besetzen:
From Middle High German ~besetzen~, from Old High German ~bisezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bisatjanؤ…~ ("to set near, set around"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~, from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~. This is the causative of *~sed-~ ("to sit") â€” literally "to cause to sit," hence "to set, to place." Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~, Yiddish ~×–×¢×¦×ں~ (~zetsn~).
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The range of meanings â€” "to occupy" (a seat, a territory), "to fill" (a position), "to cast" (a role), "to stud" (with jewels) â€” all extends from the core notion of placing things ~upon~ or ~around~ something. The political sense of ~besetzt~ ("occupied," as in military occupation) became tragically prominent in the 20th century. The telephone sense ~besetzt~ ("busy, engaged") â€” where all lines are occupied â€” is familiar to every German speaker. The causative chain from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit") through Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to cause to sit") to ~setzen~ ("to set, place") beautifully illustrates how Indo-European built transitive verbs from intransitive roots. Cognate with Dutch ~bezetten~, English ~beset~, Danish ~besأ¦tte~, Swedish ~besأ¤tta~.
en.besichtigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Sicht~ + ~-igen~ (a verbalizing suffix):

- ~Sicht~: From MHG ~siht~, from OHG ~siht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sihti~ ("sight, view"), from Proto-Germanic *~sihti-~, derived from *~sehwanؤ…~ ("to see"), from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, to notice"). Cognate with English ~sight~, Dutch ~zicht~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive â€” directing the action of seeing onto a specific object.

~Besichtigen~ means "to inspect, to view, to tour" â€” ~eine Wohnung besichtigen~ ("to view an apartment"), ~Sehenswأ¼rdigkeiten besichtigen~ ("to visit sights"), ~den Tatort besichtigen~ ("to inspect the crime scene"). The ~be-~ prefix with ~-igen~ creates a transitivized verb meaning "to apply sight to something thoroughly." The PIE root *~sekت·-~ ("to see, to notice") is one of the great sensory roots of Indo-European, producing Latin ~sequؤ«~ ("to follow" â€” originally "to see and track"), whence English ~sequence~, ~consequence~, and ~prosecute~. German preserves this root not only in ~sehen~ ("to see") but in ~Gesicht~ ("face" â€” what is seen), ~Ansicht~ ("view, opinion"), and ~Aussicht~ ("prospect, outlook").
en.besiegen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Sieg~ ("victory") + ~-en~:

- ~Sieg~: From MHG ~sige~, ~sic~, from OHG ~sigu~, from Proto-Germanic *~segiz-~, *~segu-~ ("victory"). Cognate with Old English ~sige~, ~sigor~ ("victory"), Old Norse ~sigr~, Gothic ~sigis~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the victory at a specific opponent.

~Besiegen~ means "to defeat, to conquer, to overcome" -- the ~be-~ prefix transforms the noun ~Sieg~ ("victory") into a transitive verb directed at an adversary. Without the prefix, the denominal verb ~siegen~ means simply "to win, to be victorious" (intransitive); the ~be-~ creates a grammatical object slot for the vanquished. The root ~Sieg~ is one of the most culturally significant nouns in Germanic: it appears in dozens of personal names -- ~Siegfried~ ("victory-peace"), ~Siegmund~ ("victory-protection"), ~Sigrid~ ("victory-beauty") -- reflecting the warrior culture that prized victory above all.
en.besingen:
From Middle High German ~besingen~, from Old High German ~bisingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisingwan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~singen~:

- ~singen~: From MHG ~singen~, from OHG ~singan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~singwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~singwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sengت·ت°-~ ("to sing, to recite, to utter"). Cognate with English ~sing~, Dutch ~zingen~, Old Norse ~syngva~, Gothic ~siggwan~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This inseparable prefix transforms the intransitive ~singen~ ("to sing") into the transitive ~besingen~ ("to sing about, to celebrate in song"), directing the act of singing onto a specific object.

Cognate with Dutch ~bezingen~ and, remarkably, English ~besing~. The English word is rare today but genuine â€” it appears in Old English as ~besingan~ ("to sing about, to enchant") and surfaces occasionally in literary and poetic contexts through the centuries. Shakespeare's contemporary Ben Jonson used it, and it appears in hymns and formal verse where ordinary ~sing~ would lack the transitive force needed to take a direct object: one sings, but one ~besings~ a hero or a victory.

The ~be-~ prefix follows the same transitivizing pattern across Germanic languages: just as ~klagen~ ("to lament") becomes ~beklagen~ ("to lament something"), ~singen~ becomes ~besingen~. German still coins new ~be-~ compounds freely, but English fossilized its stock centuries ago, leaving behind a sprawling graveyard of ~be-~ words that repays exploration.

English ~be-~ verbs fall into several families. The transitivizers work exactly like German ~be-~: ~bemoan~ (moan about something), ~bewail~ (wail about something), ~belabor~ (labor upon a point), ~befall~ (fall upon someone), and ~belie~ (lie against, contradict). The intensifiers mean "thoroughly" or "all over": ~bedazzle~, ~besmear~, ~bespatter~, ~bedeck~, ~bedevil~, ~befuddle~, ~bemuse~, ~beset~, ~besiege~, and ~besmirch~ (to smirch â€” already meaning "to soil" â€” with thoroughness). The denominals create verbs from nouns: ~bedew~ (cover with dew), ~befriend~ (make a friend of), ~bejewel~ (adorn with jewels), ~becloud~ (cover with cloud), ~bewitch~ (put a witch's spell on), and â€” most surprisingly â€” ~behead~, where ~be-~ means not "furnish with" but "deprive of," a rare privative sense paralleled by German ~berauben~ ("to rob someone of"). The deadjectivals make something into a quality: ~becalm~ (make calm), ~benumb~ (make numb), ~befoul~ (make foul), and ~belittle~ (make little), which Thomas Jefferson coined in 1780 â€” arguably the last time English productively used ~be-~ to create a word that stuck. The British literary establishment mocked ~belittle~ as an ugly Americanism; it outlasted all its critics. Finally, the most ancient ~be-~ words have become so opaque that speakers no longer parse the prefix at all: ~begin~ (the ~gin~ is from a lost verb meaning "to open"), ~become~, ~believe~ (the ~lieve~ is related to ~love~ and ~lief~), ~belong~ (~long~ here meant "to go along with"), ~beseech~ (from Old English ~besؤ“can~, "to seek out"), ~beware~, ~beguile~ (from Old French ~guile~, so a hybrid of Germanic prefix and Romance root), and even ~beyond~ (~be-~ + ~yond~). That English speakers say ~begin~, ~believe~, and ~beyond~ daily without any awareness of the ~be-~ inside them is perhaps the surest measure of how deeply this Proto-Germanic prefix shaped the language â€” and how completely it has receded from active use.

The PIE root *~sengت·ت°-~ is notable for its narrow semantic stability: unlike most PIE roots, whose meanings drifted dramatically across millennia, this one has meant "to sing" in virtually every daughter language for over five thousand years â€” Greek ~omphل¸—~ ("divine voice") being one of the few offshoots that ventured beyond the musical sense.
en.besitzen:
From Middle High German ~besitzen~, from Old High German ~bisizzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisittjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bisitjanؤ…~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~sitzen~:

- ~sitzen~: From MHG ~sitzen~, from OHG ~sizzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sittjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sitjanؤ…~, from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The three verbs ~sitzen~, ~setzen~, and ~besitzen~ illuminate a classical Germanic pattern: ~sitzen~ (intransitive, "to sit") â†’ ~setzen~ (causative, "to cause to sit, set") â†’ ~besitzen~ (~be-~ applied to ~sitzen~, "to sit upon, possess"). The ~be-~ prefix transforms the intransitive ~sitzen~ into a transitive verb of possession â€” one "sits upon" what one owns, reflecting the ancient connection between occupying land and owning it. (Compare English ~sit~ / ~set~ for the same intransitive/causative pair.) Cognate with Dutch ~bezitten~, Swedish ~besitta~, Danish ~besidde~, Gothic ~bisitan~.
en.besorgen:
From Middle High German ~besorgen~, from Old High German ~bisorgؤ“n~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~sorgen~:
