- ~sorgen~: From MHG ~sorgen~, from OHG ~sorgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgؤپnؤ…~, from the noun *~surgإچ~ ("worry, sorrow, care"), from earlier *~swurgإچ~, from PIE *~swergت°-~ ("to watch over, worry; be ill, suffer"). Cognate with English ~sorrow~, Lithuanian ~sirgti~ ("to be ill"), Old Irish ~serg~ ("sickness").
- ~be-~: From OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive and shifts the meaning from worrying in general to taking care of a specific matter.

The semantic development is fascinating: the PIE root meant "to worry" or "to be sick with concern," and the base verb ~sorgen~ still means "to worry" or "to take care." Adding ~be-~ shifted the meaning toward the practical result of that concern: "to procure, to obtain, to take care of (a task)." In colloquial German, ~besorgen~ can also mean "to get" something in a casual sense. The related noun ~Besorgung~ means "errand," showing how abstract worry crystallized into concrete action. English ~sorrow~ is a direct cognate of ~Sorge~ ("worry, concern"), though the English word has narrowed to the emotional sense while German preserved the broader caretaking meaning.
en.besprechen:
From Middle High German ~besprechen~, from Old High German ~besprechan~, ~besprehhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bisprekanؤ…~ ("to speak about, discuss, blame"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprأ«chen~, from OHG ~sprأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanؤ…~ ("to speak"), probably from PIE *~spregت°-~ ("to crackle"). Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix directs the speaking toward a topic â€” to speak about or over something.

~Besprechen~ means "to discuss, to talk over" and is the standard formal verb for holding a meeting or consultation: ~wir mأ¼ssen das besprechen~ ("we need to discuss this"). The derived noun ~Besprechung~ ("meeting, discussion, review") is a fixture of German office life. But ~besprechen~ has an older, more magical sense as well: in folk practice, ~eine Krankheit besprechen~ meant "to charm away an illness by speaking incantations over it" â€” the ~be-~ prefix here means "to speak upon" quite literally, directing words at the afflicted body to heal it. This incantatory meaning survives in dialects and in the compound ~Besprechung~ in its folk-medicine sense. Cognate with English ~bespeak~ (now archaic, "to speak for, to engage in advance") and Dutch ~bespreken~ ("to discuss; to reserve").
en.bestehen:
From Middle High German ~bestؤپn~, ~bestؤ“n~, from Old High German ~bistؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~bistؤپnؤ…~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From MHG ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from OHG ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The prefix ~be-~ shifts the intransitive ~stehen~ ("to stand") into a transitive verb with several distinct senses: "to pass" (an exam), "to consist of," "to insist on," and "to exist, endure." Cognate with Dutch ~bestaan~.
en.bestellen:
From Middle High German ~bestellen~, from Old High German ~bistellen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb â€” the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The OHG and MHG compound meant primarily "to surround, to occupy a place, to arrange" â€” the ~be-~ prefix giving ~stellen~ the spatial sense of placing things around or setting them in order. The modern sense of "to order (goods)" developed from the intermediate meaning "to arrange, to commission": you place an order for something by directing a craftsman or merchant to set it up for you. The same compound also yields the legal term ~Bestellung~ ("appointment to office"), which preserves the older sense of "placement into a role." Cognate with Dutch ~bestellen~.
en.bestimmen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~stimmen~:

- ~stimmen~: From MHG ~stimmen~, from OHG *~stimmen~, a denominative from ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). ~Stimme~ derives from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnإچ~, from PIE *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The root verb ~stimmen~ bears three distinct but historically connected senses: "to tune" (an instrument), "to vote," and "to be correct" (~das stimmt~). All three revolve around the idea of bringing something into alignment with a standard â€” voices tuned together, voices cast collectively, a fact that matches reality. When ~be-~ is added, the verb becomes fully transitive and the sense sharpens: "to determine" means to fix or settle something authoritatively, as if tuning it to a definitive pitch. The abstract noun ~Bestimmung~ carries the full weight of this â€” "determination," "destiny," "definition" â€” suggesting that to determine something is to give it its voice, its fixed note in the larger order. Cognate with Dutch ~bestemmen~, English ~stem~ (in the sense of to derive from a source, sharing the PIE root).
en.bestreiten:
From Middle High German ~bestrؤ«ten~ ("to fight against"), from Old High German ~bistrؤ«tan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~streiten~:

- ~streiten~: From MHG ~strؤ«ten~, from OHG ~strؤ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strؤ«dan~. Cognate with Low German ~striden~, Dutch ~strijden~ ("to fight, to struggle"), and English ~stride~.
- ~be-~: From OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, shifting from fighting in general to fighting against something specific.

The modern meanings of ~bestreiten~ have diverged strikingly from the original martial sense. Today it primarily means either "to dispute, to contest, to deny" (e.g., ~eine Behauptung bestreiten~, "to contest a claim") or "to finance, to bear the cost of" (e.g., ~die Kosten bestreiten~, "to cover the expenses"). The financial sense developed from the metaphor of "being able to cope with" or "to fight through" an obligation. The English cognate ~bestride~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic root *~bistrؤ«dan~) took yet another path, meaning "to straddle" or "to stand over." Dutch ~bestrijden~ ("to combat, to fight against") preserves the original combative sense most faithfully. The divergence of meaning among these cognates -- German "to deny / to finance," English "to straddle," Dutch "to combat" -- is a textbook example of semantic drift across sibling languages.
en.bestأ¤tigen:
A denominal verb built on a three-layer derivation chain: the PIE root *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand") gives Old High German ~stؤپn~ ("to stand"), which yields the deadjectival form ~stet~ (OHG ~stؤپti~, "steady, firm, constant"), which in turn generates the MHG adjective ~stأ¦tec~ ("firm, constant, stable") â€” the ancestor of modern ~stetig~. The verb ~bestأ¤tigen~ is then formed by adding the transitivizing prefix ~be-~ to a deadjectival stem *~stأ¤tig-en~ derived from this adjective:

- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive or adjectival-derived verb into a transitive one.
- *~stأ¤tig-~ (root): From MHG ~stأ¦tec~, from OHG ~stؤپti~ ("firm, constant"), ultimately from the root of ~stehen~ ("to stand"), from Proto-Germanic *~stؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand").

~bestأ¤tigen~ therefore means, at its etymological core, "to make something stand firm" â€” to render a fact, a claim, or an appointment as steady and settled. The word's journey from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ through OHG and MHG to the modern verb passes through three derivational layers that English rarely matches: a strong verb becomes a deadjectival form, which becomes an adjective, which becomes a denominal causative. The result is that ~bestأ¤tigen~ ("to confirm") and ~stehen~ ("to stand") are cognates â€” separated by two millennia and three steps of derivation, but sharing the same ancestral idea: the thing that is confirmed is the thing that has been made to stand. Cognate with Dutch ~bevestigen~ (which follows a parallel derivational path from ~vast~ "firm").
en.besuchen:
From Middle High German ~besuochen~ ("to seek, use, visit, investigate"), from Old High German ~bisuohhen~ ("to examine, check, try, inquire"), from Proto-West Germanic *~bisإچkijan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~suchen~:

- ~suchen~: From MHG ~suochen~, ~sأ¼echen~, from OHG ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sإچkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sإچkijanؤ…~, from PIE *~sehâ‚‚g-~ ("to seek out").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The semantic narrowing from "to seek, investigate, try" (Old High German) to "to visit" (Modern German) reflects a shift from the general act of seeking to its most socially common purpose: seeking out another person at their location. English ~beseech~ â€” "to beg, implore" â€” is a cognate that preserved a different strand of the same compound's original range of "seeking earnestly." The English false friend ~visit~ takes a wholly different route, from Latin ~vؤ«sitؤپre~ ("to go to see"), from ~vؤ«dؤ“re~ ("to see"). Cognate with Dutch ~bezoeken~, Swedish ~besأ¶ka~, English ~beseech~.
en.beteiligen:
Denominative verb derived from ~Teil~ ("part, share"). Formed in early modern German with the prefix ~be-~, the adjectival suffix ~-ig~, and the verbal ending ~-en~: ~be-~ + ~teil~ + ~-ig~ + ~-en~, meaning literally "to make someone a part of something."

- ~Teil~: From MHG ~teil~, from OHG ~teil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daili~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailiz~, tentatively from Proto-Indo-European *~dت°(hâ‚پ)-oy-lo-~ ("part, watershed"), itself possibly from *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, do"), though the morphological derivation is uncertain. Cognate with English ~deal~ (as in "a great deal"), Dutch ~deel~, Swedish ~del~.
- ~be-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The inseparable prefix ~be-~ makes an intransitive verb transitive or a denominal verb causative.

The noun ~Beteiligung~ ("participation, stake, share") follows naturally from the verb. The same PIE root underlies the English word ~deal~ â€” a semantic cousin preserved in phrases like "deal out" and "new deal," where division and distribution remain central.
en.beten:
From Middle High German ~beten~, from Old High German ~beton~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bedon~, from Proto-Germanic *~bedan~ ("to pray, to ask"), closely related to *~bidjan~ ("to ask, to request"), from PIE *~gwhedh-~ ("to ask for, to beseech"). Cognate with English ~bid~ (via Old English ~biddan~) and Dutch ~bidden~ ("to pray"), Danish ~bede~ ("to ask, to pray").

The semantic narrowing from "to ask, to request" to specifically "to pray" is a Christianization phenomenon: when Germanic peoples adopted Christianity, their word for "requesting" was pressed into service for the specific act of requesting from God. English ~bid~ took the opposite path, retaining the secular sense and losing the religious one. The related noun ~Gebet~ ("prayer"), from OHG ~gibet~, uses the ~ge-~ prefix in its collective/completive sense. The further related noun ~Bitte~ ("request, plea") was reshaped by analogy with ~bitten~ ("to ask"), showing how the ~beten~/~bitten~ pair has remained intertwined throughout their history. The verb is attested in Old High German since the 8th century, making it one of the earliest documented German words associated with Christian practice.
en.betonen: ~betonen~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Ton~ ("tone, stress, accent") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~. ~Ton~ in this sense comes from Middle High German ~tإچn~, borrowed from Latin ~tonus~, itself from Ancient Greek ~د„دŒخ½خ؟د‚~ (~tأ³nos~), from Proto-Hellenic *~tأ³nos~, from PIE *~ten-~ ("to stretch"). The Greek word referred to the stretching of a string â€” pitch and tension were the same concept for the ancient world. German has two homophones spelled ~Ton~: one meaning "clay" (from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾anhا­~, PIE *~tenk-~, "to thicken") and one meaning "tone, sound" â€” the one that gives ~betonen~. The prefix ~be-~ here marks the action of placing stress upon a syllable or emphasizing a point, turning the noun for sound into a verb for linguistic and rhetorical accent.
en.betrachten:
From Middle High German ~betrahten~, from Old High German ~bitrahtإچn~, ~bitrahten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~trachten~:

- ~trachten~: From MHG ~trahten~, from OHG ~trahtإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trahtإچn~, a borrowing from Latin ~tractؤپre~ ("to drag, handle, manage, treat"), the frequentative of ~trahإچ~ ("I drag, pull"). In OHG, ~trahtإچn~ shifted from the Latin physical sense of handling toward mental handling â€” pondering, considering, striving toward.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, and adds a sense of directed, focused attention.

A remarkable Latin-to-German journey: Latin ~tractؤپre~ (physical: "to drag repeatedly, handle") enters OHG as "to consider, strive for" (~trachten~), and with ~be-~ becomes "to look at, contemplate" (~betrachten~). The verb is a doublet of ~traktieren~ (modern German "to mistreat"), which preserves more of the original Latin physical sense. The shared root ~trahإچ~ is also the ancestor of English ~tract~, ~tractor~, and ~attract~. Cognate with Dutch ~betrachten~.
en.betragen:
Inherited from Middle High German ~betragen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from PGmc *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). Class VI strong verb. Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Betragen~ carries two distinct modern senses: "to amount to" (as in "die Summe betrأ¤gt 100 Euro") and "to behave" (as in "sich gut betragen"). Both trace back to "carrying": a sum "carries itself" to an amount, and a person "carries themselves" in a certain manner. The reflexive noun ~das Betragen~ ("conduct, behavior") preserves the older sense of personal carriage. The same metaphor of conduct as self-carriage appears in English ~comportment~ (Latin ~comportؤپre~, "to carry together") and ~bearing~. Cognate with Dutch ~bedragen~.
en.betreffen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~treffen~:

- ~treffen~: From MHG ~treffen~, from OHG ~treffan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drepan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drepanؤ…~ ("to strike, hit").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The prefix ~be-~ here adds a sense of direction and full engagement: where ~treffen~ is hitting or meeting in general, ~betreffen~ is hitting upon something specifically â€” "to concern, affect, pertain to." The common phrase ~was mich betrifft~ ("as far as I'm concerned," literally "what hits/touches me") preserves this tactile metaphor vividly. The related adjective ~betroffen~ ("affected; shocked, taken aback") illustrates how being "struck" by something can convey both relevance and emotional impact. Cognate with Dutch ~betreffen~.
en.betreiben:
A calque of Middle Low German ~bedrؤ«ven~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~treiben~:

- ~treiben~: From MHG ~trؤ«ben~, from OHG ~trؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drؤ«ban~, from PGmc *~drؤ«banؤ…~, from PIE *~dت°reybت°-~ ("to drive, push"). Class I strong verb. The English cognate is ~drive~; Dutch ~drijven~, Danish ~drive~, and Swedish ~driva~ are all close relatives.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Betreiben~ extends the forceful root sense into abstract domains: "to operate" (a business or machine â€” you ~drive~ it into action) and "to pursue" (an activity â€” you ~drive~ at it persistently). The cognate English ~drive~ has undergone exactly the same extension from physical propulsion to abstract pursuit. ~Betreiben~ is also a legal term: ~auf Betreiben von~ means "at the instigation of" â€” literally "through the driving of." Cognate with Dutch ~bedrijven~.
en.betreten:
From Middle High German ~betreten~, from Old High German ~bitretan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bitredan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trأ«ten~, from OHG ~trأ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudanؤ…~ ("to tread, step on"). Cognate with English ~tread~. The West Germanic descendants regularized the original ablaut by replacing ~u~ with ~e~ in the present stem.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the intransitive ~treten~ ("to step") into the transitive ~betreten~ ("to step onto, to enter").

The adjective ~betreten~ ("embarrassed") is identical in form to the past participle but developed an independent semantic life. The connection may lie in the image of someone caught in the act of entering a place they should not be -- frozen mid-step, literally "be-trodden."
en.betreuen:
From Middle High German ~betriuwen~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~treu~ + ~-en~:

- ~treu~: From MHG ~triuwe~, from OHG ~triuwi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~triuwؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~triwwiz~ ("faithful, loyal"). Cognate with English ~true~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive â€” directing faithfulness onto someone as an act of care.

~Betreuen~ means "to care for, to look after, to supervise" â€” ~ein Kind betreuen~ ("to look after a child"), ~Kunden betreuen~ ("to attend to clients"). The word is literally "to be true to" or "to be faithful toward" someone, making the act of caregiving an expression of loyalty. The Proto-Germanic root *~triwwiz~ descends from PIE *~deru-~ ("firm, solid," originally referring to wood or trees), which also gives us English ~true~, ~trust~, ~tree~, and ~truce~ â€” all united by the concept of firmness and reliability. The noun ~Betreuer~ / ~Betreuerin~ ("caregiver, supervisor") is ubiquitous in modern German, from childcare (~Kinderbetreuung~) to academic advising (~Doktorarbeit-Betreuer~). The idea that care is an act of faithfulness rather than mere duty gives ~betreuen~ a warmth absent from its English equivalents.
en.beurteilen:
From early 16th century. Compound of ~be-~ + ~urteilen~:

- ~urteilen~: From MHG ~urteilen~, a denominative from ~Urteil~ + ~-en~. ~Urteil~ descends from MHG ~urteil~, from OHG ~urteil~, ~urteili~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzdailijؤ…~ ("judicial decision, verdict"), literally "an out-dealing." The noun is composed of ~ur-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~, "out") + ~Teil~ ("part, share"), from MHG ~teil~, from OHG ~teil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daili~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailiz~, from PIE *~dehâ‚‚i-~ ("to divide"). Cognate with Dutch ~oordeel~, English ~ordeal~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, or intensifies a transitive verb by directing focus onto the object.

While ~urteilen~ means "to judge, pass judgment" in a general or legal sense, ~beurteilen~ means "to assess, evaluate, appraise" â€” the ~be-~ prefix shifts the focus from the act of judging to the object being judged. One ~beurteilt~ a student's work, a situation, or a wine. The relationship between ~Urteil~ and English ~ordeal~ is a striking case of semantic divergence: the Germanic root meant a formal judicial decision, but in English the word narrowed to mean a trial by fire or water â€” and eventually any severe test or painful experience â€” while German kept the original legal meaning intact.
en.bevorzugen:
A modern denominative verb built from ~be-~ + ~Vorzug~ + ~-en~:

- ~Vorzug~: A nominal formation from ~vorziehen~ ("to prefer, to pull forward"), itself from ~vor-~ ("before, forward," from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~, "before, in front") + ~ziehen~ ("to pull," from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~, "to pull, lead"). ~Vorzug~ literally means "the pulling forward," hence "preference, advantage."
- ~be-~: From Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). Makes the noun into a transitive verb.

The word's structure is a textbook example of German morphological productivity: a PIE root meaning "to pull" became ~ziehen~, which was prefixed to create ~vorziehen~ ("to pull forward, to prefer"), which was nominalized to ~Vorzug~ ("preference"), which was then re-verbalized with ~be-~ and ~-en~ to create ~bevorzugen~ ("to prefer, to favor"). Each step added a layer of abstraction. English ~prefer~ follows a strikingly parallel metaphor from Latin ~praeferre~ ("to carry before, to put forward"), showing that both Germanic and Latin speakers independently conceived of preference as "putting something in front." The fact that ~bevorzugen~ is derived from a noun (~Vorzug~) rather than directly from the verb ~vorziehen~ explains why it conjugates weakly (regularly), despite ~ziehen~ being a strong verb.
en.bewahren:
From Middle High German ~bewarn~, from Old High German ~biwarإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~biwarإچnؤ…~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~wahren~:

- ~wahren~: From MHG ~warn~, ~waren~, from OHG ~warإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~warإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~warإچnؤ…~, from *~waraz~ ("cautious, attentive"), from PIE *~wer-~ ("to become aware of, heed"). Related to ~wahr~ ("true"), from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ“raz~, from PIE *~wehâ‚پros~ ("true"); cognate with Latin ~vؤ“rus~ ("true"), Old Irish ~fأ­or~ ("true").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). Here the prefix intensifies the protective sense of the base verb.

~bewahren~ means "to preserve, protect, safeguard, keep" â€” a strengthened form of ~wahren~ ("to maintain, guard"). The phrase ~Gott bewahre!~ ("God forbid!," literally "God preserve!") is a common exclamation. Cognate with English ~beware~, which preserves the same ~be-~ + "be watchful" compound but shifted to mean "be on guard against" rather than "protect." The semantic field connecting ~wahren~ ("to guard"), ~wahr~ ("true"), and ~wahrnehmen~ ("to perceive") reflects an ancient cluster of ideas around watchfulness, awareness, and truth â€” what is carefully observed is what is real. The PIE root *~wer-~ ("to heed") and *~wehâ‚پ-~ ("true") may ultimately be related, though the precise connection is debated.
en.bewegen:
~Bewegen~ is two historically distinct verbs that have merged into a single modern form, distinguished only by their past tense:

Etymology 1 â€” weak (~bewegte/bewegt~, "to move physically"): From MHG ~bewegen~, from OHG ~biwegan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biwegan~, from PGmc *~weganؤ…~ ("to move, carry"), from PIE *~weاµت°-~ ("to convey, transport"). The same PIE root gives German ~Wagen~ ("wagon, vehicle"), English ~way~ and ~weigh~, and Latin ~vehere~ ("to convey") â€” the source of English ~vehicle~.

- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix adds transitivizing force: where *~weganؤ…~ was intransitive ("to move"), ~bewegen~ directs that motion onto an object.

Etymology 2 â€” strong (~bewog/bewogen~, "to motivate, induce"): From MHG ~bewegen~, from OHG ~biweggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biwaggjan~, an iterative-intensive causative of the same PGmc *~weganؤ…~ + causative suffix *~-janؤ…~. This yielded a separate sense: "to set into motion mentally â€” to move someone to act."

The merger of these two verbs creates a productive ambiguity in Modern High German: "Was hat dich bewogen?" uses the strong past tense and means "What motivated you?" â€” not "What moved you physically?" The two etyma share PIE *~weاµت°-~, connecting ~bewegen~ to English ~vehicle~, Latin ~via~ ("road"), and Greek ~أ³khos~ ("wagon"). Cognate with Dutch ~bewegen~.
en.beweisen: From ~be-~ + ~weisen~ ("to show, point, direct"). The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") transforms the act of showing into a transitive act of demonstrating or proving. ~Weisen~ derives from Old High German ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~wؤ«saz~ ("wise, knowing"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, know") â€” the same root that yields Latin ~videإچ~ ("I see"), Sanskrit ~veda~ ("I know"), and English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~vision~. ~Beweisen~ thus literally means "to show thoroughly" â€” to make something seen beyond doubt. The related noun ~Beweis~ ("proof, evidence") preserves the same ancient connection between seeing and knowing. Cognate with Dutch ~bewijzen~.
en.bewerben:
From Middle High German ~bewerben~, from Old High German ~biwerban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bihwerban~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~werben~:

- ~werben~: From MHG ~wأ«rben~, from OHG ~wأ«rban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hwerban~, from Proto-Germanic *~hwerbanؤ…~ ("to turn, to move about"), possibly from PIE *~kت·erp-~ ("to turn").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive and reflexive.

~Bewerben~ (reflexive: ~sich bewerben~) means "to apply (for a job, a position, a contract)." The root ~werben~ originally meant "to turn, to move about" in Proto-Germanic, and its semantic evolution is fascinating: from physical turning to bustling activity, then to soliciting and recruiting (still visible in ~Werbung~, "advertising"), and finally to courting or wooing. The prefix ~be-~ makes this reflexive: ~sich bewerben~ is to turn oneself ~toward~ something, to present oneself as a candidate. The noun ~Bewerbung~ ("application") and ~Bewerber~ ("applicant") are standard vocabulary of professional life. The Proto-Germanic root *~hwerbanؤ…~ also gave Dutch ~werven~ ("to recruit"), Icelandic ~hverfa~ ("to turn, to disappear"), and the archaic English ~wharve~ ("to turn"). The loss of the initial ~hw-~ in German (via OHG ~wأ«rban~) obscures the connection to English ~whirl~ and ~warp~, which may be distant relatives through the same PIE turning root. Cognate with Dutch ~werven~, Faroese ~hvأ¸rva~.
en.bewerten: ~bewerten~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Wert~ ("value, worth") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to evaluate, assess, rate." ~Wert~ comes from Middle High German ~wert~, from Old High German ~werd~, from Proto-Germanic *~werأ¾az~, ultimately from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn") â€” the underlying sense being that of exchange, something turned over in trade. The same PIE root gives English ~worth~ and ~weird~ (originally "fate, that which turns"). The regular sound change from Proto-Germanic ~-أ¾-~ to German ~-t-~ in the uninflected form was then generalized across the paradigm; the older ~-d-~ is preserved in related words ~Wأ¼rde~ ("dignity") and ~wأ¼rdig~ ("worthy"). A cognate of particular interest: English ~worth~ and German ~Wert~ are doublets, and the sentence "What is it worth?" maps exactly onto "Was ist es wert?" â€” same structure, same root.
en.bewirken:
From Middle High German ~bewirken~, ~bewأ¼rken~, from Old High German ~biwurken~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~wirken~:

- ~wirken~: From MHG ~wirken~, from OHG ~wirken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wirkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wirkijanؤ…~ ("to make, produce, effect"), from *~werkؤ…~ ("work"), from PIE *~werاµ-~ ("to work"). Cognate with English ~work~, Dutch ~werken~, Greek ~أ©rgon~ ("work"), and the organ name in ~Chirurg~ (from Greek ~kheirourgأ³s~, "hand-worker"). An Upper German variant *~wurkijanؤ…~ (OHG ~wurken~, MHG ~wأ¼rken~) coexisted for centuries; the Central German ~i~-form prevailed in the written language by the 18th century.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix directs the working toward a result, making the verb mean "to work upon" in the sense of causing.

~Bewirken~ means "to bring about, to cause, to effect" â€” a formal verb of causation. Where ~wirken~ alone means "to work, to have an effect, to seem," ~bewirken~ focuses on the result: ~die Medizin hat eine Verbesserung bewirkt~ ("the medicine brought about an improvement"). It occupies a register above ~verursachen~ ("to cause") and well above ~machen~ ("to make, to do"). The PIE root *~werاµ-~ is one of the most productive in the Indo-European family: it gives Greek ~أ©rgon~ ("work," whence ~energy~, ~allergy~, ~liturgy~), Latin ~urgؤ“re~ ("to press"), and Old English ~weorc~ (modern ~work~). The connection between German ~wirken~/~bewirken~ and English ~work~ is therefore genuine cognacy, not mere resemblance.
en.bewundern:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~wundern~, from ~Wunder~ ("wonder, miracle"):

- ~Wunder~: From MHG ~wunder~, from OHG ~wuntar~, from Proto-Germanic *~wundrؤ…~, from PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, desire, strive for, love"). Cognate with English ~wonder~, Dutch ~wonder~, Danish ~under~.
- ~be-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). Here it functions as a transitivizer, making the reflexive ~sich wundern~ ("to wonder at") into the transitive ~bewundern~ ("to admire").

~Bewundern~ means "to admire," while the simpler ~sich wundern~ means "to be surprised, to wonder." The prefix ~be-~ transforms passive astonishment into active admiration. The PIE root *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to desire, strive for") reveals that ~wonder~ was originally an expression of longing or desire: one wondered at what one wished for. The same root produced Latin ~Venus~ (goddess of desire), ~venؤپrؤ«~ ("to hunt," originally "to pursue with desire," whence English ~venison~), and Old English ~wynn~ ("joy, delight"). The German compound ~Wunderkind~ ("child prodigy," literally "wonder child") entered English unchanged.
en.bewأ¤hren:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~wأ¤hren~, a deadjectival derivation from ~wahr~ ("true"):

- ~wahr~: From MHG ~wؤپr~, ~wا£re~, from OHG ~wؤپr~, ~wؤپri~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤپr~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ“raz~ ("true, reliable"), from PIE *~wehâ‚پros~ ("true"), from *~wehâ‚پ-~ ("to be true"). Cognate with English archaic ~ware~ ("aware"), Dutch ~waar~ ("true"), Latin ~vؤ“rus~ ("true"), and Irish ~fأ­or~ ("true").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix adds transitivity and intensification.

The reflexive ~sich bewأ¤hren~ means "to prove oneself, to stand the test" â€” literally "to make oneself true." The adjective ~wahr~ descends from a PIE root *~wehâ‚پ-~ whose reflexes encode the concept of truth across the Indo-European world: Latin ~vؤ“rus~ ("true") gave English ~very~, ~verify~, and ~verdict~ ("a true saying"); Old Irish ~fأ­or~ means "true"; and Proto-Slavic *~vؤ›ra~ means "faith, belief" (whence Russian ~ذ²ذµر€ذ°~). The related German verb ~wahren~ means "to preserve, to maintain," while ~wahrnehmen~ means "to perceive" â€” suggesting that truth, preservation, and perception were linked in the Germanic mind. English ~aware~ is a distant relative through the same Proto-Germanic adjective.
en.bewأ¤ltigen:
From Middle High German ~bewأ¤ltigen~, derived from ~be-~ + ~waltig~ + ~-en~, in which ~waltig~ is an obsolete variant of ~gewaltig~ ("powerful, mighty"). Compound of ~be-~ + the obsolete adjective ~waltig~:

- ~waltig~: From MHG ~waltic~, related to ~walten~ ("to rule, govern"), from OHG ~waltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~waldan~ ("to reign"), from Proto-Germanic *~waldanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚welhâ‚پ-~ ("to rule, be strong"). The noun ~Gewalt~ ("power, force") shares this root, from OHG ~giwalt~, Proto-Germanic *~gawaldؤ…~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive: "to overpower" something.

The semantic development from "to exercise power over" to "to master, cope with, overcome" reflects the journey from raw physical dominance to intellectual and emotional mastery. Cognate with English ~wield~, which underwent a similar semantic broadening from ruling to skillful handling. The root also appears in Icelandic ~valda~ ("to cause"), Polish ~wladac~ ("to rule"), and Russian ~vladet'~ ("to own, command"), revealing a pan-Indo-European concept of power. The related noun ~Bewأ¤ltigung~ plays a prominent role in modern German discourse, notably in the compound ~Vergangenheitsbewأ¤ltigung~ ("coming to terms with the past"), a word so culturally specific that it is often borrowed untranslated into English.
en.bezahlen:
From Middle High German ~bezaln~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~zahlen~:

- ~zahlen~: From MHG ~zaln~, ~zalen~, from OHG ~zalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talإچn~, from PGmc *~talإچnؤ…~ ("to tell, recount; to count, reckon"). Cognate with English ~tale~ and ~tell~ (in the counting sense: "all told" means "all counted"), Dutch ~betalen~, Swedish ~tala~ ("to speak"), Danish ~tal~ ("number").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix adds a transitive, directed quality â€” while ~zahlen~ can be used intransitively ("ich zahle" â€” "I pay"), ~bezahlen~ more explicitly takes a direct object (the thing or person paid).

The conceptual bridge from "counting" to "paying" runs through the ancient practice of counting out coins aloud when settling a debt. English preserves the counting sense in ~teller~ (one who tells/counts â€” as in a bank teller). German ~Zahl~ ("number") and ~zahlen~ ("to pay") descend from the same root, making every act of paying in German etymologically an act of counting. Cognate with Dutch ~betalen~, Danish ~betale~.
en.bezeichnen:
From Middle High German ~bezeichenen~, from Old High German ~bizeihhanen~, ~bizeihnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bitaiknijan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, mark"), from MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taiknؤ…~ ("sign"), from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to point out, show"). A denominative verb: "to make a sign, to mark."
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The PIE root *~deyل¸±-~ is remarkably productive across languages: it yields ~Zeichen~ ("sign"), ~zeigen~ ("to show"), ~zeihen~ ("to accuse"), Latin ~dؤ«cإچ~ ("I say, point out"), Greek ~deiknumi~ ("I show"), and English ~teach~ (from OE ~tا£can~, from the same root). The ~be-~ prefix shifts the basic meaning of "to make a mark" toward "to designate, label, characterize." Cognate with Dutch ~betekenen~ ("to mean, signify"), English ~betoken~.
en.beziehen:
From Middle High German ~beziehen~, from Old High German ~biziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biteuhan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from PGmc *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Class II strong verb. Cognate with Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead") â€” the source of English ~duke~ and ~duct~ â€” and with English ~tow~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Beziehen~ has accumulated a striking range of meanings, all traceable to the root idea of "pulling toward or over": to move into (a flat â€” you "draw" yourself into it), to subscribe to (a journal â€” you "draw" it to yourself), to source (from a supplier â€” you "draw" goods from them), to cover (a bed or cushion â€” you "draw" fabric over it), and to refer (to something â€” you "draw" a connection). The reflexive ~sich beziehen auf~ ("to refer to") was probably influenced by Latin ~referre~ ("to carry back, refer"). The derived noun ~Beziehung~ ("relationship, connection") shows the same root abstraction â€” a relationship is a mutual drawing-toward. Cognate with Dutch ~betrekken~.
en.biegen:
From Middle High German ~biegen~, from Old High German ~biogan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beugan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beuganؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ewgت°-~ ("to bend"). Cognate with Sanskrit ~bhujأ،ti~ ("to bend, curve").

~Biegen~ is a class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~biegen~, ~bog~, ~gebogen~. It means "to bend, to curve, to turn (a corner)." The idiom ~auf Biegen und Brechen~ ("by hook or by crook," literally "by bending and breaking") pairs the verb with its semantic opposite, suggesting that one will either bend something into shape or break it trying. The PIE root *~bت°ewgت°-~ specialized in Germanic for physical bending, while in English the cognate ~bow~ (as in bowing one's head) preserved a more social and gestural sense. The English noun ~bow~ (of a ship) and the word ~elbow~ (literally "arm-bend," from OE ~elnboga~) are also related. Dutch ~buigen~, Danish ~bأ¸je~, and Swedish ~bأ¶ja~ are immediate Germanic siblings.
en.bieten: From Middle High German ~bieten~, from Old High German ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ewdت°-~ ("to be awake, aware"). A class II strong verb whose PIE root conveys notions of awareness and offering. Cognate with Low German ~bieden~, Dutch ~bieden~, English ~bid~, Danish ~byde~, Swedish ~bjuda~.
en.bilden: From Middle High German ~bilden~, ~bileden~, a merger of two Old High German verbs: ~biliden~ ("to form into an image"), from Proto-West Germanic *~biliأ¾ijan~, and ~bilidإچn~ ("to copy, represent"), from Proto-West Germanic *~biliأ¾إچn~. Both derive from the Proto-West Germanic noun *~biliأ¾ؤ«~ (whence German ~Bild~, "image, picture"). ~Bild~ itself traces from OHG ~bilidi~, ~biladi~, attested since the 8th century, first in the sense "model, exemplar" and later "likeness, copy"; its further pre-Germanic origin is obscure â€” German Wiktionary notes only a Germanic stem *~bil-~ meaning "form," with no secure PIE etymology established. The semantic history of ~bilden~ â€” from "to give something an image or form" through "to educate, cultivate" â€” reflects the deep German cultural connection between ~Bild~ ("image") and ~Bildung~ ("education, formation of character"), the latter concept central to German Idealist philosophy. ~Bildung~ is not merely instruction but the shaping of the whole person as if forming a work of art. Cognate with Dutch ~beelden~, Swedish ~bilda~.
en.binden:
From Middle High German ~binden~, from Old High German ~bintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bindan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bindanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°endت°-~ ("to bind, tie"). A class III strong verb with the characteristic ~i~â€“~a~â€“~u~ ablaut pattern (~binden~, ~band~, ~gebunden~). Cognate with Low German ~binden~, Dutch ~binden~, English ~bind~, Danish ~binde~, Gothic ~bindan~.

The PIE root *~bت°endت°-~ has left an extraordinarily wide trail across the Indo-European family. Sanskrit ~bandhati~ ("to bind, to tie") is a direct cognate â€” and the English loanword ~bandana~ descends from a Hindi derivative of the same Sanskrit root, meaning "a tied thing." The Germanic noun ~Band~ ("ribbon, bond, volume"), English ~band~ and ~bond~, and even ~bundle~ all spring from this same ancient root. To bind a book (~ein Buch binden~) is thus one of the oldest metaphors still alive in everyday German â€” the same PIE root that described tying physical bonds in the Bronze Age steppe now describes the spine of a paperback.
en.bitten:
From Middle High German ~bitten~, from Old High German ~bitten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biddjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bidjanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gت·ت°edت°-~ ("to ask for, beseech"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~bidden~, Dutch ~bidden~ ("to pray"), English ~bid~ (in the sense "to ask, invite"), Danish ~bede~.

The PIE root *~gت·ت°edت°-~ carries the sense of earnest supplication â€” the same root that underlies Old Norse ~biأ°ja~, Gothic ~bidjan~, and Sanskrit ~bؤپdh-~ (to press, urge). English ~bid~ is a direct cognate in one of its etymological strands; the modern English word has been complicated by conflation with a second, unrelated Old English verb, ~bؤ“odan~ ("to offer, announce"), from PIE *~bت°ewdت°-~, which contributed the commercial sense "to bid at auction." German ~bitten~ remained uncontaminated by that conflation and preserves the pure sense of asking or requesting. The related noun ~Bitte~ ("request, please") and the polite particle ~bitte~ â€” the most pervasive politeness word in the German language â€” descend directly from the same root. Dutch ~bidden~ drifted toward specifically religious supplication, a semantic narrowing that German ~bitten~ avoided.
en.bleiben: From Middle High German ~blؤ«ben~, ~belؤ«ben~ ("to remain in the same position, pause"), from Old High German ~bilؤ«ban~ ("to remain"), from Proto-Germanic *~bilؤ«banؤ…~ ("to remain"), a compound of *~bi-~ + *~lؤ«banؤ…~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to stick, adhere"). The semantic development from "to stick" to "to remain" reflects how physical adhesion became a metaphor for staying in place. Cognate with Dutch ~blijven~, English ~belive~ ("to remain, stay").
en.blicken:
From MHG ~blicken~, from OHG ~blicchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~blikk-~, from Proto-Germanic *~blikؤ…~ / *~blikkaz~ ("shine, appearance, look"), from PIE *~bت°leyاµ-~ ("to shine").

The root noun ~Blick~ ("glance, look") is cognate with Dutch ~blik~ ("look, glance; tin"), Danish ~blik~ ("look, glance; tin"), Icelandic ~blik~ ("gleam, shine"), and Old English ~blice~ ("an exposure, manifestation"). The PIE root *~bت°leyاµ-~ also underlies ~bleichen~ ("to bleach"), English ~bleach~, and ~blank~ â€” all carrying the idea of a bright, shining surface. A ~Blick~ is thus a flash of light turned into a directed gaze, and ~blicken~ retains that original sense of a sudden, luminous perception. In modern colloquial German, ~blicken~ has also developed the meaning "to get it, to understand" (~Du blickst es einfach nicht~ â€” "You just don't get it") â€” a semantic extension from visual to mental comprehension.
en.blockieren:
Borrowed in the 17th century from French ~bloquer~ ("to enclose, to block"), from Old French ~bloquer~, derived from ~bloc~ ("block, log"), from Middle Dutch ~blok~ ("tree trunk"), from Proto-West Germanic *~blokk~, from Proto-Germanic *~blukkؤ…~ ("beam, log"), from PIE *~bت°elاµ-~ ("thick plank, beam, prop").

~Blockieren~ is a Germanic word that traveled to France and came back. Proto-Germanic *~blukkؤ…~ ("beam, log") was borrowed into Old French as ~bloc~, which developed the verb ~bloquer~ ("to obstruct with a log"), which German then re-borrowed as ~blockieren~ in the 17th century. This round trip â€” Germanic to French to Germanic â€” is a common pattern for words that gained new semantic layers during their French sojourn. The original concrete image of blocking a road with a log has generalized to any form of obstruction: ~eine Straأںe blockieren~ ("to block a road"), ~Verhandlungen blockieren~ ("to block negotiations"), ~das Rad blockiert~ ("the wheel locks up").
en.brauchen: From Middle High German ~brإ«chen~, from Old High German ~brإ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brإ«kan~ ("to use, employ, consume"), from Proto-Germanic *~brإ«kanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ruHg-~ ("to make use of"). The semantic shift from "to use" (still preserved in the noun ~Brauch~, "custom, usage") to "to need" is a modern development; the older sense persists in formal and legal registers. Cognate with Dutch ~bruiken~, English ~brook~ ("to tolerate, bear"), Latin ~fruor~ ("to enjoy").
en.brechen: ~brechen~ comes from Middle High German ~brأ«chen~, from Old High German ~brأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~brekanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bت°reg-~ ("to break"). This is the same word as English ~break~: the two are direct cognates, separated only by the High German consonant shift (Proto-Germanic *b remained *b in English but the vowel and consonant system diverged thereafter). The same PIE root underlies Latin ~frangere~ ("to break"), whose past participle ~fractus~ gives English ~fracture~, ~fragment~, and ~fraction~. Cognate with Dutch ~breken~, Old Saxon ~brekan~, and Danish ~brأ¦kke~. ~Brechen~ covers a remarkable semantic range in modern German: to break physically, to vomit (~brechen~ colloquially), to violate a promise (~ein Versprechen brechen~), and to refract light (~Licht brechen~) â€” all held together by the PIE root's core sense of sudden separation.
en.brennen:
From Middle High German ~brennen~, a merger of two Old High German verbs: ~brennan~ ("to set on fire," transitive) from Proto-West Germanic *~brannijan~, and ~brinnan~ ("to be on fire," intransitive) from Proto-West Germanic *~brinnan~. The transitive form *~brannijan~ was the causative of *~brinnan~, formed with Proto-Germanic *~-janؤ…~. The intransitive *~brinnanؤ…~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~bت°rewhâ‚پ-~ ("to boil, brew"), making ~brennen~ an unexpected cousin of English ~brew~ and ~broth~. Cognate with English ~burn~, Dutch ~branden~, Swedish ~brinna~, Icelandic ~brenna~.

The merger of two originally distinct verbs into a single paradigm is relatively rare in German. In modern usage, ~brennen~ handles both the transitive sense ("to burn something") and the intransitive ("to be on fire"), a job that required two separate verbs in Old High German. The noun ~Brand~ ("fire, blaze") preserves the ancient strong-verb ablaut grade.
en.bringen: From Middle High German ~bringen~, from Old High German ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, probably from a pre-Germanic *~bت°renk-~, a compound root from PIE *~bت°er-~ ("to carry") + *~hâ‚پnek-~ ("to bring") or *~hâ‚‚nek-~ ("to reach, attain"). This verb exhibits a mixed conjugation in Germanic: its past tense ~brachte~ combines a strong-verb vowel change (~i~ > ~a~, from the o-grade *~bت°ronk-~) with a weak-verb dental suffix (~-te~). It shares this pattern with ~denken~ (~dachte~), though some linguists distinguish ~bringen~'s vowel change as a true ablaut grade shift rather than the umlaut removal seen in ~denken~. Cognate with Low German ~brengen~, Dutch ~brengen~, Afrikaans ~bring~, English ~bring~, West Frisian ~bringe~, Gothic ~briggan~.
en.buchen: ~Buchen~ ("to book, reserve") in its commercial sense is a denominal verb from ~Buch~ ("book"), modelled after English ~to book~. ~Buch~ itself descends from Middle High German ~buoch~, Old High German ~buoh~, Proto-West Germanic *~bإچk~, and Proto-Germanic *~bإچks~, cognate with English ~book~ and â€” more surprisingly â€” English ~beech~, from the same root *~bإچks~. Early Germanic writing was scratched onto beech-wood tablets, and the tree name became the word for a written document. There is also a separate, older German verb ~buchen~ meaning "of or relating to the beech tree" (an adjective), from ~Buche~ ("beech"). The commercial sense â€” booking a hotel, a flight â€” is thus a modern reborrowing of a concept that traveled from Germanic beech forests to Old English ~bإچc~ to modern English ~to book~ and back into German. ~Buchen~ joins English and German in a circular etymological journey spanning fifteen centuries.
en.danken:
From Middle High German ~danken~, from Old High German ~dankإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankإچnؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from *~أ¾ankaz~ ("thought, gratitude"), from Proto-Indo-European *~teng-~ ("to perceive, think, feel"). Cognate with Dutch ~danken~, English ~thank~, Danish ~takke~.

The PIE root *~teng-~ is the common ancestor of both German ~danken~ ("to thank") and ~denken~ ("to think"), and likewise of English ~thank~ and ~think~. These are not coincidentally similar words â€” they descend from the same ancient root. To thank someone originally meant to hold them in thought, to bear them in mind. The Proto-Germanic noun *~أ¾ankaz~ carried both meanings: "thought, remembrance" and "gratitude." English ~thank~ and ~think~ (from Old English ~أ¾anc~ and ~أ¾encan~) are doublets from this same source. The phrase ~vielen Dank~ ("many thanks") thus preserves, in every syllable, a word that once meant simply "a thought."
en.darlegen:
Compound of ~dar-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, the causative of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~, the causative form of *~legت°-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Danish ~lأ¦gge~.
- ~dar-~: From OHG ~dؤپr~ ("there, thither"), from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ؤپr~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ar~ ("there"). Related to ~da~ ("there") and the English prefix ~there-~ (as in ~therefore~). The prefix conveys presenting something for inspection, placing it "there" before the audience.

The compound means "to set forth, to explain, to present" â€” to "lay there" an argument or facts for examination. The prefix ~dar-~ is an archaic adverbial form of ~da~ ("there") that survives almost exclusively in a few fixed compounds: ~darlegen~, ~darstellen~ ("to present, to portray"), ~darbieten~ ("to offer"). The root ~legen~ stands in a classic causative relationship with ~liegen~ ("to lie"): ~legen~ means "to cause to lie, to lay," and the pair descends from a PIE construction where adding *~-أ©ye-~ to a verb created its causative. English preserves the same pair: ~lay~ (causative) versus ~lie~ (base).
en.darstellen:
From ~dar-~ + ~stellen~. Compound of:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb meaning "to place, set" â€” the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand").
- ~dar-~: From Old High German ~dara~ ("thither, to that place"), itself from an extended form of OHG ~thؤپr~/~dؤپr~ ("there"), from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ؤپr~. The directional -~r-~ suffix marked motion toward a place, distinguishing ~dar~ ("thither") from the static ~da~ ("there"). In Modern German, ~dar-~ is largely fossilized, surviving chiefly in compound verbs like ~darstellen~, ~darlegen~, and ~darbringen~; as a productive prefix before vowels it persists as a variant of ~da-~.

The compound ~darstellen~ â€” literally "to set forth, place there" â€” shifted from its concrete spatial meaning to the abstract senses of "to represent, depict, portray," much as English ~present~ shifted from "to place before" to "to introduce, perform." Cognate with Dutch ~voorstellen~ (same semantic development, different prefix).
en.dauern:
~dauern~ carries two entirely separate senses with distinct etymological origins that have been grafted together under one spelling.

~dauern~ "to last, endure": From Middle High German ~dإ«ren~, borrowed from Middle Low German ~dإ«ren~, itself from Latin ~dإ«rإچ~ ("to last, endure, harden"), a denominative verb from ~dإ«rus~ ("hard, lasting"). Latin ~dإ«rus~ derives from Proto-Italic *~dإ«ros~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dwehâ‚‚-~ ("long, far") â€” the same root underlying Sanskrit ~dإ«ra-~ ("far, distant") and Ancient Greek ~dؤ“rأ³s~ ("long"). The path into German ran through Low German contact in the 12th century, gradually displacing the older native form ~weren~ (a forerunner of modern ~wأ¤hren~). Cognate with Dutch ~duren~, English ~dure~.

~dauern~ "to cause pity, to feel sorry for" (as in ~er dauert mich~, "I feel sorry for him"): From Middle High German ~tإ«ren~, itself an ablaut variant of ~tiure~ ("dear, precious, costly"), the Middle High German ancestor of modern ~teuer~. The original sense was "to be dear, to be costly (to someone)" â€” hence "to be felt as a loss, to arouse pity." The initial ~d-~ in the modern form results from Low German influence on the High German form. This sense is thus a doublet of ~teuer~ by a different phonological route.
en.deaktivieren:
Compound of ~de-~ + ~aktivieren~:

- ~aktivieren~: From ~aktiv~ + ~-ieren~. ~Aktiv~ is borrowed from Latin ~ؤپctؤ«vus~ ("active, practical"), from ~ؤپctus~, past participle of ~agere~ ("to do, to drive"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ ("to drive"). The suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard German adaptation of Latin/French ~-ؤپre~/~-er~ for creating verbs from borrowed adjectives.
- ~de-~: A Latin-derived prefix meaning "un-, de-," borrowed into German from Latin ~dؤ“~ ("from, down, away"), from PIE *~de-~ ("from"). It reverses the action of the base verb.

The verb means "to deactivate, to disable." It is a fully Latinate creation on German soil â€” every morpheme traces back to Latin: ~dؤ“~ ("away") + ~ؤپctؤ«vus~ ("active") + ~-ؤپre~ (verbal suffix). The PIE root *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ ("to drive") produced an enormous family across Indo-European: Latin ~agere~ ("to do") yielded English ~act~, ~agent~, ~agenda~, ~agitate~; Greek ~أ،gein~ ("to lead") yielded ~pedagogue~ and ~demagogue~; and Sanskrit ~أ،jati~ ("to drive") continued the original sense. German borrowed ~aktivieren~ relatively late, as part of the 18th-century wave of Latinate technical vocabulary. Its antonym ~deaktivieren~ followed naturally.
en.decken: From Middle High German ~decken~, from Old High German ~decchen~, ~thecken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾akkjan~ ("to cover"), from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾akjanؤ…~ ("to cover, to roof"), a causative formation from *~أ¾akؤ…~ ("roof, thatch"), from PIE *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover"). The verb is thus etymologically "to roof" â€” to place a covering over something, as one covers a house with thatch. The PIE root *~(s)teg-~ is extraordinarily productive: it gave Latin ~tegere~ ("to cover"), whence English ~protect~, ~detect~, ~integument~; Latin ~toga~ (a garment that covers); Latin ~tؤ“gula~ ("roof tile"), whence English ~tile~ and German ~Ziegel~; and Greek ~stegos~ ("roof"), whence ~stegosaurus~ (the "roofed lizard," named for its bony back plates). Within Germanic, the same root produced English ~thatch~, Dutch ~dekken~, Swedish ~tأ¤cka~, and Danish ~tأ¦kke~. The semantic range of modern ~decken~ extends far beyond roofing: one ~deckt~ a table (sets it, literally "covers" it with cloth and dishes), ~deckt~ expenses (covers them financially), ~deckt~ an opponent in sports (covers or marks them), and ~deckt~ a mare (covers her â€” the equestrian breeding term). The noun ~Decke~ ("blanket, ceiling, cover") and ~Dach~ ("roof") are siblings from the same root.
en.definieren: ~Definieren~ ("to define") is borrowed directly from Latin ~dؤ“fؤ«nؤ«re~ ("to set a boundary, mark out limits, define"), from ~dؤ“-~ (an intensifying prefix, here meaning "completely, fully") + ~fؤ«nؤ«re~ ("to limit, bound, end"), from ~fؤ«nis~ ("boundary, end, goal"). The etymology of Latin ~fؤ«nis~ itself is disputed: it may derive from Proto-Indo-European *~dت°eygت·-~ ("to stick, fix"), which also produced Latin ~fؤ«gere~ ("to fasten") â€” a boundary marker being literally something stuck in the ground. Alternatively it may trace to *~bت°eyd-~ ("to split"), a boundary being a place of division. English ~fine~ (as in "fine arts," meaning "finished, complete"), ~final~, ~finish~, ~finance~, and ~confine~ all share this Latin ancestor. When one ~definiert~ a concept, one is etymologically "drawing its boundaries completely" â€” marking where it ends and something else begins.
en.demonstrieren:
From Latin ~dؤ“mإچnstrؤپre~ ("to point out, show, demonstrate"), a compound of ~dؤ“-~ ("from, down") + ~mإچnstrؤپre~ ("to show"), itself from ~mإچnstrum~ ("divine omen, portent"), from ~monؤ“re~ ("to warn, advise"), from PIE *~mon-eye-~ (causative of *~men-~ "to think"). Borrowed into German in the early sixteenth century with the meaning "to show, to prove." The protest sense ("to take part in a demonstration") developed after 1850 under influence from the derived noun ~Demonstration~.

The Latin etymon ~mإچnstrum~ originally meant a divine omen or warning sign -- something shown by the gods as a portent. It is the same word that gave English ~monster~ (a prodigious or unnatural creature seen as an omen). So ~demonstrieren~ and ~Monster~ are, etymologically, siblings: both descend from the act of divine showing. The PIE root *~men-~ ("to think") is extraordinarily productive: it yielded Latin ~mens~ ("mind"), Greek ~menos~ ("spirit"), Sanskrit ~manas~ ("mind"), English ~mind~, ~mental~, ~monitor~, ~monument~, and ~money~ (from ~Monؤ“ta~, an epithet of Juno, in whose temple coins were minted).
en.denken: From Middle High German ~denken~, from Old High German ~denken~, ~thenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankijanؤ…~, from PIE *~teng-~ ("to think, feel"). A mixed-conjugation verb: its past tense ~dachte~ combines a vowel change (~e~ > ~a~, from the removal of the nasal-conditioned umlaut) with a weak dental suffix (~-te~). It shares this pattern with ~bringen~ (~brachte~), though some linguists distinguish ~bringen~'s vowel change as a true ablaut grade shift rather than the umlaut removal seen in ~denken~. The same PIE root yields ~Dank~ ("thanks") and ~Gedanke~ ("thought"), reflecting the deep semantic connection between thinking and thanking in the Germanic languages. Cognate with Bavarian ~denkn~, Dutch ~denken~, Low German ~denken~, West Frisian ~tinke~, English ~think~, Icelandic ~أ¾ekkja~.
en.deuten:
From Middle High German ~diuten~, from Old High German ~diuten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾iudijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾iudijanؤ…~ ("to associate with; to interpret; to point out"), derived from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾eudإچ~ ("people"), from PIE *~tewtأ©hâ‚‚~ ("people"). Cognate with Dutch ~duiden~ ("to indicate, to interpret"), Icelandic ~أ¾أ½أ°a~ ("to translate"), Swedish ~tyda~ ("to interpret, to decipher"), Danish ~tyde~ ("to interpret").

The semantic path from "people" to "interpret" is one of the most fascinating in Germanic etymology. The original meaning was "to make intelligible to the people" â€” to translate from learned or sacred language into the common tongue. This is precisely the same word that gives German ~deutsch~ ("German"), literally "of the people," via Proto-Germanic *~أ¾iudiskaz~ ("pertaining to the people"), which distinguished the vernacular Germanic languages from Latin. To ~deuten~ was, in a sense, to speak ~deutsch~ â€” to render comprehensible in the people's language. The modern meanings â€” "to interpret" (dreams, omens, texts: ~Trأ¤ume deuten~) and "to point" (~auf etwas deuten~, "to point at something") â€” both descend from this original act of making meaning accessible. The derived noun ~Bedeutung~ ("meaning, significance") and the adjective ~deutlich~ ("clear, distinct") preserve the interpretive core. That ~deutsch~ and ~deuten~ share a root is not mere folk etymology but genuine linguistic kinship: both spring from the Proto-Germanic concept of "the people" and their language.
en.dienen: From Middle High German ~dienen~, from Old High German ~dionإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ewanإچnؤ…~ ("to serve"), a denominative verb derived from *~أ¾ewaz~ ("servant, slave"). The etymology of *~أ¾ewaz~ is uncertain; one hypothesis derives it from PIE *~tekت·-~ ("to run"), with "servant" as "one who runs (errands)"; another connects it to *~tew(hâ‚‚)-~ ("to swell, grow"), though neither etymology is secure. The same Proto-Germanic root *~أ¾ewaz~ underlies Old English ~أ¾egn~ ("thane, servant"), which by a remarkable social elevation came to mean "warrior, nobleman" and eventually became the Scottish title ~thane~ â€” immortalized in Shakespeare's ~Macbeth~. Cognate with Dutch ~dienen~, English ~thane~ (via the related noun), Danish ~tjene~.
en.diskutieren: Borrowed into German from French ~discuter~, from Latin ~discutiإچ~ ("to shake apart, scatter, examine, discuss"), a compound of ~dis-~ ("apart") + ~quatiإچ~ ("to shake"). Latin ~quatiإچ~ derives from Proto-Italic *~kت·atjإچ~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)kwot-i-~ ("to shake"). The original sense of ~discutiإچ~ was entirely physical â€” "to smash to pieces, disperse" â€” and only later shifted to the figurative sense of "to examine thoroughly, to sift through arguments." This semantic journey from "shaking apart" to intellectual analysis is a vivid metaphor: a discussion, etymologically, is a shaking-apart of ideas. German received ~diskutieren~ via French contact, using the standard ~-ieren~ suffix that marks French and Latin loanverbs and always produces weak verbs. Cognate with French ~discuter~, English ~discuss~, Spanish ~discutir~, Italian ~discutere~.
en.dokumentieren: A denominative verb derived from the noun ~Dokument~ ("document"), with the productive ~-ieren~ suffix. ~Dokument~ was borrowed from Latin ~documentum~ ("lesson, proof, example"), from the verb ~doceإچ~ ("to teach, to show"), from PIE *~doل¸±-أ©ye-ti~, the causative of *~deل¸±-~ ("to accept, to receive, to perceive"). The semantic shift from "a lesson" to "a written proof" happened in Medieval Latin, where ~documentum~ came to mean a charter or official record â€” something that ~teaches~ you the truth by ~showing~ evidence. The same PIE root *~deل¸±-~ produced Latin ~decؤ“ns~ ("fitting, proper") â€” whence English ~decent~ â€” and ~doctor~ (originally "a teacher"), as well as Greek ~dأ³ksa~ ("opinion, glory") â€” whence English ~doxology~ and ~orthodox~. German ~dokumentieren~ thus etymologically means "to teach through evidence." Cognate with Dutch ~documenteren~, English ~document~, French ~documenter~.
en.dominieren:
Borrowed in the 14th century from Latin ~dominؤپrؤ«~ ("to rule, to dominate"), from ~dominus~ ("lord, master"), from ~domus~ ("house, household"), from PIE *~dأ³m-~ ("house"), from the root *~dem-~ ("to build, to put together"). Cognate with English ~dominate~, French ~dominer~, Italian ~dominare~.

~Dominieren~ means "to dominate, to prevail, to be predominant." As a Latin borrowing, it stands alongside native Germanic synonyms like ~beherrschen~ ("to rule over") and ~vorherrschen~ ("to predominate"), but ~dominieren~ carries a specifically forceful connotation â€” asserting supremacy rather than merely holding authority. The PIE root *~dem-~ ("to build") reveals a domestic origin for the concept of dominion: the ~dominus~ was literally the master of the ~domus~, the household. This root produced an extraordinary range of descendants: Latin ~domesticus~ (whence English ~domestic~), ~domicilium~ (whence ~domicile~), Greek ~dأ©mos~ ("the people," originally "district" â€” whence ~democracy~), Russian ~dom~ ("house"), and English ~timber~ (from Proto-Germanic *~timrؤ…~, "building material"). The journey from "house" to "lord" to "to dominate" traces the expansion of authority from a single household to an empire.
en.drehen:
From Middle High German ~drأ¦jen~, ~drأ¦hen~, from Old High German ~drؤپen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rؤپan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾rؤ“anؤ…~ ("to twist, turn"), from Proto-Indo-European *~terhâ‚پ-~ ("to rub, rub by twisting, twist, turn"). Cognate with Bavarian ~drahn~, Dutch ~draaien~, English ~throw~.

The cognate with English ~throw~ is not a false friend but a true one â€” it is simply that ~throw~ has shifted its meaning dramatically since the Old English period. Old English ~أ¾rؤپwan~ meant "to twist, turn," not "to hurl," and the act of hurling was expressed by the now-archaic ~weorpan~ (cognate with German ~werfen~). At some point in the Middle English period, ~throw~ displaced ~weorp-~ as the word for tossing and was itself displaced by ~warp~ as the word for twisting â€” a near-complete swap of semantic roles. The shared ancestry with ~drehen~ is visible in Dutch ~draaien~, Low German ~draien~. The PIE root *~terhâ‚پ-~ also underlies Latin ~terere~ ("to rub, wear away"), English ~thread~ (something twisted), and English ~throw~ (in the potters' sense: "to throw a pot" still means "to shape clay on a turning wheel").
en.dringen:
From Middle High German ~dringen~, from Old High German ~dringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ringwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾rinhwanؤ…~, from PIE *~trenkت·-~ ("to beat, hew, press"). Akin to ~drأ¤ngen~ ("to push, urge") and ~Drang~ ("urge, drive"). Cognate with dialectal English ~thring~ ("to press, to crowd") and, more distantly, with English ~throng~.

~Dringen~ is a class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~dringen~, ~drang~, ~gedrungen~. It means "to press through, to penetrate, to insist urgently." The related causative ~drأ¤ngen~ ("to urge, to push") has the expected umlaut marking of Germanic causative formation: the base vowel ~a~ from the past tense grade becomes ~أ¤~ in the derived verb. The noun ~Drang~ ("drive, urge, impulse") is famous from the literary movement ~Sturm und Drang~ ("Storm and Drive"), the late 18th-century revolt against rationalist constraints that launched German Romanticism. English ~throng~ (a pressing crowd) preserves the original sense of people pressing together. The initial Proto-Germanic *~أ¾r-~ cluster regularly became ~dr-~ in Old High German through the consonant shift.
en.drohen:
From Middle High German ~drإچn~, ~drإچwen~, from Old High German ~drouwen~, ~drإچwen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rauwijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾raujanؤ…~ ("to threaten"). The archaic variant ~drأ¤uen~ preserves the older vowel pattern more faithfully. Cognate with Old English ~أ¾rؤ“agan~ ("to rebuke, threaten"), Old Saxon ~thrإچian~, Middle Dutch ~drouwen~.

The shift from ~drouwen~ to ~drohen~ involved a simplification of the diphthong ~ou~ to long ~إچ~ and loss of the ~w~, a common phonological development in Middle High German. The older form ~drأ¤uen~ (with umlaut from the ~j~ in *~أ¾rauwijan~) survives in literary German and Luther's Bible translation but is otherwise archaic. The derived noun ~Drohung~ ("threat") and the compound ~bedrohen~ ("to threaten someone directly") are far more common in modern usage than the simplex ~drohen~ in isolation. English lost its cognate ~threaten~ (from ~أ¾rؤ“at~, a different formation) but the related Old English ~أ¾rؤ“agan~ ("to rebuke") died out, leaving no direct modern English reflex of this particular Germanic root.
en.drucken:
From Middle High German ~drucken~, from Old High German ~drucchen~/~drukken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rukkijan~ ("to press"). This is a doublet of ~drأ¼cken~ ("to press, push"), which preserves the expected umlaut. The lack of umlaut in ~drucken~ reflects Upper German dialect phonology, where umlaut before a velar geminate was blocked. Since the first German printers (Gutenberg and his successors) were based in the south, this southern dialectal form became the established technical term for printing in the seventeenth century.

The semantic split is clean: ~drucken~ means "to print" (text, books, newspapers), while ~drأ¼cken~ means "to press, push" (a button, a hand). Both descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, but the printing revolution cemented two distinct verbs. The derived noun ~Druck~ can mean both "print" and "pressure," preserving the original unity. Polish ~drukowaؤ‡~ ("to print") was borrowed from German ~drucken~.
en.drأ¤ngen:
From a merger of two Middle High German verbs: (1) MHG ~drengen~, from OHG ~drengen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rangwijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾rangwijanؤ…~; and (2) the now-obsolete ~drangen~, from MHG ~drangen~, from OHG ~drangإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rangإچn~. Both derive ultimately from PIE *~trenkت·-~ ("to beat, hew, press"). Cognate with dialectal English ~thring~ ("to press, push") and, more distantly, with English ~throng~ (a pressing crowd).

The fact that two separate verbs merged into a single modern form is an unusual etymological event. The causative *~أ¾rangwijanؤ…~ ("to cause to press") and the denominative *~أ¾rangإچn~ ("to act like a press/crowd") converged because their meanings overlapped so thoroughly. The related strong verb ~dringen~ ("to penetrate, press through") preserves the intransitive sense, while ~drأ¤ngen~ took over the transitive and causative meanings ("to push, urge, press someone"). The noun ~Drang~ ("urge, drive, pressure"), as in ~Sturm und Drang~ ("Storm and Stress"), the famous 18th-century literary movement, derives from the same root. The English word ~throng~ entered the language from Old Norse ~أ¾rا«ng~ ("crowd"), itself from the same Proto-Germanic family, making it a Scandinavian cousin of this purely West Germanic verb.
en.drأ¼cken: From Middle High German ~drأ¼cken~, from Old High German ~drucchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾rukkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾rukkijanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~trewk-~ ("to press on and break"). Cognate with Dutch ~drukken~, Low German ~drأ¼cken~, Old English ~أ¾ryؤ‹ؤ‹an~ (surviving in dialectal English ~thrutch~). A striking consequence of the same root is the doublet within German itself: ~drأ¼cken~ ("to press") and ~drucken~ ("to print") descend from the same Old High German verb, with the printing sense arising from Gutenberg-era specialization of the pressing gesture. Welsh ~trychu~ ("to cut") and Lithuanian ~trإ«جپkti~ ("to tear, burst") are cognates outside Germanic, illuminating the original sense of forceful rupture.
en.durchfأ¼hren:
Compound of ~durch-~ + ~fأ¼hren~:

- ~fأ¼hren~: From MHG ~vأ¼eren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچrijanؤ…~, the causative of *~faranؤ…~ ("to go, travel"), from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). Cognate with Dutch ~voeren~, English ~fare~ (from the base verb).
- ~durch-~: From MHG ~durch~, from OHG ~duruh~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾urhw~, from PIE *~terhâ‚‚-~ ("to cross, pass through") combined with *~hâ‚ƒekت·-~ ("to see") â€” a compound root suggesting "to see across, to look through." Cognate with English ~through~ and ~thorough~, Dutch ~door~, West Frisian ~troch~, Yiddish ~durkh~.

The compound means "to carry out, to execute, to implement" â€” literally to lead something all the way through to completion. The spatial metaphor is transparent: a plan or project is guided through the full extent of its path from start to finish. The English expression ~carry through~ shares the same spatial-completive logic. Cognate with Dutch ~doorvoeren~.
en.durchlaufen:
Compound of ~durch-~ + ~laufen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupanؤ…~ ("to jump forward, to leap"), from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, to hop"). Cognate with Dutch ~lopen~, English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Danish ~lأ¸be~.
- ~durch-~: From MHG ~durch~, from OHG ~duruh~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾urhw~, from PIE *~terkت·-~ ("to cross, pass through"). Cognate with English ~through~. The prefix conveys traversal from beginning to end.

As an inseparable compound (~durchlأ،ufen~, stressed on ~-lau-~), it means "to pass through, to go through (a process, a stage)" â€” ~ein Programm durchlaufen~ ("to go through a program"). As a separable compound (~dأ؛rchlaufen~, stressed on ~durch-~), it means "to run through without stopping" or "to wear through (shoes) by running." The stress distinction changes both meaning and grammar: inseparable ~durchlaufen~ takes no ~ge-~ in the past participle (~hat durchlaufen~), while separable ~durchlaufen~ does (~ist durchgelaufen~). This stress-based split between separable and inseparable verbs is a distinctive feature of German that has no direct parallel in English. The PIE root *~terkت·-~ ("to cross through") is also the ancestor of English ~through~ and ~thorough~ â€” originally the same word.
en.durchsetzen:
~durch-~ + ~setzen~. Compound of ~durch-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, place"), the causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit").
- ~durch-~: From PIE *~terkت·-~ ("to cross, pass through"). The prefix adds the sense of pushing through or carrying through to completion.

~Durchsetzen~ is one of those German verbs whose meaning shifts dramatically depending on whether it is separable or inseparable. As an inseparable verb (~durchsetzen~, stress on ~-setz-~), it means "to intersperse, to permeate" â€” a crowd ~durchsetzt~ with undercover agents, a text ~durchsetzt~ with errors. As a separable verb (~durchsetzen~, stress on ~durch-~), it means "to push through, to enforce, to prevail" â€” one ~setzt~ a plan ~durch~ or ~setzt sich durch~ ("prevails"). The reflexive ~sich durchsetzen~ is among the most common uses: to assert oneself, to succeed against resistance. The underlying image in both cases is "setting something through" an obstacle, but whether the obstacle is permeated passively or overcome actively depends on stress placement alone. Cognate with Dutch ~doorzetten~ ("to persevere, to push through").
en.dأ¼rfen: From Middle High German ~durfen~, ~dأ¼rfen~, from Old High German ~durfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾urban~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾urbanؤ…~, from PIE *~terp-~ ("to satiate, enjoy"). A preterite-present verb â€” its present-tense conjugations descend from what was originally a past-tense (perfect) formation, giving it the irregular present paradigm typical of this class (~darf~, ~darfst~, ~darf~ rather than the expected weak endings). The original sense of "to need" (still preserved in ~bedأ¼rfen~) shifted in Modern German to "to be allowed to." Cognate with Ancient Greek ~terpo~ ("I delight, please"), English ~tharf~ (archaic, "to need").
en.ehren:
From Middle High German ~ؤ“ren~, from Old High German ~ؤ“ren~, from Proto-Germanic *~aizؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚eys-~ ("to respect, to be in awe of"). Derived from the noun ~Ehre~ ("honor"), which descends from OHG ~ؤ“ra~ ("honor, fame, sense of honor"), from Proto-West Germanic *~aiت€u~, from Proto-Germanic *~aizإچ~. Cognate with Old Norse ~eir~ ("pardon, gentleness"), Old Saxon ~ؤ“ra~ ("honor, protection, pardon"), Old English ~ؤپr~ ("honor, help, pardon"), and Dutch ~eer~ ("honor").

The verb ~ehren~ means "to honor, to respect, to pay tribute to." The connection to Latin ~aestimؤپre~ ("to value, to acknowledge"), whence English ~esteem~ and ~estimate~, has been proposed via the PIE root, suggesting that the Indo-European speakers linked the concept of honor with the act of valuation. The proverb ~Ehrlich wأ¤hrt am lأ¤ngsten~ ("Honesty lasts the longest") uses the derived adjective ~ehrlich~ ("honest," literally "honor-like"). The compound ~Ehrenamt~ ("honorary office, volunteer position") reflects the German cultural concept of unpaid civic duty performed for honor rather than payment â€” a cornerstone of German community life that has no single-word English equivalent. The expression ~jemandem die letzte Ehre erweisen~ ("to pay someone the last honor") refers to attending a funeral, connecting ~Ehre~ to rites of passage.
en.eignen:
From Middle High German ~eigenen~, from Old High German ~eiganen~, from Proto-Germanic *~aiginإچnؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from *~aiganaz~ ("owned, one's own"), the past participle of *~aiganؤ…~ ("to possess, own"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ehâ‚‚أ³yل¸±e~, from *~hâ‚‚eyل¸±-~ ("ability, possession"). The verb ~eignen~ thus originally meant "to make one's own, to appropriate" â€” a sense still visible in the reflexive ~sich etwas aneignen~ ("to acquire, appropriate something for oneself"). The modern reflexive ~sich eignen~ ("to be suited, to be fit for") developed from the idea of being the proper or fitting owner of some quality or role.

The adjective ~eigen~ ("own, characteristic") shares the same PGmc root and is cognate with English ~own~ and Dutch ~eigen~. The PIE root *~hâ‚‚eyل¸±-~ ("ability, possession") also underlies Gothic ~aigan~ ("to have") and Old English ~ؤپgan~ ("to own"), the latter giving English ~owe~ (its present tense) and ~ought~ (its past tense). Cognate with Swedish ~أ¤gna~, English ~own~.
en.einbauen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~bإ«wen~, from OHG ~bإ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bإ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~bإ«anؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°uH-~ ("to grow, to become, to dwell").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). This separable prefix adds the sense of inserting or incorporating something into a larger structure.

~Einbauen~ means "to install, to build in, to incorporate" â€” a concept central to modern engineering and construction. You ~baust eine Kأ¼che ein~ ("install a kitchen"), ~baust ein Feature ein~ ("incorporate a feature"), or ~baust einen Motor ein~ ("install an engine"). The PIE root *~bت°uH-~ ("to grow, to become") is astonishingly productive: it yields English ~be~, ~build~, ~booth~, and ~bower~, as well as Greek ~د†دچد‰~ ("to grow," whence ~physics~ and ~physiology~) and Latin ~fuؤ«~ ("I was"). The semantic chain from "growing" to "dwelling" to "building" to "building in" shows language faithfully tracking human cultural evolution from nomadic life to settled construction.
en.einbeziehen:
A modern compound of ~ein-~ + ~beziehen~:

- ~beziehen~: From MHG ~beziehen~, from OHG ~biziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biteuhan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~ziehen~:
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it means "in, into."
  - ~be-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پepi~ ("on, at, near"), makes the verb transitive.
  - ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with English ~tow~ (via Old English ~tؤ“on~), Latin ~dإ«cإچ~ ("I lead"), and English ~duke~ (via Latin).

The sense "to refer to" in ~beziehen~ is probably a calque of Latin ~referre~ ("to carry back, to refer"). The compound ~einbeziehen~ ("to include, to involve") thus literally means "to pull into," a vivid spatial metaphor for inclusion. The PIE root *~dewk-~ has produced a remarkable constellation of words across the Indo-European languages: Latin ~dإ«cإچ~ gave us ~duke~, ~educate~, ~deduce~, ~conduct~, and ~produce~, while the Germanic branch gave us ~ziehen~, ~tow~, and ~tie~. All go back to the fundamental human action of pulling or leading. Cognate with Dutch ~betrekken~ ("to involve"), which uses a different root verb but the same ~be-~ prefix pattern.
en.einbinden:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~binden~:

- ~binden~: From MHG ~binden~, from OHG ~bintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bindan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bindanؤ…~ ("to bind, tie"), from PIE *~bت°endت°-~ ("to tie, bind"). Cognate with English ~bind~, Dutch ~binden~, Danish ~binde~, Sanskrit ~bandhati~ ("binds").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پen~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, ~ein-~ conveys inward motion or incorporation.
