The original concrete sense was "to bind something in," particularly book-binding: ~ein Buch einbinden~ means "to bind a book" in a protective cover. The modern figurative meanings -- "to integrate" and "to involve" -- developed naturally from this: to bind something or someone into a larger whole. ~Jemanden in die Entscheidung einbinden~ ("to involve someone in the decision") treats participation as a kind of binding-in. The noun ~Einbindung~ ("integration, involvement") reflects this abstract usage.
en.einbringen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, probably from PIE *~bت°renk-~, a blend of *~bت°er-~ ("to carry") + *~hâ‚پnek-~ ("to bring, to reach").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix conveys the sense of bringing something inward, into a system or enclosure.

~Einbringen~ means "to bring in" â€” covering the harvest (~die Ernte einbringen~, "to bring in the harvest"), financial yield (~Gewinn einbringen~, "to yield profit"), legislative proposals (~einen Gesetzentwurf einbringen~, "to introduce a bill"), and personal contribution (~sich einbringen~, "to contribute, to get involved"). The agricultural sense is likely the oldest: gathering the crop and bringing it inside the barn. The parliamentary sense â€” introducing a bill into the legislative body â€” mirrors English ~introduce~ (Latin "to lead inward") and French ~introduire~, all sharing the metaphor of bringing something across a threshold into an enclosed deliberative space. The reflexive ~sich einbringen~ has become a popular buzzword in modern German corporate and civic discourse, meaning to invest oneself actively in a group or project. Cognate with Dutch ~inbrengen~.
en.einfallen:
From MHG ~invallen~, from OHG ~infallan~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallanؤ…~, possibly from PIE *~hâ‚ƒelhâ‚پ-~ ("to fall") with a prefix *~hâ‚‚epo-~ ("off, away"). Cognate with English ~fall~, Dutch ~vallen~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). This separable prefix conveys inward motion -- falling into something.

~Einfallen~ means "to occur to (someone), to come to mind" and also "to collapse, to cave in" and "to invade." The cognitive sense -- ~es fأ¤llt mir ein~ ("it occurs to me," literally "it falls into me") -- provides a vivid metaphor: thoughts drop into the mind unbidden, like objects falling into a container. The military sense (~in ein Land einfallen~, "to invade a country") is older and more literal: troops falling into enemy territory. The impersonal construction ~mir fأ¤llt etwas ein~ ("something occurs to me") is one of the most useful everyday expressions in German, with its opposite ~mir fأ¤llt nichts ein~ ("nothing comes to mind") being the student's eternal lament during exams.
en.einfأ¼gen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~fأ¼gen~. Compare Dutch ~invoegen~.

- ~fأ¼gen~: From MHG ~fأ¼egen~, ~fuogen~, from OHG ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچgijanؤ…~, from PIE *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to join, attach"). Cognate with Dutch ~voegen~ and the dated English ~fay~ ("to join, fit together").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n-~, from OHG ~in-~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). This prefix adds the sense of inserting or placing into.

The PIE root *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ connects ~einfأ¼gen~ to a remarkable family of words about joining and agreement. Through Latin ~pacisci~ ("to agree, make a covenant"), it produced English ~peace~, ~pact~, ~pacify~, and ~pay~ (originally "to appease"). English ~fair~ is also a distant relative, via the Germanic branch of the root. The reflexive ~sich einfأ¼gen~ means "to fit in, to adapt," capturing the social dimension of joining a group. The noun ~Fuge~ ("joint, seam") and the musical term ~Fuge~ ("fugue," a composition where voices are joined in imitation) both descend from the same root. The compound is transparent: to place (~fأ¼gen~) something in (~ein~), making it one of those German verbs that is easier to understand etymologically than its Latin-derived English equivalent ~insert~ (from Latin ~inserere~, "to plant in").
en.einfأ¼hren:
From Middle High German ~ؤ«nvأ¼eren~, equivalent to ~ein-~ + ~fأ¼hren~:

- ~fأ¼hren~: From MHG ~vأ¼eren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچrijan~, a causative of Proto-Germanic *~faranؤ…~ ("to travel"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to cross, to go through").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of bringing something inward or initiating.

~Einfأ¼hren~ means both "to introduce" (a concept, a person, a reform) and "to import" (goods into a country) â€” the prefix ~ein-~ captures the act of leading something inward, whether into awareness or across a border. The derived noun ~Einfأ¼hrung~ ("introduction") is the standard German word for an introductory text, course, or presentation. The import sense gives ~Einfuhr~ ("import"), a nominalization that pairs neatly with ~Ausfuhr~ ("export," from ~ausfأ¼hren~) â€” the in-leading and out-leading of goods. Cognate with Dutch ~invoeren~.
en.eingeben:
From Middle High German ~ؤ«ngeben~, from Old High German ~ingeban~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (which replaced the native Old English ~giefan~ only in form, keeping the meaning), Dutch ~geven~, Swedish ~giva~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a separable verbal prefix, it means "in, into."

The literal meaning is "to give in(to)," yielding two principal modern senses: "to enter, to input" (data into a computer or system) and "to inspire, to suggest" (an idea into someone's mind). The computing sense is a natural modern extension of the older meaning: feeding information into a machine is metaphorically giving something into it. The older sense of divine or creative inspiration (~Eingebung~, "inspiration, intuition") preserves the idea that ideas are "given into" the mind from an external source. Cognate with Scots ~ingive~. The PIE root *~gت°ebت°-~ shows an intriguing semantic ambiguity between "to give" and "to take" across different branches: while Germanic languages settled on "to give," Slavic languages like Russian developed "to seize, to take" from the same root -- suggesting the original sense may have been the neutral physical act of transferring, with the direction depending on perspective.
en.eingehen:
~ein-~ + ~gehen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of going into, entering.

~Eingehen~ is one of German's most semantically branched verbs. As a separable verb it means: "to enter (into an agreement), to go into (a topic), to shrink (of fabric), to wither and die (of plants), to arrive (of mail)." The image in each case is something moving inward â€” into a contract, into a subject, into smaller dimensions, into death, into a mailbox. The phrase ~auf etwas eingehen~ ("to address, respond to, engage with something") is ubiquitous in academic and professional discourse: one ~geht ein auf~ a question by entering into it. The sense "to wither" (~die Pflanze geht ein~, "the plant is dying") uses the same inward-collapse metaphor: the plant goes into itself, contracts, and ceases. The PIE root *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave") produces the paradox that "going" originally meant "leaving" â€” a reminder that direction is always relative to a starting point. Cognate with Dutch ~ingaan~.
en.einhalten:
From ~ein-~ + ~halten~, an alteration of the older ~innehalten~, which originally shared the same meanings but has since narrowed to "to pause, to wait":

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~, from PIE *~kel-~ ("to drive, to herd").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix conveys the sense of holding something inward, keeping it contained, or maintaining an internal commitment.

~Einhalten~ primarily means "to keep, to stick to, to comply with" â€” rules (~Regeln einhalten~), deadlines (~Fristen einhalten~), promises (~Versprechen einhalten~), and appointments (~Termine einhalten~). The underlying image is of holding something inside, keeping it intact rather than letting it slip. The older and now rare sense of "to hold back, to contain" survives in literary usage: ~Er konnte die nervأ¶sen Pferde nicht mehr einhalten~ ("He could no longer hold back the nervous horses"). A colloquial sense unique to German is "to hold one's pee" â€” ~Kannst du noch einhalten?~ ("Can you hold it in?") â€” holding something in quite literally. The dated literary usage ~Haltet ein!~ ("Desist!") preserves the imperative sense of stopping, halting an action by holding it in check. The derived noun ~Einhaltung~ ("compliance, observance") is a staple of legal and regulatory German. Related to ~Einhalt~ ("halt, cessation"), as in ~Einhalt gebieten~ ("to put a stop to"). Cognate with Dutch ~inhouden~ ("to contain, to hold back").
en.einholen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~holen~:

- ~holen~: From MHG ~holen~, ~holn~, from OHG ~holإچn~, ~holؤ“n~ ("to fetch, get, draw"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~halإچnؤ…~, *~hulإچnؤ…~ ("to call, fetch, summon"), from PIE *~kelhâ‚پ-~ ("to call, cry, summon"). Related to English ~haul~ (borrowed into English from Old French ~haler~, which was itself borrowed from a Germanic source).
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it means "in, into."

~Einholen~ has two distinct meanings: "to catch up with" (~einen Vorsprung einholen~ = "to close a lead") and "to fetch, to obtain" (~Erkundigungen einholen~ = "to make inquiries," ~Genehmigungen einholen~ = "to obtain approvals"). The connection between ~holen~ and English ~haul~ reveals a circular borrowing: Proto-Germanic *~halإچnؤ…~ was borrowed into Old French as ~haler~, which was then borrowed into Middle English as ~haul~. So English ~haul~ is a Germanic word that took a detour through French before returning to the Germanic family.
en.einigen:
From Middle High German ~einegen~, ~einigen~, from Old High German ~gieinؤ«gen~. Compound of ~ein~ + ~-igen~:

- ~ein~: From MHG ~ein~, from OHG ~ein~ ("one"), from Proto-West Germanic *~ain~, from Proto-Germanic *~ainaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ³ynos~ ("one, single"). Cognate with English ~one~, Dutch ~een~, Danish ~en~.
- ~-igen~: A denominal/deadjectival suffix forming verbs from adjectives or nouns.

The verb means "to unite, to agree" and reflexively ~sich einigen~ means "to come to an agreement." The derivation from ~ein~ ("one") is transparent: to ~einigen~ is to make ~einig~ ("united, in agreement"), which is to make parties into "one." The OHG form ~gieinؤ«gen~ shows the ~gi-~ prefix (modern ~ge-~) that was common in Old High German for creating perfective or resultative verbs. The word is inseparable from German political history: ~die (Wieder)vereinigung~ ("the (Re)unification") of 1990 uses the same root, and the motto ~Einigkeit und Recht und Freiheit~ ("Unity and Justice and Freedom") opens the German national anthem. The PIE root *~hâ‚پأ³ynos~ also gave Latin ~إ«nus~ ("one"), whence English ~union~, ~unite~, and ~unanimous~.
en.einkaufen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~kaufen~:

- ~kaufen~: From MHG ~koufen~, from OHG ~koufإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kaupإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaupإچnؤ…~, borrowed from Latin ~caupإچ~ ("innkeeper, petty tradesman, huckster"). Cognate with English ~cheap~ (originally "trade, bargain," from Old English ~cؤ“ap~ "bargain, cattle"), Dutch ~kopen~, Swedish ~kأ¶pa~, and Danish ~kأ¸be~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~ ("in, into"), from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys movement inward or acquisition.

The compound literally means "to buy in" â€” to bring purchased goods into the household. ~Einkaufen~ is the standard German word for grocery shopping and general retail purchasing: ~einkaufen gehen~ ("to go shopping"). The etymology of ~kaufen~ is remarkable: it is one of the oldest Latin loanwords in Germanic, borrowed during the Roman trading period along the Rhine frontier. Latin ~caupإچ~ meant a petty trader or tavern keeper, and the Germanic peoples adopted it to describe the act of commercial exchange itself. English ~cheap~ underwent an even more dramatic shift: from "trade" to "bargain" to "inexpensive." The related German noun ~Kaufmann~ ("merchant") literally means "trade-man" and preserves the original commercial sense. The expression ~Einkaufswagen~ ("shopping cart") and ~Einkaufszentrum~ ("shopping center") are compound-of-a-compound formations typical of German's love of agglutination.
en.einladen:
~ein-~ + ~laden~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~laden~:

- ~laden~: Two distinct verbs converged on this infinitive in Modern German. The weak verb "to invite" â€” the one active in ~einladen~ â€” derives from MHG ~laden~, from OHG ~ladon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laأ¾إچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~laأ¾إچnؤ…~, from *~laأ¾إچ~ ("invitation"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚lot-ehâ‚‚~, a root shared with Hittite ~halzai~ ("to invoke, call out"). The strong verb "to load" derives from OHG ~hladan~, from PIE *~klehâ‚‚-~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of direction inward or bringing into.

The semantic journey of ~einladen~ from "to load in" to "to invite" is one of German's most charming etymological surprises. The literal sense â€” loading guests into one's home or vehicle â€” crystallized as the dominant figurative meaning, completely eclipsing any loading sense in the compound. Today ~einladen~ means exclusively "to invite," while the loading sense requires the strong-conjugation ~laden~ on its own. The Hittite cognate ~halzai~ makes this verb a rare bridge to Anatolian â€” one of the oldest branches of Indo-European â€” giving ~einladen~ an etymology that reaches back roughly 4,000 years to the earliest attested Indo-European languages. The noun ~Einladung~ ("invitation") is the result: a word built on "loading" that now means only "an invitation to come in." Cognate with Dutch ~uitnodigen~.
en.einlassen:
From Middle High German ~ؤ«nlؤپzen~, from Old High German ~inlؤپzan~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, to leave behind"). Cognate with Dutch ~laten~, English ~let~.
- ~ein-~: Related to ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix conveys inward movement.

The compound means "to let in, to admit" and reflexively ~sich einlassen auf~ means "to get involved in, to engage with." The reflexive sense carries a connotation of risk â€” ~sich auf etwas einlassen~ implies committing to something uncertain, letting oneself ~in~ to a situation that may prove difficult. The root ~lassen~ belongs to the seventh class of strong verbs and retains the archaic reduplicating pattern in its past tense (~lieأں~, from the old reduplicated form). The PIE root *~lehâ‚پd-~ also produced Latin ~lassus~ ("tired, weary") â€” to let go is to become exhausted. English ~let~ is the direct cognate, while ~late~ (originally "slow, tardy") may also be related through the sense of "leaving behind, remaining."
en.einlegen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~ ("to lay"), the causative of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it conveys the sense of placing something into a container or medium.

~Einlegen~ means "to insert, to put in, to pickle" â€” literally to lay something in. Its range of meanings is remarkably broad: ~Gurken einlegen~ ("to pickle cucumbers," i.e., to lay them into brine), ~eine Pause einlegen~ ("to take a break," i.e., to insert a pause), ~ein gutes Wort einlegen~ ("to put in a good word"), and ~eine CD einlegen~ ("to insert a CD"). The pickling sense gave rise to the noun ~Einlage~ ("insert, deposit, filling"), used in contexts from banking (a deposit) to soup (the noodles or dumplings added as filling). The PIE root *~legت°-~ ("to lie, to lay") is one of the most productive in Indo-European, yielding English ~lay~, ~lie~, ~law~, ~ledger~, Latin ~lectus~ ("bed"), and Greek ~lekhos~ ("bed, lair").
en.einleiten:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~leiten~:

- ~leiten~: From MHG ~leiten~, from OHG ~leiten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laidijanؤ…~ ("to lead, guide, conduct"), a causative of *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to go, travel"). Cognate with English ~lead~, Old Saxon ~lؤ“dian~, Old Norse ~leiأ°a~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~ ("in, into"), from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys movement inward or initiation.

The compound means "to introduce, to initiate, to begin" â€” one ~leitet ein Verfahren ein~ ("initiates a proceeding"), ~leitet eine Rede ein~ ("opens a speech"), or ~leitet Maأںnahmen ein~ ("introduces measures"). The image is of leading something inward, into existence or action. The noun ~Einleitung~ ("introduction") is the standard term for the opening section of a book, essay, or piece of music. The root verb ~leiten~ is the causative partner of an older verb meaning "to go" (still preserved in ~Leitfaden~ "guideline," literally "guiding thread"): to ~leiten~ is to "cause to go," i.e., to lead. English ~lead~ descends from the same Proto-Germanic causative. The coincidence that English ~lead~ (the verb) and ~lead~ (the metal) are spelled identically is purely accidental â€” the metal comes from a completely different root.
en.einnehmen:
From Middle High German ~ؤ«nnemen~, from Old High German ~inneman~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of taking inward, receiving, or absorbing.

~Einnehmen~ covers a remarkable semantic range, all unified by the notion of taking something in: to take (a meal, medicine), to take up (a position or place), to capture (a fortress), and to win over (a person's affections). The military sense â€” ~eine Stadt einnehmen~ ("to capture a city") â€” preserves the oldest and most concrete meaning: taking something inward, under one's control. The derived noun ~Einnahme~ means "income, revenue" (money taken in) as well as "capture" (a stronghold taken in). The gentler sense of ~einnehmend~ ("charming, winning") shows how the metaphor of conquest softened: a person with an ~einnehmendes Wesen~ ("engaging manner") captures your sympathy rather than your city. Cognate with Dutch ~innemen~.
en.einreichen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From MHG ~reichen~, from OHG ~reihhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijanؤ…~ ("to stretch out, reach"), a causative o-grade derivation from PIE *~Hreyاµ-~ ("to stretch out, reach"). Cognate with English ~reach~, Dutch ~reiken~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in").

The literal sense is "to reach something in" -- to extend or hand something into an authority's domain. Modern usage has fully specialized to mean "to submit, to hand in" (a document, an application, a complaint). The underlying spatial metaphor of reaching inward persists: one ~reicht ein~ a ~Klage~ (lawsuit), a ~Bewerbung~ (application), or a ~Petition~ (petition), each time stretching the document across a threshold toward someone who will receive it.
en.einrichten:
~ein-~ + ~richten~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~rehtaz~ ("straight"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreاµtأ³s~, from *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to stretch out, straighten, direct").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of directing something inward, setting it up inside a space.

~Einrichten~ means "to set up, furnish, arrange, or establish" â€” the image of straightening things inward, into a space, until they are ordered correctly. ~Eine Wohnung einrichten~ ("to furnish an apartment") captures the core sense: making a space right by placing things within it. The related noun ~Einrichtung~ means both "furnishings" and "institution, facility" â€” a semantic duality that mirrors the two applications of the verb. The same PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ underlies Latin ~regإچ~ ("to rule, direct") and English ~right~, ~reign~, and ~regiment~. To set something right is, in the oldest layer of the language, to straighten it. Cognate with Dutch ~inrichten~.
en.einrأ¤umen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~rأ¤umen~:

- ~rأ¤umen~: From MHG ~rإ«men~, from OHG ~rإ«man~, ~rإ«mman~, derived from the noun ~Raum~ ("room, space"). ~Raum~ from MHG ~rإ«m~, from OHG ~rإ«m~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rإ«m~, from Proto-Germanic *~rإ«mؤ…~ ("room, space"), from PIE *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open, to make space"). Cognate with English ~room~, Dutch ~ruimte~, Old Norse ~rأ؛m~.
- ~ein-~: Related to ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix conveys inward placement.

The compound means "to put away (into a cupboard or room); to concede, to admit (a point); to grant (rights or authority)." The semantic leap from "putting things into a space" to "conceding a point" is bridged by the metaphor of making space for someone else's position â€” to ~einrأ¤umen~ an objection is to clear room for it in one's own argument. The root ~Raum~ is cognate with English ~room~: both descend from Proto-Germanic *~rإ«mؤ…~, and the PIE root *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open") reveals the original, physical sense of creating space by clearing or breaking open ground. The adjective ~gerأ¤umig~ ("spacious") preserves the direct connection, while the verb ~aufrأ¤umen~ ("to tidy up") uses a different prefix to mean "to clear space upward."
en.einschalten:
From ~ein-~ + ~schalten~, perhaps modeled after its antonym ~ausschalten~:

- ~schalten~: From MHG ~schalten~, from OHG ~scaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaldan~ ("to push, to shove"). The further etymology is obscure; according to Pokorny, it may derive from PIE *~(s)kelH-~ ("to split, to separate"), in which case it is related to English ~shell~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~skaldan~ ("to push").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in-~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~ ("in, into"), from PIE *~hâ‚پen~ ("in"). Cognate with English ~in~, Latin ~in~, Greek ~en~.

The original meaning of ~schalten~ was physical: "to push, to shove, to propel." From this developed the sense of operating a mechanism by pushing a lever or switch, which led to the modern meanings "to switch, to shift (gears), to control." The compound ~einschalten~ thus literally means "to push in" or "to switch in," hence "to turn on, to activate." It is also used reflexively (~sich einschalten~) to mean "to intervene, to get involved," as if inserting oneself into a process. The suggestion that ~einschalten~ may have been modeled after ~ausschalten~ is an interesting case of a word being back-formed from its antonym, though both are now completely established in their own right.
en.einschrأ¤nken:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~schrأ¤nken~:

- ~schrأ¤nken~: From MHG ~schrenken~ ("to place crosswise"), from OHG ~screnken~ ("to spread out, throw down"), from Proto-Germanic *~skrankijanؤ…~ ("to cause to shrink; to limit, restrict"). Related to ~Schrank~ ("cabinet, cupboard"), from OHG ~scranc~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrank~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrankaz~ ("grid, barrier"), probably from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to bend, turn").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پen~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, ~ein-~ conveys inward motion or confinement.

The compound literally conveys the idea of placing something within barriers or limits. The related noun ~Schranke~ ("barrier, gate") preserves the concrete sense of a physical limitation, while ~einschrأ¤nken~ has moved fully into the abstract domain of constraining, limiting, or restricting. The reflexive ~sich einschrأ¤nken~ means "to economize" or "to tighten one's belt."
en.einschأ¤tzen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~schأ¤tzen~:

- ~schأ¤tzen~: From MHG ~schetzen~, a verbal formation from ~Schatz~ ("treasure"). ~Schatz~ descends from MHG ~schaz~, from OHG ~skaz~, ~scaz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skatt~ ("cattle, treasure"), from Proto-Germanic *~skattaz~. Cognate with Old Norse ~skattr~ (whence Danish ~skat~, Swedish ~skatt~, all meaning "tax, treasure"), Gothic ~skatts~ ("money"), Dutch ~schat~, English ~scat~ (dialectal: "tax, tribute").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a separable prefix here, it adds the sense of placing something into a category or framework.

~Einschأ¤tzen~ means "to assess, to estimate, to evaluate" â€” literally to place a value (~Schatz~) on something. The semantic path from ~Schatz~ ("treasure") to ~schأ¤tzen~ ("to estimate, to value") is transparent: to appraise is to determine how much treasure something is worth. The Proto-Germanic *~skattaz~ originally meant "cattle" â€” the oldest form of wealth in pastoral Indo-European society â€” before shifting to "money, treasure." This cattle-to-currency shift is paralleled by Latin ~pecإ«nia~ ("money") from ~pecus~ ("cattle") and English ~fee~ from Proto-Germanic *~fehu~ ("cattle, property").
en.einsehen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with English ~see~, Dutch ~zien~, Danish ~se~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~ؤ«n~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys "in, into."

~Einsehen~ means "to look into" literally (a document, a file) and "to realize, to understand" figuratively. The metaphorical leap from "looking into" to "understanding" appears in many languages: English ~insight~ (literally "seeing in"), Latin ~inspicere~ ("to look into," whence ~inspect~), and Greek ~ennoeإچ~ ("to perceive," from ~en-~ "in" + ~noeإچ~ "to think"). The derived noun ~Einsicht~ ("insight, realization") perfectly mirrors its English cognate.
en.einsetzen:
From Middle High German ~ؤ«nsetzen~, ~insetzen~, from Old High German ~insezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~insattjan~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, to place down"), from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~. A causative derivation of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"): literally "to cause to sit," hence "to set, place."
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in-~, from OHG ~in-~, from Proto-Germanic *~in-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, ~ein-~ adds the sense of "into" or "in place."

The compound's range of meanings â€” "to insert," "to deploy (troops)," "to stake (money)," "to begin (music)" â€” all derive from the core sense of placing something into position or into action. Compare English ~inset~, a partial cognate. Cognate with Dutch ~inzetten~, English ~inset~.
en.einsteigen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~steigen~:

- ~steigen~: From MHG ~stؤ«gen~, from OHG ~stؤ«gan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤ«gan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤ«ganؤ…~ ("to climb, ascend"), from PIE *~steygت°-~ ("to stride, to step, to go"). Cognate with obsolete English ~sty~ ("to ascend"), Dutch ~stijgen~ ("to rise"), Danish ~stige~, and Swedish ~stiga~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it means "in, into."

~Einsteigen~ means "to board, to get in, to enter (a vehicle)" â€” literally "to climb in." One ~steigt ein~ in a bus, a train, a car, or an airplane. Figuratively, it also means "to get involved in, to enter (a business or discussion)": ~in ein Geschأ¤ft einsteigen~ ("to enter into a business"). The PIE root *~steygت°-~ is the ancestor of English ~stair~ (from Proto-Germanic *~staigrؤ«~, "that which one climbs"). The modern German opposite ~aussteigen~ ("to get out, to exit") has developed the colloquial extended meaning of "to drop out" (~aus der Gesellschaft aussteigen~ = "to drop out of society").
en.einstellen:
From Middle High German ~ؤ«nstellen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb â€” the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in-~, from OHG ~in-~, from Proto-Germanic *~in-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). This separable prefix expresses motion inward or placement into a defined space.

~einstellen~ is one of the most semantically productive compound verbs in German, carrying several meanings that look quite unrelated until the spatial metaphor underlying them is understood. To hire someone (~jemanden einstellen~) is literally to place them into the workforce. To adjust a dial (~die Temperatur einstellen~) is to set it into the correct position. To stop an activity (~die Produktion einstellen~) is to put it into a state of rest. To insert a line of text is to place it into a document. All derive from the core image of ~stellen~ with the directional force of ~ein-~: placing something into its proper slot. The reflexive ~sich einstellen~ â€” "to arrive, to occur" (as when a feeling sets in) â€” adds yet another dimension: the thing places itself in. Cognate with Dutch ~instellen~.
en.einstufen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~Stufe~ + ~-n~:

- ~Stufe~: From MHG ~stuofe~, from OHG ~stuofa~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stإچpؤپ~, related to the verb *~stappjan~ ("to tread, to step"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~stإچpo~, Dutch ~stoep~ ("stoop, front steps"), and English ~stoop~ (the porch steps of a house, a word borrowed into American English from Dutch).
- ~ein-~: Related to ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix conveys placement into a category.

The verb means "to classify, to rank, to categorize" â€” to place something or someone into a ~Stufe~ ("step, level, grade"). The noun ~Stufe~ originally meant a physical step or stair and gradually extended to abstract levels and grades. The compound ~Einstufung~ ("classification") is standard in bureaucratic and academic German: ~Einstufungstest~ ("placement test") determines which ~Stufe~ a student enters. The English word ~stoop~ (the raised platform at a house entrance) traveled from Dutch ~stoep~ to New Amsterdam (later New York) in the 17th century, preserving the same Proto-West Germanic root in an unexpected American context. The South African English ~stoep~ ("veranda") is a parallel borrowing from Afrikaans.
en.eintragen:
~ein-~ + ~tragen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, carry, bear"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to draw, drag").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of carrying into or inscribing into.

~Eintragen~ means "to enter, to register, to record" â€” to carry information into a book, a list, or a database. The underlying metaphor is physical: one ~trأ¤gt~ (carries, bears) data ~ein~ (into) a written record. The reflexive ~sich eintragen~ ("to sign in, to register oneself") and the noun ~Eintrag~ ("entry, record") are central to German bureaucratic life â€” from hotel guest books to commercial registers (~Handelsregister~). The verb can also mean "to yield, to bring in" (profit): ~das Geschأ¤ft trأ¤gt viel ein~ ("the business yields a lot"), where the image shifts from inscription to income flowing inward. Cognate with Dutch ~indragen~ ("to bring in, contribute"). The PIE root *~dت°regت°-~ connects ~tragen~ to English ~draw~ and ~drag~ â€” the physical labor of hauling something from one place to another, now abstracted to moving a pen across paper.
en.eintreten:
~ein-~ + ~treten~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trأ«ten~, from OHG ~trأ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudanؤ…~ ("to tread, to step on"). The West Germanic descendants regularized the verb's ablaut by replacing the original ~u~ vowel with ~e~ in the present tense, but the original vocalism may have survived longer in some High German dialects â€” the Medieval Latin loanword ~trottإچ~ ("to trot") likely reflects this older pronunciation. Cognate with English ~tread~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of entering, moving inward.

~Eintreten~ is a remarkably versatile compound. Its core meaning is "to enter, to step in" (~bitte treten Sie ein!~, "please come in!"), but it extends to "to occur, to happen" (~ein unerwartetes Ereignis ist eingetreten~, "an unexpected event has occurred"), "to join" an organization (~in eine Partei eintreten~, "to join a party"), and "to advocate for" (~fأ¼r die Menschenrechte eintreten~, "to stand up for human rights"). With a direct object and ~haben~, it means "to kick in" (~die Tأ¼r eintreten~, "to kick the door in") or "to tread down" (soil). This wide semantic spread â€” entering, occurring, joining, advocating, kicking â€” all stems from the physical act of stepping into a space, with each meaning extending the metaphor of crossing a threshold.
en.einziehen:
From MHG ~inziehen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with Gothic ~tiuhan~, Old English ~tؤ“on~, Dutch ~tien~ (obsolete), Old Norse ~toga~, and English ~tug~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~hâ‚پأ©n~ ("in"). This separable prefix adds the sense of movement inward.

~Einziehen~ means "to move in" (to a new home), "to draft" (into military service), "to collect" (debts, taxes), "to retract" (claws), and "to absorb" (moisture). The semantic thread is always drawing or pulling inward. The housing sense (~in eine Wohnung einziehen~) is the most common in everyday speech and pairs neatly with ~ausziehen~ ("to move out"). The PIE root *~dewk-~ also produced Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead"), whence English ~duke~, ~educate~, ~conduct~, and ~produce~.
en.empfangen:
From Middle High German ~enphؤپhen~, from Old High German ~intvؤپhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~andfanhanؤ…~. Compound of ~emp-~ (variant of ~ent-~) + ~fangen~:

- ~fangen~: From MHG ~vؤپhen~, from OHG ~fؤپhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fؤ…ج„han~, from Proto-Germanic *~fanhanؤ…~, from PIE *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to secure, to attach, to fasten").
- ~emp-~: A variant of ~ent-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Before labials (~f~, ~pf~), ~ent-~ becomes ~emp-~ for phonological reasons. The prefix here conveys the sense of receiving or taking in â€” facing something and accepting it.

~Empfangen~ means "to receive, to welcome, to conceive (a child)" â€” senses that span from the formal (~einen Gast empfangen~, "to receive a guest") to the biological. The Old High German form ~intvؤپhan~ shows the original prefix ~int-~ (= ~ent-~) still intact; the shift to ~emp-~ before ~f~ is the same sound change seen in ~empfinden~ and ~empfehlen~. Cognate with Dutch ~ontvangen~, Old Saxon ~antfؤپhan~. The PIE root *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to secure, to fasten") makes the etymological image one of catching and holding â€” reception as a deliberate act of grasping what comes toward you, which also explains why English ~fang~ (the tooth that seizes prey) shares the same ancestor.
en.empfehlen:
From Middle High German ~enpfelhen~, ~enphelhen~, ~entfelhen~, ~intfelhen~, from Old High German ~intfelhan~, ~intfelahan~. Compound of ~emp-~ + the archaic root ~felhan~:

- ~felhan~: From Proto-Germanic *~felhanؤ…~, of uncertain PIE origin. Two competing theories: if the primary sense was "to go, enter," it may be related to *~fulgؤپnؤ…~ ("to follow"); if "to conceal" was primary, it may connect to a PIE root *~pel-~ ("to cover, wrap"). The Old High German verb ~felhan~ meant "to preserve, entrust, confide in, surrender."
- ~emp-~: A variant of ~ent-~, from MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The form ~emp-~ (rather than ~ent-~) arose through assimilation before the labial consonants ~f-~ and ~pf-~, the same process that gives ~empfinden~ rather than *~entfinden~.

A critical false friend lurks here: ~empfehlen~ ("to recommend") is etymologically related to ~befehlen~ ("to command, order") â€” both descend from OHG ~felhan~ â€” but is ~not~ related to ~fehlen~ ("to be missing, to lack"), which has a different and separately borrowed history (from Old French ~faillir~). The prefix ~emp-~ shifts the archaic sense of "entrusting" into the modern sense of putting someone or something forward as worthy of confidence. Cognate with Dutch ~bevelen~ (via ~befehlen~).
en.empfinden:
From Middle High German ~enpfinden~, ~entfinden~, from Old High German ~intfindan~, equivalent to ~emp-~ (variant of ~ent-~) + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to tread, walk, find the way").
- ~emp-~: A variant of ~ent-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Before labial consonants (~f~, ~pf~), the prefix ~ent-~ assimilates to ~emp-~: compare ~empfangen~ (from ~ent-~ + ~fangen~), ~empfehlen~ (from ~ent-~ + ~fehlen/befehlen~).

~Empfinden~ means "to feel, to sense, to perceive through the sense organs" â€” a class IIIa strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~empfinden~, ~empfand~, ~empfunden~. The semantic logic of the compound is beautiful: ~ent-~ / ~emp-~ in its original sense of "facing, encountering" + ~finden~ ("to find") yields "to find oneself facing something, to encounter a sensation." The word occupies a specific register between physical sensation (~er empfand die Kأ¤lte am ganzen Kأ¶rper~, "he felt the cold all over his body") and emotional perception (~Mitleid empfinden~, "to feel compassion"). The derived noun ~Empfindung~ ("sensation, feeling") and the adjective ~empfindlich~ ("sensitive, touchy") extend this perceptual focus. The adjective ~empfindsam~ ("sentimental") was coined by G. E. Lessing in 1768 as a calque of English ~sentimental~, itself newly minted by Laurence Sterne.
en.enden:
From Middle High German ~enden~, from Old High German ~entإچn~ ("to end, to complete, to die"), attested since the 8th century. A denominative verb derived from the noun ~Ende~:

~Ende~ traces to OHG ~enti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~andؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~andijaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚entأ­os~ ("front, forehead") â€” itself from *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front").

The semantic journey from "forehead" to "end" is one of the more surprising in Germanic etymology. The PIE root *~hâ‚‚ent-~ referred to the front of the head â€” the face turned outward, the foremost surface of a thing. In Proto-Germanic, this gave *~andijaz~, which came to mean the farthest extent of something â€” the outermost point, the frontier, the edge. As the word entered OHG it had already shifted from "front" to "extremity," and from "extremity" to "terminus": the end is the point at which a thing faces outward no further. German ~Ende~ and English ~end~ are exact cognates, both descending from this root. The same PIE root *~hâ‚‚ent-~ also appears in the German prefix ~ent-~ and in Latin ~ante~ ("before") â€” a reminder that "front" and "before" are closely allied concepts. The verb ~enden~ is a simple denominative: to end is to bring something to its ~Ende~. Its OHG antecedent ~entإچn~ already carried the sense of dying â€” reaching the ultimate end â€” a usage preserved in literary German to this day.
en.engagieren: Borrowed from French ~engager~ ("to pledge, to commit, to hire"), with the verb ending replaced by the German ~-ieren~ suffix. French ~engager~ derives from Old French ~engagier~ ("to pawn, to pledge"), from ~en-~ ("in") + ~gage~ ("pledge"), from Frankish *~wadja~ ("pledge"), from Proto-Germanic *~wadjإچ~ / *~wadjؤ…~ ("pledge, guarantee"), from PIE *~wedت°-~ ("to guarantee, to pledge"). The etymology is thus circular: a Germanic word (*~wadja~) traveled into Old French via the Frankish conquerors of Gaul, evolved through centuries of French phonology into ~gage~ and then ~engager~, and was reborrowed back into German as ~engagieren~ in the 17th century â€” unrecognizable to German speakers as their own ancestor. The Proto-Germanic root also gives Gothic ~wadi~ ("pledge"), Old English ~wedd~ ("pledge, vow" â€” whence English ~wed~, originally "to pledge"), and Old High German ~wetti~ ("collateral, security agreement" â€” whence modern ~Wette~, "bet"). So ~engagieren~ and ~Wette~ are distant cousins reunited after a thousand-year detour through France. In modern German, ~engagieren~ means "to hire (an artist)" and, reflexively, ~sich engagieren~ means "to get involved, to commit oneself (to a cause)" â€” ~sich politisch engagieren~ ("to be politically active"). The derived noun ~Engagement~ retains its French pronunciation. Cognate with English ~engage~, Portuguese ~engajar~, Romanian ~angaja~.
en.entdecken:
From Middle High German ~endecken~, from Old High German ~intthecken~, ~inthecken~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~decken~:

- ~decken~: From MHG ~decken~, from OHG ~decchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾akkjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾akjanؤ…~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾akؤ…~ ("roof, cover"), from PIE *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover"). Cognate with West Frisian ~dekke~, Dutch ~dekken~, English ~thatch~, Swedish ~tأ¤cka~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The literal meaning of ~entdecken~ is "to un-cover, to remove a covering" â€” the ~ent-~ prefix reverses the action of ~decken~ ("to cover"). From this concrete sense, the verb generalized to "to discover," the same metaphor underlying English ~uncover~ ("to discover") and French ~dأ©couvrir~ ("to discover," from ~couvrir~, "to cover"). English ~discover~ itself follows the same pattern via Latin ~discooperiإچ~. The shared PIE root *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover") also shows up in English ~stegosaurus~ (the "roofed lizard") and Latin ~tؤ“ctum~ ("roof"). Cognate with Dutch ~ontdekken~, Danish ~opdage~ (different prefix, same root verb).
en.entfallen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallanؤ…~ ("to fall"), from PIE *~pإچl-~ (possibly from a compound of *~hâ‚‚epo~ "off, away" + *~hâ‚ƒelhâ‚پ-~ "to fall"). Cognate with English ~fall~, Dutch ~vallen~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~and-~, from Proto-Germanic *~anda-~ ("against, away"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, ~ent-~ typically conveys removal, reversal, or separation.

The three modern meanings elegantly reflect the prefix's separative force applied to falling: "to drop away" (something falls from one's hands), "to lapse or be cancelled" (something falls away from the schedule), and "to slip one's memory" (something falls away from one's mind). The last sense offers a vivid metaphor: a forgotten thing has literally "fallen away from" the speaker.
en.entfalten:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~falten~:

- ~falten~: From MHG ~valden~, ~valten~, from OHG ~faldan~, ~faltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~falأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~falأ¾anؤ…~, from pre-Germanic *~polt-~, from PIE *~pel-~ ("to fold"). The irregular forms with ~-t-~ arose through early levelling of ~Grammatischer Wechsel~ (Verner's law alternation) but remained rare in both Old and Middle High German. They likely established themselves by analogy with the several German verbs in ~-alten~ (~halten~, ~schalten~, ~spalten~, ~walten~), as no verbs in ~-alden~ survived. Cognate with English ~fold~, Dutch ~vouwen~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~ ("away, against"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Here the prefix reverses the base action: unfolding what was folded.

The literal sense is "to un-fold," and the semantic extension to "to develop, to unfurl, to deploy" is natural and vivid. One can ~ein Tuch entfalten~ ("unfold a cloth"), ~Aktivitأ¤ten entfalten~ ("develop activities"), or ~seine Persأ¶nlichkeit entfalten~ ("unfold one's personality"). The PIE root *~pel-~ also gives Latin ~-plex~ (as in ~duplex~, ~complex~), Greek ~-plasios~ ("-fold"), and English ~-ply~ (as in ~multiply~), making ~entfalten~ a distant relative of ~complicated~ (literally "folded together") and ~simple~ (literally "one-fold"). The English cognate ~fold~ descends from the same Proto-Germanic root but via Old English ~fealdan~, which preserved the original ~-d-~ that German analogically replaced with ~-t-~.
en.entfernen:
From Middle High German ~entverren~, analyzable as ~ent-~ + ~fern~ + ~-en~ (deadjectival verb suffix). Compound of ~ent-~ + ~fern~:

- ~fern~: From MHG ~verren~, from OHG ~ferrana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ferrai~ ("far, distant"), from Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ (locative form *~per-nأ³y~, "to cross, pass"). The same PIE root *~per-~ also underlies English ~far~, and more distantly Latin ~per-~ and Greek ~peri-~. Cognate with English ~far~, Dutch ~ver~, Danish ~fjern~, Icelandic ~fjأ¦r~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The verb literally means "to make distant, to remove into the distance" â€” the ~ent-~ prefix conveys the movement away, while ~fern~ supplies the notion of distance. The reflexive ~sich entfernen~ ("to move away, leave") is extremely common in modern German, but the transitive form ("to remove something") is equally current. The spatial metaphor â€” distance as something actively created â€” is preserved in every use of the word. Cognate with Dutch ~verwijderen~ (the functional equivalent).
en.entgehen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with English ~go~, Dutch ~gaan~, Swedish ~gأ¥~.
- ~ent-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, it means "away, un-, de-."

~Entgehen~ means "to escape, to avoid, to elude" â€” literally "to go away from." The dative construction ~jemandem entgehen~ ("to escape someone's notice") is particularly common: ~Diese Tatsache ist mir entgangen~ ("This fact escaped my notice"). The spelling with ~-h-~ in modern German ~gehen~ is a purely orthographic addition modeled on ~sehen~ ("to see"), where the ~h~ was originally etymological. In Old High German, the verb was simply ~gؤپn~ â€” no ~h~ at all.
en.enthalten:
From Middle High German ~enthalten~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~. Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The compound was influenced by ~innehalten~ and ~einhalten~ (literally "to hold inside"), which shaped both its "to contain" sense (compare ~Inhalt~, "contents," from the same root complex) and its reflexive "to abstain" sense (~sich enthalten~, "to refrain"). Cognate with Dutch ~onthouden~ ("to remember, withhold") and ~inhouden~ ("to contain"), Middle Low German ~enthإچlden~.
en.entlassen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, to leave behind"). Cognate with Dutch ~laten~, English ~let~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, it conveys reversal, removal, or release.

The compound means "to release, to discharge, to dismiss, to lay off." The ~ent-~ prefix adds the sense of releasing from an obligation or bond: a doctor ~entlأ¤sst~ a patient from the hospital, an employer ~entlأ¤sst~ an employee from their position. The noun ~Entlassung~ covers both the neutral sense of "discharge" and the harsher sense of "dismissal" or "layoff." The word carries considerable weight in German labor law, where ~Entlassung~ triggers formal legal protections (~Kأ¼ndigungsschutz~, "protection against dismissal") that are among the strongest in Europe. The combination of ~ent-~ ("away from") and ~lassen~ ("to let") creates a precise image: to let someone go away from where they have been held.
en.entlasten:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~lasten~, a denominal derivation from ~Last~ ("load, burden"):

- ~Last~: From MHG ~last~, from OHG ~last~, ~hlast~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlasti~, from PIE *~klehâ‚‚-~ ("to put, to lay out"). The initial OHG ~hl-~ cluster was simplified to ~l-~ during the Middle High German period. Cognate with English ~last~ (in the sense of a unit of weight or cargo) and ~ballast~ (which preserves the noun in a compound). Related to English ~laden~ ("loaded").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, it conveys removal or reversal.

The verb means "to relieve, to unburden, to exonerate" â€” to remove a ~Last~ ("burden") from someone. In legal contexts, ~entlasten~ means "to exonerate" (to remove the burden of guilt), and ~Entlastung~ is the legal term for "exoneration" or "acquittal." In accounting, ~Entlastung~ refers to the formal approval and discharge of a board's management ("ratification"). The ~ent-~ prefix performs its characteristic reversal: where ~belasten~ means "to burden" (using ~be-~ to place a load upon), ~entlasten~ removes the load. This elegant ~be-~/~ent-~ mirror pair â€” burdening and unburdening â€” is a productive pattern in German.
en.entnehmen:
From Middle High German ~entnأ«men~, from Old High German ~intneman~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~ent-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, ~ent-~ conveys separation, removal, or extraction â€” taking something away from where it was.

~Entnehmen~ means "to take out, to remove, to extract" in formal registers â€” ~Bitte Karte entnehmen~ ("Please remove card") is the phrase every German ATM user knows by heart. Beyond the physical sense, ~entnehmen~ also means "to learn, to conclude, to gather" from a source â€” ~Ihrem Brief entnehme ich, dass...~ ("From your letter I gather that..."). This abstract extension treats information as something physically extracted from a container (the letter, the document, the silence). The derived noun ~Entnahme~ means "removal, withdrawal, extraction" â€” ~Blutentnahme~ ("blood draw") and ~Probeentnahme~ ("sample extraction") are standard medical and scientific terms. The ~ent-~ prefix's separative force makes ~entnehmen~ more formal and precise than simple ~herausnehmen~ ("to take out"), lending it a clinical or bureaucratic register. Cognate with Dutch ~ontnemen~ ("to deprive of, to take away").
en.entscheiden:
From Middle High German ~entscheiden~, from Old High German ~intskeiden~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~scheiden~:

- ~scheiden~: From MHG ~scheiden~, from OHG ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~skey-~ ("to split, dissect"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheiden~, West Frisian ~skiede~, English ~shed~, Irish ~scian~ ("knife"), Ancient Greek ~skhأ­zإچ~ ("to split"), and Sanskrit ~cyati~ ("he cuts off").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The semantic development of ~entscheiden~ â€” from "to cut apart, separate" to "to decide" â€” parallels Latin ~dؤ“cؤ«dere~ (literally "to cut off," from ~caedere~, "to cut"), which also yielded the English word ~decide~. Two languages, one metaphor: a decision is a cut that divides options apart. Cognate with Dutch ~ontscheiden~, Low German ~entscheden~.
en.entschlieأںen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~schlieأںen~:

- ~schlieأںen~: From MHG ~slieب‌en~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sleutanؤ…~, *~slإ«tanؤ…~ ("to lock, shut"). The PIE origin is uncertain; traditionally connected to *~(s)klewd-~ ("to lock"), from *~(s)klehâ‚‚w-~ ("nail, hook, pin, key"), from *~(s)kel-~ ("to crook, bend"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~slإ«tan~, Dutch ~sluiten~, English ~slot~.
- ~ent-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix carries a sense of reversal, undoing, or decisive separation.

The literal sense is "to un-lock" â€” to open a decision that was previously closed. The reflexive ~sich entschlieأںen~ means "to decide, to resolve, to make up one's mind," and the noun ~Entschluss~ ("decision, resolution") is the standard word for a firm resolve. The metaphor is striking: a decision is imagined as a lock being opened, releasing a course of action. The base verb ~schlieأںen~ appears in an extraordinary range of compounds: ~beschlieأںen~ ("to decide, to resolve"), ~abschlieأںen~ ("to lock, to conclude"), ~einschlieأںen~ ("to lock in, to include"), ~ausschlieأںen~ ("to lock out, to exclude"). The English cognate ~slot~ â€” a narrow opening â€” preserves the physical sense of the lock mechanism. Latin ~clؤپvis~ ("key"), whence English ~clavicle~ and ~conclave~, may be a distant cousin if the traditional PIE etymology is correct.
en.entschuldigen:
From Middle High German ~entschuldigen~ ("to absolve, to exonerate"). Surface analysis: ~ent-~ + ~schuldig~ ("guilty") + ~-en~, literally "to de-guilt":

- ~schuldig~: From ~Schuld~ ("guilt, debt, fault"). ~Schuld~ from MHG ~schult~, from OHG ~sculd~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skuldi~, from Proto-Germanic *~skuldإچ~, from PIE *~skel-~ ("to owe, to be obliged"). Cognate with obsolete English ~shild~ ("debt, fault, guilt"), Dutch ~schuld~, and English ~shall~ (which originally expressed obligation: "I shall" = "I owe it").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The prefix conveys removal or reversal.

The verb means "to excuse, to apologize" â€” literally "to un-guilt." The reflexive ~sich entschuldigen~ ("to excuse oneself, to apologize") is one of the most common polite expressions in German: ~Entschuldigung!~ ("Excuse me!"). The MHG meaning was stronger: "to absolve, to exonerate," a legal and religious term for removing guilt. Over the centuries, the meaning softened from juridical absolution to social courtesy. The connection between ~Schuld~ ("guilt") and ~Schuld~ ("debt") is not coincidental â€” they are the same word, reflecting the ancient equation of moral guilt with financial obligation. English ~shall~ descends from the same root, preserving the original sense of obligation, and Gothic ~skulan~ ("to owe, to be obligated") confirms the shared Germanic ancestry.
en.entsorgen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~sorgen~, a denominal derivation via the noun ~Sorge~ ("care, worry"):

- ~sorgen~: From MHG ~sorgen~, from OHG ~sorgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~swergت°-~ ("to watch over, to worry; to be ill, to suffer"). The noun ~Sorge~ ("care, worry, sorrow") is cognate with English ~sorrow~, Dutch ~zorg~, Danish ~sorg~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The prefix conveys removal.

The verb means "to dispose of (waste)" â€” literally "to de-worry," to remove the object of concern. The word is strikingly modern: although its MHG ancestor ~entsorgen~ originally meant "to free from worry," the old meaning fell out of use, and the modern waste-disposal sense emerged only in the 1980s, during the environmental movement. Some commentators have claimed ~entsorgen~ originated as Nazi-era bureaucratic euphemism, but the evidence points instead to its independent reinvention in postwar environmental discourse. The irony is rich: a word that once meant "to relieve anxiety" now refers to the very activity â€” waste management â€” that causes considerable anxiety in environmentally conscious Germany, a country that takes its ~Mأ¼lltrennung~ ("waste sorting") with legendary seriousness.
en.entspannen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~spannen~:

- ~spannen~: From MHG ~spannen~, from OHG ~spannan~, from Proto-Germanic *~spannanؤ…~, originally a strong verb of the seventh class. The long ~-nn-~ comes from an earlier form *~spanwanؤ…-~. Perhaps ultimately an ablaut form related to *~spinnanؤ…~ ("to spin"), or alternatively from PIE *~spend-~ ("to stretch"), comparing Lithuanian ~spؤ™sti~ ("to snare") and Latvian ~spanda~ ("strap"). Cognate with English ~span~ and ~spin~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~ ("away, against"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Here the prefix acts as a reversal: undoing the tension.

The literal sense is "to un-tension," i.e., to release from a stretched or strained state, yielding "to relax, to ease." The word works both transitively (~einen Bogen entspannen~, "to unstring a bow") and reflexively (~sich entspannen~, "to relax"). The physical metaphor of releasing a taut bowstring or spring extends naturally to psychological relaxation. English ~span~ (a unit of measurement based on the stretched hand) is the closest cognate, preserving the root sense of stretching. The compound ~Entspannung~ ("relaxation, detente") gained particular political currency during the Cold War as the German equivalent of ~detente~ -- and indeed, French ~detente~ is itself a structural parallel, from ~des-~ ("un-") + ~tendre~ ("to stretch"), from Latin ~tendere~, making it a twin of ~entspannen~ built from completely different etymological material.
en.entsprechen:
From Middle High German ~entsprechen~ (implied), from Old High German ~intsprehhإچn~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprأ«chen~, from OHG ~sprأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanؤ…~, probably from PIE *~spregت°-~ ("to crackle"). The semantic development from "to crackle, make noise" to "to speak" parallels the evolution seen in many languages where words for speech derive from words for sound. Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The compound's semantic development â€” from "to speak in response to" to "to correspond to, match, comply with" â€” reflects a characteristic use of ~ent-~ to convey a reciprocal or countering relationship. ~Einer Sache entsprechen~ ("to comply with something") and ~dem Bild entsprechen~ ("to match the image") both capture this sense of meeting or answering to something. Cognate with Dutch ~overeenkomen~ (the functional equivalent, though formed differently).
en.entstehen:
From Middle High German ~entstؤ“n~, ~entstؤپn~, from Old High German ~intstantan~, ~intstantؤ“n~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From MHG ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from OHG ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The compound's semantic development from "to stand away/forth" to "to come into being, arise" reflects how the ~ent-~ prefix here conveys emergence or inception rather than its more common sense of removal.
en.enttأ¤uschen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~tأ¤uschen~ ("to deceive"). Coined in the 19th century as a German replacement for the French loanwords ~desabusiren~ (from French ~dأ©sabuser~) and ~detrompiren~ (from French ~dأ©tromper~):

- ~tأ¤uschen~: From MHG ~tiuschen~, derived from ~tإ«schen~ ("to exchange surreptitiously"), related to ~tauschen~ ("to trade, to exchange"). The semantic development was "to swap" > "to trick someone into a trade" > "to deceive" (DWDS).
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The prefix conveys reversal.

The verb means "to disappoint" â€” but its literal, etymological meaning is "to un-deceive," to tear away a deception and reveal the truth. This is the opposite of what "disappoint" feels like: one would expect "un-deceiving" to be positive (and in fact, the original 19th-century sense was neutral â€” simply "to free from an illusion"). Over time, however, the word shifted from describing the removal of delusion to describing the emotional pain that follows when reality falls short of expectation. The underlying ~tأ¤uschen~ ("to deceive") derives from ~tauschen~ ("to exchange"), with the semantic pathway "to exchange" > "to palm off, to trick into a swap" > "to deceive generally." This connection between trading and trickery is ancient and cross-linguistic: English ~trick~ may derive from Old French ~trichier~ ("to cheat"), and Latin ~dolus~ means both "cunning" and "fraud."
en.entwerfen:
From Middle High German ~entwerfen~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~werfen~:

- ~werfen~: From MHG ~wأ«rfen~, from OHG ~werfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~werpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~werpanؤ…~, from PIE *~wergت·-~ ("to bend, spin, warp"). The ~-p-~ in the Germanic form likely arose after resonants in words beginning with labial consonants. Cognate with English ~warp~ (originally "to throw"), Dutch ~werpen~, and via Greek ~rhembإچ~ ("to turn in circles"), the source of English ~rhombus~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~ ("away, against"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of reversal, removal, or inception to the base verb.

The literal sense is "to throw out" or "to cast forth," and the semantic development to "to design, to draft, to sketch" reflects the idea of casting forth an idea into physical form. Cognate with Dutch ~ontwerpen~ ("to design"), which preserves the same prefix-root combination. The relationship between ~werfen~ ("to throw") and English ~warp~ illustrates a dramatic semantic divergence from a shared ancestor: German kept the concrete "throw" meaning, while English ~warp~ shifted from "to throw" to "to twist, to distort" (with the weaving sense of "warp threads" reflecting the PIE meaning "to bend"). The noun ~Entwurf~ ("design, draft, blueprint") is the corresponding deverbal noun, parallel to how English ~draft~ originally meant "something drawn or pulled."
en.entwickeln:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~wickeln~. Calque of French ~dأ©velopper~.

- ~wickeln~: From MHG ~wickeln~ ("to wind, wrap around"), a denominative of ~wickel~, from OHG ~wickili~ ("fibres wound around the distaff"), from Proto-Germanic *~wikanؤ…~ ("to bend"), from PIE *~weg-~ ("to weave"). Related to ~Wickel~ ("anything wrapped, especially a cloth around a poultice"). The OHG noun is a diminutive of ~wiohha~ ("bundle of fibres, wick"), cognate with Dutch ~wiek~, English ~wick~, and Latin ~velum~ ("cloth, veil").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The semantic development from "to unwrap" to "to develop" parallels French ~dأ©velopper~ (from ~des-~ + Old French ~voloper~, "to wrap"), which ~entwickeln~ was coined to translate. Both languages arrived at "develop" via the metaphor of unfolding something hidden.
en.entziehen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with Low German ~tehn~, Gothic ~tiuhan~, Old English ~teon~, and Old Norse ~toga~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~and-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix conveys separation, removal, or reversal.

The compound literally means "to pull away from," yielding the modern senses of "to withdraw, deprive, extract." The reflexive form ~sich entziehen~ means "to evade, to escape from," adding a layer of agency to the withdrawal. The PIE root *~dewk-~ is remarkably productive: it yielded Latin ~ducere~ ("to lead"), giving English ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, ~reduce~, ~educate~, and ~deduce~. English ~tie~ is also from the same root, via the idea of pulling tight. English ~tug~ and ~tow~ are further relatives. The German noun ~Entzug~ ("withdrawal") is widely known in the compound ~Entzugserscheinungen~ ("withdrawal symptoms"), one of those long German compound nouns that compresses into a single word what English needs a phrase to express. The obsolete English verb ~tee~ ("to draw, lead") was a direct cognate of ~ziehen~ that did not survive.
en.erarbeiten:
~er-~ + ~arbeiten~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From MHG ~arbeiten~, from OHG ~arabeitإچn~, ~arbeitإچn~, from OHG ~arabeit~ ("labor, hardship"), from Proto-West Germanic *~arbaiأ¾i~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidiz~ ("work, hardship"), possibly from PIE *~hâ‚ƒerbت°-~ ("to change status, to orphan").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of achievement through effort or completion of a process.

~Erarbeiten~ means "to achieve through work, to develop through effort, to work out." The ~er-~ prefix transforms the simple act of working into the completed result of that work â€” one does not merely ~arbeitet~, one ~erarbeitet~ a result, a plan, a competence. The underlying etymological image is almost poignant: if ~arbeiten~ indeed descends from a root meaning "orphan, hardship" (as suggested by the connection to PIE *~hâ‚ƒerbت°-~ and Gothic ~arbaiأ¾s~, "toil, distress"), then ~erarbeiten~ captures the full arc of labor as suffering that yields an outcome. The reflexive ~sich etwas erarbeiten~ ("to earn something through hard work") emphasizes the personal investment required. There is no shortcut implied â€” unlike ~verdienen~ ("to earn," which can be passive), ~erarbeiten~ insists on active, sustained effort.
en.erbauen:
From Middle High German ~erbouwen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~buwen~, from OHG ~buan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~buwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~buwanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bhuH-~ ("to be, become, grow, dwell"). The semantic journey from "to dwell" to "to build" reflects the Germanic settlers' deepening attachment to fixed homesteads. Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different branch), Dutch ~bouwen~, and English ~be~, ~been~ (via the same PIE root).
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix intensifies the base verb, lending ~erbauen~ the elevated sense of "to construct, to edify." In modern German, the reflexive ~sich erbauen~ means "to be edified" in a spiritual or emotional sense, a meaning that grew from the literal act of building churches and monuments.
en.erbringen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~ ("to bring"), probably from a Pre-Germanic compound *~bت°renkج‚-~, combining PIE *~bت°er-~ ("to carry") and *~hâ‚پnekج‚-~ ("to bring, reach"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms "to bring" into "to bring about, to produce, to yield" -- conveying the successful result of bringing. It appears most often in formal or bureaucratic contexts: ~einen Beweis erbringen~ ("to furnish proof"), ~eine Leistung erbringen~ ("to render a service"), ~ein Opfer erbringen~ ("to make a sacrifice"). The verb ~bringen~ itself is unusual among Germanic verbs in having a mixed weak-and-strong past tense (~brachte~, ~gebracht~), a pattern unique in Proto-Germanic.
en.erfahren:
From Middle High German ~ervarn~, from Old High German ~irfaran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€faran~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~fahren~:

- ~fahren~: From MHG ~varn~, ~varen~, from OHG ~faran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~faran~, from Proto-Germanic *~faranؤ…~, from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). A class VI strong verb.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The completive ~er-~ prefix transforms the base sense of "to travel" into "to find out, learn through experience" â€” one discovers by traveling through, by undergoing a journey. This semantic link between physical travel and the acquisition of knowledge is beautifully preserved in the noun ~Erfahrung~ ("experience"), literally "a traveling through." Cognate with Old English ~ؤپfaran~ (whence obsolete English ~afare~, "to depart, set out").
en.erfassen:
From ~er-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~ ("to fetch, hold"), ultimately cognate with English ~fetch~. The connection is one of the more surprising Germanic pairs: both descend from a root meaning "to get, obtain."
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This completive prefix transforms the act of grasping into a fully achieved comprehension or registration. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound means "to grasp, to seize, to comprehend, to record (data)" â€” the ~er-~ prefix driving the simple act of grabbing through to completion: one doesn't merely touch but fully takes hold, in the physical sense (~einen Dieb erfassen~, "to apprehend a thief") and the cognitive sense (~eine Idee erfassen~, "to grasp an idea"). In modern administrative and statistical language, ~erfassen~ has become the standard term for recording and registering data â€” "to capture" in the technical sense, as in ~Daten erfassen~. The related ~Fassung~ ("composure; version") and ~unfassbar~ ("incomprehensible," literally "ungraspable") show the root's wide idiomatic reach. Cognate with Dutch ~vatten~.
en.erfinden:
From Middle High German ~ervinden~, from Old High German ~irfindan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€finأ¾an~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~finأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge"). Cognate with English ~find~, Dutch ~vinden~, Danish ~finde~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb.

The semantic journey from "to find completely" to "to invent" is a remarkable one: ~erfinden~ moved from the idea of thorough discovery to the creation of something entirely new. The PIE root *~pent-~ originally meant "to tread a path," so ~finden~ traces back to the notion of coming upon something while walking, and ~erfinden~ extends that to arriving at something that did not exist before. The noun ~Erfindung~ ("invention") and ~Erfinder~ ("inventor") are among the most productive derivatives in modern German.
en.erfolgen:
From Middle High German ~erfolgen~, from Old High German ~irfolgؤ“n~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~folgen~:

- ~folgen~: From MHG ~volgen~, from OHG ~folgؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~folgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~fulgؤپnؤ…~. Cognate with Dutch ~volgen~, Low German ~folgen~, English ~follow~, Danish ~fأ¸lge~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound's primary modern sense â€” "to occur, to result, to ensue" â€” reflects the completive force of ~er-~: an outcome that follows through to its conclusion. The cognate relationship between ~folgen~ and English ~follow~ is not immediately obvious in Modern German spelling, but the OHG form ~folgؤ“n~ makes the shared Proto-Germanic origin transparent. Cognate with Dutch ~evolgen~ (archaic).
en.erfordern:
From Middle High German ~ervordern~, from Old High German ~irfordarإچn~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~fordern~:

- ~fordern~: From MHG ~vordern~, from OHG ~fordarإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~furأ¾rإچnؤ…~, from Proto-Germanic *~furأ¾rإچnؤ…~, from *~furأ¾raz~ ("further, forward"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to cross, go through").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes the full extent of what is demanded.

The distinction between ~fordern~ ("to demand") and ~erfordern~ ("to require") is subtle but important: ~fordern~ implies an agent making a demand (~der Chef fordert Pأ¼nktlichkeit~ â€” "the boss demands punctuality"), while ~erfordern~ implies an impersonal, situational necessity (~die Situation erfordert Geduld~ â€” "the situation requires patience"). The ~er-~ prefix strips away the personal element, transforming a demand into a requirement â€” from something one person asks of another to something a situation asks of everyone. Cognate with Dutch ~vereisen~ (a different compound with the same meaning).
en.erforschen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~forschen~:

- ~forschen~: From MHG ~vorschen~, from OHG ~forscإچn~ ("to ask, to inquire, to investigate"), from Proto-West Germanic *~fraskإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fraskإچnؤ…~, likely related to *~frehnanؤ…~ ("to ask"), from PIE *~preل¸±-~ ("to ask, to request"). Cognate with Latin ~poscإچ~ ("I demand"), ~precor~ ("I pray"), Sanskrit ~prج¥cchأ،ti~ ("he asks"), and English ~pray~ (via Old French ~preier~, from Latin ~precؤپrؤ«~).
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds the sense of thorough completion â€” researching something to the end.

~Erforschen~ means "to explore, to investigate, to research thoroughly" â€” ~den Weltraum erforschen~ ("to explore outer space"), ~die Ursachen erforschen~ ("to investigate the causes"). The noun ~Forscher~ ("researcher, explorer") and ~Forschung~ ("research") are central to German academic and scientific vocabulary. The PIE root *~preل¸±-~ ("to ask") connects scientific inquiry with prayer and supplication in a profound etymological circle: to research and to pray both begin with asking. The semantic shift from "to ask questions" to "to conduct systematic investigation" occurred within German itself during the medieval period, as ~forschen~ moved from casual inquiry to the structured questioning that defines the scientific method.
en.erfreuen:
From Middle High German ~ervrأ¶uwen~, ~ervrأ¶wen~, from Old High German ~irfrewen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~freuen~:

- ~freuen~: From MHG ~vrأ¶uwen~, ~vreuwen~, from OHG ~frewen~, ~frouwen~, derived from OHG ~frإچ~, ~frao~ ("glad"). The adjective ~froh~ descends from Proto-Germanic *~frawaz~ ("quick, glad"). Equivalent to ~froh~ + ~-en~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb.

The ~er-~ prefix intensifies ~freuen~ ("to please, to be glad") into a sense of causing or experiencing full delight. While ~sich freuen~ means "to be happy," ~erfreuen~ means "to delight" someone else, or in its reflexive form ~sich erfreuen~ with the genitive, "to enjoy" something. English ~frolic~ is a distant cousin, borrowed from Dutch ~vrolijk~ ("merry"), which shares the same Proto-Germanic root *~frawaz~. The Old High German vowel ~أ¶~/~أ¶u~ in ~vrأ¶uwen~ reflects the Germanic umlaut triggered by the ~-j-~ in the verb derivation, a phonological process that left its fingerprints all over German's vowel system.
en.erfأ¼llen:
From Middle High German ~ervأ¼llen~, from Old High German ~irfullen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€fullijan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~fأ¼llen~:

- ~fأ¼llen~: From MHG ~vأ¼llen~, from OHG ~fullen~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~fullaz~ ("full"), from PIE *~plehâ‚پ-~ ("to fill"). Cognate with Dutch ~vullen~, English ~fill~, Danish ~fylde~, Swedish ~fylla~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or achievement. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix gives ~erfأ¼llen~ its characteristic sense of complete, achieving fulfillment â€” not merely filling, but filling to completion, satisfying entirely. A wish, a promise, a quota: all ~erfأ¼llt~ when fully accomplished. The PIE root *~plehâ‚پ-~ is also the ancestor of English ~full~, Latin ~plؤ“nus~ ("full"), and Greek ~plل¸—rؤ“s~ ("full"). Cognate with Dutch ~vervullen~.
en.ergeben:
From Middle High German ~ergeben~, from Old High German ~irgeban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzgebanؤ…~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with Dutch ~geven~, English ~give~, Swedish ~giva~/~ge~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic range of ~ergeben~ is notably wide: "to yield" (literally, to give out completely), "to result in" (the calculation yields a result), and â€” as the reflexive ~sich ergeben~ â€” "to surrender" or "to turn out to be."
