The sense of ~ergeben~ as an adjective ("devoted, submissive") derives from the same completive force: one who has wholly given themselves over. Cognate with obsolete English ~agive~, Dutch ~overgeven~.
en.ergreifen:
From Middle High German ~ergreifen~, from Old High German ~irgreifإچn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grؤ«fen~, from OHG ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~, Dutch ~grijpen~, Swedish ~gripa~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Adds a sense of successfully completing the action.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms "to grasp" into "to seize successfully, to take hold of" -- implying a completed act of grasping. The verb has developed rich figurative meanings: ~Maأںnahmen ergreifen~ ("to take measures"), ~die Flucht ergreifen~ ("to take flight"), ~einen Beruf ergreifen~ ("to take up a profession"), and ~ergriffen sein~ ("to be deeply moved"). This last sense -- emotional seizing -- shows how physical grasping became a metaphor for being seized by emotion. The English cognate is the humble ~grip~, which shares the same Proto-Germanic ancestor *~grؤ«panؤ…~. The word ~gripe~ (originally "to grasp," now "to complain") is another English sibling, its semantic shift from clutching to complaining perhaps reflecting the clenched frustration of one who gripes.
en.ergأ¤nzen:
From ~er-~ + ~ganz~ with the verbal suffix ~-en~, producing the original form *~ergأ¤nzen~ ("to make whole, to complete, to supplement"):

- ~ganz~: From MHG ~ganz~, from OHG ~ganz~ ("whole, sound, healthy, complete"), from Proto-West Germanic *~gant~ ("whole, sound"). The ultimate PIE source is uncertain; the form is attested only in West Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~gans~ (archaic "whole"), Low German ~ganz~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion to a derived verb.

The ~-أ¤-~ in modern ~ergأ¤nzen~ is the result of umlaut: the verb was originally denominalized as *~gأ¤nzen~ (with fronted vowel from adjectival inflection), then prefixed with ~er-~. The completive prefix is semantically apt â€” ~ergأ¤nzen~ does not merely add, but adds in a way that makes something whole. The noun ~Ergأ¤nzung~ ("supplement, complement") is a standard term in mathematics and linguistics.
en.erhalten:
From Middle High German ~erhalten~, from Old High German ~irhaltan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~. Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic development from "to hold (up/out)" to "to receive" and "to preserve" reflects the completive force of the ~er-~ prefix. Cognate with Dutch ~erhouden~ (archaic).
en.erheben:
From Middle High German ~erheben~, from Old High German ~irheffen~, ~irheven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzhabjanؤ…~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~heben~. Cognate with Middle Dutch ~erheffen~ (whence Dutch ~verheffen~), Old Saxon ~ؤپhebbian~, Old English ~ؤپhebban~, Gothic ~ushafjan~.

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, ~heven~, from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to seize, take hold of"). A class VI strong verb; the past tense ~hob~ and participle ~gehoben~ descend from the strong ablaut paradigm. Cognate with English ~heave~, Dutch ~heffen~, Danish ~hأ¦ve~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). The completive prefix reinforces the inherent upward thrust of ~heben~. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The curious shift from "to seize" (PIE) to "to lift" (Germanic) reflects the same conceptual move as in English ~heave~ â€” the act of taking hold of something is inseparable from lifting it. ~Erheben~ takes this a stage further: the ~er-~ prefix pushes ~heben~ to completion, yielding meanings that span the physical ("to raise") and the institutional ("to levy a tax," "to lodge a complaint," "to elevate to nobility"). A tax is ~erhoben~; a glass is ~erhoben~ in a toast; a voice is ~erhoben~ in protest â€” all are acts of lifting. The derived noun ~Erhebung~ covers both a geographic rise (an elevation in a landscape) and a political one (an insurrection, literally a "raising up"). A notable cognate surprise: English ~heave~ and German ~heben~ diverge from the same Proto-West Germanic root, making a sailor heaving a rope the etymological twin of a German speaker raising their glass. Cognate with Dutch ~verheffen~, English ~heave~.
en.erholen:
From Middle High German ~erholn~, from Old High German ~irholإچn~, ~irhalإچn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~holen~:

- ~holen~: From MHG ~holen~, from OHG ~holإچn~ ("to fetch, get"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~halإچnؤ…~ ("to call, fetch, summon"), from PIE *~kelhâ‚پ-~ ("to call, summon").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix carries a restorative sense â€” fetching oneself back from a depleted state.

~Sich erholen~ means "to recover, to recuperate" â€” almost exclusively used as a reflexive verb. One ~erholt sich~ from illness, exhaustion, a shock, or a financial setback. The literal image is fetching oneself back â€” summoning one's strength out of depletion and back into wholeness. The noun ~Erholung~ ("rest, recreation, recovery") is the standard word for leisure in the restorative sense: ~Erholungsgebiet~ ("recreation area"), ~Erholungsurlaub~ ("recuperative vacation"). The progression from "to fetch" (~holen~) to "to fetch back" (~erholen~) to "to recover oneself" (~sich erholen~) is a beautiful example of how German prefixes can redirect a concrete physical verb toward an abstract psychological process.
en.erhأ¶hen:
From Middle High German ~erhإ“hen~, ~erhإ“gen~, from Old High German ~irhإچhen~, ~irhohen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€hauhijan~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~hoch~ + ~-en~:

- ~hoch~: From MHG ~hإچch~, from OHG ~hإچh~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauh~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauhaz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kewk-~, a suffixed form of *~kew-~ ("to swell, be strong, be vigorous"). Cognate with Dutch ~hoog~, English ~high~, Swedish ~hأ¶g~, Danish ~hأ¸j~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix here reinforces the inherent upward directionality of the root.

The irregular stem alternation in modern ~hoch~ vs. inflected ~hoh-~ (as in ~hأ¶her~, ~Hoheit~) reflects a Middle High German sound change: OHG ~-h-~ became /x/ in coda position but /h/ elsewhere, and most such alternations were later leveled â€” except in ~hoch~, which Standard German has preserved in isolation. Cognate with Old Dutch ~irhإچen~, Middle Low German ~erhب«gen~.
en.erinnern:
From Middle High German ~erinnern~, ~erinneren~, from Old High German ~innarإچn~ ("to make someone perceive inwardly") with the later addition of the completive ~er-~ prefix. Compound of ~er-~ + ~innern~, from ~inner~ ("inner"):

- ~innern~: Denominative of ~inner~ ("inner"), from OHG ~innar~, from Proto-Germanic *~inn-~, from PIE *~hâ‚پen~ ("in"). The ~-ar~ suffix is a comparative formation meaning "more inward."
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or achievement. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic structure is thus "to bring something fully inward (into the mind)" â€” the ~er-~ prefix marking completion of the act of interiorizing. The reflexive ~sich erinnern an~ ("to remember") became the dominant construction in Early New High German; the transitive non-reflexive ~jemanden an etwas erinnern~ ("to remind someone of something") preserves the older causative sense of "to make someone bring inward." English ~remind~ and ~remember~ cover the same semantic territory through entirely different routes. Cognate with Dutch ~herinneren~ (with the parallel ~her-~ prefix), English ~inner~.
en.erkennen:
From Middle High German ~erkennen~, from Old High German ~irkennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€kannijan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~kennen~:

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~kannijanؤ…~, a causative of *~kunnanؤ…~ with the suffix *~-janؤ…~. *~kunnanؤ…~ derives from PIE *~اµnج¥nأ©hâ‚ƒti~, from *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The completive force of ~er-~ transforms the simple acquaintance of ~kennen~ ("to know, be acquainted with") into the achievement of recognition: ~erkennen~ is "to come to know, to recognize." This distinction â€” between ongoing familiarity and the moment of recognition â€” has legal weight in German: ~erkennen~ is the verb used when courts and authorities make formal determinations (~ein Urteil erkennen~). Cognate with Dutch ~erkennen~.
en.erklأ¤ren:
From Middle High German ~erklأ¦ren~, from ~er-~ + ~klأ¤ren~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~klأ¤ren~:

- ~klأ¤ren~: From MHG ~klأ¦ren~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective ~klar~ ("clear"). ~klar~ descends from MHG ~klؤپr~, ~clؤپr~, borrowed from Middle Low German ~klؤپr~ or Middle Dutch ~claer~ and reinforced by Old French ~cler~, both from Latin ~clؤپrus~ ("bright, clear, famous"). Latin ~clؤپrus~ is related to ~clؤپmؤپre~ ("to call out") and ultimately from PIE *~kelhâ‚پ-~ ("to call, shout").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic development from "to make clear" (a liquid, a room) to "to explain" (a concept) follows the same metaphor in many languages â€” English ~clarify~ and ~explain~ both draw on light and space as cognitive metaphors. Cognate with Dutch ~verklaren~, English ~clarify~ (from the same Latin root).
en.erkranken:
A denominative verb from ~krank~ ("sick") with the prefix ~er-~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~krank~ + ~-en~:

- ~krank~: From MHG ~krank~, from OHG *~krank~, from Proto-West Germanic *~krank~, from Proto-Germanic *~krangaz~, *~krankaz~ ("crooked, weak"). Cognate with English ~crank~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix marks the onset or inception of a state -- becoming sick rather than merely being sick.

The semantic shift from "crooked, bent" to "sick" parallels the metaphor found in many languages of illness as distortion or weakness. English ~crank~ preserves the original spatial meaning of something bent or twisted, while German generalized the sense to physical debility. The ~er-~ prefix here functions inceptively: ~krank sein~ is "to be sick," but ~erkranken~ is "to fall ill" -- the transition into sickness.
en.erkunden:
From ~er-~ + ~kunden~, a denominative verb from ~kund~ ("known, manifest"). Compound of:

- ~kund~: From MHG ~kunt~, from OHG ~kund~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunأ¾~, from Proto-Germanic *~kunأ¾az~, originally the past participle of *~kunnanؤ…~ ("to know"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). Cognate with English ~couth~ (archaic past participle of "can/know"), and related to English ~can~, ~know~, ~ken~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds a sense of thorough investigation â€” making something ~fully~ known.

~erkunden~ means "to explore, to reconnoiter, to investigate" â€” to make the unknown ~known~. The military sense of reconnaissance (~Erkundung~) is prominent, but the verb also applies to exploring a city or investigating a matter. The same PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ is one of the most productive in Indo-European, yielding Latin ~gnإچscere~ / ~cognإچscere~ (whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~), Greek ~gignل¹“skein~ (whence ~gnosis~, ~diagnosis~), and Sanskrit ~jأ±ؤپ-~ (whence ~jأ±ؤپna~, "knowledge"). The English word ~uncouth~ preserves the negative form of this same participle â€” literally "unknown," hence "unfamiliar, strange."
en.erlangen:
From Middle High German ~erlangen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~langen~:

- ~langen~: From MHG ~langen~, from OHG ~langؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~langإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~langإچnؤ…~. Often connected to *~langaz~ ("long"), with semantic development from "long length" to "reach, extend" to "wish to reach." Cognate with English ~long~ (in the archaic sense "to long for, to desire").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix sharpens the reaching sense of ~langen~ into one of successful attainment: "to reach for" becomes "to achieve, obtain, gain." While ~langen~ in modern German is informal and chiefly southern (~mir langt's~ = "I've had enough"), ~erlangen~ remains firmly in the formal register -- one ~erlangt~ a degree, citizenship, or recognition. Not to be confused with the Bavarian city of ~Erlangen~, whose name derives from a river name rather than from this verb.
en.erlassen:
From Middle High German ~erlؤپzen~, from Old High German ~irlؤپzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uzlؤپtan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from OHG ~lؤپzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from Proto-Indo-European *~leh1d-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with English ~let~, Dutch ~laten~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of completion or result.

The compound literally means "to let out" or "to release completely." Its modern meanings span from "to issue, decree" (a law is "released" into the world) to "to remit, waive" (a debt is "let go of"). This semantic duality -- both promulgating and pardoning -- springs from the same root idea of releasing something from one's control.
en.erlauben:
From Middle High German ~erlouben~, from Old High German ~irlouben~, ~arlouben~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€laubijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzlaubijanؤ…~. Compound of ~er-~ + the extinct root *~laubijanؤ…~:

- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion â€” "allowing fully."
- *~laubijanؤ…~: From Proto-Germanic *~laubijanؤ…~ ("to allow, approve of, commend"), from *~laubإچ~ ("permission"). From PIE *~lewbت°-~ ("to desire, love, praise, care for").

The root *~laubijanؤ…~ is extinct as an independent verb in modern German â€” it survives only inside compounds like ~erlauben~ and ~glauben~ ("to believe"). The believe connection is one of the most surprising in the Germanic lexicon: ~glauben~ descends from *~galaubijanؤ…~ ("to praise, commend, allow, believe"), which is the same *~laubijanؤ…~ root with the collective prefix *~ga-~. The semantic path runs from "to care for, value" â†’ "to hold dear" â†’ "to trust" â†’ "to believe." English ~believe~ follows the same route (from Old English ~ؤ،elؤ«efan~, from *~galaubijanؤ…~). German ~erlauben~ ("to permit") and English ~believe~ are therefore distant cousins: both descend from PIE *~lewbت°-~, one through the "granting approval" branch, the other through the "placing trust" branch. The English cognate of ~erlauben~ specifically is the archaic verb ~leave~ (as in "by your leave"), meaning "permission." Cognate with Old English ~ؤپlؤ«efan~, ~ؤپlب³fan~ ("to allow"), Dutch ~verloven~ (archaic "to permit").
en.erleben:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~leben~:

- ~leben~: From MHG ~lأ«ben~, from OHG ~lebؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~libbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~libjanؤ…~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to leave, cling, linger"). Cognate with Dutch ~leven~, English ~live~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound's semantic development from "to live through (something)" to "to experience" reflects the completive force of ~er-~: what one fully lives through, one has experienced. The noun ~Erlebnis~ ("experience, adventure") derives from this verb. ~Erleben~ conveys a more vivid, personal, emotionally charged experience than the parallel ~Erfahrung~ (from ~erfahren~), which emphasizes accumulated knowledge â€” a distinction felt by every German speaker.
en.erledigen:
From ~er-~ + ~ledig~ + ~-igen~, a deadjectival verb meaning "to free from, to settle, to take care of":

- ~ledig~: From MHG ~lأ«dic~, ~lأ«dec~ ("free, unoccupied, unmarried"), from OHG *~lidig~, from Proto-Germanic *~liأ¾ugaz~ ("flexible, free"). Related to ~Glied~ ("limb, member") and ultimately to PIE *~leyH-~ ("to let go, release").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes finishing or resolving something fully.

~Erledigen~ means "to take care of, to settle, to finish off" â€” etymologically, "to make free," to resolve something so that it is no longer pending. The colloquial sense "to finish off, to exhaust" (~Ich bin erledigt~ â€” "I'm done for, I'm wiped out") extends the metaphor: what is ~erledigt~ is so thoroughly freed from obligation that it has no energy left. The base adjective ~ledig~ survives primarily in the sense "unmarried, single" (~ledig~ on official forms), preserving the older meaning of "free, unattached." The semantic path from "free" to "taken care of" is counterintuitive until you consider the German perspective: to ~erledigen~ a task is to free yourself from it. Cognate with Dutch ~afhandelen~ (a different compound with similar meaning).
en.erleichtern:
From ~er-~ + ~leicht~ + ~-ern~, a deadjectival verb meaning "to lighten, to ease, to relieve":

- ~leicht~: From OHG ~lؤ«ht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤ«ht~, from Proto-Germanic *~linhtaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚پlengت·ت°-~ ("light, not heavy"). Cognate with English ~light~ (not heavy), Dutch ~licht~, Danish ~let~.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes achieving the lightened state.

~Erleichtern~ means "to make easier, to relieve, to facilitate" â€” etymologically, "to make light." The reflexive ~sich erleichtern~ can mean "to relieve oneself" (a euphemism for urination), while the nominal ~Erleichterung~ ("relief") is one of German's most common emotional nouns: the feeling when a burden is lifted. The adjective ~leicht~ and its English cognate ~light~ descend from the same Proto-Germanic *~linhtaz~, which itself derives from PIE *~hâ‚پlengت·ت°-~ â€” making the connection between German ~erleichtern~ and the English phrase "to lighten the load" a genuine cognate parallel, not a coincidence. The ~-ern~ suffix (the same verbal derivation pattern seen in ~begeistern~, ~verbessern~) converts the adjective into a causative verb: "to cause to become light."
en.erleiden:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~leiden~:

- ~leiden~: From MHG ~lؤ«den~, from OHG ~lؤ«dan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤ«أ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to go, to travel"). Cognate with Dutch ~lijden~, English ~lithe~ (archaic: "to go"). The meaning shifted from "go, travel" through "endure" to "suffer," reinforced by MHG ~leiden~ ("to be sorry, to suffer"), from OHG ~leidؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laiأ¾ijan~, and the originally unrelated noun ~Leid~ ("sorrow"), cognate with English ~loath~.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of enduring a process to its completion â€” suffering through something fully.

~Erleiden~ means "to suffer, to sustain, to undergo" â€” always something negative: ~einen Verlust erleiden~ ("to suffer a loss"), ~eine Niederlage erleiden~ ("to sustain a defeat"). The ~er-~ prefix intensifies the suffering, marking it as a completed experience rather than an ongoing state. The semantic history of ~leiden~ is a remarkable case of contamination: Proto-Germanic *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ meant simply "to go, to travel," but the hardships of medieval travel â€” and the phonetic similarity to the unrelated *~laiأ¾az~ ("hateful, hostile," whence English ~loath~) â€” gradually pulled the verb from motion into misery. English preserves the original motion sense in the archaic ~lithe~ ("to go") and the word ~lithesome~ ("moving gracefully").
en.erlernen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~lernen~:

- ~lernen~: From MHG ~lernen~, from OHG ~lernؤ“n~, ~lirnؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liت€nؤ“n~, from a PIE mediopassive causative formation *~lis-nج¥hâ‚‚-tأ³r~ from the root *~leys-~ ("to trace, to track"). Cognate with English ~learn~ (from Old English ~leornian~). Related to German ~lehren~ ("to teach"), from Proto-Germanic *~laizijanؤ…~, the active causative of the same root.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion â€” reaching the end of a process.

The literal sense is "to learn completely" â€” to master, to acquire fully. While ~lernen~ means simply "to learn" (an ongoing process), ~erlernen~ emphasizes the completed achievement: ~eine Sprache erlernen~ means to master a language, not merely to study it. The PIE root *~leys-~ ("to trace, to track") reveals a profound metaphor: learning is tracking â€” following trails of knowledge, tracing patterns until they become familiar. This root also produced Proto-Germanic *~laizإچ~ ("teaching, lore"), whence Old English ~lؤپr~ (modern English ~lore~), and *~listiz~ ("art, cunning"), whence German ~List~ ("cunning, ruse") and English ~list~ (in the archaic sense of "craft"). The twin siblings ~lehren~ ("to teach") and ~lernen~ ("to learn") derive from the same root via different derivational paths: one causative ("to make someone track"), the other middle-voice ("to track for oneself").
en.erlأ¤utern:
From ~er-~ + ~lأ¤utern~, a deadjectival verb from ~lauter~ ("pure, clear"):

- ~lأ¤utern~: From MHG ~liutern~, ~lإ«tern~, from the adjective ~lإ«ter~ ("clean, clear, pure"), from OHG ~hlإ«tar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlإ«tr~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlإ«traz~ ("clear, pure"). Cognate with English ~luster~ (a distant relative via Latin, not a direct cognate).
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes bringing something to full clarity.

~Erlأ¤utern~ means "to explain, to elucidate, to clarify" â€” etymologically, "to make something pure/clear all the way through." The base adjective ~lauter~ survives in modern German with two distinct meanings: as an adjective meaning "pure, sincere" (~lautere Absichten~, "pure intentions") and as an adverb meaning "nothing but, sheer" (~lauter Unsinn~, "sheer nonsense"). The verb ~lأ¤utern~ itself means "to purify, to refine" (both physically and morally â€” one ~lأ¤utert~ gold or one's character), while the ~er-~ prefix shifts the meaning from physical purification to intellectual clarification. The derived noun ~Erlأ¤uterung~ ("explanation, commentary") is standard in academic and legal German. The old ~hl-~ initial cluster (OHG ~hlإ«tar~) was simplified to ~l-~ in Middle High German, just as in ~lachen~ from ~hlahjan~.
en.ermitteln:
From ~er-~ + ~Mittel~ + ~-n~, a denominal verb coined in Early New High German. The middle element is the noun ~Mittel~ ("means, medium, remedy"), from Middle High German ~mittel~, from Old High German ~mittil~, from Proto-Germanic *~midlؤ…~ ("middle"), cognate with Dutch ~middel~, English ~middle~.

- ~Mittel~: From the Proto-Germanic base *~medja-~ ("middle"), from PIE *~medhyo-~ ("middle"). The same PIE root underlies English ~mid~, ~middle~, Latin ~medius~ ("middle," source of ~medium~ and ~Mediterranean~), and Sanskrit ~mأ،dhya-~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). The completive prefix frames the use of means as arriving at a result â€” finding out, determining. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The verb's literal meaning is "to ascertain by means," and it specializes in investigation: detectives ~ermitteln~ facts, courts ~ermitteln~ evidence, statistics ~ermitteln~ values. The noun ~Ermittlung~ ("investigation, finding") is the standard legal and journalistic term for an inquiry. The semantic journey â€” from "middle" to "means" to "to investigate" â€” is anchored in the ancient intuition that the middle is the place where things are measured and weighed: a mediator stands in the middle, and the investigator who reaches the truth has found the measure of the matter. The cognate chain connecting German ~Mittel~ to English ~middle~ and Latin ~medium~ passes through the same PIE root *~medhyo-~, making ~ermitteln~ a distant relative of English ~mediate~ and ~immediate~. Cognate with Dutch ~vaststellen~ (conceptually parallel; no direct cognate for this compound).
en.ermأ¶glichen:
Denominal compound of ~er-~ + ~mأ¶glich~ + ~-en~. The adjective ~mأ¶glich~ derives from ~mأ¶gen~ + the adjectival suffix ~-lich~.

- ~mأ¶glich~: From ~mأ¶gen~ + ~-lich~. ~Mأ¶gen~ descends from MHG ~mugen~, OHG ~mugan~, Proto-West Germanic *~magan~, Proto-Germanic *~maganؤ…~, PIE *~megت°-~ ("to be able, have power").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound literally means "to make possible" â€” the ~er-~ prefix imparts a resultative sense ("to bring about the state of being ~mأ¶glich~"). The adjectival suffix ~-lich~ (cognate with English ~-ly~ and ~-like~) converts the modal verb into an adjective, before the verbal suffix ~-en~ reconverts it into a verb: ability â†’ possible â†’ to make possible. Cognate with Dutch ~mogelijk maken~.
en.ernennen:
From Middle High German ~ernennen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~nennen~:

- ~nennen~: From MHG ~nennen~, ~nemmen~, ~nemnen~, from OHG ~nemnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~namnijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~namnijanؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from *~namأ´~ ("name"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h1nomn~ ("name"). Cognate with English dialectal ~nemn~ ("to name"), Swedish ~namna~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of definitiveness or official result.

The compound literally means "to name out" or "to name forth," hence "to appoint, to designate." The act of naming has carried legal and political force since ancient times -- to name someone to a position was to confer authority. ~Nennen~ is simply "to call, to name," but ~ernennen~ elevates the act to an official appointment. The PIE root *~h1nomn~ is one of the most stable words in the language family, surviving nearly unchanged across thousands of years and dozens of languages.
en.erneuern:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~neu~ + ~-ern~:

- ~neu~: From MHG ~niuwe~, from OHG ~niuwi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~niwi~, from Proto-Germanic *~niwjaz~, from PIE *~nأ©wos~ ("new"). Cognate with English ~new~, Dutch ~nieuw~, Danish ~ny~, Latin ~novus~, Greek ~neos~, Sanskrit ~navas~.
- ~er-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here it conveys achievement or completion: making something fully new.

~Erneuern~ means "to renew, to renovate, to restore." The PIE root *~nأ©wos~ is one of the most widely preserved words in the Indo-European family -- virtually every daughter language retains a recognizable reflex: English ~new~, Latin ~novus~ (whence ~novel~, ~novice~, ~innovate~), Greek ~neos~ (whence ~neon~, ~neologism~), Welsh ~newydd~, Russian ~novyj~, Persian ~now~ (as in the holiday ~Nowruz~, "new day"), and Hindi ~nayؤپ~. To ~erneuern~ is to bring something back to its ~neue~ state, a concept so fundamental that the word for it has survived five millennia of linguistic change virtually intact.
en.ernten:
From Middle High German ~ernen~, from Old High German ~arnon~, from Proto-Germanic *~azanإچnؤ…~. This verb is denominative, derived from the noun *~azaniz~ ("harvest"), from Proto-Indo-European *~seh1-~ ("to sow"). The modern form with ~-t-~ (first attested in MHG ~ernde~) stems from a merger with the related noun OHG ~arnإچt~, from Proto-Germanic *~azanإچdaz~.

Cognate with English ~earn~, which shares the same Proto-Germanic ancestor *~azanإچnؤ…~ but diverged dramatically in meaning. The connection is that both words originally denoted reaping the fruits of labor: German preserved the agricultural sense ("to harvest"), while English generalized it to "to gain by labor." The PIE root *~seh1-~ ("to sow") also produced Latin ~semen~ ("seed") and English ~sow~ and ~seed~, making ~ernten~ a distant cousin of all of them.
en.ernأ¤hren:
From Middle High German ~ernern~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~nأ¤hren~:

- ~nأ¤hren~: From MHG ~neren~, ~nern~, from OHG ~nerien~, from Proto-West Germanic *~nazjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~nazjanؤ…~. The original OHG meaning was "to rescue, to keep alive," which gradually narrowed to "to nourish, to feed." Cognate with obsolete Dutch ~neren~ ("to feed, to provide for oneself").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness or sustaining completion.

The shift from "to rescue" to "to nourish" is intuitive: in a subsistence world, to save someone and to feed someone were nearly the same act. The ~er-~ prefix intensifies this life-sustaining sense, making ~ernأ¤hren~ mean "to nourish fully, to sustain." The reflexive ~sich ernأ¤hren~ ("to feed oneself, to make a living") preserves the older idea of self-preservation.
en.erobern:
From MHG ~geoberen~, ~oberen~, from OHG ~giobarإچn~ ("to overcome, to delay"), ~obarإچn~ ("to delay, to cancel"), and ~irobarإچn~ ("to get tired, to be exhausted"). Related to ~obere~ ("upper") and ~ober~ ("above"), from OHG ~obar~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubar~ ("over, above"), from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). The prefix ~er-~ (from OHG ~ir-~, ~ge-~) adds the sense of achievement, of overcoming from above.

~Erobern~ means "to conquer, to capture, to win" â€” ~eine Stadt erobern~ ("to conquer a city"), ~jemandes Herz erobern~ ("to win someone's heart"). The noun ~Eroberung~ ("conquest") and ~Eroberer~ ("conqueror") are direct derivatives. The etymology reveals a spatial metaphor for conquest: to conquer is to gain the upper position (~ober~, "upper"), to overcome by rising above. This is the same root that gives German ~أ¼ber~ ("over"), ~Oberflأ¤che~ ("surface," literally "upper surface"), and ~Oberst~ ("colonel" â€” originally the uppermost-ranking officer). The PIE source *~upأ©ri~ also produced Greek ~hyper~ ("over, beyond"), Latin ~super~ ("above"), and Sanskrit ~upأ،ri~ ("above") â€” making ~erobern~ a distant cousin of English ~super~, ~hyper~, and ~over~.
en.erreichen:
From Middle High German ~erreichen~, from Old High German ~irreihhen~, ~irreihhإچn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From MHG ~reichen~, from OHG ~reihhen~, ~reichen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijanؤ…~. Cognate with Dutch ~reiken~, English ~reach~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms the base meaning of "to reach, extend" into "to achieve, attain" â€” the goal is reached to completion. Cognate with Low German ~erreiken~, Dutch ~ereiken~ (archaic).
en.errichten:
From Middle High German ~errihten~, from Old High German ~irrihten~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~rihten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijanؤ…~ ("to make straight, direct"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to straighten, direct").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes bringing something to its full upright state.

~Errichten~ means "to erect, to establish, to build" â€” it takes the base sense of ~richten~ ("to direct, to make straight") and, with the completive ~er-~, pushes it to the endpoint: something fully raised, standing upright, brought into being. One ~errichtet~ a building, a monument, a foundation, or a state. The word carries a formality that the simpler ~bauen~ ("to build") lacks â€” one ~baut~ a house but ~errichtet~ a cathedral or an institution. The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to straighten") also gives Latin ~rؤ“x~ ("king," literally "the one who directs"), English ~right~, ~regal~, and ~reign~ â€” to erect and to rule share a common ancestor in the act of straightening. Cognate with Dutch ~oprichten~.
en.erschaffen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~schaffen~ (strong verb):

- ~schaffen~ (strong verb): From MHG ~schaffen~, from OHG ~scafan~, ~scaffen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skappjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skapjanؤ…~ ("to create"). Cognate with English ~shape~ and Dutch ~scheppen~.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds a sense of achievement and completion â€” bringing something into existence.

~Erschaffen~ means "to create" in the highest, most deliberate sense â€” divine or artistic creation. It exclusively takes the strong conjugation (~erschuf~, ~erschaffen~), unlike the simple verb ~schaffen~, which has split into two: a strong verb (~schuf~, ~geschaffen~ = "to create") and a weak verb (~schaffte~, ~geschafft~ = "to manage, accomplish"). ~Am Anfang erschuf Gott Himmel und Erde~ ("In the beginning God created heaven and earth") is the classic usage. The connection to English ~shape~ reveals that the PIE speakers conceived of creation not as conjuring from void but as forming, carving, shaping raw material.
en.erscheinen:
From Middle High German ~erschؤ«nen~, from Old High German ~irskؤ«nan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€skؤ«nan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~scheinen~:

- ~scheinen~: From MHG ~schؤ«nen~, from OHG ~skؤ«nan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skؤ«nan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skؤ«nanؤ…~, from PIE *~(s)ل¸±ehâ‚پy-~ ("to shine, shimmer"). Cognate with Dutch ~schijnen~, English ~shine~, Danish ~skinne~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The completive ~er-~ prefix shifts ~scheinen~'s sense of "to shine, to appear" toward "to appear fully, to emerge into visibility" â€” ~erscheinen~ describes something coming into view rather than merely glimmering. This explains the verb's dominant modern use for publications: a book ~erscheint~ ("appears, comes out") in the sense of coming fully into the world. The same compound is found in Dutch ~verschijnen~ (with ~ver-~ rather than ~er-~, but the same conceptual result). Cognate with Dutch ~verschijnen~ and Low German ~erschienen~.
en.erschlieأںen:
From the prefix ~er-~ + ~schliessen~:

- ~schliessen~: From MHG ~sliezen~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slutanan~, *~sleutanan~ ("to lock, to shut"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~slutan~, Dutch ~sluiten~ ("to close"), and English ~slot~ (originally the bolt of a lock).
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ur-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion, achievement, or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound ~erschliessen~ means "to open up, to develop, to tap (a resource), to infer." At first glance, the combination of a completive prefix with a verb meaning "to close" producing a verb meaning "to open" seems paradoxical. The key lies in the older meaning of ~schliessen~: originally it meant not just "to close" but "to fasten a lock," and by extension "to conclude" (as in English "to come to a conclusion"). With the completive ~er-~ prefix, the sense became "to unlock completely," hence "to open up, to make accessible." The use in the sense of "to infer" (~daraus erschliessen~) parallels ~schliessen~ in its meaning of "to conclude logically." English ~disclose~ captures a similar reversal of "closing" into "opening up."
en.erschweren:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~schwer~ + ~-en~:

- ~schwer~: From MHG ~swأ¦r~, from OHG ~swؤپr~, from Proto-West Germanic *~swؤپr~, from Proto-Germanic *~swؤ“raz~, from PIE *~swer-~ ("heavy, grave, serious"). Cognate with Dutch ~zwaar~, Old Norse ~svأ،rr~, and Latin ~sؤ“rius~ ("serious," possibly from the same root).
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds a causative, intensifying force â€” making something more so.

~Erschweren~ means "to make difficult, to aggravate, to complicate" â€” literally "to make heavy." The verb treats difficulty as weight, a metaphor deeply embedded in German (and English). The antonym ~erleichtern~ ("to facilitate, to make easier") employs the same ~er-~ prefix but with ~leicht~ ("light, easy"), forming a perfect heavy/light pair: ~erschweren~/~erleichtern~, as complementary as gravity and buoyancy.
en.ersetzen:
From Middle High German ~ersetzen~, from Old High German ~irsezzen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, place down"), from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~. A causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"): literally "to cause to sit."
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or achievement to the base verb.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms ~setzen~ ("to place, set") into ~ersetzen~ ("to replace, compensate") â€” the completive force of ~er-~ implies that the original is fully displaced and something new set in its place. The legal and financial sense of ~ersetzen~ ("to reimburse, make good for a loss") is older than the everyday sense of "to substitute"; the OHG compound ~irsezzen~ carried the sense of "to restore to its proper position." Cognate with Dutch ~vervangen~ (functional equivalent).
en.ersparen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~sparen~:

- ~sparen~: From MHG ~sparn~, from OHG ~sparen~, ~sparon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sparؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~sparإچnؤ…~, *~sparؤپnؤ…~ ("to save, keep, spare"), from Proto-Indo-European *~sper-~ ("to be productive, to earn"). Cognate with English ~spare~, Dutch ~sparen~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds the sense of achieving a result through the base verb's action.

While ~sparen~ means "to save" in the general sense of economizing, ~ersparen~ means "to spare someone something" or "to save up (a specific amount)." The ~er-~ prefix narrows the action to a definite result: not just the habit of saving but the achievement of having saved. The PIE root *~sper-~ originally meant "to be productive," and its semantic descendants span from English ~spare~ (to refrain, to have extra) to German ~sparen~ (to economize) -- complementary facets of the same idea of surplus and restraint.
en.erstatten:
From Middle High German ~erstaten~, derived from ~Statt~ ("place, stead"). Compound of ~er-~ + ~statten~:

- ~statten~: A denominative verb from ~Statt~ ("place, stead"), an orthographic variant of ~Stadt~ ("city"). From MHG ~stat~, from OHG ~stat~, from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~ ("place"), from Proto-Indo-European *~steh2tis~ ("standing, location"), from the root *~steh2-~ ("to stand"). Cognate with English ~stead~, Doublet of ~Stأ¤tte~ and ~Stadt~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys restoration or completion.

The literal sense is "to put back in its place" or "to restore to its proper standing," hence modern meanings of "to reimburse, to refund" and "to file, to render" (as in ~Bericht erstatten~, "to file a report"). The connection to "standing" and "place" runs deep: to reimburse someone is to restore them to the position they occupied before a loss.
en.erstellen:
From ~er-~ + ~stellen~. Compound of two elements:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb, the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or upward result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

~Erstellen~ is a Modern German formation (not attested in OHG or MHG), where the ~er-~ prefix adds the sense of bringing something fully into existence â€” placing it up into being. Its primary modern meaning is "to create, produce, compile" (a document, report, or structure), reflecting the completive force of ~er-~: not merely placing something, but establishing it fully. The word is especially common in administrative and digital contexts (~eine Datei erstellen~, "to create a file"), where it is the standard synonym for ~anfertigen~ or ~verfassen~. Cognate with Dutch ~opstellen~ ("to draw up, compose," with a different but semantically parallel prefix).
en.erstrecken:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~strecken~:

- ~strecken~: From MHG ~strecken~, from OHG ~strecken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strakkjan~ ("to stretch, make taut"), from PIE *~(s)treg-~ or *~streg-~ ("stiff, rigid"). Cognate with English ~stretch~, Dutch ~strekken~, Danish ~strأ¦kke~, Swedish ~strأ¤cka~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys the result or extent of the stretching action.

The verb ~erstrecken~ is used almost exclusively as a reflexive (~sich erstrecken~, "to extend, to stretch, to span"), describing something that reaches or spreads over a given area or period. While ~strecken~ means "to stretch" physically (as in stretching one's limbs), ~erstrecken~ abstracts the notion to spatial or temporal extent. The PIE root *~(s)treg-~ with its "stiff, taut" sense also gave rise to English ~stark~ and ~starch~, all connected through the idea of rigidity and extension. The ~s-mobile~ (optional initial ~s~) in the PIE root is a classic phenomenon where some daughter languages kept the ~s~ and others dropped it.
en.erteilen:
From Middle High German ~erteilen~, from Old High German ~irteilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€dailijan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~ ("to divide, part, deal"), from Proto-Germanic *~dailijanؤ…~, from *~dailiz~ ("part, share"), from PIE *~dehâ‚‚i-~ ("to divide"). Cognate with English ~deal~, Dutch ~delen~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

In modern German, ~erteilen~ means "to grant, bestow, issue" (permission, a lesson, a command) â€” one ~erteilt~ an ~Erlaubnis~ (permission) or a ~Lektion~ (lesson). Historically, ~erteilen~ was synonymous with ~urteilen~ ("to judge"), both deriving from the idea of dealing out or apportioning justice. Indeed, ~urteilen~ displaced ~erteilen~ in the judicial sense during the Middle High German period. The noun ~Urteil~ ("judgment, verdict") preserves this original meaning, while ~erteilen~ shifted toward the more general notion of dispensing or conferring. The phrase ~jemandem eine Abfuhr erteilen~ ("to give someone a rebuff," literally "to issue someone a removal") shows the verb at its most formal and decisive. Cognate with English ~ordeal~, which descends from the same Proto-Germanic root *~uzdailijؤ…~ ("judicial decision").
en.erwarten:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~warten~:

- ~warten~: From MHG ~warten~, from OHG ~wartؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wardؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~wardؤپnؤ…~ ("to guard, protect, watch over"), from Proto-Germanic *~warduz~ ("guard"), from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~wardإچn~, Swedish ~vأ¥rda~, English ~ward~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix shifts ~warten~ (to wait, to watch) from a state of watchful waiting into a forward-oriented expectation: ~erwarten~ is not merely to wait but to await with anticipation, to count on an outcome. The same PIE root *~wer-~ ("to heed") underlies English ~ward~, ~warden~, and ~aware~; and, borrowed via Old French from Frankish, English ~guard~ traces to the same ancestral root. Cognate with Dutch ~verwachten~ (a parallel compound with ~ver-~ in place of ~er-~).
en.erwecken:
From Middle High German ~erwecken~, from Old High German ~irwekken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uzwakkjan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~wecken~:

- ~wecken~: From MHG ~wecken~, from OHG ~wecchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wakkjan~ ("to wake up, to awaken"), the causative form of *~wakؤ“n~ ("to be awake"), from Proto-Indo-European *~weg-~ ("to be lively, active"). Cognate with English ~wake~, Dutch ~wekken~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds completeness or thoroughness.

The compound intensifies the causative meaning: while ~wecken~ is simply "to wake (someone) up," ~erwecken~ means "to awaken, to arouse, to rouse" -- often in a figurative or spiritual sense. One wakes a sleeping child with ~wecken~, but one ~erweckt~ feelings, memories, or even the dead. The biblical ~Auferweckung~ ("resurrection") is built on this same verb. The PIE root *~weg-~ ("to be lively, active") also produced Latin ~vigil~ ("watchful"), giving English ~vigil~, ~vigilant~, and ~vigor~.
en.erweisen:
~er-~ + ~weisen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~ ("to show, point out"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of achievement, completion, or emergence into evidence.

~Erweisen~ means "to prove, to show, to render (a service)." The reflexive ~sich erweisen als~ ("to prove to be, to turn out as") is particularly common: ~er erwies sich als zuverlأ¤ssig~ ("he proved to be reliable"). The non-reflexive can mean "to render" in formal contexts: ~jemandem einen Dienst erweisen~ ("to render someone a service"), ~jemandem Ehre erweisen~ ("to pay someone honor"). The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see") connects seeing, knowing, and showing in a single etymological thread: to ~er-weisen~ is to make something visibly evident, to bring knowledge out into the open. The same root gave Latin ~vidؤ“re~ ("to see"), Greek ~eidos~ ("form"), and Sanskrit ~veda~ ("knowledge") â€” making ~erweisen~ a distant cousin of English ~wisdom~, ~witness~, and ~video~. Cognate with Dutch ~erwijzen~ (archaic).
en.erweitern:
From ~er-~ + ~weitern~, where ~weitern~ is an obsolete verb derived from the comparative of ~weit~ ("wide, broad, far"):

- ~weit~: From MHG ~wؤ«t~, from OHG ~wؤ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«daz~. Cognate with Dutch ~wijd~, English ~wide~, Low German ~wied~, Danish ~vid~, Swedish ~vid~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix transforms "to make wider" into "to expand, to extend fully."

The derivation runs: ~weit~ â†’ comparative ~weiter~ ("wider") â†’ denominative *~weitern~ ("to make wider") â†’ prefixed ~erweitern~ ("to expand, enlarge, broaden"). The intermediate verb ~weitern~ fell out of use, surviving only in this compound and a few dialects. The noun ~Erweiterung~ ("expansion, extension, widening") is used in mathematics (~Erweiterung eines Bruchs~, "extension of a fraction") and in everyday speech (~EU-Erweiterung~, "EU enlargement").
en.erwerben:
From Middle High German ~erwerben~, from Old High German ~irwerban~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~werben~. Compare Dutch ~verwerven~.

- ~werben~: From MHG ~wأ«rben~, from OHG ~wأ«rban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hwerban~, from Proto-Germanic *~hwerbanؤ…~ ("to turn, move around"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~werven~ ("to recruit"), Icelandic ~hverfa~ ("to turn, disappear"), Faroese ~hvأ¸rva~ ("to disappear"), and â€” more distantly â€” English ~whirl~ via a parallel PIE root.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This completive prefix transforms the turning motion of ~werben~ into a fully achieved acquisition. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic journey of ~werben~ is one of German's most striking: from "to turn around" (Proto-Germanic) to "to move about on business" (Old High German), then to "to court, recruit, advertise" (Modern German), and in the compound ~erwerben~ to "to acquire, earn." The connecting thread is the turning motion of commerce â€” hawking wares, making rounds, drumming up custom. That the same verb underlies both ~Werbung~ ("advertising") and ~erwerben~ ("to acquire") reveals an ancient truth: advertising is the turning that produces getting. The English near-cognate ~whirl~ preserved the literal spinning sense, while the Germanic mercantile senses developed along their own path. A surviving compound that keeps the older sense is ~bewerben~ ("to apply for a job," literally "to turn toward something"). Cognate with Dutch ~verwerven~, Icelandic ~hverfa~.
en.erwischen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~wischen~:

- ~wischen~: From MHG ~wischen~, from OHG ~wisken~ ("to wipe, to move quickly"), from Proto-Germanic *~wiskijanؤ…~ ("to wipe, to move swiftly"). The original sense was a quick, darting motion â€” wiping or whisking.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, ~ar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("out of, from"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). As an inseparable prefix, it conveys achievement, attainment, or sudden completion.

The verb ~erwischen~ means "to catch, to nab, to get hold of" â€” ~einen Dieb erwischen~ ("to catch a thief"), ~den letzten Zug erwischen~ ("to catch the last train"), ~jemanden beim Schummeln erwischen~ ("to catch someone cheating"). The semantic development from "quick wiping motion" to "catching" passed through the idea of a swift grabbing movement: you ~erwischen~ someone the way you might ~wischen~ (swipe) at a fly. The ~er-~ prefix adds the sense of successful completion â€” not just swiping but actually catching. Colloquially, ~es hat ihn erwischt~ ("it got him") can mean anything from catching a cold to falling in love to dying, depending on context â€” a remarkable range of fates all described by the same image of being suddenly seized. The related English ~whisk~ (from Old Norse ~viska~, a Germanic cousin of ~wischen~) preserves the sense of rapid sweeping motion.
en.erwأ¤hnen:
A 16th-century alteration of the earlier form ~gewأ¤hnen~, from Middle High German ~gewأ¤henen~, ~gewahen~, from Old High German ~giwahanen~, ~giwahan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gawahwan~ ("to say, mention"), from Proto-Germanic *~gawahwanؤ…~, from *~ga-~ + *~wahwanؤ…~, from PIE *~wekت·-~ ("to speak").

The 16th-century reshaping swapped the prefix ~ge-~ for ~er-~, bringing the verb into line with other achievement verbs (the ~er-~ prefix conveys completion: "to bring to mention"). This substitution can create the impression that ~erwأ¤hnen~ is related to ~wأ¤hnen~ ("to suppose, imagine"), which descends from a different root entirely â€” Proto-West Germanic *~wؤپnijan~, from a stem *~wإچni-~ related to PIE *~wen-~ ("to desire, strive for"). The resemblance is a coincidence of form: the two verbs are not cognate. The true root of ~erwأ¤hnen~, PIE *~wekت·-~, is the same root underlying Latin ~vإچx~ ("voice"), ~vocؤپre~ ("to call"), and English ~voice~ and ~vocal~ â€” all ultimately from the same "speaking" root. Cognate with Dutch ~gewagen~ ("to mention, refer to").
en.erzeugen:
From ~er-~ + ~zeugen~:

- ~zeugen~: From MHG ~ziugen~, ~geziugen~, from OHG ~giziugon~, a denominative verb from ~Zeug~ ("stuff, gear, equipment, tools"). ~Zeug~ itself derives from MHG ~ziuc~, from OHG ~giziug~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teug~, ultimately a nominal derivative of *~teuhanؤ…~ ("to pull, lead"), which also underlies ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw"), from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). The completive prefix turns the act of producing or witnessing into the fully achieved result: something brought into being. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The root ~zeugen~ has two seemingly unrelated modern senses â€” "to beget, procreate" and "to testify, bear witness" â€” but both trace to the same image: producing or bringing forward. A witness produces testimony; a parent produces offspring. ~Zeuge~ ("witness") shares this root, as does the archaic noun ~Gezeugs~ ("tools, implements," i.e., things produced). The deeper PIE root *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead") ties ~zeugen~ to Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead"), the source of English ~duke~, ~duct~, and ~produce~. This makes ~erzeugen~ a distant relative of English ~produce~ â€” both meaning "to bring something forward" from a common ancestor who simply pulled. ~أœberzeugen~ ("to convince"), another compound on the same root, is the rhetorical version of the same act: to pull someone over to one's position through testimony. Cognate with Dutch ~getuigen~ (witness sense), English ~tow~ (via the same PIE *~dewk-~ root shared with ~ziehen~).
en.erzielen:
From ~er-~ + ~zielen~, where ~zielen~ is a denominative verb from ~Ziel~ ("goal, target, aim"):

- ~zielen~: From ~Ziel~ + verbal ~-en~. ~Ziel~: from MHG ~zil~, from OHG ~zil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~til~, from Proto-Germanic *~tilؤ…~ ("goal, mark"). Cognate with Old English ~til~ ("goal"), which survives in the preposition ~till~ (originally "up to the mark").
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix turns the act of aiming (~zielen~) into the achievement of hitting or obtaining a result.

The word ~Ziel~ itself covers both the physical sense (a target on an archery range) and the abstract sense (a goal in life or sport). ~Erzielen~ specializes in the achievement of quantifiable results: one ~erzielt~ a goal in football (~ein Tor erzielen~), a profit (~einen Gewinn erzielen~), or a record price. The completive ~er-~ is semantically precise: aiming (~zielen~) becomes scoring (~erzielen~). The Old English cognate ~til~ links to the modern preposition ~till/until~, whose core sense was "reaching the mark."
en.erzأ¤hlen:
From Middle High German ~erzellen~, from Old High German ~irzellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€talljan~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~zأ¤hlen~. Compound of two elements:

- ~zأ¤hlen~: From MHG ~zel(e)n~, from OHG ~zellan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~taljanؤ…~, which carried both "to count" and "to recount, tell" from its earliest attestation.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic shift from "counting" to "narrating" â€” powered by the completive ~er-~ prefix â€” is one of German's most striking lexical developments. The underlying Proto-Germanic root *~taljanؤ…~ already held both meanings, but ~erzأ¤hlen~ specialized entirely in the narrative sense: to give a full, complete account. The parallel with English is striking: ~tell~ (from the same root) underwent the same journey from counting to narrating, while ~teller~ at a bank and ~telling beads~ preserve the counting sense. The English word ~recount~ (to narrate) makes the metaphor explicit: to re-count events is to tell them. Cognate with Dutch ~vertellen~ ("to narrate," with a different prefix but the same root).
en.erأ¶ffnen:
From Middle High German ~eroffenen~, from Old High German ~iroffanإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uت€upanإچjan~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~أ¶ffnen~:

- ~أ¶ffnen~: From MHG ~أ¶ffenen~, ~offenen~, from OHG ~offanإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~upanإچn~, a denominative from Proto-Germanic *~upanaz~ ("open"), from PIE *~upo~ ("up, up from under"). Cognate with English ~open~, Dutch ~openen~, Danish ~أ¥bne~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix lends a sense of initiation or ceremonial beginning â€” "opening up" to completion.

While ~أ¶ffnen~ is the everyday word for opening a door or window, ~erأ¶ffnen~ carries a more formal or initiatory sense: one ~erأ¶ffnet~ a conference, a bank account, a trial, or a new shop. The completive ~er-~ transforms a simple action into an event. The related noun ~Erأ¶ffnung~ appears in legal, musical, and commercial contexts â€” a ~Erأ¶ffnungsfest~ is a grand opening, and a symphony's ~Erأ¶ffnungssatz~ is its opening movement.
en.essen:
From Middle High German ~أ«ب¥ب¥en~, from Old High German ~أ«ب¥ب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~etan~, from Proto-Germanic *~etanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚پed-~ ("to eat"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~eten~, Dutch ~eten~, English ~eat~, Danish ~أ¦de~, Norwegian ~ete~, Swedish ~أ¤ta~, Yiddish ~×¢×،×ں~ (esn), Gothic ~itan~. Non-Germanic cognates include Latin ~edإچ~, Ancient Greek ~أ©dإچ~, Sanskrit ~أ،dmi~, Russian ~yestت¹~, and Scottish Gaelic ~ith~.

Few PIE roots are as conservative as *~hâ‚پed-~. Across five thousand years and dozens of languages, the word for "to eat" has scarcely changed: Latin ~edإچ~, Greek ~أ©dإچ~, Sanskrit ~أ،dmi~, German ~essen~, and English ~eat~ all reflect the same ancient root, differing only by the regular sound changes of each branch. The PIE root also yields Latin ~edإچ~, the source of English ~edible~, ~obese~ (from ~ob-esus~, "eaten up"), and ~comestible~ â€” making ~essen~ a distant relative of all of those. The geminate ~ss~ in the German infinitive reflects a Proto-West Germanic pattern of consonant strengthening. Notably, ~essen~ in Modern German is conventionally reserved for human eating â€” one says ~fressen~ when animals eat (and, rudely, when humans eat greedily), a distinction without an exact parallel in English.
en.etablieren:
Borrowed in the 17th century from French ~أ©tablir~ ("to establish"), from Old French ~establir~, from Latin ~stabilؤ«re~ ("to make firm, to confirm, to establish"), from ~stabilis~ ("firm, steady"), from ~stؤپre~ ("to stand"), from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand"). The German suffix ~-ieren~ marks the standard adaptation pattern for Romance verbs entering German.

The semantic journey from "to make firm" to "to establish" is straightforward, but the word's social history in German is more interesting. In the 18th and 19th centuries, ~etablieren~ carried a strong commercial sense: a merchant ~etablierte~ a business, and the reflexive ~sich etablieren~ ("to establish oneself") implied achieving financial and social respectability. The derived French loanword ~Etablissement~ took on a seedier coloring in German, often euphemistically referring to a nightclub or house of ill repute â€” a sense the French original does not share. Modern German has largely rehabilitated ~etablieren~ into neutral usage: political parties ~etablieren sich~ in parliament, scientific theories ~etablieren sich~ in textbooks. The related adjective ~etabliert~ ("established, well-established") carries no negative connotation. Cognate roots appear across the Indo-European spectrum: Latin ~stabilis~ gives English ~stable~, ~establish~, and ~state~; the same PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ yields German ~stehen~, ~Stand~, ~Staat~, and ~stabil~ â€” making ~etablieren~ a long-distance cousin, returned via French, of words that never left German.
en.existieren: ~Existieren~ ("to exist") is borrowed from Latin ~exsistere~ / ~existere~ ("to step forth, emerge, come into being, exist"), from ~ex-~ ("out, forth") + ~sistere~ ("to cause to stand, to place, to halt"), from Proto-Italic *~sistإچ~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand"). That PIE root is one of the most productive in Indo-European: it produced Latin ~stؤپre~ ("to stand"), English ~stand~, ~state~, ~station~, ~statute~, ~stable~, and ~substance~, as well as Ancient Greek ~ل¼µدƒد„خ·خ¼خ¹~ (~hأ­stؤ“mi~, "I cause to stand") and Sanskrit ~à¤¤à¤؟à¤·à¥چà¤ à¤¤à¤؟~ (~tأ­ل¹£ل¹­hati~, "he stands"). The Latin compound ~exsistere~ thus literally means "to stand out" â€” to emerge from non-being into being. English ~exist~ traveled the same route via Old French ~exister~. A philosophically interesting cognate: ~Bestand~ ("existence, stock, inventory") and ~bestehen~ ("to exist, persist, pass a test") share the same PIE root, showing that German preserved the standing metaphor natively while also borrowing the Latin learned form.
en.fahren: From Middle High German ~varn~, ~varen~, from Old High German ~faran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~faran~, from Proto-Germanic *~faranؤ…~, from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). A class VI strong verb whose broad original sense of "to go, travel" narrowed in Modern German to emphasize vehicular travel, while the older general sense of "going" survives in derivatives like ~Erfahrung~ ("experience," literally "a traveling through") and ~Gefahr~ ("danger," literally "a lying in wait on the journey"). Cognate with Dutch ~varen~, English ~fare~, Danish ~fare~.
en.fallen: From Middle High German ~vallen~, from Old High German ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallanؤ…~. The ultimate PIE origin is uncertain; one proposed reconstruction connects it to a compound *~hâ‚‚pehâ‚ƒlH-~ ("to fall away, be destroyed"), related to Ancient Greek ~apأ³llإ«mi~ ("to destroy, kill") and Lithuanian ~pأ¹lti~ ("to fall, rush"). A class VII strong verb with a reduplicated past tense in Proto-Germanic. The semantic range of ~fallen~ has remained remarkably stable across two millennia â€” the word for a leaf falling in Old High German is the same word used today for falling in love (~sich verlieben~) or for a soldier falling in battle (~fallen im Krieg~). Cognate with Low German ~fallen~, Dutch ~vallen~, English ~fall~, Danish ~falde~, Icelandic ~falla~, Swedish ~falla~.
en.fangen: ~fangen~ comes from Middle High German ~vؤپhen~, from Old High German ~fؤپhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fؤ…ج„han~, from Proto-Germanic *~fanhanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to secure, attach, fasten"). The original strong verb had the vowel pattern ~fؤپhan~ / ~fieng~ / ~gefangan~; the modern present stem ~fang-~ was borrowed from the past forms under the influence of Middle Low German ~vangen~, spreading through Central German dialects in the early modern period. The same analogical leveling shaped ~hأ¤ngen~, though ~hأ¤ngen~ completed the shift more quickly because it already had two variant verbs reinforcing each other. Cognate with English ~fang~ (originally "that which seizes"), Dutch ~vangen~, West Frisian ~fange~, and â€” more distantly â€” Albanian ~peng~ ("to hinder, hold captive"). The PIE root *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ also produced Latin ~pangere~ ("to fix, fasten") and ~pax~ ("peace, compact"), making ~fangen~ a distant relative of English ~peace~.
en.fassen:
From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~ ("to hold, seize"), from PIE *~pod-nأ©hâ‚‚-ti~, from *~pod-~ ("to fetch"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.

The connection between ~fassen~ and English ~fetch~ is one of the more startling cognate pairs in the Germanic family: both descend from the same PIE root, yet they diverged dramatically â€” German keeping the sense of grasping and holding, English developing the sense of going to retrieve. The Proto-Germanic verb *~fatإچnؤ…~ was a weak class 2 verb meaning "to hold, seize," and its descendants in German spread across an exceptionally rich idiomatic range: ~sich fassen~ ("to compose oneself, pull oneself together"), ~Fassung~ ("composure; version of a text"), ~unfassbar~ ("incomprehensible, literally ungraspable"), ~fassungslos~ ("speechless, stunned"), and the compound ~umfassen~ ("to encompass"). The cognitive metaphor of understanding as physical grasping runs deep: in German one ~fasst~ an idea, in English one ~grasps~ it, and in Latin one ~comprehendit~ it â€” all three verbs from different roots sharing the same bodily image. The noun ~Fass~ ("barrel, cask") shares the same root â€” a container that holds things. Note that ~fassen~ is not related to the English verb "to fast" (dietary abstinence), which derives from Proto-Germanic *~fastإچnؤ…~ ("to hold firm, abstain") â€” a different root that produced a false-friend surface resemblance.
en.fehlen: From Middle High German ~vأ¦len~, ~velen~, ~valen~, borrowed from Old French ~faillir~ ("to fail, be lacking"), from Latin ~fallإچ~ ("to deceive, cause to stumble"), from PIE *~(s)gت·ت°hâ‚‚el-~ ("to stumble"), with a semantic development from "to stumble" through "to deceive." This makes ~fehlen~ one of the relatively few common German verbs that entered the language via Old French rather than through Germanic inheritance â€” a legacy of medieval courtly and trade contact. The semantic shift from "to deceive" through "to fall short" to "to be missing, to be lacking" follows the trajectory of French ~faillir~: what fails is what is not there when needed. The same Latin root gives English ~fail~, ~false~, and ~fallible~. Cognate with English ~fail~, French ~faillir~, Italian ~fallire~.
en.feiern:
From Middle High German ~vؤ«ren~, from Old High German ~fؤ«rإچn~, a denominative verb derived from Old High German ~fؤ«ra~ (whence modern ~Feier~), itself borrowed from Latin ~fؤ“riae~ ("religious holidays, festivals"). Cognate with Dutch ~vieren~.

Latin ~fؤ“riae~ is from Proto-Italic *~fؤ“siؤپs~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dت°أ©hâ‚پs-yo-s~, derived from *~dت°أ©hâ‚پs~ ("god, godhead, deity"). The same PIE root underlies Latin ~fؤ“stus~ ("festive") â€” whence English ~feast~ and ~festival~ â€” and Ancient Greek ~theأ®os~ ("divine"). ~Feiern~ is thus one of the rare German verbs whose etymology leads directly into Latin and ultimately into the divine: a holiday (~Feiertag~) was originally a day set aside for the gods. The route from Latin into German passed through the early Christian church, which adopted the Roman calendar vocabulary for its own feast days. The modern sense of ~feiern~ â€” throwing a birthday party, celebrating New Year's â€” is secular, but the word retains its sacred pedigree. Cognate (distantly) with English ~feast~ and ~festival~ via the Latin root.
en.fertigen: From Middle High German ~vertigen~, ~vertegen~ ("to prepare, to make ready, to keep ready"), equivalent to ~fertig~ + ~-en~. The adjective ~fertig~ ("ready, finished") descends from MHG ~fertec~, from OHG ~fartؤ«g~, from the noun ~fart~ ("journey"). The original meaning of ~fertig~ was "ready for a journey" â€” prepared, equipped â€” before broadening to "ready" and then "finished, done." The noun ~Fart~ comes from the same root as ~fahren~: Proto-Germanic *~farؤ…~ ("journey"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to cross, to pass through"). Cognate with Dutch ~vaardig~ ("capable, swift"), Low German ~fardig~, Old English ~fierd~ ("expedition, military campaign"). ~Fertigen~ in modern German means "to manufacture, to produce" â€” especially in industrial and technical contexts (~Bauteile fertigen~, "to manufacture components"). The semantic arc from "journey-ready" to "manufactured" traces the cultural shift from a travel-based to a production-based economy: what was once preparedness for departure became readiness as a finished product.
en.festhalten:
~fest-~ + ~halten~. Compound of ~fest-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~ ("to hold, to tend, to herd"), from PIE *~kel-~ ("to drive, to herd").
- ~fest-~: From PIE *~pastV~ ("solid, stable"). The prefix adds the sense of firmness, fixedness, or permanence.

~Festhalten~ means "to hold firm, to hold onto, to record, to note down" â€” a verb whose meanings span from the physical grip to the documentary record. One ~hأ¤lt~ a railing ~fest~ (grips it tightly), ~hأ¤lt~ an a belief ~fest~ (clings to it), or ~hأ¤lt~ observations ~fest~ (records them). The reflexive ~sich festhalten an~ ("to hold onto") is the instruction on every German bus and train: ~Bitte festhalten!~ ("Please hold on!"). The documentary sense â€” to fix information in place, to write it down â€” appears in the noun ~Feststellung~ ("determination, establishment of fact") and the participial compound ~festgehalten~ ("recorded, captured"). This semantic extension from physical to abstract is natural: what one grips firmly does not escape, and what one writes down is similarly secured against forgetting. The PIE root *~kel-~ ("to drive, herd") originally described the activity of cattle-herding â€” keeping animals together and under control â€” from which the sense of "holding" and "keeping" naturally developed. Cognate with Dutch ~vasthouden~ ("to hold tight").
en.festlegen:
~fest-~ + ~legen~. Compound of ~fest-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, a causative of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~.
- ~fest-~: From MHG ~vest~, from OHG ~festi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fast~, from Proto-Germanic *~fastuz~, probably from PIE *~pastV-~ ("solid, stable"). Cognate with English ~fast~ (in its "firm, fixed" sense, as in "hold fast") and Gothic ~fastan~.

~Festlegen~ means "to fix, determine, establish, pin down" â€” literally "to lay firm." The combination of "solid" and the causative "to lay" produces the sense of laying something down in a settled, permanent way: a rule, a date, a decision. The reflexive ~sich festlegen~ ("to commit oneself, to be pinned down") has a slightly anxious quality in modern usage â€” one ~legt sich fest~ when one has to make an irrevocable decision. English ~fast~ in "hold fast," "fasten," and "steadfast" preserves the same Proto-Germanic root *~fastuz~. The ~fest-~ prefix is unusual in that it derives from an adjective rather than a preposition or directional adverb â€” making ~festlegen~ a rare type of German separable compound built on an adjectival particle. Cognate with Dutch ~vastleggen~.
en.feststellen:
Compound of ~fest-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb â€” the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~fest-~: From MHG ~vest~, ~veste~, from OHG ~festi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fastؤ«~, from PIE *~pastV-~ ("solid, stable"). Cognate with English ~fast~ (in the archaic sense of "firmly fixed," as in "hold fast"), Dutch ~vast~, Swedish ~fast~.

The literal meaning of ~feststellen~ is "to place firmly" â€” to put something down in such a way that it stays put. From this spatial metaphor come both of the verb's principal modern senses: "to establish a fact" (to fix it as true, to put it on the record so that it cannot be moved) and "to determine, to ascertain" (to arrive at a settled conclusion). The compound is a relatively modern coinage; MHG and OHG had no single equivalent word, relying instead on circumlocutions. Its closest English functional parallel is "to establish" or "to determine," though neither captures the Germanic concreteness of ~feststellen~'s spatial metaphor as precisely. The same ~fest-~ element appears in ~festhalten~ ("to hold fast"), ~festlegen~ ("to fix, to lay down"), and ~festigen~ ("to consolidate") â€” a family of verbs all built on the image of gripping or placing something so securely that it cannot shift. Cognate with Dutch ~vaststellen~.
en.filmen:
A denominative verb from ~Film~ + ~-en~. The noun ~Film~ was borrowed from English ~film~, which descends from Old English ~filmen~ ("membrane, thin skin"), from Proto-West Germanic *~filmin-~, from Proto-Germanic *~filmin-~ ("thin skin, membrane"), from Proto-Indo-European *~pel-mo-~ ("membrane"), from the root *~pel-~ ("to cover, skin").

In a charming etymological boomerang, the word traveled from Germanic to English, where it underwent a remarkable semantic shift -- from "membrane" to "thin coating" (1577) to "photographic coating" (1845) to "motion picture" (1895) -- before being borrowed back into German in the early twentieth century. Old English ~filmen~ meant "foreskin" or "caul," and no Anglo-Saxon could have imagined that this humble anatomical term would one day name an art form.
en.finanzieren: From ~Finanz~ + ~-ieren~. ~Finanz~ derives from French ~finance(s)~, from Middle French ~finer~ ("to pay a ransom, to settle a debt"), from ~fin~ ("end"), from Latin ~fؤ«nis~ ("end, boundary, limit"). The semantic chain is elegant: a debt is "ended" by payment, so ~finance~ originally meant "an ending" â€” specifically, the ending of an obligation through payment. The sense shifted from "ransom payment" (mid-fifteenth century) to "taxation" (late fifteenth century) to "management of money" (eighteenth century). German naturalized the verb with the standard ~-ieren~ suffix, making ~finanzieren~ ("to finance, to fund") feel thoroughly German despite its Romance pedigree. The Latin root ~fؤ«nis~ also gives German ~Finale~, ~Definition~, and ~Infinitiv~ â€” the last being a grammatical term meaning "the form without an ending," a fitting irony for a verb-conjugation app. Cognate with English ~finance~, French ~financer~, Italian ~finanziare~.
en.finden: From Middle High German ~vinden~, from Old High German ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~finأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge"). The semantic development from "to go, pass" to "to find" parallels similar shifts in Slavic languages. Cognate with Bavarian ~findn~, Low German ~finden~, Dutch ~vinden~, English ~find~, Danish ~finde~.
en.fixieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~fixum~ ("that which is fixed, a fixed sum"), the neuter past participle of ~figere~ ("to fasten, to fix"), possibly influenced by Medieval Latin ~fixؤپre~ ("to make fast"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of French/Latin ~-ier~/~-ؤپre~ verb endings.

The Latin verb ~figere~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~dت°eygت·-~ ("to stick, to fix"). The meanings of ~fixieren~ branched in the sixteenth century when alchemists adopted ~fix~ to mean "stable, non-volatile" (a substance that would not evaporate). From this came the sense of "to make permanent" and then "to stare at fixedly." In modern German, ~fixieren~ can mean "to fix in place," "to stare at intently," or "to define precisely" -- all descendants of the alchemists' quest to make the volatile stable.
en.fliegen: ~fliegen~ comes from Middle High German ~vliegen~, from Old High German ~fliogan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fleugan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fleuganؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~plewk-~ ("to flow, fly"). The PIE root *~plewk-~ is an extended form of *~plew-~ ("to flow, float, swim"), which also produced ~flieأںen~ ("to flow"), Latin ~pluere~ ("to rain"), and Greek ~plein~ ("to sail"). Motion through a fluid medium â€” whether water or air â€” was once a single conceptual domain. The High German consonant shift turned Proto-Germanic *fl- into German fl- unchanged (labial fricatives were unaffected), but the vowel shift from Proto-Germanic *eu to OHG *io to MHG *ie is characteristic of German Class II strong verbs. Cognate with Dutch ~vliegen~, English ~fly~, Danish ~flyve~, and Low German ~flegen~. Compare also Bavarian ~fliagn~, which preserves an older vowel.
en.fliehen:
From Middle High German ~vliehen~, from Old High German ~fliohan~ ("to flee"), from Proto-West Germanic *~fleuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fleuhanؤ…~ ("to flee"), probably from PIE *~plewk-~ ("to fly, flee"), an enlargement of *~plew-~ ("to flow, run"). A class II strong verb. Cognate with English ~flee~ (from OE ~flؤ“on~), Dutch ~vlieden~, and Gothic ~أ¾liuhan~ (with secondary ~أ¾-~).

The PIE root *~plewk-~ is also the source of ~fliegen~ ("to fly") and ~Flucht~ ("flight, escape"), making ~fliehen~ and ~fliegen~ etymological siblings -- the original root encompassed rapid motion through any medium. The English cognates ~flee~ and ~fly~ mirror this German pair perfectly. Gothic ~أ¾liuhan~ is puzzling: it shows an unexpected ~أ¾-~ (th-sound) where all other Germanic languages have ~f-~. Linguists debate whether the Gothic form preserves an archaic consonant or reflects a secondary change unique to Gothic. The noun ~Flأ¼chtling~ ("refugee"), derived from ~Flucht~, entered international discourse during the European migration crisis of 2015 -- a word with five-thousand-year-old roots describing an experience as old as humanity itself.
en.flieأںen: From Middle High German ~vlieب¥en~, from Old High German ~flioب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fleotan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fleutanؤ…~ ("to float, to stream, to flow"), from PIE *~plewd-~ ("to flow, to run, to swim"). A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~flieأںen~, ~floss~, ~geflossen~. The PIE root *~plewd-~ produced an extensive water-vocabulary across Indo-European: Latin ~pluvia~ ("rain"), whence English ~pluvial~; Latvian ~plإ«di~ ("flood"); Lithuanian ~plأ،uti~ ("to wash"); and Greek ~pleإچ~ ("I sail"). Within Germanic, the root gave English ~fleet~ (originally "a place where water flows," then the ships gathered there), ~float~, ~flood~, Dutch ~vlieten~ ("to flow," archaic/literary), Swedish ~flyta~ ("to float"), and Icelandic ~fljأ³ta~. The zero-grade form *~flut-~ produced German ~Flut~ ("flood, high tide"), English ~flood~, and Dutch ~vloed~. The derived noun ~Fluss~ ("river") is literally "a flowing" â€” Germanic languages conceptualize rivers as the action of flowing made permanent, whereas Latin ~flإ«men~ (from ~fluere~, "to flow," a different PIE root) took the same metaphorical path independently. The compound ~Einfluss~ ("influence") calques Latin ~influentia~ â€” a flowing-in of one thing upon another.
