en.folgen: From Middle High German ~volgen~, from Old High German ~folgؤ“n~ (attested from the 8th century, meaning "to follow, serve faithfully, pursue"), from Proto-West Germanic *~folgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~fulgؤپnؤ…~. The ultimate pre-Germanic origin is uncertain. The Old High German forms reveal an early semantic range that blended physical following, loyal service, and pursuit â€” reflecting the social structures of early Germanic society in which following a lord was the fundamental act of the ~comitatus~ (the warrior band). German ~folgen~ and English ~follow~ are doublets: both descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, but ~follow~ entered Middle English via Old Norse ~fylgja~ through Old English ~folgian~. Cognate with Dutch ~volgen~, English ~follow~, Danish ~fأ¸lge~, Swedish ~fأ¶lja~.
en.fordern:
From Middle High German ~vodern~, ~vordern~, from Old High German ~fordarإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~forأ¾(a)rإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~furأ¾ؤ…~, from PIE *~pإ•ج¥-to-~, from *~per-~ ("forward, through"). The semantic development from "to push forward" to "to demand, claim" reflects the notion of asserting or advancing a claim. The PIE root *~per-~ is shared with the separable prefix ~vor-~ and with English ~forth~, ~forward~, and ~further~ â€” all sharing the spatial sense of moving ahead.

German learners must take care to distinguish ~fordern~ ("to demand") from the near-homophone ~fأ¶rdern~ ("to promote, advance, support"). Despite their near-identical sound, they descend from the same Proto-Germanic root and once overlapped in meaning; the umlaut in ~fأ¶rdern~ (from MHG ~vأ¼rdern~, OHG ~furdiren~, from PWGmc *~furأ¾rijan~) marks its more specifically "forward-moving" sense, while ~fordern~ settled on the sense of making a forward claim or assertion. Cognate with English ~further~ (via the same PIE root), Dutch ~vorderen~.
en.formen: From Middle High German ~formen~, a denominative verb derived from the noun ~Form~, from Middle High German ~form~, borrowed from Latin ~forma~ ("shape, figure, appearance"). The ultimate origin of Latin ~forma~ is uncertain, but it may derive from Etruscan *~morma~, itself from Ancient Greek ~morphe~ ("shape, fashion, outward form"), with dissimilation of *~m-m~ to *~f-m~, as also seen in Latin ~formica~ ("ant") and ~formido~ ("scarecrow, ghost"). This Latin-to-Greek connection means that ~formen~ and English ~morph~ are distant cousins, both tracing back to the Greek concept of visible shape. The German ~-ieren~ suffix was not used here, unlike with many other Latin-derived verbs; instead German created a straightforward denominative from the noun, giving ~formen~ its clean, native-sounding conjugation. English ~form~ (both noun and verb) is a sibling borrowing from the same Latin source, arriving via Old French ~fourme~.
en.formulieren: Borrowed from French ~formuler~, itself from Latin ~fإچrmula~ ("a small pattern or mold, form, rule, principle, method"), the diminutive of ~forma~ ("form, shape, figure"). The Latin ~forma~ is of uncertain origin; some scholars connect it to Greek ~morphل¸—~ ("form, shape") via metathesis, while others derive it from an Etruscan source. German ~formulieren~ follows the standard ~-ieren~ suffix pattern for verbs borrowed from Romance languages â€” a productive pattern since the Middle High German period, when courtly culture imported hundreds of French verbs. The diminutive origin is telling: a ~formula~ was not just any form but a ~small~ form, a precise template â€” and ~formulieren~ preserves this sense of careful, deliberate shaping of language. The related noun ~Form~ entered German directly from French ~forme~, while ~Formel~ ("formula") came through Latin. English ~formulate~ traveled a parallel path, from the same Latin diminutive via French. Cognate with Dutch ~formuleren~, Italian ~formulare~, Spanish ~formular~.
en.fortsetzen:
Compound of ~fort-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, to place"), a causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~fort-~: From MHG ~vort~, from OHG ~fort~ ("forth, onward"), from Proto-West Germanic *~furأ¾~, from Proto-Germanic *~furأ¾~ ("forth, forward"), from PIE *~per~ ("to cross; across, before"). As a verbal prefix, ~fort-~ conveys continuation or onward motion. Cognate with English ~forth~.

The compound literally means "to set forth" or "to place onward," yielding the modern sense "to continue." The derived noun ~Fortsetzung~ ("continuation, sequel") is familiar to anyone who has seen the words ~Fortsetzung folgt~ ("to be continued") at the end of a serialized story.
en.fotografieren: Derived from the noun ~Fotografie~, which comes ultimately from Ancient Greek ~phos~ (genitive ~photos~, "light") + ~grapho~ ("I write"), literally "light-writing." The verb was formed by adding the German ~-ieren~ suffix (from French ~-er~ via Latin ~-are~) to the stem ~fotograf-~. The word ~Fotografie~ itself may have been influenced by English ~photography~, which was coined around 1839, likely by Sir John Herschel, though the Franco-Brazilian inventor Hercules Florence also used the term independently. German originally spelled the word with ~Ph-~ as ~Photographieren~, faithfully reflecting the Greek ~phi~; the reformed spelling with ~F-~ was officially adopted in 1996 as part of the German orthographic reform. The shift from ~Ph~ to ~F~ remains one of the most visible and sometimes contested changes of that reform. English ~photograph~ and French ~photographier~ are direct cognates, all children of the same Greek compound.
en.fragen: From Middle High German ~vrؤپgen~, from Old High German ~frؤپgؤ“n~, ~frahؤ“n~/~frؤپhؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frؤپgؤ“n~ (a denominative derived from *~frؤپgu~, "question"), from Proto-Germanic *~frؤ“gإچ~ ("question"), from PIE *~preل¸±-~ ("to ask"). The PIE root *~preل¸±-~ is widespread and productive: it yields Latin ~precor~ ("I pray, beseech"), Sanskrit ~pل¹›cchؤپti~ ("asks"), and Persian ~porsؤ«dan~ ("to ask"). The -~h~- spellings in Old High German (~frahؤ“n~) reflect a retention of the Proto-Germanic laryngeal trace; the ~-g-~ forms ultimately prevailed. Cognate with Saterland Frisian ~frأ¤igje~, Dutch ~vragen~, Low German ~fragen~, English ~frain~ (archaic, "to ask, inquire"), Swedish ~frأ¥ga~, Icelandic ~fregna~.
en.freigeben:
Compound of ~frei~ + ~geben~:

- ~frei~: From MHG ~vri~, from OHG ~fri~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fri~, from Proto-Germanic *~frijaz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~prey-~ ("to love, to free"). Cognate with English ~free~, Dutch ~vrij~, Danish ~fri~.
- ~geben~: From MHG ~geben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~ ("to give"), from Proto-Indo-European *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (via Norse), Dutch ~geven~.

The compound literally means "to give free" or "to release." Its modern uses range from "to release, to approve" (freeing a product or document for distribution) to "to take time off" (~einen Tag freigeben~). The PIE root *~prey-~ originally meant "to love" -- in the ancient Germanic world, a "free" person was one among the beloved kin group, as opposed to slaves and outsiders. Freedom was, at its root, belonging.
en.fressen: From Middle High German ~vrezzen~, from Old High German ~frezzan~ (also ~firezzan~), from Proto-West Germanic *~fraetan~. This is a compound of the prefix ~fr-~ (an unstressed form of ~ver-~) + ~essen~ ("to eat"). The base verb ~essen~ traces from MHG ~ezzen~, OHG ~ezzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~etan~, from Proto-Germanic *~etanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚پed-~ ("to eat"). Cognate with Dutch ~vreten~, English ~fret~ (in its archaic sense "to eat away, corrode"), Gothic ~fraitan~, and Swedish ~frأ¤ta~ ("to corrode"). The prefix ~ver-~/~fr-~ adds a sense of consuming completely or destructively, giving ~fressen~ the connotation of devouring or eating voraciously. In modern German, ~fressen~ is used exclusively for animals eating (or, pejoratively, for humans eating in a rude or greedy manner), while ~essen~ is reserved for polite human eating. This human/animal distinction is a uniquely German innovation not found in other Germanic languages. English ~fret~, though now meaning "to worry," preserves the original sense of gnawing or eating away; a fretted surface is one that has been corroded, and fretwork in architecture involves cutting or eating into wood.
en.freuen: From Middle High German ~vro(u)wen~, ~vre(u)wen~, from Old High German ~frewen~, ~frouwen~, a denominative derivation of Old High German ~fro~, ~frao~ ("glad"). The underlying adjective ~froh~ descends from MHG ~vro~, OHG ~fro~, from Proto-Germanic *~frawaz~ ("quick, glad"). Compare Dutch ~vrolijk~, whence also English ~frolic~.
en.fungieren:
From Latin ~fungor~ ("to perform, discharge, execute") + the German verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin ~fungor~ descends from Proto-Italic *~fungor~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bت°ewg-~ ("to enjoy, to be of use"), formed with a nasal infix (*~bت°u-ne-g-ti~).

The PIE root *~bت°ewg-~ also produced Sanskrit ~bhunakti~ ("he enjoys") and possibly Latin ~fructus~ ("enjoyment, fruit, profit"), though the latter connection is debated. The semantic path from "to enjoy" to "to perform a function" passed through the intermediate sense of "to be occupied with" -- one first enjoys a role, then simply occupies it. In modern German, ~fungieren~ is a formal word meaning "to serve as, to act as," typically describing an official role or capacity.
en.funktionieren: Attested ca. 1800, from ~Funktion~ + the verbal suffix ~-ieren~, modeled on French ~fonctionner~. ~Funktion~ is a borrowing from Latin ~fإ«nctiإچ~ ("performance, execution"), itself from ~fungor~ ("to perform, execute, discharge"), from Proto-Italic *~fungإچr~, from PIE *~bت°ewg-~ ("to enjoy, be of use"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard naturalizer for Latin and French loanverbs in German, entering via Old French contact in the 12thâ€“13th century; it always produces weak verbs. This contrasts sharply with earlier Latin borrowings like ~schreiben~ (from Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~), which arrived during the Roman period and were fully assimilated into the strong-verb system. Cognate with French ~fonctionner~, English ~function~, Dutch ~functioneren~. The deep PIE root *~bت°ewg-~ also underlies English ~brook~ ("to tolerate, make use of").
en.fأ¤llen:
From Middle High German ~vellen~, from Old High German ~fellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallijanؤ…~ ("to make fall"), a causative of *~fallanؤ…~ ("to fall"), possibly from PIE *~hâ‚ƒelhâ‚پ-~ ("to fall, to perish"), with a prefix *~hâ‚‚epo~ ("off, away"). Cognate with English ~fell~ (as in "to fell a tree"), Dutch ~vellen~.

The causative relationship between ~fallen~ ("to fall") and ~fأ¤llen~ ("to cause to fall, to fell") is a textbook example of the Proto-Germanic ~jan~-causative, exactly parallel to ~sitzen~/~setzen~ ("sit"/"set") and ~liegen~/~legen~ ("lie"/"lay"). The umlaut in ~fأ¤llen~ (a to أ¤) is the footprint of this ancient causative suffix, which contained a front vowel that pulled the root vowel forward. In modern German, ~fأ¤llen~ means both "to fell" (a tree) and "to render, to pass" (a judgment: ~ein Urteil fأ¤llen~). The judicial sense developed from the image of a decisive, downward stroke -- just as a tree is brought down with finality, so too is a verdict delivered. The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒelhâ‚پ-~ may connect to Greek ~apollب³mi~ ("to destroy, to kill") and Latin ~aboleإچ~ ("to destroy"), though this etymology remains debated among specialists.
en.fأ¶rdern: From Middle High German ~vأ¼rdern~, from Old High German ~furdiren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~furأ¾rijan~ ("to move forward, to bring to the front"). A denominative of the archaic comparative adverb ~fأ¼rder~ ("further, forward"), itself from Proto-West Germanic *~forأ¾ar~, from PIE *~per-~ ("before, in front, first") with a comparative suffix. The modern spelling with ~أ¶~ â€” rather than the expected ~أ¼~ â€” arose through conflation with the related word ~vorder~ (whence modern ~fordern~, "to demand"), probably reinforced by Middle Low German ~vب«rder~ / ~vب«rderen~. The near-homophone pair ~fأ¶rdern~ ("to promote, support") and ~fordern~ ("to demand") is a persistent pitfall even for native speakers and a nearly untranslatable distinction for learners. Cognate with English ~further~ (both as adverb and as verb "to further a cause"), Dutch ~vorderen~.
en.fأ¼gen: From Middle High German ~fأ¼egen~, ~fuogen~, from Old High German ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچgijanؤ…~, from PIE *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to join, to attach, to fasten"). ~Fأ¼gen~ means "to join, to fit together" and, reflexively, "to submit, to comply" (~sich fأ¼gen~) â€” the person who ~sich fأ¼gt~ joins themselves to the arrangement of fate or authority. The noun ~Fأ¼gung~ means "decree of fate, providence" â€” events that have been joined together by a higher hand. ~Sich in sein Schicksal fأ¼gen~ ("to accept one's fate") is one of the great stoic phrases of the German language. The PIE root *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ connects ~fأ¼gen~ to a surprisingly wide family: Latin ~pؤپx~ ("peace" â€” literally a state of being joined, fastened together) yields English ~peace~, ~pact~, and ~pacify~; Latin ~pangإچ~ ("to fasten, to fix") gives ~compact~ and ~page~ (a sheet fastened in a book). The archaic English verb ~fay~ ("to fit closely, to join") is a direct Germanic cognate of ~fأ¼gen~, from Old English ~fؤ“gan~, though it survives today only in specialized carpentry usage. Dutch ~voegen~ ("to join, to add") is the living cognate. The distance from PIE "to fasten" to German "to accept one's fate" traces a philosophical journey: what is fastened together by destiny cannot be unfastened by human will.
en.fأ¼hlen: From northern Middle High German ~vأ¼elen~ (generally spelled ~vulen~), from Old High German ~fuolen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچlijan~ ("to feel"). The ultimate etymology is uncertain; the root is presumably related to Old Norse ~falma~ ("to grope, feel; to be astonished"), and possibly a secondary causative of a strong verb *~falanؤ…~, from PIE *~pelhâ‚پ-~ ("to stroke"). Possible cognates include Latin ~palpإچ~ ("to stroke, soothe") and Ancient Greek ~psؤ“laphؤپإچ~ ("to feel, grope"). Upper German dialects of Middle High German used ~enphinden~ instead; Modern German ~empfinden~ ("to sense, perceive") has been chiefly confined to higher register as a result, while ~fأ¼hlen~ dominates everyday speech. Cognate with Dutch ~voelen~, English ~feel~, Danish ~fأ¸le~.
en.fأ¼hren: From Middle High German ~vأ¼eren~, from Old High German ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچrijanؤ…~, the causative of *~faranؤ…~ ("to go, travel"), from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). The causative formation â€” "to make go," hence "to lead" â€” follows a productive Germanic pattern in which a long-vowel derivative of a strong verb creates a new weak verb with transitive meaning. Cognate with Dutch ~voeren~, English ~fare~ (from the base verb).
en.fأ¼llen: ~Fأ¼llen~ ("to fill") descends from Middle High German ~vأ¼llen~, Old High German ~fullen~, and Proto-West Germanic *~fullijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullijanؤ…~ ("to make full"). This is the causative formation of *~fullaz~ ("full"), from Proto-Indo-European *~plج¥hâ‚پnأ³s~ ("full"). Cognates include Dutch ~vullen~, Danish ~fylde~, Swedish ~fylla~, and English ~fill~ â€” all from the identical Proto-Germanic causative. English ~fill~ and German ~fأ¼llen~ are thus perfect cognates: each is the causative of its native word for "full" (*~full~ / ~voll~), with the characteristic Proto-Germanic causative suffix *~-ijanؤ…~. Latin ~plenus~ ("full") descends from the same PIE root, giving English ~plenty~, ~complete~, and ~replenish~. The simple parallel â€” "to full" meaning "to make full" â€” survived intact in both West Germanic branches.
en.fأ¼rchten:
From Middle High German ~vأ¼rhten~, from Old High German ~furihtan~, a denominative verb derived from the noun ~Furcht~ ("fear"). The noun ~Furcht~ descends from MHG ~vorht~, ~vorhte~, from OHG ~forhta~, from Proto-Germanic *~furhtإچ~ ("fear"), from PIE *~prج¥k-~ ("to fear"). The modern vowel ~-أ¼-~ reflects mutual influence between the verb (which had umlauted forms) and the noun (which originally had ~-o-~). Cognate with obsolete Dutch ~vrucht~ (still preserved in ~godsvrucht~, "fear of God"), Middle Low German ~vruchte~, and more distantly with English ~fright~, though that word derives from a different nominal stem (Proto-Germanic *~furhtؤ¯ج„~). The Old English adjective ~forht~ ("afraid") is the closest English relative of ~Furcht~ itself.

The PIE root *~prج¥k-~ connects fear to a physical response. In Luther's Bible, ~fأ¼rchten~ famously appears in the phrase ~Fأ¼rchte dich nicht~ ("Fear not"), one of the most frequently repeated commands in Scripture. The reflexive construction ~sich fأ¼rchten~ ("to be afraid") is the standard colloquial form, while the transitive ~fأ¼rchten~ ("to fear something") is somewhat more literary. This split between transitive and reflexive mirrors a common pattern in German emotion verbs: the transitive form treats the emotion as directed outward, while the reflexive form internalizes it.
en.fأ¼ttern:
From Middle High German ~vuotern~, from Old High German ~fuotiren~, derived from the noun ~fuotar~ ("fodder"), from Proto-Germanic *~fإچdrؤ…~ ("fodder"), from Proto-Indo-European *~peh2-~ ("to guard, graze, feed"). Equivalent to ~Futter~ + ~-n~. Cognate with English dialectal ~to fodder~, Dutch ~voeren~ ("to feed").

There is a second, homonymous ~fأ¼ttern~ meaning "to line (a garment)," also from MHG ~vuotern~, but derived from a different (though probably distantly related) noun OHG ~fuotar~ ("sheath, wrapping, lining"). The PIE root *~peh2-~ ("to feed, to guard") is remarkably productive: it also gave rise to English ~food~, ~feed~, ~foster~, ~pasture~, and Latin ~pؤپbulum~ ("food, fodder") and ~pؤپstor~ ("shepherd") -- the shepherd being, at root, the feeder.
en.garantieren:
From French ~garantir~, from Old French ~guarantir~ ("to protect"), from Old French ~guarant~ (whence modern French ~garant~, "guarantor"), from the present participle of an early Romance verb ~garir~ / ~guarir~, from Frankish *~warjan~ ("to protect, to warrant").

The Frankish root *~warjan~ is also the ancestor of English ~warrant~, ~warranty~, and ~ware~ (as in ~beware~) â€” all carrying the sense of protective vigilance. Old French ~guarantir~ also gave English ~guarantee~ (via Anglo-Norman). So German ~garantieren~ and English ~guarantee~ and ~warrant~ are all cousins, their shared Frankish ancestor having entered both languages via Norman French.
en.geben: From Middle High German ~gأ«ben~, from Old High German ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with Dutch ~geven~, English ~give~ (from Old Norse ~gefa~), Swedish ~giva~/~ge~, Danish ~give~.
en.gebieten:
From Middle High German ~gebieten~, from Old High German ~gibiotan~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~bieten~:

- ~bieten~: From MHG ~bieten~, from OHG ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ewdت°-~ ("to be awake, aware"). Cognate with English ~bid~ (in the sense "to offer, to command"), Dutch ~bieden~, Swedish ~bjuda~.
- ~ge-~: From OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ga-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~ ("together, with"), from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). In this verb it adds a sense of collective authority or completive force.

Cognate with Old English ~ؤ،ebؤ“odan~ and Old Saxon ~gibiodan~. The semantic journey from PIE "to be aware" through Proto-Germanic "to offer, to announce" to modern German "to command" reveals a fascinating power dynamic: awareness became announcement, and announcement became command. English ~bid~ preserves both the "offer" sense ("to bid at auction") and the "command" sense ("to bid someone do something"), having merged two originally separate Old English verbs (~biddan~ "to ask" and ~bؤ“odan~ "to offer"). The related noun ~Gebot~ ("commandment") appears in the Ten Commandments (~die Zehn Gebote~), making this one of the most culturally resonant words in German.
en.gebrauchen:
From Old High German ~gibruhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gabrukan~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~brauchen~:

- ~brauchen~: From MHG ~bruchen~, from OHG ~bruhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brukan~ ("to use, employ, consume"), from Proto-Germanic *~brukanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bت°ruHg-~ ("to make use of, to enjoy"). Cognate with English ~brook~ (in the archaic sense "to tolerate, to make use of"), Dutch ~gebruiken~.
- ~ge-~: From OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ga-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~ ("together, with"), from Proto-Indo-European *~kom~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix here adds a sense of collective completion or thoroughness.

The ~ge-~ prefix intensifies the sense of using something to its full purpose. Dutch ~gebruiken~ is a precise cognate, showing the same prefix pattern. English ~brook~, now archaic outside the phrase "will not brook," once meant "to use, to enjoy" -- the same meaning that German ~brauchen~ has retained for centuries. The shift in English from "to use" to "to tolerate" represents a narrowing from active enjoyment to mere endurance.
en.gebأ¤ren: From Middle High German ~gebأ«rn~, from Old High German ~gibأ«ran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gaberan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gaberanؤ…~, a compound of *~ga-~ + *~beranؤ…~ ("to carry, bear"), the latter from PIE *~bت°أ©reti~, from *~bت°er-~ ("to carry, bear"). The prefix *~ga-~ (the ancestor of modern ~ge-~, from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~, "beside, near, by, with") here marks completion or resultant state. The simplex *~bأ¤ren~ ("to give birth") is extinct in standard Modern German; ~gebأ¤ren~ is effectively the only survivor of the family in the birthing sense. English ~bear~ (to give birth) and Latin ~ferإچ~ ("I carry") are cognates through *~bت°er-~, making ~gebأ¤ren~, English ~bear~, Latin ~ferإچ~, and Greek ~pherإچ~ all distant relatives. One of the oldest and most archaic verbs in the language â€” its strong ablaut class (class IV) and the now-defunct simplex root attest to enormous age. Cognate with English ~bear~, Dutch ~baren~, Gothic ~bairan~.
en.gefallen:
From Middle High German ~gevallen~, from Old High German ~gifallan~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallanؤ…~, from PIE *~pإچl-~ ("to fall"). A class VII strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~vallen~, English ~fall~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, ~ga-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). Here the prefix marks completion or arrival at a state â€” the terminative aspect.

The semantic leap is striking: the root means "to fall" and the prefix marks completion or arrival, yet the compound means "to please." The likely bridge is the idiom of falling into favor â€” something that "falls" pleasingly to one's taste. Medieval German ~gevallen~ already carried this sense of "coming to please, meeting with approval." The dative construction ~mir gefأ¤llt~ ("it pleases me," literally "it falls to me") preserves this spatial metaphor of something landing agreeably within one's sphere. Cognate with Dutch ~gevallen~ ("to please, to fall").
en.gefأ¤hrden:
From late Middle High German ~gevؤ“rden~, derived from the noun ~Gefahr~ ("danger"). The noun ~Gefahr~ descends from MHG ~gevؤپre~, ~gevأ¦re~, from OHG ~fؤپra~, from Proto-Germanic *~fؤ“rإچ~ ("danger, ambush"). The ~ge-~ prefix (from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~, "beside, near, by, with") here serves as a collective intensifier on the noun, and the verb is formed by adding the ~-en~ suffix to the noun. An alternative derivation treats ~gefأ¤hrden~ as a prefixed form of the now-obsolete verb ~fأ¤hrden~ with the prefix ~ge-~.

Cognate with English ~fear~ and Dutch ~gevaar~ ("danger"). The connection between German ~Gefahr~ ("danger") and English ~fear~ is a striking example of semantic parallel: both derive from the same Proto-Germanic root *~fؤ“rإچ~, and both revolve around the concept of threat and peril. The Proto-Germanic word originally meant something like "ambush" or "hostile approach," which naturally split into the danger itself (~Gefahr~) and the emotion it provokes (~fear~). Old English ~fا£r~ meant "sudden danger, calamity" before shifting to the emotional response. The verb ~gefأ¤hrden~ ("to endanger, to jeopardize") is thus etymologically something like "to put into a state of ambush."
en.gehen: From Middle High German ~gan~, ~gen~, from Old High German ~gan~, ~gen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave"). The conjugation ~gen~ instead of ~gan~ is of Bavarian origin. The ~-h-~ was introduced into the spelling by analogy with ~sehen~, in which it had become mute but was retained in spelling. Cognate with Dutch ~gaan~, Low German ~gaan~, ~gahn~, English ~go~, Swedish and Danish ~gأ¥~.
en.gehأ¶ren:
From Middle High German ~gehإ“ren~ ("to listen to, obey"), from Old High German ~gihإچrian~ ("to hearken to"), from Proto-West Germanic *~gahauت€ijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gahauzijanؤ…~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~hأ¶ren~:

- ~hأ¶ren~: From MHG ~hإ“ren~, from OHG ~hإچren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ل¸±hâ‚‚owsyأ©ti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), from *~hâ‚‚eل¸±-~ ("sharp") + *~hâ‚‚ows-~ ("ear"). Cognate with Dutch ~horen~, English ~hear~, Danish ~hأ¸re~.
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

The semantic shift from "to listen to, obey" to "to belong to" reflects the medieval feudal sense of owing obedience: what one "hearkens to" is what one is subject to, and hence belongs to.
en.gelangen: From Middle High German ~gelangen~, from Old High German ~gilangإچn~, synchronically analyzed in Modern German as an ablaut formation from ~gelingen~ ("to succeed"), though the historical relationship is with ~langen~ ("to reach"). ~Langen~ derives from Middle High German ~langen~, from Old High German ~langؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~langإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~langإچnؤ…~, a denominative of *~langaz~ ("long"), from PIE *~dlongت°os~ ("long"). The prefix ~ge-~ (from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~, "beside, near, by, with") marks completion or arrival â€” "to reach all the way to a destination." The semantic path is vivid: to "long" (reach, extend) is to span the distance between oneself and an endpoint, and ~gelangen~ captures that arrival. Cognate with Dutch ~geraken~ (parallel compound), English ~long~ (via *~langaz~).
en.geleiten:
From MHG ~geleiten~, from OHG ~gileitan~, ~gileiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~galaidijanؤ…~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~leiten~:

- ~leiten~: From MHG ~leiten~, from OHG ~leiten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laidijanؤ…~, a causative of *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to pass, to go by"), from PIE *~leyt-~ ("to go, depart, die"). Cognate with Old English ~lا£dan~ (whence English ~lead~), Old Saxon ~lؤ“dian~, Old Norse ~leiأ°a~.
- ~ge-~: From PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of collective completion -- accompanying thoroughly.

~Geleiten~ means "to escort, to accompany, to conduct" -- to lead someone with care through or toward something. The ~ge-~ prefix intensifies the companionship aspect: not just leading, but accompanying throughout. The word carries a formal, even ceremonial tone: one ~geleitet~ a dignitary to their seat or the deceased to their grave (~das letzte Geleit~, "the last escort"). The derived noun ~Geleit~ ("escort, safe conduct") was a legal term in medieval Germany -- a ~Geleitbrief~ guaranteed safe passage through a lord's territory.
en.gelingen:
From Middle High German ~gelingen~, from Old High German ~gilingan~, a prefixed form of ~lingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lingwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lingwanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚پlengت·ت°-~ ("lightweight; swift"). Compound of ~ge-~ + the archaic simplex ~lingen~:

- ~lingen~: From OHG ~lingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lingwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lingwanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚پlengت·ت°-~ ("lightweight; swift"). The simplex ~lingen~ (meaning "to make progress, proceed, succeed") survived into Middle Low German but is no longer a standalone verb in Modern German.
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

The semantic development from "swift, lightweight" to "to proceed, succeed" reflects a conceptual link between ease of movement and successful outcome â€” what moves swiftly meets no resistance and therefore succeeds. ~Gelingen~ is an impersonal verb in Modern German: one says ~es gelingt mir~ ("it succeeds for me") rather than constructing a personal subject, a grammatical relic of the verb's impersonal origins. Cognate with Middle Low German ~lingen~, Yiddish ~×’×¢×œ×™× ×’×¢×ں~ (~gelingen~).
en.gelten: From Middle High German ~gelten~, from Old High German ~geltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~geldanؤ…~, possibly from PIE *~gت°eldت°-~. The semantic range has narrowed over time: in Old High German, ~geltan~ meant "to pay, repay, be worth," while Modern German ~gelten~ centers on "to be valid, to count, to apply." Cognate with Dutch ~gelden~, English ~yield~ (from the same root, with a semantic shift from "paying" to "giving way"), Icelandic ~gjalda~.
en.genehmigen:
From ~genehm~ ("acceptable, agreeable") + ~-igen~ (verb-forming suffix). The adjective ~genehm~ descends from Middle High German ~genaeme~, ultimately from the root of ~nehmen~ ("to take"):

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nemen~, from OHG ~neman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). Cognate with English ~nim~ ("to take, filch"), Dutch ~nemen~.

The semantic thread is elegant: something ~genehm~ is literally "takeable" or "acceptable" -- that which one is willing to take. To ~genehmigen~ is therefore "to make acceptable," hence "to approve, to authorize." The PIE root *~nem-~ had the intriguing double meaning of both giving and taking what is owed, which survives in Greek ~nemein~ ("to distribute, to manage") and ~nomos~ ("law, custom") -- the source of English ~economy~, ~nomad~, and ~astronomy~. To approve something is, at its deepest root, to declare it lawfully distributed.
en.generieren: From Latin ~generؤپre~ ("to beget, to produce, to generate"), with the German ~-ieren~ suffix replacing the Latin infinitive ending. Latin ~generؤپre~ derives from ~genus~ ("birth, descent, origin, kind"), from PIE *~اµenhâ‚پ-~ ("to beget, to produce, to give birth"). This PIE root is extraordinarily productive across Indo-European languages, yielding Latin ~genus~, ~gens~ ("clan"), and ~gignere~ ("to beget"), Greek ~genesis~ ("origin"), and English ~kin~, ~kind~, ~king~ (literally "son of the clan"), and ~gentle~ (via Old French from Latin ~gentilis~). German borrowed ~generieren~ as a learned term, and it has seen explosive growth in the age of computing: ~generierte Inhalte~ ("generated content") is now commonplace. Related German borrowings from the same Latin root include ~Generation~, ~Generator~, ~generativ~, and ~Genre~ (the last via French). The word ~genieren~ ("to embarrass"), despite its similar appearance, comes from a completely different French source and is a false friend.
en.genieأںen:
From Middle High German ~geniezen~, from Old High German ~giniozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ganeutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~neutanؤ…~, from PIE *~newd-~ ("to seize, grasp, use"). Compound of ~ge-~ + the archaic simplex ~nieأںen~ ("to use, enjoy"):

- ~nieأںen~: From MHG ~nieب¥en~, from OHG ~nioب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~neutanؤ…~, from PIE *~newd-~ ("to seize, grasp, use").
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

The simplex ~nieأںen~ has largely vanished from Modern German, surviving only in derivatives: ~genieأںen~ ("to enjoy"), ~Nutznieأںer~ ("beneficiary"), and the legal term ~Nieأںbrauch~ ("usufruct," literally "use-enjoyment"). The PIE root *~newd-~ is the same one that underlies ~nutzen~ â€” both verbs trace to the same ancient idea of taking what is useful. German thus has two descendants of the same root: the everyday ~nutzen~ ("to use") and the more exalted ~genieأںen~ ("to enjoy"), the latter having acquired a sense of pleasurable fullness through the completive ~ge-~ prefix. Cognate with Dutch ~genieten~, Yiddish ~genisn~.
en.genأ¼gen: From Middle High German ~genأ¼egen~, from Old High German ~ginuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ganإچgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ganإچgijanؤ…~, from the adjective *~ganإچgaz~ ("enough, sufficient"), ultimately from PIE *~hâ‚‚ehâ‚‚nأ³ل¸±e~ ("he has reached, he has attained"), the perfective of *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ ("to reach, to attain"). The semantic evolution is elegant: what is "enough" is what has been ~reached~ â€” the point of sufficiency. The collective prefix *~ga-~ (modern ~ge-~) intensifies the sense: not merely reached but ~fully~ reached. The related adjective ~genug~ ("enough") descends from the same Proto-Germanic adjective *~ganإچgaz~. English ~enough~ is a direct cognate (from Old English ~genإچg~, from the same source), as are Dutch ~genoeg~, Icelandic ~nog~, and Norwegian ~nok~. The verb ~genأ¼gen~ is formally a ~-jan- causative: it meant "to cause to be enough, to satisfy," though in modern German it is simply intransitive ("to suffice, to be enough"). The related adjective ~genأ¼gsam~ ("frugal, easily satisfied") preserves the older sense beautifully: someone who is ~genأ¼gsam~ is someone for whom the point of ~enough~ is easily reached. Cognate with Dutch ~genoegen~ ("to satisfy").
en.geraten:
From Middle High German ~gerؤپten~, from Old High German ~girؤپtan~ ("to counsel, decide, turn towards, take care of"), from Proto-West Germanic *~garؤپdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~garؤ“danؤ…~ ("to have a plan for, to take forethought of, to purpose, to intend"), equivalent to ~ge-~ + ~raten~:

- ~raten~: From MHG ~rؤپten~, from OHG ~rؤپtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rؤپdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ“danؤ…~ ("to decide, to advise"), from PIE *~Hrehâ‚پdت°-~ ("to think, arrange").
- ~ge-~: From PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). As a historical inseparable prefix, ~ge-~ originally conveyed collectivity or completion.

~Geraten~ means "to turn out, to succeed; to thrive; to get into (a state)" â€” senses that have drifted far from the root verb ~raten~ ("to advise, to guess"). The semantic path runs from "to counsel, decide" through "to arrange oneself, to find one's way" to "to turn out (well or badly)." The construction ~in etwas geraten~ ("to get into something") â€” ~in Schwierigkeiten geraten~ ("to get into trouble"), ~in Vergessenheit geraten~ ("to fall into oblivion"), ~auأںer Kontrolle geraten~ ("to get out of control") â€” is among the most productive in modern German. Cognate with Middle Low German ~gerأ¢den~, Dutch ~geraden~ ("to form an assumption, guess"), and Gothic ~garؤ“dan~ ("to provide for, intend"). English ~read~ is a distant cousin: it descends from the same PIE root *~Hrehâ‚پdت°-~, having shifted from "to advise, interpret" to "to interpret written symbols" â€” a semantic leap that German ~raten~ never made.
en.geschehen:
From Middle High German ~geschehen~, ~geschؤ“n~, ~geschegen~, from Old High German ~giskehan~, ~scehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skehan~ ("to spring, come up, emerge, happen"). In contrast to most German ~ge-~ verbs, the ~ge-~ in ~geschehen~ is not a separable or inseparable prefix added to an existing simplex â€” it fused into the root at the Proto-Germanic level, and no simplex *~schehen~ exists or has ever existed in German. The whole word is the root.

The Proto-West Germanic form derives from a root meaning "to spring, shoot, come quickly into being" â€” a vivid physical image for the sudden occurrence of events. The same root appears in Old High German ~scehan~ ("to happen"), which co-existed with the prefixed form before the ~ge-~ variant won out entirely. Cognate with Dutch ~geschieden~ ("to happen, to occur; to divorce" â€” the two senses having diverged from the same root). The related noun ~Geschehnis~ ("occurrence, event") and the participial use of ~das Geschehene~ ("what has happened, the done deed") preserve the verb's core sense of things irrevocably coming to pass.
en.gestalten:
Denominal verb from the noun ~Gestalt~ ("form, shape, figure") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Gestalt~ itself descends from the 14th century as a participial substantive of ~stellen~ â€” literally "the placed, arranged thing" â€” from Middle High German ~gestalt~ (past participle used as a noun), from ~ge-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~, from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put").
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

German Wiktionary notes that while ~gestalten~ is structurally a conversion from the noun ~Gestalt~, an archaic simplex ~stalten~ once existed independently before vanishing. The noun ~Gestalt~ achieved global fame through Gestalt psychology (~Gestaltpsychologie~), founded in the early 20th century, which took the word's core sense â€” a whole that is more than the sum of its arranged parts â€” as its central insight. The verb ~gestalten~ thus means not merely "to form" but "to actively shape into a coherent whole." Cognate with Dutch ~gestalte~ (noun).
en.gestatten:
From Middle High German ~gestaten~, from Old High German ~gistatإچn~, related to ~Statt~ ("place, stead"), from MHG ~stat~, from OHG ~stat~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stadi~, from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~, from PIE *~stأ©hâ‚‚tis~ ("standing, place"). Cognate with English ~stead~.

~Gestatten~ means "to permit, to allow" -- a formal, even courtly alternative to ~erlauben~. The semantic path from "place" to "permission" runs through the idea of granting someone a place or a standing: to ~gestatten~ is, etymologically, to give someone a stead, a place where they may act. The prefix ~ge-~ here functions as a perfective marker (as in OHG ~gistatإچn~), completing the act of granting. The polite formula ~Gestatten Sie?~ ("May I?" or "Do you permit?") is still common in formal German, spoken when entering a room or introducing oneself. English ~instead~ preserves the same root: "in (the) stead" means "in (the) place (of)."
en.gewinnen: From Middle High German ~gewinnen~, ~winnen~, from Old High German ~winnan~, ~giwinnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~winnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~winnanؤ…~, from PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to strive, wish, desire, love"). The PIE root *~wenhâ‚پ-~ conveys striving and desire, and its descendants branched widely: Latin ~venus~ ("love, desire") and the goddess ~Venus~ descend from the same root, as does English ~win~. The semantic shift from "to strive, toil" to "to win, gain" reflects how effort became identified with its reward â€” the same trajectory seen in archaic OHG ~winnan~ ("to struggle, suffer") moving toward Modern German ~gewinnen~ ("to win, to obtain"). The ~ge-~ prefix adds a collective or completive sense. Cognate with Dutch ~winnen~, Low German ~winnen~, Old English ~ؤ،ewinnan~ (English ~win~), Norwegian ~vinne~, and Swedish ~vinna~. Also related to English ~Venus~ and Latin ~venus~ via PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~.
en.gewأ¤hren:
From Middle High German ~gewern~, from Old High German ~giwerؤ“n~, ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *~warjan~ ("to defend, ward off"), from Proto-Germanic *~warjanؤ…~, from an ~o~-grade ablaut of PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard"). The ~ge-~ prefix (from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~, "beside, near, by, with") adds a completive or collective sense. Cognate with English ~guarantee~ and ~warrant~, both of which entered English via Old French but trace back to the same Frankish Germanic root.

The semantic evolution from "to defend" to "to grant" is a fascinating case of perspective shift: what was originally an act of protection (warding off threats) came to be understood from the beneficiary's perspective as an act of granting safety or permission. English ~ware~ ("cautious"), ~wary~, ~ward~, and ~guard~ (via French, from Frankish *~wardإچn~) all descend from the same PIE root *~wer-~. Even ~garage~ is a distant relative, from French ~garer~ ("to shelter"), from Frankish *~warإچn~. The modern German phrase ~Gewأ¤hr leisten~ ("to guarantee") preserves the original protective sense, while ~jemandem etwas gewأ¤hren~ ("to grant someone something") reflects the completed semantic shift from protection to permission.
en.gewأ¤hrleisten: A compound of ~Gewأ¤hr~ ("guarantee, warranty") + ~leisten~ ("to accomplish, perform"). Not a prefixed verb but a verbal noun compound. ~Gewأ¤hr~ derives from Old High German (a related form to ~wahren~, "to safeguard"), ultimately connected to Proto-West Germanic *~warإچn~ from Proto-Germanic *~warإچ~ ("care, attention"), possibly from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard"). ~Leisten~ derives from Middle High German ~leisten~, from Old High German ~leisten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laistijan~, originally "to follow a track" (see *~laistaz~, "footprint"), evolving to "to accomplish, fulfill." The compound literally means "to perform a guarantee" â€” to carry out the act of warranting something. The noun ~Gewأ¤hr~ shares its root with ~wahr~ ("true") and ~wahren~ ("to safeguard"), a cluster of words about truthfulness, reliability, and protection. Cognate with Dutch ~garanderen~ (parallel meaning via different roots), English ~warranty~ (from a parallel Germanic source through Old French).
en.gewأ¶hnen:
From Middle High German ~gewenen~, a byform of ~gewennen~, from Old High German ~giwennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gawannjan~, a prefixed form of *~wannjan~. The base verb traces to Proto-Germanic *~wanjanؤ…~ ("to accustom"), from *~wanaz~ ("accustomed"), from PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to love, to desire, to strive for"). Cognate with English ~wean~ (originally "to accustom," later narrowed to "to accustom a child away from the breast"), Dutch ~wennen~ or ~gewennen~. The prefix ~ge-~ (from PIE *~ل¸±أ³m~, "beside, near, by, with") adds a perfective or collective nuance.

The modern spelling with ~أ¶~ is a historical curiosity: it results from sporadic rounding of MHG /e/ near labial consonants, reinforced by association with related words that had original /o/, such as ~Gewohnheit~ ("habit"), ~gewohnt~ ("accustomed"), and ~gewأ¶hnlich~ ("usual, ordinary"). Speakers reinterpreted ~gewأ¶hnlich~ as a derivative of ~gewأ¶hnen~, but historically it descends independently from MHG ~gewonlich~. The PIE root *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to love, to desire") connects ~gewأ¶hnen~ to Latin ~Venus~ (goddess of love) and ~venia~ ("favor"), revealing that at the deepest level, becoming accustomed to something was once understood as growing to love it.
en.gieأںen:
From Middle High German ~giezen~, from Old High German ~giozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geutan~ ("to pour"), from Proto-Germanic *~geutanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gت°ewd-~ ("to pour").

Cognate with Dutch ~gieten~, Old Saxon ~giotan~, Old English ~geotan~, Old Norse ~gjota~ (whence Danish ~gyde~, Swedish ~gjuta~), Gothic ~giutan~. Further Indo-European cognates include Latin ~fundإچ~ ("to pour, to smelt"), Greek ~cheo~ ("to pour"), and Sanskrit ~juhoti~ ("he sacrifices" -- by pouring libations). English ~gush~ is a related form.

The sense of "pouring" metals and glass is attested since Old High German times through the participle ~gigozzan~ ("poured, cast"). This makes ~gieأںen~ one of the oldest technical terms still in daily use: a ~Gieأںkanne~ ("watering can") and a ~Gieأںerei~ ("foundry") both preserve the ancient act of controlled pouring. The connection to Latin ~fundإچ~ (whence English ~found~, as in founding metals, ~foundry~, and ~fusion~) shows that pouring and casting were among the earliest specialized activities to receive their own vocabulary across the Indo-European world.
en.glauben:
From Middle High German ~gelouben~, ~gelأ¶uben~, from Old High German ~gilouben~, from Proto-West Germanic *~galaubijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~galaubijanؤ…~, a compound of *~ga-~ + *~laubijanؤ…~. The root *~laubijanؤ…~ derives from *~laubإچ~ ("permission, leave"), from PIE *~lewbت°-~ ("to like, desire, love"). The semantic trajectory runs from "to hold dear, to love" through "to grant leave, to permit" to "to believe, trust" â€” a striking path from affection to credence. The Upper German form with ~-au-~ (rather than the Central German umlauted variant) was reinforced by the related noun ~Glaube~ ("belief, faith"), while the umlaut survives in derived forms like ~glأ¤ubig~ ("devout, credulous") and ~aberglأ¤ubisch~ ("superstitious"). The connection to love is preserved cross-linguistically: English ~believe~ descends from the same PIE root, and English ~love~ is a direct cognate of the *~lewbت°-~ family. Cognate with Bavarian ~glaubn~, Dutch ~geloven~, Low German ~glأ¶ven~, Gothic ~galaubjan~, and English ~believe~.

The pairing of ~glauben~ and ~believe~ conceals a puzzle: both words share the same Proto-Germanic root *~laubijanؤ…~, yet they wear different prefixes. German chose *~ga-~ (which became ~ge-~, then eroded to the ~g-~ in ~glauben~), while English chose *~bi-~ (which became ~be-~). Both prefixes were available in both languages â€” Old English had ~gelؤ«efan~ (with ~ge-~) alongside ~belب³fan~ (with ~be-~), and it was ~belب³fan~ that won out. Old High German settled on ~gilouben~ (with ~gi-~, later ~ge-~), and that form prevailed, the prefix gradually wearing down: ~gilouben~ â†’ ~gelouben~ â†’ ~glouben~ â†’ ~glauben~. The two prefixes were not even doing very different work: in early Germanic, both *~bi-~ and *~ga-~ could serve as perfective or intensifying markers on verbs, nudging the meaning from "to hold dear" toward the completed state of "to have come to trust." It is one of those cases where two sister dialects made an arbitrary lexical choice and then each committed to it for a millennium. The result is that German learners of English â€” and English learners of German â€” routinely fail to recognize ~glauben~ and ~believe~ as cognates, when in fact they are the same word dressed in different prefixes. Dutch ~geloven~ sided with German (keeping ~ge-~), Gothic ~galaubjan~ likewise, while the Scandinavian languages abandoned both forms in favor of unrelated words (~tro~ in Swedish, Danish, and Norwegian, from Old Norse ~trأ؛a~, cognate with English ~true~ and ~trust~). So the modern Germanic languages offer three different solutions to the same semantic need: German and Dutch built "believe" from "love" with ~ge-~, English built it from "love" with ~be-~, and Scandinavian started over entirely with "trust." The erosion of *~ga-~ to ~g-~ in ~glauben~ is not an isolated accident â€” the same prefix met the same fate across the Germanic family, and English took the process one step further. English ~enough~ and German ~genug~ are cognates from Proto-Germanic *~ganإچgaz~ (*~ga-~ + *~nإچgaz~, from *~nahanؤ…~, "to suffice"). In Old English the word was still ~genإچg~, transparently prefixed; by Middle English it had weakened to ~inough~ and ~ynough~; modern ~enough~ retains only a silent-looking ~e-~ as the last ghost of the prefix. So where German wore *~ga-~ down to ~g-~ (~glauben~, ~genug~), English wore it all the way to nothing â€” or nearly nothing. (A tempting parallel is ~yesterday~/~gestern~, but that is a false friend: the ~ge-~ in ~gestern~ is not the prefix at all but part of the root, from PIE *~اµت°dyes-~ + the comparative suffix *~-tero-~, cognate with Latin ~herؤ«~ and Greek ~khthأ©s~.)
en.gleichen:
From MHG ~gelؤ«chen~, from OHG ~gilؤ«hhon~, ~gilihhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~galؤ«kإچnؤ…~ ("to make alike, to resemble"), derived from *~galؤ«kaz~ ("alike, similar"), a compound of *~ga-~ (collective prefix) + *~lؤ«kam~ ("body, form"), from PIE *~leyg-~ ("image, likeness, similarity"). Cognate with Dutch ~gelijken~, English ~like~ (both the adjective and the verb), Old English ~gelؤ«cian~ ("to please") and ~gelؤ«c~ ("alike"), Old Norse ~glأ­kr~, Danish ~ligne~.

~Gleichen~ means "to resemble, to be equal to" and is a strong verb (glich, geglichen), one of the few strong verbs derived from a denominal/deadjectival base. The adjective ~gleich~ ("equal, same, alike") lost its prefix ~ge-~ through phonetic erosion in Early New High German, but the verb preserved it as the initial ~gl-~. The underlying metaphor is illuminating: PIE *~leyg-~ meant "body, form" â€” to be alike was originally to share the same bodily form. This root produced English ~like~, ~alike~, ~likeness~, ~lich~ (a corpse â€” literally "a body"), the suffix ~-ly~ (originally "having the body/form of"), and ~ilk~ ("same kind"). The word ~like~ in English descends from the same Proto-Germanic *~galؤ«kaz~, meaning that ~gleichen~ and ~like~ are doublets â€” siblings that traveled different phonetic paths from the same ancestor.
en.glأ¤nzen:
From Middle High German ~glenzen~, from Old High German ~glanzen~, from Proto-Germanic *~glantaz~ ("shining, bright"), from Proto-Indo-European *~gت°lond-o-~, from the root *~gت°lend-~ ("to shine, to gleam").

This verb belongs to a rich cluster of Germanic "gl-" words denoting light and vision: ~gleiأںen~ ("to glitter"), ~glimmen~ ("to glow"), ~glitzern~ ("to sparkle"), ~Glut~ ("embers"), and ~Glas~ ("glass," originally meaning "amber" or "something shiny"). English preserves the same pattern in ~gleam~, ~glitter~, ~glow~, ~gloss~, ~glint~, and ~glisten~. This "gl-" phonestheme -- where an initial consonant cluster carries consistent semantic associations without being a true morpheme -- is one of the most striking sound-meaning patterns in the Germanic languages.
en.gratulieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~grؤپtulؤپrؤ«~ ("to wish joy, to congratulate"), from ~grؤپtus~ ("pleasing, thankful"), from PIE *~gت·erH-~ ("to praise, to welcome"). Cognate with Latin ~grؤپtia~ ("grace, favor"), whence English ~grace~, ~grateful~, ~gratitude~, and ~congratulate~.

~Gratulieren~ is used with the dative case: ~jemandem gratulieren~ ("to congratulate someone"), most commonly ~zum Geburtstag gratulieren~ ("to wish a happy birthday"). The PIE root *~gت·erH-~ ("to praise") is the source of an enormous family of "positivity" words: Latin ~grؤپtia~ produced English ~grace~, ~gracious~, ~grateful~, ~gratis~ (originally "out of kindness"), and ~ingrate~; the same root likely produced Sanskrit ~gإ«rtأ­-~ ("praise, welcome").
en.greifen:
From Middle High German ~grأ®fen~, from Old High German ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~grijpen~, Low German ~griepen~, English ~gripe~, and Old Norse ~gripa~.

The PIE root *~gت°reyb-~ is attested in Baltic cognates as well: Lithuanian ~griل؛½bti~ ("to seize") and ~graibأ½ti~ share the same ancestry, as does Latvian ~gribأھt~ ("to wish, desire") â€” a striking semantic divergence in which the Baltic branch preserved a sense of striving or wanting from what was originally a physical act of grasping. German has developed ~greifen~ into a rich family of compounds: ~begreifen~ ("to comprehend," literally "to grasp around"), ~eingreifen~ ("to intervene," literally "to grasp in"), ~angreifen~ ("to attack, to corrode"), and ~ergreifen~ ("to seize, to move emotionally"). The idea that understanding is a form of grasping â€” ~begreifen~ â€” is one of the most striking conceptual metaphors in the language, and it is ancient. The ablaut series ~greift~/~griff~/~gegriffen~ follows the class I pattern, the same as ~reiten~ and ~schneiden~.
en.grأ¼nden:
Inherited from Middle High German ~grأ¼nden~, from Old High German ~grunten~ ("to fathom, to investigate"). A denominative verb formed from ~Grund~ ("ground, bottom, foundation") with derivational umlaut (u â†’ أ¼):

~Grund~ traces to OHG ~grunt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grundu~, from Proto-Germanic *~grunduz~, from PIE *~gت°rج¥mtu-~. Cognate with West Frisian ~grأ»n~, Dutch ~grond~, and English ~ground~.

The OHG verb ~grunten~ meant "to fathom" â€” to sound the depth of water, to investigate the bottom of a matter. This sense still echoes in ~ergrأ¼nden~ ("to fathom, to get to the bottom of something"). ~grأ¼nden~, by contrast, shifted from probing the ground to creating it: "to found, to establish" â€” to put something on a secure foundation. The same metaphor appears in English ~ground~ (as in "to ground one's argument"). The umlaut â€” ~Grund~ â†’ ~grأ¼nden~ â€” is a hallmark of denominative weak verbs in German: it signals that the verb was derived from the noun rather than inherited directly. The noun ~Grund~ itself remains semantically rich: it serves simultaneously as "ground" (the earth underfoot), "bottom" (the floor of a lake), "reason" (the ground of an argument), and "foundation" (the basis of a building) â€” all the more remarkable because these senses converge in a single word that goes back to a PIE root simply meaning "earth, soil." Cognate with Dutch ~gronden~.
en.grأ¼أںen:
From Middle High German ~grأ¼ezen~, from Old High German ~gruozzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grإچtijan~. The noun ~Gruأں~ ("greeting") is a deverbal form from Middle High German ~gruoب¥~. Cognate with Dutch ~groeten~, West Frisian ~groetsje~, and English ~greet~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic root, without any prefix).

The semantic range of ~grأ¼أںen~ is narrower than its English cognate ~greet~: where English ~greet~ can mean both "to welcome" and "to react with emotion" (as in "the news was greeted with dismay"), German ~grأ¼أںen~ remains anchored in the act of salutation â€” a wave, a nod, a spoken hello. The compound ~begrأ¼أںen~ adds the ~be-~ prefix to make the greeting act outwardly directed and transitive: one ~grأ¼أںt~ in general, but one ~begrأ¼أںt~ a specific person or proposal. The Proto-West Germanic root *~grإچtijan~ likely carried the original sense of physical approach or hailing â€” a greeting as an act of reaching toward someone.
en.gucken:
From Middle High German ~gأ¼cken~, ~guggen~ ("to look inquisitively, to peek, to peer"), of uncertain origin. Perhaps from a specialized use of MHG ~gأ¼cken~, ~gucken~ ("to cuckoo, to call or cry like a cuckoo"), from Old High German ~guckإچn~, ~gukkإچn~ ("to cuckoo") â€” the semantic link being the cuckoo's habit of poking its head out from cover, or the notion of peeping out curiously. The common pronunciation with initial ~k-~ (also spelled ~kucken~) is often attributed to influence from Low German ~kieken~ (from Middle Low German ~kؤ«ken~), though this pronunciation extends surprisingly far southward (compare Luxembourgish ~kucken~ and Dutch ~kijken~).

~Gucken~ is the everyday, colloquial word for "to look" in northern and central Germany, while southern German and Austrian varieties prefer ~schauen~. It rarely appears in formal writing, where ~sehen~, ~blicken~, or ~schauen~ take its place. Despite its informal register, ~gucken~ is enormously productive: ~angucken~ ("to look at"), ~zugucken~ ("to watch"), ~weggucken~ ("to look away"), ~durchgucken~ ("to look through"), ~Guckloch~ ("peephole"). The compound ~Kuckuck~ ("cuckoo") â€” if the etymological connection holds â€” would make ~gucken~ one of the few verbs potentially derived from a bird's call. The spelling debate between ~gucken~ (standard) and ~kucken~ (reflecting actual pronunciation) has generated more heat among German orthography enthusiasts than one might expect for a monosyllabic verb.
en.gأ¶nnen:
From MHG ~gunnen~, from OHG ~giunnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gaunnan~ ("to grant"), from the prefix *~ga-~ + *~unnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~unnanؤ…~ ("to grant"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒnehâ‚‚-~ ("to enjoy"). Cognate with Dutch ~gunnen~, Yiddish ~ginen~. The PIE root also appears in Greek ~oninؤ“mi~ ("to be of use, to help") and ~aponؤ“to~ ("to have joy of something").

~Gأ¶nnen~ is one of those German words that English simply cannot translate with a single term. It means "to not begrudge someone something" â€” to be genuinely happy that another person has or enjoys something. ~Ich gأ¶nne dir den Erfolg~ means "I'm happy for your success" with no trace of envy. The negation ~missgأ¶nnen~ ("to begrudge, to envy") and the noun ~Missgunst~ ("envy, resentment") expose the default expectation: ~Gunst~ ("favor, goodwill") is the positive baseline. The reflexive ~sich etwas gأ¶nnen~ means "to treat oneself, to allow oneself a pleasure" â€” again, granting oneself permission to enjoy. The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒnehâ‚‚-~ ("to enjoy") has been remarkably faithful to its original meaning across five millennia, even as the social dimension â€” the emphasis on non-envious generosity â€” is a distinctly Germanic innovation.
en.haben:
From Middle High German ~haben~, from Old High German ~habؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~habjanؤ…~, from PIE *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to grasp, seize"). Cognate with Dutch ~hebben~, English ~have~, Danish ~have~, Old Norse ~hafa~, Gothic ًگŒ·ًگŒ°ًگŒ±ًگŒ°ًگŒ½ (~haban~).

~Haben~ and Latin ~habؤ“إچ~ ("to have") are one of the most celebrated false-cognate pairs in Indo-European linguistics. Despite near-identical spelling, identical meaning, and centuries of scholarly confusion, the two words descend from entirely different PIE roots: ~haben~ from *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to grasp, seize") and ~habؤ“إچ~ from *~gت°ehâ‚پbت°-~ ("to grab, take"). The resemblance is pure coincidence â€” the kind of accident that makes historical linguistics treacherous. ~Haben~'s actual Latin cousin is ~capiإچ~ ("I take, I seize"), the ancestor of English ~captive~, ~capture~, ~capable~, ~captain~, and ~accept~ (from ~ad-~ + ~capiإچ~). German ~akzeptieren~ thus descends from the same PIE root as ~haben~, via Latin rather than Germanic inheritance. The same root also gives German ~heben~ ("to lift") â€” to have and to heave are, at bottom, the same word: to grasp something is both to possess it and to lift it.

Perhaps the deepest irony is that Germanic and Romance languages independently chose their respective "have" verbs â€” etymologically unrelated â€” to build compound past tenses: German ~ich habe gesungen~ and French ~j'ai chantأ©~ are structurally identical constructions ("I have sung"), yet ~haben~ and ~avoir~ (from ~habؤ“إچ~) share no common ancestor. This parallel development is a hallmark of the Western European ~Sprachbund~ â€” a linguistic area in which neighboring but unrelated languages converge on shared grammatical structures through centuries of contact. The ~Sprachbund~ also produced the definite article, the relative clause with a relative pronoun, and the distinction between ~sein~ and ~haben~ as perfect auxiliaries (compare French ~je suis allأ©~ / ~j'ai mangأ©~ with German ~ich bin gegangen~ / ~ich habe gegessen~) â€” all features that feel natural to European speakers but are typologically unusual worldwide. That two unrelated "have" verbs independently became the scaffolding for compound tenses across the Roman ~limes~ is a reminder that grammar can be borrowed just as readily as vocabulary.
en.haften:
From ~Haft~ + ~-en~. ~Haft~ descends from MHG ~haft~, ~hafte~ ("captivity"), from OHG ~haft~, ~hafta~, immediately cognate with Middle Dutch ~hachte~, Middle Low German ~hafte~, Old English ~hأ¦ft~ ("captive"), and further with Latin ~captus~ ("seized") and Old Irish ~cacht~ ("captive"). The Latin and Germanic forms both derive from PIE *~khâ‚‚p-tأ³-~ (from *~kehâ‚‚p-~ "to seize, grasp"), making ~haften~ and English ~captive~ cognates from the same root.

~Haften~ means "to stick, adhere, cling" and "to be liable, to be responsible" -- two senses united by the "captivity" root. The "adhesion" sense developed from the idea of being held fast, clinging to a surface. The legal meaning is especially productive: ~haftbar~ ("liable"), ~Haftpflicht~ ("liability"), ~Haftpflichtversicherung~ ("liability insurance"), and ~Haft~ itself now primarily means "custody, imprisonment." That Latin ~captus~ and Germanic ~haft~ are cognates is beautifully confirmed by the same semantic range: both moved from "seized" to "held" to "bound."
en.halten: From Middle High German ~halten~, from Old High German ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~. Cognate with Bavarian ~hoidn~, Low German ~holden~, Dutch ~houden~, English ~hold~, Danish ~holde~, Icelandic ~halda~, Swedish ~hأ¥lla~.
en.handeln: From Middle High German ~handeln~, from Old High German ~hantalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~handulإچn~, a denominative verb derived from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~ ("hand") with the frequentative suffix *~-lإچnؤ…~. The underlying noun ~Hand~ traces from MHG ~hant~, from OHG ~hant~, from Proto-West Germanic *~handu~, from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~. The semantic development from "to handle (with the hand)" to "to act, deal, trade" reflects a concrete-to-abstract broadening typical of denominative verbs. Cognate with Dutch ~handelen~, English ~handle~, Danish ~handle~, Swedish ~handla~.
en.hassen:
From Middle High German ~hazzen~, from Old High German ~hazzon~, from Proto-Germanic *~hatؤپnؤ…~, a stative-fientive verb derived from *~hataz~ ("hate, hostility"), from Proto-Indo-European *~keh2d-~ ("grief, hatred").

Cognate with English ~hate~, Dutch ~haten~, Old Norse ~hata~, Gothic ~hatan~. The PIE root *~keh2d-~ also produced Greek ~kؤ“dos~ ("care, grief, mourning") and Welsh ~cas~ ("hatred"). The Proto-Germanic noun *~hataz~ survives in Old English ~hete~ and is the source of English ~hate~. Interestingly, the Greek descendant ~kؤ“dos~ developed in the opposite emotional direction, coming to mean "care, concern" and eventually "funeral rites" -- suggesting that the PIE root may have originally denoted intense emotional engagement rather than a specific negative emotion.
en.hauen:
From MHG ~houwen~, from OHG ~houwan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hawwanؤ…~, from PIE *~kewhâ‚‚-~ ("to beat, hew, forge"). Cognate with English ~hew~.

~Hauen~ means "to hit, to strike, to chop, to hew" and is one of German's oldest action verbs. Originally a class VII strong verb (~hauen~, ~hieb~, ~gehauen~), it has partially weakened in colloquial speech, where ~haute~ competes with ~hieb~ as the past tense. In everyday German, ~hauen~ is the child's word for hitting: ~Er hat mich gehauen!~ ("He hit me!") is the universal playground complaint. But the verb also has a vigorous literary and technical life: ~in Stein hauen~ ("to carve in stone"), ~Holz hauen~ ("to chop wood"), ~hau ab!~ ("beat it!, get lost!"). The English cognate ~hew~ has retained the more dignified "chop, carve" meaning. The PIE root *~kewhâ‚‚-~ ("to beat, forge") also connects to Latin ~cإ«dere~ ("to strike, to forge").
en.heben:
From Middle High German ~heben~, ~heven~, from Old High German ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~habjanؤ…~, from PIE *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to seize, take hold of"). A class VI strong verb; the past tense ~hob~ and participle ~gehoben~ descend from the strong ablaut paradigm. Cognate with English ~heave~, Dutch ~heffen~, Danish ~hأ¦ve~, Swedish ~hأ¤va~.

The curious shift from "to seize" (PIE) to "to lift" (Germanic) reflects the same conceptual move as in English ~heave~ â€” the act of taking hold of something is inseparable from lifting it. The same PIE root *~kehâ‚‚p-~ also produced Latin ~capiإچ~ ("I take, I seize"), giving English ~capture~, ~capable~, ~captain~, and â€” via the frequentative ~acceptؤپre~ â€” German ~akzeptieren~. So ~heben~ and ~akzeptieren~, despite looking nothing alike, are distant PIE cousins: both descend from the same ancient root meaning "to grasp." The derived noun ~Hebel~ ("lever") preserves the physical mechanics of the verb: a lever is the tool that lifts. Cognate with Dutch ~heffen~, English ~heave~.
en.heilen:
From Middle High German ~heilen~, from Old High German ~heilen~ (causative, "to make whole") and ~heilؤ“n~ (inchoative, "to become whole"), from Proto-West Germanic *~hailijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hailijanؤ…~ ("to heal, make whole, save"), derived from *~hailaz~ ("whole, healthy, uninjured"), from PIE *~kأ©hâ‚‚ilos~ ("healthy, whole"). Cognate with English ~heal~ (from OE ~hا£lan~), Dutch ~helen~, Old Norse ~heila~.

The adjective ~heil~ ("whole, sound") is the source of both ~heilen~ and English ~whole~ (from OE ~hؤپl~, with the same meaning). The ~w-~ in ~whole~ is a late English spelling addition; historically ~hale~ and ~whole~ are the same word. English ~hale~ ("robust, healthy") preserves the original form. The connection between wholeness and health runs deep: to ~heilen~ is to restore something to its ~heile~ ("whole") state. The word ~heilig~ ("holy") is also from ~heil~ -- to be holy is, etymologically, to be whole and inviolate.
en.heiraten:
From Middle High German ~hؤ«rؤپten~, derived from MHG ~hؤ«rؤپt~ ("marriage"), whence modern German ~Heirat~. The noun comes from Old High German ~hؤ«rؤپt~ ("wedding, marriage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~hؤ«warؤپd~. The compound literally meant something like "household counsel" or "household arrangement," from *~hؤ«wa-~ ("household, family") + *~rؤپd~ ("counsel, plan"). Cognate with Old English ~hؤ«red~ ("family, household"), whence the English word ~hird~ (a Scandinavian royal retinue, re-borrowed through Old Norse ~hirأ°~ from Old English).

The etymology reveals that marriage in Germanic society was fundamentally a household arrangement -- not a matter of romantic love but of establishing a domestic partnership. The ~-rؤپt~ element is the same word that appears in ~Rat~ ("counsel, advice") and in the ~-rad~ that underlies names like ~Konrad~ ("bold counsel"). English ~hird~ -- a king's household guard -- preserves the same Proto-West Germanic compound but specialized toward the military-household meaning. Modern English has no direct cognate for ~heiraten~; instead it uses ~marry~, borrowed from French ~marier~, itself from Latin ~marؤ«tؤپre~. The German word thus preserves a distinctly Germanic conception of marriage as domestic establishment, while English adopted the Roman view.
en.heiأںen: From Middle High German ~heiب¥en~, from Old High German ~heiب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haitan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haitanؤ…~, tentatively from PIE *~ل¸±eyd-~ ("to call"). The original sense was "to command, order" (still preserved in the construction ~jemanden etwas heiأںen~), with the meaning "to be called, be named" developing from the passive use: "to be called (something)" became the dominant sense. Cognate with Bavarian ~haأںn~, ~hoaأںn~, Yiddish ~heysn~, Dutch ~heten~, Low German ~heten~, ~heiten~, Old Norse ~heita~, Gothic ~haitan~. Compare also English ~hest~, ~behest~.
en.helfen: From Middle High German ~hأ«lfen~, from Old High German ~hأ«lfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~helpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~helpanؤ…~, from PIE *~ل¸±elb-~, *~ل¸±elp-~. A class III strong verb with the characteristic ~e~-~a~-~o~ ablaut pattern (~helfen~, ~half~, ~geholfen~). Cognate with Low German ~helpen~, Dutch ~helpen~, English ~help~, Danish ~hjأ¦lpe~, Norwegian ~hjelpe~.
en.heranziehen:
Compound of ~heran-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to pull, to lead"). Cognate with Old English ~teon~ ("to pull, to draw"), Latin ~ducere~ ("to lead"), and English ~tow~, ~tug~, and ~duke~ (via Latin).
- ~heran-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~an~ ("on, at"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~ ("this"), from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The combined prefix conveys movement approaching the speaker.

The compound means "to draw near, to pull closer," and by extension "to consult, to cite" (drawing a source toward oneself) or "to train, to raise" (drawing a person upward toward competence). The PIE root *~dewk-~ has an astonishingly wide range of descendants: Latin ~ducere~ yielded ~duke~, ~duchess~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, ~educate~, and ~deduce~. To ~heranziehen~ a source in an argument is, etymologically, to lead it toward you.
en.herausfinden:
~heraus-~ + ~finden~, where ~heraus-~ is itself a compound of ~her-~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~aus-~ ("out"). Compound of ~her-~ + ~aus-~ + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to tread, to go, to find the way").
- ~her-~: From PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here"). The prefix indicates direction toward the speaker.
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds a sense of outward extraction.

~Herausfinden~ means "to find out, to discover, to figure out." The double prefix creates a vivid spatial metaphor: to find something ~out~ and bring it ~here~ â€” to extract hidden knowledge and draw it toward oneself. Compare English ~find out~, which Wiktionary notes as a semantic parallel. The PIE root *~pent-~ ("to tread, to go") reveals an ancient connection between finding and walking: you ~find~ things by treading the ground, by going along a ~path~ (English ~path~ is from the same root). Latin ~pإچns~ ("bridge," originally "path over water") is a cognate, as is Greek ~pأ³ntos~ ("sea" â€” the thing you cross). The double-prefix pattern (~heraus-~, ~herein-~, ~herأ¼ber-~) is characteristically German: it specifies both the origin point and the direction of motion in a single verb, achieving a spatial precision that English typically requires a phrasal verb to match.
en.herausgeben:
Compound of ~heraus-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~geben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~ ("to give"), from Proto-Indo-European *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (via Norse), Dutch ~geven~.
- ~heraus-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~aus~ ("out"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The element ~aus~ descends from OHG ~uz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Together they mean "out toward the speaker."

The compound literally means "to give out (toward the speaker)," yielding the modern senses of "to publish" (giving a book out to the world), "to return, hand over" (giving change back), and "to edit" (in the sense of preparing for publication). A ~Herausgeber~ is an editor or publisher. Dutch ~uitgeven~ ("to publish, to spend") shows the same semantic development with its own prefix ~uit-~ ("out"), confirming that the metaphor of publication as "giving out" runs deep in the Germanic languages.
en.herausstellen:
Compound of ~heraus-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand"). Cognate with English ~stall~ and ~still~, Dutch ~stellen~.
- ~heraus-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~aus~ ("out"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The element ~aus~ descends from OHG ~uz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Together they mean "out toward the speaker."

The compound literally means "to place out (toward the speaker)," hence "to expose, to highlight, to emphasize." The reflexive ~sich herausstellen~ means "to turn out, to become apparent" -- something places itself out into the open. This metaphor of truth as spatial emergence is ancient: what is hidden is inside, what is revealed comes out. The PIE root *~stel-~ also produced Greek ~stellein~ ("to set, to send") and Latin ~locus~ (from an older *~stlocus~), making ~herausstellen~ a distant cousin of English ~locate~.
en.herrschen:
From MHG ~hؤ“rschen~, ~hؤ“rsen~, from OHG ~hؤ“rrisإچn~, ~hؤ“risإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hairisإچn~ ("to be strong, to dominate"). The surface form was remodeled after ~Herr~ already during the MHG period.

~Herr~ itself descends from MHG ~hؤ“rre~, OHG ~hؤ“riro~ / ~hؤ“rro~, originally the comparative of ~hؤ“r~ ("grey-haired, noble, venerable"), from Proto-Germanic *~hairaz~ ("grey"). Cognates: Dutch ~heer~, Old English ~hؤپr~, English ~hoar~ and ~hoary~, Old Norse ~hأ،rr~. The same root also gives modern German ~hehr~ ("sublime, noble") â€” so ~Herr~, ~herrschen~, ~herrlich~, and ~Herrschaft~ all descend from an ancestral word meaning "grey-haired," the authority of an elder embedded in each.
en.herstellen:
Compound of ~her-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb â€” the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~her-~: From MHG ~her~, from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiri~ ("here, hither"), from PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here"). This separable prefix conveys motion toward the speaker â€” "hither" â€” as opposed to ~hin-~, which conveys motion away.

~herstellen~ had a long first life as a purely spatial verb: in the 16th century it meant only "to place here, to set down toward the speaker" â€” the directional counterpart of ~hinstellen~ ("to place over there"). Its modern sense of "to manufacture, to produce" is surprisingly recent, developing only around 1900 as a shortening of ~wiederherstellen~ ("to restore, to reconstruct"). The original ~wiederherstellen~ meant to bring something back to its former state â€” to restore it to where it had been. When speakers began dropping the ~wieder-~ prefix and using ~herstellen~ alone, the sense broadened from "restore" to "make" in general. This makes ~herstellen~ one of the few common German verbs whose dominant modern meaning is the product of a late semantic compression rather than centuries of gradual development. The noun ~Hersteller~ ("manufacturer, producer") followed the verb into this new sense and is now indispensable in business and product labeling. Cognate with Dutch ~herstellen~ (in the narrower sense of "restore").
en.herunterladen:
A calque of English ~download~. Compound of ~herunter-~ ("down") + ~laden~ ("to load"):

- ~laden~: From MHG ~laden~ (strong verb), from OHG ~hladan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaأ¾anؤ…~ ("to load"), from PIE *~klehâ‚‚-~ ("to put, lay out"). Cognate with English ~laden~ and ~lade~. Related to Lithuanian ~klأ³ti~ ("to lay, spread, pave"), Russian ~klad~ ("treasure" -- something laid away).
- ~herunter-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~unter~ ("under"), ultimately from PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here") + *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under").

~Herunterladen~ is a modern German calque (loan-translation) of English ~download~, mapping ~down-~ to ~herunter-~ and ~-load~ to ~-laden~. The strong conjugation of ~laden~ (~lأ¤dt~, ~lud~, ~geladen~) carries over, producing forms like ~er lأ¤dt herunter~, ~er lud herunter~, ~er hat heruntergeladen~. The competing anglicism ~downloaden~ also exists in colloquial German but is considered less standard. The informal contraction ~runterladen~ is common in everyday speech. The antonym ~hochladen~ ("to upload") follows the same calque pattern. These digital-age compounds demonstrate German's productive capacity for translating English technical vocabulary through native morphology rather than simply borrowing -- a tradition stretching back to seventeenth-century language purists like Philipp von Zesen.
en.hervorheben:
Compound of ~hervor-~ + ~heben~:

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, ~heven~ (rarer ~heffen~), from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~habjanؤ…~ ("to lift, to heave"), from PIE *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to seize, to grasp"). Cognate with English ~heave~, Dutch ~heffen~, Danish ~haeve~.
- ~hervor-~: A compound prefix from ~her-~ (from PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~, "this (here)," conveying motion toward the speaker) + ~vor-~ (from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~, "before, in front"), together meaning "forth, out from."

~Hervorheben~ means "to emphasize, to highlight, to bring forth" â€” literally to lift something out into view. The noun ~Hervorhebung~ ("emphasis, highlighting") is used in typography, rhetoric, and everyday speech. The PIE root *~kehâ‚‚p-~ ("to seize, to grasp") connects lifting with grasping across the Indo-European branches: Latin ~capiإچ~ ("I seize") gave English ~capture~, ~capable~, ~accept~, and ~anticipate~. The related noun ~Hefe~ ("yeast") preserves the original fricative â€” yeast is literally "that which lifts" the dough.
en.hervorrufen:
Compound of ~hervor-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~, ~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hropan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hropanؤ…~ ("to call, to cry out"). Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~ ("to call"), English dialectal ~roop~.
- ~hervor-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~vor~ ("before, forward"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The element ~vor~ descends from OHG ~fora~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura~, from Proto-Indo-European *~per~ ("before, in front"). Together they convey "forward out toward the speaker."

The compound literally means "to call forth (toward the speaker)," hence "to cause, to evoke, to provoke." One ~ruft~ Gefأ¼hle ~hervor~ ("calls forth feelings") or ~ruft~ eine Reaktion ~hervor~ ("provokes a reaction"). The OHG form ~hruofan~ preserves an initial ~h-~ that was later lost, a common Germanic development (compare ~hring~ becoming ~Ring~). The metaphor of causation as summoning -- calling something out of hiding into existence -- is shared with English ~evoke~ (from Latin ~evocare~, "to call out") and ~provoke~ (from Latin ~provocare~, "to call forth").
en.hindern: From Middle High German ~hindern~, from Old High German ~hintaren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hindrإچnؤ…~ ("to hinder"), derived from the adverb *~hinder~ ("back, behind"). The adverb itself likely comes from PIE *~ل¸±em-tأ©r-~ ("down, back, through"), from *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near"), though an alternative theory derives it from *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this"). The semantic path from "behind" to "to obstruct" is intuitive: what is behind you blocks the way forward. Cognate with English ~hinder~, Dutch ~hinderen~. The related Latin ~contrؤپ~ ("against") descends from the same PIE root, making ~hindern~ a distant cousin of English ~counter~ and ~contrary~.
en.hinnehmen:
From Middle High German ~hinnemen~, from Old High German ~hinaneman~. Compound of ~hin-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
