- ~hin-~: From Old High German ~hina~, from Proto-Germanic *~hina~ ("away from here, thither"), from PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here"). This directional prefix indicates motion away from the speaker.

The literal sense is "to take away," but the modern meaning has shifted to "to accept, to put up with" -- often with a connotation of reluctant resignation. The PIE root *~nem-~ is the ancestor of Greek ~nemein~ ("to distribute"), which gave rise to ~Nemesis~, the goddess who distributed what was due. English ~nimble~ is a cognate through the same Germanic root. Cognate with Dutch ~nemen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp").
en.hinterfragen:
A modern compound of ~hinter-~ + ~fragen~:

- ~fragen~: From MHG ~vrؤپgen~, from OHG ~frؤپgؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frؤپgؤ“n~. Cognate with Dutch ~vragen~, Swedish ~frأ¥ga~, English dialectal ~frain~ ("to inquire").
- ~hinter-~: From Old High German ~hintar~ ("behind"), from Proto-Germanic *~hinder~, ultimately from PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here") via a comparative formation. As an inseparable verbal prefix, ~hinter-~ adds a sense of going behind or beneath the surface.

The verb ~hinterfragen~ means "to question critically, to scrutinize" -- literally to ask behind something, probing beyond the surface. It is a relatively recent coinage, entering standard German in the twentieth century as part of intellectual and journalistic discourse. Unlike the simple ~fragen~ ("to ask"), ~hinterfragen~ implies skepticism and analytical depth.
en.hinterlassen:
Compound of ~hinter-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, to allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, to allow"), from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, to leave behind").
- ~hinter-~: Meaning "behind," from Proto-Germanic *~hinder~ ("behind"), from PIE *~ل¸±om-tero-~ ("on this side"), a comparative formation from *~ل¸±om~ ("beside, near"). As an inseparable prefix, ~hinter-~ conveys leaving something in one's wake or behind one.

~Hinterlassen~ means "to leave behind, to bequeath" â€” ~ein Erbe hinterlassen~ ("to leave an inheritance"), ~Spuren hinterlassen~ ("to leave traces"), ~eine Nachricht hinterlassen~ ("to leave a message"). The inseparable prefix ~hinter-~ distinguishes this from the separable ~hinter etwas lassen~ (which would mean "to leave behind something" in a more literal, spatial sense). The PIE root *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to leave behind") makes ~hinterlassen~ doubly emphatic about departure: both the prefix and the root verb encode the concept of leaving. Cognate with English ~let~ (from the same Germanic root) and Dutch ~nalaten~ ("to bequeath," using a different prefix but the same root verb). The noun ~Hinterlassenschaft~ ("estate, legacy") preserves the formal, testamentary register of the verb.
en.hinweisen:
~hin-~ + ~weisen~. Compound of ~hin-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~wؤ«saz~ ("wise, knowing"), from PIE *~weyd-so-s~ ("knowledgeable"), from *~weyd-~ ("to see, know").
- ~hin-~: From PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here"), with a directional shift away from the speaker. ~Hin-~ indicates movement or pointing away from the speaker's position â€” toward some other place or thing.

~Hinweisen~ means "to point out, to indicate, to draw attention to." The prefix ~hin-~ directs the act of showing away from the speaker toward the referent â€” one ~weist hin auf~ something by directing another's knowledge or gaze toward it. The noun ~Hinweis~ ("hint, pointer, reference, note") is ubiquitous in German signage, footnotes, and official communication. The underlying PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see, know") is the same root that gives Latin ~videإچ~ ("I see"), Sanskrit ~veda~ ("I know"), and English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~wizard~. To point out is, at the deepest etymological layer, to cause someone to see â€” a remarkably literal metaphor. Cognate with Dutch ~aanwijzen~.
en.hinzufأ¼gen:
~hin-~ + ~zu-~ + ~fأ¼gen~. A double-prefix compound:

- ~fأ¼gen~: From MHG ~fأ¼egen~, ~fuogen~, from OHG ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچgijanؤ…~ ("to join, fit together"), from PIE *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to join, attach, fasten").
- ~hin-~: From PIE *~ل¸±أ­s~ ("this, here"). The prefix indicates motion away from the speaker.
- ~zu-~: From PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). The prefix adds directionality toward a goal or target.

~Hinzufأ¼gen~ means "to add" â€” literally "to join (something) toward (a destination) away from here." The double prefix ~hinzu-~ creates a vivid spatial image: something is fitted (~fأ¼gen~) in the direction of (~zu-~) a destination that is away from the speaker (~hin-~). The root verb ~fأ¼gen~ itself is related to English ~fay~ (an archaic word meaning "to fit closely") and Dutch ~voegen~ ("to join, grout"). The PIE root *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to fasten") also gave Latin ~pؤپx~ ("peace" â€” originally "a binding agreement") and ~pangere~ ("to fasten, fix"), making ~hinzufأ¼gen~ a distant relative of English ~peace~ and ~pact~. In computing, ~hinzufأ¼gen~ has become the standard German equivalent of "add" â€” the button you click to add items to a cart or entries to a list.
en.hochladen:
A modern compound of ~hoch~ + ~laden~, and a calque of English ~upload~:

- ~hoch~: From Middle High German ~hإچch~, from Old High German ~hإچh~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauh~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauhaz~, from PIE *~kewk-~ ("to bend; crooked"). The semantic shift from "bent, arched" to "high" may reflect the image of an arching peak.
- ~laden~: From MHG ~laden~ (strong verb), from OHG ~hladan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaأ¾an~. Cognate with English ~laden~ and ~load~.

This is a textbook example of a loan translation: German speakers needed a word for the digital concept of uploading and calqued it directly from English ~upload~ by substituting ~hoch~ ("up") + ~laden~ ("to load"). Its counterpart ~herunterladen~ ("to download") is calqued the same way. The computing sense dates to the 1990s, but ~laden~ itself has been loading cargo since the early medieval period.
en.hoffen:
From Middle High German ~hoffen~ (first attested 12th century), from northern Old High German *~hoffإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hopإچn~. The ultimate pre-Germanic origin is uncertain. The word's early attestation is exclusively in northern and central German dialects; it was absent from Upper German, where it only became established in later Middle High German, gradually displacing the now-obsolete ~gedingen~ ("to have hope, trust"). Some scholars have proposed that all continental Germanic forms derive from Old English ~hopian~, introduced to the continent by Anglo-Saxon missionaries â€” which would make ~hoffen~ a remarkably late addition to the German vocabulary, arriving through religious rather than inherited channels.

Whether missionary loan or parallel inheritance, the word displaced a native competitor (~gedingen~, related to ~Ding~, "thing, assembly") and established itself as the sole common word for hope in German. Its exact PIE ancestry remains debated, with no secure cognate outside Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~hopen~, English ~hope~, Danish ~hأ¥be~, Swedish ~hoppas~, Yiddish ~×”×گض¸×¤ض؟×ں~ (~hofn~).
en.holen: From Middle High German ~holen~, ~holn~, from Old High German ~holإچn~, ~holؤ“n~ ("to fetch, get, draw"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holإچn~, possibly from PIE *~kelhâ‚پ-~ ("to call, cry, summon"). The semantic development would be from "to summon, call (someone to come)" â†’ "to go and fetch, retrieve." The connection to PIE *~kelhâ‚پ-~ is uncertain but paralleled by similar "call â†’ fetch" developments in other languages. Wiktionary directs readers to English ~haul~ for related forms. The cognate English word ~haul~ (from Old French ~haler~, of Germanic origin) shares the sense of pulling something toward oneself, pointing to an underlying idea of directed movement. Cognate with Low German ~halen~, Dutch ~halen~ ("to fetch, get"), English ~haul~ (via French).
en.hأ¤ngen:
Modern German ~hأ¤ngen~ is the result of the gradual merger of three historically distinct Old High German verbs, all related in meaning and ancestry:

1. OHG ~hؤپhan~ (strong, chiefly transitive), past ~hieng~, participle ~gehangan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hؤ…ج„han~, from Proto-Germanic *~hanhanؤ…~ â€” the original strong verb meaning "to be suspended."
2. OHG ~hangؤ“n~ (weak, intransitive) â€” a related form also meaning "to hang."
3. OHG ~hengen~ (weak, transitive/causative), from Proto-West Germanic *~hangijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hangijanؤ…~ â€” a causative formation meaning "to hang something, to cause to hang."

Verbs 1 and 2 merged first, combining the present stem ~hang-~ from ~hangؤ“n~ with the strong past forms (~hing~, ~gehangen~) of ~hؤپhan~. This merger is first attested in the north and spread southward through Middle Low German and into Central German during the 14th century. The causative verb 3 â€” ancestor of modern transitive ~hأ¤ngen~ â€” was kept formally separate into the 20th century, at least by prescriptive grammarians, but colloquial usage steadily conflated all three. Today the distinction is upheld primarily in the past tense: intransitive ~hing~/~gehangen~ ("the coat was hanging") versus transitive ~hأ¤ngte~/~gehأ¤ngt~ ("she hung the coat"). This mirrors the English ~lie~/~lay~ distinction, which likewise collapses in casual speech. Cognate with Dutch ~hangen~, English ~hang~.
en.hأ¶ren: From Middle High German ~hإ“ren~, from Old High German ~hإچren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚ل¸±hâ‚‚owsyأ©ti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), from *~hâ‚‚eل¸±-~ ("sharp") + *~hâ‚‚ows-~ ("ear"). The PIE root is a compound construction meaning literally "to have sharp ears" â€” a vivid image of acute hearing built into the very fabric of the word. Cognate with Dutch ~horen~, English ~hear~, Danish ~hأ¸re~, Gothic ~hausjan~.
en.identifizieren: Borrowed from French ~identifier~, from Medieval Latin ~identificؤپre~, a compound of Latin ~identicus~ ("identical") + ~faciإچ~ ("to make, to do"). ~Identicus~ derives from Late Latin ~identitؤپs~ ("sameness"), from Latin ~idem~ ("the same"), from the demonstrative pronoun ~is~ ("he, it") + ~-dem~ (an emphatic particle), ultimately from PIE *~ey-~ ("he, that"). The ~-fizieren~ ending reflects the standard German adaptation of Latin ~-ficؤپre~ (from ~faciإچ~, from PIE *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ "to put, to place, to make"). To ~identifizieren~ is thus, etymologically, "to make the same" â€” to establish that two things are one. The verb entered German during the Enlightenment period, when scientific taxonomy demanded precise language for classification. The reflexive form ~sich identifizieren mit~ ("to identify with") extends the etymology beautifully: to declare oneself ~the same as~ another. Cognate with Dutch ~identificeren~, English ~identify~, Spanish ~identificar~.
en.ignorieren:
From Latin ~ignإچrإچ~ ("I do not know, I disregard"), with the German suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin verb ultimately derives from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know, to recognize"), with the Latin privative prefix ~in-~ (becoming ~ig-~ before ~n~) negating the root. The derivation within Latin is disputed: it may be a denominative from ~ignؤپrus~ ("ignorant"), or it may have shifted from the ~-ؤپ-~ to ~-إچ-~ stem under the influence of ~ignإچtus~ ("unknown").

The PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ is one of the most productive in all of Indo-European, giving Latin ~nإچscإچ~ and ~cognإچscإچ~ ("I learn, I know"), Greek ~gignإچskإچ~ (whence English ~diagnosis~ and ~prognosis~), and the Germanic family including English ~know~, ~can~ (originally "to know how"), ~ken~ (Scottish "to know"), ~cunning~ (originally "knowing"), and German ~kennen~ ("to know a person") and ~kأ¶nnen~ ("to be able"). The irony is that ~ignorieren~ -- a word about not knowing -- is built from one of the richest "knowing" roots in human language. German borrowed this verb from Latin in the 16th century, and it coexists with the native German synonym ~missachten~ ("to disregard"). In modern usage, ~ignorieren~ specifically implies deliberately choosing not to acknowledge something, whereas ~nicht wissen~ is simply not knowing -- a crucial distinction that the Latin original also made.
en.informieren:
Borrowed from French ~informer~, from Latin ~ؤ«nfإچrmإچ~ ("to shape, form, train, instruct"), a compound of Latin ~in-~ ("into") + ~fإچrma~ ("form, shape"). The Latin ~fإچrma~ has an uncertain etymology, possibly borrowed from Etruscan, itself perhaps from Greek ~morphؤ“~ ("shape, form"), with a dissimilation *~m~-~m~ > *~f~-~m~.

The original Latin sense of ~ؤ«nfإچrmإچ~ was literal and physical: "to shape, give form to." The semantic shift from "to give form to" to "to instruct, inform" reflects the metaphor of education as shaping the mind â€” the same metaphor underlying English ~inform~ and ~formation~. German received the verb through French during the period when -~ieren~ borrowings were flooding the language (roughly 12thâ€“17th centuries via Old French and later French contact), giving it the characteristic weak -~ieren~ conjugation that marks Latin-French loanwords in German. Like ~funktionieren~, ~prأ¤sentieren~, and ~konjugieren~, it entered the language as a fully formed loanword rather than being built from native roots. Cognate with English ~inform~, French ~informer~, Spanish ~informar~, Italian ~informare~.
en.inspirieren:
Borrowed in the sixteenth century from Latin ~ؤ«nspؤ«rؤپre~ ("to breathe into, to blow upon"), from ~in-~ ("into") + ~spؤ«rؤپre~ ("to breathe"). The Latin verb's earliest sense was literally "to breathe into," used of divine breath animating creation. Related to Latin ~spؤ«ritus~ ("breath, spirit"), whence German ~Spiritus~ and English ~spirit~. Also related to German ~konspirieren~ ("to conspire," literally "to breathe together") and ~transpirieren~ ("to transpire").

The theological sense -- God breathing life or creative power into a person -- was the primary meaning when the word entered German. The secular sense "to give someone a creative idea" developed later, preserving the metaphor of something being breathed into the mind from outside. English ~inspire~ arrived via Old French from the same Latin source.
en.installieren: ~Installieren~ is a borrowing from Medieval Latin ~installؤپre~ ("to place in a stall, to install in an office â€” especially to seat a canon in his choir stall"), composed of Latin ~in-~ ("in, into") and ~stallؤپre~, derived from ~stallus~ ("stall, seat, choir stall"). ~Stallus~ descends from Proto-Germanic *~stallaz~ ("standing place"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand still"). That PIE root is the ancestor of English ~stall~, ~stale~, and ~install~ itself (via the same Latin path), and of German ~Stall~ ("stable, livestock pen"). The original ecclesiastical meaning â€” "to seat a newly appointed canon in his stall in the choir" â€” survives in English "to install someone in office." The jump to technology ("to install software") is a 20th-century extension of the same idea: placing something in its proper location so it can function.
en.integrieren: ~Integrieren~ is borrowed from Latin ~integrؤپre~ ("to make whole, renew, restore"), from ~integer~ ("untouched, whole, sound, honest"), from Proto-Italic *~ة™ntagros~, built from *~nج¥-~ (the PIE negative prefix) + *~tehâ‚‚g-~ ("to touch"). That touching root also produced Latin ~tangere~ ("to touch"), ~tactus~ ("touch"), and English ~tangent~, ~tact~, ~contact~, and ~contagion~. So ~integer~ literally means "not-touched" â€” hence "whole, intact, uncorrupted." The noun ~Integritأ¤t~ and the mathematical term ~Integral~ share this ancestry: an integer is a number left whole, not cut into fractions. English ~entire~ comes from the same root via Old French. A cultural note: in German, ~integrieren~ carries a strong second meaning of social integration â€” incorporating people into a community â€” making its etymology ("making whole") feel especially apt.
en.interessieren: Borrowed in the 17th century from French ~intأ©resser~ ("to interest, to concern"), from Latin ~interesse~ ("to be between, to be present, to matter"), a compound of ~inter-~ ("between, among") + ~esse~ ("to be"), from PIE *~hâ‚پes-~ ("to be"). The semantic leap from "to be between" to "to matter, to concern" reflects the Roman legal and financial use of ~interest~ (third-person singular present of ~interesse~) as a formula meaning "it makes a difference" â€” and from there, the noun ~interest~ in the financial sense of a stake or share in something. The abstract ~inter-esse~, "being-between," captures the idea of having a stake in something â€” of standing between pure detachment and full possession. Cognate with English ~interest~, French ~intأ©resser~, Spanish ~interesar~, Dutch ~interesseren~.
en.interpretieren: From French ~interprأ©ter~, from Old French ~enterpreter~, from Latin ~interpretؤپrؤ«~ ("to explain, expound, interpret"), a deponent verb derived from the noun ~interpres~ ("agent, intermediary, interpreter"). Latin ~interpres~ is composed of ~inter~ ("between") and a derivation from PIE *~per-~ ("to sell, to traffic in; forward, through"), yielding an original sense of "one who goes between" -- a broker, a middleman, a go-between. The proposed reconstruction traces through *~enter-poro-~ ("going between") to *~enterpress~ via regular sound changes. German adopted the verb through French with the ~-ieren~ suffix, the standard pathway for Romance and Latin verbs into German. Cognate with English ~interpret~, French ~interprأ©ter~, Italian ~interpretare~. The word ~Dolmetscher~ ("interpreter" in the oral-translation sense) is a fascinating contrast: it entered German from Turkic ~dilmaأ§~ via Hungarian, showing that German has two words for "interpreter" from opposite ends of the ancient world. ~Interpretieren~ is used for textual, musical, and legal interpretation, while ~dolmetschen~ is reserved for oral translation between languages.
en.investieren: ~Investieren~ is borrowed from Latin ~investؤ«re~ ("to clothe, to surround, to envelop"), from ~in-~ ("in, into") + ~vestؤ«re~ ("to clothe"), from ~vestis~ ("garment, clothing"), from Proto-Italic *~westis~, from Proto-Indo-European *~wes-~ ("to be dressed, wear clothes"). That PIE root is the source of English ~wear~, Gothic ~wasti~ ("garment"), and Old Armenian ~ص¦gest~. The financial sense of ~investieren~ â€” to put capital to work â€” arose in 17th-century Italian banking: ~investire~ was used to describe "dressing" money in productive assets, cloaking capital in ventures that would return profit. The metaphor traveled from Italian into French ~investir~ and then into English ~invest~ and German ~investieren~. The original Latin ~investؤ«re~ also gave English ~investiture~ (the ceremony of cloaking someone in the robes of an office) and ~investigate~ â€” literally "to trace footprints," via a different Latin compound.
en.jagen:
From MHG ~jagen~ ("to hurry, drive, pursue, hunt"), from OHG ~jagإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~jagإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~jakkإچnؤ…~. The Proto-Germanic form derives from a ~nؤپ~-iterative formation, likely rebuilt from PIE *~hâ‚پeyاµت°-~ ("to strive, chase"). Attested since the eighth century. Further pre-Germanic etymology is obscure.

~Jagen~ means "to hunt, to chase, to drive" and is one of the oldest action verbs in the Germanic lexicon, reflecting the centrality of hunting to early Germanic society. The noun ~Jagd~ ("hunt") derives from the same root and appears in countless compounds: ~Jagdhund~ ("hunting dog"), ~Jagdhorn~ ("hunting horn"), ~Jagdrevier~ ("hunting grounds"). The verb has developed rich figurative uses: ~jemanden aus dem Amt jagen~ ("to chase someone out of office"), ~einen Schauer أ¼ber den Rأ¼cken jagen~ ("to send a shiver down someone's spine"). The compound ~Jأ¤ger~ ("hunter") is also famous as a military term -- ~Jأ¤ger~ units were light infantry in the Prussian and Austrian armies, originally recruited from professional hunters and gamekeepers.
en.kaufen: From Middle High German ~koufen~, from Old High German ~koufإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kaupإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaupإچnؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from *~kaupأ´~ ("merchant, innkeeper"). *~kaupأ´~ is a loanword from Latin ~caupإچ~ ("innkeeper, shopkeeper, petty trader"), itself likely a common Mediterranean trade word. This Latin borrowing entered Germanic during the period of Roman-Germanic commercial contact along the Rhine and Danube frontiers. The word spread widely: Old English ~cؤ“apian~ ("to trade, buy") yielded ~cheap~ â€” originally meaning "bargain, price, trade" (as in the London market district ~Cheapside~) â€” and the place-name element ~Chipping-~ appears in English market towns (Chipping Norton, Chipping Campden). The Danish capital ~Kأ¸benhavn~ ("Copenhagen") literally means "merchants' harbor": *~kaupأ´~ + *~hafnإچ~ ("harbor"). Cognate with Dutch ~kopen~, English ~cheap~ and ~cheapen~, Danish ~kأ¸be~, Swedish ~kأ¶pa~, Icelandic ~kaupa~. Related to German ~Kauf~ ("purchase") and ~verkaufen~ ("to sell").
en.kehren: A homograph concealing two distinct verbs. ~Kehren~ (1), "to turn," comes from Middle High German ~kؤ“ren~, from Old High German ~kؤ“ran~, ~chؤ“ren~, from Proto-Germanic *~kairijanؤ…~ or *~kausijanؤ…~, of uncertain PIE origin â€” possibly connected to PIE *~gers-~ ("to twist, bend, turn"), though Kluge's etymological dictionary calls this "a difficult word to explain both etymologically and phonetically." ~Kehren~ (2), "to sweep," comes from Middle High German ~kern~, ~keren~, from Old High German ~kerian~, ~cherian~, from Proto-Germanic *~karjanؤ…~ ("to scrape, scratch"), from PIE *~ger-~ ("to scrape"). Despite their identical modern forms, these are etymologically unrelated verbs that merged through phonological convergence. The "turn" sense survives in ~umkehren~ ("to turn around, to reverse"), ~Kehrtwendung~ ("about-face"), ~zurأ¼ckkehren~ ("to return"), and the idiom ~den Rأ¼cken kehren~ ("to turn one's back"). The "sweep" sense lives in ~auskehren~ ("to sweep out"), ~Kehricht~ ("sweepings, rubbish"), and the compound ~Kehrwoche~ â€” the famously Swabian tradition where apartment residents take turns sweeping shared spaces on a rotating schedule. English ~car~ (via Latin ~carrus~) is not related, despite superficial similarity.
en.kennen: From Middle High German ~kennen~, from Old High German ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijanؤ…~ ("to know"), a causative form of *~kunnanؤ…~ with the suffix *~-janؤ…~. *~kunnanؤ…~ derives from PIE *~اµnج¥nأ©hâ‚ƒti~, from *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). The causative origin means ~kennen~ originally signified "to make known, to cause to know" before narrowing to "to know, be acquainted with." Cognate with Bavarian ~kennan~, Dutch ~kennen~, Low German ~kennen~, Scots and English ~ken~ ("to know").
en.kennenlernen:
A double-verb compound of ~kennen~ + ~lernen~, meaning "to get to know, to become acquainted with":

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijanؤ…~, a causative form of *~kunnanؤ…~ ("to know how, to be able"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). Cognate with Scots and English ~ken~ ("to know"), Dutch ~kennen~.
- ~lernen~: From MHG ~lernen~, from OHG ~lernؤ“n~, ~lirnؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liznؤ“n~. Cognate with Old English ~leornian~ (Modern English ~learn~). Related to ~lehren~ ("to teach").

~Kennenlernen~ is one of German's few double-verb compounds â€” two full verbs fused into one separable unit (~Ich lerne sie kennen~, "I get to know her"). The combination is semantically precise: ~kennen~ means "to know" (a person or thing, through acquaintance), while ~lernen~ means "to learn" â€” together, "to learn to know." The distinction between ~kennen~ ("to be acquainted with") and ~wissen~ ("to know a fact") mirrors the French ~connaأ®tre~ vs. ~savoir~ and the Spanish ~conocer~ vs. ~saber~ â€” all Indo-European languages that distinguish experiential knowledge from propositional knowledge. English ~ken~ (from the same root as ~kennen~) survives mainly in Scottish English and the fixed phrase "beyond my ken." The PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ is among the most prolific in all of Indo-European: it also gives Latin ~cognإچscإچ~ ("I learn, I recognize," whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~), Greek ~خ³خ¹خ³خ½دژدƒخ؛د‰~ (~gignإچskإچ~, whence ~gnosis~, ~diagnosis~), and Sanskrit ~jؤپnؤپti~ ("he knows").
en.kennzeichnen:
Compound of ~Kennzeichen~ + ~-en~ (verb-forming suffix). The noun ~Kennzeichen~ is itself a compound of ~kennen~ + ~Zeichen~:

- ~kennen~ ("to know"): From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~kannijanؤ…~ ("to know"), a causative form of *~kunnanؤ…~ ("to be able, to know"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). Cognate with English ~ken~ (Scottish/dialectal "to know"), Dutch ~kennen~.
- ~Zeichen~ ("sign"): From MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~ ("sign, symbol"), from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taiknؤ…~ ("sign"), from PIE *~deykج‚-~ ("to show"). Cognate with English ~token~, Dutch ~teken~, Danish ~tegn~.

The verb thus means "to mark with a recognizable sign," used both concretely (to label, to flag) and abstractly (to characterize, to be indicative of). The noun ~Kennzeichen~ has a very concrete everyday use in German: it refers to a vehicle's license plate (~Kraftfahrzeugkennzeichen~), literally a "recognition sign."
en.klagen: From Middle High German ~klagen~, from Old High German ~klagإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~klagإچn~ ("to lament, complain"), a denominative verb from *~klagu~ ("lamentation, complaint"). The ultimate origin is uncertain; it is often compared to Proto-Indo-Iranian *~garjت°-~ ("to complain"), but phonological difficulties make this connection questionable, and some scholars favor an onomatopoeic origin -- the word imitating the sound of wailing. German ~klagen~ preserves both the emotional sense ("to lament, mourn") and a legal one ("to sue, file a complaint"), whence the noun ~Klage~ means both "lament" and "lawsuit." English ~claim~ is unrelated, coming instead from Latin ~clؤپmؤپre~.
en.klappen: From Middle Low German ~klappen~, from Old Saxon *~klappإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~klappإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~klappإچnؤ…~. The verb is onomatopoeic â€” it imitates the sharp percussive sound of a flat surface snapping shut. Cognate with Dutch ~klappen~ ("to clap, snap"), English ~clap~, and Old High German ~klapfإچ~ ("a clapping sound"). Also related to ~klaffen~ ("to gape, stand wide open"), which represents the same root shifted toward the open position rather than the shut one. The modern senses of ~klappen~ span the acoustic original ("to clatter, snap"), a physical result ("to fold shut"), and an abstract success meaning â€” "es klappt" ("it works out, it clicks") â€” where the satisfying snap of something fitting together became a metaphor for things going smoothly.
en.kleben:
From Middle High German ~kleben~, from Old High German ~klebؤ“n~ ("to stick"), from Proto-West Germanic *~klibؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~klibؤ“nؤ…~, from PIE *~gleyb-~ ("to stick, adhere"). This is a durative formation from the strong verb root *~kleibؤ“nؤ…~ ("to adhere, to climb"), with cognates in Old English ~cleofian~ ("to cleave to, adhere"), Old Norse ~klؤ«fa~ ("to climb"), and Old High German ~-klؤ«ban~ ("to stick").

The PIE root *~gleyb-~ reveals a surprising family reunion: German ~kleben~ ("to stick"), English ~cleave~ (in the sense "to cling to"), and ~climb~ (from OE ~climban~, originally "to cling one's way upward") are all cousins. The relationship between sticking and climbing is intuitive -- to climb a cliff face, one must cling to it. Even more remarkably, English has two verbs spelled ~cleave~ with opposite meanings: "to cling to" (from *~gleyb-~, the same root as ~kleben~) and "to split apart" (from *~glewbت°-~, a different root entirely). These are among the most famous auto-antonyms in English.
en.klettern: An iterative verb formed in Early New High German from ~kletten~, from PIE *~gleyt-~ ("to cling to, cleave, stick"), from the root *~gley-~ ("to stick, smear"). Related to ~Klette~ ("burr") -- the prickly seed casing that clings to clothing and fur -- and, surprisingly, to ~Kleid~ ("dress, garment"), which originally referred to something that clings to the body. The iterative ~-ern~ suffix emphasizes repeated action, capturing the hand-over-hand nature of climbing. The semantic connection between clinging and climbing is vivid: a climber clings to the rock face the way a burr clings to a wool coat.
en.klicken: ~Klicken~ ("to click") is attested from the 16th century, derived from Middle High German *~klicken~, as preserved in ~zerklicken~ ("to break an eggshell"). It is either cognate with or formed in parallel to Dutch ~klikken~ (15th century), English ~click~ (16th century), and French ~cliquer~ (14th century) â€” all ultimately imitative of the sharp, dry sound a latch, buckle, or button makes. The computing sense â€” pressing a mouse button â€” is a 20th-century semantic loan from English ~click~. ~Klicken~ is thus one of the few words in this app to have two distinct origin stories in the same form: an ancient onomatopoeic root and a modern borrowing wearing its face. The verb is wholly regular and weak, befitting something so thoroughly modern.
en.klingen:
From Middle High German ~klingen~, from Old High German ~klingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~klingan~, from Proto-Germanic *~klinganؤ…~. A class III strong verb. Cognate with Middle Dutch ~klinken~, English ~clink~.

The Proto-Germanic root *~klinganؤ…~ is almost certainly of onomatopoeic origin â€” a phonological icon of the ringing, resonant sound metal makes when struck. English ~clink~ is a direct descendant via Old English *~clincan~. Related by sound-symbolic extension are English ~clang~, ~cling~ (in the sense of a resonant sound), and possibly ~call~. The verb's surviving class III ablaut pattern â€” ~klingt~/~klang~/~geklungen~ â€” shows the vowel series (i, a, u) characteristic of Germanic strong verbs with nasal infixes, the same pattern as ~singen~ and ~springen~. German has generalized ~klingen~ from pure sound to metaphorical resonance: a speech can ~klingen wie Musik~, and a name can ~gut klingen~ â€” uses that extend the root's sonic origin into the domain of general quality or impression.
en.klأ¤ren: From Middle High German ~klأ¦ren~, denominal from ~klar~ ("clear, bright"). The adjective ~klar~ was borrowed into German from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch ~klؤپr~, itself from Old French ~cler~, from Latin ~clؤپrus~ ("bright, illustrious, renowned"). English ~clear~ arrived by the same Latin route, also via Old French ~cler~, making ~klأ¤ren~ and English ~to clear~ distant cousins by borrowing rather than direct inheritance. Latin ~clؤپrus~ also gives English ~clarity~, ~declare~, and ~clarion~. The verb covers both the literal sense (clarifying a liquid by letting it settle) and the abstract sense (clarifying a question or situation) â€” exactly parallel to English ~to clear up~.
en.knأ¼pfen:
From Middle High German ~knأ¼pfen~, from Old High German ~knupfen~, ~chnuphan~, related to ~Knopf~ ("button, knob"). ~Knopf~ descends from MHG ~knopf~, from OHG ~knopf~, from Proto-Germanic *~knuppaz~ ("lump, clod, knob"). The word belongs to a large Germanic cluster of ~kn-~ words expressing ideas of lumps, knots, and protuberances: ~Knoten~ ("knot"), ~Knochen~ ("bone"), ~Knollen~ ("tuber"), ~Knauf~ ("knob, pommel").

~Knأ¼pfen~ means "to tie, to knot, to forge (a connection)" and carries both literal and figurative weight. One ~knأ¼pft~ a net, a carpet (~Teppich knأ¼pfen~ = to hand-knot a carpet), or a bond (~Kontakte knأ¼pfen~ = to forge contacts, ~Bedingungen daran knأ¼pfen~ = to attach conditions). The Germanic ~kn-~ cluster is one of the most expressive sound-symbolic groupings in the language family: ~knead~, ~knot~, ~knob~, ~knuckle~, ~knee~, ~knoll~ â€” all words evoking compressed, rounded, or lumpy shapes.
en.kochen: From Middle High German ~kochen~, from Old High German ~kohhإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kokإچn~ ("to cook"), borrowed from Late Latin ~cocإچ~, from Classical Latin ~coquإچ~ ("I cook, I boil"), from PIE *~pekت·-~ ("to cook, to ripen"). This is one of the clearest Latin loanwords in the Germanic lexicon, entering the language during the Roman period when Germanic peoples along the Rhine and Danube adopted Roman culinary techniques and vocabulary. The word replaced whatever native Germanic cooking terms existed. Cognate with Dutch ~koken~, English ~cook~ (also from Latin via Old English ~cإچc~), Swedish ~koka~, and Danish ~koge~. The PIE root *~pekت·-~ also gave Sanskrit ~pacati~ ("he cooks"), Greek ~peptein~ ("to cook, to digest") â€” whence English ~peptic~ and ~dyspepsia~ â€” and Russian ~pech'~ ("to bake"). Fascinatingly, Hittite and Lydian preserve what may be the original meaning of *~pekت·-~: "to pound, crush (grain)," suggesting the root shifted from grain processing to cooking as agricultural societies developed. The Latin-derived ~Koch~ ("cook," the noun) was borrowed so early into Germanic that it underwent the High German consonant shift from ~k~ to ~ch~ in the verb form, proving the borrowing occurred before or during the shift (roughly 3rd-6th centuries CE).
en.kombinieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~combؤ«nؤپre~, a Late Latin formation from ~con-~ ("together") + ~bؤ«nؤ«~ ("two by two, in pairs"), from ~bis~ ("twice"), from PIE *~dwis~ ("twice"). The Latin verb ~combؤ«nإچ~ meant "to combine, to unite."

The word entered many European languages via French ~combiner~ or directly from Latin: English ~combine~, Dutch ~combineren~, Polish ~kombinowaؤ‡~, and German ~kombinieren~ are all Latin borrowings. The German word carries an additional colloquial meaning, "to deduce" (~kombinieren~ as in reasoning something out, the detective's art), which is idiomatic to German and not shared by the English ~combine~.
en.kommen: From Middle High German ~quemen~, ~komen~, ~kumen~, from Old High German ~queman~ (rarer ~cuman~, ~chuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت·em-~. Cognate with Low German ~kamen~, Dutch ~komen~, English ~come~, Danish ~komme~.
en.kommentieren: Derived from ~Kommentar~ with the verbal suffix ~-ieren~. In use since the 18th century. ~Kommentar~ was borrowed from Latin ~commentؤپrius~, ~commentؤپrium~ ("notebook, commentary"), itself from ~commentؤپrؤ«~ ("to ponder, discuss"), an intensive form of ~comminiscؤ«~ ("to think, devise"), from ~com-~ ("together") + ~meminisse~ ("to remember"), from PIE *~men-~ ("to think"). The PIE root *~men-~ is one of the most productive in the Indo-European family, yielding Latin ~mens~ ("mind"), Greek ~menos~ ("spirit"), Sanskrit ~manas~ ("mind"), and an enormous number of English derivatives: ~mind~, ~mental~, ~memory~, ~mention~, ~mentor~, ~mania~, ~mnemonic~, ~amnesia~, and ~monastery~ (from Greek ~monazein~, "to live alone," via the idea of contemplation). The German ~-ieren~ suffix naturalizes the Latin verb into standard German conjugation. The word ~Kommentar~ in German has the same double life as English ~commentary~: it can be a scholarly annotation or a casual remark. The phrase ~kein Kommentar~ ("no comment") is a direct calque of the English journalistic formula.
en.kommunizieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~commإ«nicؤپre~ ("to share, to make common, to impart"), derived from the adjective ~commإ«nis~ ("common, shared, public"), which itself is from ~com-~ ("with, together") + ~mإ«nus~ ("duty, gift, service"), from PIE *~mey-~ ("to change, exchange"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of the Latin/French verbal ending ~-ؤپre~/~-er~, used productively since the Middle Ages to integrate foreign verbs.

This is one of the most internationally recognizable roots in European languages: English ~communicate~, French ~communiquer~, Italian ~comunicare~, Spanish ~comunicar~ all descend from the same Latin source. The underlying PIE root *~mey-~ ("to exchange") also yielded Latin ~mإ«tؤپre~ ("to change," whence English ~mutation~, ~mutual~, ~commute~) and even ~immune~ (literally "not subject to duty"). The German word entered the language during the Early Modern period as part of the massive wave of Latin and French scholarly vocabulary. Unlike many such borrowings that remained confined to academic registers, ~kommunizieren~ has become thoroughly colloquial, though German also retains the native equivalent ~mitteilen~ ("to share with, to inform"), which offers a neat semantic parallel: ~mit-~ ("with") + ~teilen~ ("to divide, share") mirrors Latin ~com-~ + ~mإ«nicؤپre~.
en.konfrontieren:
Borrowed from Medieval Latin ~confrontؤپre~ ("to border on, to be adjacent"), composed of Latin ~con-~ ("with, together") and ~frإچns~ (genitive ~frontis~, "forehead, front"). The word entered German likely via French ~confronter~ in the 17th century.

The original Latin sense was spatial: two properties ~confronting~ each other simply shared a boundary. The metaphorical shift to "facing someone with unpleasant facts or an adversary" developed in French legal and diplomatic language and was borrowed wholesale into German. ~Konfrontieren~ means "to confront, to face with" â€” ~jemanden mit der Wahrheit konfrontieren~ ("to confront someone with the truth"). The Latin root ~frإچns~ ("forehead") reveals the underlying image: confrontation is a forehead-to-forehead encounter, a face-to-face meeting. This same root gives English ~front~, ~frontier~, ~affront~ (literally "to strike in the face"), and ~effrontery~ ("shamelessness," literally "without a forehead," i.e., without the capacity to blush). The reflexive ~sich konfrontiert sehen~ ("to find oneself confronted") is a staple of journalistic German.
en.kontaktieren:
Formed from ~Kontakt~ + ~-ieren~. The noun ~Kontakt~ was borrowed from Latin ~contؤپctus~ ("a touching, contact"), the past participle of ~contingere~ ("to touch upon"), from ~con-~ ("together") + ~tangere~ ("to touch"), from PIE *~tehâ‚‚g-~ ("to touch"). The ~-ieren~ suffix adapts the noun into a verb meaning "to establish contact with." A doublet of the less common ~kontakten~.

The word is a relatively modern formation, reflecting the influence of Latin-derived vocabulary in German bureaucratic and business language. English ~contact~ (as a verb) followed a similar path, though its use as a verb was once controversial among English prescriptivists.
en.kontrollieren:
Borrowed from French ~contrأ´ler~, from Old French ~contreroller~, ~contreroler~, from ~contrerole~, from Medieval Latin ~contrarotulum~ ("a counter-roll or register used to verify accounts"), composed of Latin ~contrؤپ~ ("against, opposite") + Medieval Latin ~rotulus~, from Latin ~rotula~ ("a little wheel"), diminutive of ~rota~ ("a wheel"), from PIE *~Hret-~ ("to run, roll"). The circumflex in French ~contrأ´ler~ marks the loss of an older ~s~ (from ~contresroller~), a pattern visible across French (compare ~hأ´pital~ from ~hospital~).

The semantic journey is remarkable: from a physical wheel (~rota~) to a small scroll (~rotula~, because scrolls are rolled up) to a counter-register used to double-check accounts (~contrarotulum~) to the act of checking itself (~contrأ´ler~) to the broader sense of mastery and dominance (~kontrollieren~, "to control"). German preserves both major senses: "to check, inspect" (the older meaning, closer to the Latin) and "to control, direct" (the newer meaning, influenced by English). The German suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard adaptation pattern for Romance verbs entering German. Related: ~Kontrolle~ ("inspection, control"), ~Kontrolleur~ ("inspector").
en.konzentrieren: ~Konzentrieren~ ("to concentrate") is borrowed from French ~concentrer~, formed from Latin ~con-~ ("together, with") + the root of ~centrum~ ("center, midpoint"), from Ancient Greek ~خ؛خ­خ½د„دپخ؟خ½~ (~kأ©ntron~, "sharp point, goad, center of a circle"), from Proto-Hellenic *~kأ©ntron~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ل¸±ent-~ ("to prick, sting"). The Greek root is also the source of English ~center~ and ~centrifuge~. The underlying image of ~konzentrieren~ is therefore "to bring to a point" â€” to gather dispersed things to a single sharp focus. In chemistry, a ~konzentrierte Lأ¶sung~ (a concentrated solution) is one from which the solvent has been reduced so the dissolved substance is dense and undiluted. In everyday use, ~sich konzentrieren~ means "to focus one's attention," preserving the original geometrical metaphor of converging toward a center point.
en.konzipieren:
From Late Middle High German ~concipieren~, borrowed from Latin ~concipere~ ("to take in, to conceive, to grasp"), from ~con-~ ("together") + ~capere~ ("to take, to seize"). Latin ~capere~ descends from Italic ~kapiإچ~, from Proto-Italic *~kapjإچ~, from PIE *~kap-~ ("to seize, grab"). Cognate with Albanian ~kap~ ("to grab"), Welsh ~cael~ ("to get"), Breton ~kavout~ ("to find"), Lithuanian ~kampt~ ("to take hold"), Greek ~kaptإچ~ ("to gulp down"), and â€” remarkably â€” English ~have~ and ~heave~, which descend from the same PIE root through the Germanic branch.

German ~konzipieren~ means "to conceive, to draft, to design" â€” to seize upon an idea and give it initial shape. The noun ~Konzept~ ("concept, draft, rough plan") is the more everyday derivative. Latin ~concipere~ carried a vivid physical sense: to take things together, to gather in â€” and from this, to conceive (a child, an idea, a plan). The same Latin root, through the alternate prefix form ~-cipere~, produced an enormous family of English words: ~accept~ (~ad-~ + ~capere~), ~except~, ~intercept~, ~receive~ (via French, from ~re-~ + ~capere~), ~perceive~, ~deceive~, and ~capable~. The ~-ieren~ suffix is the hallmark of Latin and French loans in German, corresponding to French ~-er~ and English ~-ate~.
en.kopieren: From Medieval Latin ~copiؤپre~ ("to transcribe, to reproduce"), derived from the noun ~Kopie~, which was borrowed from Latin ~copia~ ("abundance, plenty"). Latin ~copia~ is itself composed of ~co-~ (from ~cum~, "with, together") + ~ops~ ("wealth, resources, power"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒep-~ ("to work, produce"). The semantic chain from "abundance" to "copy" runs through the medieval scribal sense: a ~copia~ was originally a transcript made from an abundant supply of text, then any reproduction. The German verb entered the language via the Latinate ~-ieren~ suffix, the standard mechanism for adapting foreign verbs into German conjugation. Cognate with English ~copy~ and French ~copier~, all from the same Latin source. The modern sense has expanded well beyond manuscripts to encompass photocopying, digital duplication, and even imitating behavior. The related noun ~Kopie~ is used in everyday German exactly like English "copy."
en.korrigieren: From Middle High German ~corrigieren~, borrowed from Latin ~corrigere~ ("to set right, to correct"), a compound of ~con-~ ("together, with") + ~regere~ ("to direct, to rule"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreg-~ ("to straighten, to direct"). The Latin prefix ~con-~ intensifies the meaning, giving the sense of "thoroughly setting straight." The verb entered German during the medieval period of heavy Latin influence on scholarly and administrative language. Cognate with English ~correct~ (via Old French from the same Latin source), as well as with ~regime~, ~regal~, ~regulate~, and ~rex~, all from the same PIE root. The German ~-ieren~ suffix marks this as a loanword verb, following the standard pattern for adapting foreign verbs into the German conjugation system. Interestingly, the native German equivalent would be something like ~berichtigen~ (from ~richtig~, "right, correct"), which itself descends from the same PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreg-~ via the Germanic branch, making ~korrigieren~ and ~berichtigen~ distant etymological cousins that converged on the same meaning from Latin and Germanic paths respectively.
en.kosten:
~kosten~ carries two entirely unrelated Latin etymologies that have converged on a single German spelling.

~kosten~ "to cost": From Middle High German ~kosten~, borrowed from Old French ~coster~ (modern French ~coأ»ter~), from Latin ~cإچnstإچ~ ("to stand firm, be fixed, cost"), equivalent to ~con-~ + ~stإچ~ ("to stand"), from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand"). The metaphor is of a price "standing firm" â€” a cost being fixed and settled. English ~cost~ traveled the same Old French route. Latin ~cإچnstإچ~ is the same verb that underlies English ~constant~.

~kosten~ "to taste, to try": From Old High German ~kostإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kustإچnؤ…~ ("to try, taste"), from Proto-Indo-European *~اµews-~ ("to taste, try"). This is an entirely unrelated word that happens to be spelled identically in modern German. The PIE root *~اµews-~ also underlies Latin ~gustus~ ("taste"), which gave English ~gusto~, ~disgust~ (via Italian ~disgusto~, originally "a distaste for"), and ~gust~. The Proto-Germanic root *~kustإچnؤ…~ also underlies Old English ~costnian~ ("to tempt, try, prove"), and English ~choose~ shares the same PIE source via a different derivational path through Proto-Germanic *~keusanؤ…~. Two entirely different Latin and PIE roots thus converged on the same German spelling â€” one concerned with standing firm (prices), the other with tasting and testing.
en.kreieren: Borrowed from French ~crأ©er~, itself from Latin ~creإچ~ ("I create, bring forth"), with the German verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. Latin ~creإچ~ derives from PIE *~ل¸±rehâ‚پ-~ ("to grow, bring into being"), from the root *~ل¸±er-~ ("to grow, become bigger"), which also yielded Latin ~crؤ“scإچ~ ("I grow") -- the source of English ~crescent~, ~increase~, and ~crescendo~. German ~kreieren~ coexists with the native ~schaffen~ and ~erschaffen~, but carries a more refined or artistic connotation, often used in fashion, cuisine, and the arts. The word entered German during the period of strong French cultural influence in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
en.kriegen: From Middle High German ~kriegen~ ("to war, to strive for, to conquer property or land"), a verbalization of ~Krieg~ ("war"), from OHG ~krig~, from Proto-West Germanic *~krؤ«g~. The origin of the noun *~krؤ«g~ is debated: it may be related to OHG ~krؤ“g~ ("stubbornness, defiance") or to a pre-Germanic substrate word. The semantic evolution is striking: the original meaning was "to wage war, to strive for" â€” one ~kriegte~ land or possessions through military effort. Over time, the "striving to obtain" sense overshadowed the military one, and in colloquial modern German ~kriegen~ simply means "to get, to receive," making it the everyday synonym for ~bekommen~. The older martial sense survives fossilized in the noun ~Krieg~ and in formal compound verbs like ~bekriegen~ ("to wage war on"). Cognate with Bavarian ~kriagn~, Dutch ~krijgen~ ("to get, to receive" â€” the same semantic shift occurred independently), and Hunsrik ~krien~. The Dutch parallel is particularly illuminating: both German and Dutch independently drifted from "to fight for" to "to obtain," suggesting that the shift was natural and perhaps inevitable.
en.kritisieren: From French ~critiquer~ with the verb ending replaced by the standard German ~-ieren~ suffix for naturalized foreign verbs. French ~critiquer~ derives from ~critique~ ("critical analysis"), from Latin ~criticus~, from Ancient Greek ~خ؛دپخ¹د„خ¹خ؛دŒد‚~ (~kritikأ³s~, "able to judge, discerning"), from ~خ؛دپخ¯خ½د‰~ (~krأ­nإچ~, "to separate, to decide, to judge"), from PIE *~krey-~ ("to sift, to separate, to distinguish"). The PIE root reveals that criticism was originally an act of sorting â€” separating the wheat from the chaff, the worthy from the unworthy. The same root gives English ~crisis~ (a decisive moment of separation), ~criterion~ (a standard for judging), ~crime~ (an act judged as wrongful â€” via Latin ~crؤ«men~), ~discern~ (to separate with the eyes), and ~secret~ (something separated away â€” via Latin ~sؤ“cernere~). German borrowed the word during the 17th-century vogue for French intellectual vocabulary, and the ~-ieren~ suffix slots it neatly into German's productive system for assimilating Romance verbs. Cognate with English ~criticize~, Italian ~criticare~, Spanish ~criticar~. The journey from PIE "to sift grain" to modern "to write a book review" spans about six thousand years.
en.kأ¤mpfen: From Middle High German ~kempfen~, from Old High German *~kempfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kampijan~, a denominal verb from *~kamp~ ("field, battlefield"), itself borrowed from Latin ~campus~ ("field, open plain"). The word originally denoted the battlefield itself; the sense shifted early in High German from "the place of battle" to "the act of fighting." Latin ~campus~ is the common ancestor of English ~camp~, ~champion~ (via Old French ~champion~, "fighter in the field"), and the modern word ~campus~ ("university grounds," originally "the field" at Princeton). The Germanic borrowing was purely military â€” the word entered the Germanic vocabulary through Roman contact and immediately took on the specialized sense of armed contest rather than pastoral plain.
en.kأ¶nnen: From Middle High German ~kأ¼nnen~, ~kunnen~, from Old High German ~kunnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~kunnanؤ…~, from PIE *~اµnج¥nأ©hâ‚ƒti~, from *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). A preterite-present verb whose original meaning was "to know (how)" and shifted to "to be able to." Cognate with Dutch ~kunnen~, English ~can~, Old Norse ~kunna~, Gothic ~kunnan~.
en.kأ¼mmern: ~Kأ¼mmern~ ("to concern, bother; to care for") descends from Middle High German ~kumbern~, derived from the noun ~kumber~ ("debris, rubble, obstruction, distress"). That noun was probably borrowed from Old French *~combre~ ("obstruction, barrier"), from Medieval Latin ~combrus~ ("barricade"), traced either to Latin ~cumulus~ ("heap") or to Gaulish *~komberإ«~ â€” from Proto-Celtic *~kombereti~ ("to bring together"), itself from *~kom-~ and *~bereti~ ("to bear"). An alternative derivation traces it to Proto-West Germanic *~kumbr~, Proto-Germanic *~kumbraz~ ("burden, trouble, sorrow"). Cognates include Luxembourgish ~kأ«mmeren~ and Dutch ~kommer~ ("distress"). The related noun ~Kummer~ ("grief, sorrow") preserves the older sense of obstruction that has hardened into emotional weight. The reflexive construction ~sich um jemanden kأ¼mmern~ ("to take care of someone") shows the verb's shift from passive obstruction to active concern â€” etymologically, to be ~encumbered~ by something came to mean attending to it.
en.kأ¼ndigen: From Middle High German ~kأ¼ndigen~ ("to make known, to announce"), derived from the adjective ~kأ¼ndic~ (modern ~kundig~, "knowledgeable, skilled"), from OHG ~kundig~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunأ¾ؤ«g~, itself from Proto-Germanic *~kunأ¾az~ ("known"), the past participle of *~kunnanؤ…~ ("to know, to be able"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). The modern meaning of "to terminate (a contract), to give notice, to quit" only became standard in the 19th century, clipped from longer compounds like ~aufkأ¼ndigen~ ("to renounce"), ~abkأ¼ndigen~ ("to announce the end of"), and ~verkأ¼ndigen~ ("to proclaim"). The semantic journey from "to make known" to "to give notice of termination" is logical: one ~kأ¼ndigt~ by making one's departure known. The PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ is one of the most prolific in Indo-European: it produced Latin ~gnإچscere~, ~cognإچscere~ (whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~), Greek ~gnإچsis~ ("knowledge"), Sanskrit ~jnؤپ~ ("to know"), English ~know~ and ~can~ (from the same Germanic *~kunnanؤ…~), and German ~kennen~, ~kأ¶nnen~, and ~Kunst~ ("art," literally "that which one knows how to do"). The related noun ~Kunde~ has split into two meanings: ~der Kunde~ ("customer" â€” originally "one who is known, a familiar") and ~die Kunde~ ("news, tidings" â€” information that is made known).
en.kأ¼rzen: A deadjectival verb formed from ~kurz~ ("short"), which descends from Middle High German ~kurz~, from Old High German ~kurz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kurt~, borrowed from Latin ~curtus~ ("shortened, cut short"). Latin ~curtus~ derives from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to cut"), the same root behind English ~shear~, ~short~, ~shirt~, and ~skirt~. The native Germanic cognate of ~curtus~ was OHG *~skurz~, which survives in ~Schurz~ ("apron") -- originally something cut short. The verb ~kأ¼rzen~ thus means "to shorten," forming a neat pair with ~verlأ¤ngern~ ("to lengthen"). English ~curt~ (meaning brusquely short in manner) is a doublet borrowed directly from the same Latin source.
en.lachen:
From Middle High German ~lachen~, from Old High German ~lahhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlahhjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlahjanؤ…~, from PIE *~kleg-~ ("to cry out, sound"). Cognate with Dutch ~lachen~, English ~laugh~, Danish ~le~, Swedish ~le~, Gothic ~hlahjan~.

The initial ~hl-~ cluster in Proto-Germanic (preserved in Gothic ~hlahjan~) was lost in all the modern West Germanic languages â€” Old High German simplified it to ~l-~, and English did the same (via Old English ~hlأ¦hhan~ â†’ ~laugh~). The PIE root *~kleg-~ originally meant simply "to make a sound, to cry out," with no specification of whether the sound was joyful or mournful â€” laughter and lamentation share the same ancestral noise. The spelling of English ~laugh~ (with ~gh~) fossilizes the Old English velar fricative that German still pronounces as ~ch~ in ~lachen~ â€” a rare case where German pronunciation better preserves the Proto-Germanic sound than English spelling does.
en.laden:
Two historically distinct verbs have merged under a single infinitive in Modern German, distinguished by context and sometimes by register.

~laden~ (strong, "to load") derives from Middle High German ~laden~ (strong verb), from Old High German ~hladan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaأ¾anؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~klehâ‚‚-~ ("to put, lay out"). Cognate with Lithuanian ~klل¹“ti~ ("to lay, spread"), Russian ~ذ؛ذ»ذ°ذ´~ ("treasure, hoard"), and English ~lade~ (as in ~bill of lading~). The English adjective ~laden~ â€” "loaded, burdened" â€” is a direct borrowing of the German past participle.

~laden~ (weak, "to invite, summon") derives from Middle High German ~laden~ (weak verb), from Old High German ~ladon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laأ¾إچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~laأ¾إچnؤ…~, from *~laأ¾إچ~ ("invitation"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚lot-ehâ‚‚~, a root shared with Hittite ~halzai~ ("to invoke, call out") and Luwian ~ل¸«alta~ ("to call"). This root is an isogloss found only in Germanic and Anatolian, making it one of the rarer traces of the Indo-Hittite connection. ~Einladen~ ("to invite") keeps this second meaning alive as the commonest modern form.

The two verbs converged in form in Middle High German, creating a rich ambiguity: in certain contexts ~Laden~ can mean both a physical shop (something "loaded" with goods) and an invitation to enter.
en.lagern:
Derived from the noun ~Lager~ ("camp, store, warehouse"), from MHG ~lأ«ger~, from OHG ~lأ«gar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~legr~, from Proto-Germanic *~legrؤ…~. Cognate with English ~lair~ (a sleeping place for an animal â€” originally any place of lying down), Dutch ~leger~, Danish ~lejr~, Swedish ~lأ¤ger~. The modern German form with ~-a-~ is of East Central German origin, influenced by the related word ~Lage~ ("position, situation"). The original front-vowel form survives in some rare alternate plurals.

The verb ~lagern~ means "to store, to warehouse" (transitive) and "to be stored, to camp, to lie" (intransitive/reflexive). The semantic path from "lying place" to "storage" is natural: goods are laid down for safekeeping. English borrowed ~lager~ directly from German in the 19th century â€” ~Lagerbier~ is beer that has been ~gelagert~ (stored, aged) in cool cellars for weeks or months, as opposed to top-fermented ales. The darker compound ~Konzentrationslager~ ("concentration camp") tragically demonstrates the word's range, from the innocuous sense of "encampment" to one of history's most terrible coinages.
en.landen: Denominal from ~Land~ ("land, country"), via Middle Low German ~landen~. The noun ~Land~ descends from Proto-West Germanic *~land~, from Proto-Germanic *~landؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~lendت°-~ ("land, heath"). An earlier native German verb ~lأ¤nden~ was displaced by the borrowed Low German form. Both the German noun ~Land~ and the English noun and verb ~land~ share the same Proto-Germanic ancestor *~landؤ…~, making ~landen~ and English ~to land~ direct cognates â€” two sister-languages coining the same verb from the same noun independently.
en.lassen: From Middle High German ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from Old High German ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with Bavarian ~lassn~, Dutch ~laten~, English ~let~.
en.laufen: From Middle High German ~loufen~, from Old High German ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupanؤ…~. A class VII strong verb. The initial ~hl-~ cluster was lost during the Old High German period. Cognate with Dutch ~lopen~, English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Danish ~lأ¸be~.
en.lauten: ~Lauten~ ("to read, run, be worded; to sound") descends from Middle High German ~lإ«ten~ ("to sound, ring"), Old High German ~hlإ«tؤ“n~, and Proto-West Germanic *~hlإ«dؤ“n~. It is equivalent to ~Laut~ ("sound") plus the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Laut~ itself comes from the Proto-Germanic adjective *~hlإ«daz~ ("loud"), the same root that gives English ~loud~, Dutch ~luid~, and Old Norse ~hljأ³أ°~. A subtle distinction in modern German: ~lauten~ means "to read as, to run" (as in ~das Urteil lautet~ â€” "the verdict reads"), while ~lأ¤uten~ ("to ring bells") is historically the factitive (causative) form of the same root â€” "to make sound." Over time, ~lأ¤uten~ absorbed the general sense of sounding, while ~lauten~ narrowed to the meaning of textual or verbal content. They share an ancestor but now describe entirely different acts.
en.leben: From Middle High German ~lأ«ben~, from Old High German ~lebؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~libbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~libjanؤ…~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to leave, cling, linger"). The semantic development from "to cling, stick" to "to remain alive" parallels the related ~bleiben~ ("to remain"), which shares the same PIE root. Cognate with Old Saxon ~libbian~ (Middle Low German ~leven~), Dutch ~leven~, English ~live~, West Frisian ~libje~, Old Norse ~lifa~ (Swedish ~leva~), Gothic ~liban~.
en.legen: From Middle High German ~legen~, from Old High German ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, a causative derivation of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie") with the suffix *~-janؤ…~, from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~. The causative formation â€” "to cause to lie," hence "to lay" â€” mirrors exactly the relationship between ~liegen~ ("to lie") and ~legen~ ("to lay"), and between English ~lie~ and ~lay~. The same causative pattern appears in German ~setzen~ (from ~sitzen~, "to sit") and ~stellen~ (from ~stehen~, "to stand"): in each pair, a strong intransitive verb of posture spawns a weak transitive causative. This systematic grammatical pairing descends from Proto-Indo-European. Cognate with Dutch ~leggen~, English ~lay~, Danish ~lأ¦gge~, Swedish ~lأ¤gga~.
en.lehnen:
From a merger of two interrelated Old High German verbs:

1. MHG ~linen~/~lenen~ (intransitive "to lean"), from OHG ~hlinؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlinؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlinؤپnؤ…~ ("to lean"), from PIE *~kج‚ley-~ ("to lean, incline"). Cognate with Latin ~clؤ«nإچ~ ("to slope, incline") and Greek ~klinإچ~ ("to bend, lean").
2. MHG ~leinen~ (transitive/causative "to lean something"), from OHG ~hleinen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlainijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlainijanؤ…~, the causative form of the above.

The merger occurred in Central and Low German dialects, where the vowels converged to ~ؤ“~. Some Upper German dialects still distinguish the transitive and intransitive forms. Cognate with Dutch ~leunen~ and English ~lean~.

A second, archaic ~lehnen~ ("to lend, borrow"), from OHG ~lؤ“hanإچn~, survives only in the derivative ~entlehnen~ ("to borrow a word or concept") and in compounds like ~Lehnwort~ ("loanword") and ~Lehnأ¼bersetzung~ ("calque, loan translation").
en.lehren:
From Middle High German ~lؤ“ren~, from Old High German ~lؤ“ren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laiت€ijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laizijanؤ…~ ("to teach"), a causative derived from *~lizanؤ…~ ("to learn"), from PIE *~leys-~ ("track, furrow, trace, trail"). The underlying metaphor is that teaching means "to cause someone to follow a track." Cognate with Low German ~lehren~, Dutch ~leren~, Afrikaans ~leer~, Danish ~lأ¦re~, Norwegian ~lأ¦re~, Swedish ~lأ¤ra~, and archaic English ~lear~ ("to learn, to teach").

Closely related to German ~lernen~ ("to learn"), which descends from the same Proto-Germanic root *~lizanؤ…~ through a different derivational path. English ~lore~ ("accumulated knowledge") is another sibling, from Proto-Germanic *~laizإچ~ ("teaching, lore"). The PIE root *~leys-~ ("track, furrow") reveals an agricultural metaphor at the heart of education: to learn was to follow a furrow, and to teach was to cause someone to follow one. In some Middle English dialects, ~learn~ could itself mean "to teach" (a usage that survives dialectally in "I'll learn you!"), preserving the original ambiguity between the causative and stative senses that German resolved by splitting into two separate verbs.
en.leiden: ~leiden~ comes from Middle High German ~lؤ«den~, from Old High German ~lؤ«dan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤ«أ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to go, travel"). The original sense was purely spatial: to go, to travel, to pass through. Over time the meaning narrowed to "to endure" and then "to suffer" â€” a trajectory from journeying to hardship that mirrors the lived experience of passage. The semantic shift was reinforced by convergence with Middle High German ~leiden~ ("to be sorry, suffer, be hated"), itself from Old High German ~leidؤ“n~, and by the noun ~Leid~ ("sorrow"), whose surface similarity made the merger feel natural. A classic false friend: ~leiden~ is NOT cognate with English ~lead~ â€” it is instead cognate with English ~lithe~ ("flexible, supple"), Dutch ~lijden~, and Low German ~lieden~. The spatial ancestor survives in the compound prefix ~-leiten~ ("to guide, conduct") and in place names such as ~Leiden~ (the Dutch city: "the watery passage").
en.leisten: From Middle High German ~leisten~, from Old High German ~leisten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laistijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laistijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~laistaz~ ("track, trace, footprint"), from Proto-Indo-European *~leys-~ ("to track, pursue; track, furrow, trace, trail"). The verb originally meant "to track, follow" â€” to follow in someone's footsteps â€” and evolved into "to perform, carry out, accomplish." This semantic journey from literal path-following to figurative achievement is a remarkably concrete-to-abstract shift: to fulfill an obligation is to follow the track it has laid out. English ~last~ (in "lasting shoe") is a direct cognate (OE ~lا£stan~, "to follow, perform"), and the shoemaker's ~last~ â€” the foot-shaped form used to make shoes â€” is the noun *~laistaz~ itself, a "footprint" in wood. Cognate with English ~last~ (OE ~lا£stan~), Old Frisian ~lؤ“sta~, Old Saxon ~lؤ“stian~, Dutch ~leisten~ (archaic).
en.leiten: ~Leiten~ ("to lead, guide, conduct") descends from Middle High German ~leiten~, Old High German ~leiten~, and Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~. It is the causative of the older strong verb *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to pass, go, travel"), built with the Proto-Germanic causative suffix *~-janؤ…~: "to cause to go, to make travel." The PIE root is *~leyt-~ ("to go, depart, die"), which also produced Latin ~lؤ«tus~ ("shore" â€” the place where the sea "goes"), Avestan ~iriخ¸iieiti~ ("to die, pass away"), and Tocharian A ~litؤپ-~ ("to pass on"). The English cognate ~lead~ (to guide) is its exact parallel â€” Old English ~lا£dan~ from the same Proto-Germanic causative. A false-friend trap: ~leiten~ ("to guide") is unrelated to ~leiden~ ("to suffer"), which comes from a completely different root meaning "to permit, allow."
en.lenken: From Middle High German ~lenken~, from Old High German ~lanca~ ("hip, flank"), related to ~Gelenk~ ("joint"). The noun ~Gelenk~ descends from MHG ~gelenke~ ("joint, bend, waist"), from OHG ~gilenki~, from Proto-Germanic *~gahlankijؤ…~, from *~hlankaz~ ("flexible, lank, slender"). Cognate with English ~lank~ (related to ~link~) and Hunsrik ~lenke~. The semantic journey from "hip/joint" to "to steer, guide, direct" traveled through the idea of bending and turning at a joint. A joint is a point of articulation that allows redirection of movement, so ~lenken~ came to mean directing or steering something along a new path. The compound ~Lenkrad~ ("steering wheel," literally "steering-wheel") is the everyday reminder of this etymology, as is ~Ablenkung~ ("distraction, deflection"). The relationship between ~lenken~ and ~Gelenk~ mirrors the English connection between ~joint~ and ~articulate~ (from Latin ~articulus~, "small joint"). English ~lank~ preserves the physical sense of the root: something long, thin, and flexible. The related OHG ~hlanka~ ("side, flank, hip") also produced the French loanword ~flanc~ (whence English ~flank~), showing a Germanic word that traveled into Romance.
en.lernen: From Middle High German ~lernen~, from Old High German ~lernؤ“n~, ~lirnؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liت€nؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~lizanؤ…~, from PIE *~leys-~ ("track, furrow, trace, trail"). The PIE root conveys the image of following a track or tracing a path â€” and from this physical metaphor, Germanic developed the meaning of acquiring knowledge: to learn is to follow the trail laid down by those who came before. This same root underlies ~lore~ and ~lear~ (archaic English for "learning, knowledge"). The historically close verb ~lehren~ ("to teach") shares the same Germanic stem in its causative function â€” "to cause to learn." ~Lernen~ is attested in German since the 8th century. Cognate with Old English ~leornian~ (Modern English ~learn~), Old Frisian ~lernia~, Middle Low German ~lernen~, and Dutch ~lernen~.
en.lesen: From Middle High German ~lأ«sen~, from Old High German ~lأ«san~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lesan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lesanؤ…~ ("to gather"), from PIE *~les-~ ("to gather"). The semantic shift from "to gather" to "to read" â€” paralleled in Latin ~legإچ~ ("I gather; I read") â€” reflects how reading was originally conceived as gathering letters or words. Cognate with Low German ~lesen~, Dutch ~lezen~, Afrikaans ~lees~, English ~lease~ (archaic, "to gather").
en.leuchten:
From OHG ~liuhten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liuhtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~liuhtijanؤ…~, a factitive/denominative verb derived from *~leuhtؤ…~ ("light") or *~leuhtaz~ ("light, bright"), both from PIE *~lewk-~ ("bright, to shine, to see"). Cognate with MHG ~liuhten~. The verb is attested since the 8th century.

The PIE root *~lewk-~ is one of the great etymological treasures of the Indo-European family. It produced Latin ~lإ«x~ ("light"), whence English ~lucid~, ~translucent~, ~elucidate~, and the name ~Lucifer~ ("light-bearer"); Greek ~leukos~ ("white"), whence ~leukemia~ (a disease of "white" blood cells); Sanskrit ~rokأ،-~ ("light"); and Lithuanian ~laإ©kas~ ("pale"). The Germanic branch developed *~lewk-~ into the noun *~leuhtؤ…~ ("light") and the adjective *~leuhtaz~ ("bright"), whence both the German verb ~leuchten~ ("to shine, to glow") and the noun ~Licht~ ("light"). English ~light~ is a cognate. The modern German word ~Leuchtturm~ ("lighthouse," literally "shining tower") and ~Leuchtstoff~ ("fluorescent material") build directly on this ancient root.
en.lieben:
Formally from Middle High German ~lieben~, an alteration (modeled on the adjective ~liep~) of the rarer MHG ~liuben~ ("to make dear, treat in a friendly way"), from Old High German ~liuben~. Denominative of the adjective ~lieb~ ("dear, beloved"), from MHG ~liep~, from OHG ~liob~, from Proto-West Germanic *~leub~, from Proto-Germanic *~leubaz~ ("dear, beloved"), from PIE *~lewbت°-~ ("to desire, love").

The Modern German verb ~lieben~ has a complicated history: the Old High German simplex ~liobإچn~ ("to love") apparently left no direct continuation, and the modern sense was derived in late Middle High German from the noun ~Liebe~ ("love") on the model of ~minnen~ ("to love"), from ~Minne~ (the courtly word for love), which had acquired a sexual overtone by that period. As a result, ~lieben~ is absent from most traditional German dialects, which prefer constructions like ~lieb haben~ or ~gern haben~ instead. English ~love~ derives from a related but distinct Proto-Germanic root *~lubإچ~ (noun), while the adjective ~lief~ (archaic English, "beloved, dear") is a direct cognate of German ~lieb~. Cognate with Dutch ~lieven~, English ~lief~, archaic English ~love~ (the verb, via *~lubإچnؤ…~).
en.liefern:
From Middle High German ~lieveren~ (attested before 1350), probably borrowed via Middle Dutch and Middle Low German ~lؤ“veren~ (the ~ie~ vocalism in German being due to hypercorrection), ultimately from Old French ~livrer~, from Latin ~lؤ«berإچ~ ("I free, release, deliver"), from ~lؤ«ber~ ("free"). The word was part of the technical vocabulary of the Hanseatic League, the powerful trading network of northern European merchants, which spread it from Low German into standard High German â€” a linguistic footprint of medieval commercial power.

The route from Latin "to free" to German "to deliver" is semantically transparent: to hand over goods is to release them to another party. The same Latin root ~lؤ«berإچ~ gives English ~liberate~, ~deliver~ (via Old French ~delivrer~), and the name ~Liberia~. Note that German ~liefern~ ("to deliver") is therefore a distant cousin of ~liberally~ and ~liberty~ â€” trading and freedom share a root. Cognate with Dutch ~leveren~, Swedish ~leverera~, French ~livrer~.
en.liegen: From Middle High German ~ligen~, from Old High German ~ligen~ (from earlier ~liggen~, with the stem generalized from the second- and third-person singular), from Proto-West Germanic *~liggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ligjanؤ…~, from PIE *~legت°-~ ("to lie down"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~liggen~, Dutch ~liggen~, English ~lie~, Danish ~ligge~, Gothic ~ligan~.
en.loben:
From Middle High German ~loben~, from Old High German ~lobؤ“n~/~lobإچn~ ("to praise, approve, hold dear"), from Proto-West Germanic *~lubإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~lubإچ~, from PIE *~lewbت°-~ ("to love, desire, praise"). Cognate with Old English ~lofian~ ("to praise"), Old Norse ~lofa~ ("to praise, promise"), Dutch ~loven~ ("to praise"), Swedish ~lova~ ("to promise, praise").

~Loben~ is related to the noun ~Lob~ ("praise") and to ~lieb~ ("dear, beloved") and ~Liebe~ ("love") -- all from PIE *~lewbت°-~. The root reveals an ancient equation between praising and loving: to praise someone was to declare one's affection. English ~love~ comes from the same PIE root via a different branch (OE ~lufu~). Old English ~lofian~ ("to praise") survives fossilized in the word ~loft~ (originally a raised gallery in a church from which hymns of praise were sung) and in ~aloft~. The compound ~Lobgesang~ ("hymn, song of praise") echoes the medieval world where ~loben~ was the language of devotion.
en.locken:
From Middle High German ~locken~, from Old High German ~lockإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lokkإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~lukkإچnؤ…~ ("to entice, to lure"). Related to ~lأ¼gen~ ("to lie"), Latin ~laciإچ~ ("I entice, I lure"), and Slovak ~lأ،kaإ¥~ ("to allure"). There is a second, homonymous verb ~locken~ ("to curl") from OHG ~lochإچn~, derived from the noun ~Locke~ ("curl"), but the two verbs are etymologically distinct.

The connection between ~locken~ ("to lure") and ~lأ¼gen~ ("to lie") hints at a deep Germanic association between enticement and deception -- to lure someone is, at root, a form of lying. The Latin cognate ~laciإچ~ likewise means "to entice" and is the ancestor of English ~lace~ (originally a snare or noose) and ~delicious~ (through ~dؤ“liciae~, "enticements, delights") -- though these Latin connections are disputed. English has no direct Germanic cognate of ~locken~ in common use; it instead uses ~lure~, borrowed from Old French. German ~Lockvogel~ ("decoy bird," literally "lure-bird") preserves the original concrete sense of baiting traps, while the modern figurative use -- ~ein Angebot, das Kunden lockt~ ("an offer that attracts customers") -- shows how trapping became marketing.
en.lohnen: From Middle High German ~lإچnen~, from Old High German ~lإچnإچn~, a denominal verb from ~lإچn~ ("reward, wage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~laun~, from Proto-Germanic *~launؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~lehâ‚‚w-~ ("gain, prize, booty"). Cognate with Old English ~lؤ“an~ ("reward, recompense"). The English cognate ~lean~ in this sense is now obsolete, but it survived long enough to be recorded in Old English poetry. The PIE root also underlies the idea of payment as something captured or won â€” an early conceptual link between labor and the spoils of contest.
en.lأ¤cheln:
From Middle High German ~lecheln~, formed from ~lachen~ ("to laugh") + the diminutive suffix ~-eln~:

- ~lachen~: From MHG ~lachen~, from OHG ~lahhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlahhjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlahjanؤ…~ ("to laugh"). Cognate with English ~laugh~, Dutch ~lachen~, and Old Norse ~hlأ¦ja~.
- ~-eln~: A diminutive verbal suffix indicating a lesser, gentler, or more tentative version of the base action.

~Lأ¤cheln~ is a diminutive of ~lachen~ â€” literally "to laugh a little." The vowel change from ~a~ to ~أ¤~ (umlaut) is triggered by the diminutive suffix, just as ~Haus~ becomes ~Hأ¤uschen~. This is one of the rare cases where German's diminutive system extends to verbs rather than nouns. The distinction between ~lachen~ (open, audible laughter) and ~lأ¤cheln~ (a quiet, gentle smile) exactly parallels the diminutive's function: softening and miniaturizing. English ~laugh~ and German ~lachen~ are exact cognates, but English has no equivalent diminutive verb â€” ~smile~ comes from an entirely different root (Proto-Germanic *~smؤ«lؤپnؤ…~). The Mona Lisa's enigmatic expression is a ~Lأ¤cheln~, never a ~Lachen~.
en.lأ¶schen: Two etymologically distinct verbs have merged under this spelling. The first, meaning "to extinguish," descends from Middle High German ~leschen~, Old High German ~lescan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~leskan~, from Proto-Germanic *~leskanؤ…~ ("to go out, be extinguished"). Cognate with Dutch ~lessen~. The original Proto-Germanic verb meant the fire goes out on its own; the transitive causative meaning â€” to put out a fire â€” developed later. The second, meaning "to delete, unload (cargo)," was borrowed from Low German ~lأ¶ssen~, ~lossen~, from Middle Low German ~lأ¶ssen~, derived from the ancestor of ~los~ ("free, rid of"). The spelling ~lأ¶schen~ for this second verb arose from later association with the first: unloaded cargo was figuratively "extinguished" from the ship's manifest. Today the digital-age meaning "to delete a file" continues exactly this metaphor.
en.lأ¶sen: From Middle High German ~lإ“sen~, ~lإچsen~, from Old High German ~lإچsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lausijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lausijanؤ…~, a causative of *~leusanؤ…~ ("to lose, let loose"), from Proto-Indo-European *~lewhâ‚پ-~ ("to cut, sever, separate, loosen, lose"). The verb is denominative via the adjective *~lausaz~ ("loose, free") â€” literally "to make loose." The same PIE root underlies English ~lose~ and ~loose~, Latin ~luere~ ("to release, atone"), Ancient Greek ~lأ؛إچ~ ("to loosen, liberate"), and Sanskrit ~lإ«na~ ("cut, severed"). The semantic range of ~lأ¶sen~ â€” from untying a knot to solving a problem to buying a ticket â€” all trace back to the original idea of releasing something that was bound. Cognate with Dutch ~lozen~, English ~loose~ (via Old Norse ~lauss~), obsolete English ~leese~ (from Old English ~lؤ«esan~), Danish ~lأ¸se~, Faroese ~loysa~, Icelandic ~leysa~, Norwegian ~lأ¸se~, Swedish ~lأ¶sa~.
en.machen: From Middle High German ~machen~, from Old High German ~mahhإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~makإچn~, from PIE *~mag-~ ("to knead, mix, make"). Cognate with Dutch ~maken~, Low German ~maken~, English ~make~.
en.malen:
From Middle High German ~mؤپlen~, from Old High German ~mؤپlإچn~, ~mؤپlؤ“n~, a denominative verb from the noun ~mؤپl~ ("spot, stain, mark"), from Proto-West Germanic *~mؤپlijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mؤ“lijanؤ…~, itself derived from *~mؤ“lؤ…~ ("mark, spot"). The ultimate PIE root is *~melhâ‚‚-~ ("dark color"), which also gives Lithuanian ~mؤ—las~ ("black") and Greek ~melas~ ("dark-colored, black"), the source of English ~melancholy~ (literally "black bile") and ~melanin~.

The semantic path from "mark, stain" to "to paint" is intuitive: the earliest painting was essentially making marks. Cognate with Dutch ~maal~ ("mark") and the German noun ~Mal~ ("mark, time, occasion") and ~Mahl~ ("meal" -- originally a "marked time" for eating). Be careful not to confuse ~malen~ ("to paint") with ~mahlen~ ("to grind"), which despite their similar sound have completely different origins: ~mahlen~ comes from Proto-Germanic *~malanؤ…~ ("to grind"), from PIE *~melhâ‚‚-~ ("to grind"). Ironically, the "to paint" and "to grind" roots may ultimately be related at the deepest PIE level, both involving the notion of rubbing or crushing, since dark pigments were made by grinding minerals -- but at the Proto-Germanic stage they were already distinct words.
en.markieren:
Borrowed from French ~marquer~ ("to mark, to indicate"), from Frankish *~markإچn~ ("to mark, to note"), from Proto-Germanic *~markإچ~ ("boundary, border, sign"), from PIE *~merاµ-~ ("edge, boundary"). The German verb was formed with the standard ~-ieren~ suffix for naturalized Romance verbs.

The word's journey is circular: a Frankish Germanic root traveled into Old French, was reshaped by Romance phonology, and then returned to German with a French suffix. The native German noun ~Marke~ ("brand, mark, stamp") was separately borrowed from French ~marque~, which has the same Frankish origin. English ~mark~ is a direct cognate of the original Germanic root, having never taken the detour through French. In modern internet-era German, ~markieren~ has acquired the additional meaning "to tag" someone on social media.
en.meinen: From Middle High German ~meinen~, from Old High German ~meinen~, from Proto-Germanic *~mainijanؤ…~ ("to mean, think; lament"), from PIE *~moyn-أ©ye-ti~ ("to intend, give an opinion"), a causative formation from the root *~meyn-~. The semantic range of the PIE root covered intending, opining, and expressing â€” a cluster that persists across its descendants. The Germanic cognates include both the intellectual sense ("to mean, think") and an emotional one: ~meinen~ is also related to English ~moan~, where the root's sense of "lamenting, expressing" took a sorrowful turn.
