The Irish cognate ~mأ­an~ ("wish, desire") preserves yet another facet of the original meaning. Cognate with Dutch ~menen~, West Frisian ~miene~, English ~mean~ and ~moan~, and Old Irish ~mأ­an~.
en.meistern:
From Middle High German ~meisteren~, from Old High German ~meistarإچn~ ("to master, to direct"), derived from the noun ~Meister~ ("master"), from OHG ~meistar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~maistar~, borrowed from Latin ~magister~ ("master, teacher, chief"), from PIE *~meg-~ ("great") via the superlative suffix *~-is-tero-~. The noun ~Meister~ is thus a doublet of ~Magister~ and ~Maestro~, all from the same Latin source.

The verb means "to master, to overcome, to manage successfully" â€” ~eine Herausforderung meistern~ ("to master a challenge"), ~eine schwierige Situation meistern~ ("to handle a difficult situation"). The Latin source ~magister~ literally meant "the greater one" (from ~magnus~ "great"), a formation parallel to ~minister~ ("the lesser one," from ~minus~ "less") â€” the master commands while the minister serves. This pair reveals Roman social hierarchy frozen in morphology. In the German trades, ~Meister~ carries enormous prestige: the ~Meisterbrief~ ("master craftsman's certificate") is the highest qualification in the vocational system, and many trades legally require it to open a business. The compound ~Meisterstأ¼ck~ ("masterpiece") originally referred to the work a journeyman had to produce to earn the ~Meister~ title â€” a tradition dating to the medieval guild system that is still practiced in some crafts today.
en.melden: From Middle High German ~mأ«lden~, from Old High German ~mأ«ldإچn~, ~mأ«ldؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~melأ¾إچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~melأ¾إچnؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~meldت°-~ ("to ask, pray, speak"). The same PIE root underlies Lithuanian ~maldأ½ti~ ("to implore"), Proto-Slavic *~modlأ¬ti~ ("to ask, pray"), Hittite ~mؤپldi~ ("to recite, make a vow"), and Old Armenian ~maإ‚tت»~ ("prayer, supplication"). The original sense was religious or supplicatory speech; ~melden~ retains the communicative dimension while shedding the devotional one, now meaning simply "to report, announce, register." A curious parallel: the PIE *~meldت°-~ root appears across Indo-European in prayer and invocation contexts, but in German it survives only in the bureaucratic sense of filing a report. Cognate with Dutch ~melden~, Low German ~mellen~, English ~meld~ (a partial cognate, via Dutch ~melden~, used in card games to mean "to declare").
en.merken: From Middle High German ~merken~, from Old High German ~merchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~markijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~markijanؤ…~ ("to mark, notice"), a denominal verb from *~markؤ…~ ("mark, sign"). Cognate with Dutch ~merken~, Icelandic ~merkja~, Old English ~marc~, and modern English ~mark~ (including ~remark~, which entered English via French but traces to the same Germanic root). The core semantic movement is from physical inscription â€” leaving a mark on a surface â€” to cognitive attention: to notice something is, in an older idiom, to leave a mental mark on it. German ~Marke~ ("brand, token, stamp") and ~merken~ share this common ancestor, making the act of noticing etymologically an act of marking.
en.messen:
From Middle High German ~mezzen~, from Old High German ~mezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~metan~, from Proto-Germanic *~metanؤ…~, from PIE *~med-~ ("to measure, to take appropriate measures"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~meten~, English ~mete~ (as in "to mete out"), Old English ~metan~.

The PIE root *~med-~ is spectacularly productive across the Indo-European family: it gives Latin ~medeor~ ("I heal" â€” originally "I take the right measure"), whence English ~medicine~, ~remedy~, and ~moderate~; Greek ~خ¼خ­خ´د‰~ ("I rule over, I measure") and ~خ¼خ­د„دپخ؟خ½~ (~metron~, "measure"), whence ~meter~, ~metric~, ~geometry~, and ~symmetry~; and Latin ~meditؤپrؤ«~ ("to reflect upon"), whence ~meditate~. The semantic thread connecting all these descendants is "taking the right measure" â€” whether of a physical quantity (~messen~), of a punishment (~mete out~), of a disease (~medicine~), or of one's thoughts (~meditate~). German ~Maأں~ ("measure") and ~Meter~ (borrowed back from Greek via French) both descend from the same ancient root, one through native inheritance and one through learned borrowing.
en.mieten: From Middle High German ~mieten~, from Old High German ~mietan~, a denominative verb formed from the noun ~Miete~ ("rent, reward"). The noun ~Miete~ descends from MHG ~miete~, from OHG ~mieta~, from Proto-West Germanic *~miت€du~ ("reward"), from Proto-Germanic *~mizdإچ~, from PIE *~misdت°أ©hâ‚‚~ ("reward, compensation"). The PIE root reveals a deep connection between rental payment and the ancient concept of reciprocal reward. Cognate with Gothic ~mizdo~ ("reward"), Old English ~meord~ ("reward"), Greek ~misthos~ ("wages, pay"), and Sanskrit ~midha~ ("reward, prize"). The semantic development from "reward" to "rent" reflects the narrowing of a general concept of compensation into a specific financial transaction. English ~meed~ ("a deserved share, reward"), now archaic and poetic, is the direct English cognate, sharing the same Proto-Germanic ancestor. The modern German word ~Miete~ has also taken on the secondary meaning of an earthen clamp for storing root vegetables, though that ~Miete~ is a separate word entirely, borrowed from Low German and ultimately from Latin ~mؤ“ta~ ("cone").
en.minimieren: Derived from ~Minimum~ with the verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. ~Minimum~ is borrowed from Latin ~minimum~, the neuter superlative of ~minimus~ ("smallest, least"), from ~minor~ ("smaller"), ultimately from PIE *~mey-~ ("small"), the same root that gave English ~minus~, ~minor~, ~minute~, ~diminish~, and ~minister~ (originally "a lesser servant"). The suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard German mechanism for creating verbs from Latin and French borrowings, corresponding to French ~-er~ and English ~-ize~. The antonym ~maximieren~ is built on the same pattern from Latin ~maximum~.
en.mischen:
From Middle High German ~mischen~ ("to mix"), from Old High German ~misken~ ("to mix"), from Proto-Germanic *~miskijanؤ…~ ("to mix"), from PIE *~meyل¸±-~ ("to mix, knead"), via the inchoative present *~miل¸±-sل¸±أ©-~. Cognate with English ~mix~, Latin ~misceإچ~ ("to mix"), Greek ~mأ­sgo~ ("to mix"), Old Church Slavonic ~mؤ›إ،ati~ ("to mix"), Lithuanian ~maiإ،yti~ ("to mix"), Sanskrit ~miإ›rأ،~ ("mixed"), Welsh ~mysgu~ ("to mix").

The PIE root *~meyل¸±-~ has an astonishingly wide reach across the Indo-European family, appearing in nearly every branch with the same core meaning. The Latin descendant ~misceإچ~ gave English ~miscellaneous~ and ~promiscuous~ (literally "thoroughly mixed"). The Germanic path through *~miskijanؤ…~ produced both German ~mischen~ and (via Old English *~mixian~) English ~mix~. French ~mأ©lange~ ("mixture") is also probably of Germanic origin, borrowed from Frankish *~misken~ with a fossilized Germanic suffix. The German verb covers both the general sense "to mix" and the specialized card-game sense "to shuffle."
en.mitbringen:
From ~mit-~ + ~bringen~. Compound of:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, from a pre-Germanic compound root *~bت°renل¸±-~, likely from PIE *~bت°er-~ ("to carry") + *~hâ‚پneل¸±-~ ("to bring, reach"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~mit-~: From MHG ~mit-~, from OHG ~mit-~, ~miti-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~midi~, from Proto-Germanic *~midi~, from PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys accompaniment â€” doing something along with someone or bringing something along.

~bringen~ is one of the most unusual verbs in Germanic: it has a mixed past tense combining both strong (ablaut: ~brachte~) and weak (dental suffix: ~-te~) features â€” the only known case of this mixture in Proto-Germanic. This anomaly arises from its compound PIE origin. ~mitbringen~ ("to bring along") is among the most common separable-prefix verbs in everyday German â€” one ~bringt~ a gift ~mit~ to a party, or ~bringt~ experience ~mit~ to a job. The question ~Was soll ich mitbringen?~ ("What should I bring?") is a fixture of German social life. Cognate with Hunsrik ~mitbringe~.
en.mitmachen:
Compound of ~mit-~ + ~machen~:

- ~machen~: From MHG ~machen~, from OHG ~mahhإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~makإچn~, from PIE *~mag-~ ("to knead, mix, make"). Cognate with English ~make~, Dutch ~maken~, Low German ~maken~.
- ~mit-~: From MHG ~mit(e)~, from OHG ~mit(i)~, from Proto-Germanic *~midi~ ("with, among"), from PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). Cognate with English ~mid~ (as in ~amid~, ~midway~), Greek ~meta~ ("with, after").

The literal sense is "to make along with, to do along," meaning "to participate, to join in." The verb is remarkably versatile in colloquial German: ~eine Reise mitmachen~ ("to go along on a trip"), ~eine Mode mitmachen~ ("to follow a fashion"), ~viel mitmachen mأ¼ssen~ ("to have had to endure a lot"), and ~da mache ich nicht mit~ ("I'm not going along with that"). The English cognate ~make~ and German ~machen~ have tracked closely in meaning for over a millennium, both expanding from a concrete PIE sense of kneading or mixing into the most general-purpose "doing" verb in their respective languages. Cognate also with Hunsrik ~mitmache~, carried to Brazil by German settlers in the 19th century.
en.mitnehmen:
Compound of ~mit-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~mit-~: From PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). This separable prefix adds the sense of accompaniment or inclusion â€” taking something along.

~Mitnehmen~ means "to take along, to take with" â€” ~Nimm einen Regenschirm mit!~ ("Take an umbrella along!"). But it has a vivid colloquial extension: ~mitgenommen~ (past participle) means "worn out, battered, exhausted" â€” someone who has been "taken along" by life's hardships looks ~mitgenommen~. The fast-food phrase ~zum Mitnehmen~ ("to go, takeaway") has become ubiquitous. The PIE root *~nem-~ is fascinating because it meant both "to give" and "to take" one's allotted portion â€” the ambiguity reflects a world where distribution and receipt were two sides of the same act. Greek ~خ½خ­خ¼د‰~ ("to distribute, to allot") and ~Nemesis~ (the goddess of rightful distribution turned retribution) descend from the same root. Cognate with Dutch ~meenemen~, Low German ~mitnehmen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, to grasp" â€” from the "take in" sense).
en.mitteilen:
From Middle High German ~miteteilen~, equivalent to ~mit-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailijanؤ…~, from PIE *~dehâ‚‚y-~ ("to divide, to share").
- ~mit-~: From PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). The prefix adds the sense of sharing or participating.

~Mitteilen~ literally means "to share a part with" â€” to divide something (information, news) and give a portion to someone else. The derived noun ~Mitteilung~ ("communication, notification, message") is standard in formal and administrative German. The compound is transparent in its structure: ~mit~ ("with") + ~teilen~ ("to divide") = to divide with someone, i.e., to share information. The related noun ~Mitleid~ ("compassion," literally "suffering-with") shows the same prefix in its empathic sense. Cognate with Dutch ~meedelen~.
en.montieren: Borrowed from French ~monter~ with the addition of the German verbal suffix ~-ieren~. The French source traces from Middle French ~monter~, from Old French ~monter~, from Vulgar Latin *~montؤپre~, from Latin ~mons~ (~montem~, "mountain"). The original French sense was "to go up, to climb," from the idea of ascending a mountain. The semantic shift to "to mount, to assemble" arose from the notion of placing something up or putting components together, as one might mount a horse or mount a piece of equipment into position. The ~-ieren~ suffix is German's standard mechanism for adapting French and Latin verbs, paralleling patterns like ~reparieren~, ~organisieren~, and ~installieren~. English ~mount~ is a direct cognate from the same French source, and the technical sense of ~montieren~ in German closely mirrors English ~to mount~ in mechanical and engineering contexts. In film, ~Montage~ (borrowed from French) preserves the assembly metaphor, referring to the cutting and joining of shots into a unified sequence.
en.motivieren: Borrowed from French ~motiver~, itself derived from ~motif~ ("motive") + the verbal suffix ~-er~. French ~motif~ descends from Medieval Latin ~motivus~ ("moving, impelling"), from Latin ~motus~, the past participle of ~movere~ ("to move"), from PIE *~mewhâ‚پ-~ ("to push away, move"). The German verb adds the standard ~-ieren~ suffix used for French and Latin loanwords. The word entered German during the 18th century as part of the broader wave of French intellectual vocabulary that shaped the language of Enlightenment discourse. English ~motivate~ arrived via the same Latin root but took a slightly different path through the adjective ~motive~. The PIE root *~mewhâ‚پ-~ is extraordinarily productive: it also yielded Latin ~momentum~ (a "movement," giving English ~moment~ and ~momentum~), ~mobile~ ("movable"), ~emotion~ (literally "a moving out"), and ~motor~. German ~Motiv~ serves double duty as both "motive" (reason for action) and "motif" (recurring artistic theme), preserving a unity that English splits between two borrowed words.
en.mأ¶gen: From Middle High German ~mugen~, from Old High German ~mugan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~magan~, from Proto-Germanic *~maganؤ…~, from PIE *~megت°-~ ("to be able, have power"). A preterite-present verb: its present-tense conjugations descend from original past-tense forms, which accounts for the absence of personal endings in the first- and third-person singular (~mag~, not *~magt~). Cognate with Dutch ~mogen~, English ~may~, Icelandic ~mega~, Gothic ~magan~.
en.mأ¼ssen: From Middle High German ~mأ¼eب¥en~, from Old High German ~muoب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~mإچtan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mإچtanؤ…~. A preterite-present verb derived from a secondary perfect of PIE *~mؤ“d-~ ("to measure, ensure compliance, take care of"). Alternatively connected to the same root as Proto-Germanic *~mإچtؤ…~ ("meeting"). The preservation of the original diphthong can still be seen in Bavarian ~muass~. Cognate with Dutch ~moeten~, English ~must~, Old Norse ~mega~.
en.nachdenken:
~nach-~ + ~denken~. Compound of ~nach-~ + ~denken~:

- ~denken~: From MHG ~denken~, from OHG ~denken~, ~thenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankijanؤ…~, from PIE *~tong-أ©ye-ti~, causative of *~teng-~ ("to think, feel, perceive, know").
- ~nach-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±~ ("to reach, to attain"). The separable prefix adds a sense of following after, pursuing.

~Nachdenken~ means "to reflect, to ponder, to think carefully about." Where ~denken~ is simply "to think," ~nachdenken~ is to think ~after~ â€” to follow a thought to its conclusion, to trace its implications. The prefix ~nach-~ here conveys pursuit: the thinker chases after an idea, turning it over repeatedly. The construction ~أ¼ber etwas nachdenken~ ("to reflect on something") is one of the most common intellectual verbs in German. As a neuter noun, ~das Nachdenken~ ("reflection, contemplation") is used without an article in the elegant phrase ~nach reiflichem Nachdenken~ ("after mature reflection"). Cognate with Hunsrik ~nohdenke~, Dutch ~nadenken~ ("to think over, to ponder").
en.nachfragen:
A compound of ~nach-~ + ~fragen~:

- ~fragen~: From MHG ~vrؤپgen~, from OHG ~frؤپgؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frؤپgؤ“n~. Cognate with Dutch ~vragen~, Swedish ~frأ¥ga~.
- ~nach-~: From Old High German ~nؤپh~ ("near, close"), from Proto-West Germanic *~nؤپhw~, from Proto-Germanic *~nؤ“hwaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ ("to reach, attain"). As a verbal prefix, ~nach-~ adds a sense of "after" or "following up."

The verb means "to inquire further, to follow up with a question" -- asking after something that has already been stated or left unclear. The PIE root *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ also gave rise to Latin ~nancؤ«scor~ ("I obtain") and Sanskrit ~naإ›ati~ ("to reach"), all sharing the idea of reaching or arriving at something. Cognate with Dutch ~navragen~.
en.nachgehen:
From Middle High German ~nؤپchgؤپn~, from Old High German ~nؤپhgؤپn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~nؤپhwagؤپn~. Compound of ~nach-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave, go").
- ~nach-~: From OHG ~nؤپh~ ("near, close"), from Proto-West Germanic *~nؤپhw~, from Proto-Germanic *~nؤ“hwaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ ("to reach, attain"). As a prefix, it conveys "after, following."

The literal meaning is "to go after," which branches into several figurative senses: "to pursue," "to investigate" (as in ~einer Sache nachgehen~), and "to be slow" (of a clock). The spelling with ~-h-~ in ~gehen~ was introduced by analogy with ~sehen~, where the ~h~ had become silent but was retained in writing. Cognate with Dutch ~nagaan~.
en.nachlesen:
Compound of ~nach-~ + ~lesen~:

- ~lesen~: From MHG ~lأ«sen~, from OHG ~lأ«san~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lesan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lesanؤ…~ ("to gather"), from PIE *~les-~ ("to gather"). Cognate with English ~lease~ (with an obsolete meaning "to gather"), Dutch ~lezen~ ("to read"), Afrikaans ~lees~ ("to read").
- ~nach-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±~ ("to reach, attain"). This separable prefix conveys the idea of following after, reviewing, or looking something up after the fact.

~Nachlesen~ means "to look up, to read up on, to check a reference" -- literally "to read after." The semantic development of ~lesen~ from "to gather" to "to read" is one of the most famous in Germanic linguistics: just as Latin ~legere~ independently shifted from "to gather" to "to read" (whence English ~legible~, ~lesson~, ~lecture~), Germanic speakers transferred the concept of gathering scattered items to gathering meaning from scattered letters. The parallel evolution in two unrelated branches of Indo-European suggests something deep about how humans conceptualize reading: as picking up meaning, symbol by symbol, just as one gathers grapes from a vine -- and indeed, German ~Weinlese~ ("grape harvest") preserves the original "gathering" sense.
en.nachvollziehen:
Compound of ~nach-~ + ~vollziehen~:

- ~vollziehen~: Itself a compound of ~voll~ ("full") + ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw"), from MHG ~volleziehen~, from OHG ~follaziohan~. ~Voll~ descends from OHG ~foll~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullaz~, from PIE *~plج¥hâ‚پnأ³s~ ("full"). ~Ziehen~ from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Compare Dutch ~voltrekken~.
- ~nach-~: From MHG ~nؤپch~, from OHG ~nؤپh~, from Proto-Germanic *~nؤ“hw~ ("near, close"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ ("to reach, attain"). As a prefix, it means "after, following, re-."

The literal sense is "to carry out after" â€” to retrace and reconstruct in one's mind what someone else has done or thought. ~Nachvollziehen~ means "to comprehend, to understand by mentally retracing" and fills a gap that English struggles to express in a single word. It is not mere understanding but empathetic reconstruction â€” following the same mental path another person walked. The word is a mid-20th-century coinage, surprisingly young for such a deeply embedded concept. The base verb ~vollziehen~ means "to carry out, to execute" (a law, a sentence) â€” itself a calque of Latin ~exsequؤ«~ ("to follow out"). The PIE root *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead") also yielded Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead"), whence English ~duke~, ~conduct~, and ~education~.
en.nachweisen:
~nach-~ + ~weisen~. Compound of ~nach-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~ ("to show, to point out"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see").
- ~nach-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±~ ("to reach, attain"). The prefix adds the sense of following after, tracing back, or verification through pursuit.

~Nachweisen~ means "to prove, to demonstrate, to detect, to verify" â€” literally "to show after" or "to point out by following the trail." The prefix ~nach-~ contributes a forensic quality: one does not merely show something (~weisen~), one traces it, follows the evidence, and then shows it to be true. The derived noun ~Nachweis~ ("proof, evidence, certificate, verification") is ubiquitous in German bureaucracy, science, and law â€” one needs a ~Nachweis~ for everything from vaccination status (~Impfnachweis~) to proof of income (~Einkommensnachweis~) to scientific detection (~DNA-Nachweis~). The verb can take a dative object: ~jemandem etwas nachweisen~ ("to prove something against someone"), often in a legal or investigative context â€” "to catch someone out." The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see") thus achieves its most rigorous expression here: ~nachweisen~ is seeing that has been verified through systematic pursuit. Cognate with Dutch ~nawijzen~ (rare).
en.nehmen: From Middle High German ~nأ«men~, from Old High German ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~nehmen~, Dutch ~nemen~, English ~nim~ ("to take, filch"), Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp"), Swedish ~fأ¶rnimma~ ("to perceive").
en.neigen:
From a merger of MHG ~nؤ«gen~ (intransitive, strong) and ~neigen~ (transitive, weak), from OHG ~nؤ«gan~ and ~neigen~ respectively, from Proto-Germanic *~hnؤ«ganؤ…~ (strong, intransitive: "to bow, to bend") and its causative *~hnaigijanؤ…~ (weak, transitive: "to make bow, to incline"). The strong verb *~hnؤ«ganؤ…~ descends from PIE *~kneygت·ت°-~ ("to bend, droop"). Cognate with Dutch ~nijgen~ and ~neigen~, Old English ~hnؤ«gan~ ("to bow") and ~hnا£gan~ ("to cause to bow"), Old Norse ~hnؤ«ga~ and ~hneiga~.

The strong forms (~neigen~, ~nieg~, ~geniegen~) vanished from the standard language by the 16th century, though they survive in dialects â€” making ~neigen~ an example of a once-strong verb that went entirely weak. The modern verb means "to incline, to lean, to tend" â€” both physically (to tilt) and metaphorically (~dazu neigen~, "to tend to, to be inclined to"). The noun ~Neigung~ ("inclination, tendency") carries both senses. The PIE root *~kneygت·ت°-~ also produced Latin ~conؤ«veإچ~ ("I blink, I overlook" â€” literally "I bend together [the eyelids]") and ~nؤ«tor~ ("I lean on"), showing how the concept of bending diverged into remarkably different physical actions across the Indo-European branches.
en.nennen: From Middle High German ~nennen~, ~nemmen~, ~nemnen~, from Old High German ~nemnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~namnijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~namnijanؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from Proto-Germanic *~namأ´~ ("name"), from PIE *~hâ‚پnأ³mnج¥~ ("name"). The verb literally means "to name" â€” that is, "to perform the act of giving a name" â€” and has not meaningfully drifted from its PIE origin across more than five millennia. ~Nennen~ exhibits the same mixed conjugation as ~bringen~ and ~denken~: it forms its past tense ~nannte~ with a vowel change plus a weak dental suffix, rather than following either a purely strong or purely weak paradigm. Like ~kennen~ (its close relative via the same PIE root *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ "to know"), ~nennen~ has umlaut suppression in the past tense. Cognate with Old English ~nemnan~, Middle English ~nemnen~, Swedish ~nأ¤mna~, and Hunsrik ~nenne~.
en.nerven: A modern denominative verb formed from the noun ~Nerv~ ("nerve"), via the colloquial expression ~auf die Nerven gehen~ ("to get on someone's nerves"). ~Nerv~ was borrowed from Latin ~nervus~ ("sinew, tendon"), partly through French ~nerf~. Latin ~nervus~ comes from earlier *~neuros~ (by metathesis), from Proto-Italic *~(s)nؤ“uros~, from PIE *~snل¸—hâ‚پwrج¥~ ("sinew, tendon"). Cognate with Greek ~neuron~ (whence English ~neuron~ and ~neurotic~) and Old English ~sinu~ ("sinew"). The meaning shift from "sinew" to "nerve" occurred in medical Latin; the further shift to "to annoy" is a twentieth-century colloquial development unique to German, where the anatomical term became a metaphor for irritation.
en.notieren: From Middle High German ~notieren~, borrowed from Latin ~notإچ~ ("I mark, note, observe"), from ~nota~ ("a mark, sign, note"). ~Nota~ is likely related to ~nإچscإچ~ ("I come to know"), from PIE *~اµnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"), the same root behind English ~know~, ~notice~, Greek ~gnإچsis~, and Sanskrit ~jأ±ؤپ~. The verb entered German through medieval scholarly Latin, reflecting the transmission of literate culture from Roman to Germanic lands. In modern German, ~notieren~ means "to jot down, to note," and also has a specialized financial sense: "to be quoted at" (a price), paralleling English ~note~ in stock-exchange usage. Cognate with French ~noter~, Italian ~notare~, English ~note~.
en.nutzen: From Middle High German ~nutzen~, ~nأ¼tzen~, from Old High German ~nuzzإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~nutإچnؤ…~, from *~nutإچ~ ("use"), from *~nutiz~ ("useful"), from *~neutanؤ…~ ("to enjoy, use, catch"), from PIE *~newd-~ ("to acquire, make use of"). The verb was originally restricted to Upper German and East Central German dialects, the former preferring the conjugation ~nutzen~, the latter preferring ~nأ¼tzen~. By way of hypercorrection, ~nأ¼tzen~ has now become a chiefly southern conjugation; Low German and West Central German originally used other verbs (namely ~brauchen~ for the transitive function) and prefer ~nutzen~ in modern standard German. Cognate with modern ~Nutzen~ (noun, "benefit, use").
en.nأ¤hen:
From MHG ~nؤپian~, ~nأ¦jen~, from OHG ~nؤپjen~, ~nؤپian~, from Proto-Germanic *~nؤ“anؤ…~ ("to sew"). Cognate with German ~Nadel~ ("needle") and English ~needle~ (both from the instrument noun formed on the same root), Dutch ~naaien~, Gothic ~nؤ“أ¾la~ ("needle").

The PIE root *~(s)nehâ‚پ-~ ("to spin, to sew") is notable for its optional initial ~s-~ â€” the so-called ~s-mobile~, a mysterious phonological feature of Proto-Indo-European where certain roots could appear with or without an initial ~s-~ with no change in meaning. The ~s-~less variant produced Latin ~nؤ“re~ ("to spin") and Greek ~nأھma~ ("thread"), whence English ~nematic~ (describing thread-like liquid-crystal arrangements). The instrument noun *~nehâ‚پ-dlehâ‚‚~ ("that which sews") traveled through Proto-Germanic *~nؤ“أ¾lإچ~ to produce both German ~Nadel~ and English ~needle~ â€” one of the clearest cases where a common tool name reveals a shared craft tradition stretching back to the prehistoric Indo-European homeland.
en.nأ¤hern:
From the comparative form ~nأ¤her~ ("nearer") with the verbal suffix ~-n~, itself from the adjective ~nah~ ("near"), from MHG ~nؤپch~, from OHG ~nؤپh~, from Proto-West Germanic *~nؤپh~, from Proto-Germanic *~nؤ“hwaz~ ("near"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ ("to reach, attain"). Cognate with English ~nigh~ (from OE ~nؤ“ah~), Dutch ~na~.

~Nأ¤hern~ is almost always reflexive: ~sich nأ¤hern~ ("to approach, to draw near"). The word is a denominal verb built from a comparative: "to make nearer." The PIE root *~hâ‚‚neل¸±-~ also produced Sanskrit ~naإ›ati~ ("to reach, attain") and Latin ~nancؤ«scؤ«~ ("to obtain"). German ~nach~ ("after, toward") is a doublet of ~nah~ -- both descend from the same root, but ~nach~ specialized as a preposition while ~nah~ remained an adjective. The English cognate ~nigh~ survives mainly in poetry and the fixed phrase "nigh impossible."
en.nأ¼tzen: From Middle High German ~nأ¼tzen~, a variant of ~nutzen~, from Old High German ~nuzzإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~nutإچnؤ…~, *~nutjإچnؤ…~, derived from *~nutإچ~ ("use, enjoyment"), from *~nutiz~ ("useful"), from *~neutanؤ…~ ("to enjoy, use"), from PIE *~newd-~ ("to acquire, make use of"). The ~أ¼~ form arose in East Central German dialects through umlaut, while Upper German dialects preferred ~nutzen~ without umlaut. By hypercorrection, ~nأ¼tzen~ became the standard southern form, while northern Germany adopted ~nutzen~. Both forms coexist in modern standard German with identical meaning. Cognate with Lithuanian ~nauda~ ("benefit"), Russian ~nuzhda~ ("need"). English ~enjoy~ is unrelated, but the archaic English ~nait~ ("to use") descends from the same Proto-Germanic verb *~neutanؤ…~.
en.offenbaren:
From ~offenbar~ ("manifest, obvious") + ~-en~. ~Offenbar~ is composed of ~offen~ ("open") + ~-bar~ ("-able, carrying"):

- ~offen~: From MHG ~offen~, from OHG ~offan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~opan~, from Proto-Germanic *~upanaz~, originally a past participle meaning "lying on top" and hence "uncovered, open," derived from *~upp~ ("up"), from PIE *~upo~ ("up, over"). Cognate with English ~open~, Dutch ~open~, Swedish ~أ¶ppen~.
- ~-bar~: From OHG ~-bؤپri~, from Proto-Germanic *~-bؤ“rijaz~ ("carrying, bearing"), from *~beranؤ…~ ("to bear"), from PIE *~bت°er-~ ("to carry, bear").

The literal sense of ~offenbar~ is thus "carrying openness" -- something that bears itself openly, that is evident. ~Offenbaren~ means "to reveal, to disclose," and in religious usage, "to reveal" in the divine sense, as in die ~Offenbarung~ des Johannes (the Book of Revelation). The past participle may appear as either ~offenbart~ (the prescribed form) or ~geoffenbart~ (with the ~ge-~ prefix that is normally suppressed in inseparable-prefix verbs).
en.optimieren: A denominative verb formed from the noun ~Optimum~ ("optimum, the best state"), with the productive ~-ieren~ suffix. ~Optimum~ was borrowed from Latin ~optimum~, the neuter of ~optimus~ ("best"), the superlative of ~bonus~ ("good"). ~Optimus~ derives from an earlier Latin *~opitumus~, from ~ops~ ("power, resources, wealth"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒep-~ ("to work, to produce"). The same root gave Latin ~opus~ ("work"), ~opera~ ("effort, work"), ~opulentus~ ("wealthy"), and ~officium~ ("duty, office") â€” whence English ~operate~, ~opulent~, and ~office~. German ~optimieren~ thus etymologically means "to make the most powerful, to bring to peak performance." The word entered German in the 20th century as a technical term from mathematics and engineering, then spread to everyday language. The related adjective ~optimal~ and noun ~Optimierung~ ("optimization") are ubiquitous in modern German business and technology discourse. A nice irony: the PIE root *~hâ‚ƒep-~ ("to work") also produced Latin ~opus~, so ~optimieren~ is, at its deepest root, about ~working~ something into its best state. Cognate with Dutch ~optimaliseren~, English ~optimize~, French ~optimiser~.
en.ordnen:
From Middle High German ~ordenen~, from Old High German ~ordinإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ordinإچn~ ("to order, put in order"), borrowed from Latin ~إچrdinؤپre~ ("to arrange, set in order"), from ~إچrdإچ~ ("row, rank, order"), from Proto-Italic *~ordإچ~, probably from PIE *~hâ‚‚er-~ ("to fit together"). Doublet of ~ordinieren~ ("to ordain"). Cognate with English ~order~ (via Old French, from the same Latin source), Dutch ~ordenen~, and Italian ~ordinare~.

The journey from PIE *~hâ‚‚er-~ ("to fit together") to modern ~ordnen~ traces a remarkable semantic stability: the idea of fitting things into their proper arrangement has survived over five millennia. Latin ~إچrdإچ~ originally described a row of threads in a loom â€” a warp that held everything in place â€” before generalizing to any row, rank, or sequence. The derivative ~Ordnung~ ("order, tidiness") is practically a national concept: ~Ordnung muss sein~ ("there must be order") is the quintessential German maxim.
en.organisieren: Borrowed from French ~organiser~ (attested from the 17th century), from Medieval Latin ~organizؤپre~, from Latin ~organum~ ("instrument, tool, organ"), itself a borrowing from Ancient Greek ~أ³rganon~ ("tool, instrument"), from PIE *~werاµ-~ ("to work"). The Greek ~أ³rganon~ is the same word that gave the world the philosophical term "organon" (as in Aristotle's logical works, his tool for reasoning), the musical ~organ~, and the biological ~organ~. The semantic path from "tool" to "organization" runs through the idea of coordinating instruments or parts into a working whole â€” as individual pipes are organized into a functioning organ. German received ~organisieren~ through French in the same period as many other learned ~-ieren~ borrowings flooded the language. The ~-ieren~ suffix, entering via Old French contact from the 12th century onward, marks it as a loanverb and guarantees weak conjugation. Cognate with English ~organize~, French ~organiser~, Spanish ~organizar~, Italian ~organizzare~.
en.orientieren: Borrowed from French ~orienter~, itself from ~orient~ ("east, the direction of the rising sun"), from Old French ~oriant~, from Latin ~oriens~ ("rising"), the present active participle of ~orior~ ("to rise, to be born"), from Italic *~orjإچr~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒer-~ ("to stir, to rise"). The underlying image is vivid: medieval European maps placed east at the top (toward the ~Orient~, where the sun rises), so ~orienter~ originally meant "to align a map with east at the top" â€” that is, to find one's bearings by the sunrise. The reflexive ~sich orientieren~ ("to orient oneself, to get one's bearings") preserves this navigational metaphor. German added the standard ~-ieren~ suffix for French-derived verbs, and the Hunsrik cognate ~orأ¯enteere~ shows the same borrowing pattern among German emigrants in Brazil. Cognate with English ~orient~, Italian ~orientare~, Spanish ~orientar~. The Latin root *~hâ‚ƒer-~ also gives us Greek ~ل½„دپخ½ل؟،خ¼خ¹~ ("to stir up") and Sanskrit ~ل¹›ل¹‡أ³ti~ ("to move").
en.packen: Borrowed from Middle Low German ~packen~, from Old Saxon *~pakko~, from Proto-West Germanic *~pakkإچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~pakkأ´~ ("bundle, pack"). The ultimate origin is debated: the word may derive from an early contact with Vulgar Latin *~paccإچ~ or Celtic languages, or it may be a native Germanic formation; the absence of the expected High German consonant shift (~p~ should have become ~pf~ or ~f~ in OHG) confirms it entered High German from the north via Low German trade routes rather than descending through the native High German line. ~Packen~ means "to pack" but also "to grab, to seize" â€” the two senses unite in the physical act of grasping and bundling: you ~packst~ things into a suitcase, but you also ~packst~ someone by the arm. The colloquial ~Das packen wir!~ ("We can handle it!") extends the grabbing sense to mastery over a situation. The imperative ~Pack dich!~ ("Beat it!") adds another layer: to pack yourself away, to bundle yourself off. Cognate with English ~pack~, Dutch ~pakken~, Swedish ~packa~, Danish ~pakke~. The noun ~Pack~ in German also means "rabble, riffraff" â€” a pejorative use where people are treated as a mere bundle, a mass without individuality.
en.passen: Borrowed from French ~passer~, with Dutch ~passen~ as an intermediary and reinforcing influence; compare the German noun ~Pass~. French ~passer~ derives from Middle French ~passer~, from Old French ~passer~, from Vulgar Latin *~passؤپre~, a denominative verb from Latin ~passus~ ("step, pace"), from ~pandإچ~ ("to spread, stretch out"), from PIE *~pet-~ ("to spread, extend"). The Latin word ~passus~ measured the Roman double-step â€” roughly 1.5 meters â€” and gave English ~pace~ and the unit ~passus~ in Roman surveying. German ~passen~ entered primarily through Dutch trade contact and retains the Dutch sense of "to fit, to suit" alongside the French-influenced sense of "to pass." A notable false friend lurks nearby: English ~pass~ and German ~passen~ overlap in sports contexts (passing a ball), but ~passen~ most commonly means "to fit" or "to suit," not "to pass by." The noun ~Pass~ (mountain pass, passport) is a separate borrowing from the same Latin root. Cognate with French ~passer~, Dutch ~passen~, Italian ~passare~, English ~pass~, ~pace~.
en.passieren:
Borrowed from French ~passer~, from Old French ~passer~, from Vulgar Latin *~passؤپre~, derived from Latin ~passus~ ("step"), the past participle of ~pandere~ ("to spread out, stretch"), from Proto-Italic *~patnإچ~, from PIE *~pethâ‚‚-~ ("to spread out"). A "step" (Latin ~passus~) was thus literally "a spreading" â€” of the legs in walking.

German ~passieren~ carries two distinct meanings that developed along different semantic lines: "to pass (a place, a border, an obstacle)" and "to happen, occur." The second sense ("Was ist passiert?" â€” "What happened?") is a calque of the French intransitive use of ~passer~ in the same sense, particularly in the construction ~il se passe quelque chose~ ("something is happening"). English ~pass~ is a direct cognate from the same Old French source. The Latin unit of measurement the ~passus~ (roughly 1.48 meters, a double step) gave the world the ~mille passuum~ â€” "a thousand paces" â€” from which English ~mile~ derives. Cognate with English ~pass~, Dutch ~passeren~, Spanish ~pasar~, Italian ~passare~.
en.pflanzen: From Middle High German ~pflanzen~, from Old High German ~pflanzإچn~, a denominative verb from ~pflanza~ ("plant"), which was borrowed from Latin ~planta~ ("a shoot, cutting, plant"). The phonological change from Latin ~pl-~ to German ~pfl-~ is part of the High German consonant shift and is one of the most distinctive markers of Old High German. The same shift turned Latin ~piper~ into German ~Pfeffer~ and Latin ~platea~ into ~Platz~. Latin ~planta~ itself is of uncertain origin but may be related to ~planus~ ("flat") -- a plant being something pressed flat into the soil. English ~plant~ was also borrowed from Latin but shows no consonant shift, having come via Old English from the same Latin source. Cognate with Luxembourgish ~Planz~.
en.pflegen: From Middle High German ~phlأ«gen~, from Old High German ~plأ«gan~ ("to take care of, be responsible for, stand up for"), from Proto-West Germanic *~plehan~. The Proto-West Germanic verb is a strong class-5 verb meaning "to stand up for, be responsible for," and its past tense forms survive in the archaic German literary past tense ~pflog~ (rarely used today but occasionally found in elevated or ironic registers). Cognate with Dutch ~plegen~, Old English ~plؤ“on~, Old Norse ~plega~, and Old Saxon ~plegan~. The descendants of *~plehan~ in the reconstruction include a borrowing path into Medieval Latin as *~pleviإچ~ ("to pledge"), which fed into Old French ~plege~ and ultimately into English ~pledge~ â€” making ~pflegen~ and English ~pledge~ distant relatives, the one meaning "to tend, nurture, maintain" and the other "a solemn promise," both rooted in the ancient idea of taking responsibility for something.
en.planen:
Denominal verb derived from the noun ~Plan~, which was borrowed from French ~plan~ ("plan, ground plan"). French ~plan~ derives either from the verb ~planter~ ("to plant, set firmly") or from Latin ~planus~ ("flat, level"), the latter being the more widely accepted source. Latin ~planus~ traces from PIE *~plehâ‚‚-~ ("flat, broad"). The architectural sense â€” a flat drawing representing a structure â€” then generalized to the modern sense of any scheme or design.

Interestingly, English ~plan~ entered the language via the same French source as German ~Plan~, meaning both languages acquired the word independently through the same medieval and early-modern channel of French architectural and technical vocabulary. The related Latin ~planus~ also gives German ~Plan~ its second meaning of "plain, flat terrain" (as in ~Hochebene~), though the planning sense has long since become dominant. Cognate with Dutch ~plannen~, English ~plan~, Swedish ~planera~, French ~planifier~.
en.platzieren: From French ~placer~ ("to place") with the German suffix ~-ieren~, a verb-forming ending used to adapt foreign (especially French and Latin) words into German conjugation patterns. A later spelling reform reanalyzed the word as ~Platz~ + ~-ieren~, aligning it visually with its semantic root. The noun ~Platz~ descends from MHG ~plaz~, borrowed from Old French ~place~, from Latin ~platؤ“a~ ("broad street"), from Greek ~plateia hodos~ ("broad way"), from PIE *~plethâ‚‚-~ ("broad, flat"). This makes ~platzieren~ a case of round-trip borrowing: a Latin word traveled into French, was borrowed into German as a noun (~Platz~), and then the French verb derived from the same Latin root was borrowed separately with ~-ieren~, only for German speakers to later reinterpret the verb as derived from their own noun. Cognate with English ~place~ (via Norman French), Dutch ~plaatsen~, Italian ~piazzare~. The PIE root *~plethâ‚‚-~ also gives English ~flat~, ~plaza~, and ~platypus~ ("flat-footed"), making this humble rental-office verb a distant cousin of the duck-billed platypus.
en.positionieren: Derived from ~Position~ ("position") with the verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. ~Position~ was borrowed from Latin ~positiإچ~ ("a placing, position"), from ~positus~ ("placed"), the past participle of ~pإچnإچ~ ("I place, put"), from an older form *~posinإچ~, a compound of *~po-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚epo~ "off, away") + *~sinإچ~ ("I let, place"). The PIE root *~tل¸±ey-~ ("to settle, dwell") has been proposed as the ultimate source. German ~positionieren~ means "to position" and has gained currency in both physical and abstract senses (as in ~sich positionieren~, "to take a stance"). The suffix ~-ieren~ marks it as a learned borrowing, distinguishing it from native Germanic compounds like ~hinstellen~.
en.posten:
Borrowed from English ~post~ (in the internet sense "to post a message"), itself from English ~post~ ("mail, dispatch"), from French ~poste~, from Italian ~posta~ ("station, mail"), from Vulgar Latin *~posta~, from Latin ~posita~ (feminine past participle of ~pإچnere~ "to place"), originally referring to a relay station where horses were ~placed~ for messengers.

~Posten~ is one of the youngest verbs in the German language, entering common usage in the 1990s with the rise of internet forums and then social media. It means "to post" a message, photo, or update online. The irony of its etymology is rich: a word that began with Roman relay riders placing fresh horses at stations along imperial roads now describes placing digital content on servers. The German language already had an older ~posten~ (from the noun ~Posten~, "post, position, guard duty"), but the internet sense is unmistakably an English borrowing. The ~Duden~ dictionary officially recognized the internet sense in the early 2000s. Unlike many English borrowings that German speakers resist (the infamous ~Denglisch~ debate), ~posten~ has been widely accepted, perhaps because the German alternatives â€” ~verأ¶ffentlichen~ ("to publish"), ~einstellen~ ("to place in") â€” feel too formal for the casual act of sharing a photo of one's lunch.
en.probieren:
From Latin ~probإچ~ ("to approve, test, examine"), from ~probus~ ("good, fit"), from PIE *~pro-bت°uH-s~ ("being in front"), composed of *~pro-~ ("forward") + *~bت°uH-~ ("to be"). A doublet of ~proben~ ("to rehearse") and ~prأ¼fen~ ("to test, examine"), all three descending from the same Latin source by different routes and at different periods.

The meaning "to try, to taste, to sample" represents the examining/testing sense of Latin ~probإچ~ rather than the approving sense. The German proverb ~Probieren geht أ¼ber Studieren~ ("Trying beats studying") captures the empirical spirit the word carries. The ~-ieren~ suffix marks it as a learned Latin borrowing, distinguishing it from the more natively adapted ~prأ¼fen~ (which entered earlier and underwent more thorough sound changes, passing through Old French ~prover~ and Middle High German ~prأ¼even~). The same Latin root gives English ~prove~, ~probe~, ~probable~, ~probation~, and ~approbation~. The Italian ~provare~, Spanish ~probar~, and French ~prouver~ are all sisters of ~probieren~.
en.produzieren: Borrowed from Latin ~prإچdإ«cإچ~ ("to lead forth, bring out, extend, produce"), a compound of ~prإچ-~ ("forward, forth") + ~dإ«cإچ~ ("to lead, draw"), from Proto-Italic *~doukإچ~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to draw, pull"). English ~tow~ (as in to tow a boat) is a cognate via the Germanic branch of the same PIE root. The Latin verb's core meaning was spatial â€” "to bring something forward" or "to extend" â€” which extends naturally to agricultural and theatrical production: ~prإچdإ«cere~ was used of bringing a crop to fruition or of staging a performance. German borrowed ~produzieren~ with the productive ~-ieren~ suffix, which always creates weak verbs from Latin and French loanwords. Cognate with English ~produce~, French ~produire~, Spanish ~producir~, Italian ~produrre~. The related Latin ~dux~ ("leader," from the same ~dإ«cإچ~) gave Italian ~duce~ and English ~duke~ â€” a "leader" is, etymologically, someone who draws others forward.
en.profitieren: Borrowed from French ~profiter~ ("to benefit, profit"), from Old French ~profit~ (modern French ~profit~), from Latin ~prإچfectus~ ("advance, progress, growth, increase, profit"), the past participle of ~prإچficiإچ~ ("to go forward, advance, make progress, be useful"), a compound of ~prإچ-~ ("forward") + ~faciإچ~ ("to make, do"). The Latin root sense is fundamentally one of forward motion: profit is progress. English ~profit~ arrived via the same Old French channel. German ~profitieren~ was formed with the standard ~-ieren~ suffix, marking it as a French loanverb with weak conjugation. Cognate with English ~profit~, French ~profiter~, Spanish ~profitar~, Italian ~profittare~.
en.prأ¤gen: ~Prأ¤gen~ ("to mint, coin; to shape, leave an impression") derives from Middle High German ~brأ¦chen~ / ~prأ¦chen~ ("to press in, to depict"), from Old High German ~giprؤپhhan~ / ~prؤپhhan~ ("to press in or scratch by breaking the surface"). These forms arose from the first and third person plural preterite forms of ~prؤ“chan~ / ~prؤپchun~ ("to break"), making ~prأ¤gen~ a verb that is likely related to ~brechen~ ("to break"). The noun ~Prأ¤gung~ ("imprint, coinage, stamp") shares the root. The semantic development moves from physically breaking or incising a surface â€” the action of a die striking metal to mint a coin â€” to the broader sense of leaving a lasting impression: ~ein Erlebnis prأ¤gt einen~ ("an experience shapes you"). The abstract sense dominates in modern usage, but the metalworking origin remains visible in ~Mأ¼nzprأ¤gung~ ("coin minting").
en.prأ¤sentieren: Inherited from Middle High German ~prؤ“sentieren~, borrowed from Old French ~prأ©senter~ (modern French ~prأ©senter~). The French verb derives from Latin ~praesentإچ~ ("to place before, present"), a denominative of ~praesens~ ("present, at hand"), itself the present active participle of ~praesum~ ("to be before, preside over"), from ~prae-~ ("before") + ~sum~ ("to be"), from PIE *~hâ‚پes-~ ("to be"). The ~-ieren~ suffix â€” German's productive borrowing suffix for foreign verbs â€” arrived through Old French contact beginning in the 12thâ€“13th centuries and became the standard treatment for learned Latin and French borrowings, distinguishing them from native Germanic strong verbs (compare ~schreiben~, borrowed from Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~ directly in the Roman period and fully assimilated into the strong-verb system). A word that has traveled from PIE "to be" through Latin "to be before" through French "to place before" to modern German boardrooms and PowerPoint slides. Cognate with French ~prأ©senter~, English ~present~ (verb), Spanish ~presentar~, Italian ~presentare~.
en.prأ¼fen: From Middle High German ~prأ¼even~ (first attested in the 13th century), ultimately from Latin ~probؤپre~ ("to test, approve, prove"), a denominative of ~probus~ ("good, honest, upright"), from Proto-Italic *~proخ²wos~, from Proto-Indo-European *~pro-bت°uH-wأ³-s~ ("being foremost, excellent"), from *~pro-~ ("forward") + *~bت°uH-~ ("to be"). Unlike the ~-ieren~ borrowings, ~prأ¼fen~ entered German very early â€” before the ~-ieren~ suffix pattern was productive â€” and was fully assimilated into the native weak-verb system. The route was almost certainly through Middle Dutch or Middle Low German, which would explain the umlaut (~أ¼~ rather than ~o~): the form *~prإچfijan~ underwent High German umlaut to produce ~prأ¼even~. English ~prove~ arrived from the same Latin source via Old French, as did ~probe~ (a more direct Latin borrowing). That ~prأ¼fen~, ~prove~, and ~probe~ are all triplets from the same Latin root â€” with ~prأ¼fen~ the oldest German survivor â€” illustrates how a single Latin verb can splinter into phonologically distinct words across centuries of borrowing. Cognate with English ~prove~, ~probe~, Dutch ~proeven~, French ~prouver~.
en.punkten:
From the noun ~Punkt~ ("point, dot"), from Middle High German ~punct~, borrowed from Late Latin ~pإ«nctus~ ("point, dot"), from Latin ~pungإچ~ ("to prick, puncture"), from PIE *~pewاµ-~ ("to prick, punch").

~Punkten~ is a relatively modern back-formation meaning "to score, to gain points." While ~Punkt~ has been in German since the medieval period, the verb ~punkten~ only became common in the 20th century, initially in sports journalism. The PIE root *~pewاµ-~ ("to prick") also produced Latin ~pugnus~ ("fist") and Greek ~pugmل¸—~ ("fist, boxing") â€” whence English ~pugilist~. The semantic journey from "prick" to "point" to "score" is delightful: a tiny puncture mark became a dot, a dot became a unit of scoring, and the noun became a verb. German ~Punkt~ is also a false friend for English speakers: ~Punkt drei Uhr~ means "at three o'clock sharp."
en.qualifizieren:
From Medieval Latin ~qualificؤپre~ ("to characterize, to designate"), a compound of Latin ~quؤپlis~ ("of what kind") + ~-ficؤپre~ (from ~facere~ "to make"), from PIE *~kت·o-~ ("who, what," the interrogative stem) + *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, to make"). Borrowed into German in the early modern period.

~Qualifizieren~ means "to qualify" -- both transitively ("to make suitable, to describe") and reflexively (~sich qualifizieren~, "to become qualified"). The PIE interrogative stem *~kت·o-~ is one of the most productive roots in the family: it generated Latin ~quis~ ("who"), ~quod~ ("what"), ~quandإچ~ ("when"), English ~who~, ~what~, ~when~, ~where~, ~which~, ~how~, German ~wer~, ~was~, ~wann~, ~wo~, ~welch~, and ~wie~. Every question word in both languages traces back to this single interrogative particle.
en.raten:
From Middle High German ~rؤپten~, from Old High German ~rؤپtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rؤپdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ“danؤ…~ ("to decide, advise, interpret"), from PIE *~Hrehâ‚پdh-~ ("to think, arrange"). Cognate with Dutch ~raden~, English ~read~ (originally "to counsel, interpret"), Danish ~rأ¥de~.

The dual meanings "to advise" and "to guess" both descend from the original Germanic sense of deliberation and interpretation. English ~read~ underwent an even more dramatic semantic shift: from "to interpret, counsel" (as in Old English ~rا£dan~) to the modern sense of deciphering written text. The German noun ~Rat~ ("counsel, advice") and ~Rathaus~ ("town hall," literally "counsel house") preserve the older advisory meaning. The archaic English word ~rede~ ("counsel") is a direct cognate.
en.rauchen: From Middle High German ~rouchen~, ~rأ¶uchen~, from Old High German ~rouhhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raukijan~, derived from the noun *~rauki-~ ("smoke"), from Proto-Germanic *~raukiz~, from *~reukanؤ…~ ("to smoke"), possibly from PIE *~hâ‚پrewg-~ ("to belch, emit"). The noun survives as modern ~Rauch~ ("smoke"). Cognate with English ~reek~, Dutch ~rook~ ("smoke"), Swedish ~rأ¶k~, Danish ~rأ¸g~. In Old English, ~rؤ“ocan~ meant simply "to smoke" or "to steam," but its descendant ~reek~ has taken on a strongly negative connotation ("to stink"). The specific sense "to smoke tobacco" entered German in the late seventeenth century as a calque of French ~fumer~, itself a semantic extension from "to emit smoke" to "to inhale smoke." The related verb ~rأ¤uchern~ ("to smoke food, to fumigate") preserves the umlauted form that was once a variant of ~rauchen~ itself.
en.reagieren: Borrowed from Medieval Latin ~reagإچ~ ("to act back, react"), a compound of ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~agإچ~ ("to drive, act"), from Proto-Italic *~agإچ~, from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚eاµ-~ ("to drive"). Latin ~agإچ~ is one of the most productive roots in the Latin lexicon: it underlies ~agent~, ~agenda~, ~action~, ~exact~, and ~navigate~ (via ~navis~ + ~agere~, "to drive a ship"). The Medieval Latin ~reagإچ~ is itself a scholarly coinage of the early modern period, formed in chemistry and natural philosophy to describe the behavior of substances upon one another. German borrowed ~reagieren~ directly from this Neo-Latin scientific vocabulary in the 17thâ€“18th century, using the standard ~-ieren~ suffix that naturalizes Latin verbs into the German weak-verb system. Cognate with English ~react~, French ~rأ©agir~, Dutch ~reageren~, Spanish ~reaccionar~.
en.realisieren:
Borrowed from French ~rأ©aliser~, from Middle French ~real~ ("actual") + ~-iser~, from Old French ~reel~, from Latin ~reؤپlis~ ("of or relating to things"), from ~rؤ“s~ ("thing, event, deed, fact").

The French verb ~rأ©aliser~ entered German carrying the sense "to make real, to implement" â€” its core meaning. The sense "to become aware of" (~to realize~ in the English cognitive sense) is a semantic loan from English ~realize~ and is colloquially common but considered an anglicism, sometimes frowned upon by purists. So ~realisieren~ in formal German means "to bring something into existence," not merely to notice it â€” an important distinction for learners translating English sentences. The Latin ~rؤ“s~ is one of the most ancient and productive roots in the language: it also underlies ~real~, ~republic~ (~rؤ“s pإ«blica~, "public matter"), and ~reify~.
en.recherchieren: Borrowed from French ~rechercher~ ("to search again, investigate"), from Old French ~recerchier~, a compound of ~re-~ ("again") + ~cerchier~ ("to look for, search"), from Latin ~circؤپre~ ("to go around"), from ~circus~ ("circle"), from Greek ~kirkos~, from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to turn, bend"). The word thus carries the literal sense of "searching again" -- going back over ground already covered. English ~research~ comes from the same French source. Interestingly, ~recherchieren~ in German specifically means investigative research (as a journalist or detective would do), while academic research is typically ~forschen~. French ~recherchأ©~ ("sought after, exquisite") comes from the same root and has been borrowed directly into English as a term for something elaborately refined.
en.rechnen:
From Middle High German ~rechenen~, from Old High German ~rehhanإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rekanإچn~ ("to count, explain"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to make straight or right"). Cognate with Low German ~rekenen~, Dutch ~rekenen~, English ~reckon~, Danish ~regne~.

The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ is also the ancestor of Latin ~rectus~ ("straight, right") and its English descendants â€” ~correct~, ~direct~, ~erect~. German ~recht~ ("right, correct") is a sibling from the same root. The semantic link is not accidental: to reckon is to make things come out right, to straighten the accounts. English ~reckon~ (from Old English ~recenian~) shares this ancestry directly â€” it was first attested meaning "to explain, set in order" before narrowing to calculation and then to the colloquial sense "to suppose, figure." The connection between rightness and reckoning runs deep: justice was long conceived as a kind of arithmetic, and ~Gericht~ ("court of law") shares the same ~recht~ root. Cognate with Dutch ~rekenen~, English ~reckon~, Latin ~rectus~.
en.rechtfertigen:
From Middle High German ~rأ«htvertegen~/~rأ«htvertigen~, derived from MHG ~rأ«htvertec~/~rأ«htvertic~ ("just, proper, righteous"). The compound consists of ~recht~ + ~fertigen~:

- ~recht~: From MHG ~rأ«ht~, from OHG ~rأ«ht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~reht~ ("right, straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rehtaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to straighten, direct"). Cognate with English ~right~, Dutch ~recht~, Latin ~rؤ“ctus~.
- ~fertigen~: From MHG ~vertigen~/~vertegen~ ("to prepare, make ready"), from the adjective ~fertig~, from OHG ~fartؤ«g~, derived from ~fart~ ("journey"). The original sense was "to make ready for a journey."

The compound literally meant "to make right" or "to prepare righteousness," evolving into the modern legal and moral sense of "to justify, to defend one's actions." The derived noun ~Rechtfertigung~ is the standard German equivalent of English ~justification~. In theology, ~Rechtfertigung~ is the key term for the Protestant doctrine of justification by faith (~sola fide~), central to Luther's Reformation. English ~justify~ comes from Latin ~iustificؤپre~, a calque of the same concept: ~iustus~ ("just") + ~facere~ ("to make").
en.reden: From Middle High German ~reden~, from Old High German ~redإچn~, ~rediإچn~ ("to speak"), a denominal verb from ~reda~, ~redia~ ("speech, word, opinion, view, mind"), from Proto-Germanic *~raأ¾jا­~ ("accountability, speech"). Cognate with Latin ~ratiإچ~ ("account, reason, proof") â€” a striking parallel: the Latin noun that gives us English ~ratio~, ~rational~, and ~reason~ is a cognate of the German word for everyday talk. The shared PIE root carried the sense of organized, accountable speech â€” speaking with clarity and purpose. English ~rede~ ("counsel, advice") is also a cognate, surviving in archaic or dialectal use, and the same root underlies German ~Rat~ ("counsel, advice"). To speak (~reden~), in this etymology, is to render an account.
en.reduzieren: Borrowed from Latin ~redإ«cإچ~ ("to lead back, bring back, restore, reduce"), a compound of ~re-~ ("back") + ~dإ«cإچ~ ("to lead, draw"), from Proto-Italic *~doukإچ~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to draw, pull") â€” the same root underlying ~produzieren~ and, via the Germanic branch, English ~tow~. The original Latin sense of ~redإ«cإچ~ was entirely spatial and military: "to lead troops back, to withdraw." The mathematical sense â€” reducing a fraction, reducing an equation â€” is a metaphorical extension of the idea of "bringing back" to a simpler or more fundamental state. German borrowed ~reduzieren~ through French ~rأ©duire~ or directly from the Latin learned vocabulary, applying the standard ~-ieren~ suffix. Cognate with English ~reduce~, French ~rأ©duire~, Spanish ~reducir~, Italian ~ridurre~. The Latin ~dux~ ("leader," from the same ~dإ«cإچ~) underlies English ~duke~ â€” making ~reduzieren~ and ~Herzog~ surprising distant relatives by way of the PIE root *~dewk-~.
en.reflektieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~reflectere~ ("to bend back, to turn back"), composed of ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~flectere~ ("to bend"). Entered German in the early seventeenth century with the optical meaning "to reflect (light)," then developed the intellectual meaning "to reflect (upon), to contemplate" in the second half of that century, and by the mid-eighteenth century acquired the further sense "to aspire to, to have designs on" (~auf etwas reflektieren~).

~Reflektieren~ is a remarkable case of semantic layering across centuries: the physical meaning (a mirror ~reflektiert~ light), the intellectual meaning (~أ¼ber etwas reflektieren~, "to reflect on something"), and the aspirational meaning (~auf eine Stelle reflektieren~, "to have one's eye on a position") coexist in modern German. The Latin root ~flectere~ ("to bend") also produced English ~flexible~, ~deflect~, ~inflect~, and ~genuflect~. The aspirational third sense is uniquely German -- neither English ~reflect~ nor French ~reflأ©chir~ developed this usage, making it a false friend in that particular register.
en.regeln: ~Regeln~ is a denominal verb formed directly from the noun ~Regel~ ("rule, regulation") with the infinitive suffix ~-n~. ~Regel~ entered German in Old High German as ~regula~, borrowed from Latin ~rؤ“gula~ ("straight stick, ruler, rule"), a diminutive of Latin ~regere~ ("to rule, direct, keep straight"), from Proto-Italic *~regإچ~ and Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to straighten, make right"). That PIE root is extraordinarily productive: it yielded Latin ~rؤ“x~ ("king"), Sanskrit ~rؤپjati~ ("to rule"), and â€” via French â€” English ~rule~, ~royal~, ~rector~, and ~correct~. The cognate English ~rail~ (as in a fence rail) also descends from ~rؤ“gula~ via Old French. So when one ~regelt~ a Situation, one is etymologically "putting it on a straight stick."
en.regen:
From MHG ~regen~ ("to make protrude, to erect, to excite, to move"), from Proto-Germanic *~hragjanؤ…~ ("to make stand, wake up, incite"). By synchronic analysis, the causative of MHG ~ragen~ ("to project, to tower").

~Regen~ (the verb, not to be confused with ~Regen~ the noun meaning "rain," which has a completely different etymology) means "to stir, to move, to rouse." It is most commonly encountered in the reflexive ~sich regen~ ("to stir, to move, to show signs of life"): ~Kein Blatt regt sich~ ("Not a leaf stirs"), ~der Widerstand regt sich~ ("resistance is stirring"). The proverb ~Sich regen bringt Segen~ ("To stir brings blessing," i.e., hard work pays off) is one of Germany's most beloved rhyming proverbs. The derived adjective ~rege~ ("lively, active, brisk") preserves the positive-energy sense. The compound ~an-regen~ ("to stimulate, to inspire") and its noun ~Anregung~ ("stimulus, suggestion") are among the verb's most productive descendants.
en.registrieren:
Borrowed from Medieval Latin ~registrؤپre~ ("to record, enter in a register"), from ~registrum~ ("register, list"), a singular form of Late Latin ~regesta~ ("items recorded"), neuter plural of ~regestus~, past participle of Latin ~regerere~ ("to carry back, to record"), from ~re-~ ("back") + ~gerere~ ("to carry, bear"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of French/Latin ~-er~ / ~-ؤپre~ verb endings, used productively for learned and technical borrowings since the Middle High German period.

The word entered German during the late medieval expansion of administrative and legal Latin. Cognate in formation with French ~registrer~, Italian ~registrare~, English ~register~. Some scholars note that the meaning of ~registrum~ was influenced by association with unrelated Latin ~regere~ ("to rule"), giving it an added connotation of official authority. The verb remains firmly technical and administrative in register: one ~registriert~ births, trademarks, and domain names. The reflexive ~sich registrieren~ ("to register oneself") has gained new life in the digital age as the standard verb for creating online accounts.
en.regnen: From Middle High German ~regenen~, from Old High German ~reganإچn~, a denominative verb derived from the noun ~regan~ ("rain"), from Proto-West Germanic *~regn~, from Proto-Germanic *~regnؤ…~. The PIE origin is uncertain but possibly *~Hreاµت°-~ ("to flow water"), which may also be the source of Latin ~rigإچ~ ("I irrigate, moisten") and Albanian ~rrjedh~ ("to flow"). The verb is impersonal in German (~es regnet~, "it rains"), a construction inherited from Proto-Germanic, where weather verbs lacked a real subject. Cognate with English ~rain~, Dutch ~regenen~, Danish ~regne~, Swedish ~regna~. The English expression "it's raining cats and dogs" has no German equivalent; instead, German says ~es gieأںt wie aus Eimern~ ("it pours as if from buckets").
en.reichen:
From Middle High German ~reichen~, from Old High German ~reihhen~, ~reichen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijanؤ…~, from PIE *~Hroyاµ-أ©ye-ti~, a causative o-grade of *~Hreyاµ-~ ("to stretch out, reach"). Cognate with Lithuanian ~rأ،iإ¾ytis~ ("to keep stretching"), Old Irish ~rigid~ ("to stretch").

The PIE causative formation â€” "to cause to stretch" â€” evolved into the broad semantic range of Modern German ~reichen~: "to reach, extend," "to hand (something) to someone," "to suffice, be enough." The sense of "sufficiency" (as in ~das reicht~, "that's enough") developed from the physical image of something extending far enough to arrive at its goal. English ~reach~ (from the same root) covers the first two senses but not the third; no single English word captures all three meanings. The ~er-~ prefixed form ~erreichen~ ("to achieve, attain") strips away the sense of sufficiency and focuses on completion â€” the goal is not just approached but fully reached. Cognate with Dutch ~reiken~, English ~reach~, Danish ~rأ¦kke~.
en.reinigen: From Middle High German ~reinegen~, ~reinigen~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective ~rein~ ("pure, clean"). ~Rein~ descends from MHG ~reine~, from OHG ~reini~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrainؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrainiz~, from PIE *~krey-~ ("to divide, to sift, to separate"). The semantic path from "sifting" to "clean" is intuitive: purity is what remains after impurities have been sifted away. The same PIE root *~krey-~ produced Greek ~krؤ«جپnإچ~ ("to separate, to decide, to judge") â€” whence English ~crisis~, ~critic~, and ~criterion~ â€” and Old Irish ~criathar~ ("sieve"). English ~riddle~ (the sieve, not the puzzle) is also a cognate. The Germanic *~hr-~ cluster (from PIE *~kr-~) lost its initial ~h~ in High German but survives in Old Norse ~hreinn~ ("clean, pure"), which via Norse settlers entered Old French as ~raأ¯ncer~ ("to cleanse"), giving English ~rinse~. So ~reinigen~ and ~rinse~ are distant cousins, reunited through a chain of borrowings. Cognate with Dutch ~reinigen~, Swedish ~ren~ ("clean"), Icelandic ~hreinn~.
en.reisen: From Middle High German ~reisen~ ("to set out on a campaign, to travel"), from Old High German ~reisإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raisإچn~, derived from a form akin to Proto-Germanic *~raizijanؤ…~ ("to cause to rise"). The Proto-Germanic root *~rؤ«sanؤ…~ ("to rise") is the direct ancestor of both Old English ~rؤ«san~ and thus English ~rise~. German ~reisen~ is a cognate of English ~rise~, with the German verb retaining an older meaning "to fall" alongside the travel sense â€” both descending from a root about directional movement, setting out, departing. The semantic shift from "rising up, setting out" to "traveling" happened through the military context: a ~Reise~ was originally a campaign, a sortie, an armed departure. Only later did the word shed its martial sense and become the everyday word for a journey.
en.reiten: From Middle High German ~rؤ«ten~, from Old High German ~rؤ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rؤ«dan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ«danؤ…~, from PIE *~Hreydت°-~ ("to set in motion, ride"), likely a suffixed form of *~hâ‚ƒreyH-~ ("to move, churn"). Cognate with English ~ride~, Dutch ~rijden~, Danish ~ride~, Swedish ~rida~, Old Irish ~rأ©idid~ ("to ride, drive"). German ~reiten~ has narrowed to mean riding on horseback (or on an animal), whereas English ~ride~ has broadened to include vehicles. For traveling by car or bus, German uses ~fahren~. In Dutch, ~rijden~ has taken the opposite path from German, primarily meaning "to drive (a vehicle)" rather than horseback riding. The past participle ~geritten~ preserves the old strong conjugation pattern with vowel change (ei to i), a relic of the Indo-European ablaut system.
en.reiأںen:
From Middle High German ~rؤ«ب¥en~, from Old High German ~rؤ«ب¥an~ ("to scratch"), from a conflation of two similar Proto-West Germanic verbs:

- *~wrؤ«tan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wrؤ«tanؤ…~, from PIE *~wer-~ ("to cut, scratch, tear, sketch an outline")
- *~hrؤ«tan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrؤ«tanؤ…~

Cognate with Dutch ~rijten~ ("to rip, tear"), Low German ~rieten~ ("to rip, tear"), Luxembourgish ~rأ¤issen~ ("to scratch, tear").

The merger of two distinct verbs into one is a rare etymological event, and it left ~reiأںen~ with an unusually broad semantic field: "to tear, rip, pull, snatch, sketch." The sketching sense (as in ~Grundriss~ = "floor plan," literally "ground-sketch") preserves the older "scratch, inscribe" meaning from *~wrؤ«tanؤ…~. This same root gave English ~write~ â€” which originally meant "to scratch, carve" (runes into wood or stone) before shifting to its modern meaning. Thus German ~reiأںen~ ("to tear") and English ~write~ are distant cousins, both descending from the act of scratching marks into a surface. The connection is also visible in Swedish ~rita~ ("to draw, sketch") and Icelandic ~rأ­ta~ ("to scratch, write"). Compare also Dutch ~wrijten~ ("to argue, quarrel"), where the "scratching" metaphor extends to verbal conflict.
en.rennen:
From a merger of two Old High German verbs: transitive/causative ~rennan~ ("to make something run or flow"), from Proto-Germanic *~rannijanؤ…~, the causative of *~rinnanؤ…~ ("to run, to flow"); and intransitive ~rinnan~ ("to run, to flow"), from Proto-Germanic *~rinnanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreyH-~ ("to undulate, churn"). A similar merger of transitive and intransitive roots occurred in Dutch ~rennen~ and English ~run~.

The PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreyH-~ connects running with flowing water â€” a vivid equation of rapid human motion with the surge of a stream. This root also produced Middle Irish ~rian~ ("river, way"), Proto-Slavic *~rؤ›ka~ ("river," whence Russian ~rekأ،~), Latin ~rؤ«vus~ ("stream," whence English ~river~ and ~rival~ â€” originally "one who shares the same stream"), and, most strikingly, the Celtic-Gaulish ~Rؤ“nos~ ("that which flows"), which is the source of the name of the river ~Rhine~. The semantic collapse of two distinct Proto-Germanic verbs â€” causative *~rannijanؤ…~ and intransitive *~rinnanؤ…~ â€” into a single modern verb is mirrored in English ~run~, where the transitive ("to run a business") and intransitive ("to run fast") coexist in one lemma. The German noun ~Rinne~ ("gutter, channel") preserves the original water-flow sense.
en.reparieren:
Borrowed in the 16th century from Latin ~reparؤپre~ ("to restore, renew, repair"), from ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~parؤپre~ ("to prepare, provide, arrange"), from PIE *~perhâ‚ƒ-~ ("to grant, provide"). Compare English ~repair~ (via Old French ~reparer~, from the same Latin source).

The Latin root ~parؤپre~ ("to prepare") is spectacularly productive in European languages. From it descend English ~prepare~, ~compare~, ~separate~, ~apparatus~, and ~pare~ â€” each prefixed form taking the "arrange, make ready" sense in a different direction. Unlike the native German word ~flicken~ ("to patch, mend"), which evokes homespun repair, ~reparieren~ carries a more professional, technical connotation â€” one ~flickt~ a torn sock but ~repariert~ a broken machine.
en.reprأ¤sentieren: Borrowed from Latin ~repraesentؤپre~ ("to bring before, make present, represent"), a compound of ~re-~ ("again, back") + ~praesentؤپre~ ("to present, show"), from ~praesؤ“ns~ ("present, at hand"), from ~prae-~ ("before") + the present participle of ~esse~ ("to be"). The Latin verb originally meant "to make something present again" -- to bring an absent thing vividly before the mind. German ~reprأ¤sentieren~ entered the language through learned Latin usage, with the ~أ¤~ reflecting German phonological adaptation. It carries both the political sense ("to represent a constituency") and the social sense ("to make an impressive appearance"), the latter being particularly prominent in German. The suffix ~-ieren~ marks it as a learned Latinate borrowing, as with ~positionieren~ and ~reservieren~.
en.reservieren: Borrowed from French ~rأ©server~, from Latin ~reservؤپre~ ("to keep back, save up"), a compound of ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~servؤپre~ ("to preserve, keep, guard"). Latin ~servؤپre~ derives from a denominative to Proto-Italic *~serwؤپ~ or *~serwom~ ("observation, heedance"), ultimately from PIE *~ser-~ ("to guard, watch over"). The same Latin root gives English ~reserve~, ~preserve~, ~conserve~, and ~observe~ (literally "to watch toward"). In German, ~reservieren~ is used primarily for booking tables, seats, and hotel rooms, where English would say "to reserve." The related noun ~Reservierung~ ("reservation") and ~Reserve~ ("reserve, backup") are also thoroughly naturalized in German. Cognate with French ~rأ©server~, Italian ~riservare~, Spanish ~reservar~.
en.retten: From Middle High German ~retten~, from Old High German ~hretten~ or ~retten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hraddjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hradjanؤ…~ ("to save, rescue"). Cognate with Dutch ~redden~, English ~redd~ (dialectal for "to tidy, clear"), and the partially related English ~rid~ â€” the latter borrowed partly from an Old English form of the same root, ~hreddan~ ("to deliver, rescue"). The English connection is a surprise: when speakers say "to rid oneself of something," they are reaching for a word that shares ancient ancestry with German ~retten~ ("to rescue, save").
en.richten:
From Middle High German ~richten~, from Old High German ~rihten~ ("to straighten, set right, direct"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to make straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijanؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective *~rehtaz~ ("straight, right"), from PIE *~hâ‚ƒreاµtأ³s~ ("right, straightened"), from *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ ("to stretch out, straighten, direct"). The same PIE root *~hâ‚ƒreاµ-~ underlies Latin ~regإچ~ ("I rule, direct"), Sanskrit ~rأ،jati~ ("rules"), and ultimately English ~right~, ~reign~, and ~regiment~. A causative denominative: "to make (something) straight/right."

The modern semantic range of ~richten~ extends in several directions from the original sense of straightening: "to direct" (~den Blick richten~, "to direct one's gaze"), "to judge" (literally "to set right according to law"), "to execute" (~hinrichten~, still in use), and "to set up" (~Richtfest~, the topping-out ceremony when roof beams are set straight). The judicial sense is ancient: Gothic ~garaihtjan~ already carried it. Cognate with Dutch ~richten~, English ~right~, Old English ~rihtian~, Gothic ~raihtjan~.
en.riechen:
From MHG ~riechen~, from OHG ~riohhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~reukan~, from Proto-Germanic *~reukanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚پrewg-~ ("to belch, to roar"). Cognate with English ~reek~, Dutch ~rieken~, Danish ~ryge~ ("to smoke"), Swedish ~ryka~ ("to steam, smoke"). Related to German ~Rauch~ ("smoke") and ~Geruch~ ("smell, odor").

The semantic journey from PIE "to belch" to German "to smell" is a wonderfully circuitous one. The original sense involved emitting something â€” vapor, smoke, belching gas. Proto-Germanic *~reukanؤ…~ shifted toward the emission of smoke or fumes, and the meaning then pivoted from the emitter to the perceiver: from "to give off fumes" to "to detect fumes" to "to smell" in general. English ~reek~ preserves an intermediate stage: it means "to stink" â€” still focused on the emission, but specifically an unpleasant one. German ~riechen~ generalized to cover all smelling, pleasant or foul. The noun ~Geruch~ ("smell, odor") and the related ~Rauch~ ("smoke") both spring from the same root, preserving the ancient link between smoke and sensation. Greek ~erygganإچ~ ("I belch") is a cousin from the same PIE root, confirming the original oral-emission sense.
en.rollen:
From Middle High German ~rollen~, borrowed from Old French ~roeler~, from Medieval Latin ~rotulؤپre~ ("to roll"), from Latin ~rotula~ ("small wheel"), diminutive of ~rota~ ("wheel"). The Latin ~rota~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~ret-~ ("to run, to roll"). Cognate with Dutch ~rollen~, English ~roll~ (also borrowed from Old French). The related noun ~Rolle~ entered German in the fifteenth century, likewise via Old French ~rolle~ and probably through Middle Dutch ~rolle~.

The semantic extension of ~Rolle~ from "scroll, cylinder" to "theatrical role" was a later semantic loan from French ~role~, originally referring to the rolled-up script an actor read from. English ~role~ followed the same French path.
en.rufen:
From Middle High German ~ruofen~, from Old High German ~ruofan~/~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrإچpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrإچpanؤ…~. A class VII strong verb. The origin of *~hrإچpanؤ…~ is uncertain. One theory connects it to *~hrإچأ¾iz~ ("glory, fame," the first element of names like ~Hrodulf~/~Rudolf~), though the phonetic relationship is problematic. Another theory proposes a connection to the raven's call â€” comparing Ancient Greek ~kأ³rax~ ("raven"), Latin ~corvus~ ("raven"), and Lithuanian ~إ،أ،rka~ ("magpie") â€” treating the root as an imitation of a carrying cry that was generalized to all calling out. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~.

The historical forms show an initial ~hr-~ cluster â€” Old High German ~hruofan~ â€” that was reduced to ~r-~ as Germanic languages systematically shed their initial ~h~ before consonants. The same process affected ~hring~ â†’ ~Ring~ and ~hrإچأ¾~ â†’ ~Ruhm~. A parallel weak form of ~rufen~ also existed in Middle High German and persisted into the 19th century; its legacy can be seen in the modern present tense, which lacks the umlaut that would be expected from a purely strong paradigm (~du rufst~, ~er ruft~ rather than the historically expected *~du rأ¼fst~, *~er rأ¼ft~).
en.ruhen:
From MHG ~ruo(we)n~, from OHG ~ruowؤ“n~ ("to rest"), derived from the noun ~ruowa~ ("rest, peace, quiet"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rإچu~ ("calm, rest"), from Proto-Germanic *~rإچwإچ~, from PIE *~hâ‚پrehâ‚پ-~ ("to rest"). Cognate with ~Ruhe~ ("rest, peace, quiet"), Dutch ~rusten~ (from a different formation on the same base), Gothic ~rimis~ ("rest").

~Ruhen~ means "to rest, to lie still, to be at peace" â€” both physically and metaphorically. One can say ~die Arbeit ruht~ ("work has ceased"), ~hier ruht...~ ("here lies..." on a gravestone), or ~auf sich ruhen~ ("to be at ease with oneself"). The PIE root *~hâ‚پrehâ‚پ-~ had a remarkably narrow semantic range: it meant simply "to be still, to rest," and this sense has remained stable across millennia and language branches. The phrase ~Ruhe in Frieden~ ("rest in peace") is the German equivalent of Latin ~requiؤ“scat in pؤپce~, though the German and Latin terms derive from entirely different PIE roots â€” a case of cultural convergence in funerary language.
en.runden:
From ~rund~ + ~-en~. ~Rund~ descends from MHG ~runt~, borrowed from Old French ~ront~, from Latin ~rotundus~ ("round"), from ~rota~ ("wheel, disk"), from PIE *~Hrأ³thâ‚‚o~ (from *~ret-~ "to run, to roll").

~Runden~ means "to round (off), to make round" and is especially common in mathematical and commercial contexts: ~auf ganze Zahlen runden~ ("to round to whole numbers"), ~abrunden~ and ~aufrunden~ ("to round down" and "to round up"). The etymological journey is striking: a PIE root meaning "to roll" produced Latin ~rota~ ("wheel"), which produced ~rotundus~ ("round"), which was borrowed into Old French as ~ront~, which was borrowed into Middle High German as ~runt~ -- making this one of the few everyday German adjectives that arrived via Latin and French rather than directly from Proto-Germanic. English ~round~ took the same French detour.
en.rutschen: From Middle High German ~rutzen~, ~rأ¼tzen~, an onomatopoeic formation imitating the sound of sliding, with a later shift from ~-tz-~ to ~-tsch-~ (as also seen in ~fletschen~). Perhaps related to Old High German ~rإ«tezzen~. English ~rutch~ and ~rutsch~ are borrowed from German or its Low German cognates.
