The noun ~Rutsche~ ("slide, chute") derives from the verb, and the festive New Year's greeting ~Guten Rutsch!~ ("Happy New Year!," literally "good slide") is a beloved German idiom, though some etymologists have proposed a folk-etymological connection to Yiddish ~Rosh~ (as in ~Rosh Hashanah~, "head of the year").
en.rأ¤umen:
From Early New High German ~reumen~, a Central German umlaut variant of the regular form ~raumen~, from MHG ~rإ«men~, from OHG ~rإ«men~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rإ«mijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rإ«mijanؤ…~, a factitive verb derived from the adjective *~rإ«maz~ ("roomy, spacious"), from PIE *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open"). Cognate with Bavarian ~rama~. The form ~raumen~ (without umlaut) was standard in Upper German regions until the 17th century and survives in dialects.

The verb means "to clear, to vacate, to tidy up" â€” etymologically, "to make room." The adjective ~gerأ¤umig~ ("spacious") preserves the original adjectival sense. The noun ~Raum~ ("room, space") is a sibling from the same Proto-Germanic root. English ~room~ is a cognate (from Old English ~rإ«m~, from the same *~rإ«maz~), as is the archaic ~roomy~. The PIE root *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open") â€” also the source of Latin ~rإ«s~ ("country," i.e., open land) â€” reveals the original image: spaciousness as something torn or cleared open. The related ~aufrأ¤umen~ ("to tidy up, to clear away") and ~einrأ¤umen~ ("to put away, to concede") extend the root with different prefixes.
en.rأ¼cken: From Middle High German ~rأ¼cken~, from Old High German ~rucchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rukkijan~. Cognate with Old Norse ~rykkja~, Old English ~roccian~, and modern English ~rock~ (as in "to rock back and forth"). The verb means "to move, to shift, to push" â€” often with the sense of a short, deliberate motion: ~einen Stuhl rأ¼cken~ ("to move a chair"), ~nأ¤her rأ¼cken~ ("to move closer"), ~in den Mittelpunkt rأ¼cken~ ("to move into the spotlight"). The noun ~Rأ¼cken~ ("back"), from MHG ~rأ¼cke~, from OHG ~rucki~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrugi~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrugjaz~, is a separate word etymologically â€” cognate with English ~ridge~ â€” though folk etymology naturally connects the two (one moves by pushing with one's back). The verb ~rأ¼cken~ is also the base of ~verrأ¼ckt~ ("crazy," literally "shifted, displaced") â€” perhaps the most entertaining derivative, implying that a ~verrأ¼ckter~ person has been mentally "moved out of place."
en.rأ¼hren: From Middle High German ~rأ¼eren~, from Old High German ~ruoren~, ~hruoren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrإچzijanؤ…~. The initial ~hr-~ cluster, common in older Germanic, was gradually simplified in German (compare English loss of initial ~wr-~ in ~write~). Cognate with Dutch ~roeren~ and the now-rare English ~rear~ ("to move, stir"), which survives mainly in the phrase "to rear up." The semantic range has stayed remarkably stable across a millennium: ~rأ¼hren~ still means "to stir" both literally (a pot) and figuratively (emotions). The compound ~anrأ¼hren~ preserves an older nuance of "to touch," while ~berأ¼hren~ ("to touch, to affect") shows the emotional dimension at full strength.
en.sagen: From Middle High German ~sagen~, from Old High German ~sagؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~saggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sagjanؤ…~, ultimately from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to say, tell"). Cognate with Low German ~seggen~, Dutch ~zeggen~, English ~say~, Danish ~sige~, Swedish ~sأ¤ga~.
en.sammeln:
From Middle High German ~samelen~, ~samlen~, regarded as formed by dissimilation from ~samenen~, which ultimately derives from Proto-Germanic *~samnإچnؤ…~ ("to bring together, gather, collect, assemble"). Alternatively, inherited from Old High German *~samalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~samalإچn~. The Proto-Germanic root *~samnإچnؤ…~ is derived from *~samanai~ ("together") combined with the verbal suffix *~-إچnؤ…~, and *~samanai~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~sem-~ ("one, same, together").

The PIE root *~sem-~ is one of the most productive in Indo-European: it underlies Latin ~semel~ ("once"), ~similis~ ("similar"), ~simul~ ("at the same time"), and English ~same~, ~seem~, ~simple~, and ~some~. The idea of oneness and togetherness are the same idea â€” to make something one is to bring its parts together. German ~zusammen~ ("together") preserves *~sem-~ in its ~sam-~ portion. The same root appears in Greek ~homos~ ("same"), whence ~homogeneous~, ~homologous~, and the prefix ~homo-~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzamelen~, Swedish ~samla~, Danish ~samle~, Gothic ~samjan~.
en.schaden:
From Middle High German ~schaden~, from Old High German ~scadإچn~, denominal from the noun ~Schaden~ ("damage, harm"). The noun derives from OHG ~scado~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaأ¾إچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaأ¾أ´~ ("injury, harm"), whence also the verb *~skaأ¾إچnؤ…~ ("to injure, harm"). Cognate with English ~scathe~ (via Old Norse), Dutch ~schade~, Swedish ~skada~, Danish ~skade~.

The English cognate ~scathe~ survives mainly in the negative adjective ~unscathed~ ("without harm") and the derived ~scathing~ ("harshly critical"). German ~schaden~ takes the dative case -- ~das schadet ihm~ ("that harms him") -- a pattern typical of verbs expressing disadvantage or harm. The idiomatic expression ~Es kann nicht schaden~ ("It can't hurt") is the German equivalent of the English shrug-and-try-it phrase.
en.schaffen:
Two historically distinct verbs have merged under a single infinitive in Modern German, distinguished only by their conjugation:

The strong verb (~schuf~, ~geschaffen~, "to create") descends from Middle High German ~schaffen~, from Old High German ~scaffan~, a byform of ~skephen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skappjan~. The form with ~-f-~ was chiefly back-formed from the past participle ~giscaffan~ but was also influenced by the weak verb below. The older form ~skephen~ survives in the archaic variant ~schأ¶pfen~ ("to create"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheppen~, English ~shape~.

The weak verb (~schaffte~, ~geschafft~, "to manage, accomplish") descends from Middle High German ~schaffen~, from Old High German ~skaffإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skapإچn~, from the same root as the strong verb. The semantic distinction between the two conjugations â€” strong for divine or artistic creation, weak for practical accomplishment â€” only established itself clearly in Modern German.
en.schalten:
From Middle High German ~schalten~, from Old High German ~scaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaldan~ ("to push, shove"). Further etymology is debated: according to Pokorny, from PIE *~(s)kelH-~ ("to split, separate"), which would make it related to English ~shell~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~skaldan~ ("to push").

The semantic development from "to push, shove" to modern "to switch, shift (gears), to manage" is striking. The original physical sense of shoving a lever or bolt evolved into the technical meaning of operating a switch or gear mechanism. In modern German, ~schalten~ is the standard verb for switching electrical devices, shifting gears in a car (~Schaltgetriebe~ = "manual transmission"), and by extension, for managing or directing (~schalten und walten~ = "to do as one pleases," literally "to switch and govern"). The compound ~Schalter~ means both "switch" and "counter/window" (at a bank or post office) â€” two kinds of interfaces where something gets directed. The idiom ~schalten und walten~ was already proverbial in Middle High German, pairing two near-synonyms for emphasis.
en.schauen: From Middle High German ~schouwen~, from Old High German ~scouwإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~skawwإچnؤ…~ ("to look, see, watch"), from PIE *~(s)kewhâ‚پ-~ ("to heed, look, feel, take note of"). The same PIE root, via Old English ~scؤ“awian~, gives modern English ~show~ â€” a striking semantic reversal: the Germanic descendants of this root divide between the intransitive "to look" (German ~schauen~) and the transitive "to make visible" (English ~show~). The verb is characteristic of southern German, Austrian, and Swiss usage, where ~Schau!~ and ~schau mal!~ serve as colloquial equivalents of standard German ~guck mal!~ Cognate with Old English ~scؤ“awian~, English ~show~, Dutch ~schouwen~ ("to inspect").
en.scheiden: From Middle High German ~scheiden~, from Old High German ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiأ¾anؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~skeyt-~ ("to cut, separate"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheiden~, West Frisian ~skiede~, and English ~shed~ (as in "to shed" skin or tears, originally "to separate"). The PIE root produced a rich network of cognates across the Indo-European family: Irish ~scian~ ("knife"), Lithuanian ~skiesti~ ("to separate"), Old Church Slavonic ~cؤ›diti~ ("to filter, strain"), Greek ~skhizein~ ("to split," source of English ~schism~ and ~schizophrenia~), and Sanskrit ~chyati~ ("he cuts off"). The German noun ~Entscheidung~ ("decision") literally means "a cutting apart" of alternatives.
en.scheinen: From Middle High German ~schؤ«nen~, from Old High German ~skؤ«nan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skؤ«nan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skؤ«nanؤ…~, from PIE *~(s)ل¸±ehâ‚پy-~ ("to shine, shimmer"). A class I strong verb. The modern verb covers two related senses â€” "to shine" (physical light) and "to seem, appear" â€” a duality that reflects the ancient metaphor of appearances as light cast onto the observer. This ambiguity between shining and seeming is shared by Latin ~lucؤ“re~ ("to shine") and its derivative ~illإ«siإچ~. The German cognate ~Schein~ ("shine; appearance; certificate") preserves the full range, and the pejorative ~zum Schein~ ("for appearances only") shows the "seeming" sense at its most skeptical. Cognate with Dutch ~schijnen~, Low German ~schienen~, English ~shine~, Danish ~skinne~, and the Esperanto borrowing ~إ‌ajni~ ("to seem").
en.scheitern: A verbal formation from ~Scheiter~, the plural of ~Scheit~ ("log, billet of firewood"), from Middle High German ~schؤ«t~, from Old High German ~scؤ«t~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skؤ«d~ ("piece of wood"), from Proto-Germanic *~skؤ«dؤ…~, from PIE *~skeyd-~ ("to split, to separate"), an extension of *~skey-~ ("to cut"). In the 16th century, the compounds ~zuscheitern~ and ~zerscheitern~ appeared first, meaning "to break into pieces (logs)" â€” like a ship shattering against rocks into firewood-sized fragments. The shortened form ~scheitern~ likely arose by analogy with phrases like ~zu Scheitern gehen~ ("to go to pieces, to fall apart"). The semantic shift from "to splinter like a log" to "to fail" is vivid and dramatic: a project that ~scheitert~ doesn't merely not succeed â€” it breaks apart catastrophically, like a vessel dashed on the shoals. The root noun ~Scheit~ is cognate with Old Norse ~skأ­أ°~ ("plank, billet, ski") â€” yes, ~Ski~ is a doublet of ~Scheit~, both meaning "a piece of split wood." One piece you burn for warmth; the other you strap to your feet and slide downhill. Also cognate with English ~shide~ (archaic "a piece of split wood"), Greek ~دƒد‡خ¯خ¶خ±~ ("splinter"), and Lithuanian ~skأ¬edra~ ("chip"). The PIE root *~skey-~ also yields ~scheiden~ ("to separate, to divorce") and English ~shed~ â€” all preserving the ancient concept of cutting apart.
en.schenken:
From Middle High German ~schenken~, from Old High German ~scenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skankijan~ ("to pour, serve"). Related to ~Schenke~ ("tavern, inn") and ~Schenkel~ ("thigh, shank"). Cognate with Dutch ~schenken~ ("to pour; to give"), English ~skink~ (archaic, "to pour, to serve drink").

The semantic journey from "to pour" to "to give as a gift" is one of German's most vivid etymological stories. In medieval Germanic culture, the act of pouring a drink for a guest was itself the paradigmatic act of generosity â€” the host who poured was giving. Over time, the meaning broadened from pouring drink to giving any gift, while the original "pouring" sense retreated to fixed expressions like ~Wein einschenken~ ("to pour wine") and the noun ~Schenke~ ("tavern," originally a place where drinks were poured). Dutch ~schenken~ preserves both senses equally â€” "to pour" and "to give" â€” making the Dutch usage a living fossil of the semantic transition. English ~shank~ (the leg bone) is a cognate of ~Schenkel~ from the same root, the original connection being the tilting motion of pouring.
en.schicken: From Middle High German ~schicken~ ("to outfit oneself, fit in, arrange appropriately"), from Old High German *~skihhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skikkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skikkijanؤ…~ ("to order, send"), the causative of the verb underlying Middle High German ~geschehen~ ("to happen, rush"). The modern sense "to send" developed from the earlier "to dispatch, make ready to go" â€” to arrange something so that it departs. Related to English ~chic~ (via French, ultimately from the same Germanic source), which preserves the original sense of fitting arrangement. The family of words also includes ~geschickt~ ("skilled, adept") â€” skill being a matter of fitting arrangement â€” and ~Schicksal~ ("fate, destiny"), where fate is the dispatch one cannot refuse.
en.schieben: From Middle High German ~schieben~, from Old High German ~skioban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeuban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skeubanؤ…~, from PIE *~skewbت°-~ ("to push, to shove, to thrust"). The word is attested since the 9th century. As a class II strong verb, ~schieben~ displays the ablaut series ~ie~ > ~o~ > ~o~ (~schieben~, ~schob~, ~geschoben~). ~Schieben~ means "to push, to shove" â€” physically moving something by applying force from behind. But it also means "to slide" (as in sliding a drawer), "to traffic" or "to profiteer" in colloquial usage (~Schwarzmarktgeschأ¤fte schieben~, "to run black-market deals"), and in the expression ~die Schuld auf jemanden schieben~ ("to shift the blame onto someone"), where the pushing is metaphorical. The phrase ~auf die lange Bank schieben~ ("to put off, to procrastinate" â€” literally "to push onto the long bench") may originate from the medieval practice of storing unresolved court documents on a long bench, where they gathered dust. Cognate with English ~shove~ (which underwent the Great Vowel Shift while German kept the diphthong), Dutch ~schuiven~, Low German ~schuven~, Danish ~skubbe~. The PIE root *~skewbت°-~ may also be related to Lithuanian ~skubؤ—ti~ ("to hurry" â€” to push oneself along). The derived noun ~Schub~ ("push, thrust, batch") and ~Schublade~ ("drawer" â€” literally "push-load," something you push and pull) round out the family.
en.schieأںen: From Middle High German ~schiezen~, from Old High German ~sciozan~, ~sciozzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeotan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skeutanؤ…~ ("to shoot, to shove"), possibly from PIE *~(s)kewd-~ ("to throw, shoot, pursue"). A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schieأںen~, ~schoss~, ~geschossen~. The Proto-Germanic root yielded an impressive family of cognates: English ~shoot~, Dutch ~schieten~, Swedish ~skjuta~, Danish ~skyde~, Icelandic ~skjأ³ta~, and Old Norse ~skjأ³ta~. The related noun ~Schuss~ ("shot") entered English directly as a skiing term â€” a straight, fast downhill run, preserving the original sense of rapid forward motion. The zero-grade form Proto-Germanic *~skutiz~ ("a shooting, a shot") is the ancestor of ~Schuss~. The original meaning of ~schieأںen~ was broader than firearms: it meant any rapid, propulsive motion â€” shooting an arrow, of course, but also shooting a bolt (on a door), or a plant shooting up from the ground. This last sense survives in the compound ~aufschieأںen~ and in the idiom ~ins Kraut schieأںen~ ("to run to seed," literally "to shoot into the herb"), used figuratively when something proliferates out of control.
en.schildern: Borrowed in the 16th century from Middle Low German or Dutch ~schilderen~ ("to paint, to coat"). The Dutch verb derives from the noun ~schilder~ ("painter"), itself from ~schild~ ("shield"). In medieval times, shields were decorated with coats of arms, and the craftsman who painted these heraldic designs onto shields was called a ~schilder~. This artisan gradually expanded beyond heraldic work to paint other subjects, and the verb broadened accordingly. Dutch ~schild~ descends from Middle Dutch ~schilt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeldu~, from Proto-Germanic *~skelduz~ ("shield"), from PIE *~(s)keyt-~ ("shield, cover"). Cognate with English ~shield~ and Danish ~skjold~. The semantic shift from "to paint a shield" to "to paint" to modern German "to describe, to depict (in words)" is a beautiful case of metaphorical expansion: first painting concrete images on shields, then painting pictures generally, and finally "painting pictures with words." English ~depict~ follows a parallel trajectory from the physical to the verbal.
en.schlafen: ~schlafen~ comes from Middle High German ~slؤپfen~, from Old High German ~slؤپfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~slؤپpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slؤ“panؤ…~. Cognate with English ~sleep~, Dutch ~slapen~, Low German ~slapen~, and Gothic ~ًگچƒًگŒ»ًگŒ´ًگچ€ًگŒ°ًگŒ½~ (slؤ“pan) â€” all of identical meaning across the Germanic family. The Proto-Germanic origin is uncertain: it may be related to *~slapaz~ ("weak, lax, limp"), the ancestor of German ~schlaff~ ("slack, limp, flaccid"), connecting sleep to the physical limpness of the sleeping body. This possible German-internal cognate pair â€” ~schlafen~ / ~schlaff~ â€” captures something real: sleep is the state of going slack. An older theory derives the root from PIE *~slehâ‚پ-~, possibly related to ~languor~ and Old English ~slأ¦c~ ("lazy"), but the connection remains debated. The Class VII strong verb ~schlafen~, ~schlief~, ~geschlafen~ belongs to the reduplicating class, whose Prأ¤teritum (~schlief~) reflects an ancient pattern of vowel modification rather than a simple ablaut grade.
en.schlagen: From Middle High German ~slahen~, from Old High German ~slahan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~slahan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slahanؤ…~, from a Proto-Indo-European *~slak-~ ("to hit, strike, throw"), apparently of onomatopoeic origin. The modern ~-g-~ in the infinitive and present tense is a secondary insertion by analogy with the past-tense forms (~schlug~) and the related noun ~Schlag~ ("blow, strike, beat") â€” a phenomenon known as Grammatischer Wechsel (Verner's Law alternation later regularized by analogy). The same root produced an extraordinary range of modern German compounds: ~vorschlagen~ ("to suggest," literally "to strike forward"), ~nachschlagen~ ("to look up in a reference"), ~beschlagen~ ("to shoe a horse"), ~erschlagen~ ("to strike dead"), ~einschlagen~ ("to hammer in; to take a path"). English ~slay~ and ~slog~ both derive from the same Proto-Germanic root (Old English ~slؤ“an~, "to strike, beat, smite"). Cognate with Low German and Dutch ~slaan~, Old English ~slؤ“an~, Danish ~slأ¥~, Swedish ~slأ¥~, Norwegian ~slأ¥~.
en.schlieأںen: From Middle High German ~slieب¥en~, from Old High German ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slإ«tanؤ…~, *~sleutanؤ…~ ("to lock, shut"). The PIE root is not definitively established, but the Germanic root carried the sense of fastening or locking â€” the sliding of a bolt into place. A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schlieأںen~, ~schloss~, ~geschlossen~. The modern spelling with ~sch-~ reflects the High German consonant shift from earlier *~sl-~, while Dutch ~sluiten~ preserves the unshifted cluster. One peculiarity of English: the Wiktionary entry lists ~slot~ as a cognate of ~schlieأںen~. The relevant ~slot~ here is not the common English word (which comes from Old French ~esclot~ via Old Norse) but rather the archaic and dialectal English ~slot~ meaning "a bar, bolt, or fastening," directly from the same Proto-Germanic root. Cognate with Old Saxon ~slإ«tan~, Dutch ~sluiten~, archaic English ~slot~ (a bolt).
en.schmecken:
From Middle High German ~smecken~, from Old High German ~smecchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~smakkijan~ ("to taste"), from Proto-West Germanic *~smakku~ ("a taste"). Cognate with English ~smack~ (as in "it smacks of"), Dutch ~smaken~, Lithuanian ~smagأ¹~ ("cheerful, enjoyable, pleasant").

The English expression "it smacks of" â€” meaning "it has the flavor or character of" â€” is a direct semantic and etymological cognate of German ~schmecken~. Both descend from the same Proto-West Germanic root *~smakku~, and both retain the original sense of tasting or perceiving flavor. In regional German (especially southern dialects and Austrian German), ~schmecken~ means not just "to taste" but also "to smell" â€” a reminder that the Proto-Germanic root encompassed sensory perception more broadly than modern Standard German allows. The noun ~Geschmack~ ("taste, flavor") is a collective formation with the ~Ge-~ prefix, literally "the totality of what is tasted." The relationship between ~schmecken~ and English ~smack~ is one of the more surprising Germanic cognate pairs hiding in plain sight.
en.schneiden: ~schneiden~ comes from Middle High German ~snؤ«den~, from Old High German ~snؤ«dan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~snؤ«أ¾an~ ("to cut"). Cognate with Dutch ~snijden~ ("to cut, carve, intersect"), Low German ~snieden~, Swedish ~snida~ ("to carve, engrave"), Icelandic ~snأ­أ°a~ ("to trim, tailor"), and the archaic English ~snithe~ ("to cut") â€” a word related to the modern English adjective ~snide~, whose original sense was "cutting" in the literal sense before shifting to "cutting" in the figurative one. No secure PIE root has been established for the Proto-West Germanic ancestor; the word may be a Germanic innovation. The Class I strong verb pattern (~schneiden~, ~schnitt~, ~geschnitten~) shows the characteristic *ei / *i / *i ablaut, and the dental alternation between German ~-d-~ and English ~-th-~ (Proto-Germanic *أ¾) reflects a regular West Germanic sound change.
en.schonen: From Middle High German ~schإچnen~, derived from the adjective ~schأ¶n~ ("beautiful"). The adjective traces to Old High German ~scإچni~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaunؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~skauniz~, whose original meaning was likely "to look at, to behold" and hence "worth beholding." The semantic development from "beautiful" to "to spare, to take care of" reflects the idea that one treats beautiful things gently. Cognate with English ~sheen~ (originally "beautiful, bright"). The reflexive ~sich schonen~ ("to take it easy, to spare oneself") is common in medical contexts, while ~Schonzeit~ ("closed season") refers to periods when hunting is forbidden to let animal populations recover.
en.schreiben: From Middle High German ~schrؤ«ben~, from Old High German ~scrؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrؤ«banؤ…~, an early borrowing from Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~ ("I write"), from Proto-Italic *~skreiخ²إچ~, from PIE *~(s)kreybت°-~ ("to scratch, cut, incise"). The word entered Germanic through contact with the Roman world along the Rhine frontier; before this borrowing, Germanic peoples used *~wrؤ«tanؤ…~ ("to scratch, carve") for writing-related activities â€” the ancestor of English ~write~. Because this borrowing occurred during the Roman period (roughly 1stâ€“4th century CE) â€” well before the ~-ieren~ suffix became productive through Old French contact in the 12thâ€“13th century â€” ~schreiben~ was fully assimilated into the strong-verb system (class I: ~schreiben~, ~schrieb~, ~geschrieben~), following the same ablaut pattern as native verbs like ~treiben~. Later Latin and French borrowings received the weak ~-ieren~ treatment instead (~studieren~, ~konjugieren~). Cognate with Dutch ~schrijven~, Low German ~schrieven~.
en.schreien:
From Middle High German ~schrؤ«en~, from Old High German ~scrؤ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrؤ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrؤ«anؤ…~ ("to cry, scream"). A class I strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schreien~, ~schrie~, ~geschrien~. Cognate with Dutch ~schreien~ ("to cry, weep"), Frisian ~skrieme~ ("to weep"), and related to English ~scream~ (via Middle Dutch ~scremen~) and Old Norse ~skrأ¦ma~ ("to terrify"), whence Swedish ~skrأ¤mma~ ("to frighten") and Danish ~skrأ¦mme~ ("to scare").

The semantic spread across Germanic languages is revealing: German ~schreien~ means "to scream, to shout, to cry out" (a loud vocalization), while Dutch ~schreien~ means "to cry, to weep" (a quiet lamentation). English ~scream~ preserves the loud sense. The shift from "loud cry" to "weeping" in Dutch parallels what happened to English ~cry~ itself, which originally meant "to call out" (from Old French ~crier~, from Latin ~quiritؤپre~ "to wail") before acquiring its modern weeping sense. The expression ~zum Schreien~ ("hilarious," literally "enough to make you scream") captures the verb's emotional intensity, while ~Schreihals~ ("screamer, loudmouth," literally "scream-throat") is one of German's more vivid compound insults.
en.schulen: Derived from the noun ~Schule~ with the verbal suffix ~-en~. From Middle High German ~schuole~, from Old High German ~scuola~, borrowed from Latin ~schola~ ("leisure for learning, school"), itself from Ancient Greek ~scholؤ“~ (~دƒد‡خ؟خ»خ®~, "leisure, rest, holding back"), from PIE *~seاµت°-~ ("to hold"). The Greek word originally denoted the leisure required for intellectual pursuits, since only those free from labor could devote time to study. Latin ~schola~ entered Germanic languages during the Christianization period, when monastic schools became centers of education. The semantic irony is delicious: the root means "leisure," yet school has become synonymous with hard work for generations of students. English ~school~ is a cognate borrowed from the same Latin source, and even ~scholar~ traces back to Greek ~scholؤ“~. The German verb ~schulen~ means "to train, to school" and carries a more specialized, professional sense than simply "to teach" (~lehren~).
en.schweigen:
From MHG ~swؤ«gen~, from OHG ~swؤ«gؤ“n~ ("to be silent"), from Proto-West Germanic *~swؤ«gؤ“n~. The verb was originally weak but developed strong forms in early Middle High German (~sweic~, ~swigen~, ~geswigen~). Cognate with Old Saxon ~swؤ«gإچn~ (weak), Old English ~swؤ«gian~ ("to be silent").

The Old Norse cognates ~svأ­gja~ ("to bow, yield") and ~svigna~ ("to bend, give way") suggest an original semantic field around yielding -- from which "falling silent" may have developed as a metaphor for yielding in conversation. The transition from weak to strong conjugation in Middle High German is unusual; most German verbs have moved in the opposite direction over the centuries. Modern ~schweigen~ conjugates as a strong verb (~schwieg~, ~geschwiegen~) with no trace of its originally weak past. The noun ~Schweigen~ carries almost poetic weight: ~das Schweigen brechen~ ("to break the silence"), ~Schweigen ist Gold~ ("silence is golden," the second half of the proverb ~Reden ist Silber, Schweigen ist Gold~). In legal contexts, ~Schweigepflicht~ ("duty of confidentiality") binds doctors, lawyers, and priests.
en.schwimmen:
From Middle High German ~swimmen~, from Old High German ~swimman~ ("to swim"), from Proto-West Germanic *~swimman~, from Proto-Germanic *~swimmanؤ…~ ("to swim, float"), from PIE *~swem-~ ("to be in motion, to swim"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schwimmen~, ~schwamm~, ~geschwommen~. Cognate with English ~swim~, Dutch ~zwemmen~, Low German ~swimmen~, Danish ~svأ¸mme~, and Swedish ~simma~.

The PIE root *~swem-~ originally meant general motion in or through a fluid medium. The modern English cognate ~swim~ descends unchanged from Old English ~swimman~, making this one of the most transparent Germanic cognate pairs. The German-Dutch consonant correspondence ~schw-~/~zw-~ is perfectly regular: it appears in ~Schwester~/~zuster~ ("sister"), ~schwarz~/~zwart~ ("black"), ~Schwert~/~zwaard~ ("sword"), and ~Schwein~/~zwijn~ ("pig"). Dutch underwent a further sound shift from ~sw-~ to ~zw-~ that German did not. The expression ~ins Schwimmen kommen~ ("to start swimming," figuratively "to become uncertain, to flounder") captures the helplessness of being cast into deep water. The noun ~Schwamm~ ("sponge") â€” etymologically the thing that absorbs in water â€” derives from the same ablaut series.
en.schأ¤tzen: ~Schأ¤tzen~ ("to estimate, value; to appreciate") is a denominal verb from ~Schatz~ ("treasure, darling"). ~Schatz~ descends from Middle High German ~schaz~, Old High German ~scaz~, and Proto-West Germanic *~skatt~ ("cattle, treasure") â€” an early Germanic word in which cattle served as a measure of wealth. Cognates include Old Norse ~skattr~ (whence Danish ~skat~ and Swedish ~skatt~, both meaning "tax"), Gothic ~skatts~, and Old English ~sؤ‹eatt~. The Scandinavian descendants shifted decisively from "treasure" to "tax" â€” the compulsory yielding of wealth to authority â€” while German retained the positive sense of treasure and added the intellectual sense of estimating or valuing. A German speaker who ~schأ¤tzt~ something either places a rough value on it or genuinely appreciates it. The card game Skat, despite its resemblance to ~Schatz~, is actually borrowed from Italian ~scarto~ ("discard") â€” a false cognate that trips up many players.
en.schأ¼tteln: From Middle High German ~schأ¼telen~, ~schأ¼tteln~, from Old High German ~scutilإچn~, an iterative (frequentative) formation from ~schأ¼tten~ ("to pour, to shake"), indicating repeated or vigorous shaking. The base verb ~schأ¼tten~ derives from Old High German ~scuttؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~skudjanؤ…~, perhaps from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)kewt-~ ("to shake, jolt"). Cognate with Dutch ~schudden~ ("to shake"). The iterative ~-eln~ suffix marks ~schأ¼tteln~ as describing repeated back-and-forth motion, distinguishing it from the single decisive motion of ~schأ¼tten~. The compound ~Kopfschأ¼tteln~ ("head-shaking") is frequently used as a noun meaning bewilderment or disapproval.
en.schأ¼tzen: From late Middle High German ~schأ¼tzen~ ("to protect"), attested only from around 1300, and debated in origin. Wiktionary notes two competing derivations: either from the root of ~schieأںen~ ("to shoot"), with an original sense of "to advance a barrier (to shoot something forward as a bulwark)," from Proto-Germanic *~skeutanؤ…~, from PIE *~(s)kewd-~ ("to advance, propel"); or from ~schأ¼tten~ ("to pour, heap up"), with an original sense of "to pile up a protective wall," from Proto-Germanic *~skudjanؤ…~, from PIE *~(s)kewt-~ ("to shake, jolt"). Both derivations converge on the notion of physical construction or projection of a barrier. The late attestation of the simplex makes the etymology difficult to resolve. A striking false friend: English ~shoot~ and German ~schأ¼tzen~ share a possible common ancestor but have traveled in very different semantic directions â€” one projects a missile, the other stops one. Cognate with Dutch ~schutten~ ("to dam, pen in, lock [a canal]"), which preserves the barrier sense more literally.
en.sehen: From Middle High German ~sأ«hen~, from Old High German ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with Low German ~sehn~, Dutch ~zien~, English ~see~, Danish ~se~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
en.sein:
From Middle High German ~sؤ«n~, from Old High German ~sؤ«n~ ("to be"). Traces to Proto-Germanic *~wesanؤ…~ ("to be"), suppleted with *~beunؤ…~ ("to be, exist, become"). Cognate with Dutch ~zijn~, Low German ~sien~.

~Sein~ is the most radically suppletive verb in German. ~Suppletion~ (from Latin ~supplؤ“re~, "to fill up, to supplement") occurs when an inflectional paradigm draws its conjugations from etymologically unrelated roots â€” not merely irregular forms of a single root (as ablaut produces ~singen~, ~sang~, ~gesungen~ from one PIE ancestor), but genuinely different words that merged into one paradigm because the original paradigm had gaps that needed filling. Three distinct PIE roots contribute conjugations to ~sein~:

- *~hâ‚پes-~ ("to be, to exist"): Supplies the third-person present forms ~ist~ (from PGmc *~isti~) and ~sind~ (from PGmc *~sinti~), as well as the infinitive ~sein~ itself (via OHG ~sؤ«n~, from PGmc *~sؤ«nؤ…~, a contracted form).
- *~bت°uH-~ ("to grow, to become, to come into being"): Supplies the first- and second-person present forms ~bin~ and ~bist~ (from PGmc *~beunؤ…~). The original sense of becoming or coming into existence lingers in the ~b-~ forms: ~ich bin~ is, at its etymological root, "I am becoming."
- *~hâ‚‚wes-~ ("to dwell, to stay, to remain"): Supplies the entire past tense ~war~, ~warst~, ~waren~ and the past participle ~gewesen~ (from PGmc *~wesanؤ…~). To say ~ich war~ is, etymologically, to say "I dwelt" â€” existence conceived as inhabiting a place.

English ~to be~ preserves exactly the same three-way suppletion: ~is~ and ~am~ from *~hâ‚پes-~, ~be~, ~been~, and ~being~ from *~bت°uH-~, and ~was~ and ~were~ from *~hâ‚‚wes-~. German and English thus carry the same three-headed fossil in their most fundamental verb.

The same phenomenon extends into the Romance copulae, and the parallels are striking. Latin ~esse~ ("to be") was itself already suppletive: its present tense ~sum~, ~est~, ~sunt~ descended from *~hâ‚پes-~, while its perfect tense ~fuؤ«~, ~fuit~ and future participle ~futإ«rus~ came from *~bت°uH-~ â€” the same two roots that supply German ~ist~ and ~bin~. The Romance languages inherited this Latin suppletion and then deepened it by recruiting Latin ~stؤپre~ ("to stand") as a third contributor. Most Romance languages â€” Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Catalan, Galician â€” maintained ~stؤپre~ as a separate copula alongside the ~esse~-derived one, splitting "to be" into two verbs: Spanish ~ser~/~estar~, Italian ~essere~/~stare~, Portuguese ~ser~/~estar~, each distinguishing essential being from temporary state. French is the exception: it merged everything into a single verb ~أھtre~, and ~stؤپre~ survives only as a fossil in the past participle ~أ©tأ©~ â€” and in the otherwise-extinct legal verb ~ester~ (~ester en justice~, "to appear in legal proceedings"), a frozen relic of ~stؤپre~ preserved in the amber of juridical French. Within each Romance copula, the older two-way suppletion persists: Italian ~sono~/~أ¨~ (from *~hâ‚پes-~), ~fui~ (from *~bت°uH-~), ~stato~ (from ~stؤپre~); French ~suis~/~est~/~sont~ (from *~hâ‚پes-~), ~fus~/~fut~ (from *~bت°uH-~), ~أ©tأ©~ (from ~stؤپre~).

That both Germanic and Italic independently maintained the *~hâ‚پes-~ / *~bت°uH-~ suppletion â€” and that both language families later recruited a third root (~*hâ‚‚wes-~ in Germanic, ~stؤپre~ in Romance) to patch further gaps â€” suggests that copulae are uniquely prone to this kind of merger. The likeliest explanation is frequency: the copula is the most commonly used verb in virtually every Indo-European language, and high-frequency words resist regularization. Speakers memorize ~bin~/~ist~/~war~ as individual wholes rather than deriving them from rules, so multiple roots can coexist within one paradigm indefinitely, each reinforced by constant use. The copula is also semantically porous â€” the boundary between "to be," "to exist," "to dwell," "to become," and "to stand" is thin enough that neighboring verbs are perpetually absorbed into its paradigm, filling whatever slots need filling. The result is that the most basic verb in the language is, paradoxically, the least regular â€” a patchwork of three ancient words stitched together so long ago that only etymology can see the seams.
en.senden: ~Senden~ ("to send, broadcast") descends from Middle High German ~senden~, Old High German ~senten~, and Proto-West Germanic *~sandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sandijanؤ…~. The Proto-Indo-European root is *~sont-eye-~, a causative of *~sent-~ ("to walk, travel") â€” so the original sense was "to cause to go." Cognates include Dutch ~zenden~, Danish ~sende~, Swedish ~sأ¤nda~, Gothic ~sandjan~, and English ~send~. A notable feature of ~senden~ is its double past tense: ~sendete~ is used for broadcasting (radio, television), while ~sandte~ â€” a historically strong-influenced form â€” is used for physically or formally sending a letter or message. This split preserved an archaic form that might otherwise have been regularized. The verb is related to Old English ~sand~ ("a sending, mission, message"), visible in the old word ~errand~.
en.senken: From Old High German ~senken~, from Proto-Germanic *~sankwijanؤ…~, a causative of *~sinkwanؤ…~ ("to sink"), from PIE *~sengت·-~ ("to sink, to fall"). As a causative, ~senken~ originally meant "to cause to sink, to make something go down" â€” the active counterpart to the intransitive ~sinken~. This causative relationship (where a weak verb means "to cause the action of the corresponding strong verb") is one of the great structural features of Germanic: ~sinken~/~senken~ parallels ~trinken~/~trأ¤nken~ ("to drink"/"to water, to give drink"), ~springen~/~sprengen~ ("to jump"/"to blow up, to scatter"), and ~fallen~/~fأ¤llen~ ("to fall"/"to fell"). The vowel alternation (~i~ > ~e~) in these pairs reflects the Proto-Germanic causative *~-jan-~ suffix, which triggered umlaut (here rounding *~a~ to *~e~ after the high front vowel was lost). Modern ~senken~ means "to lower" â€” prices (~Preise senken~), one's gaze (~den Blick senken~), or a coffin into the ground (~einen Sarg ins Grab senken~). The derived noun ~Senke~ means "a depression in the terrain" â€” a place that has been made to sink. Cognate with Gothic ~sagqjan~ ("to cause to sink"), Old Norse ~sأ¸kkva~.
en.servieren: Borrowed from French ~servir~ with the addition of the German ~-ieren~ verb suffix. French ~servir~ descends from Old French ~servir~, from Latin ~servire~ ("to serve, to be a slave to"), from Latin ~servus~ ("slave, servant"). The ultimate origin of ~servus~ is debated: it may derive from Proto-Italic *~serwos~ ("guardian"), from PIE *~ser-~ ("to watch over, to protect"), which would make the original meaning something like "guardian" rather than "slave," a striking semantic reversal. The German borrowing is specifically tied to the sense of serving food and drink; broader senses of "to serve" are covered by the native ~dienen~. Cognate with English ~serve~ (via Old French from the same Latin source), ~service~, ~servant~, and also with ~serf~ (from the same Latin ~servus~). The word entered German during the period of heavy French cultural influence on German courtly and culinary vocabulary in the 17th and 18th centuries.
en.setzen: From Middle High German ~setzen~, from Old High German ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, to place down"), from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~. A causative derivation of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit") with the suffix *~-janؤ…~: literally "to cause to sit," hence "to set, place." The causative relationship between ~sitzen~ ("to sit," intransitive) and ~setzen~ ("to set," transitive) is a productive pattern preserved from Proto-Indo-European, paralleling English ~sit~/~set~. Cognate with Dutch ~zetten~, English ~set~, Yiddish ~zetsn~.
en.sichern:
A denominative verb derived from the adjective ~sicher~ ("safe, secure, certain"). Old High German ~sihhurإچn~ ("to justify, to vouch for, to guarantee"), attested from the 9th century, developed into Middle High German ~sichern~ ("to make safe, to give assurance").

~sicher~ itself entered Germanic as a loanword: from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~, borrowed from Latin ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ ("worryless, carefree, secure"). The Latin adjective is a compound of ~sؤ“-~ ("without, apart from") + ~cإ«ra~ ("care, concern") â€” literally "without care." Latin ~sؤ“-~ in turn derives from PIE *~sue-~ ("self, one's own"), the reflexive particle.

The word's journey from the Roman world into Germanic is a vivid example of cultural transmission along the Rhine frontier: Roman administrators and soldiers brought the legal and military vocabulary of ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ into contact with Germanic-speaking populations, and the word was adopted wholesale. Its sibling entered English via French: ~secure~ and ~sure~ both descend from the same Latin root, as does ~sinecure~ (from Latin ~sine cإ«rؤپ~, "without care" â€” a position requiring no work). The OHG sense of ~sihhurإچn~ â€” "to justify oneself, to give surety" â€” is notably more active and legally weighted than the modern sense of "to secure, to safeguard": it meant to perform the act of pledging or vouching, not merely to make safe. The modern passive-causative sense ("to secure a building, to back up a file") is the later development.
en.sicherstellen:
A compound of ~sicher~ + ~stellen~, meaning "to ensure, to secure, to confiscate":

- ~sicher~: From MHG ~siher~, from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~ ("secure, sure"), borrowed from Latin ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ ("worryless; carefree; secure"), from ~sؤ“-~ ("without") + ~cإ«ra~ ("care"). Cognate with English ~secure~ and ~sure~ (both from the same Latin source via different routes).
- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stallijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stallijanؤ…~, from PIE *~stelhâ‚‚-~ ("to put, to stand").

~Sicherstellen~ is not a standard prefix verb but an adjective-verb compound: to place something in a state of security. Its meanings range from "to ensure" (~die Versorgung sicherstellen~, "to ensure the supply") to "to confiscate, to seize as evidence" (~die Polizei stellte die Waffe sicher~, "the police confiscated the weapon"). The confiscation sense is particularly interesting: to ~sicherstellen~ evidence is to "place it in safety" â€” to secure it from tampering, a bureaucratic euphemism that frames seizure as preservation. The adjective ~sicher~ is itself a fascinating Latin borrowing: it entered Proto-West Germanic from Latin ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ during the Roman period, making it one of the earliest Latin loanwords in German. English received the same Latin word twice â€” once as ~sure~ (via Old French ~seur~) and once as ~secure~ (directly from Latin) â€” while German inherited it through direct contact along the ~limes~.
en.siegen: Derived from the noun ~Sieg~ ("victory") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Sieg~ traces to Middle High German ~sige~, ~sic~, from Old High German ~sigu~, from Proto-Germanic *~segaz~, *~seguz~ ("victory"). The word is deeply embedded in Germanic personal names: ~Siegfried~ ("victory-peace"), ~Siegmund~ ("victory-protection"), and ~Sieglinde~ ("victory-shield"). The Proto-Germanic root may be connected to Proto-Indo-European *~segت°-~ ("to hold, overpower"), also the source of Greek ~ekhein~ ("to hold") and the ~-seige~ in English ~besiege~. The exclamation ~Sieg Heil~ was appropriated by the Nazi regime, permanently darkening the word's connotations in modern German, though compound names like ~Siegfried~ remain in common use.
en.singen: ~singen~ comes from Middle High German ~singen~, from Old High German ~singan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~singwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~singwanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~sengت·ت°-~ ("to recite, intone, chant"). The PIE root is connected to Ancient Greek ~ل½€خ¼د†خ®~ (omphل¸—, "divine voice, oracle"), suggesting the deep cultural link between singing and prophecy in early Indo-European thought â€” the poet-singer as the mouth of the gods. ~Singen~ is one of the most stable words in the Germanic family: Old English ~singan~, Gothic ~ًگچƒًگŒ¹ًگŒ²ًگŒ²ًگچ…ًگŒ°ًگŒ½~ (siggwan), Old Norse ~syngja~, Dutch ~zingen~, and Swedish ~sjunga~ all carry the same meaning with minimal semantic drift across three thousand years. The Class III strong verb ablaut (~singen~, ~sang~, ~gesungen~) is among the most-cited examples in every German grammar, making it something of a paradigm case. The English cognate ~sing~ underwent the same vowel alternation (sing, sang, sung), reflecting the shared Proto-Germanic inheritance.
en.sinken:
From Middle High German ~sinken~, from Old High German ~sinkan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sinkwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sinkwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sengت·-~ ("to sink, to drop"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~sinken~, ~sank~, ~gesunken~. Cognate with Low German ~sinken~, Dutch ~zinken~, English ~sink~, Danish ~synke~, Swedish ~sjunka~. The Armenian ~ankanim~ ("to fall") is a distant cognate from the same PIE root.

The related causative verb ~senken~ ("to lower, to sink something") represents the same ablaut alternation seen across Germanic: the intransitive strong verb ~sinken~ ("it sinks") versus the transitive weak causative ~senken~ ("to make it sink"). This pattern â€” strong intransitive vs. weak causative â€” is one of the most productive in Germanic morphology: compare ~trinken~/~trأ¤nken~, ~springen~/~sprengen~, ~finden~/~finden~ (lost causative). The Proto-Germanic labiovelar *~kw~ in *~sinkwanؤ…~ simplified to ~k~ in West Germanic, but its trace appears in the Old Norse descendant ~sأ¸kkva~ (Icelandic ~sأ¶kkva~, Faroese ~sأ¸kka~), where the labiovelar survives as ~kv~.
en.sitzen: From Middle High German ~sitzen~, from Old High German ~sizzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sittjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sitjanؤ…~, from PIE *~sأ©dyeti~, from the root *~sed-~ ("to sit"). A class V strong verb. The same PIE root *~sed-~ underlies ~setzen~ â€” the causative pair is exactly parallel to English ~sit~/~set~: ~sitzen~ (intransitive, "to be sitting") : ~setzen~ (transitive, "to cause to sit, place"). ~besitzen~ ("to possess," literally "to sit upon") derives from this verb through the ~be-~ prefix. Ancient Greek ~hأ©zomai~ ("I sit") is a formal cognate. Cognate with Dutch ~zitten~, Low German ~sitten~, English ~sit~, Danish ~sidde~.
en.sollen: From Middle High German ~scholn~, ~schuln~, from Old High German ~sculan~, ~skulan~, ~scolan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skulan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skulanؤ…~, from PIE *~skel-~ ("to be obligated, owe, be guilty"). The change of ~sk~ to ~s~ observed in many Germanic languages is probably attributable to the word's common appearance in unstressed position. Cognate with Dutch ~zullen~, English ~shall~ and ~should~, Danish ~skulle~, Swedish ~skall~ and ~skulle~.
en.sorgen: From Middle High German ~sorgen~, from Old High German ~sorgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgؤپnؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from *~surgإچ~ ("worry, sorrow, care"), ultimately from PIE *~swergت°-~ ("to watch over, worry; be ill, suffer"). The semantic duality of the PIE root â€” watchful care and anxious suffering â€” is still visible in Modern German: ~sorgen fأ¼r~ ("to care for, provide for") carries the positive sense of attentiveness, while ~sich sorgen um~ ("to worry about") carries the negative sense of anxiety. The noun ~Sorge~ preserves both meanings simultaneously. The same root took only the suffering side in English ~sorrow~. Cognate with Dutch ~zorgen~, English ~sorrow~, Danish ~sأ¸rge~, Swedish ~sأ¶rja~.
en.sortieren: Borrowed from Italian ~sortire~, itself from Latin ~sortؤ«rؤ«~ ("to cast lots, to select by lot"), a derivation of the noun ~sors~ (genitive ~sortis~, "lot, fate, share"), which also gave English ~sort~, ~sorcery~ (originally divination by lots), and ~consort~. The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of Romance ~-ire~/~-ir~ verb endings, seen in hundreds of loanwords like ~studieren~, ~telefonieren~, and ~organisieren~. Though Latin ~sors~ originally concerned fate and chance, ~sortieren~ has lost all mystical connotations and simply means "to sort" or "to arrange." Cognate with French ~sortir~ (which shifted meaning to "to go out") and Spanish ~sortear~ ("to raffle, to dodge").
en.sparen:
From Middle High German ~sparn~, from Old High German ~sparؤ“n~, ~sparإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sparؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~sparaz~ ("sparing, frugal"), from Proto-Indo-European *~spehâ‚پ-~ ("to thrive, prosper; to leave untouched"). Cognate with English ~spare~, Dutch ~sparen~, Swedish ~spara~, Icelandic ~spara~.

English ~spare~ is the direct cognate, descended from Old English ~sparian~, and the two verbs share the same fundamental sense: to leave something untouched, to refrain from consuming or harming it. The same PIE root *~spehâ‚پ-~ produced Latin ~prosperus~ ("lucky, favorable") â€” whence English ~prosper~ and ~prosperity~. The original meaning hovered between restraint and abundance: to spare something was both to hold back and to ensure it remained plentiful. Old Church Slavonic ~sporإ­~ ("plentiful") and Sanskrit ~sphirأ،~ ("thick, abundant") preserve the prosperity side of the root; German ~sparsam~ ("frugal, economical") and English ~spare~ (adj., "extra, scanty") preserve the restraint side. ~Sparen~ and English ~spare~ thus meet in the same paradox: to save is both to go without and to ensure you have enough.
en.speichern: ~Speichern~ ("to store, save") is a denominal verb formed from ~Speicher~ ("storehouse, granary, attic"). ~Speicher~ descends from Middle High German ~spؤ«cher~, Old High German ~spؤ«hhؤپri~, and Proto-West Germanic *~spؤ«kؤپrؤ«~, borrowed from Late Latin ~spؤ«cؤپrium~ ("granary"), itself derived from ~spؤ«ca~ ("ear of grain, spike"). The Latin word also gives English ~spike~. The semantic journey is remarkable: a word for storing grain in a granary became the standard German word for saving a file to a hard drive or the cloud. Computer ~Speicher~ ("memory, storage") follows the same path. The ear-of-grain sense is preserved in the related English word ~spice~, which entered English via Old French from the same Latin root â€” both grain and seasoning were stored together in ancient storehouses.
en.spenden: From Middle High German ~spenden~, from Old High German ~spentإچn~ ("to consume, use, spend"), from Proto-West Germanic *~spendإچn~ ("to spend"), borrowed from Latin ~expendere~ ("to weigh out, pay out"). The PIE root is *~(s)pend-~ ("to pull, stretch, spin"), which also produced Latin ~pendere~ ("to hang, weigh") and ~pondus~ ("weight"). English ~spend~ is a direct cognate, descending from Old English ~spendan~, which was borrowed from the same Proto-West Germanic form. Both the German and English words share this remarkable path through church Latin, where monastic communities would ~expendere~ their resources in acts of charity. However, the two languages diverged semantically: English ~spend~ generalized to mean any outlay of money or effort, while German ~spenden~ narrowed to mean specifically "to donate, to give charitably." So a German speaker who wants to say "spend money" must use ~ausgeben~, while ~spenden~ carries the moral weight of generosity. The Latin root also produced English ~expend~, ~dispense~, ~pension~, and ~ponder~, all connected by the original image of weighing something out on a scale.
en.sperren:
From Middle High German ~sperren~, from Old High German ~sperran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sparrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sparrijanؤ…~ ("to bar, to block"), a denominative verb derived from *~sparrأ´~ ("wooden beam, log"). Cognate with English ~spar~ (the verb "to bolt, bar," and the noun for a wooden beam), Dutch ~sperren~.

The original concrete sense of barring a passage with a wooden beam expanded into a wide range of figurative blocking: ~absperren~ ("to cordon off"), ~einsperren~ ("to lock up"), ~aussperren~ ("to lock out"). In computing, ~sperren~ means "to lock" or "to block" access. The related noun ~Sperre~ ("barrier, blockade") and adjective ~sperrig~ ("bulky, unwieldy") both retain the idea of obstruction.

An interesting typographic meaning survives: ~gesperrt drucken~ means to print with spaced-out letters, the traditional German method of emphasis before bold and italic type became standard.
en.spezialisieren:
Borrowed from French ~spأ©cialiser~, derived from ~spأ©cial~ ("special"), from Latin ~speciؤپlis~ ("particular, individual"), from ~speciؤ“s~ ("appearance, form, kind"), from PIE *~speل¸±-~ ("to look, to observe"). The word entered German through French in the 18th century.

The verb means "to specialize" â€” ~sich auf etwas spezialisieren~ ("to specialize in something"). The PIE root *~speل¸±-~ ("to look") produced an extraordinary family of words across the Indo-European languages: Latin ~specere~ ("to look") gave English ~spectacle~, ~spectrum~, ~specimen~, ~species~, ~special~, ~specific~, ~inspect~, ~respect~, ~suspect~, ~prospect~, and ~aspect~ â€” all built on the idea of looking. The semantic journey from "looking" to "special" passed through "outward appearance" (Latin ~speciؤ“s~) to "a particular kind" and then "pertaining to a particular kind." The reflexive usage ~sich spezialisieren~ is standard in German, while the non-reflexive ~spezialisieren~ (with a direct object: ~ein Krankenhaus auf Herzchirurgie spezialisieren~) is rarer. The noun ~Spezialist~ and the adjective ~speziell~ entered German through the same French-Latin channel.
en.spiegeln:
From Middle High German ~spiegeln~, ~spiegelen~, a denominative verb from the noun ~Spiegel~ ("mirror"). ~Spiegel~ descends from MHG ~spiegel~, from OHG ~spiegal~, ~spؤ“gal~, from Proto-West Germanic *~spؤ“gl~ ("mirror"), an early borrowing from Latin ~speculum~ ("mirror"), which derives from ~specere~ ("to look at"), from PIE *~speل¸±-~ ("to see, to observe"). Compare Middle Dutch ~spiegelen~ (modern Dutch ~spiegelen~).

The borrowing of Latin ~speculum~ into Germanic must have occurred during the Roman occupation of the Rhineland, when Germanic peoples first encountered Roman glass mirrors -- a luxury technology that replaced the polished metal surfaces they had previously used. The PIE root *~speل¸±-~ is extraordinarily productive: from Latin it gave English ~spectacle~, ~species~, ~specimen~, ~spectrum~, ~speculate~, ~suspect~, ~respect~, ~inspect~, and ~spy~; from the Germanic side, it is the source of German ~spأ¤hen~ ("to spy, to scout"). The verb ~spiegeln~ means both "to mirror, to reflect" and figuratively "to reflect upon" (~widerspiegeln~, "to reflect back"). The noun ~Spiegel~ is also familiar as the name of the famous German news magazine ~Der Spiegel~ ("The Mirror"), founded in 1947 with the aspiration of reflecting society back to itself.
en.spielen: From Middle High German ~spil(e)n~, from Old High German ~spilإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~spilإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~spilإچnؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from the noun *~spilؤ…~ ("play, game, dance"), from Proto-West Germanic *~spil~ ("dance"). The ultimate pre-Germanic origin is uncertain. The semantic range of Old High German ~spilإچn~ was broader than modern ~spielen~, encompassing dance, bodily movement, and entertainment generally. Cognate with Dutch ~spelen~, Low German ~spelen~.
en.sprechen: From Middle High German ~sprأ«chen~, from Old High German ~sprأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanؤ…~, probably from PIE *~spregت°-~ ("to crackle"). The semantic development from an onomatopoeic root meaning "to crackle, make noise" to "to speak" parallels the evolution seen in many languages where words for speech derive from words for sound. Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
en.springen:
From Old High German ~springan~, from Proto-Germanic *~springanؤ…~, from PIE *~sprenاµت°-~, a nasalized form of *~sperاµت°-~ ("to move energetically, to hasten, to spring"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~springen~, ~sprang~, ~gesprungen~. Cognate with Low German ~springen~, Dutch ~springen~, West Frisian ~springe~, English ~spring~, Danish ~springe~, and Greek ~spأ©rkhإچ~ ("to set in rapid motion, to hasten"). Related to Sanskrit ~spل¹›hأ،yati~ ("to be eager"), Albanian ~shpأ«rthej~ ("to pop open, explode"), and Lithuanian ~sprؤ™جƒgti~ ("to push in").

The verb covers a wider semantic range in German than its English cognate: beyond "to jump, to leap, to bounce," it also means "to break, to burst, to pop" (as in glass cracking) and, in southern dialects and Swiss German, "to run, to dash" (~spring mal schnell zum Bأ¤cker!~ for "run to the bakery quickly!"). The "bursting" sense is the original Proto-Germanic meaning alongside "jumping" â€” both share the image of sudden, forceful motion. The noun ~Sprung~ ("jump; crack") preserves this dual meaning: a ~Sprung in der Schأ¼ssel~ is a crack in a bowl. Derived: ~Springer~ ("jumper; knight" in chess â€” because the knight leaps), ~Sprungbrett~ ("diving board, springboard").
en.spأ¼ren: From Middle High German ~spأ¼ren~, ~spuren~, ~spurn~, from Old High German ~spurien~, ~spurren~, from Proto-Germanic *~spurjanؤ…~ ("to feel, touch; to search, examine"). That verb is a derivative of *~spurؤ…~ ("track, trace"), the ancestor of modern ~Spur~. English ~spur~ (the boot-heel implement) comes from a closely related Proto-Germanic root *~spurأ´~ ("to kick"), both branches ultimately tracing to Proto-Indo-European *~sperH-~ ("to kick, step"). The original sense of ~spأ¼ren~ was following an animal's tracks â€” reading the earth for signs â€” and only later generalized to the interior act of sensing or feeling. Cognate with dialectal English ~speer~ ("to ask, inquire"), Swedish ~spأ¶rja~, and Dutch ~speuren~ ("to track, sniff out").
en.stammen:
A denominative verb derived from ~Stamm~ ("trunk, stem, tribe, stock") with the infinitive suffix ~-en~. ~Stamm~ derives from Middle High German ~stam~, from Old High German ~stam~, from Proto-Germanic *~stamnaz~, *~stamniz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand, stay"). Cognate with English ~stem~.

The PIE root *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand") is among the most generative in the language: it underlies German ~stehen~ ("to stand"), ~Staat~ ("state"), ~Stadt~ ("city"), ~Stall~ ("stable"), ~Stahl~ ("steel," via the notion of something solid and standing), Latin ~stؤپre~ ("to stand"), English ~stand~, ~stay~, ~station~, ~statute~, ~status~, ~stable~, and ~understand~ (to stand among or under). The concrete image of a tree trunk â€” the standing, upright part from which branches and roots extend â€” gave ~Stamm~ its cluster of meanings: a tribe is the stock from which descendants branch; a grammatical stem is the standing root form. ~Stammen~ thus means literally "to have one's trunk" in something, to originate from a standing point. English ~stem~ (from Old English ~stefn~, ~stemn~) is the direct cognate, preserving the same tree-and-origin duality. Cognate with English ~stem~, Dutch ~stam~, Swedish ~stam~, Danish ~stamme~.
en.starten: Borrowed from English ~start~, with the German verbal suffix ~-en~ added; compare Dutch ~starten~. English ~start~ itself traces from Middle English ~sterten~ ("to leap up suddenly"), from Old English ~styrtan~ ("to leap up, start"), from Proto-West Germanic *~sturtijan~ ("to startle, move, set in motion"), from PIE *~(s)ter-~ ("to be stiff"). The original sense was sudden bodily movement â€” a startled leap or a jolt â€” rather than the modern sense of beginning. That native English cognate is German ~stأ¼rzen~ ("to plunge, tumble, overturn"), from Old High German ~sturzen~, which descended from the same Proto-West Germanic root. So ~starten~ and ~stأ¼rzen~ are cousins, both going back to the same image of sudden, violent movement. The modern sense of "to begin (an engine, a race, a program)" developed first in English and entered German via mechanical and sporting vocabulary in the 19th and 20th centuries. Cognate with Dutch ~storten~ ("to pour, dump, collapse"), German ~stأ¼rzen~, Danish ~styrte~.
en.stattfinden:
Attested in Old High German as ~stat findan~ and in Middle High German as ~stat vinden~, documented since the 9th century. A compound of the noun ~Statt~ ("place, stead") used as a separable verbal particle and the strong verb ~finden~ ("to find"):

- ~Statt~: An orthographic variant of ~Stadt~ ("city") and a doublet of ~Stأ¤tte~ ("place, site"). From OHG ~stat~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stadi~, from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~, from PIE *~stأ©hâ‚‚tis~ ("a standing, a standing place"), from *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand"). Cognate with English ~stead~ (as in ~homestead~, ~instead of~), Dutch ~stad~, and Old English ~stede~. The noun ~Statt~ is not a standard verb prefix and should not be treated as one: it is an unproductive lexical fossil occurring only in this compound and in the preposition ~statt~ ("instead of").
- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~finأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge"). Cognate with English ~find~, Dutch ~vinden~, Danish ~finde~.

~stattfinden~ means literally "to find a place" â€” an event ~findet statt~ when it finds its place in time and space, when it locates itself in the world. This spatial metaphor is deeply embedded in German idiom: German speakers say an event "takes place" by having it actively seek and find its location. The PIE root *~pent-~ also gives English ~path~ and ~find~ â€” reflecting the ancient association between traveling a path and encountering something along the way. The related noun ~Stأ¤tte~ survives in elevated or archaic usage (~Kultstأ¤tte~, "place of worship"; ~Gedenkstأ¤tte~, "memorial site"), while ~Stadt~ narrowed to mean "city" specifically â€” making ~Statt~ the sole surviving instance where the word retains its fully general sense of "place" in productive modern usage.
en.stechen: From Middle High German ~stأ«chen~, from Old High German ~stأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stekanؤ…~ ("to stab, prick"), from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)teg-~ ("to stick, poke"). Cognate with Dutch ~steken~ ("to stab, sting"), Old Norse ~stinga~ ("to sting"), and English ~stick~ (originally "to stab, pierce"). The card-game term ~Stich~ ("trick," literally "stab") derives from this verb, preserving the idea of one card defeating another. The compound ~Mأ¼ckenstich~ ("mosquito bite," literally "mosquito sting") and the expression ~in See stechen~ ("to put to sea") show the verb's wide semantic range from piercing to departing.
en.stecken: Modern ~stecken~ reflects a merger of two historically distinct verbs. The transitive weak verb ("to put, stick something in") derives from Middle High German ~stecken~, from Old High German ~steckإچn~, a geminate (doubled-consonant) derivative of ~stأ«hhan~ â€” the ancestor of modern ~stechen~ ("to prick, stab"). The strong intransitive verb (archaic ~stak~/~gestocken~, "to be stuck, be fixed in place") belonged to the same root family but followed a strong conjugation before the two merged in Early Modern German. Both trace to Proto-West Germanic *~stekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stikanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)teyg-~ ("to be sharp, stab, goad"). English ~stick~ and ~steak~ (the latter originally "meat on a stick") are cognates, as is the archaic English verb ~stitch~ in its older sense of "to pierce." The merger of the transitive and intransitive forms parallels that of ~hأ¤ngen~, where a strong intransitive and a weak transitive likewise fused over time.
en.stehen: From Middle High German ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from Old High German ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand"). The ~-h-~ was introduced into the spelling by analogy with ~sehen~, in which it had become mute but was retained in writing. The past-tense conjugations (~stand~, ~standen~) derive from Old High German ~stuond~, ~stuonden~, themselves suppletive forms from Proto-West Germanic *~standan~; their ~a~-vocalism arose by analogy with other strong verbs such as ~binden~ (~band~) and ~finden~ (~fand~), dating back to Middle High German. Cognate with Dutch ~staan~, English ~stand~, Danish ~stأ¥~, Swedish ~stأ¥~.
en.stehlen: From Middle High German ~stأ«ln~, from Old High German ~stelan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stelan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stelanؤ…~ ("to steal"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to rob, steal"). This is a common Germanic verb with remarkably consistent meaning: cognate with Gothic ~stilan~, Old Norse ~stela~, Old English ~stelan~ (whence English ~steal~), Dutch ~stelen~, Swedish ~stjأ¤la~, Danish ~stjأ¦le~, and Icelandic ~stela~. The reflexive ~sich stehlen~ ("to steal away, to sneak") preserves an older sense of furtive movement rather than theft. The idiomatic ~jemandem die Show stehlen~ ("to steal the show from someone") is a calque from English, demonstrating how borrowed idioms travel between related languages.
en.steigen: From Middle High German ~stأ®gen~, from Old High German ~stؤ«gan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤ«gan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤ«ganؤ…~, from PIE *~steygت°-~ ("to walk, climb"). A class I strong verb (~steigen~, ~stieg~, ~gestiegen~). The same PIE root gave Ancient Greek ~steأ­khإچ~ ("I go, march") and Old Church Slavonic ~stignا«~ ("I come"). The cognate English word ~sty~ (as in "pig sty") originally meant an enclosure approached by a path â€” preserving the sense of going or climbing to a place. The archaic English verb ~sty~ ("to climb") shares the same root directly. Cognate with Dutch ~stijgen~, Low German ~stiegen~, Danish ~stige~, Swedish ~stiga~, Icelandic ~stأ­ga~, Gothic ~steigan~.
en.steigern: ~Steigern~ is the causative of ~steigen~ ("to climb, rise"), formed with the German ~-ern~ suffix that creates intensive or causative verbs: "to make (something) climb, to cause to rise, to increase." ~Steigen~ descends from Middle High German ~stأ®gen~, Old High German ~stؤ«gan~, Proto-West Germanic *~stؤ«gan~, and Proto-Germanic *~stؤ«ganؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~steygت°-~ ("to walk, climb, stride"). That ancient root surfaces in English ~stair~ (from Old English ~stا£ؤ،er~, literally "that by which one climbs") and in the obsolete English verb ~sty~ ("to climb"), as well as in Ancient Greek ~دƒد„خµخ¯د‡د‰~ (~steأ­khإچ~, "I go"). The ~-ern~ suffix is especially transparent in German: compare ~fأ¶rdern~ ("to promote," causative of ~fahren~ in its older sense), ~steigern~ ("to increase"), and ~أ¤ndern~ ("to alter"). In grammar, ~steigern~ takes on a second, technical life: it means "to form the comparative and superlative of an adjective" â€” that is, to make an adjective "climb" through its degrees of comparison.
en.stellen: From Middle High German ~stellen~, from Old High German ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb meaning "to place, set" â€” the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand"). Cognate with Dutch ~stellen~, Low German ~stellen~, English ~stell~ ("to place, fix"), Swedish ~stأ¤lla~, Danish ~stille~, Yiddish ~shteln~.
en.sterben:
From Middle High German ~stأ«rben~, from Old High German ~stأ«rban~, from Proto-Germanic *~sterbanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)terp-~ ("to lose force, become numb, be motionless") or *~sterbت°-~ ("to become stiff"), both traceable to the root *~ster-~ ("stiff, rigid"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~sterven~, Low German ~starven~/~sterven~, West Frisian ~stjerre~, English ~starve~.

The false-friend relationship between German ~sterben~ and English ~starve~ is one of the most striking semantic divergences in the Germanic family. Both words descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, which originally meant "to become stiff, to become rigid" â€” a reference to the bodily stiffening of death. German ~sterben~ has retained the general sense of dying from any cause. English ~starve~ narrowed dramatically to dying specifically of hunger, a restriction that solidified by the early modern period, though dialectal English retained the broader sense ("to starve of cold") well into the 19th century. The underlying PIE root is also connected to German ~starr~ ("stiff, rigid") and to English ~stark~ and ~stare~, all preserving some trace of the original sense of rigidity. The ablaut series ~stirbt~/~starb~/~gestorben~ follows the class III pattern shared with ~werfen~ and ~verderben~.
en.steuern:
From MHG ~stiuren~, from OHG ~stiuren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stiurijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stiurijanؤ…~.

The root noun ~Steuer~ carries two distinct meanings that share a single ancestor: (1) ~das Steuer~ ("rudder, helm," neuter), from PGmc *~stiurijؤ…~, cognate with English ~steer~ and Dutch ~stuur~; and (2) ~die Steuer~ ("tax," feminine), whose semantic path ran from "helm/rudder" to "supporting post" to "support" to "financial support" to "tax." Both ultimately derive from the same PGmc verbal root. ~Steuern~ thus means both "to steer, navigate, control" and, in archaic or Swiss usage, "to pay a tax." The English cognate ~steer~ shares the navigation sense exactly. That the word for navigating a ship and the word for paying taxes share a single ancestor makes ~steuern~ one of German's richest semantic clusters.
en.stimmen: From Middle High German ~stimmen~, from Old High German *~stimmen~ (attested in OHG ~gistimmen~, "to bring into harmony"), a denominative derived from the noun ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). ~Stimme~ derives from MHG ~stimme~, from OHG ~stimma~, ~stimna~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnإچ~ or *~stamnijإچ~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle"). Cognate with Old English ~stefn~, ~stemn~ ("voice"), Old Frisian ~stifne~, ~stemme~, Old Saxon ~stemna~, Dutch ~stem~ ("voice, vote"), Gothic ~stibna~ ("voice"), and Ancient Greek ~stأ³ma~ ("mouth") â€” the last being particularly striking as a cross-family cognate that connects German ~Stimme~ to the Greek anatomical term. English ~steven~ (archaic, "voice, clamor") is a direct cognate. The word ~stimmen~ spans a wide semantic range today: "to tune" (an instrument), "to be correct" (~das stimmt~, "that's right"), "to vote," and "to put in a mood" (~jemanden frأ¶hlich stimmen~, "to cheer someone up"). All of these meanings radiate from the central idea of a voice â€” bringing things into accord, casting a voice in a vote, being "in tune." Cognate with Dutch ~stemmen~, Danish ~stemme~, Swedish ~stأ¤mma~.
en.stoppen:
Borrowed from Middle Low German ~stoppen~, from Old Saxon *~stoppإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stoppإچn~. The ultimate origin is debated: possibly from Proto-Germanic *~stuppإچnؤ…~ ("to stop, close"), from PIE *~(s)tewp-~ ("to push, stick"), or possibly borrowed from Latin ~stuppإچ~ ("to stop up, plug"). The direction of borrowing between Germanic and Latin is itself disputed.

The original concrete meaning was "to plug, to stop up" (a hole, a leak). The figurative sense "to halt, to cease" developed from the image of stopping a flow by plugging it. Only in this figurative meaning did ~stoppen~ enter standard High German, reinforced by the influence of English ~stop~. The older High German doublet ~stopfen~ ("to stuff, to plug") preserves the original concrete sense. A third relative, ~staffieren~ ("to decorate, furnish"), arrived via a more circuitous Romance route. The compound ~Stoppuhr~ ("stopwatch") reflects the verb's secondary meaning "to measure time with a stopwatch."
en.stoأںen: ~stoأںen~ comes from Middle High German ~stإچzen~, from Old High German ~stإچzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stautan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stautanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stأ³wd-e-ti~, an o-grade intensive formation from *~(s)tewd-~ ("to hit, push"). The PIE root also produced Latin ~tundere~ ("to beat, pound") â€” whose past participle ~tإ«sus~ gives English ~contuse~ and ~obtuse~ â€” and Sanskrit ~tudأ،ti~ ("to strike, goad"). The related Proto-Germanic formation *~stautijanؤ…~ (a frequentative meaning "to push repeatedly") is the likely ancestor of English ~stutter~ and Dutch ~stotteren~, where the repeated-action meaning of the frequentative maps neatly onto the repeated-syllable nature of stuttering. Cognate with Old Norse ~stauta~ and ~steyta~ (whence Danish ~stأ¸de~), Gothic ~ًگچƒًگچ„ًگŒ°ًگŒ؟ًگچ„ًگŒ°ًگŒ½~ (stautan), Dutch ~stoten~, and dialectal English ~stot~ ("to bounce, ricochet").
en.strahlen: A 16th-century denominative verb from the noun ~Strahl~ ("ray, beam"), with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Strahl~ traces to Middle High German ~strؤپle~, from Old High German ~strؤپla~ ("arrow"), from Proto-West Germanic *~strؤپlu~ ("arrow"). Cognate with Dutch ~straal~ and the rare English ~streal~. The semantic journey from "arrow" to "ray of light" is a remarkable metaphor: arrows fly in straight lines from a source, just as light beams radiate from the sun. This metaphorical extension happened in the 17th century when ~Strahl~ began to describe light and sunbeams, then expanded in the 18th century to water jets (~Wasserstrahl~) and around 1800 to heat (~Wأ¤rmestrahlung~, "radiation"). Today ~strahlen~ means both "to radiate/beam" and figuratively "to beam with joy."
en.streben: From Middle High German ~strأ«ben~, from Old High German ~strأ«bؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stribؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~stribؤپnؤ…~ ("to strive, resist"). The verb carries connotations of earnest, directed effort toward a goal. The derived noun ~Streber~ ("overachiever, teacher's pet") has decidedly negative connotations in German, unlike the neutral English ~striver~. The compound ~Bestreben~ ("endeavor") and the participial adjective ~strebsam~ ("ambitious, industrious") maintain more positive associations. Cognate with Dutch ~streven~ ("to strive") and English ~strive~ (via Old French ~estriver~, itself borrowed from a Germanic source related to ~streben~).
en.streichen:
From Middle High German ~strؤ«chen~, from Old High German ~strؤ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strؤ«kan~, from Proto-Germanic *~strؤ«kanؤ…~ ("to stroke, to rub"), from PIE *~streyg-~ ("to stroke, rub, press"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~strijken~ ("to iron, to stroke"), English ~strike~.

The relationship between ~streichen~ and English ~strike~ is a case study in how cognates can drift apart while remaining connected by a shared core meaning. Both descend from the same Proto-Germanic root meaning "to move in one direction, to rub, to stroke." English ~strike~ hardened the action into a blow; German ~streichen~ softened it into a gliding motion. Modern ~streichen~ means "to stroke, to caress" (~einem Kind أ¼ber den Kopf streichen~, "to stroke a child's head"), "to spread" (~Butter aufs Brot streichen~, "to spread butter on bread"), "to paint" (~die Wand streichen~, "to paint the wall"), and "to cross out, to cancel, to delete" (~ein Wort streichen~, "to strike out a word"). This last meaning â€” crossing out â€” is the closest to English ~strike~ (as in "strike from the record"). In a delightful irony, German borrowed the English cognate back as ~Streik~ ("labor strike") and ~streiken~ ("to go on strike") in the 19th century, because the native ~streichen~ had drifted too far from the combative sense. The noun ~Strich~ ("line, stroke, dash") and the diminutive ~streicheln~ ("to pet, to caress") round out the family.
en.streiten:
From MHG ~strؤ«ten~, from OHG ~strؤ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strؤ«dan~, from Proto-Germanic *~strؤ«danؤ…~, probably from Pre-Germanic *~streydت°hâ‚پ-~, a compound of PIE *~strey-~ ("to resist") + *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, place"). Cognate with Low German ~striden~, Dutch ~strijden~ ("to fight, struggle"), and English ~stride~.

The English cognate ~stride~ reveals a remarkable semantic divergence: while German ~streiten~ means "to quarrel, to argue, to dispute," English ~stride~ means "to walk with long steps." The connection lies in the physical posture of combat -- striding aggressively toward an opponent. Dutch ~strijden~ ("to fight, to battle") preserves the martial sense more faithfully than either German or English. In modern German, ~streiten~ typically refers to verbal conflict (~sich streiten~ = "to argue") rather than physical combat, though the legal compound ~Streit~ ("dispute, lawsuit") maintains a formal register.
en.studieren: ~Studieren~ ("to study, attend university") descends from Middle High German ~studieren~, borrowed from Latin ~studؤ“re~ ("to be eager, to apply oneself, to strive after"), from Proto-Italic *~studؤ“إچ~ and Proto-Indo-European *~(s)tewd-~, from the root *~(s)tew-~ ("to push, strike, hit"). Related Latin words include ~tundإچ~ ("to beat, strike"), and the English cognate ~stub~ (something you strike your foot against) carries the same root. The semantic journey is striking: from "to push or knock toward something" to "to strive after" to "to pursue earnestly" to "to study." In modern German, ~studieren~ is typically reserved for university-level study (one ~studiert~ at a ~Universitأ¤t~), whereas ~lernen~ covers school learning more generally. A false friend: English speakers sometimes assume ~studieren~ means "to study" in the broad sense, but a German schoolchild ~lernt~, not ~studiert~.
en.stأ¤rken: ~Stأ¤rken~ ("to strengthen") is a denominal verb inherited from Middle High German ~sterken~ and Old High German ~sterken~, formed from the adjective ~stark~ ("strong") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. The adjective ~stark~ and the English adjective ~stark~ are direct cognates, both from Proto-Germanic *~starkuz~ ("stiff, strong"), from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)terg-~ ("rigid, stiff"). The semantic split between German and English is a classic false-friend trap: German ~stark~ still means "strong," while English ~stark~ shifted toward "bare, utter, rigid" â€” as in ~stark naked~ or ~stark raving mad~. English kept the "stiffness" nuance and lost the "strength" nuance. The same root also gives English ~starch~ (via the stiffening property of the substance). So ~stأ¤rken~ is cognate with both English ~stark~ and ~starch~, words that feel nothing alike.
en.stأ¶ren:
From Middle High German ~stأ¶ren~, from Old High German ~stإچren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~staurijan~. Related to Proto-Germanic *~steuran~ and cognate with Dutch ~storen~, Old English ~styrian~ ("to stir, to move, to agitate"), English ~stir~.

The connection between ~stأ¶ren~ ("to disturb") and English ~stir~ is direct: both descend from the same Germanic root meaning "to move, to agitate." To disturb someone is etymologically to stir them from their rest â€” to set them in unwanted motion. The derived noun ~Stأ¶rung~ ("disturbance, malfunction") appears everywhere from psychology (~psychische Stأ¶rung~, "psychological disorder") to technology (~Netzwerkstأ¶rung~, "network disruption"), while the compound ~zerstأ¶ren~ intensifies the disturbance to total destruction. Cognate with Dutch ~storen~, English ~stir~.
en.stأ¼rzen:
From Middle High German ~stأ¼rzen~, from Old High German ~sturzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sturtijan~ ("to fall down, tumble"), of uncertain ultimate origin. Possibly from PIE *~ster-~ ("to be stiff") via a dental extension *~sterd-~, though the derivation is debated. Cognate with English ~start~ â€” a doublet descended from the same Proto-West Germanic source, though the meanings have diverged radically.

The connection between ~stأ¼rzen~ ("to fall, to plunge, to overthrow") and English ~start~ ("to begin, to set in motion") is one of the more surprising cognate pairs in Germanic. Both descend from Proto-West Germanic *~sturtijan~, but English preserved the sense of sudden, jerky motion (a startled horse ~starts~), while German kept the sense of precipitous falling. The verb covers an enormous semantic range: intransitively, "to fall, to crash, to plunge, to rush" (~er stأ¼rzte vom Dach~, "he fell from the roof"; ~sie stأ¼rzte aus dem Zimmer~, "she rushed out of the room"); transitively, "to hurl, to overturn, to overthrow" (~eine Regierung stأ¼rzen~, "to overthrow a government"). The reflexive ~sich auf etwas stأ¼rzen~ ("to pounce on something, to dive into something") adds eagerness to the violence.
