The derived noun ~Sturz~ ("fall, plunge, crash") and the compound ~Absturz~ ("crash, precipitous drop") â€” used equally for aircraft and computer programs â€” extend the imagery into every domain of German life.
en.stأ¼tzen: Attested as a simplex verb only since the 17th century. Probably modeled after Middle Low German ~stأ¼tten~, as a back-formation from the inherited compound ~unterstأ¼tzen~, from Middle High German ~understأ¼zzen~, from Old High German ~untarstuzzen~, ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *~stuttijan~. The verb is related to Proto-Germanic *~stuأ¾s~ ("post, prop"), whence English ~stud~ (in the architectural sense of "upright post"). Cognate with Dutch ~stutten~ ("to support"), and, through Low German borrowing, Norwegian ~stأ¸tte~, Swedish ~stأ¶tta~, and probably dialectal English ~stut~ ("to support"). The semantic core of "propping up" has remained remarkably stable across a millennium. In modern German, ~stأ¼tzen~ covers both physical support ("to brace, to prop") and abstract support ("to back, to substantiate"), a metaphorical extension shared with its English cousin ~support~ (though the two words are unrelated etymologically). The fact that the simplex ~stأ¼tzen~ is a relatively late back-formation from ~unterstأ¼tzen~ reverses the usual pattern, where prefixed verbs derive from simpler roots -- here the compound came first.
en.suchen: From Middle High German ~suochen~, ~sأ¼echen~, from Old High German ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sإچkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sإچkijanؤ…~, from PIE *~sehâ‚‚g-~ ("to seek out"). The modern form without umlaut reflects the Upper German dialect, which resisted umlaut of long rounded vowels before velars; Central German forms show the expected umlaut (compare Luxembourgish ~sichen~). Cognate with Dutch ~zoeken~, Low German ~sأ¶ken~, Danish ~sأ¸ge~, English ~seek~, Gothic ~sإچkjan~.
en.tanzen:
From Middle High German ~tanzen~, borrowed via Central German dialects from Old French ~dancier~ (modern French ~danser~). The adapted onset ~t-~ (rather than ~d-~) in Upper German dialects and standard German proves that the word was not borrowed directly from French but entered through Middle Low German or Middle Dutch ~dansen~, passing through Central German before reaching the south, where the High German consonant shift changed ~d-~ to ~t-~.

The ultimate origin of the Old French word is disputed, but it may derive from Frankish *~dansإچn~ ("to draw along, to pull"), which would make it a Germanic word that traveled to France with the Franks and then boomeranged back into German. If this Frankish etymology is correct, ~tanzen~ is a remarkable round-trip borrowing: a Germanic word exported to Romance and then re-imported in altered form. English ~dance~ was likewise borrowed from Old French ~dancier~, making ~tanzen~ and ~dance~ doublets of sorts -- both borrowed from the same French source, but through different routes and at different times. The word appears in German texts from around the 12th century, coinciding with the age of courtly ~Minnesang~, when French cultural influence on German aristocratic life was at its peak.
en.tauchen:
From Middle High German ~tإ«chen~, ~touchen~, from Old High German *~tإ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dإ«kan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dإ«kanؤ…~ ("to duck, to dive under"). A class II strong verb that became weak during the early modern period. The word had fallen out of use in Upper German dialects but was reintroduced from Central German ~dإ«chen~/~tإ«chen~ and Middle Low German ~dإ«ken~, which had remained common. In the course of this revival, the originally strong conjugation was regularized to weak. Cognate with Dutch ~duiken~, Swedish ~dyka~. A Low German consonant variant of the same root survives as German ~ducken~ ("to duck, to crouch"), cognate with and synonymous with English ~duck~ (the verb).

The etymology of Proto-Germanic *~dإ«kanؤ…~ is uncertain. One hypothesis connects it to *~duganؤ…~ ("to be fit, to avail"), suggesting a Pre-Germanic form *~dت°ugت°nأ©hâ‚‚-~ with an original meaning of "to fall" that evolved to "to happen, be fitting" (compare ~Zufall~, literally "falling-to," meaning "chance, coincidence"). The transitive sense "to immerse, to submerge, to bathe" (~die أ„pfel in heiأںes Karamell tauchen~, "to dip the apples in hot caramel") coexists with the intransitive "to dive" â€” a neat semantic pairing of agent and object. The derivative ~auftauchen~ ("to emerge, to surface, to turn up") is among the most common compounds: things, people, and problems all ~tauchen auf~ in German.
en.tauschen:
From Middle High German ~tiuschen~, derived from ~tإ«schen~, related to ~tأ¤uschen~ ("to deceive"), both of uncertain further origin. The semantic shift ran from "to deceive" to "to trick someone into buying one's goods" to "to trade, exchange, barter."

The shared ancestry of ~tauschen~ ("to exchange") and ~tأ¤uschen~ ("to deceive") reveals a deeply cynical medieval view of commerce: trading was, at its root, a form of trickery. The two verbs differentiated only gradually, with ~tauschen~ losing its negative connotation while ~tأ¤uschen~ retained it. The pair is a classic example of semantic bifurcation, where a single word splits into two with divergent meanings. In modern German, the two words are completely distinct: ~tauschen~ is neutral ("to swap, exchange, barter"), while ~tأ¤uschen~ is negative ("to deceive, mislead"). The derivative ~enttأ¤uschen~ ("to disappoint") literally means "to un-deceive" -- to strip away an illusion, revealing a less pleasant reality.
en.teilen: From Middle High German ~teilen~ and Old High German ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~ ("to divide, part, deal"), from Proto-Germanic *~dailijanؤ…~, a denominative of *~dailiz~ ("part, share"), from PIE *~dت°(hâ‚پ)-oy-lo-~ (alternatively *~dت°ayl-~), tentatively from *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, set, place"), though the precise PIE derivation is uncertain. The semantic core of "dividing into parts" is consistent and stable across the entire recorded history of the word. The same root yields ~Teil~ ("part"), ~Abteil~ ("compartment"), and the legal term ~Anteil~ ("share, stake"). Cognate with Dutch ~delen~, English ~deal~ (in both the commercial sense of "to deal cards / goods" and the older sense of "a part, a great deal"), Danish ~dele~, Swedish ~dela~.
en.teilnehmen:
A calque of Latin ~participإچ~ ("to share in, partake of"), itself from ~pars~ ("part") + ~capere~ ("to take"). Compound of ~Teil~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~Teil~: From MHG ~teil~, from OHG ~teil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daili~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailiz~, tentatively from PIE *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, do") via a stem meaning "portion, watershed." Cognate with English ~deal~ (as in "a great deal," a portion), Dutch ~deel~.
- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb.

Unlike standard prefix compounds (~ab-~, ~an-~, ~be-~, etc.), ~teilnehmen~ is built from a noun (~Teil~, "part") plus a verb â€” a verbal noun compound rather than a prefixed form. The noun ~Teil~ in this construction is considered grammaticalized, which is why it is written lowercase when separated: ~ich nehme teil~. The parallel calques across neighboring languages are striking: Dutch ~deelnemen~, Icelandic ~taka أ¾أ،tt~, and English ~take part~ all replicate the same Latin model independently, each "translating" Latin ~participإچ~ into native materials. Cognate with Dutch ~deelnemen~.
en.testen: Borrowed from English ~test~, plus the German verbal suffix ~-en~. English ~test~ comes from Middle English ~test~, ~teste~, from Old French ~test~, ~teste~ ("an earthen vessel, especially a pot in which metals were tried"), from Latin ~testum~ ("earthen pot, lid of a clay vessel"), related to Latin ~testa~ ("clay pot, potsherd") and ~terra~ ("earth, land"). The leap from clay pot to examination came via assaying: medieval metallurgists placed ore in a ~testum~ to fire and analyze it, and this concrete act of proving metal by heat became the general metaphor for any examination of quality. The word entered German in the twentieth century as a scientific and technical borrowing, making ~testen~ one of the youngest verbs in the standard vocabulary â€” a word born in the laboratory.
en.thematisieren:
From ~Thema~ + ~-isieren~:

- ~Thema~: Borrowed from Latin ~thema~, from Ancient Greek ~thأ©ma~ ("that which is placed, a proposition"), from the verb ~tithأ©nai~ ("to put, place"), from PIE *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, place, set").
- ~-isieren~: The standard German suffix for creating verbs from adjectives or nouns, borrowed from French ~-iser~, ultimately from Greek ~-أ­zein~.

~Thematisieren~ means "to bring up as a topic, to make something a theme of discussion, to address (an issue)." The PIE root *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, place") is one of the most prolific roots in the entire family: it produced Greek ~thأ©sis~ ("a placing," whence English ~thesis~), Latin ~facere~ ("to do, make," from an extended form, whence English ~fact~, ~factory~, ~fashion~), Sanskrit ~dأ،dhؤپti~ ("places"), and Lithuanian ~dؤ—جپti~ ("to put"). The original Greek ~thأ©ma~ meant simply "something placed down" â€” a proposition laid on the table for discussion â€” which is exactly what ~thematisieren~ does: placing something into the arena of discourse.
en.tippen: From Low German, where the standard High German equivalent would have been ~tأ¼pfen~ ("to dab, dot"). The word has two distinct semantic branches in modern German. In the sense of "to type" or "to tap," it derives from the onomatopoeic imitation of light touching. In the sense of "to bet, to guess" (as in ~Lotto tippen~), it comes from the noun ~Tipp~, itself borrowed from English ~tip~ ("a piece of advice, a hint"). The coexistence of these two meanings -- tapping keys and placing bets -- makes ~tippen~ a minor source of amusement: ~Ich tippe auf Bayern~ could mean either "I'm typing about Bayern" or "I'm betting on Bayern."
en.tragen: From Middle High German ~tragen~, from Old High German ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). A class VI strong verb whose semantic range broadened from "to drag, pull" to "to carry, bear, wear." Cognate with Dutch ~dragen~, English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Danish ~drage~, Icelandic ~draga~, Portuguese ~trazer~.
en.trainieren: From English ~train~ with the German ~-ieren~ suffix, attested since the first half of the 19th century, when organized athletic training and horse racing adopted English terminology. The English word ~train~ descends from Old French ~traأ¯ner~ ("to pull out, to draw"), from Vulgar Latin *~traginإچ~, from *~tragإچ~, from Latin ~trahإچ~ ("to pull, to draw"), ultimately from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, to draw, to drag"). The original sense of "pulling" or "drawing along" evolved through the notion of "pulling a person through exercises" to "training." The same Latin root gives German ~Traktor~ (something that pulls) and ~Attraktion~ (something that draws you in). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German tool for naturalizing foreign verbs â€” compare ~telefonieren~, ~funktionieren~, ~finanzieren~. Unlike many ~-ieren~ verbs borrowed from French, ~trainieren~ came via English, reflecting the rise of British sporting culture in nineteenth-century Continental Europe.
en.transportieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~trؤپnsportؤپre~ ("to carry across") in the sixteenth century, with the German verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin compound consists of ~trؤپns~ ("across"), from PIE *~terhâ‚‚-~ ("to cross over"), and ~portؤپre~ ("to carry"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to carry forth").

The word is one of many Latin-derived ~-ieren~ verbs that coexist with native Germanic synonyms: ~befأ¶rdern~ ("to convey, transport") is the Germanic alternative. The noun ~Transport~ and the suffix ~-ieren~ together illustrate how German readily adapted Latin vocabulary through a French-influenced verbal pattern, creating a hybrid word that is Latin in root but Germanic in conjugation.
en.trauen: From Middle High German ~trإ«wen~, from Old High German ~trإ«wؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trإ«wؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~trإ«wؤپnؤ…~ ("to trust, believe"). The PIE root is *~deru-~ ("to be firm, hard, solid"), which also produced the words for ~tree~ and ~true~ in English, both referring to things that are firm and reliable. The semantic web is stunning: from a single root meaning "solid" grew German ~trauen~ ("to trust"), ~Trost~ ("comfort, consolation"), ~treu~ ("faithful"), and ~Trauung~ ("wedding ceremony," literally an act of entrusting). English ~trust~, ~true~, ~tree~, ~truce~, and ~troth~ all descend from the same PIE root, as do Gothic ~trausti~ ("alliance, pact") and Old Norse ~traust~ ("confidence"). The German verb ~trauen~ has a delightful range: ~ich traue dir~ means "I trust you," ~sich trauen~ means "to dare" (to trust oneself), and ~trauen~ in a church context means "to marry" (to entrust two people to each other). The related ~zutrauen~ means "to believe someone capable of," while ~misstrauen~ means "to distrust." German ~Trost~ and English ~trust~ are doublets from the same Proto-Germanic root *~traustؤ…~.
en.treffen: From Middle High German ~treffen~, from Old High German ~treffan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drepan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drepanؤ…~ ("to strike, push, beat"), from PIE *~dت°rebت°-~, *~dت°reb-~ ("to crack, crush, strike, kill"). The Proto-Germanic root carried a primary meaning of violent striking â€” preserved most clearly in Old Norse ~drepa~ ("to kill, strike") and in German ~betreffen~ ("to concern, affect," literally "to strike upon"). Modern German ~treffen~ has largely shed its violent sense, shifting toward "to meet" and "to hit a target," but the original force remains audible in phrases like ~ein Unglأ¼ck trifft jemanden~ ("a misfortune strikes someone"). The High German consonant shift from *~dr-~ to ~tr-~ obscures the cognate relationship with Old English ~drepan~ ("to strike"). Cognate with Old Norse ~drepa~, Old English ~drepan~.
en.treiben: ~treiben~ comes from Middle High German ~trؤ«ben~, from Old High German ~trؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~drؤ«banؤ…~. This is the cognate of English ~drive~: the two words share a Proto-West Germanic ancestor, separated by the High German consonant shift that regularly converted Proto-Germanic *d to German *t. The semantic range in modern German is wide â€” to drive (animals), to drift (on water), to pursue (a hobby or trade: ~Sport treiben~), to force or compel, and to sprout (~Knospen treiben~) â€” but all share the underlying sense of impelling motion. Cognate with Dutch ~drijven~, Low German ~drieven~, Swedish ~driva~, and Danish ~drive~. The Class I strong verb ablaut pattern (~treiben~, ~trieb~, ~getrieben~) is one of the most regular in the language, directly reflecting the Proto-Germanic vowel grades *ei / *ai / *i.
en.trennen: From Middle High German ~trennen~, from Old High German ~trennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trannijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trannijanؤ…~. Cognate with Low German ~trennen~ and Dutch ~tornen~ (archaic ~tarnen~, from Middle Dutch ~ternen~, a metathesis of an older form ~trennen~). The deeper etymology is uncertain; the Proto-Germanic root is not securely connected to any PIE root, and proposed connections to a root meaning "to tear" remain speculative. Dutch ~tornen~ has narrowed to mean specifically "to unpick stitches" â€” a particularly apt descendant for a verb of separation.
en.treten: From Middle High German ~trأ«ten~, from Old High German ~trأ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudanؤ…~. A class V strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~treten~, ~trat~, ~getreten~. The Proto-Germanic root is probably a root aorist with alternating vocalism: present singular *~tred-~, non-singular *~trud-~, from a possible pre-Germanic *~dret-~, which may be an extension of PIE *~dr-~ ("to run"), related to roots like *~drem-~ ("to run, sleep") and Sanskrit ~drؤپ~ ("to run, make haste"). The semantic shift from "to run" to "to step, tread" reflects a narrowing from general movement to deliberate foot placement. The same Proto-West Germanic base *~tredan~ gives English ~tread~, so the two verbs are direct cognates with essentially the same meaning. Derived forms extend the core sense in vivid directions: ~eintreten~ ("to step in, to occur"), ~auftreten~ ("to appear, perform"), ~betreten~ ("to enter, to step on") â€” every social entry and stage appearance in German involves the language of footsteps. Cognate with Dutch ~treden~, English ~tread~, Danish ~trأ¦de~, Old Norse ~troأ°a~.
en.trinken: ~trinken~ comes from Middle High German ~trinken~, from Old High German ~trinkan~ (attested since the 8th century), from Proto-West Germanic *~drinkan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drinkanؤ…~. This is the same word as English ~drink~: the two are exact cognates separated only by the High German consonant shift, which regularly converted Proto-Germanic *d to German *t (the "dental" portion of the Second Sound Shift). The semantic identity across all Germanic branches is remarkable â€” Old Saxon ~drinkan~, Old Dutch ~drinkan~, Old English ~drincan~, Old Frisian ~drinka~, Old Norse ~drekka~, and even Gothic ~ًگŒ³ًگچ‚ًگŒ¹ًگŒ²ًگŒ؛ًگŒ°ًگŒ½~ (drigkan) all mean "to drink." No non-Germanic cognate has been securely identified, suggesting the word may be a Germanic innovation, possibly derived from an older root for "drawing liquid." The compound ~ertrinken~ ("to drown") shows how the same root covers both drinking and the lethal overconsumption of water.
en.trocknen: A deadjectival verb from the adjective ~trocken~ ("dry"), formed with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Trocken~ traces to Middle High German ~trucken~, from Old High German ~truckan~, ~trokkan~ ("dried out, parched"), from Proto-West Germanic *~drukn~, from Proto-Germanic *~druknaz~, *~druhnaz~ ("dry"), from Proto-Indo-European *~dت°erاµت°-~ ("to strengthen, become hard or solid"), an extension of *~dت°er-~ ("to hold, hold fast"). The PIE root connects dryness with firmness and solidity, reflecting how drying hardens materials. The form ~trucken~ was originally predominant, but the word eventually standardized with the western variant ~-o-~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~drokno~ ("dry," adverb) and Old English ~gedrycnan~ ("to dry up"). The compound ~Trockenheit~ ("drought, dryness") and ~Wأ¤schetrockner~ ("clothes dryer") are common modern derivatives.
en.trأ¤umen:
From Middle High German ~troumen~ and Old High German ~troumen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~draumijan~, a denominative verb from the noun *~draum~ (whence ~Traum~). The noun derives from Proto-Germanic *~draumaz~, from PIE *~dت°rewgت°-~ ("to deceive, to harm"), suggesting that the ancient Indo-Europeans viewed dreams as fundamentally deceptive experiences. Cognate with Dutch ~dromen~, English ~dream~, and Swedish ~drأ¶mma~.

The connection between dreaming and deception runs deep: the PIE root *~dت°rewgت°-~ also yields Old English ~drؤ“ogan~ ("to endure, to suffer") and Sanskrit ~druh-~ ("to harm"). The Germanic ~Traum~/~dream~ words originally may have meant "apparition" or "phantom" rather than the neutral "sleep vision" we intend today. Curiously, Old English ~drؤ“am~ originally meant "joy, music, revelry" -- it only acquired the sense of "sleep vision" in Middle English, possibly under influence from Old Norse ~draumr~. This means German ~Traum~ and English ~dream~ are etymological cognates that arrived at the same meaning through different semantic paths -- German kept the original sense while English took a detour through "music" before circling back.
en.tun: From Middle High German ~tuon~, from Old High German ~tuon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~dإچnؤ…~, from PIE *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, set, place"). One of the most irregular verbs in German, ~tun~ belongs to the small class of Germanic anomalous verbs that resist classification into strong or weak paradigms. Its Proto-Germanic present tense derives from the PIE perfective *~dت°أ©hâ‚پt~ (though the o-grade *~dت°أ³hâ‚پt~ is unexpected), while its past tense derives from the reduplicated imperfective *~dت°أ©dت°ehâ‚پti~. This dual PIE origin accounts for the verb's highly irregular conjugation. Cognate with English ~do~, Dutch ~doen~, Danish ~gidde~ (archaic).
en.tأ¤tigen:
Derived from the adjective ~tأ¤tig~ ("active") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Tأ¤tig~ itself derives from the noun ~Tat~ ("deed"), from MHG ~tؤپt~, OHG ~tؤپt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dؤپdi~, from Proto-Germanic *~dؤ“diz~, from PIE *~dت°أ©hâ‚پ-tis~ ("a placing, a doing"), built on the root *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, to place, to do"). This is the same root that gives German ~tun~ ("to do") â€” so ~Tat~ and ~tun~ are, at the deepest level, the same word. Cognate with Dutch ~daad~, English ~deed~, Danish ~dأ¥d~, Gothic *~dؤ“أ¾s~, Old Norse ~dأ،أ°~.

The PIE root *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ is one of the most prolific in the Indo-European family. It produced Greek ~tأ­thؤ“mi~ ("I place"), whence ~thأ©sis~ ("a placing, a proposition") and English ~theme~, ~thesis~, ~apothecary~, and ~thesaurus~ (originally "a storehouse" â€” a place where things are put). In Latin, the zero-grade *~dت°hâ‚پ-~ yielded ~facere~ ("to do, to make") via an Italic sound change, giving English ~fact~, ~factory~, ~faculty~, ~affair~, and ~perfect~ (literally "thoroughly done"). Sanskrit ~dأ،dhؤپti~ ("he places") shows the reduplicated present that was the original PIE formation.

The verb ~tأ¤tigen~ is a relatively modern coinage, attested from the 18th century, and belongs to commercial and legal register: ~einen Kauf tأ¤tigen~ ("to make a purchase"), ~eine Investition tأ¤tigen~ ("to make an investment"), ~einen Anruf tأ¤tigen~ ("to make a call"). In everyday speech, Germans strongly prefer simpler alternatives â€” ~kaufen~, ~investieren~, ~anrufen~ â€” making ~tأ¤tigen~ a hallmark of bureaucratic German (~Amtsdeutsch~). The journey from PIE *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to place") through Germanic ~Tat~ ("deed") to the stiff formality of ~eine أœberweisung tأ¤tigen~ ("to execute a bank transfer") is a small history of civilization itself: from the concrete act of placing something with one's hands to the abstract machinery of modern commerce.
en.tأ¶ten:
From Middle High German ~tإ“ten~, ~tإ“den~, from Old High German *~tإچten~, ~tإچden~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daudijan~ ("to make dead, to kill"), from Proto-Germanic *~daudijanؤ…~, a factitive (causative) verb derived from *~daudaz~ ("dead") + *~-janؤ…~ (the factitive suffix meaning "to make X"). Cognate with Dutch ~doden~, Old Norse ~deyأ°a~ (Icelandic ~deyأ°a~), Gothic ~dauأ¾jan~. The surface analysis is transparent: ~tot~ ("dead") + ~-en~, literally "to make dead." Compare English ~deaden~, which follows the same derivation pattern from ~dead~ but has a weaker meaning ("to dull, to muffle" rather than "to kill").

The High German consonant shift transformed the initial ~d~ of the Proto-Germanic root to ~t~: *~daudaz~ became OHG ~tإچt~, hence ~tأ¶ten~ rather than the expected *~dأ¶den~ (which is precisely the Dutch form: ~doden~). In colloquial German, ~umbringen~ is strongly preferred over ~tأ¶ten~; the latter sounds clinical and formal, appropriate for news reports (~zehn Menschen wurden getأ¶tet~, "ten people were killed") but jarring in everyday speech. The word ~Neuntأ¶ter~ ("red-backed shrike," literally "nine-killer") reflects folk belief that this small bird impales nine prey items on thorns before eating â€” a grisly name for a songbird.
en.umbauen:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From Middle High German ~bإ«wen~, from Old High German ~bإ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bإ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~bإ«anؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bت°uH-~ ("to become, to grow, to dwell"). Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different Germanic branch) and, more surprisingly, with English ~be~ (from PIE *~bت°uH-~ in its "to become" sense).
- ~um-~: From Middle High German ~umbe~, from Old High German ~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~, from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚mج¥bت°i~ ("around, on either side of"). As a separable prefix, it conveys the sense of rebuilding or restructuring.

The separable ~UMbauen~ means "to rebuild, to renovate, to remodel" -- literally "to build around" in the sense of restructuring what already exists. The PIE root *~bت°uH-~ is one of the most productive in the Indo-European family, generating ~be~, ~been~, ~booth~, ~bower~, ~build~, ~neighbor~ (literally "near-dweller"), and German ~Bauer~ ("farmer," literally "one who dwells/cultivates").
en.umfassen:
From ~um-~ + ~fassen~. Compound of ~um-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~ ("to fetch, hold"), from Proto-Indo-European *~pod-nأ©hâ‚‚-ti~, from *~pod-~ ("to fetch"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.
- ~um-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚mج¥bت°i~ ("around, on either side of"). This separable prefix in ~umfassen~ is used in its inseparable sense ("to encompass, surround").

The connection between ~fassen~ and English ~fetch~ is one of the more surprising Germanic cognate pairs: both descend from the same PIE root meaning "to get, obtain." ~Umfassen~ â€” literally "to grasp around" â€” developed its abstract sense of "to comprise, encompass, include" via the image of something being held within an encircling grip. The related noun ~Umfang~ ("scope, extent, circumference") shares this spatial metaphor. ~Fassen~ itself has a rich idiomatic life in German: ~sich fassen~ ("to compose oneself"), ~Fassung~ ("composure; version of a text"), and ~unfassbar~ ("incomprehensible, literally ungraspable").
en.umgeben:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~geben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanan~, from PIE *~ghebh-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (though the English form was heavily influenced by Old Norse ~gefa~), Dutch ~geven~, Swedish ~giva~.
- ~um-~: From MHG ~um(b)(e)-~, from OHG ~umbi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~ ("around"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚mbhi~ ("around, on either side of"). Cognate with Latin ~ambi-~ (as in ~ambiguous~, literally "going around both sides") and Greek ~amphi-~ (as in ~amphitheater~).

As an inseparable compound (stressed on ~-ge-~), ~umgeben~ means "to surround," literally "to give around." The metaphor is of encircling something by "giving" or placing oneself all around it. The PIE root *~ghebh-~ has a surprisingly broad semantic range: alongside "to give," it also carried notions of "to grab, to seize" in some branches (compare Latvian ~gabties~, "to snatch," and Old Church Slavonic ~gabati~, "to seize"). The shift between "to give" and "to grab" may reflect different perspectives on the same physical exchange. The participle ~umgeben~ also functions as an adjective meaning "surrounded" (~von Bergen umgeben~, "surrounded by mountains"), one of those German participles that has essentially become a standalone word in its own right.
en.umgehen:
~um-~ + ~gehen~. Compound of ~um-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave").
- ~um-~: From PIE *~hâ‚‚mج¥bت°i~ ("around, on either side of"). The prefix in its separable use adds a sense of going around or circulating; in its inseparable use, a sense of bypassing or circumventing.

~Umgehen~ is one of the classic German separability pairs, where stress placement alone changes meaning. The separable ~umgehen~ (stress on ~um-~) means "to circulate, go around, deal with" â€” as in ~mit jemandem umgehen~ ("to deal with, to treat someone") or a rumor ~geht um~ ("goes around, circulates"). The inseparable ~umgehen~ (stress on ~ge-~) means "to circumvent, bypass, avoid" â€” as in ~ein Gesetz umgehen~ ("to circumvent a law"). Both meanings trace to the same spatial logic of going around something, but the separable form emphasizes circulation while the inseparable emphasizes avoidance. This stress-based distinction â€” inseparable meaning derived from uninterruptible prefix fusion â€” is one of the most productive patterns in German morphology. The PIE root *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave") produces the paradox that "going" originally meant "leaving" â€” direction is always relative to a starting point. Cognate with Dutch ~omgaan~.
en.umsetzen:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set, place down"), from PIE *~sodأ©yeti~. A causative of *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"): literally "to cause to sit."
- ~um-~: From MHG ~um(b)-~, from OHG ~umbi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi-~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚mج¥bت°i~ ("around, on either side of"). This separable prefix adds a sense of turning around, repositioning, or encircling.

The core sense of ~umsetzen~ is "to re-place, set in a new position" â€” moving a plant to a new pot, a player to a new position, or a plan into action (~in die Tat umsetzen~, "to implement"). This last sense, "to implement, realize," developed in Early New High German and is now the dominant meaning in business and administrative German. The financial sense, ~Umsatz~ ("turnover, revenue"), derives from this same root â€” money that has been "turned over," set around in circulation. Cognate with Middle English ~umsetten~ ("to surround, beset").
en.umstellen:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand"). Cognate with English ~stall~ and Dutch ~stellen~.
- ~um-~: From OHG ~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~ ("around"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚mج¥bت°i~ ("around, on either side of").

~Umstellen~ is a textbook example of how German prefix stress determines meaning. With stress on ~um-~ (separable: ~er stellt die Mأ¶bel um~), it means "to rearrange" â€” literally "to place around." With stress on ~-stellen~ (inseparable: ~die Polizei umstellt das Gebأ¤ude~), it means "to surround, to encircle." Same prefix, same root, but different stress patterns yield different semantics, different syntax, and different past participles (~umgestellt~ vs. ~umstellt~).
en.umwandeln:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~wandeln~:

- ~wandeln~: From MHG ~wandeln~, from OHG ~wantalإچn~ ("to change, to wander"), from Proto-West Germanic *~wandalإچn~, a frequentative derived from Proto-Germanic *~wandijanؤ…~ ("to turn, to change"), from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, to wind"). Cognate with English ~wend~ (as in "to wend one's way"), ~wander~, ~wind~ (the verb), Dutch ~wandelen~ ("to walk, to stroll").
- ~um-~: From MHG ~um(be)~, from OHG ~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚mج¥bت°i~ ("around, on either side of"). As a separable prefix here, it conveys the sense of changing something over, transforming.

~Umwandeln~ means "to transform, to convert, to transmute" â€” literally to turn something around, to change it into something else. The noun ~Umwandlung~ ("transformation, conversion") is used in chemistry (~Stoffumwandlung~, "material conversion"), law (~Umwandlung einer Strafe~, "commutation of a sentence"), and biology (~Umwandlung von Energie~, "energy conversion"). The PIE root *~wendت°-~ ("to turn") is one of the great motion roots of Indo-European, producing English ~wind~ (the verb), ~wander~, ~wend~, and through the Gmc tribal name *~Wandilإچz~ ("the wanderers"), the name of the ~Vandals~, the Germanic tribe whose sack of Rome in 455 CE made their name synonymous with destruction.
en.unterbrechen:
From MHG ~underbrأ«chen~, from OHG ~untarbrehhan~, a calque of Latin ~interrumpere~ ("to break between"):

- ~brechen~: From MHG ~brأ«chen~, from OHG ~brأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~brekanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°reg-~ ("to break"). Cognate with English ~break~, Dutch ~breken~, Old Saxon ~brekan~.
- ~unter-~: From PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). In this compound it carries the sense of "between, in the middle of."

~Unterbrechen~ is a rare example of a documented calque â€” a word-for-word translation â€” from Latin into Old High German. Latin ~inter-~ ("between") + ~rumpere~ ("to break") was rendered as OHG ~untar-~ + ~brehhan~, producing a Germanic compound with identical internal structure. The verb means "to interrupt" â€” to break into the middle of something. The noun ~Unterbrechung~ ("interruption") follows naturally. The PIE root *~bت°reg-~ is one of the most broadly preserved roots in the family: it appears in Latin ~frangere~ ("to break," whence English ~fracture~, ~fragment~, and ~fraction~), and it has remained semantically stable â€” meaning "to break" â€” in an unbroken line from PIE through Proto-Germanic to modern German and English. Compare Dutch ~onderbreken~, another parallel calque.
en.unterbringen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°renk-~ ("to bring"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Afrikaans ~bring~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~ ("under, between"), from PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As a separable prefix, it means "under" or "among," here conveying the idea of placing something into shelter or accommodation.

The literal sense is "to bring under (a roof)," i.e., to accommodate, to house, to place somewhere. From this spatial meaning, ~unterbringen~ has developed figurative uses: ~Waren unterbringen~ ("to store goods"), ~ein Kind unterbringen~ ("to place a child," as in foster care), and ~eine Bemerkung unterbringen~ ("to slip in a remark"). The underlying metaphor of bringing something under shelter or into a contained space applies equally to people, objects, and words. English ~bring~ is the direct cognate of ~bringen~, and the two verbs have maintained remarkably parallel meanings for over a millennium. The PIE root *~bت°renk-~ is notable for being one of the few Proto-Indo-European roots whose Germanic reflexes show an anomalous nasal infix that has never been fully explained -- ~bringen~ is simply an etymological puzzle that linguists continue to debate.
en.unterhalten:
From Middle High German ~underhalten~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~, of uncertain PIE origin â€” possibly from *~ل¸±el-~ ("to hide, conceal") or *~kelH-~ ("to rise"). Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~, Icelandic ~halda~, Swedish ~hأ¥lla~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix (as in this verb), it carries a sustaining or supporting sense rather than a purely spatial one.

The verb ~unterhalten~ is notable for bearing two quite different meanings: "to entertain, to converse with" (reflexive ~sich unterhalten~) and "to maintain, to support" (e.g. a family or a building). Both senses derive from the underlying image of "holding something up from below" â€” supporting it. The entertainment sense arose from the idea of sustaining someone's attention or spirits. The noun ~Unterhaltung~ similarly means both "entertainment" and "maintenance" (as in ~Straأںenunterhaltung~ = "road maintenance"). Cognate with Hunsrik ~unnerhalle~. The inseparable prefix distinguishes ~unterhalten~ from separable ~untergehen~ ("to go under, to perish") â€” a morphological contrast that encodes whether ~unter-~ modifies the verb's meaning structurally or merely directionally.
en.unterliegen:
From Middle High German ~unterligen~, from Old High German ~untarliggan~ ("to lie beneath"), equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~liegen~:

- ~liegen~: From MHG ~ligen~, from OHG ~ligen~ (earlier ~liggen~), from Proto-West Germanic *~liggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ligjanؤ…~, from PIE *~legت°-~ ("to lie down").
- ~unter-~: From PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys the sense of being beneath or being subjected to.

~Unterliegen~ means "to be subject to" (~einer Pflicht unterliegen~, "to be subject to an obligation") or "to be defeated, to succumb" (~dem Gegner unterliegen~, "to lose to the opponent"). The physical image is powerful: the one who lies underneath has been pinned, overcome, forced down. This is the exact semantic equivalent of English ~underlie~ (and the related ~succumb~, from Latin ~sub-~ + ~cumbere~, "to lie under"), confirming that the metaphor of spatial position as power relation is deeply rooted in Indo-European thought. Cognate with Dutch ~onderliggen~, English ~underlie~, Gothic ~ًگŒ»ًگŒ¹ًگŒ²ًگŒ°ًگŒ½~.
en.unternehmen:
From Middle High German ~undernأ«men~, from Old High German ~untarnأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~underneman~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~unter-~: From PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, ~unter-~ conveys the sense of support or undertaking from below.

The modern meaning of "to undertake, to attempt, to do" was first attested in the 16th century. The derived noun ~Unternehmen~ ("enterprise, company") and ~Unternehmer~ ("entrepreneur") emerged later, turning the verbal concept of "taking something on from underneath" into the institutional foundations of capitalism. English ~undertake~ is a precise calque: ~under-~ + ~take~ mirrors ~unter-~ + ~nehmen~ exactly, and both languages extended the meaning from "to take upon oneself" to more specialized senses (English ~undertaker~ famously narrowing to the funeral business, while German ~Unternehmer~ stayed with commerce). Cognate with Dutch ~ondernemen~ and obsolete English ~undernim~.
en.unterrichten:
From Middle High German ~underrihten~, from Old High German compound of ~untar-~ + ~rihten~. Equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to right, to straighten, to direct"), related to ~recht~ ("right, correct"). Cognate with English ~right~ (both the adjective and the verb).
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~ ("under, between, among"), from PIE *~ndher~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix, ~unter-~ here conveys a sense of "between" or supportive direction, as in guiding someone.

The literal meaning is something like "to direct (someone) underneath" or "to set right among," which evolved into "to instruct, to teach." The verb ~unterrichten~ is an inseparable compound (stress falls on ~-rich-~, not ~unter-~), distinguishing it from the separable ~unter|richten~ (which does not exist as a standard verb). Cognate with Middle Low German ~underrichten~ and Saterland Frisian ~unnergjuchte~. The semantic connection between "directing" and "teaching" is natural: a teacher directs the understanding of students, setting their knowledge "right." English ~instruct~ follows the same logic, from Latin ~instruere~ ("to build up, to arrange, to inform").
en.unterscheiden:
From Middle High German ~underscheiden~, equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~scheiden~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~scheiden~:

- ~scheiden~: From MHG ~scheiden~, from OHG ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~skey-~ ("to split, dissect"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheiden~, English ~shed~, Ancient Greek ~skhأ­zإچ~ ("to split").
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). In this compound, ~unter-~ does not mean "beneath" but rather "between" â€” the sense of being positioned in the space separating two things, which is the conceptual basis for distinguishing them.

The semantic logic of ~unterscheiden~ â€” "to distinguish, differentiate" â€” mirrors that of ~entscheiden~ ("to decide"): both rely on the metaphor of cutting. Where ~entscheiden~ cuts options apart (deciding between them), ~unterscheiden~ cuts between two things to reveal the gap that separates them. Latin ~discernere~ ("to distinguish") uses the same spatial logic: ~dis-~ ("apart") + ~cernere~ ("to sift, separate"). English ~discern~ and ~discriminate~ belong to the same conceptual family. Cognate with Dutch ~onderscheiden~.
en.unterschreiben:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~schreiben~:

- ~schreiben~: From MHG ~schrؤ«ben~, from OHG ~scrؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrؤ«banؤ…~, an early borrowing from Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~ ("I write"), from PIE *~(s)kreybت°-~ ("to scratch, cut, incise"). Cognate with English ~scribe~, ~shrive~, Dutch ~schrijven~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys "under, beneath."

~Unterschreiben~ means "to sign" (a document) -- literally "to write under." The concept is universal: English ~subscribe~ is a calque from Latin ~subscrؤ«bere~ ("to write under"), Dutch has ~ondertekenen~ ("to sign under"), and French uses ~souscrire~. All preserve the ancient practice of writing one's name beneath the text of a document to indicate consent. The root verb ~schreiben~ is itself a fascinating cultural borrowing: the Germanic peoples had no native word for writing (their runic inscriptions used ~ritzen~, "to scratch"), so when they encountered Roman literacy, they borrowed the Latin verb wholesale. The original PIE root *~(s)kreybت°-~ meant "to scratch" or "to cut" -- a reminder that the earliest writing was carved, not inked.
en.unterstellen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~stellen~, with two distinct etymologies and meanings depending on stress:

- ~stellen~: From Middle High German ~stellen~, from Old High German ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~unter-~: From Middle High German ~unter~, from Old High German ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Indo-European *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As a separable prefix it means "under" literally; as an inseparable prefix it takes on abstract meanings.

The separable ~UNTERstellen~ means "to put underneath, to shelter" (~das Auto unterstellen~, "to put the car under cover"). The inseparable ~unterSTELLen~ has two meanings: "to assume, to suppose" (calqued from Latin ~suppإچnere~, literally "to place under," the same metaphor that gives English ~suppose~) and "to impute, to accuse" (~jemandem etwas unterstellen~, "to accuse someone of something"). Dutch ~veronderstellen~ preserves the same Latin-influenced semantic development.
en.unterstreichen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~streichen~:

- ~streichen~: From MHG ~strichen~, from OHG ~strihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strikan~, from Proto-Germanic *~strikanan~, from PIE *~streyg-~ ("to stroke, to rub, to press"). Cognate with English ~strike~ and Dutch ~strijken~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~ ("under, beneath"), from PIE *~ndher~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys the spatial sense of "underneath."

The literal meaning is "to stroke underneath," which is to say, to draw a line under something, to underline. English ~underline~ and ~underscore~ are exact calques of the same concept. The figurative extension to "to emphasize, to stress" follows naturally from the typographic practice of underlining important text. The base verb ~streichen~ itself has a remarkably wide semantic field: "to stroke, to paint, to spread (butter), to cross out, to cancel, to roam." All these meanings trace back to the basic motion of moving something across a surface. The cognate English ~strike~ shares the physical-motion core but developed its own distinctive range of meanings ("to hit, to strike a match, to go on strike").
en.unterstأ¼tzen:
From Middle High German ~understأ¼zzen~, from Old High German ~untarstuzzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~understuttijan~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~stأ¼tzen~:

- ~stأ¼tzen~: Attested as a simplex only since the 17th century, probably after Middle Low German ~stأ¼tten~ as a back-formation from the inherited compound ~unterstأ¼tzen~ itself. Ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *~stuttijan~. Related to Proto-Germanic *~stuأ¾s~, whence English ~stud~ ("protrusion; post").
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix, ~unter-~ adds a sense of support from below.

The simplex ~stأ¼tzen~ was probably extracted from this compound rather than the reverse: ~unterstأ¼tzen~ is the historically primary form. Cognate with Dutch ~stutten~ ("to support"), and, by borrowing from Low German, Norwegian ~stأ¸tte~, Swedish ~stأ¶tta~.
en.untersuchen: First attested in the 15th century as ~undersuochen~. The existence of an unattested Old High German *~untarsuohhen~ is probable; compare Old Saxon ~undarsإچkian~ (whence Low German ~أ¼nnersأ¶ken~), Old Dutch ~undersuoken~ (whence Dutch ~onderzoeken~), and Old English ~undersecan~ (whence archaic English ~underseek~), all from Proto-West Germanic *~undersإچkijan~ ("to search under or between"). Equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~suchen~. ~Unter-~ derives from PIE *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). ~Suchen~ derives from Old High German ~suohhen~, from Proto-Germanic *~sإچkijanؤ…~, from PIE *~sehâ‚‚g-~ ("to seek out"). The spatial metaphor â€” searching beneath the surface â€” evolved naturally into the modern sense of "to investigate, examine." Dutch ~onderzoeken~, English ~underseek~ (archaic), and Old Saxon ~undarsإچkian~ are direct structural parallels. Cognate with Dutch ~onderzoeken~, Low German ~أ¼nnersأ¶ken~.
en.unterzeichnen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, symbol"), from Middle High German ~zeichen~, from Old High German ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taiknؤ…~ ("sign"), from Proto-Indo-European *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show"). Cognate with English ~token~ and Dutch ~teken~.
- ~unter-~: From Middle High German ~unter~, from Old High German ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Indo-European *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath"). Here the prefix literally means "under" -- one writes one's name under the text of a document.

~Unterzeichnen~ ("to sign") is always inseparable and carries a formal register, used for treaties, contracts, and official documents. The verb literally means "to draw beneath" -- to place one's mark under the text. Compare English ~undersign~ and the related noun ~Unterzeichner~ ("signatory"). The PIE root *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show") also produced Latin ~dؤ«cere~ ("to say," originally "to point out"), Greek ~deiknynai~ ("to show"), and English ~teach~ (originally "to show").
en.unterziehen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From Middle High German ~ziehen~, from Old High German ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with obsolete English ~tee~ ("to draw, lead"), Gothic ~tiuhan~, and from the same PIE root as Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead"), which gave English ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, and ~educate~.
- ~unter-~: From Middle High German ~unter~, from Old High German ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Indo-European *~nج¥dت°أ©r~ ("under, beneath").

The separable ~UNTERziehen~ means "to put on underneath" (clothing). The inseparable ~unterZIEHen~ means "to subject to" or reflexively ~sich unterziehen~ ("to undergo"), as in ~sich einer Operation unterziehen~ ("to undergo surgery"). The metaphor of being "pulled under" something captures the sense of submitting to a process. The PIE root *~dewk-~ is remarkably productive: through Latin alone it generated ~duct~, ~duke~, ~educate~, ~deduce~, ~introduce~, ~reduce~, and ~seduce~.
en.variieren:
Borrowed from French ~varier~, from Latin ~variؤپre~ ("to change, to make different, to diversify"), from ~varius~ ("diverse, variegated, changeable"), from PIE *~wehâ‚پ-ro-~ ("bent, crooked") or possibly *~wehâ‚‚-ro-~. The word entered German in the 16th century.

Latin ~varius~ originally described something spotted or multicolored â€” a surface with contrasting patches â€” before generalizing to "diverse, changeable." The same root produced English ~vary~, ~various~, ~variety~, and ~varicose~ (veins that are "bent" or "twisted"). ~Variieren~ in German means "to vary, to modify, to diversify" and is used in both everyday and technical contexts: recipes ~variieren~, opinions ~variieren~, and musical themes ~variieren~ (the ~Variation~ being a fundamental form in classical music). Bach's ~Goldberg-Variationen~, composed in Leipzig, gave the word particular cultural resonance in German-speaking lands. The derived noun ~Variante~ ("variant") and the adjective ~variabel~ ("variable") entered German through the same Latin-French channel.
en.verabschieden:
From ~ver-~ + ~Abschied~ + ~-en~. A denominal verb formed from ~Abschied~ ("farewell, parting"):

- ~Abschied~: From MHG ~abeschit~, equivalent to ~ab-~ ("away, off," from PIE *~hâ‚‚epأ³~) + ~Schied~ ("separation, divorce"), from ~scheiden~ ("to separate"), from OHG ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~skeyd-~ ("to split, to separate"). The same root as ~scheitern~ above.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completeness or formality to the act.

~Verabschieden~ is a double-prefix construction (~ver-~ + ~ab-~) built on a noun rather than directly on the verb ~scheiden~. It means "to say goodbye to" (reflexive: ~sich verabschieden~), "to see off, to dismiss," and â€” in parliamentary usage â€” "to pass, to adopt (a law)." The parliamentary sense is the most surprising: when the Bundestag ~verabschiedet ein Gesetz~, it "sends the law on its way" â€” the legislative body bids farewell to the bill as it leaves the chamber and enters the world as binding law. The reflexive ~sich aus der Politik verabschieden~ ("to take leave of politics") preserves the original farewell sense in a formal register. The root verb ~scheiden~ is cognate with English ~shed~ (to separate, to cast off), Greek ~دƒد‡خ¯خ¶د‰~ ("to split," whence ~schism~), and Lithuanian ~skأ­esti~ ("to separate").
en.verankern:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~ankern~:

- ~ankern~: From Middle High German ~ankern~, a denominative verb from the noun ~Anker~ ("anchor"). ~Anker~ traces to Old High German ~anker~, ~ankero~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ankurإچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~ankurأ´~. The Germanic word was borrowed from Latin ~ancora~ (also spelled ~anchora~), itself from Greek ~ankyra~ ("anchor"), related to ~ankos~ ("bend, hook"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚enk-~ ("to bend").
- ~ver-~: From Middle High German ~ver-~, from Old High German ~far-~, ~fir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Indo-European *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or intensification.

~Verankern~ means "to anchor firmly" both literally (a ship) and figuratively (a principle in a constitution: ~in der Verfassung verankert~, "enshrined in the constitution"). The ~ver-~ prefix intensifies the anchoring, emphasizing permanence. The PIE root *~hâ‚‚enk-~ ("to bend") also produced Latin ~angulus~ ("angle, corner"), English ~angle~ and ~ankle~, and German ~Angelhaken~ ("fishhook") -- all reflecting the concept of a bent or hooked shape.
en.veranlassen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~Anlass~ + ~-en~:

- ~Anlass~: From MHG ~anelؤپz~, ~anlؤپz~, a deverbal noun from ~anlassen~ ("to start, set going"), literally "a letting-on." The verb ~lassen~ descends from MHG ~lؤپzen~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with English ~let~ and Dutch ~laten~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a causative, completive sense â€” bringing about a cause.

~Veranlassen~ means "to cause, to prompt, to arrange for something to happen" â€” an elegant three-layered word. At its core is ~lassen~ ("to let"), prefixed with ~an-~ to form ~Anlass~ ("occasion, cause" â€” literally "a letting-on"), then further prefixed with ~ver-~ to create a causative verb: "to create the occasion for." The noun ~Veranlassung~ ("instigation, cause") appears frequently in official correspondence: ~auf Veranlassung des Ministers~ ("at the instigation of the minister"). The PIE root *~lehâ‚پd-~ has a satisfying doublet in English: ~let~ (Germanic path) and ~late~ (from the same root via the adjective meaning "slow, tardy" â€” one who has been "left behind").
en.veranstalten:
From ~ver-~ + ~Anstalt~ + ~-en~, attested from the early 18th century. Compound of ~ver-~ + the denominal verb derived from ~Anstalt~:

- ~Anstalt~: From MHG ~anstalt~, a deverbal noun from ~anstellen~ ("to set up, arrange"), itself from ~an-~ + ~stellen~. ~Stellen~: from MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness and completion â€” to bring an arrangement fully into being.

~Veranstalten~ means "to organize, to arrange, to stage" â€” exclusively for events and occasions: concerts, festivals, competitions, demonstrations. One ~veranstaltet~ a wedding reception but not a bookshelf. The word is structurally layered: ~stellen~ ("to place") became ~anstellen~ ("to set up") became ~Anstalt~ ("institution, arrangement") became ~veranstalten~ ("to arrange an event") â€” each derivation adding a level of organizational complexity. The derived nouns ~Veranstalter~ ("organizer") and ~Veranstaltung~ ("event") are ubiquitous in German cultural life. ~Anstalt~ itself followed a separate semantic path from "arrangement" to "institution," particularly ~Irrenanstalt~ ("insane asylum") and ~Strafanstalt~ ("penal institution"), giving it a darker coloring that ~veranstalten~ has entirely escaped. The colloquial accusation ~was veranstaltest du fأ¼r einen Lأ¤rm?~ ("what kind of racket are you staging?") adds a note of mock-theatrical indignation.
en.verarbeiten:
From ~ver-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From OHG ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijanؤ…~, ultimately from PIE *~hâ‚ƒأ³rbت°os~ ("orphan"). The semantic arc from "orphan" through "hardship" to "labor" reflects the lot of the fatherless in ancient society â€” whose work was the defining condition of their existence. Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, English ~orphan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a thoroughgoing, transformative sense â€” working something all the way through until it is processed into a new state.

The prefix ~ver-~ applied to ~arbeiten~ produces a verb meaning "to process, to work through, to come to terms with." Material is ~verarbeitet~ when it is transformed into a product; grief or trauma is ~verarbeitet~ when one has worked through it emotionally â€” a usage that would have been familiar to Freud, for whom the German term for psychological processing was literally the verb for industrial transformation. The metaphor of the factory and the metaphor of mourning share the same word. Cognate with Dutch ~verwerken~.
en.verbauen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~bإ«wen~, from OHG ~bإ«an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bإ«an~, from Proto-Germanic *~bإ«anؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°uH-~ ("to be, to grow, to dwell"). Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different derivation), Dutch ~bouwen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, *~fur-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). As a verbal prefix, it can mean "away" (consuming), "wrongly" (spoiling), or "completion."

The prefix ~ver-~ gives ~verbauen~ two distinct senses, both involving the "using up" or "spoiling" meanings of the prefix. First, "to use up in building" â€” ~das ganze Geld verbauen~ ("to spend all the money on construction"). Second, and more commonly, "to block or obstruct by building" â€” ~jemandem die Aussicht verbauen~ ("to block someone's view by building"). The figurative extension ~sich die Zukunft verbauen~ ("to ruin one's future," literally "to build over one's future") is especially vivid: the image is of someone erecting walls that block their own path forward. A third sense, "to build incorrectly" (~ein schlecht verbautes Haus~), draws on the ~ver-~ prefix's "wrongly" meaning. This semantic range â€” consuming, obstructing, and botching â€” illustrates why ~ver-~ is often called the most versatile and unpredictable of all German prefixes.
en.verbergen:
From Middle High German ~verbergen~, from Old High German ~firbergan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frabergan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~bergen~:

- ~bergen~: From MHG ~bأ«rgen~, from OHG ~bergan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bergan~, from Proto-Germanic *~berganؤ…~ ("to take care of, preserve, protect"), from PIE *~bت°ergت°-~ ("to take care of, protect, preserve"). Cognate with Old English ~forbeorgan~ ("to forbear, refrain"), Dutch ~verbergen~ ("to hide").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb's sense of concealment.

The base verb ~bergen~ has a fascinating semantic range: "to recover, to salvage, to rescue." It is the word used for recovering bodies from wreckage, salvaging a sunken ship, or rescuing mountaineers. The noun ~Burg~ ("castle, fortress") is a relative, from Proto-Germanic *~burgaz~ -- a place that shelters and protects. ~Verbergen~ takes the protective sense of ~bergen~ and turns it inward: to shelter something from sight, to conceal. The past participle ~verborgen~ ("hidden, secret") has become a common adjective in its own right.
en.verbessern:
From ~ver-~ + ~bessern~:

- ~bessern~: A denominative from the adjective ~besser~ ("better"), from MHG ~beب¥ب¥er~, from OHG ~beب¥ب¥iro~, from Proto-West Germanic *~batiت€إچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~batizأ´~ (the comparative of *~bataz~, "good"), from PIE *~bت°ed-~ ("good"). Cognate with English ~better~, Dutch ~beter~, Sanskrit ~bhadrأ،~ ("blessed, fortunate, good").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive sense â€” improving something all the way, bringing it to a better state.

The chain runs from PIE *~bت°ed-~ ("good") through Proto-Germanic's comparative form *~batizأ´~ ("better"), to OHG ~beب¥ب¥iro~, to ~besser~, to the denominative verb ~bessern~ ("to better, to improve"), and finally to ~verbessern~ with the completive ~ver-~ prefix driving it toward "to improve thoroughly, to correct." In a pedagogical context, ~verbessern~ is the standard word for correcting a student's work â€” a teacher ~verbessert~ an essay. The Sanskrit cognate ~bhadrأ،~ ("fortunate, good") is a reminder that the PIE root for goodness spread from the steppes to the subcontinent. Cognate with Dutch ~verbeteren~.
en.verbieten:
From Middle High German ~verbieten~, from Old High German ~farbiotan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~bieten~:

- ~bieten~: From MHG ~bieten~, from OHG ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ewdت°-~ ("to be aware, to make aware").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here reverses or negates the action of offering â€” what is offered is now forbidden.

~Verbieten~ means "to forbid, to prohibit, to ban." The semantic relationship between ~bieten~ ("to offer") and ~verbieten~ ("to forbid") is one of German's most elegant prefix transformations: the ~ver-~ prefix negates the offering, turning "I present this to you" into "I withhold this from you." The derived noun ~Verbot~ ("prohibition, ban") is formed with the ~-t~ suffix (compare ~Gebot~, "commandment," from ~gebieten~) and is ubiquitous in German public life â€” ~Rauchverbot~ ("smoking ban"), ~Fahrverbot~ ("driving ban"), ~Verbotsschild~ ("prohibition sign"). The English cognate ~forbid~ (from Old English ~forbؤ“odan~) is a perfect structural parallel: ~for-~ corresponds to ~ver-~, and ~bid~ to ~bieten~, both from the same PGmc *~beudanؤ…~. So ~verbieten~ and ~forbid~ are not merely similar â€” they are the same compound, independently preserved in two sister languages. Cognate with Dutch ~verbieden~, English ~forbid~.
en.verbinden:
From Middle High German ~verbinden~, from Old High German ~firbintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frabindan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~binden~:

- ~binden~: From MHG ~binden~, from OHG ~bintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bindan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bindanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°endت°-~ ("to bind, tie"). A class III strong verb with ablaut ~binden~, ~band~, ~gebunden~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or intensive sense â€” binding something thoroughly or together.

The ~ver-~ prefix amplifies the root sense of binding into "to connect, unite, link" and, in a medical context, "to bandage." The noun ~Verband~ ("association, bandage, compound") derives from the same compound. English ~band~, ~bond~, and ~bind~ all descend from the same PIE *~bت°endت°-~, as does Sanskrit ~bandhana~ ("binding, bond") and Latin ~offendimentum~ ("chin-strap"). The cognate Old English ~forbindan~ ("to bind up, bandage") followed nearly the same compounding strategy. Cognate with Dutch ~verbinden~, Old English ~forbindan~, Sanskrit ~bandhana~.
en.verbleiben:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~bleiben~:

- ~bleiben~: From MHG ~blؤ«ben~, ~belؤ«ben~ ("to remain, stay"), from OHG ~bilؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~bilؤ«banؤ…~ ("to remain"), from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to stick, to adhere"). Cognate with Dutch ~blijven~ ("to remain"), archaic English ~belive~ ("to remain, stay").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of continuity and formality â€” remaining in a fixed state.

~Verbleiben~ is the formal, administrative cousin of ~bleiben~. While ~bleiben~ is the everyday word for "to stay," ~verbleiben~ appears in official contexts: ~im Amt verbleiben~ ("to remain in office"), ~Wir verbleiben mit freundlichen Grأ¼أںen~ ("We remain with friendly greetings" â€” a formal letter closing). The noun ~Verbleib~ ("whereabouts") is standard in missing-person reports. The PIE root *~leyp-~ ("to stick, adhere") also produced Greek ~lأ­pos~ ("grease, fat") and English ~life~ (via Proto-Germanic *~lؤ«bؤ…~, originally "that which persists, continued existence"). The semantic chain from "to stick" to "to remain" to "to stay alive" is beautifully logical.
en.verbrauchen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~brauchen~:

- ~brauchen~: From MHG ~brإ«chen~, from OHG ~brإ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brإ«kan~ ("to use, employ, consume"), from Proto-Germanic *~brإ«kanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°ruHg-~ ("to make use of, to enjoy"). Cognate with English ~brook~ ("to tolerate, endure") and Dutch ~bruiken~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, *~fur-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it carries the sense of "away, through, completely" â€” using something until it is gone.

The compound means "to consume, to use up, to expend" â€” ~Strom verbrauchen~ ("to consume electricity"), ~Benzin verbrauchen~ ("to use up gasoline"). The ~ver-~ prefix pushes ~brauchen~ ("to need, to use") past mere usage into complete consumption: what is ~verbraucht~ is spent, exhausted, used up. The English cognate ~brook~ (as in "I will not brook any interference") has narrowed dramatically from "to use" to "to tolerate," surviving mainly in literary English. The noun ~Verbraucher~ ("consumer") is the standard German term in economics and law, parallel to ~Verbraucherschutz~ ("consumer protection"). The PIE root *~bت°ruHg-~ is distinct from the similar-looking *~bت°ewg-~ ("to enjoy, to bend"), though both involve a sense of deriving benefit â€” a coincidence that has confused more than one etymologist.
en.verbreiten:
From ~ver-~ + ~breiten~, a deadjectival verb from ~breit~ ("broad, wide"):

- ~breiten~: From the adjective ~breit~, from MHG ~breit~, from OHG ~breit~, from Proto-West Germanic *~braid~, from Proto-Germanic *~braidaz~ ("broad, wide"). Cognate with English ~broad~, Dutch ~breed~, Swedish ~bred~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of spreading outward or making widely known.

~Verbreiten~ means "to spread, to disseminate, to propagate" â€” etymologically, "to make broad." The reflexive ~sich verbreiten~ ("to spread oneself") is used for diseases, rumors, species, and ideas â€” anything that extends its range. The derived noun ~Verbreitung~ ("distribution, spread, prevalence") is standard in scientific and media contexts. German ~breit~ and English ~broad~ are direct cognates from PGmc *~braidaz~, though they have diverged phonologically: the German diphthong ~ei~ (from earlier ~ai~) corresponds to English ~oa~ (from earlier ~ؤپ~). The adjective ~verbreitet~ ("widespread, common") functions as a participial adjective â€” ~weit verbreitet~ ("widely spread") is one of German's most common collocations. Cognate with Dutch ~verbreiden~.
en.verbrennen:
From Middle High German ~verbrennen~, from Old High German ~firbrinnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frabrinnan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~brennen~:

- ~brennen~: From MHG ~brennen~, a merger of two OHG verbs: ~brennan~ ("to set on fire," transitive) from Proto-West Germanic *~brannijan~, and ~brinnan~ ("to be on fire," intransitive) from *~brinnan~. The transitive *~brannijan~ was the causative of *~brinnan~, both ultimately from PIE *~bت°rewhâ‚پ-~ ("to boil, brew"), making ~brennen~ an unexpected cousin of English ~brew~ and ~broth~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of complete destruction -- burning something up entirely.

The archaic English cognate ~forburn~ ("to burn up completely") preserves the same prefix-plus-verb pattern, though it fell out of use centuries ago. Cognate with Dutch ~verbranden~. The completive sense of ~ver-~ in ~verbrennen~ is the same one found in ~verbrauchen~ ("to use up") and ~verschwinden~ ("to vanish") -- things that are gone for good.
en.verbringen:
From Middle High German ~verbringen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringanؤ…~, from PIE *~bت°renk-~ ("to bring").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of completion or passing through a duration.

~Verbringen~ means almost exclusively "to spend (time)" â€” ~den Sommer am Meer verbringen~ ("to spend the summer at the sea"). The ~ver-~ prefix transforms the physical act of bringing into a temporal one: time is "brought through" to completion. This is one of the cases where ~ver-~ functions as a perfectivizing prefix, marking the passage of a duration from beginning to end. The word is notably more formal than the colloquial alternative ~Zeit totschlagen~ ("to kill time"), and it carries no negative connotation â€” one ~verbringt~ time pleasantly or productively. Cognate with Dutch ~doorbrengen~ (a calque using ~door-~ instead of ~ver-~).
en.verdanken:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~danken~:

- ~danken~: From MHG ~danken~, from OHG ~dankإچn~ ("to thank"), from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾ankإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankإچnؤ…~, derived from *~أ¾ankaz~ ("thought, gratitude"). Cognate with English ~thank~, Dutch ~danken~, Danish ~takke~, Old English ~أ¾ancian~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). In this compound, the prefix shifts the direction of gratitude â€” not thanking someone directly, but owing something to someone.

~Verdanken~ means "to owe, to have to thank for" â€” ~Das verdanke ich dir~ ("I owe that to you," "I have you to thank for that"). Unlike ~danken~ ("to thank"), which is the active expression of gratitude, ~verdanken~ describes the relationship of indebtedness: it identifies the source to which something is owed. The PIE root *~teng-~ ("to think, to feel") links thinking and thanking in a profound way: to thank someone is literally to think of them, to hold them in mind. This thought-gratitude connection is perfectly preserved in English, where ~think~ and ~thank~ are doublets from the same root. The German noun ~Dank~ ("thanks, gratitude") and ~Gedanke~ ("thought") are likewise siblings, both descending from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ankaz~.
en.verdeutlichen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~deutlich~ + ~-en~:

- ~deutlich~: From MHG ~diuticlؤ«che~ (adverb), from ~diuten~ ("to interpret, explain"), from OHG ~diuten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾iudijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾iudijanؤ…~, derived from *~أ¾eudإچ~ ("people"). Cognate with Old English ~أ¾ؤ“od~ ("people, nation") and English ~Dutch~ (originally meaning simply "of the people").
- ~ver-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it functions as a causative intensifier, meaning "to make (something) so."

~Verdeutlichen~ means "to clarify, to make clear." The deep history of ~deutlich~ is remarkable: its ancestor *~أ¾eudإچ~ meant "people," and the derived verb *~أ¾iudijanؤ…~ meant "to make popular, to make accessible to the people" -- hence "to interpret, to explain in plain language." Over centuries, "explained for ordinary folk" became simply "clear, plain." The prefix ~ver-~ intensifies this into "to make clear." English ~Dutch~ and German ~deutsch~ are the same word: both originally meant "of the people, vernacular," as opposed to Latin, the language of the clergy.
en.verdienen:
From MHG ~verdienen~, from OHG ~firdionإچn~, ~firthiniإچn~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~dienen~, and a calque of Latin ~deservio~:

- ~dienen~: From MHG ~dienen~, from OHG ~dionإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~أ¾iunإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ewaz~ ("servant, bondsman"), from PIE *~tekت·-~ ("to run, flee"). Cognate with Old English ~أ¾ؤ“ow~ ("servant"), Gothic ~أ¾ius~ ("servant").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix strengthens the root toward completion â€” serving all the way through, thereby earning.

The fact that ~verdienen~ is a documented calque of Latin ~deservio~ ("to serve fully, to deserve") is linguistically remarkable. Latin ~deservio~ is itself built from ~de-~ ("thoroughly") + ~servio~ ("to serve") â€” the same structural logic as ~ver-~ + ~dienen~. Both languages independently hit upon the same conceptual move: to earn or deserve something is to have served for it completely. The modern German word carries both senses â€” ~verdienen~ means both "to earn" (money) and "to deserve" (merit) â€” just as English ~deserve~ preserves only the merit sense while ~earn~ covers the financial one. Cognate with Dutch ~verdienen~.
en.vereinbaren:
From Middle High German ~vereinbأ¦ren~, analyzable as ~ver-~ + ~ein~ + ~-bar~ + ~-en~:

- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here conveys completion or thoroughness â€” making something fully ~ein~ ("one").
- ~ein~: From MHG ~ein~, from OHG ~ein~, from Proto-Germanic *~ainaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚أ³ynos~ ("one"). Cognate with English ~one~, Dutch ~een~, Latin ~إ«nus~.
- ~-bar~: An adjectival suffix meaning "able to be, capable of" (compare English ~-able~).
- ~-en~: The verbal infinitive suffix.

The morphological logic is exquisite: to ~vereinbaren~ is literally "to make-one-able" â€” to bring multiple parties or positions into unity, to make agreement possible. The word means "to agree upon, to arrange, to reconcile" and is the standard verb for scheduling appointments (~einen Termin vereinbaren~), reaching agreements (~eine Regelung vereinbaren~), and reconciling contradictions (~diese Aussagen lassen sich schwer vereinbaren~, "these statements are hard to reconcile"). The derived noun ~Vereinbarung~ ("agreement, arrangement") is ubiquitous in legal and business German. The related verb ~verlautbaren~ ("to announce, to make public") follows an identical formation pattern: ~ver-~ + ~laut~ + ~-bar~ + ~-en~.
en.vereinen:
~ver-~ + ~einen~. From ~ver-~ + ~ein~ + ~-en~:

- ~ein~: From MHG ~ein~, from OHG ~ein~ ("one"), from Proto-Germanic *~ainaz~, from PIE *~hâ‚پأ³ynos~ ("one"). Cognate with English ~one~, Dutch ~een~, Latin ~إ«nus~ (from the same PIE root via Italic).
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness and completion â€” to make fully one.

~Vereinen~ means "to unite, to join together, to combine." The word achieved its greatest rhetorical weight in the phrase ~die Vereinten Nationen~ ("the United Nations") and in the historic context of German reunification: ~das vereinte Deutschland~ ("united Germany"). The closely related verb ~vereinigen~ â€” built with the same components but an additional ~-ig-~ suffix â€” is nearly synonymous but tends toward more abstract or political usage (~Wiedervereinigung~, "reunification"). The derived noun ~Verein~ ("club, association, society") is one of the most characteristically German institutions: from sports clubs to choral societies to rabbit-breeding associations, the ~Verein~ is the organizing principle of German civic life. The wry observation ~die Deutschen grأ¼nden einen Verein~ ("Germans found a club") whenever two or more share a hobby captures something real about German associational culture. The PIE root *~hâ‚پأ³ynos~ ("one") ultimately gives both German ~ein~ and Latin ~إ«nus~, making ~vereinen~ and English ~unite~ (from Latin ~unؤ«re~) etymological cousins expressing the same idea through entirely different derivational paths.
en.vereinfachen:
A denominative verb formed with ~ver-~ + ~einfach~ + ~-en~:

- ~einfach~: From ~ein-~ ("one") + ~-fach~ ("fold"), literally "onefold" -- compare English ~onefold~ and ~simple~ (from Latin ~simplex~, also literally "one-fold").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix functions as a verbalizer, turning an adjective into a verb meaning "to make [adjective]."

The ~ver-~ prefix in its verbalizing role appears across dozens of German adjectives: ~vereinfachen~ ("to simplify"), ~vergrأ¶أںern~ ("to enlarge"), ~verbessern~ ("to improve"). The underlying adjective ~einfach~ entered German in the 16th century, modeled on Latin ~simplex~, and ~vereinfachen~ followed naturally. The English cognate of ~-fach~ survives in ~manifold~, ~twofold~, and ~threefold~.
en.verfassen:
From Middle High German ~verfazzen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~ ("to fetch, hold"), from PIE *~pod-~ ("to fetch"), itself possibly related to *~ped-~ ("to walk, step"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix can indicate completion, transformation, or the result of an action.

The semantic journey from "to grasp, seize" (~fassen~) to "to compose, write" (~verfassen~) runs through the intermediate sense of "to put into a form" -- one still visible in ~fassen~ itself, which can mean "to formulate, express." The ~ver-~ prefix adds completive force, so ~verfassen~ is roughly "to fully formulate" and hence "to author, compose, draft." The derivative ~Verfassung~ means both "constitution" (a nation's foundational document) and "state, condition" (one's physical or mental constitution) -- the same word for a country's governing text and for one's personal well-being.
en.verfolgen:
From ~ver-~ + ~folgen~, a calque of Latin ~persequor~:

- ~folgen~: From MHG ~volgen~, from OHG ~folgؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fulgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~fulgؤپnؤ…~. Cognate with English ~follow~, Dutch ~volgen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds persistent, forward direction â€” following relentlessly onward.

The calque of Latin ~persequor~ â€” itself ~per-~ ("through, thoroughly") + ~sequor~ ("to follow") â€” shows the same structural thinking as ~ver-~ + ~folgen~. In English, the Latin-derived descendant is ~persecute~ (via the past-participial stem ~persecutus~), which has narrowed to its most extreme sense; German ~verfolgen~ covers the full range from "to pursue" (a goal) to "to follow" (a story) to "to prosecute" (in law) to "to persecute" (historically). The historical weight of ~verfolgen~ in German is thus considerable: the noun ~Verfolgung~ ("pursuit, persecution") carries the legacy of political and religious persecution across centuries. Cognate with Dutch ~vervolgen~, Swedish ~fأ¶rfأ¶lja~.
en.verfأ¼gen:
From Middle High German ~vervأ¼egen~, ~ferfأ¼egen~, from ~ver-~ + ~fأ¼gen~:

- ~fأ¼gen~: From MHG ~fأ¼egen~, ~fuogen~, from OHG ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fإچgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fإچgijanؤ…~, from PIE *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to join, attach"). Cognate with Dutch ~voegen~, and distantly with English ~fair~ (from the same PIE root via a different branch), Latin ~pؤپx~ ("peace"), and English ~peace~ and ~pact~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, wrongly, or completion.

The compound ~verfأ¼gen~ developed two distinct senses from the core meaning of "to join, arrange completely": "to decree, to order" (arranging something officially into place) and â€” as ~verfأ¼gen أ¼ber~ â€” "to have at one's disposal" (having something joined to one's hand, within reach). The PIE root *~pehâ‚‚ل¸±-~ ("to join") also underlies English ~fay~ (archaic, "to join two surfaces perfectly") and the peace/pact cluster through Latin. Cognate with German Low German ~verfأ¶gen~, Dutch ~vervoegen~, Danish ~forfأ¸je~, Swedish ~fأ¶rfoga~.
en.vergeben:
From Middle High German ~vergeben~, from Old High German ~fargeban~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of giving away completely or giving over.
