~Vergeben~ means both "to forgive" (~jemandem vergeben~, "to forgive someone") and "to award, to assign" (~einen Auftrag vergeben~, "to award a contract," ~eine Stelle ist schon vergeben~, "the position is already taken"). The forgiveness sense derives from the idea of giving something away completely â€” releasing the debt, surrendering the grievance. The awarding sense derives from the idea of giving something out, distributing it. The derived noun ~Vergebung~ ("forgiveness") is elevated and theological, while ~Vergabe~ ("allocation, awarding") is administrative. English ~forgive~ (from Old English ~forؤ،iefan~) is a structural cognate: ~for-~ corresponds to ~ver-~, and ~give~ to ~geben~, both from PGmc *~gebanؤ…~. The parallel is exact â€” ~vergeben~ and ~forgive~ are the same compound word preserved independently in two branches of West Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~vergeven~, English ~forgive~.
en.vergehen:
From Middle High German ~vergؤپn~, ~vergؤپngؤ“n~, from Old High German ~firgؤپn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fragؤپn~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with English ~go~, Dutch ~gaan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it conveys the sense of passing away, disappearing.

The literal sense is "to go away completely," yielding "to pass (of time), to fade, to expire." The past participle ~vergangen~ is used as an adjective meaning "past" (~die vergangene Woche~, "last week"), and the noun ~Vergangenheit~ means "the past" -- literally "the state of having gone away." The verb also has a darker reflexive use: ~sich an jemandem vergehen~ means "to commit an offense against someone," where "going away" from proper conduct becomes transgression. English preserves a striking parallel in ~to pass away~ (to die) and ~the past~ (what has gone), showing that both Germanic languages independently developed this metaphor of time as motion. The ~ver-~ prefix here works in its "away, to disappearance" sense, the same one found in ~verschwinden~ ("to vanish"), ~verlieren~ ("to lose"), and ~vergessen~ ("to forget").
en.vergessen:
From Middle High German ~vergأ«ب¥ب¥en~, from Old High German ~firgأ«ب¥ب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fragetan~ ("to give up, forget"). Compound of ~ver-~ + the extinct root *~getan~:

- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of losing or going wrong â€” "seizing wrongly" â†’ "losing one's grip on."
- *~getan~: From Proto-Germanic *~getanؤ…~ ("to find a way, attain, acquire"), from PIE *~gت°e(n)d-~ ("to take, seize"). Cognate with Latin ~prehendإچ~ ("to snatch, seize"), Ancient Greek ~د‡خ±خ½خ´خ¬خ½د‰~ (~khandأ،nإچ~, "to hold, contain"). The simplex is extinct in modern German â€” it survives only inside compounds.

The original sense of *~fragetanؤ…~ was "to give up one's grasp on, release." From "releasing grip" â†’ "losing hold" â†’ "forgetting" is a natural semantic slide. What makes ~vergessen~ a superb teaching example is that English ~forget~ follows the identical path: from Old English ~forؤ،ietan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fragetan~ â€” the same compound, the same PIE root, the same metaphor. The German prefix ~ver-~ and the English prefix ~for-~ are cognates (both from *~fra-~); the root *~getan~ is the ancestor of the English dialectal ~yet~ and archaic ~get~ in the sense of "to reach, find." Cognate with Dutch ~vergeten~, West Frisian ~fergette~, English ~forget~.
en.vergleichen:
From Middle High German ~verglؤ«chen~, from Old High German ~firgalؤ«hhإچn~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~gleichen~:

- ~gleichen~: From MHG ~gelؤ«chen~, from OHG ~gilؤ«hhإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~galؤ«kإچnؤ…~, a denominative of *~galؤ«kaz~ ("having the same form or body"), from *~ga-~ (collective prefix) + *~-lؤ«kaz~ ("body, form"), from PIE *~leyg-~ ("image, likeness, similarity"). Cognate with English ~like~ (verb), Dutch ~lijken~, Swedish ~lika~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completive and comparative force â€” bringing a likening all the way through, converting resemblance into systematic analysis.

The PIE root *~leyg-~ ("image, likeness") gave both German ~gleich~ ("same, equal") and English ~like~. Old English ~lؤ«cian~ ("to please, resemble") is the same verb as ~gleichen~, but the two diverged over time: English ~like~ shifted toward approval and pleasure while German ~gleichen~ stayed with resemblance. ~Vergleichen~ with its completive ~ver-~ prefix became the word for deliberate comparison â€” not merely noticing similarity but measuring it. The legal noun ~Vergleich~ ("comparison; out-of-court settlement") reflects this: parties to a dispute are ~verglichen~, brought into formal equivalence and resolved. Cognate with Dutch ~vergelijken~, English ~like~.
en.vergrأ¶أںern:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~grأ¶أںer~ (comparative of ~groأں~) + ~-n~:

- ~groأں~: From MHG ~grإچب¥~, from OHG ~grإچب¥~ ("big, thick, coarse"), from Proto-Germanic *~grautaz~, from PIE *~gت°rewd-~ ("to grind, crush"), from *~gت°er-~ ("to rub, grind"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~grإچt~, Dutch ~groot~, English ~great~.
- ~ver-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it functions as a causative intensifier: "to make (more so)."

~Vergrأ¶أںern~ means "to enlarge, to magnify, to increase." Like ~nأ¤hern~ (from the comparative ~nأ¤her~), it is built from a comparative form: ~grأ¶أںer~ ("bigger") becomes a verb meaning "to make bigger." The underlying adjective ~groأں~ has a surprising origin in PIE *~gت°rewd-~ ("to grind"): the original sense was "coarse, coarse-grained" (as in ground material), which shifted to "thick, large, great." English ~great~ is the cognate, and its semantic journey parallels the German: from "coarse, thick" to "big" to "important, magnificent." The related German words ~Grieأں~ ("semolina"), ~Grأ¼tze~ ("groats"), and ~grob~ ("coarse") all preserve the original grinding sense.
en.verhalten:
From Middle High German ~verhalten~, from Old High German ~firhaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frahaldan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldanؤ…~, from PIE *~kel-~ ("to drive, to herd").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of holding something in check or directing one's manner of holding.

The reflexive ~sich verhalten~ ("to behave, to conduct oneself") is the dominant modern usage â€” literally "to hold oneself in a particular way." The impersonal ~es verhأ¤lt sich so~ ("the matter stands thus, it is the case that") uses ~verhalten~ to describe how facts are configured relative to each other. The noun ~Verhalten~ ("behavior") and adjective ~verhalten~ ("restrained, subdued") both descend from the same source. Martin Heidegger famously used ~sich verhalten zu~ ("to comport oneself toward") as a key term in ~Sein und Zeit~: ~Dasein~ is the being that can ~sich verhalten~ toward its own existence â€” that is, relate to and take a stance on its own being. The legal jargon sense ~sich zu etwas verhalten~ ("to take a position on, to address") extends the metaphor of holding to judicial deliberation. Cognate with Middle Low German ~vأ¶rhإچlden~, Dutch ~verhouden~ ("to be in proportion"), English ~forhold~ (archaic).
en.verhandeln:
From ~ver-~ + ~handeln~. Compound of:

- ~handeln~: From OHG ~hantalإچn~ ("to grasp, take in hand, handle"), from MHG ~handeln~ ("to do, carry out, conduct"), from ~Hand~ ("hand"), from Proto-West Germanic *~handu~, from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~. The semantic journey from "grasping with the hand" to "dealing, trading" occurred via merchant language in the 16th century.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of thoroughness and completion.

The progression from physical handling to abstract negotiation is mirrored in English ~handle~ (from the same Germanic root). German ~verhandeln~ literally means "to deal through completely" -- to negotiate until a matter is resolved. The noun ~Verhandlung~ ("negotiation, trial") reveals the word's importance in legal and diplomatic language. Cognate with English ~handle~, Dutch ~verhandelen~.
en.verheiraten:
From ~ver-~ + ~heiraten~. Compound of:

- ~heiraten~: From MHG ~hؤ«rؤپten~, derived from ~hؤ«rؤپt~ ("marriage"), from OHG ~hؤ«rؤپt~ ("wedding, marriage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~hؤ«warؤپd~. This compound word originally meant something like "household arrangement" -- from *~hؤ«wؤ…~ ("household, family") + *~rؤپdؤ…~ ("counsel, plan").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of completion -- the definitive act of joining in marriage.

The Old English cognate ~hؤ«red~ meant "family, household, retinue," showing the same root but a different semantic path. The reflexive form ~sich verheiraten~ ("to get married") uses ~ver-~ to mark the irrevocable change of state, much as ~sich verlieben~ marks the irreversible plunge into love. The root *~hؤ«wؤ…~ is also the ancestor of English ~hide~ (a unit of land sufficient to support one household).
en.verhindern:
From MHG ~verhindern~, from OHG ~firhintaren~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~hindern~:

- ~hindern~: From MHG ~hindern~, from OHG ~hintaren~. Denominative from ~hinter~ ("behind, in back of"). ~Hinter~ is from OHG ~hintar~, from Proto-Germanic *~hinder~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ل¸±em-tأ©r-~ ("down, back, along, through"), related to *~ل¸±أ³m~ ("beside, near, by, with"). Cognate with Old English ~hinder~ ("in the rear"), Gothic ~hindar~ ("beyond").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or intensifying sense â€” blocking something all the way, preventing it entirely.

The structural metaphor of ~verhindern~ is vivid: to place something ~hinter~ ("behind") an action, using ~ver-~ to make the hindrance complete, thereby preventing it. The English cognate ~hinder~ (as both verb and adjective) descends directly from the same Proto-Germanic root, and the archaic English adverb ~hinder~ (meaning "in the rear") is the same word. Cognate with English ~hinder~, Dutch ~verhinderen~.
en.verkaufen:
From Middle High German ~verkoufen~, from Old High German ~firkoufen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frakإچpإچn~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~kaufen~:

- ~kaufen~: From MHG ~koufen~, from OHG ~koufإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kaupإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaupإچnؤ…~, itself a denominative of *~kaupأ´~ ("merchant, innkeeper"), borrowed from Latin ~caupإچ~ ("innkeeper, shopkeeper, petty trader") â€” likely a pre-Latin Mediterranean loanword. The noun *~kaupأ´~ also yields modern German ~Kaufmann~ ("merchant") and the place-name element ~-kauf~ in towns like ~Kaufbeuren~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion, disposal, or doing something definitively away.

A remarkable Latin borrowing that spread across all of Germanic: Latin ~caupإچ~ (the lowly innkeeper who sold food and drink) gave Proto-Germanic *~kaupإچnؤ…~, which became ~kaufen~ (to buy), ~verkaufen~ (to sell), Dutch ~kopen~ / ~verkopen~, and â€” via a different route â€” English ~cheap~ (originally "a bargain, a market," from the same root, as in the London place name ~Cheapside~). Cognate with Dutch ~verkopen~, Yiddish ~×¤ض؟×گض·×¨×§×•×™×¤ض؟×ں~ (~farkoyfn~).
en.verknأ¼pfen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~knأ¼pfen~:

- ~knأ¼pfen~: From MHG ~knأ¼pfen~, from OHG ~knupfen~, related to ~Knopf~ ("button, knob"), from Proto-Germanic *~knuppaz~ ("lump, clod, knob"), from PIE *~gnewbت°-~ (an extension of *~gen-~, "to compress, to pinch"). Cognate with Dutch ~knoop~ ("knot, button"), Old Irish ~gnobh~ ("knot"), Lithuanian ~gniأ،ubti~ ("to embrace").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). In this verb, ~ver-~ adds a sense of completion or togetherness to the action.

The basic verb ~knأ¼pfen~ means "to tie, to knot," while ~verknأ¼pfen~ intensifies this to "to link, to connect, to combine." In modern usage, ~verknأ¼pfen~ frequently appears in computing contexts (~Dateien verknأ¼pfen~, "to link files") and abstract reasoning (~Ideen verknأ¼pfen~, "to connect ideas"). The relationship between ~knأ¼pfen~, ~Knopf~ ("button"), and ~Knoten~ ("knot") reveals a whole family of Germanic words built around the tactile notion of pressing and tying. English ~knot~ is a cousin from the same Germanic stock, and the silent ~k~ in English ~knot~, ~knob~, and ~knack~ preserves an ancient consonant cluster that German still pronounces.
en.verkأ¼nden:
From Middle High German ~verkunden~, from Old High German ~farkunden~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frakunthijan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~kunden~:

- ~kunden~: From MHG ~kunden~, from OHG ~kunden~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunthijan~ ("to make known"), from Proto-Germanic *~kunthijanan~, a causative formed from *~kunthaz~ ("known"), which derives from *~kunnanan~ ("to know"), from PIE *~gnehâ‚ƒ-~ ("to know"). Cognate with English ~kithe~ (Scottish dialectal, "to make known"), Dutch ~verkondigen~, and Old English ~forcythan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~far-~, ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth, completely"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix here intensifies the action, giving a sense of making something widely or completely known.

The PIE root *~gnehâ‚ƒ-~ is extraordinarily productive across Indo-European languages: it gives English ~know~, ~can~ (originally "to know how"), ~kenning~, ~cunning~, Latin ~gnoscere~ (whence ~recognize~, ~cognition~), Greek ~gnosis~, and Sanskrit ~jnana~. The semantic core of ~verkunden~ is "to make known completely" or "to announce publicly," used especially in solemn, official, or religious contexts: ~das Evangelium verkunden~ ("to proclaim the Gospel"), ~ein Urteil verkunden~ ("to pronounce a verdict").
en.verkأ¼rzen:
From ~ver-~ + ~kأ¼rzen~. Compound of:

- ~kأ¼rzen~: A deadjectival verb from ~kurz~ ("short"), from MHG ~kurz~, from OHG ~kurz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kurt~, borrowed from Latin ~curtus~ ("shortened, cut short"). Latin ~curtus~ itself comes from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to cut").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix functions as a verbalizer and intensifier, meaning "to make thoroughly [adjective]."

The Latin origin of ~kurz~ makes it one of the earliest Latin loanwords in Germanic, adopted during the Roman military presence along the Rhine and Danube. English ~curt~ (blunt, rudely brief) comes from the same Latin source but has narrowed to a purely figurative sense, while German ~verkأ¼rzen~ retains both physical shortening and the abstract sense of "to shorten, abridge." Cognate with Dutch ~verkorten~, English ~curtail~ (from Old French ~courtald~, also from Latin ~curtus~).
en.verlangen:
From Middle High German ~verlangen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~langen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~langen~:

- ~langen~: From MHG ~langen~, from OHG ~langؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~langإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~langإچnؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective *~langaz~ ("long"), from PIE *~dlongت°os~ ("long"). The base verb ~langen~ means "to reach (for), to extend" â€” literally "to make long, to stretch out toward."
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds an intensifying or completive force â€” "longing fully, reaching outward."

The connection to ~lang~ ("long") is preserved transparently in ~verlangen~: to desire something is to "stretch out toward" it, to reach for what is not yet within grasp. The same spatial metaphor of extension underlies English ~long~ (as in "to long for"), which is a cognate from the same PIE *~dlongت°os~. Latin ~longus~ ("long") descends from the same root, making ~verlangen~ and English ~longing~ etymological relatives by a common PIE ancestor. Cognate with Dutch ~verlangen~.
en.verlassen:
From Middle High German ~verlؤپzen~, from Old High German ~firlؤپzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fralؤپtan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fralؤ“tanؤ…~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~, from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, leave behind").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix here adds a completive or intensive sense â€” leaving finally, leaving behind entirely.

The ~ver-~ prefix intensifies ~lassen~'s sense of "letting go" into a more complete, often emotionally weighted act of abandonment. The reflexive construction ~sich auf jemanden verlassen~ ("to rely on someone") developed from the notion of fully entrusting oneself to another â€” abandoning oneself to their care â€” which is semantically the mirror image of the active sense "to abandon." This double life (to abandon / to rely on) makes ~verlassen~ one of the most semantically rich reflexive pairs in German. Cognate with Dutch ~verlaten~, archaic English ~forlet~ ("to abandon, forsake"), Middle Low German ~vorlaten~.
en.verlaufen:
From Middle High German ~verloufen~, from Old High German ~farlaufan~, ~farloufan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~laufen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~loufan~, ~hloufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupanؤ…~, from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, stumble").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of running its course, going astray, or passing through time.

~Verlaufen~ means "to proceed, to run its course" (~Die Verhandlungen verliefen gut~, "The negotiations went well"), "to get lost" (reflexive: ~sich verlaufen~, "to lose one's way"), and "to fade, to dissipate" (~Die Farbe verlأ¤uft~, "The color bleeds/fades"). All three meanings derive from the image of running forward and away: events run their course, a person runs off the path, colors run past their boundaries. The derived noun ~Verlauf~ ("course, progress, progression") is standard in medical (~Krankheitsverlauf~, "course of an illness"), temporal (~im Verlauf des Tages~, "over the course of the day"), and narrative contexts. Cognate with Dutch ~verlopen~.
en.verlegen:
From Middle High German ~verlegen~ ("to misplace, obstruct"), from Old High German ~firleggen~ ("to cover"). Compound of ~ver-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~ ("to lay, place in a resting position"), the causative of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~, from the root *~legت°-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of "away, wrongly" (in "misplace") and of completion or transformation (in "publish, lay down").

~Verlegen~ is a master class in German polysemy. A single verb covers: to misplace (~die Brille verlegen~), to lay or install (~ein Kabel verlegen~), to reschedule (~eine Besprechung verlegen~), to transfer (~Arbeitskrأ¤fte verlegen~), and to publish (~ein Buch verlegen~). The publishing sense gave rise to ~Verlag~ ("publishing house") and ~Verleger~ ("publisher"). As an adjective, ~verlegen~ means "embarrassed" -- from an entirely separate etymology tracing back through MHG to OHG ~firlegan~ ("sluggish, adulterous"), from ~firliggen~ ("to lie wrongly"), literally preserving the medieval association between idleness and moral failing.
en.verleihen:
From Middle High German ~verlؤ«hen~, from Old High German ~firlؤ«han~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~leihen~:

- ~leihen~: From MHG ~lؤ«hen~, from OHG ~lؤ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤ«hwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ«hwanؤ…~, from PIE *~leykت·-~ ("to leave behind"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with English ~loan~ (via Old Norse ~lأ،n~, from Proto-Germanic *~laihnؤ…~, derived from the same PIE root), Dutch ~lenen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a conferring sense â€” lending or granting something out completely, placing it with another.

The PIE root *~leykت·-~ ("to leave behind") is the ancestor of both ~leihen~ and English ~loan~. The conceptual link is direct: to lend is to leave something with another temporarily. English ~loan~ entered the language through Old Norse ~lأ،n~, a Norse borrowing that displaced the native Old English cognate ~lا£n~ ("loan, grant"). The semantic range of ~verleihen~ is broader than simple lending: one can ~verleihen~ a prize (to award it), a quality (to lend it to something), or a tool (to loan it out). The bestowing and lending senses both flow from the root idea of leaving something with another. Cognate with Dutch ~verlenen~, English ~loan~.
en.verletzen:
From ~ver-~ + ~letzen~, an archaic verb meaning "to harm, to injure":

- ~letzen~: From MHG ~letzen~, from OHG ~lezzen~, from Proto-Germanic *~latjanؤ…~. Related to ~letzt~ ("last") and ~Leid~ ("suffering").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here intensifies the harmful action.

~Verletzen~ means "to injure, to hurt, to violate" â€” one can ~verletzen~ a person (physically or emotionally), a law, a treaty, or someone's rights. The base verb ~letzen~ is archaic in modern German but survives in the adjective ~letzt~ ("last") â€” the conceptual bridge being "the final blow," the one that finishes. MHG ~letzen~ had the remarkable enantiodromic property of meaning both "to refresh, to comfort" (as in a farewell feast, a ~Letze~) AND "to harm, to injure" â€” opposite meanings in a single word. The refreshment sense survives in the Swiss German expression ~Letzi~ (a farewell party) and in the archaic noun ~Labsal~ / ~Letze~ ("refreshment at parting"). The ~ver-~ prefix resolved this ambiguity by fixing the meaning firmly on the harmful side. The derived noun ~Verletzung~ ("injury, violation") is among the most common words in medical and legal German. Cognate with Dutch ~kwetsen~ (a different root) and English ~last~ (a distant relative via the same PGmc root).
en.verlieben:
From ~ver-~ + ~lieben~. Compound of:

- ~lieben~: From ~Liebe~ ("love"), from MHG ~liebe~ ("joy, affection"), from OHG ~liob~, ~lioba~ ("fortune, joy, pleasantness"), from Proto-West Germanic *~leubu~ ("love"), from Proto-Germanic *~lubإچ~, from PIE *~lewbت°-~ ("to love, desire").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the inchoative sense of entering a new state.

The reflexive ~sich verlieben~ ("to fall in love") captures the moment of transformation: the ~ver-~ prefix signals an irrevocable crossing into a new emotional state. English ~love~ descends from the same PIE root *~lewbت°-~ via a different Germanic branch (Old English ~lufu~). The PIE root also gave Latin ~lubؤ“re~, ~libؤ“re~ ("to please"), source of English ~libido~. Cognate with Dutch ~verliefd~ ("in love").
en.verlieren:
From Middle High German ~verliesen~, from Old High German ~firliosan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fraleusan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fraleusanؤ…~. Compound of ~ver-~ + the archaic root verb ~liesen~ ("to lose"):

- ~liesen~: The archaic simplex survives only in compounds. From Proto-Germanic *~leusanؤ…~, from PIE *~leu-~ ("to detach, divide, cut"). Cognate with Ancient Greek ~خ»دچد‰~ (~lأ؛إچ~, "to loosen, untie, dissolve"), Latin ~solvإچ~ ("to loosen, dissolve"), and ultimately related to English ~lose~, ~loose~, ~loss~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix reinforces and doubles down on the meaning of loss already present in the root â€” a semantically emphatic pattern sometimes called pleonastic prefixation.

The semantic reinforcement â€” the prefix ~ver-~ piling "wrongly" or "away" onto a root already meaning "to lose" â€” is a vivid Germanic habit of underlining loss through compounding. The archaic simplex ~liesen~ survives in ~Verlies~ ("dungeon"), originally a place where things (or people) are "lost." A notable cognate is English ~forlorn~, from Old English ~forloren~, the past participle of ~forleosan~ ("to lose entirely"), built from the same Proto-Germanic components. Cognate with Dutch ~verliezen~, English ~forlorn~.
en.verlأ¤ngern:
From ~ver-~ + ~lأ¤ngern~ (to lengthen), a denominative verb from ~lأ¤nger~ (longer), comparative of ~lang~ (long). Compound of ~ver-~ + the root adjective ~lang~:

- ~lang~: From MHG ~lanc~, from OHG ~lang~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lang~, from Proto-Germanic *~langaz~, from PIE *~dlongت°os~ ("long"). Cognate with English ~long~, Dutch ~lang~, Latin ~longus~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). In this word, the prefix carries an intensive or augmentative force â€” making something thoroughly longer.

The verb is formed by a common German derivational pattern: adjective comparative + ~-ern~ suffix (as in ~vergrأ¶أںern~, ~verbessern~). The umlaut in ~lأ¤ngern~ comes from the comparative suffix ~-er~, which triggered i-mutation of the root vowel ~a~ to ~أ¤~. The PIE root *~dlongت°os~ appears across the Indo-European world with remarkable semantic consistency â€” Latin ~longus~, Greek ~dأ³likhos~ ("long"), Sanskrit ~dؤ«rghأ،ل¸¥~ ("long") â€” making "long" one of the most stable adjective meanings over five thousand years of linguistic change.
en.vermeiden:
From MHG ~vermؤ«den~, from OHG ~firmؤ«dan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~meiden~:

- ~meiden~: From MHG ~mؤ«den~, from OHG ~mؤ«dan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~mؤ«أ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~mؤ«أ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~meythâ‚‚-~ ("to change, switch"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~mijden~, Old English ~mؤ«أ¾an~ ("to avoid, conceal"), Old Saxon ~mؤ«than~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the act of shunning â€” avoiding something completely, keeping well away.

The PIE root *~meythâ‚‚-~ ("to change, switch") gives the original sense of switching away from something, turning aside â€” which in Germanic narrowed specifically to avoidance and shunning. The expected Grammatischer Wechsel forms of the past tense (*~mitt~, *~gemitten~) were levelled out in modern German â€” possibly to avoid confusion with the preposition ~mit~ and the adverb ~mitten~. Cognate with Dutch ~vermijden~, Old English ~mؤ«أ¾an~.
en.vermissen:
From MHG ~vermissen~, from OHG ~firmissen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~missen~:

- ~missen~: From MHG ~missen~, from OHG ~missan~ ("to miss, to lack"), from Proto-Germanic *~missijanؤ…~ ("to miss, to fail"), from *~missإچ~ ("wrongly, amiss"), from PIE *~mey(t)-~ ("to change, to exchange"). Cognate with English ~miss~, Old English ~missan~, Old Norse ~missa~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the sense of lacking, of being without.

~Vermissen~ means "to miss (someone or something absent)" â€” to feel the lack of a person, an object, or a quality. This is the emotional missing: ~Ich vermisse dich~ ("I miss you"), ~Wir vermissen ihn sehr~ ("We miss him greatly"). It should not be confused with ~verpassen~ ("to miss" in the sense of failing to catch a train or opportunity). The Proto-Germanic root *~missإچ~ ("wrongly, amiss") reveals the original sense: to miss is to err, to go wrong, to fail to hit the mark. This same root produced English ~miss~ in all its senses (missing a target, missing a person), ~mistake~ (a miss-taking), and ~amiss~ ("wrongly"). The emotional sense â€” the ache of absence â€” is a medieval semantic development, extending the idea of "lacking" from a factual observation to a felt experience.
en.vermitteln:
From MHG ~vermitteln~. By surface analysis, ~ver-~ + ~Mittel~ + ~-n~:

- ~Mittel~: From MHG ~mittel~, from OHG ~mittil~, from Proto-Germanic *~midlؤ…~ ("middle"), a derivative of *~midjaz~ ("middle"), from PIE *~mأ©dت°yos~ ("middle"). Cognate with Dutch ~middel~, English ~middle~, Latin ~medius~ ("middle"), Sanskrit ~madhya~ ("middle").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or channeling sense â€” mediating something all the way through to resolution.

The chain ~mأ©dت°yos~ â†’ ~midlؤ…~ â†’ ~mittil~ â†’ ~Mittel~ â†’ ~vermitteln~ traces the concept of "the middle" becoming "the means" (the thing in the middle position between two parties) and then "to mediate" (to act as that middle thing). The PIE root *~mأ©dت°yos~ is extraordinarily productive: it yields Latin ~medius~ (â†’ English ~medium~, ~mediation~, ~mediocre~), Greek ~mأ©sos~ ("middle"), Sanskrit ~madhya~, and â€” through Proto-Germanic â€” English ~mid~, ~middle~, and ~midst~. German ~vermitteln~ is thus the verbalized form of this ancient spatial concept, applied to the social act of standing between two parties. Cognate with Dutch ~bemiddelen~, English ~mediate~ (via Latin, as a parallel form).
en.vermuten:
~ver-~ + ~Mut~/~muten~. From Middle Low German ~vormإچden~, composed of ~vor-~ ("for") + ~mإچden~ ("to want, ask for"), synchronically analyzable as ~ver-~ + ~muten~, from ~Mut~ + ~-en~:

- ~Mut~: From MHG ~muot~, from OHG ~muot~, from Proto-West Germanic *~mإچdd~, from Proto-Germanic *~mإچdaz~ ("mind, sense, courage, anger"), from PIE *~mإچ-tأ³-s~, a lengthened-grade nominal formation from *~mehâ‚پ-~ ("to endeavor; will, temper"), which may be a semantic extension of *~mehâ‚پ-~ in its more basic sense "to measure." Cognate with English ~mood~, Dutch ~moed~, and possibly Latin ~mإچs~ ("will, humor, custom").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here conveys an attempt or approximation â€” a mental reaching-forward.

~Vermuten~ means "to assume, suppose, suspect" â€” literally something like "to have a presentiment about, to pre-sense." The semantic path from ~Mut~ ("courage, mood, mind") to ~vermuten~ ("to suppose") runs through the older meaning of ~Mut~ as "mind, inner state": to ~ver-muten~ was to cast one's mind forward toward something not yet certain. The English cognate ~mood~ preserves the psychological sense but has shed the intellectual one, while German split the word's inheritance: ~Mut~ narrowed to "courage" in most modern contexts, while ~vermuten~ kept the cognitive dimension alive. A false friend lurks nearby: English ~mood~ shares the same Proto-Germanic ancestor as ~Mut~, but ~vermuten~ has nothing to do with "being moody."
en.vermأ¶gen:
From Middle High German ~vermأ¼gen~ or ~vermugen~, from Old High German ~firmugan~ or ~firmagan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~framagan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~mأ¶gen~:

- ~mأ¶gen~: From MHG ~mugen~, from OHG ~mugan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~magan~, from Proto-Germanic *~maganؤ…~, from PIE *~megت°-~ ("to be able, to have power"). Cognate with English ~may~, Dutch ~mogen~, Icelandic ~mega~, and Gothic ~magan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix intensifies the base verb, adding a sense of thoroughgoing ability.

The PIE root *~megت°-~ is the source of English ~may~, ~might~, and ~machine~ (via Greek ~mؤ“khanؤ“~, "contrivance," from ~mekhos~, "means"). In German, ~vermأ¶gen~ means "to be capable of" and also functions as a noun meaning "fortune, wealth, assets," reflecting the idea that one's ability is one's true wealth. The OHG forms show both ~a~-grade (~firmagan~) and ~u~-grade (~firmugan~), reflecting the ablaut patterns of this ancient preterite-present verb. English ~may~ and German ~mأ¶gen~ are thus siblings, both descending from the same Proto-Germanic modal verb, though English lost the ~ver-~ compound entirely.
en.vernichten:
From MHG ~vernihten~, from ~nihten~, from ~niht~ ("nothing"). Surface analysis: ~ver-~ + ~nicht~ + ~-en~:

- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of completion and thoroughness -- here transforming "nothing" into the act of making nothing.

~Vernichten~ means "to annihilate, to destroy utterly, to exterminate" -- literally "to make into nothing." It is one of the most absolute verbs in the German language, admitting no degrees: what is ~vernichtet~ is gone completely. The word's etymology mirrors that of English ~annihilate~, which derives from Latin ~ad-~ + ~nihil~ ("nothing") -- both languages independently built "destroy" from "nothing," but through their own linguistic materials. The derived adjective ~vernichtend~ ("devastating, crushing") is common in both military and figurative contexts: ~eine vernichtende Niederlage~ ("a crushing defeat"). The noun ~Vernichtung~ ("annihilation, destruction") carries heavy historical weight in German.
en.verpassen:
~ver-~ + ~passen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~passen~:

- ~passen~: Borrowed from French ~passer~ ("to pass"), via Dutch ~passen~, ultimately from Vulgar Latin *~passؤپre~, from Latin ~passus~ ("step").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix adds a sense of missing, passing beyond, or failing.

~Verpassen~ means "to miss (an opportunity, a bus, a deadline)" â€” one of the most common verbs of everyday frustration. The prefix ~ver-~ here carries its sense of "going past, going wrong": to ~verpassen~ a train is to let it ~pass beyond~ you. The root ~passen~ itself has a layered borrowing history, entering German from French via Dutch during the medieval trade networks of the Low Countries. Latin ~passus~ ("step") comes from ~pandإچ~ ("to spread, extend"), from PIE *~pethâ‚‚-~ ("to spread out"). A second, colloquial meaning of ~verpassen~ is "to give someone something unpleasant" â€” ~jemandem eine Ohrfeige verpassen~ ("to give someone a slap") â€” where the ~ver-~ prefix shifts from "missing" to "inflicting." Cognate with Dutch ~verpassen~.
en.verpflichten:
From Middle High German ~verphlichten~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~Pflicht~ + ~-en~:

- ~Pflicht~: From OHG ~pfliht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~plihti~, related to ~pflegen~ ("to care for, tend"), from MHG ~phlأ«gen~, from OHG ~plأ«gan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~plehan~. The noun ~Pflicht~ ("duty, obligation") is built on the same root as ~pflegen~ â€” duty as the care one owes. Cognate with English ~plight~ (in the sense of a sworn obligation, as in "to plight one's troth"), Dutch ~plicht~, Danish ~pligt~, Yiddish ~×¤ض؟×œ×™×›×ک~ (~flikht~).
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or binding sense â€” bringing someone fully under an obligation.

English ~plight~ in the phrase "to plight one's troth" (to pledge faithfulness) is the same word as ~Pflicht~: both descend from Proto-West Germanic *~plihti~ ("care, responsibility, duty"). The modern English ~plight~ in the sense of "a bad situation" is a different word (from Old French ~pleit~, "condition") â€” a false friend within English itself. ~Verpflichten~ binds someone to a duty completely, committing them through the totality of their care. Cognate with Dutch ~verplichten~.
en.verraten:
From Middle High German ~verrؤپten~, from Old High German ~firrؤپtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frarؤپdan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~raten~:

- ~raten~: From MHG ~rؤپten~, from OHG ~rؤپtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rؤپdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ“danؤ…~, from PIE *~Hrehâ‚پdت°-~ ("to arrange, to consider").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries a sense of wrongdoing, misdirection, or reversal â€” turning counsel into betrayal.

The semantic shift in ~verraten~ is dramatic: ~raten~ means "to advise, to guess," but ~verraten~ means "to betray, to give away (a secret)." The ~ver-~ prefix twists counsel into treachery â€” advice given to the wrong party becomes betrayal. This negative transformation by ~ver-~ is paralleled in English ~bewray~ (archaic "to betray," from ~be-~ + ~wray~ "to accuse"). Cognate with Dutch ~verraden~. The root verb ~raten~ is cognate with English ~read~ (which originally meant "to advise, to interpret") and Danish ~rأ¥de~ ("to advise, to rule") â€” a remarkable case where "reading" and "advising" share an ancestor in the PIE concept of arranging or making sense of things.
en.verringern:
From ~ver-~ + archaic ~ringern~ ("to make lighter, soften, weaken"), a verbal formation from ~gering~ ("little, low"):

- ~gering~: From MHG ~geringe~, from OHG ~giringi~, ~ringi~ ("low, plain, simple, light, easy"), from Proto-Germanic *~ringwaz~ ("small, light, quick"), of uncertain origin, though possibly from PIE *~wrengت·ت°-~ ("to squeeze, wring"). Cognate with Yiddish ~×’×¨×™× ×’~ (~gring~, "light, easy").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completive force.

The intermediate verb ~ringern~ ("to lessen, make small") is now archaic, leaving ~verringern~ as the surviving compound. The adjective ~gering~ ("little, insignificant") is still very much alive, but its direct verbal derivative has been supplanted by the prefixed form. The possibly related PIE root *~wrengت·ت°-~ ("to squeeze, wring") would suggest an original image of something being squeezed small -- though this connection remains uncertain. The Greek cognate ~دپخ¯خ¼د†خ±~ (~rhأ­mpha~, "fast, quickly") preserves the "quick, light" sense of the Proto-Germanic ancestor rather than the "small" sense that prevailed in German.
en.versammeln:
From ~ver-~ + ~sammeln~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzamelen~. Compound of:

- ~sammeln~: From MHG ~sameln~, ~samlen~, probably by dissimilation from ~samenen~, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *~samnإچnؤ…~ ("to gather, collect"). Alternatively inherited from OHG *~samalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~samalإچn~ ("to bring together, assemble").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb, giving the sense of gathering completely, assembling as a whole.

The Proto-Germanic root *~samnإچnؤ…~ is also the source of English ~same~ (things gathered together are alike) and ~some~ (a gathered portion). The ~Ver-sammlung~ ("assembly") has been a key word in German political life since the medieval ~Reichsversammlung~ (imperial assembly). Dutch ~verzamelen~ ("to collect") is an exact cognate, while English ~assemble~ took a parallel path through Old French from Latin ~assimulؤپre~.
en.verschaffen:
From ~ver-~ + ~schaffen~. Compound of:

- ~schaffen~ (weak verb, "to manage, procure"): From MHG ~schaffen~ (strong verb), from OHG ~scafan~, ~scaffen~, a byform of ~skephen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skappjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skapjanؤ…~, of uncertain PIE origin â€” possibly related to *~skabanؤ…~ ("to shave, scrape") via a semantic shift from "to scrape, hollow out" to "to form, shape." The strong verb form ~skephen~ survives in the archaic variant ~schأ¶pfen~ ("to create"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheppen~, English ~shape~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of completion or procurement â€” bringing something about fully.

~verschaffen~ means "to procure, obtain, provide" (~sich Zugang verschaffen~ = "to gain access," ~jemandem Arbeit verschaffen~ = "to find someone work"). It uses the weak conjugation of ~schaffen~ (~schaffte~, ~geschafft~, meaning "to manage, accomplish") rather than the strong conjugation (~schuf~, ~geschaffen~, meaning "to create"). This strong/weak split in ~schaffen~ is one of the most interesting in German: ~Gott schuf die Welt~ ("God created the world") versus ~Ich habe es geschafft~ ("I managed it"). ~verschaffen~ always takes the weak form, focusing on the practical act of procurement rather than divine creation. The etymological connection to English ~shape~ â€” both from Proto-Germanic *~skapjanؤ…~ â€” is obscured by centuries of sound change but the semantic parallel of "forming, making" is still visible.
en.verschenken:
From ~ver-~ + ~schenken~. Compound of:

- ~schenken~: From MHG ~schenken~, from OHG ~scenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skankijan~ ("to pour, serve"), from Proto-Germanic *~skankijanؤ…~, a causative from *~skankaz~ ("askew, crooked"), from PIE *~(s)keng-~ ("to limp, be crooked").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of giving away completely, parting with something irrevocably.

The semantic journey from "crooked" to "give as a gift" is one of the most entertaining in German etymology: the original meaning was "to pour drink" (tilting the vessel at an angle), then "to serve drink at a tavern," then "to give freely" (because the innkeeper sometimes poured one on the house). German ~Geschenk~ ("gift") and ~einschenken~ ("to pour a drink") preserve both ends of this journey. English ~skink~ (an archaic word for "to pour drink") is a distant cousin through the same Germanic root.
en.verschicken:
From ~ver-~ + ~schicken~. Compound of:

- ~schicken~: From MHG ~schicken~ ("to arrange, order, put in place"), from Proto-West Germanic *~skikkijan~ ("to arrange, put in order"), related to Proto-Germanic *~skikkijanؤ…~. The original meaning was "to arrange, put in order," and the modern sense "to send" developed from the idea of dispatching something to its proper place.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of sending away to a distance.

The semantic shift from "arrange" to "send" happened in the late medieval period, when arranging goods for transport naturally came to mean dispatching them. The related noun ~Schicksal~ ("fate, destiny") preserves the older sense of "arrangement" -- fate as what has been arranged for you. English ~ship~ (as in "to ship goods") underwent a strikingly similar shift from a vessel to the act of sending. Cognate with Dutch ~beschikken~ ("to arrange, dispose").
en.verschieben:
From Middle High German ~verschieben~, from Old High German ~firskioban~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~schieben~:

- ~schieben~: From MHG ~schieben~, from OHG ~skioban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeuban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skeubanؤ…~, from PIE *~skewbت°-~ ("to push, to shove").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix conveys completion or transformation of the base action â€” pushing something into a different position, whether spatially or temporally.

~Verschieben~ means both "to move, to shift (physically)" and "to postpone, to defer" â€” a beautiful case where spatial displacement became temporal displacement. You can ~einen Schrank verschieben~ ("shift a wardrobe") or ~einen Termin verschieben~ ("postpone an appointment"), and the underlying metaphor is identical: pushing something to a different place. Cognate with Dutch ~verschuiven~. The root verb ~schieben~ is cognate with English ~shove~, Low German ~schuven~, and Danish ~skubbe~ â€” all from the same PIE root describing forceful pushing.
en.verschlieأںen:
From ~ver-~ + ~schlieأںen~. Compound of:

- ~schlieأںen~: From MHG ~slieب‌en~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slإ«tanؤ…~, *~sleutanؤ…~ ("to lock, shut"). The original sense was specifically "to lock with a bolt or bar."
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of completeness -- locking something up tight.

The Proto-Germanic root gave Dutch ~sluiten~ ("to close"), while English ~slot~ (originally "a bar to lock a door") preserves the same source with a narrowed meaning. German ~Schlأ¼ssel~ ("key") is a derivative, literally "the thing that locks." The ~ver-~ prefix in ~verschlieأںen~ adds the nuance of sealing away, locking up completely -- one cannot simply ~schlieأںen~ a secret, but one can ~verschlieأںen~ it. The past participle ~verschlossen~ has also come to describe a person's character: reserved, closed-off.
en.verschwinden:
From Middle High German ~verswinden~, from Old High German ~firswintan~, ~farswindan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~schwinden~:

- ~schwinden~: From MHG ~swinden~, from OHG ~swintan~, from Proto-Germanic *~swindanؤ…~. A class III strong verb. The simplex ~schwinden~ still exists in literary German with the meaning "to dwindle, wane, fade." Cognate with Dutch ~zwinden~ ("to disappear"), Danish ~svinde~, Swedish ~svinna~, Norwegian ~svinne~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completive and directional force â€” dwindling away entirely, disappearing to completion.

The most surprising cognate is English ~swindle~. The English verb is a back-formation from ~swindler~, which was borrowed directly from German ~Schwindler~ â€” a person who causes things (including money) to vanish, from ~schwindeln~ ("to be dizzy, to swindle"). So English ~swindle~ traces back through German to the same Proto-Germanic root *~swindanؤ…~ that underlies ~verschwinden~. To make something disappear and to defraud someone have always been close neighbors. Cognate with Dutch ~verzwinden~, English ~swindle~ (via German ~Schwindler~).
en.versehen:
From Middle High German ~versehen~, from Old High German ~firsehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frasehwan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sehen~, from OHG ~sehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, to perceive").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries multiple semantic loads depending on the construction: completion (to provide fully), misdirection (to see wrongly), and anticipation (to foresee).

~Versehen~ splits into fascinatingly divergent meanings all rooted in altered seeing. As a transitive verb, ~mit etwas versehen~ means "to provide with, to equip with" â€” to see to it that something is furnished. As a reflexive ~sich versehen~, it means "to make a mistake" â€” to see wrongly, to misjudge. The archaic reflexive ~sich eines Besseren versehen~ meant "to expect better" â€” to foresee. The noun ~Versehen~ ("oversight, mistake") crystallizes the negative sense: a failure of seeing. Cognate with Old English ~forsؤ“on~ ("to overlook, to despise") and Dutch ~verzien~ â€” both sharing the "wrongly seen" semantics. The Old English cognate's meaning of "to despise" (literally "to look past someone") shows how the ~ver-~ prefix could push "seeing" toward contemptuous disregard.
en.versenden:
From MHG ~versenden~, from OHG ~firsenten~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~senden~:

- ~senden~: From MHG ~senden~, from OHG ~senten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sandijanؤ…~, a causative of *~sinأ¾anؤ…~ ("to go, to travel"), from PIE *~sent-~ ("to go"). Cognate with English ~send~, Dutch ~zenden~, Danish ~sende~, Gothic ~sandjan~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix adds a sense of sending away, dispatching completely.

The literal sense is "to send away" â€” to dispatch, to ship. ~Versenden~ means "to send out, to ship, to dispatch" and is the standard verb for mailing packages, shipping goods, or distributing mailings. It is more formal and comprehensive than simple ~senden~ ("to send"), implying distribution to multiple recipients or dispatch through a logistics system. The noun ~Versand~ ("dispatch, shipping") is ubiquitous in e-commerce. The PIE root *~sent-~ ("to go") underwent a causative transformation in Proto-Germanic: *~sandijanؤ…~ means literally "to cause to go" â€” a beautifully transparent etymology for the act of sending. The same root appears in Old Irish ~sأ©t~ ("path, way"), Armenian ~ة™ntت؟anac~ ("way"), and Latin ~sentؤ«re~ ("to feel" â€” originally perhaps "to go mentally toward"), whence English ~sense~, ~sentence~, and ~sentiment~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzenden~ ("to send, to ship").
en.versetzen:
From Middle High German ~versetzen~, from Old High German ~firsezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frasattjan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, a causative derived from Proto-Germanic *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of displacement -- setting something elsewhere.

Cognate with Dutch ~verzetten~ ("to move, shift") and archaic English ~forset~. Like ~verlegen~, ~versetzen~ is richly polysemous: to move or displace (~einen Baum versetzen~), to transfer (~nach Karlsruhe versetzt werden~), to promote to the next school grade (~er wird versetzt~), to put into a state (~in Angst versetzen~ -- "to frighten"), to pawn (~das Silberbesteck versetzen~), to deal a blow (~einen Klaps versetzen~), and colloquially to stand someone up (~er hat mich versetzt~). The common thread is displacement -- setting something or someone into a different position, state, or situation. In literary German, ~versetzte er~ was once a standard speech tag meaning "he replied, retorted," now largely archaic. The proverbial ~der Glaube kann Berge versetzen~ ("faith can move mountains") preserves the physical-displacement sense at its most dramatic.
en.versichern:
From Middle High German ~versichern~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~sichern~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~sichern~:

- ~sichern~: From ~sicher~ + ~-n~, where ~sicher~ descends from MHG ~siher~, from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~ ("secure, sure"), borrowed from Latin ~sؤ“cإ«rus~ ("without care, carefree"), from ~sؤ“-~ ("without") + ~cإ«ra~ ("care, concern").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix adds a sense of completion or thoroughness.

The insurance sense of ~versichern~, which emerged in the 17th century, is a calque (loan translation) of Italian ~assicurare~ ("to insure"), itself built from ~a-~ + ~sicuro~ ("secure"). This means German and Italian independently constructed the same metaphor from the same Latin root: "to make secure." English ~assure~ and ~insure~ followed the same pattern through French. The dual meaning of ~versichern~ elegantly mirrors this history: "to assure" (to make someone feel secure in a belief) and "to insure" (to make someone financially secure against loss). The noun ~Versicherung~ ("insurance") is one of the most common German words in daily life. The ultimate Latin root ~cإ«ra~ ("care") also produced English ~cure~, ~curious~, ~accurate~, and ~secure~, all revolving around the concept of care and its absence.
en.versorgen:
From ~ver-~ + ~sorgen~:

- ~sorgen~: From MHG ~sorgen~, from OHG ~sorgؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgؤپnؤ…~, from *~surgإچ~ ("worry, sorrow, care"), from PIE *~swergت°-~ ("to watch over, worry; be ill, suffer"). The semantic duality of watchful care and anxious suffering is present in the root itself. Cognate with Dutch ~zorgen~, English ~sorrow~, Danish ~sأ¸rge~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a thoroughgoing, providing sense â€” caring for something completely, ensuring that needs are fully met.

~Versorgen~ takes the dual root of ~sorgen~ â€” care and concern â€” and resolves it firmly toward the positive pole: to provide for, to supply, to look after. While ~sorgen~ can still tip toward worry (~sich sorgen um~, "to worry about"), ~versorgen~ almost exclusively denotes active provision. A hospital ~versorgt~ patients; a government ~versorgt~ the population with electricity; parents ~versorgen~ their children. The ~ver-~ prefix converts the act of caring into the act of ensuring that care is carried through to its object. Cognate with Dutch ~verzorgen~.
en.versprechen:
From Middle High German ~versprechen~ ("to guarantee"), from Old High German ~firsprehhan~ ("to defend verbally, to warrant"), from Proto-West Germanic *~frasprekan~. Analyzable as ~ver-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprأ«chen~, from OHG ~sprأ«hhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanؤ…~, probably from PIE *~spregت°-~ ("to crackle, make noise"). Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix originally gave a completive or forward-directed sense â€” speaking forth, speaking on behalf of.

~Versprechen~ carries two opposite meanings held in grammatical tension. When transitive (~ich verspreche dir~, "I promise you"), it means to commit â€” to speak something forth and bind yourself to it. When reflexive (~ich verspreche mich~, "I misspeak"), it means to err in speaking â€” a later transparent derivation exploiting the prefix's sense of deviation. The verb thus embodies the double nature of ~ver-~: forward completion (a promise) and errant deviation (a slip of the tongue) are both housed in a single form. The sense "to promise" is attested from Old High German; the reflexive "to misspeak" is a later development. Cognate with Low German ~vorspreken~.
en.verstecken:
From Middle High German ~verstecken~, from Old High German ~firstekken~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stecken~:

- ~stecken~: From MHG ~stecken~, from OHG ~steckإچn~, a geminated (consonant-doubled) derivative of OHG ~stehhan~ (whence modern ~stechen~, "to sting, prick, stab"). The semantic development from "to pierce, stick" to "to be fixed in place, to put into" runs through the idea of a pointed object driven into a surface and remaining there.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies and completes the action: to stick something away so thoroughly that it cannot be found.

~Verstecken~ means "to hide, to conceal." The reflexive ~sich verstecken~ ("to hide oneself") is the verb every German child knows from the game ~Verstecken~ ("hide and seek"). The related noun ~Versteck~ ("hiding place") drops the verbal suffix. Cognate with Dutch ~versteken~ ("to hide"). The underlying verb ~stecken~ preserves a fascinating grammatical fossil: transitively it has always been weak (~steckte~), but intransitively it once had strong past-tense forms (~stak~, Konjunktiv II ~stأ¤ke~). These strong forms, though still technically attested, have become archaic and unintelligible to many native speakers â€” a rare case of a strong-verb pattern dying within recorded history.
en.verstehen:
From Middle High German ~verstؤپn~, ~virstؤ“n~, ~vorstؤ“n~, ~ferstؤپn~, from Old High German ~firstؤپn~, ~firstؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~frastؤپnؤ…~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From MHG ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from OHG ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, wrongly, or completion.

The semantic development from "to stand before, against" to "to understand" parallels English ~understand~ (literally "to stand among") and Latin ~comprehendere~ ("to grasp"). The notion is of positioning oneself in relation to something in order to grasp it â€” a spatial metaphor for cognition shared across the Germanic languages. Cognate with Bavarian ~versteh~, Dutch ~verstaan~, Middle Low German ~vorstؤپn~. Compare also archaic English ~forstand~ ("to understand").
en.versterben:
From MHG ~versterben~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~sterben~:

- ~sterben~: From MHG ~stأ«rben~, from OHG ~stأ«rban~, from Proto-Germanic *~sterbanؤ…~, from PIE *~sterbت°-~ ("to be stiff, become stiff"). Cognate with Dutch ~sterven~, Low German ~starven~, Frisian ~stjerre~, and English ~starve~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of completion and finality -- dying to the end, dying completely.

~Versterben~ is the formal, euphemistic, and respectful way to say "to die" in German -- roughly equivalent to English "to pass away." Where ~sterben~ is neutral and direct, ~versterben~ adds a veil of formality appropriate for obituaries, official announcements, and solemn contexts. The past participle ~verstorben~ functions as an adjective meaning "deceased" (~der verstorbene Professor~, "the late professor"). The English cognate ~starve~ underwent a dramatic semantic narrowing: while German ~sterben~ retained the general meaning "to die," English ~starve~ specialized to mean "to die of hunger" -- a shift that occurred in Middle English. The PIE root *~sterbت°-~ ("to be stiff, become stiff") connects death to rigor mortis -- a grimly literal etymology.
en.verstoأںen:
From MHG ~verstإچzen~, from OHG ~firstإچzan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stoأںen~:

- ~stoأںen~: From MHG ~stإچzen~, from OHG ~stإچzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stautan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stautanؤ…~, from PIE *~(s)tewd-~ ("to push, hit"). Cognate with Gothic ~stautan~, Old Norse ~stauta~, Dutch ~stoten~ ("to push, bump"), and dialectal English ~stot~ ("to bounce"). Non-Germanic cognates include Latin ~tundإچ~ ("I beat") and Sanskrit ~tudأ،ti~ ("to strike, goad").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of completion, excess, or wrongful action -- here intensifying the thrust into an act of expulsion.

~Verstoأںen~ means "to expel, to cast out, to violate (a law or rule)" -- the ~ver-~ prefix transforms a physical push into an act of permanent exclusion. ~Ein verstoأںenes Kind~ ("a cast-out child") carries immense emotional weight. The legal sense ~gegen ein Gesetz verstoأںen~ ("to violate a law") shifts the metaphor: one pushes through or against a boundary that should hold firm. The PIE root *~(s)tewd-~ connects ~stoأںen~ to Latin ~tundإچ~ ("I beat, I pound"), whence English ~contusion~ -- a bruise being literally a place where flesh has been struck.
en.verstأ¤rken:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stأ¤rken~:

- ~stأ¤rken~: From MHG ~sterken~, from OHG ~sterken~, a denominative verb from ~stark~ ("strong"), from Proto-West Germanic *~stark~, from Proto-Germanic *~starkuz~, from PIE *~ster-~ / *~sterg-~ ("stiff, rigid, strong").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb, carrying the sense of thorough or complete transformation.

~Verstأ¤rken~ means "to reinforce, to amplify, to strengthen" â€” the ~ver-~ prefix pushes ~stأ¤rken~ ("to strengthen") toward completion or intensification. You ~verstأ¤rkst~ a wall (reinforce it), a signal (amplify it), a team (bolster it), or a tendency (intensify it). The derived noun ~Verstأ¤rker~ ("amplifier") is the standard term in electronics and music â€” a guitar ~Verstأ¤rker~ is an amp. ~Verstأ¤rkung~ means "reinforcement" in both military and general senses. The root adjective ~stark~ is cognate with English ~stark~ (which shifted from "strong" to "utter, bare, harsh" â€” a ~stark~ contrast originally meant a ~strong~ contrast) and Dutch ~sterk~ ("strong"). The PIE root *~ster-~ / *~sterg-~ ("stiff, rigid") also connects to English ~stare~ (with rigid eyes) and ~stork~ (the stiff-legged bird), Latin ~re-stringere~ ("to restrict" â€” whence ~strict~), and Greek ~دƒد„خµدپخµدŒد‚~ ("solid, firm" â€” whence ~stereo~).
en.versuchen:
From ~ver-~ + ~suchen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~suchen~:

- ~suchen~: From MHG ~suochen~, ~sأ¼echen~, from OHG ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sإچkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sإچkijanؤ…~, from PIE *~sehâ‚‚g-~ ("to seek out"). Cognate with Dutch ~zoeken~, Low German ~sأ¶ken~, Danish ~sأ¸ge~, English ~seek~, Gothic ~sإچkjan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, wrongly, or completion.

The ~ver-~ prefix gives ~versuchen~ the sense of seeking something out to its conclusion â€” hence "to try, attempt." The word is famously polysemous: in religious contexts it means "to tempt" (as in the Lord's Prayer: ~fأ¼hre uns nicht in Versuchung~, "lead us not into temptation"), while in everyday speech it simply means "to try." The two senses share the underlying idea of putting something â€” or someone â€” to the test by seeking its limits. Compare archaic English ~forseek~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzoeken~.
en.verteidigen:
From Middle High German ~verteidingen~, from ~vertagedingen~, itself derived from ~teidinc~, ~tagedinc~ -- originally meaning "court day with negotiations," from ~tac~ ("day") + ~ding~ ("negotiation, thing"). The semantic development ran: "court day" > "to defend in court" > "to defend" generally. Cognate with Dutch ~verdedigen~.

- ~Ding~: From MHG ~ding~, from OHG ~ding~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾ingؤ…~ ("thing, assembly, matter").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first").
- ~Tag~: From MHG ~tac~, from OHG ~tag~, from Proto-Germanic *~dagaz~, from PIE *~dت°egت·ت°-~ ("to burn").

The etymology of ~verteidigen~ is one of the most colorful in German. It has nothing to do with a root meaning "defend" -- it grew out of the medieval Germanic legal assembly (~Ding~, ~Thing~), the same institution that gave English its word ~thing~ (originally "assembly, matter brought before an assembly"). A ~Tagedinc~ was a day appointed for legal proceedings; to ~vertagedingen~ was to conduct business at such a proceeding, specifically to argue one's case. The word was then phonologically compressed through ~verteidingen~ to modern ~verteidigen~, losing all visible trace of ~Tag~ and ~Ding~ along the way. The obsolete synonym ~defendieren~, borrowed directly from Latin ~dؤ“fendere~, never displaced the native formation.
en.verteilen:
From Middle High German ~verteilen~, from Old High German ~farteilen~, ~firteilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fradailijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fradailijanؤ…~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailijanؤ…~, from *~dailiz~ ("part, share"), tentatively from PIE *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, set, place"). Cognate with English ~deal~ (in the card-dealing sense), Dutch ~delen~, Swedish ~dela~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a distributive sense â€” dividing something out completely among recipients.

The distinction between ~teilen~ and ~verteilen~ is the difference between dividing and distributing: ~teilen~ splits something into parts; ~verteilen~ sends those parts out among recipients. A teacher ~teilt die Klasse~ ("divides the class") but ~verteilt die Aufgaben~ ("distributes the tasks"). The connection to English ~deal~ is worth noting â€” to deal cards, to deal goods, to make a deal all involve distributing portions, the same semantic core. Dutch ~verdelen~ is a direct cognate. Cognate with Dutch ~verdelen~.
en.vertiefen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~tief~ + ~-en~:

- ~tief~: From MHG ~tief~, ~tiuf~, from OHG ~tiuf~, ~tiof~, from Proto-West Germanic *~deup~, from Proto-Germanic *~deupaz~, from PIE *~dت°ewbت°-~ ("deep"). Cognate with English ~deep~, Dutch ~diep~, Danish ~dyb~, Old Norse ~djأ؛pr~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries a sense of intensification â€” making something more deeply so.

~Vertiefen~ means "to deepen" both literally (a channel, a groove) and figuratively (~sich in ein Thema vertiefen~ = "to immerse oneself in a topic," ~Kenntnisse vertiefen~ = "to deepen one's knowledge"). The reflexive ~sich vertiefen~ beautifully captures the image of sinking deeper into something â€” a book, a problem, a thought. English ~deep~ and German ~tief~ are exact cognates, differing only in the regular consonant shift. The modern German idiom ~in Gedanken vertieft~ ("lost in thought," literally "deepened in thoughts") captures the metaphor of intellectual depth.
en.vertragen:
From Middle High German ~vertragen~, from Old High German ~firtragan~, ~fartragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fradraganؤ…~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag, carry"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to drag, pull").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of endurance and bearing through.

The meaning "to bear, endure, tolerate" comes from the literal sense of carrying a burden through to the end. The reflexive ~sich vertragen~ ("to get along") extends the metaphor: two people who can bear each other's weight. The noun ~Vertrag~ ("contract, treaty") captures the idea of mutual obligation borne by both parties. English ~draw~ and ~drag~ descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, as does Dutch ~verdragen~ ("to tolerate"). Cognate with Low German ~verdragen~.
en.vertrauen:
From Middle High German ~vertrإ«wen~, ~vertriuwen~, from Old High German ~firtrإ«wؤ“n~, ~firtrإ«ؤ“n~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~trauen~:

- ~trauen~: From MHG ~trإ«wen~, from OHG ~trإ«wؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trإ«wؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~trإ«wؤپnؤ…~, derived from *~triwwiz~ ("faithful, loyal"), from PIE *~drewhâ‚‚-~ ("steady, firm, solid"). Cognate with English ~true~, ~trust~, ~truce~, Dutch ~vertrouwen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb â€” to trust ~thoroughly~ or ~completely~.

The PIE root *~drewhâ‚‚-~ ("steady, firm") is also the source of *~dأ³ru~ ("tree, wood") â€” the connection being that a tree is the archetypal firm, solid thing. Thus ~vertrauen~ ("to trust"), English ~true~, and English ~tree~ all share a common ancestor, linked by the concept of steadfastness. The noun ~Vertrauen~ ("trust, confidence") is among the most important words in German public discourse â€” ~Vertrauensfrage~ ("vote of confidence") can bring down a government, and ~Vertrauensvorschuss~ ("advance trust") describes the credit of goodwill extended to someone not yet proven. Cognate with Dutch ~vertrouwen~, Low German ~vertruggen~.
en.vertreiben:
From Middle High German ~vertrؤ«ben~, from Old High German ~firtrؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fradrؤ«ban~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~treiben~:

- ~treiben~: From MHG ~trؤ«ben~, from OHG ~trؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~drؤ«banؤ…~, from PIE *~dت°reybت°-~ ("to drive, push"). Cognate with English ~drive~, Dutch ~drijven~, Swedish ~driva~, Danish ~drive~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it adds a sense of driving away completely.

Cognate with Old English ~fordrؤ«fan~ ("to drive away") and Dutch ~verdrijven~. The relationship between German ~treiben~ and English ~drive~ is one of the most elegant demonstrations of Grimm's Law in action: the PIE *~dت°-~ became Germanic *~d-~ became Old High German ~t-~ (via the High German consonant shift), while English preserved the Germanic ~d-~. The semantic range of ~vertreiben~ is striking: it means both "to drive out, to expel" (~die Bewohner vertreiben~, "to expel the residents") and "to sell, to market" (~Waren vertreiben~, "to sell goods") and even "to pass time" (~sich die Zeit vertreiben~, "to while away the time"). The commercial sense developed from the idea of "driving goods to market," while the temporal sense treats boredom as something to be driven away.
en.vertreten:
From MHG ~vertrأ«ten~, from OHG ~firtretan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fratredan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trأ«ten~, from OHG ~trأ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudanؤ…~. A class V strong verb. Cognate with English ~tread~, Dutch ~treden~, Old Norse ~troأ°a~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix shifts the spatial sense of stepping into the abstract registers of representation and advocacy â€” stepping in on behalf of another.

The semantic range of ~vertreten~ is impressively wide and rests entirely on spatial metaphor: to step in front of someone (blocking them â€” ~jemandem den Weg vertreten~), to step in place of someone (representing them â€” ~jemanden vertreten~), and reflexively, to accidentally step wrong (~sich den Fuأں vertreten~, "to sprain one's ankle"). The political and legal senses â€” an attorney who ~vertritt~ a client, a parliamentarian who ~vertritt~ constituents â€” flow naturally from "stepping into the position of another." Cognate with Dutch ~vertreden~, English ~tread~ (underlying root).
en.verursachen:
From ~ver-~ + ~Ursache~ + ~-en~:

- ~Ursache~: From MHG ~ursache~, equivalent to ~ur-~ + ~Sache~. ~Ur-~: From MHG ~ur-~, from OHG ~ur-~, ~ir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("out, from within"); doublet of ~er-~. ~Sache~: From MHG ~sache~, from OHG ~sahha~, from Proto-West Germanic *~saku~, from Proto-Germanic *~sakإچ~. Cognate with English ~sake~ (as in "for your sake"), Dutch ~zaak~, Swedish ~sak~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix has a causative or instrumental sense â€” making something the originating cause of an effect.

~Ursache~ literally means "the original thing" or "the primal matter" â€” the ~ur-~ prefix (cognate with the prefix in ~ursprأ¼nglich~, "original") marking something as first or primordial, and ~Sache~ meaning "thing, matter, affair." English ~sake~ is a cognate of ~Sache~: "for your sake" originally meant "for your matter, your cause" â€” the same legal and causal sense that ~Ursache~ carries in German. ~Verursachen~ ("to cause") is the verb built on this compound noun. Cognate with Dutch ~veroorzaken~, English ~sake~ (cognate of ~Sache~).
en.verurteilen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~urteilen~:

- ~urteilen~: From MHG ~urteilen~, a denominative verb from ~Urteil~ ("judgment, verdict"), which displaced the synonymous MHG ~erteilen~. ~Urteil~ derives from MHG ~urteil~, from OHG ~urteil~, ~urteili~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzdailijؤ…~ ("judicial decision, verdict"), literally "a dealing out," a compound of *~uz-~ ("out") + *~dailiz~ ("part, portion"), from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("out") + *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to put, do"). Cognate with Dutch ~oordeel~ and English ~ordeal~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb toward a decisive, final judgment.

The connection between ~Urteil~ ("judgment") and English ~ordeal~ is striking: both descend from the Germanic concept of "dealing out" justice, but their meanings diverged radically. English ~ordeal~ originally referred to a trial by divine judgment (walking on hot coals, being thrown into water), while German ~Urteil~ stayed close to the core juridical sense. The ~ver-~ prefix in ~verurteilen~ pushes the meaning from neutral "to judge" toward "to condemn, convict, sentence" -- judgment with a negative outcome.
en.verwalten:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~walten~:

- ~walten~: From MHG ~walten~, from OHG ~waltan~ ("to rule, hold sway"), from Proto-West Germanic *~waldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~waldanؤ…~ ("to rule"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚welhâ‚پ-~ ("to rule, be strong"). Cognate with English ~wield~, Icelandic ~valda~, Polish ~wإ‚adaؤ‡~ ("to wield, rule"), Russian ~ذ²ذ»ذ°ذ´ذµر‚رŒ~ ("to possess, own").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). As a prefix, ~ver-~ can indicate completion, transformation, or intensification.

The simple verb ~walten~ is now archaic and largely restricted to fixed expressions like ~schalten und walten~ ("to do as one pleases") and ~seines Amtes walten~ ("to perform one's duties"). The prefix ~ver-~ transformed the elevated, sovereign meaning of "to rule" into the more bureaucratic "to manage, administer." The related nouns ~Gewalt~ ("power, force, violence") and ~Verwaltung~ ("administration") both descend from the same root, showing the full range from raw power to orderly governance.
en.verwandeln:
From Middle High German ~verwandelen~, ~ferwandelen~, from Old High German ~firwantalإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~frawandlإچnؤ…~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~wandeln~:

- ~wandeln~: From MHG ~wandeln~, from OHG ~wantalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandalإچn~, a frequentative of *~wandijan~ ("to turn"), from Proto-Germanic *~wandijanؤ…~, causative of *~windanؤ…~ ("to wind, to turn"), from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, to wind, to braid").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix adds a sense of thoroughgoing transformation.

~Verwandeln~ means "to transform, to metamorphose, to convert." The prefix ~ver-~ intensifies ~wandeln~ ("to change, to walk") into a complete, irreversible transformation. The PIE root *~wendت°-~ ("to turn") connects ~verwandeln~ to a vast family of turning words: English ~wind~ (the verb), ~wander~, ~wend~; German ~winden~ ("to wind"), ~Wand~ ("wall" â€” originally a woven wattle partition), and ~wandern~ ("to wander, to hike"). The related noun ~Verwandlung~ ("transformation, metamorphosis") is the German title of Kafka's famous novella, published in 1915, about Gregor Samsa's transformation into an insect. The word ~verwandt~ ("related, akin") is the past participle of an older sense of ~verwenden~ ("to turn toward"), reflecting the idea that relatives are people "turned toward" each other. Cognate with Dutch ~verwandelen~ (archaic), Low German ~verwanneln~.
en.verweigern:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~weigern~:

- ~weigern~: From MHG ~weigeren~, from OHG ~weigarإچn~ ("to refuse, to be stubborn"), from Proto-West Germanic *~waigrإچn~ ("to be stubborn"), derived from OHG ~weigar~ ("stubborn, reluctant"). The adjective likely traces to Proto-Germanic *~waigraz~ ("resistant").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, *~fur-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it intensifies the refusal expressed by the base verb.

The verb ~verweigern~ means "to refuse, to deny, to withhold" â€” ~den Gehorsam verweigern~ ("to refuse obedience"), ~die Aussage verweigern~ ("to refuse to testify"), ~den Zutritt verweigern~ ("to deny entry"). The ~ver-~ prefix intensifies the refusal: while ~sich weigern~ means "to refuse" (reflexively), ~verweigern~ is transitive and more formal, focusing on what is being withheld. The related noun ~Verweigerung~ appears in the compound ~Kriegsdienstverweigerung~ ("conscientious objection to military service"), a word of 28 letters that is entirely unremarkable in German legal discourse. The concept of ~Wehrdienstverweigerung~ ("refusal of military service") became especially significant in postwar Germany, where the right to refuse military service was enshrined in the ~Grundgesetz~ (Basic Law) as Article 4, paragraph 3 â€” a direct response to the moral catastrophe of citizens who did not refuse.
en.verweisen:
From Middle High German ~verwؤ«sen~, from Old High German ~firwؤ«san~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wؤ«sen~, from OHG ~wؤ«san~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~ ("to show, direct"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, know").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of directing away or directing toward.

~Verweisen~ carries two seemingly opposed meanings that are actually two sides of the same coin: "to refer" (~auf etwas verweisen~, "to point to something") and "to expel" (~jemanden des Landes verweisen~, "to banish someone from the country"). Both senses derive from the act of directing: one directs attention toward a reference, or one directs a person away from a place. The derived noun ~Verweis~ means both "reference" and "reprimand" â€” a formal finger-pointing that can be informational or punitive. The academic use (~auf eine Quelle verweisen~, "to cite a source") and the disciplinary use (~einen Verweis erteilen~, "to issue a reprimand") coexist peacefully, unified by the underlying gesture of pointing. Cognate with Dutch ~verwijzen~.
en.verwenden:
From Middle High German ~verwenden~, ~ferwenden~, from Old High German ~firwenten~ ("to turn away, avert, dissuade, hold back"). Compound of ~ver-~ + ~wenden~:

- ~wenden~: From MHG ~wenden~, from OHG ~wenten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wandijanؤ…~, a causative of *~windanؤ…~ ("to wind, wrap") with the suffix *~-janؤ…~, from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, wind, braid").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, wrongly, or completion.

The semantic development from "to turn away" to "to use, employ" reflects the completive force of the ~ver-~ prefix applied to the notion of turning: what is fully turned toward a purpose is thereby used up or applied. Cognate with Middle Low German ~vorwenden~, Low German ~verwenden~.
en.verwirklichen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~wirklich~ + ~-en~:
