- ~wirklich~: From ~wirken~ ("to work, to have effect") + ~-lich~ ("having the quality of"). ~Wirken~ descends from MHG ~wirken~, from OHG ~wirken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wirkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wirkijanؤ…~ ("to work, to effect"), from PIE *~werاµ-~ ("to work, to do"). Cognate with English ~work~, Greek ~أ©rgon~ ("work," whence ~energy~, ~ergonomic~), ~organ~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix adds the sense of bringing something to completion, making it fully real.

~Verwirklichen~ means "to realize, to make real, to actualize" â€” ~einen Traum verwirklichen~ ("to realize a dream"), ~sich selbst verwirklichen~ ("to fulfill oneself, to achieve self-actualization"). The reflexive ~Selbstverwirklichung~ ("self-actualization") became a key concept in postwar German psychology and philosophy. The word's structure is deeply layered: ~wirken~ ("to work, to effect") becomes ~wirklich~ ("real, actual" â€” literally "having the quality of working, of being effective"), which becomes ~verwirklichen~ ("to make real" â€” to bring into the realm of the effective and actual). The PIE root *~werاµ-~ connects working and reality across the Indo-European world: Greek ~أ©rgon~ ("work") gave English ~energy~ ("in-work"), ~liturgy~ ("people's work"), ~metallurgy~ ("metal-working"), and ~surgeon~ (via Greek ~kheirourgأ³s~, "hand-worker").
en.verwأ¶hnen:
From Middle High German ~verwenen~, ~vorwenen~, ~ferwenen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + the now-extinct verb ~wأ¶hnen~. Cognate with Low German ~verwennen~, Dutch ~verwennen~. Compound of:

- ~wأ¶hnen~: An extinct verb related to ~gewأ¶hnen~ ("to accustom"), from MHG ~wenen~, ~wennen~, from OHG ~wennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wannjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wanjanؤ…~ ("to accustom"), from PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, love, strive for").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of doing something to excess or in a wrong direction.

The umlaut ~أ¶~ (instead of expected ~e~) arose through sporadic rounding in Middle High German, likely reinforced by related words with original /o/ like ~Gewohnheit~ ("habit") and ~gewohnt~ ("accustomed"). English ~wean~ descends from the same Proto-West Germanic root *~wannjan~ -- originally meaning "to accustom (a child to food other than milk)." The semantic path of ~verwأ¶hnen~ runs from "to accustom wrongly" to "to pamper, spoil" -- the ~ver-~ prefix marking that the accustoming has gone too far.
en.verzeichnen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, mark, token"), from MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taiknؤ…~ ("sign, token, indicator"), from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show, indicate, point out"). Cognate with English ~token~, Dutch ~teken~, Swedish ~tecken~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of thorough completion.

The base verb ~zeichnen~ means "to draw, to sketch" -- to make signs on paper. ~Verzeichnen~ shifts the meaning to "to record, to list, to register" -- creating an authoritative written record. The reflexive ~sich verzeichnen~ means "to misdraw," where the ~ver-~ prefix takes its other common sense of "wrongly, astray." The noun ~Verzeichnis~ ("directory, index, catalogue") is one of the most common derivatives.
en.verzichten:
An 18th-century denominal verb derived from the noun ~Verzicht~ ("renunciation, waiver") + the verb-forming suffix ~-en~. ~Verzicht~ itself derives from Middle High German ~verziht~, from ~ver-~ + a derivative of ~zeihen~ ("to accuse, blame"). The root ~zeihen~ descends from OHG ~zؤ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tؤ«han~, from Proto-Germanic *~tؤ«hanؤ…~, from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show, point out").

- ~zeihen~: From OHG ~zؤ«han~, from Proto-Germanic *~tؤ«hanؤ…~ ("to show, point out; to blame, accuse"), from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show"). Cognate with Latin ~dؤ«cإچ~ ("to speak, say"), Ancient Greek ~خ´خµخ¯خ؛خ½د…خ¼خ¹~ (~deأ­knumi~, "to show, point out"), and English ~token~ (from the related *~taiknؤ…~, "sign").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix moves the sense from accusation to abdication â€” declaring against oneself.

The semantic journey of ~verzichten~ is a three-act drama. Act one: PIE *~deyل¸±-~ means "to show, point out." Act two: ~zeihen~ means "to accuse" â€” pointing a finger at someone to declare their guilt. Act three: ~ver-~ inverts the direction, making it a declaration against oneself â€” "to formally renounce, to waive." Formal legal and ecclesiastical language crystallized this meaning: ~auf etwas verzichten~ is to make a public declaration of giving something up. The root *~tؤ«hanؤ…~ is extinct as a simplex in modern German; only ~zeihen~ (highly archaic) and compounds like ~verzichten~ and the noun ~Verzicht~ preserve it. Cognate with Middle Low German ~vortichten~, Middle Dutch ~vertichten~.
en.verأ¤ndern:
From ~ver-~ + ~أ¤ndern~:

- ~أ¤ndern~: From MHG ~endern~, a denominative from ~ander~ ("other, different"), from Proto-Germanic *~anأ¾eraz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚أ©nteros~ ("the other of two"). Cognate with English ~other~, Dutch ~ander~, Gothic ~anأ¾ar~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or transformative sense â€” changing something thoroughly, bringing it into a wholly different state.

The doubled sense of transformation â€” ~ver-~ ("completion, away") applied to a verb already built on the concept of "making other" â€” produces one of German's most precise words for change. Unlike ~أ¤ndern~ alone (which can mean merely to adjust or tweak), ~verأ¤ndern~ implies a more thoroughgoing alteration. The reflexive ~sich verأ¤ndern~ ("to change oneself") is culturally ubiquitous, used for everything from personal growth to career changes. Cognate with Dutch ~veranderen~, Yiddish ~farendern~.
en.verأ¶ffentlichen:
From ~ver-~ + ~أ¶ffentlich~ + ~-en~. Compound of:

- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or bringing into a state.
- ~أ¶ffentlich~: From MHG [unattested as compound], from OHG ~offanlؤ«h~ ("open, public"), itself from ~offen~ + ~-lich~. ~Offen~ derives from MHG ~offen~, OHG ~offan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~opan~, from Proto-Germanic *~upanaz~, the past participle of a hypothetical verb *~إ«panؤ…~ ("to open, to put up"), from *~upp~ ("up"). The literal sense was "lying on top" > "clear, open." Cognate with Dutch ~open~, English ~open~, Swedish ~أ¶ppen~.
- ~-en~: Standard verbal suffix converting the adjective ~أ¶ffentlich~ into a verb.

The verb ~verأ¶ffentlichen~ â€” literally "to make (completely) public/open" â€” follows the productive German pattern of ~ver-~ + adjective + ~-en~ for causative verbs (compare ~verdeutlichen~, "to make clear"; ~vereinfachen~, "to simplify"). The chain from PIE *~upp~ ("up") â†’ PGmc *~upanaz~ ("open") â†’ OHG ~offan~ â†’ ~أ¶ffentlich~ â†’ ~verأ¶ffentlichen~ traces an ancient directional concept ("upward, above") all the way to the modern act of publication. Cognate with Dutch ~publiceren~ (different root, same semantic niche).
en.vollziehen:
Compound of ~voll-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with English ~tow~ (from OE ~togian~), Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead," whence ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~educate~).
- ~voll-~: From MHG ~vol~, from OHG ~fol~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullaz~ ("full"), from PIE *~plehâ‚پ-~ ("to fill"). Cognate with English ~full~, Latin ~plؤ“nus~ ("full," whence English ~plenty~, ~replenish~).

~Vollziehen~ means "to carry out, to execute, to consummate." Literally "to pull to fullness," it conveys completion of an official act: ~ein Urteil vollziehen~ ("to execute a sentence"), ~die Ehe vollziehen~ ("to consummate a marriage"), ~eine Maأںnahme vollziehen~ ("to implement a measure"). The derived noun ~Vollzug~ ("execution, enforcement") appears in the compound ~Strafvollzug~ ("penal enforcement"). Latin ~dإ«cere~, from the same root as ~ziehen~, produced an astonishing family of English words: ~duke~, ~duct~, ~deduce~, ~produce~, ~reduce~, ~educate~, ~conduct~ -- all from the simple idea of pulling or leading.
en.vorantreiben:
Compound of ~voran-~ + ~treiben~:

- ~treiben~: From MHG ~trؤ«ben~, from OHG ~trؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drؤ«ban~, from Proto-Germanic *~drؤ«banؤ…~, from PIE *~dت°reybت°-~ ("to drive, push"). Cognate with Dutch ~drijven~, English ~drive~, Danish ~drive~, Swedish ~driva~.
- ~voran-~: Compound separable prefix from ~vor-~ (from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ "before, in front") + ~an-~ (from PIE *~hâ‚‚en~ "on, onto"). Together they mean "ahead, forward, onward."

~Vorantreiben~ means "to push forward, to advance, to drive on" -- literally "to drive something ahead." It is the quintessential verb of progress and ambition: ~ein Projekt vorantreiben~ ("to push a project forward"), ~die Forschung vorantreiben~ ("to advance research"). The English cognate ~drive~ is a perfect semantic match in many contexts: to "drive" progress forward, to "drive" a project.
en.voraussetzen:
Compound of ~voraus-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, a causative from Proto-Germanic *~satjanؤ…~ ("to set"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~ and Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~voraus-~: Compound prefix from ~vor~ ("before, forward," from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~) + ~aus~ ("out," from PIE *~أ؛d~). Together, ~voraus~ means "ahead, in advance."

~Voraussetzen~ means "to presuppose, to assume as given" â€” literally "to set out ahead," placing a condition before the argument. The noun ~Voraussetzung~ ("prerequisite, precondition") is ubiquitous in academic and bureaucratic German. The PIE root *~sed-~ ("to sit") is one of the most productive roots in the Indo-European family, generating Latin ~sedؤ“re~ ("to sit"), English ~sit~, ~set~, ~seat~, ~settle~, ~saddle~, ~cathedral~ (via Greek ~kathedra~, "seat"), and ~nest~ (from *~ni-sd-os~, "where one sits down").
en.vorbereiten:
From ~vor-~ + ~bereiten~, itself a double-prefix compound. ~Bereiten~ descends from Old High German ~bireitan~, formed from ~bi-~ (an ancestral form of the modern prefix ~be-~) + a verb derived from Proto-Germanic *~raidijanؤ…~ ("to arrange, make ready").

- ~bereiten~: From MHG ~bereiten~, from OHG ~bireitan~, from the prefix ~bi-~ + a denominal verb from the Proto-Germanic adjective *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged"), from PIE *~rؤ“y-~ ("to arrange, prepare"). The adjective *~raidaz~ also yields the German adjective ~bereit~ ("ready, prepared") and, across the North Sea, English ~ready~ â€” a direct cognate. Proto-Germanic *~raidijanؤ…~ ("to arrange") is itself a denominative causative: "to make ready."
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura-~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚-~ ("before, in front"). The prefix adds the sense of doing something in advance â€” preparing before the need arises.

The double-prefix structure of ~vorbereiten~ (vor- + be- + reitan) mirrors its meaning: thorough prior preparation involves multiple stages of making ready. The OHG simplex ~reitan~ ("to make ready, to equip") â€” ancestor of both ~bereiten~ and English ~ready~ â€” has no direct continuation in Modern German as a simplex; it survives only in the compound and in the adjective ~bereit~. English ~ready~ and German ~bereit~ are thus cognates meaning precisely the same thing, both descending from the same Proto-Germanic adjective *~raidaz~, while ~bereiten~ and ~vorbereiten~ are the verbal expressions of that same root applied to the act of preparation. Cognate with Dutch ~voorbereiden~, English ~ready~ (via *~raidaz~).
en.vorbeugen:
From ~vor-~ + ~beugen~. Compound of:

- ~beugen~: From MHG ~bأ¶ugen~, from OHG ~bougen~, from Proto-Germanic *~baugijanؤ…~, a causative of *~beuganؤ…~ ("to bow, bend"), from PIE *~bت°ewgت°-~ ("to bend, bow").
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, ~vأ¼r-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura-~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). As a verbal prefix, it indicates doing something in advance.

The literal meaning is "to bend forward" (as when bowing), but the figurative sense "to prevent, forestall" -- bending events away from their course before they arrive -- became the primary meaning. The English idiom "to bow before" captures a similar spatial metaphor. The related strong verb ~biegen~ ("to bend") shares the same PIE root *~bت°ewgت°-~, which also gave English ~bow~ and ~elbow~ (literally "arm-bend"). In early modern German, ~beugen~ and ~biegen~ nearly merged entirely; grammarians later re-separated them, assigning ~beugen~ to figurative and ~biegen~ to physical bending.
en.vorgeben:
From ~vor-~ + ~geben~. Compare Middle Low German ~vب«rgؤ“ven~ ("to pretend"), English ~foregive~, and Danish ~foregive~ ("to pretend"). Compound of:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~ ("to bring, reach, give"), from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, ~vأ¼r-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura-~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). As a verbal prefix, it indicates putting something forward or in advance.

The primary modern meaning "to claim, pretend" (literally "to give forth, put forward") reflects the idea of presenting something that may not be genuine. Middle Low German ~vب«rgؤ“ven~ and Danish ~foregive~ developed the same "pretend" sense independently, suggesting this metaphor was widespread across the Germanic-speaking world. The secondary meaning "to set, dictate" (as in ~das Tempo vorgeben~, "to set the pace") preserves the more literal sense of putting something forward as a standard.
en.vorgehen:
Compound of ~vor-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gan~, ~gen~, from OHG ~gan~, ~gen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ganan~, from PIE *~ghehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave, to go"). Cognate with Dutch ~gaan~, Swedish ~ga~, Danish ~ga~.
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor(e)-~, from OHG ~fora~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fura~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura~ ("before"), from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). This prefix adds a sense of moving forward or preceding.

The PIE root *~ghehâ‚پ-~ is notably not the source of English ~go~, which comes instead from a different root; the German-English cognate pair here is ~gehen~/~gang~ (as in English ~gang~, originally "a going, a way"). The form ~gen~ (with ~e~ instead of ~a~) is of Bavarian origin but became standard. The spelling with ~-h-~ was introduced by analogy with ~sehen~, where it had become silent but was kept in writing. As a separable verb, ~vorgehen~ means "to proceed, to go ahead, to take action," but also "to go on, to be happening" (~Was geht hier vor?~, "What is going on here?") and even "to be fast" when referring to a clock. This polysemy makes it one of the most contextually versatile ~vor-~ compounds.
en.vorkommen:
From Middle High German ~vorekomen~, ~vurekomen~, from Old High German ~furaqueman~, ~furikweman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~forakweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~furaikwemanؤ…~. Compound of ~vor-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~komen~, from OHG ~queman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت·em-~ ("to come, to go").
- ~vor-~: From PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). The prefix adds a sense of coming forward, appearing before someone.

~Vorkommen~ means "to occur, to happen; to seem, to appear; to be found (in nature)." The literal sense â€” "to come forward, to come before" â€” underlies all three modern meanings: what ~occurs~ comes forward into reality; what ~seems~ a certain way comes before your judgment; a mineral that ~is found~ in rock comes before the prospector. The impersonal construction ~es kommt mir vor~ ("it seems to me") preserves the original image of something presenting itself before the speaker. The OHG form ~furaqueman~ shows the old consonant: ~qu~ (from Proto-Germanic *~kw~), which simplified to ~k~ in modern German ~kommen~ but survives in English ~come~ (from the same root). Cognate with English ~forecome~ (archaic), Dutch ~voorkomen~ ("to occur; to prevent").
en.vorlegen:
From ~vor-~ + ~legen~. Compound of:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~. This is the causative of *~legت°-~ ("to lie down") â€” literally "to cause to lie," hence "to lay, to place." Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~, Danish ~lأ¦gge~.
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, ~vأ¼r-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fura~, from Proto-Germanic *~furai~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys the sense of placing something forward or presenting it.

The semantic range of ~vorlegen~ â€” "to present, to submit, to serve (food), to put on (a lock)" â€” all derives from the core image of placing something ~before~ someone. One ~legt~ a document ~vor~ (presents it), ~legt~ food ~vor~ (serves it), or ~legt~ a padlock ~vor~ (puts it on). The noun ~Vorlage~ ("template, submission, model") is the corresponding nominalization, widely used in legislative contexts (~Gesetzesvorlage~ = "draft bill") and in soccer (~eine Vorlage geben~ = "to deliver an assist"). The causative relationship between ~legen~ ("to lay") and ~liegen~ ("to lie") â€” one makes something do what the other describes â€” is a textbook example of Germanic ablaut morphology.
en.vorliegen:
Equivalent to ~vor-~ + ~liegen~:

- ~liegen~: From MHG ~ligen~, from OHG ~liggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ligjanؤ…~, from PIE *~legت°-~ ("to lie down").
- ~vor-~: From PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). The prefix adds the sense of being present or existing before someone.

~Vorliegen~ means "to be present, to exist, to be available" â€” it is impersonal and formal, used especially in bureaucratic, legal, and scientific contexts: ~Es liegt ein Fehler vor~ ("An error exists"), ~Die Ergebnisse liegen vor~ ("The results are available"). The physical image is of something lying before you â€” laid out for inspection. The derived noun ~Vorlage~ ("template, draft, original") preserves the concrete sense of a document laid out in front of someone for reference. The past participle ~vorliegend~ functions as an adjective meaning "present, at hand" (~der vorliegende Fall~, "the case at hand"), making it one of the most common participial adjectives in formal German prose.
en.vornehmen:
~vor-~ + ~nehmen~. Compound of ~vor-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~vor-~: From PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). The prefix adds the sense of forward projection or undertaking something in advance.

~Vornehmen~ means "to undertake, to intend, to resolve to do" â€” literally "to take forward." The reflexive ~sich vornehmen~ ("to make up one's mind to do something, to set oneself a task") is ubiquitous in everyday speech. The PIE root *~nem-~ also underlies ~nehmen~ and preserves the sense of taking one's due in the idea of taking on a task or intention. A related semantic neighbor is the adjective ~vornehm~ ("distinguished, refined, upper-class"), which derives from the same compound but traveled a different path: "taking forward" came to mean "foremost in rank," hence distinguished. English ~nim~ ("to pilfer, take") is a direct cognate of ~nehmen~, now mostly archaic outside of card-game contexts. Cognate with Dutch ~voornemen~.
en.vorschlagen:
Compound of ~vor-~ + ~schlagen~:

- ~schlagen~: From MHG ~slahen~ (the modern ~-g-~ was inserted by analogy with past forms and the noun ~Schlag~), from OHG ~slahan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~slahan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slahanؤ…~ ("to strike, hit"). Cognate with Dutch ~slaan~, English ~slay~ and ~slog~, Danish ~slأ¥~, and Swedish ~slأ¥~.
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura-~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). This prefix adds the sense of putting something "forward" or "before" others.

The compound literally means "to strike before" someone, as in laying a suggestion in front of them for consideration. This metaphor of "striking" or "hitting upon" an idea is shared across languages: English uses ~strike~ in "it struck me" and ~hit~ in "he hit upon an idea." The noun ~Vorschlag~ ("proposal, suggestion") is one of those satisfying German compounds that wears its etymology on its sleeve. The base verb ~schlagen~ shows an interesting case of ~grammatischer Wechsel~ (Verner's Law alternation): the original ~h~ in OHG ~slahan~ alternated with ~g~ in certain forms, and the ~g~ eventually won out in the infinitive. English ~slay~ preserves the older consonant pattern.
en.vorschreiben:
From ~vor-~ + ~schreiben~. Compound of:

- ~schreiben~: From MHG ~schrؤ«ben~, from OHG ~scrؤ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrؤ«ban~ ("to write"), borrowed from Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~ ("I write, scratch, draw"), from PIE *~(s)kreybت°-~ ("to scratch, carve").
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, ~vأ¼r-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura-~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). As a verbal prefix, it indicates doing something in advance or setting something out beforehand.

The meaning "to prescribe, dictate, stipulate" comes from writing down rules in advance -- what is ~vorgeschrieben~ has been "pre-written" and therefore mandated. English ~prescribe~ follows exactly the same logic through Latin ~praescrؤ«bere~ ("to write before"), using the Latin equivalent of ~vor-~ (namely ~prae-~) with the Latin source verb of ~schreiben~. The Germanic adoption of Latin ~scrؤ«bإچ~ likely occurred during the Roman occupation of the Rhineland, when writing was introduced to the Germanic peoples. Before this borrowing, the native Germanic word for scratching runes was *~wrؤ«tanؤ…~ -- preserved in English ~write~.
en.vorsehen:
From Middle High German ~vorsehen~, equivalent to ~vor-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~, from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, to notice").
- ~vor-~: From PIE *~prehâ‚‚~ ("before, in front"). The prefix adds the sense of looking ahead, planning, or intending.

~Vorsehen~ means "to plan, to intend, to provide for" â€” etymologically, "to see ahead." The past participle ~vorgesehen~ ("planned, intended, designated") is ubiquitous in German bureaucratic and organizational language: ~Das Gesetz sieht Strafen vor~ ("The law provides for penalties"), ~Der Plan sieht eine Erweiterung vor~ ("The plan envisions an expansion"). The reflexive ~sich vorsehen~ means "to be careful, to watch out" â€” to see what is coming. The derived noun ~Vorsehung~ ("providence, divine foresight") carries theological weight: God's ~Vorsehung~ is the act of seeing and planning ahead on behalf of humanity. The Latin calque ~prإچvidentia~ ("foresight," from ~prإچ-~ + ~vidؤ“re~, "to see before") â€” whence English ~providence~ â€” mirrors the German compound exactly: both are "seeing before," one in Latin and one in Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~voorzien~.
en.vorstellen:
From ~vor-~ + ~stellen~. Compound of two elements:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb, the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand").
- ~vor-~: From MHG ~vor-~, from OHG ~fora-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura-~, from PIE *~prehâ‚‚-~ ("before, in front"). As a separable prefix, ~vor-~ adds a sense of forward placement or presentation.

The semantic range of ~vorstellen~ is remarkably broad: "to place in front of," "to introduce (a person)," "to imagine," and "to represent." The reflexive ~sich vorstellen~ covers both "to introduce oneself" and "to imagine" â€” two quite different acts united by the underlying metaphor of placing something before one's mind or another person. Cognate with Dutch ~voorstellen~ ("to propose; to introduce; to imagine").
en.wachsen:
From Middle High German ~wahsen~, from Old High German ~wahsan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wahsan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wahsijanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚weg-~ ("to grow, increase"). A class VI strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~wassen~, Dutch ~wassen~, West Frisian ~waakse~, English ~wax~, Danish ~vokse~, Swedish ~vأ¤xa~, Gothic ~wahsjan~. Non-Germanic cognates include Ancient Greek ~aأ©xein~ ("to increase") and Latin ~auxilium~ ("help," from a sense of "increase of strength").

English preserves the same verb as the literary and poetic ~wax~ ("the moon waxes"), enshrined in the phrase ~wax and wane~. In everyday modern English the verb ~wax~ (to grow) is archaic except in that fixed phrase and in expressions like "to wax poetic." German ~wachsen~ has remained the ordinary word for growing throughout â€” of plants, of children, of economies. The PIE root *~hâ‚‚weg-~ is also the ancestor of Latin ~augؤ“re~ ("to increase"), giving English ~augment~, ~auction~ (from "increase by bidding"), and the imperial title ~Augustus~ ("the increased, the revered one"). The ablaut series ~wأ¤chst~/~wuchs~/~gewachsen~ follows class VI. The compound ~entwachsen~ ("to outgrow") and the noun ~Wuchs~ ("growth, stature") preserve the root's productive legacy.
en.wagen: From Middle High German ~wؤپgen~ ("to risk, to dare"), ultimately derived from Old High German ~wؤپga~ ("scales, balance; weight") â€” the modern word ~Waage~ ("scales"). The underlying metaphor is vivid: to ~wagen~ is to place something on the scales without knowing where the balance will fall. The OHG noun ~wؤپga~ descends from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤپgu~ ("scales"), from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ“gإچ~, related to *~weganؤ…~ ("to move, to weigh"), from PIE *~weاµت°-~ ("to move, to transport, to carry"). This root is spectacularly productive: it gave Latin ~vehere~ ("to carry") and its derivatives ~vehicle~, ~convex~, ~invective~; English ~way~, ~weigh~, ~wain~ ("wagon"); and German ~Weg~ ("way"), ~bewegen~ ("to move"), ~wiegen~ ("to weigh"), and ~Wagen~ ("car, wagon"). The noun ~Wagen~ and the verb ~wagen~ are thus false friends in disguise: ~Wagen~ comes from the "vehicle" branch of the root, while ~wagen~ comes from the "scales" branch â€” yet both trace back to the same PIE notion of weighing and moving. Cognate with Dutch ~wagen~ ("to dare").
en.wahrnehmen: From Middle High German ~war nemen~, from Old High German ~wara neman~, literally "to take attention." A compound of ~wahr-~ (not the adjective ~wahr~ "true," but a separate word meaning "heed, attention, care") + ~nehmen~ ("to take"). The ~wahr-~ element derives from Proto-West Germanic *~warإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~warإچ~ ("care, attention, heed"), possibly from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard, protect"). This *~warإچ~ also underlies ~wahren~ ("to safeguard, protect") and ~bewahren~ ("to preserve") â€” and, through a parallel Germanic branch, English ~wary~ and ~aware~. ~Nehmen~ derives from Old High German ~nأ«man~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). The compound is a beautiful example of a metaphor now hidden beneath surface spelling: to perceive is literally "to take notice" â€” attention is grasped, not merely received. The homophony with ~wahr~ ("true") creates a productive ambiguity: ~wahrnehmen~ can feel as if it means "to take something as true," reinforcing the philosophical weight the verb carries in German. Cognate with Dutch ~waarnemen~, English ~wary~ and ~aware~ (via the *~warإچ~ root).
en.wandeln:
From Middle High German ~wandeln~, from Old High German ~wantalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandalإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~wandإچnؤ…~, from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, wind, braid"). Cognate with Dutch ~wandelen~ ("to walk, stroll"), English ~wend~ (from OE ~wendan~, "to turn"), and ~wind~ (the verb, "to coil").

~Wandeln~ means both "to walk, to stroll" (an elevated, literary synonym for ~gehen~) and "to change, to transform" (~sich wandeln~). These senses seem unrelated, but both descend from the root idea of turning: to walk is to turn one's steps in a direction, and to change is to turn into something else. The same dual meaning survives in the related noun ~Wandel~ ("change, transformation") and the famous compound ~Wanderlust~, which passed into English unchanged. The tribal name ~Vandals~ (~Wandali~) may derive from the same root, possibly meaning "the wanderers."
en.wandern: From Middle High German ~wandern~, from Old High German *~wantarإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandarإچn~, itself formed from *~wandإچn~ ("to turn, to change") with the frequentative suffix *~-rإچn~, suggesting repeated or habitual turning or moving about. The root traces to Proto-Germanic *~wandإچnؤ…~ ("to turn"), from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, to wind"). The frequentative formation captures the essential nature of wandering: not a single purposeful journey but a repeated, meandering motion. The word is attested since the 13th century and originally carried the sense of journeying on foot as a tradesman â€” the ~Wanderjahre~ ("years of wandering") were the years a journeyman spent traveling from workshop to workshop after completing his apprenticeship, a tradition formalized in the medieval guild system and still practiced by some German tradespeople today. Cognate with English ~wander~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic source), Dutch ~wandelen~ ("to stroll, to walk for pleasure"), Danish ~vandre~, Swedish ~vandra~. The PIE root *~wendت°-~ also gives English ~wind~ (the verb, "to twist") and ~wend~, German ~wenden~ ("to turn"), and Latin ~ventus~ ("wind" â€” air that turns and twists). The cultural concept of ~Wanderlust~ â€” that restless longing for the open road â€” was borrowed into English directly from German, one of those rare words that filled a gap no English term could.
en.warnen: From Middle High German ~warnen~ ("to watch over, to protect"), from Old High German ~warnإچn~ ("to deny, to refuse, to decline"), from Proto-West Germanic *~warnإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~warnإچnؤ…~, from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, to perceive, to watch out for"). The semantic evolution is noteworthy: in Old High German, ~warnإچn~ meant "to refuse, to deny" â€” an act of self-protection. By Middle High German, the sense had shifted to "to watch over, to guard, to caution" â€” protecting others rather than oneself. In Modern German, ~warnen~ means specifically "to warn, to caution" (~vor etwas warnen~, "to warn against something"). The shift from refusal to warning follows a natural logic: one who refuses danger for oneself eventually tells others to do the same. Cognate with Old English ~wyrnan~ ("to refuse, to withhold"), Old Norse ~varna~ ("to refuse"), Old Saxon ~wernian~ ("to decline, to withhold"), and â€” most strikingly â€” English ~warn~, which underwent the same semantic shift independently. The PIE root *~wer-~ also yields Latin ~verؤ“ri~ ("to fear, to respect," whence English ~revere~), German ~wahren~ ("to safeguard"), and ~Wart~ ("watchman," as in ~Torwart~, "goalkeeper"). The derived noun ~Warnung~ ("warning") and the ~Warnweste~ ("high-visibility vest") both preserve the ancient watching-over sense.
en.warten:
From Middle High German ~warten~, from Old High German ~wartؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wardؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~wardؤپnؤ…~ ("to guard, protect, watch over"), derived from *~warduz~ ("a guard"), ultimately from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard").

The semantic shift from "to guard, watch over" to "to wait" reflects the experience of a sentinel: one who keeps watch necessarily also waits. This same shift occurred in English ~ward~ (from the same root) and in French ~garder~ ("to guard") and ~attendre~ ("to wait," literally "to attend to"). In Modern German, ~warten~ retains both senses in certain contexts â€” ~warte auf mich~ ("wait for me") and the older sense of tending/caring persists in ~warten~ as "to service, maintain" (as in ~ein Auto warten~, "to service a car"), a usage that connects back directly to the original "to watch over, tend." Cognate with Old Saxon ~wardإچn~, Swedish ~vأ¥rda~ ("to tend, care for"), English ~ward~, Dutch ~bewaren~ ("to keep, preserve").
en.waschen:
From Middle High German ~waschen~, from Old High German ~wascan~, ~waskan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~waskan~, from Proto-Germanic *~waskanؤ…~ ("to wash"), from an earlier *~watskanؤ…~, a ~ske~-present formation from PIE *~wed-~ ("wet, water"). The same PIE root gives English ~water~, ~wet~, and ~wash~ itself. Cognate with Dutch ~wassen~, English ~wash~, West Frisian ~waskje~, Danish ~vaske~.

The PIE derivation is revealing: ~waschen~ is built on a present-tense formation with the ~ske~-suffix (PIE *~wod-ske-~), a morphological pattern used to create iterative or inchoative presents. The basic meaning was "to make wet (repeatedly)" â€” washing as the iterative application of water. German ~waschen~ and English ~wash~ descend from the same Proto-Germanic word and have maintained nearly identical meanings across a millennium of independent development. The verb is also notable as an anagram of ~wachsen~ ("to grow") â€” a coincidence with no etymological significance but one that generations of German schoolchildren have noticed. The reflexive ~sich die Hأ¤nde waschen~ ("to wash one's hands") carries the same metaphorical meaning of moral disavowal as in English, both ultimately from the biblical account of Pontius Pilate.
en.wechseln:
From Middle High German ~wأ«hseln~, from Old High German *~wehsalإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~wihslإچnؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~weyk-~, *~weyل¸±-~ ("to change, bend, yield"). Cognate with Dutch ~wisselen~, Luxembourgish ~wiesselen~, Latin ~vicis~ ("change, turn, alternation"), English ~week~, German ~Woche~.

The connection between ~wechseln~ and English ~week~ runs through the Proto-Germanic root *~wikا­~ ("sequence, turn"), itself from *~wؤ«kanؤ…~ ("to bend, yield, give way"). A week is a "turn" of days, a cycle that yields to the next. The same root produced Latin ~vicis~ ("change, alternation, turn") â€” found in ~vice versa~ ("with the turn reversed"), ~vicar~ (one who stands in another's turn or place), and ~vicious~ (originally relating to a flaw in the pattern). German ~Woche~ and English ~week~ are thus cognates of ~wechseln~, all sharing the sense of something that changes, turns, or yields its place. The notion of currency exchange (~Geldwechsel~) preserves the oldest concrete sense: money, like days, passes from hand to hand in alternating turns. Cognate with Dutch ~wisselen~, English ~week~, Latin ~vicis~ ("turn, change").
en.wecken:
From Middle High German ~wecken~, from Old High German ~wecchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wakkjan~ ("to wake up, to awaken"), from Proto-Germanic *~wakjanؤ…~, a causative formation from *~wakanؤ…~ ("to be awake"), from PIE *~weاµ-~ ("to be lively, to wake"). The causative relationship is transparent: *~wakanؤ…~ means "to be awake" (intransitive), *~wakjanؤ…~ means "to make awake" (transitive) â€” and this is precisely the modern German distinction between ~wachen~ ("to be awake, to keep watch") and ~wecken~ ("to wake someone up"). Cognate with Dutch ~wekken~, English ~watch~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic source but with a different semantic development).

The English cognate ~watch~ reveals a surprising semantic drift: from "to wake, to be alert" (Old English ~wأ¦ccan~) to "to observe attentively" to "a timepiece used to mark the hours of wakefulness." German ~wecken~ stayed closer to the original causative meaning. The derived noun ~Wecker~ ("alarm clock") is transparently "the thing that wakes you" â€” a straightforward agent noun. The compound ~aufwecken~ ("to wake up," with emphasis on the completion of the action) adds the ~auf-~ prefix for thoroughness. The related word ~erwecken~ ("to awaken, to arouse") has developed a strong figurative and religious sense: ~Erweckung~ ("awakening, revival") refers to spiritual renewal, as in the ~Erweckungsbewegung~ ("revival movement").
en.wehren:
From MHG ~weren~, from OHG ~werien~, from Proto-West Germanic *~warjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~warjanؤ…~ ("to defend, to ward off, to protect"), from PIE *~wer-~ ("to close, to cover, to protect"). Cognate with English ~weir~ (a defensive barrier across a stream), ~ward~, ~warden~, ~guard~ (via Old French, from Frankish *~wardإچn~), Dutch ~weren~, Old Norse ~verja~, Gothic ~warjan~.

~Wehren~ means "to defend, to ward off" and is most commonly used reflexively: ~sich wehren~ ("to defend oneself, to resist"). The noun ~Wehr~ gives rise to ~Bundeswehr~ ("Federal Defense" â€” Germany's armed forces), ~Feuerwehr~ ("fire defense" â€” the fire department), and ~Wehrmacht~ ("defense force" â€” the name of Germany's armed forces from 1935 to 1945). The PIE root *~wer-~ ("to close, to cover, to protect") is enormously productive: it yielded Latin ~aperؤ«re~ ("to open" â€” literally "to un-cover," with privative ~a-~), whence English ~aperture~; Latin ~operؤ«re~ ("to cover"), whence ~cover~ itself (via Old French); and Sanskrit ~vrج¥ل¹‡أ³ti~ ("he covers, he closes"). The English words ~warn~, ~warrant~, ~garrison~, and ~guarantee~ all trace back through different paths to this same defensive root.
en.weichen:
From Middle High German ~wؤ«chen~, from Old High German ~wؤ«chan~ ("to yield, give way"), from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«kwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«kwanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒweyg-~ ("to start moving, to distance oneself, to bend, wind"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Old English ~wؤ«can~ ("to yield, give way"), Old Norse ~vؤ«kja~ ("to turn, retreat"), Swedish ~vika~ ("to give way, fold").

~Weichen~ means "to yield, to give way, to retreat." It is not to be confused with the weak verb ~weichen~ ("to soften, to soak"), which derives from a different source (~weich~ "soft"). The strong verb's PIE root *~hâ‚ƒweyg-~ is also the ancestor of Old Norse ~vأ­k~ ("bay, inlet" -- a place where the coastline bends), which gave us the word ~Viking~: people of the inlets, or perhaps people who turn aside (i.e., raiders who venture out). The connection between yielding and bending is preserved in the cognate Swedish ~vika~, which means both "to yield" and "to fold."
en.weinen:
From Middle High German ~weinen~, from Old High German ~weinإچn~ ("to cry, lament, cry woe"), from Proto-Germanic *~wainإچnؤ…~ ("to wail, weep"), from PIE *~wأ،y~ ("woe!"). Cognate with Gothic ~wainon~ ("to suffer"), Old Norse ~veina~ ("to wail"), Old English ~wؤپnian~ ("to bewail"), and Dutch ~wenen~ ("to weep, to cry"). Related to ~Weh~ ("woe, pain"), from Proto-Germanic *~waiwأ´~, from the same exclamatory root.

The connection between ~weinen~ and English ~woe~ reveals that both descend from what was originally an exclamation of grief â€” a raw sound of pain that became grammaticalized into a verb ("to express woe") and a noun ("the state of grief"). The PIE exclamation *~wأ،y~ also produced Latin ~vae~ ("woe!," as in ~vae victis~ "woe to the vanquished") and Old Irish ~fأ،e~ ("woe"). The German noun ~Wein~ ("wine") is an unrelated homograph, borrowed from Latin ~vؤ«num~ â€” a false-friend trap that has confused many a beginning German student. The distinction between ~weinen~ ("to cry, to weep") and ~heulen~ ("to howl, to bawl") mirrors the ~lأ¤cheln~/~lachen~ pair in reverse: ~weinen~ is dignified, quiet grief, while ~heulen~ is loud and unrestrained.
en.weisen:
From Middle High German ~wؤ«sen~, from Old High German ~wؤ«sen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wؤ«sijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«sijanؤ…~, a denominative verb derived from *~wؤ«saz~ ("wise, knowing"), from Proto-Indo-European *~weyd-so-s~ ("knowledgeable"), from *~weyd-~ ("to see, know"). A class I strong verb.

The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see, know") is one of the most productive in the entire Indo-European family. It yields Latin ~videإچ~ ("I see"), Sanskrit ~veda~ ("I know"), and Ancient Greek ~eأ­dإچ~ ("I see; I know"). In Germanic, the root produced both ~wissen~ ("to know") â€” a preterite-present verb â€” and the adjective ~weise~ ("wise"), whence ~weisen~. English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~wizard~ all derive from the same PIE root, as does English ~vision~ (via Latin ~vidؤ“re~). The two modern German senses of ~weisen~ â€” "to show, point" and "to direct, expel" (as in ~ausweisen~, "to expel or identify," and ~verweisen~, "to refer or rebuke") â€” both flow from the underlying idea of directing knowledge or attention: to point is to make someone see, and to expel is to direct away. The noun ~Beweis~ ("proof, evidence") is a nominalization of the same root, preserving the ancient link between seeing and knowing.
en.weiterentwickeln:
A transparent modern compound of ~weiter-~ + ~entwickeln~:

- ~entwickeln~: From ~ent-~ ("un-") + ~wickeln~ ("to wrap"), a calque of French ~dأ©velopper~ (itself from Old French ~des-~ + ~voloper~ "to wrap"). ~Wickeln~ derives from MHG ~wickeln~, a denominative of ~wickel~, from OHG ~wickilؤ«~ ("fibers wound around the distaff"), from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«kanؤ…~ ("to bend"), from PIE *~weg-~ ("to weave"). The ~ent-~ prefix reverses the wrapping, yielding "to unwrap" and hence "to develop."
- ~weiter-~: From ~weit~ ("wide, far") + comparative suffix ~-er~. ~Weit~ descends from MHG ~wؤ«t~, OHG ~wؤ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«daz~ ("wide"), from PIE *~wi-ito-~ ("gone apart"). As a verbal prefix, ~weiter-~ means "further, onward," indicating continuation or advancement.

The metaphor is vivid: to ~entwickeln~ is to "un-wrap," revealing what was concealed, and to ~weiterentwickeln~ is to carry that unfolding further. English ~wick~ (as in a candle wick) is a cognate of ~wickeln~, both descending from the same OHG root for fibers wound around a distaff. The word ~entwickeln~ as a calque of French ~dأ©velopper~ is itself a remarkable cultural artifact -- 18th-century German intellectuals systematically replaced French loanwords with native German equivalents, and this one stuck permanently. Its separable-prefix compound ~weiterentwickeln~ is a characteristically German formation that would require a whole phrase in English: "to develop further" or "to advance."
en.weitergeben:
A transparent compound of ~weiter-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (via Old Norse ~gefa~, which displaced the native Old English ~giefan~), Dutch ~geven~, Swedish ~ge~.
- ~weiter-~: From ~weit~ ("wide, far") + comparative suffix ~-er~. ~Weit~ from MHG ~wؤ«t~, OHG ~wؤ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«daz~, from PIE *~wi-ito-~ ("gone apart"). As a separable prefix, ~weiter-~ means "further, onward," indicating continuation or transmission.

The literal sense is "to give further" -- to pass something along. English ~give~ is the direct Germanic cognate, though modern English actually owes its form to Old Norse ~gefa~ rather than the native Old English ~giefan~ (which would have yielded something like *~yive~). Both ultimately trace back to PIE *~gت°ebت°-~, which also gave Latin ~habؤ“re~ ("to have") -- a fascinating semantic flip where "to give" and "to have" share a root, possibly through an original meaning of "to grasp, to take." The separable prefix makes for memorable word order in main clauses: ~Er gibt die Nachricht weiter~ ("He passes the message on"), with the prefix stranded at the end of the sentence, a feature that famously bewildered Mark Twain.
en.weitergehen:
Compound of ~weiter-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from OHG ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from PIE *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave, to go"). Cognate with Dutch ~gaan~, English ~go~, Swedish ~gأ¥~, and Danish ~gأ¥~.
- ~weiter-~: From the comparative form of ~weit~ ("wide, far"), from MHG ~wؤ«t~, from OHG ~wؤ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«daz~ ("wide"), from PIE *~wi-~ ("apart"). As a verbal prefix, it means "further, onward, continuing."

The compound means "to continue, to go on, to move forward" â€” ~Das Leben geht weiter~ ("Life goes on"), ~Bitte gehen Sie weiter~ ("Please move along"). The ~h~ in modern ~gehen~ was inserted by scribal analogy with ~sehen~ ("to see"), where the ~h~ had become silent but was retained in spelling; Old High German ~gؤپn~ had no ~h~ at all. The PIE root *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ is notable for being a suppletive verb in many daughter languages: English ~go~/~went~ borrows its past tense from ~wend~; German ~gehen~/~ging~ borrows from a different root entirely. The expression ~So geht das nicht weiter~ ("This can't go on like this") is a quintessential German complaint, while ~Weitergehen!~ as a one-word command from police or authorities ("Move along!") captures the authoritarian efficiency of the separable-prefix verb used as an imperative.
en.weiterleiten:
From ~weiter-~ + ~leiten~. Compound of:

- ~leiten~: From MHG ~leiten~, from OHG ~leiten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laidijanؤ…~, a causative of *~lؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to pass, go by"), from PIE *~leyt-~ ("to go forth, depart"). Cognate with English ~lead~.
- ~weiter-~: From ~weiter~, the comparative form of ~weit~ ("wide, far"), from MHG ~wؤ«ter~, from OHG ~wؤ«tar~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«daz~ ("wide"), ultimately from PIE *~wأ©yhâ‚پteros~, a comparative of *~wأ©yhâ‚پ-~ ("to pursue, go after"). The prefix conveys the sense of continuing further, passing onward.

The compound literally means "to lead further" -- to forward, redirect, or relay. In modern usage, ~weiterleiten~ is the standard word for forwarding an email or passing along a message, giving an ancient causative verb a thoroughly digital-age application. The base verb ~leiten~ as a causative of "to go" means literally "to cause to go" -- making ~weiterleiten~ "to cause to go further." Cognate with English ~lead~ (from Old English ~lا£dan~, the same Proto-West Germanic causative).
en.weiterlesen:
~weiter-~ + ~lesen~. Compound of ~weiter-~ + ~lesen~:

- ~lesen~: From MHG ~lأ«sen~, from OHG ~lesan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lesan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lesanؤ…~ ("to gather, collect"), from PIE *~les-~ ("to gather, collect").
- ~weiter-~: The comparative form of ~weit~ ("far, wide"), meaning "further, continuing." ~Weit~ derives from MHG ~wؤ«t~, from OHG ~wؤ«d~, from Proto-Germanic *~wؤ«daz~, from PIE *~hâ‚پweydت°hâ‚پ-~ ("to divide, separate"), a univerbation from *~dwi-~ ("apart, asunder") + *~dت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to do, place"). As a separable prefix, ~weiter-~ means "to continue doing (an action)."

~Weiterlesen~ means "to continue reading, to read on." The prefix ~weiter-~ is one of the most productive separable prefixes in modern German, attachable to almost any activity verb to express continuation: ~weiterarbeiten~ ("to keep working"), ~weitermachen~ ("to carry on"), ~weitersprechen~ ("to keep talking"). The underlying image is spatial â€” reading ~further~, progressing to a more distant point in a text. The root verb ~lesen~ has its own famous semantic shift: PIE *~les-~ meant "to gather, collect" (as in gathering scattered grains), and Latin ~legere~ underwent the identical evolution from "to gather" to "to read" â€” presumably because reading was originally conceived as gathering letters or meanings from a surface, just as one gathered crops from a field. English ~wide~ is a direct cognate of ~weit~, sharing the PIE root.
en.weiأںen: Derived from the adjective ~weiأں~ ("white") with the verbal suffix ~-en~, meaning "to whiten, to whitewash." The adjective ~weiأں~ descends from Middle High German ~wؤ«z~, from Old High German ~wؤ«z~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hwؤ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~hwؤ«taz~, from PIE *~ل¸±weytos~ ("bright, to shine"). Cognate with English ~white~, Dutch ~wit~, Swedish ~vit~, and Gothic ~hweits~, all from the same Germanic root. The initial ~hw-~ cluster, preserved in English spelling as ~wh-~, was lost in German (as in ~was~/~what~, ~wer~/~who~). The verb ~weiأںen~ specifically denotes the act of applying whitewash or lime-paint to walls, a practice so common in German-speaking lands that it gave rise to the surname ~Weiأںer~. The PIE root *~ل¸±weytos~ also produced Sanskrit ~sveta~ ("white, bright"), Lithuanian ~sviesti~ ("to shine"), and Old Church Slavonic ~svetإ­~ ("light"), revealing an ancient Indo-European association between whiteness and luminosity. In German, the same root branches into ~Weizen~ ("wheat," the pale grain), connecting agriculture and color in a single etymological thread.
en.wenden: From Middle High German ~wenden~, from Old High German ~wenten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wandijanؤ…~ ("to turn"), the causative of *~windanؤ…~ ("to wind, wrap"), from Proto-Indo-European *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, wind, braid"). English ~wend~ is a direct cognate, surviving in the fixed phrase "to wend one's way." Old English ~wendan~ ("to turn, go") gave Middle English ~wenden~ before it was gradually displaced by ~go~; only the idiom "wend one's way" endures. The causative relationship between *~windanؤ…~ and *~wandijanؤ…~ is the same pattern seen in German ~winden~ ("to wind") and ~wenden~ ("to turn something") â€” the a-grade vowel in ~wenden~ is the Indo-European causative ablaut grade, meaning "to cause to wind." Armenian ~gind~ ("ring") is a non-Germanic cognate preserving the original sense of circular turning.
en.werben: From Middle High German ~werben~, from Old High German ~werban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hwerban~, from Proto-Germanic *~hwerbanؤ…~ ("to turn, move about, wander"). Cognate with Dutch ~werven~ ("to recruit"), English ~wharve~ ("to turn"), and Icelandic ~hverfa~ ("to turn, disappear"). The semantic evolution from "to turn, move about" to "to recruit, advertise, woo" followed a path through "to be busy, to go about doing something" to "to go about seeking" and finally "to solicit." This is remarkably parallel to the English word ~advertise~, which comes from Latin ~advertere~ ("to turn toward"). Both languages independently linked the concept of turning with the act of seeking attention. The related noun ~Werbung~ ("advertising") is ubiquitous in modern German, and ~Bewerber~ ("applicant") preserves the old sense of one who "goes about" seeking a position. English ~wharf~ is a distant cousin, from Old English ~hwearf~ ("heap, embankment"), related to the same root via the notion of a turning-place for ships. The Faroese cognate ~hvأ¸rva~ ("to disappear") preserves yet another semantic branch: turning away until one vanishes.
en.werden: From Middle High German ~wأ«rden~, from Old High German ~werdan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~werأ¾an~, from Proto-Germanic *~werأ¾anؤ…~, from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn"), with semantic shift "to turn" > "to become (turn into)." The use as a passive auxiliary is old and found throughout West Germanic, but the use as a future auxiliary is a Middle High German innovation, originating in inchoative constructions with the present participle. Since the 14th century, the participle began to be replaced with the infinitive, probably by analogy with the older future auxiliaries ~wollen~ and ~sollen~, which ~werden~ has since displaced in Modern German. Also cognate with English ~weird~, from Old English ~wyrd~ ("fate, destiny"), literally "that which becomes" â€” a noun derived from the same Proto-Germanic *~werأ¾anؤ…~. Shakespeare's Weird Sisters in ~Macbeth~ were originally the Fate Sisters; the sense shifted from "fate" â†’ "supernatural" â†’ "uncanny" â†’ "strange." Other cognates include Dutch ~worden~, obsolete English ~worth~, Swedish ~varda~, Latin ~vertإچ~ ("to turn").
en.werfen:
From Middle High German ~wأ«rfen~, from Old High German ~werfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~werpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~werpanؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~wergت·-~ ("to bend, spin, warp"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with Old Saxon ~werpan~, Dutch ~werpen~, Old Norse ~verpa~, Gothic ~wairpan~, and English ~warp~.

The PIE root *~wergت·-~ ("to bend, spin, warp") reflects the original sense of a rotational or twisting motion â€” the physical action of winding up before releasing a projectile. English ~warp~ preserves the root in a different semantic branch: the ~warp~ of a loom is the set of threads stretched lengthwise under tension, and the verb "to warp" means to bend or twist out of shape. German ~werfen~ has developed an exceptionally wide range of compound and idiomatic uses: ~verwerfen~ ("to reject," literally "to throw away"), ~entwerfen~ ("to design, draft," literally "to throw out a plan"), ~einwerfen~ ("to throw in, interject"), and the noun ~Wurf~ ("throw, litter of animals"). The ablaut series ~wirft~/~warf~/~geworfen~ follows the class III pattern shared with ~sterben~.
en.werten:
Derived from ~Wert~ ("value, worth"). ~Wert~ descends from MHG ~wert~, from OHG ~werd~, from Proto-Germanic *~werأ¾az~ ("worth, worthy"), from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn"), related to Latin ~versus~ ("turned") and English ~worth~. The modern spelling with ~-t~ (rather than historical ~-d~) was generalized from the uninflected form.

~Werten~ means "to evaluate, to rate, to judge the value of" â€” a denominal verb transparently formed from ~Wert~. It is common in sports commentary (~Die Jury wertet die Leistung~, "The judges rate the performance"), academic contexts, and legal language. The deeper etymology is striking: PIE *~wert-~ meant "to turn," and the connection to value is through the concept of exchange â€” turning goods over in a transaction. This same root produced Latin ~vertere~ ("to turn"), whence English ~verse~, ~version~, ~convert~, and ~controversy~. The Germanic branch took the "exchange" sense and solidified it into "worth" â€” what something is valued at when turned over in trade. English ~worth~, ~worthy~, and ~worship~ (originally "worthship") all descend from this root.
en.widersprechen:
From Middle High German ~widersprechen~, from Old High German ~widarsprehhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~withrasprekan~. Compound of ~wider-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprechen~, from OHG ~sprehhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekanan~, probably from PIE *~spregh-~ ("to crackle"). Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~ and English ~speak~.
- ~wider-~: From MHG ~wider-~, from OHG ~widar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~withra~, from Proto-Germanic *~withra~ ("against, back"), from PIE *~wi-tero-~ ("further apart, against"). This prefix adds the sense of opposition or contradiction. Not to be confused with ~wieder~ ("again"), though both descend from the same root.

The PIE root *~spregh-~ originally meant "to crackle" or "to make a sharp noise," and the semantic path from "crackling sounds" to "speaking" likely passed through the notion of rapid, animated vocal sounds. Lithuanian ~sprageti~ ("to crackle, to patter") preserves the original onomatopoeic sense. The compound ~widersprechen~ literally means "to speak against," and it is cognate with the archaic English ~withspeak~ (now obsolete but attested in Middle English). The distinction between ~wider~ ("against") and ~wieder~ ("again") is one of the classic pitfalls for German learners, though historically they are the same word that split into two spellings to differentiate two divergent meanings.
en.widmen: From Middle High German ~widemen~, from Old High German ~widimen~ ("to gift, give as a gift"), from ~widimo~ ("dowry, gift"), from Proto-West Germanic *~weأ¾mإچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~wetmأ´~ ("dowry, bride-price"). The noun ~Wittum~ (archaic for "dower, widow's portion") preserves this root in modern German. Note that despite the superficial resemblance, ~Wittum~ is not related to ~Witwe~ ("widow") â€” they are false friends at the etymological level. The semantic journey of ~widmen~ runs from "to give a bride-price" to "to assign, bestow, dedicate" to the modern sense of dedicating a book or composition to someone.
en.wiedergeben:
From Middle High German ~widergأ«ben~, from Old High German ~widargeban~. Compound of ~wieder-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~ ("to bring, reach, give"), from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~wieder-~: From MHG ~wider-~, from OHG ~widar~, from Proto-Germanic *~wiأ¾ra-~ ("against, back"), from PIE *~wi-~ ("apart, in two"), with an original sense of "in return."

The literal meaning "to give back, return" extends to "to reproduce, render, depict" -- giving back in the sense of representing faithfully. A translation ~gibt den Originaltext wieder~ ("renders the original text"); a recording ~gibt die Stimme wieder~ ("reproduces the voice"). Old High German ~widargeban~ already carried this dual sense over a thousand years ago. The PIE root *~gت°ebت°-~ also gave Irish ~gabhaim~ ("I take") -- a reminder that "give" and "take" were once semantically closer, both involving the transfer of something between hands.
en.wiederholen:
From Middle High German ~widerholen~, from Old High German ~widarholإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wiأ¾raholإچn~. Compound of ~wieder-~ + ~holen~:

- ~holen~: From MHG ~holen~, ~holn~, from OHG ~holإچn~, ~holؤ“n~ ("to fetch, to get, to draw"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holإچn~. Cognate with English ~haul~.
- ~wieder-~: Meaning "again, back," from Proto-Germanic *~wiأ¾ra~ ("against, back"), from PIE *~wi-tero-~ ("more apart"), a comparative formation from *~wi-~ ("apart, in two"). As an inseparable prefix (stress on ~-ho-~), ~wieder-~ conveys repetition.

~Wiederholen~ (inseparable, stress on ~-ho-~) means "to repeat, to review" â€” literally "to fetch again." This is distinct from the separable ~wieder holen~ (stress on ~wie-~), which means "to fetch back, to retrieve" â€” ~Hol dir das Buch wieder!~ ("Go get the book back!"). The stress difference creates a minimal pair: ~widerHOLen~ = "to repeat" vs. ~WIEderholen~ = "to retrieve." Cognate with Dutch ~wederhalen~, Saterland Frisian ~wierhoalje~, OHG ~widarhalإچn~. The concept of learning through repetition is baked into the word: to learn is to fetch knowledge again and again, each time drawing it closer.
en.wiegen:
German has two distinct verbs spelled ~wiegen~, both from Middle High German ~wigen~:

Sense 1 ("to weigh"): From MHG ~wigen~, a northwestern variant of ~wegen~, which generalized the present-singular stem vowel ~i~ (from MHG ~wige~, ~wiges~, ~wiget~) across the whole paradigm. From OHG ~wegan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wegan~, from Proto-Germanic *~weganؤ…~ ("to move, carry, weigh"), from PIE *~weاµت°-~ ("to convey, move"). Cognate with English ~weigh~, Dutch ~wegen~, Danish ~veje~, Icelandic ~vega~. The original ~e~-form survives in the doublet ~wأ¤gen~, now restricted to figurative senses and derivatives like ~abwأ¤gen~ ("to weigh up") and ~erwأ¤gen~ ("to consider").

Sense 2 ("to rock, cradle"): From MHG ~wigen~, derived from ~wige~ ("cradle," modern ~Wiege~), from OHG ~wؤ«ga~, ~wiega~, likely related to Proto-West Germanic *~waggjan~ ("to cause to move"), from PIE *~weاµت°-~ (the same root as sense 1). The connection is movement: weighing involves the motion of a balance, and rocking involves the swaying of a cradle.

Both senses thus trace back to the same PIE notion of movement, though they arrived at their modern meanings by different paths. The PIE root *~weاµت°-~ also gave Latin ~vehere~ ("to carry"), source of English ~vehicle~, ~vector~, and ~convey~.
en.wirken: From Middle High German ~wirken~, from Old High German ~wirken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wirkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wirkijanؤ…~, derived from *~werkؤ…~ ("work"), from PIE *~wأ©rاµom~, from PIE *~werاµ-~ ("to make, work"). This root also yields the English noun ~work~ and, via Greek ~ل¼”دپخ³خ؟خ½~ ("work", the direct reflex of PIE *~wأ©rاµom~), the English loanword ~energy~. Early on, ~wirken~ merged with the dialectal ~wأ¼rken~ (from OHG ~wurken~, PWGmc *~wurkijan~, PGmc *~wurkijanؤ…~, a closely related form); the ~i~-form prevailed in Central German, the ~أ¼~-form in Upper German, with the ~أ¼~-form displaced from the written language in the 18th century. Cognate with English ~work~ and ~wrought~ (the archaic past tense of ~work~, from the same PGmc *~wurkijanؤ…~), Dutch ~werken~.
en.wissen: From Middle High German ~wiب¥ب¥en~, from Old High German ~wiب¥ب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~witan~, from Proto-Germanic *~witanؤ…~, from PIE *~woyde~ ("to see, to know"), a stative perfect from the root *~weyd-~ ("to see"). A preterite-present verb â€” its present-tense conjugation (~weiأں~, ~weiأںt~, ~weiأں~) descends from an original perfect formation meaning "I have seen" and hence "I know," while its past tense ~wusste~ was built anew as a weak form. Cognate with Dutch ~weten~, Danish ~vide~, Swedish ~veta~, English ~wit~, and Latin ~video~ ("I see").
en.wohnen:
From Middle High German ~wonen~, from Old High German ~wonؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wunؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~wunؤپnؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, love, strive for"). Cognate with Dutch ~wonen~, English ~wont~ ("accustomed"), Old English ~ؤ،ewunian~ ("to be accustomed to, dwell").

The PIE root *~wenhâ‚پ-~ connects dwelling with desiring: to inhabit a place is to love it enough to remain. The same root underlies German ~Wonne~ ("bliss, rapture"), ~wأ¼nschen~ ("to wish"), and ~gewinnen~ ("to win, gain") â€” in each case, the core sense is striving toward what one loves or desires. English ~wont~ (as in "as was his wont") descends from Old English ~ؤ،ewunian~ ("to be accustomed to, dwell"), the same verb as Proto-West Germanic *~wunؤ“n~: to be ~wont~ is to be "dwelt," habituated into a place or practice. English ~win~ (from the same *~wenhâ‚پ-~ root via *~winnanؤ…~) preserves the striving sense â€” to win was originally to labor, struggle toward the desired goal, not merely to come first. The cluster of ~wohnen~, ~Wonne~, ~wأ¼nschen~, ~gewinnen~, and English ~wont~ and ~win~ all radiate from the same ancient notion: the thing you love enough to reach for, dwell in, or be shaped by. Cognate with Dutch ~wonen~, Afrikaans ~woon~, English ~wont~, Gothic ~wunan~.
en.wollen: From Middle High German ~wollen~, ~wأ¶llen~, ~wullen~, ~wellen~, from Old High German ~wollen~, ~wellen~, from a blend of Proto-West Germanic *~willjan~ and *~walljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wiljanؤ…~ and *~waljanؤ…~, ultimately from PIE *~welhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, choose"). A preterite-present modal verb. Cognate with Low German ~willen~, Dutch ~willen~, English ~will~, West Frisian ~wolle~, Danish ~ville~, Swedish ~vilja~. Related to ~wأ¤hlen~ ("to choose"), from the same PIE root.
en.wundern:
From Middle High German ~wundern~, from Old High German ~wuntarإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~wundrإچnؤ…~, derived from the noun *~wundrؤ…~ ("wonder, miracle"), whence German ~Wunder~. The noun *~wundrؤ…~ may derive from PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to desire, wish for, strive after, win, love") via a form *~wnج¥hâ‚پ-tro-m~, suggesting the original sense was something "wished for" or "longed for" -- hence marvellous.

The verb's reflexive usage, ~sich wundern~ ("to be surprised, to wonder"), is the most common in modern German. The non-reflexive transitive ~das wundert mich~ ("that surprises me") places the wondering person as the object, not the subject -- a grammatical pattern shared by several German verbs of emotion (~freuen~, ~أ¤rgern~, ~langweilen~). English ~wonder~ is not a direct cognate; it derives from Old English ~wundrian~, from Proto-Germanic *~wundrإچnؤ…~, making it in fact a sibling -- both descend from the same Proto-Germanic verb, though their respective noun bases (~Wunder~ vs. ~wonder~) diverged phonetically through the High German consonant shift.
en.wأ¤hlen: From Middle High German ~weln~, from ~welen~, ~wellen~, from Old High German ~wellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~walljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~waljanؤ…~, ultimately from PIE *~welhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, choose"). The modern umlaut vowel ~أ¤~ developed from the influence of the second- and third-person singular forms in Middle High German. As the ~wollen~ entry notes, ~wأ¤hlen~ and ~wollen~ share the same PIE root â€” an elegant reminder that willing and choosing were once a single concept in Proto-Indo-European. Cognate with English ~well~ (in the archaic sense "to wish well"), Dutch ~willen~ (from the same root via a parallel path).
en.wأ¤hren:
From Middle High German ~wأ«ren~, ~wأ«rn~, from Old High German ~wأ«rؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~weت€ؤ“n~, related to OHG ~wesen~ ("to be, exist"). The deeper connection runs through Proto-Germanic *~wؤ“raz~ ("true"), from PIE *~wehâ‚پros~ ("true"), from *~wehâ‚پ-~ ("true, trustworthy").

The semantic thread connecting "to last, endure" with "true" is the idea that what is true is what stands firm and persists. This same root gave German ~wahr~ ("true"), ~Wahrheit~ ("truth"), and the now-archaic ~Wesen~ ("being, essence"). The famous German proverb ~Was lange wأ¤hrt, wird endlich gut~ ("What lasts long turns out good in the end") captures the verb's core meaning. Latin ~vؤ“rus~ ("true"), Irish ~fأ­or~ ("true"), and Slavic *~vؤ›ج€ra~ ("faith") are all cognates from the same PIE root, showing that the ancient link between duration and truth runs across the entire Indo-European family.
en.wأ¼nschen: From Middle High German ~wأ¼nschen~, ~wunschen~, from Old High German ~wunsken~ ("to choose, wish, desire"), from Proto-West Germanic *~wunskijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wunskijanؤ…~, derived from *~wunskaz~ ("wish, desire"), from PIE *~wenhâ‚پ-~ ("to wish, love"). The same PIE root underlies German ~Wonne~ ("bliss, rapture") and English ~win~ â€” both preserving the old sense of "desired thing, pleasure." The connection between wishing and winning reflects a Proto-Germanic world in which what one wished for was what one strove to obtain. Cognate with Dutch ~wensen~, English ~wish~, Danish ~أ¸nske~, and â€” across a vast linguistic distance â€” Sanskrit ~vؤپل¹ƒchati~ ("to wish, desire").
en.wأ¼rdigen:
From ~wأ¼rdig~ ("worthy") + ~-en~. ~Wأ¼rdig~ derives from ~Wأ¼rde~ ("dignity, worth") + ~-ig~. ~Wأ¼rde~ descends from MHG ~wirde~, from OHG ~wirdؤ«~, ultimately related to Proto-Germanic *~werأ¾az~ ("valuable, worthy"), from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn"). Cognate with English ~worth~ and ~worthy~.

~Wأ¼rdigen~ means "to honor, to appreciate, to acknowledge the worth of" -- and, in a less common construction, ~keines Blickes wأ¼rdigen~ ("to not deem worthy of a single glance"), which expresses withering contempt. The PIE root *~wert-~ ("to turn") connects ~Wأ¼rde~ to Latin ~vertere~ ("to turn"), whence English ~convert~, ~divert~, and ~versus~; the Germanic branch produced ~werden~ ("to become," literally "to turn into"), ~-wأ¤rts~ ("-ward," as in ~vorwأ¤rts~), and English ~worth~. The development from "turning" to "exchanging" to "having value" to "being worthy" to "dignity" traces a beautiful arc across five millennia of semantic evolution.
en.zahlen:
From Middle High German ~zaln~, ~zalen~, from Old High German ~zalإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~talإچnؤ…~ ("to tell, recount; to count, reckon"). Cognate with Bavarian ~zoin~, Dutch ~talen~, Swedish ~tala~, English ~tale~.

~Zahlen~ ("to pay") and ~zأ¤hlen~ ("to count") are siblings from the same Proto-Germanic root *~talإچnؤ…~. This root carried both counting and narrating from the beginning â€” a duality visible in English ~tell~ (to count: ~teller~ at a bank, ~telling beads~; to narrate: to tell a story) and ~tale~ (a story, but also archaic "a count, tally"). The semantic path from "to count" to "to pay" is natural: payment requires counting out the correct amount. In medieval German, ~zalen~ meant both to count and to pay, and the modern distinction between ~zahlen~ and ~zأ¤hlen~ reflects the same productive split that gave English ~tell~ and ~tale~ their divergent senses. Cognate with Dutch ~betalen~ (via the same root), English ~tale~ and ~tell~, Danish ~tale~ ("to speak"), Swedish ~tala~ ("to speak").
en.zaubern:
From Middle High German ~zoubern~, from Old High German ~zouberإچn~, ~zoubarإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taubrإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taubrإچnؤ…~, derived from *~taubrؤ…~ ("magic, sorcery"). Cognate with English ~tiver~ (a red pigment, perhaps originally used in magical rituals) and Dutch ~toveren~ ("to do magic"), Old Norse ~taufr~ ("magic, sorcery").

The origin of Proto-Germanic *~taubrؤ…~ is uncertain, but it may be linked to *~tawjanؤ…~ ("to make, prepare"), suggesting that magic was originally understood as a kind of crafting or making. The Gothic Bible translator Wulfila used ~taujan~ for "to do, make," and the idea of magic as fabrication -- making something appear from nothing -- fits this connection. The noun ~Zauber~ ("magic, spell") and the fairy-tale figure ~Zauberer~ ("wizard, sorcerer") both stem from this root. Interestingly, the old Germanic word for magic survived where many other pagan-era terms did not, resisting replacement by Latin or Christian vocabulary.
en.zeichnen: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, symbol"), from Middle High German ~zeichen~, from Old High German ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taiknؤ…~, *~taiknaz~ ("sign"), from Proto-Indo-European *~deyل¸±-~ ("to show, point"). Cognate with Dutch ~teken~, English ~token~, Danish ~tegn~, Swedish ~tecken~. The PIE root *~deyل¸±-~ is extraordinarily productive: it also underlies German ~zeigen~ ("to show"), Latin ~dؤ«cere~ ("to say," giving English ~dictate~, ~judge~, ~index~), Greek ~deiknynai~ ("to show"), and Sanskrit ~diإ›-~ ("direction"). To draw (~zeichnen~) is etymologically to make a sign â€” and the sign itself descends from the same ancestral root as the act of pointing and showing. English ~token~ and German ~Zeichen~ are cognates across a millennium of linguistic divergence: both mean "a sign left behind to be read."
en.zeigen: From Middle High German ~zeigen~, from Old High German ~zeigإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taigإچn~, possibly from Proto-Germanic *~taikإچnؤ…~ (an iterative formation). An intensive derivative of Old High German ~zؤ«han~ ("to accuse"), whence modern ~zeihen~ ("to blame"). Ultimately from PIE *~deyل¸±-~ ("to point out, show"), whence also ~Zeichen~ ("sign") and English ~teach~. In Modern German, ~zeigen~ absorbed the synonymous but entirely unrelated Middle High German ~zأ¶ugen~ (cognate with Gothic ~ataugjan~).
en.zerstأ¶ren:
From Middle High German ~zerstإ“ren~, equivalent to ~zer-~ + ~stأ¶ren~:

- ~stأ¶ren~: From MHG ~stأ¶ren~, from OHG ~stإچren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~staurijan~. Cognate with English ~stir~.
- ~zer-~: From PIE *~dwأ­s~ ("twice, doubly, in two"). The prefix expresses shattering, breaking apart, or total destruction.

~Zerstأ¶ren~ means "to destroy" â€” etymologically, "to disturb to pieces." The ~zer-~ prefix is one of German's most violent: it takes the relatively mild ~stأ¶ren~ ("to disturb") and escalates it to total annihilation. The same prefix appears in ~zerbrechen~ ("to shatter"), ~zerreiأںen~ ("to tear apart"), ~zerfallen~ ("to disintegrate"), and ~zerschlagen~ ("to smash") â€” in each case converting a moderate action into a catastrophic one. The derived noun ~Zerstأ¶rung~ ("destruction") and the agent noun ~Zerstأ¶rer~ ("destroyer" â€” also the German word for the naval warship class) are transparently formed. The semantic distance between ~stأ¶ren~ ("to disturb") and ~zerstأ¶ren~ ("to destroy") â€” the difference between an annoying noise and total devastation â€” is carried entirely by the prefix ~zer-~, making it one of the most powerful morphological intensifiers in the German language. Cognate with Dutch ~verstoren~ (which uses ~ver-~ instead of ~zer-~).
en.zeugen:
From Middle High German ~ziugen~, ~geziugen~, from Old High German ~giziugon~, from ~ziug~ ("equipment, gear"), ultimately from the root of ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw, move"). The underlying noun ~Zeug~ comes from MHG ~ziuc~ ("stuff, gear"), from OHG ~giziug~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teug~, from Proto-Germanic *~teugؤ…~, related to *~teuhanؤ…~ ("to pull"), from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead").

The original meaning of ~zeugen~ was "to produce, bring forth through technical activity" -- literally to pull something into existence using tools (~Zeug~). From this developed two distinct modern senses: "to beget, procreate" (producing a child) and "to testify, bear witness" (producing evidence). The noun ~Zeuge~ ("witness") comes from the verb, as does ~Zeugnis~ ("testimony, certificate"). English ~tug~ descends from the same PIE root *~dewk-~. Cognate with Dutch ~tuig~ ("equipment, gear"), Old Norse ~tygi~ ("gear"). The semantic range of ~zeugen~ -- from making things, to making children, to making a case in court -- is a remarkable study in conceptual extension.
en.ziehen: From Middle High German ~ziehen~, from Old High German ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). A class II strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~tehn~, Gothic ~tiuhan~, Old English ~tؤ“on~, Old Norse ~toga~, West Frisian ~tsjen~. Compare obsolete English ~tee~ ("to draw, lead, proceed"); also related to English ~tie~, from the same PIE root.
en.zielen:
From ~Ziel~ ("target, goal") + ~-en~. ~Ziel~ descends from MHG ~zil~, from OHG ~zil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~til~, from Proto-Germanic *~tilؤ…~ ("aim, goal, fixed point"). Cognate with Old English ~til~ ("suitable, fitting"), whence English ~till~ (preposition: "up to a fixed point").

~Zielen~ means "to aim" â€” both physically (~auf ein Ziel zielen~, "to aim at a target") and figuratively (~auf etwas zielen~, "to be directed at something"). The verb is a relatively transparent denominal formation: to aim is to direct oneself toward a fixed point (~Ziel~). The Proto-Germanic *~tilؤ…~ connects to a semantic field of fitness and suitability rather than spatial targets â€” Old English ~til~ meant "suitable, good, apt," and the preposition ~till~ ("up to") preserves the sense of reaching a fixed endpoint. German ~Ziel~ narrowed to the spatial and aspirational senses: the place you aim for, the goal you strive toward. The noun ~Zielscheibe~ ("target disc") makes the archery metaphor explicit, while ~Ziellinie~ ("finish line") extends it to racing.
en.zitieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~citؤپre~ ("to summon, to call forth, to set in motion"), a frequentative of ~ciؤ“re~ ("to move, stir"), from PIE *~keyhâ‚‚-~ ("to set in motion"). The Latin verb's primary sense was legal: to summon a person to appear before a court. The scholarly sense "to quote a text as evidence" developed as a metaphorical extension -- calling a written authority to testify, as it were.

German borrowed ~zitieren~ with the ~-ieren~ suffix typical of Latin and French loan verbs. It retains both the quotation sense ("to cite a passage") and, more formally, the summoning sense ("to call someone to appear"). English ~cite~ arrived via Old French from the same Latin source, while ~excite~ and ~incite~ are formed from the same root with different prefixes.
en.zubereiten:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~bereiten~:

- ~bereiten~: From MHG ~bereiten~, from OHG ~bireiten~, derived from the adjective ~bereit~ ("ready"), which descends from OHG ~bireiti~, ~reiti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raidؤ«~, from Proto-Germanic *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged"), from PIE *~hâ‚‚rehâ‚پdت°-~ ("to put in order, arrange"). Cognate with English ~ready~, Dutch ~bereid~ ("prepared"), Gothic ~garaiأ¾s~ ("arranged, ordered"), and Swedish ~redo~ ("ready").
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ ("to"), from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it means "to, toward" and often conveys purposeful completion.

The compound means "to prepare" â€” specifically food: ~eine Mahlzeit zubereiten~ ("to prepare a meal"), ~einen Cocktail zubereiten~ ("to mix a cocktail"). While ~vorbereiten~ means "to prepare" in a general sense (preparing for an exam, a trip), ~zubereiten~ is reserved almost exclusively for culinary preparation, making it one of the more semantically specialized ~zu-~ compounds. The underlying adjective ~bereit~ ("ready") connects German and English more closely than most speakers realize: English ~ready~ descends from the exact same Proto-Germanic root *~raidaz~. The Old English form ~ؤ،erا£de~ still wore its Germanic prefix ~ge-~ openly, while German replaced it with ~be-~. Both prefixes carry a sense of collective completion, and both languages independently chose to prefix the same root to create their word for "prepared."
en.zugeben:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (from Old Norse ~gefa~, displacing OE ~giefan~), Dutch ~geven~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ ("to"), from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys "to, toward" or "in addition."

~Zugeben~ means both "to admit, to confess" and "to add" (an ingredient). The confession sense is "to give to" the truth, to yield toward an admission. The culinary sense is more literal: giving something into the mixture. The duality mirrors the English verb ~concede~, from Latin ~concؤ“dere~ ("to yield, to give way"), which likewise spans both "to admit a point" and "to grant, to hand over."
en.zugreifen:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grؤ«fen~, from OHG ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~gripe~ and ~grip~, Dutch ~grijpen~, Gothic ~greipan~. The Lithuanian cognate ~griebti~ ("to seize") confirms the PIE pedigree.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ ("to"), from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it adds a sense of reaching toward and seizing.

The literal sense is "to grab toward" â€” to reach out and take hold. ~Zugreifen~ means "to grab it, to help oneself" (as when food is offered: ~greifen Sie zu!~ â€” "help yourself!"), "to seize an opportunity," or "to take decisive action." The English cognate ~grip~ preserves the physical sense most transparently, while ~gripe~ has narrowed to mean "to complain" (originally "to clutch, to cause a gripping pain"). The noun ~Zugriff~ ("access, grasp") is ubiquitous in computing and law alike. The PIE root *~gت°reyb-~ also appears in Lithuanian ~graibأ½ti~ ("to seize"), confirming that the sense of grasping has been stable for over five thousand years.
en.zukommen:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~komen~, ~quemen~, from OHG ~queman~ (rarer ~cuman~, ~chuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanan~, from PIE *~gwem-~ ("to come, to go"). Cognate with English ~come~, Dutch ~komen~, Danish ~komme~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo-~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tإچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ ("to"), from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). This prefix adds a directional sense of motion toward something.
