The initial ~qu-~ of Old High German ~queman~ (reflecting the original labio-velar *~gw-~) was gradually lost during the Middle High German period, yielding modern ~kommen~. This same PIE root *~gwem-~ also produced Latin ~venire~ ("to come"), showing how the labio-velar *~gw~ developed differently in ~Italic~ versus ~Germanic~ branches. The verb ~zukommen~ has two main modern senses: "to come toward someone" and "to be entitled to, to be due to" (~jemandem steht etwas zu~), the latter reflecting a metaphorical extension where something "comes to" a person by right.
en.zulassen:
From Middle High German ~zuolؤپب¥en~, equivalent to ~zu-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپب¥en~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥ب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤ“tan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~, from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, to leave, to be weary").
- ~zu-~: From PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). The prefix adds the sense of granting access or permitting something toward a goal.

~Zulassen~ means "to allow, to permit, to admit" â€” from allowing a possibility (~eine Ausnahme zulassen~, "to allow an exception") to admitting a person (~zum Studium zugelassen~, "admitted to study") to certifying a product (~ein zugelassenes Medikament~, "an approved medication"). The derived noun ~Zulassung~ is the standard word for official authorization â€” a vehicle registration (~Fahrzeugzulassung~), a professional license, or a university admission. The prefix ~zu-~ here implies opening a gate toward someone: what is ~zugelassen~ has been let through. Cognate with Dutch ~toelaten~.
en.zulegen:
From ~zu-~ + ~legen~. Compound of:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~, a causative of *~ligjanؤ…~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~, a causative formation from *~legت°-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo-~, from OHG ~zuo-~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~, from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it indicates direction toward, addition, or closure.

The literal meaning "to lay toward, add to" generates several figurative senses: ~sich zulegen~ can mean "to acquire, get oneself" (laying something toward one's own pile), or "to pick up speed" (~zulegen~ in the sense of adding pace). The causative relationship between ~legen~ ("to lay," transitive) and ~liegen~ ("to lie," intransitive) mirrors exactly the English pair ~lay~/~lie~, both descending from the same PIE causative formation. English ~lay~ comes from Old English ~lecgan~, from the same Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~.
en.zunehmen:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due, to allot, to distribute"). A class IV strong verb. Cognate with English ~nim~ ("to take, filch"), Dutch ~nemen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp"), and Swedish ~fأ¶rnimma~ ("to perceive").
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ ("to"), from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys addition, increase, or closure.

The compound means "to increase, to grow, to gain weight" â€” the moon ~nimmt zu~ ("waxes"), a patient ~nimmt zu~ ("gains weight"), and traffic ~nimmt zu~ ("increases"). The image is of something "taking to itself," accumulating more. Its antonym ~abnehmen~ ("to decrease, to lose weight") uses the opposite prefix ~ab-~ ("away"). The PIE root *~nem-~ had the original sense of distributing or allotting â€” it also produced Greek ~nemein~ ("to distribute, to pasture"), whence ~Nemesis~ (the goddess who distributes what is due) and ~nemesis~ (one's deserved comeuppance). The semantic journey from "distributing fairly" (PIE) to "taking" (Germanic) to "increasing" (German compound) shows how a single root can travel through three thousand years of speakers who each nudged its meaning a little further from the original.
en.zuordnen:
From ~zu-~ + ~ordnen~. Compound of:

- ~ordnen~: From MHG ~ordenen~, from OHG ~ordinإچn~, borrowed from Latin ~إچrdinؤپre~ ("to put in order, arrange"), from ~إچrdإچ~ ("row, rank, order"). The Latin source also gave rise to German ~ordinieren~ (a learned doublet) and, via French, to English ~ordain~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo-~, from OHG ~zuo-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tإچ~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~, from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys direction toward or assignment.

~zuordnen~ means "to assign, allocate, classify" â€” to place something ~toward~ its proper order or category. It is the standard verb for mapping, assigning, or attributing in both everyday and technical German (~einer Kategorie zuordnen~ = "to assign to a category"). The fact that ~ordnen~ is a Latin loanword naturalized so thoroughly that it takes native Germanic prefixes (~zu-~, ~an-~, ~ein-~, ~unter-~) illustrates how deeply Latin administrative vocabulary penetrated German during the medieval period. The noun ~Zuordnung~ ("assignment, mapping") is ubiquitous in mathematics and data science, where it denotes any mapping from one set to another.
en.zurأ¼ckfأ¼hren:

- ~fأ¼hren~: From MHG ~vأ¼eren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, a causative of ~fahren~ ("to travel, go"). The verb ~fahren~ descends from Proto-West Germanic *~faran~, from Proto-Germanic *~faranؤ…~, from PIE *~per-~ ("to carry, bring forth"). Cognate with English ~fare~ and Dutch ~varen~.
- ~zurأ¼ck-~: A compound prefix from ~zu~ + ~rأ¼ck~, where ~zu~ derives from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~, from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"), and ~rأ¼ck~ from ~Rأ¼cken~ ("back"), ultimately related to PIE *~(s)krewk-~ ("heap, hill"). Together they convey "back, returning."

As a causative, ~fأ¼hren~ originally meant "to cause to go," i.e., "to lead," while ~fahren~ simply meant "to go, travel." This causative pair is one of the clearest in German. The compound ~zurأ¼ckfأ¼hren~ means "to lead back, to trace back, to attribute to," and the intellectual sense of tracing an idea or phenomenon back to its origin is perhaps the most common usage today: ~Das lأ¤sst sich auf X zurأ¼ckfأ¼hren~ ("That can be traced back to X"). English ~fare~ preserves the intransitive root: a bus ~fare~ is the cost of going. The PIE root *~per-~ also produced Greek ~poros~ ("passage"), English ~ford~ (a crossing), and ~ferry~ (to carry across water). The noun ~Fأ¼hrer~ ("leader") is simply the agent noun of ~fأ¼hren~, though its historical associations have made the word nearly unusable in modern German outside of compound forms like ~Reisefأ¼hrer~ ("travel guide") or ~Fأ¼hrerschein~ ("driver's license").
en.zurأ¼ckgreifen:
Compound of ~zurأ¼ck-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grؤ«fen~, from OHG ~grؤ«fan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grؤ«pan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grؤ«panؤ…~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gت°reyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~, Dutch ~grijpen~.
- ~zurأ¼ck-~: From PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to") + *~(s)krewk~ ("heap, hill"); literally "to the back." The prefix means "back, returning."

The literal sense is "to grab back" â€” to reach back for something already known or available. ~Zurأ¼ckgreifen~ is typically used figuratively: ~auf etwas zurأ¼ckgreifen~ means "to fall back on something, to resort to, to draw upon" (a resource, an experience, a method). Unlike ~zugreifen~ (which means to seize something present), ~zurأ¼ckgreifen~ implies reaching into the past or into reserves. The spatial metaphor is vivid: one turns around and grasps something behind oneself. There is no single English equivalent â€” "fall back on," "resort to," "draw upon," and "have recourse to" all approximate its meaning. The prefix ~zurأ¼ck-~ is itself a compound (from ~zu~ + an old form related to ~Rأ¼cken~, "back"), making ~zurأ¼ckgreifen~ a triple-layered word: "to-back-grasp."
en.zurأ¼ckkehren:

- ~kehren~: From MHG ~kؤ“ren~ ("to turn"), from OHG ~kؤ“ren~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaizwijanؤ…~ ("to turn, direct"), of uncertain PIE origin. Kroonen derives it from PIE *~geys-~ ("to turn, become"), comparing Hittite ~kؤ«إ،a(ri)~ ("to happen, occur, become"). Cognate with Danish ~kأ¸re~ ("to drive"), Swedish ~kأ¶ra~ ("to drive"), Icelandic ~keyra~ ("to drive").
- ~zurأ¼ck-~: From MHG ~ze rأ¼cke~ ("to the back"), from ~zu~ + ~Rأ¼cken~ ("back"). Ultimately from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to") + *~(s)krewk~ ("heap, hill; back").

The Scandinavian cognates evolved differently: Danish ~kأ¸re~ and Swedish ~kأ¶ra~ now mean "to drive (a vehicle)," preserving only the sense of directing motion. The base verb ~kehren~ in the "turn" sense is largely archaic in Germany proper (replaced by ~umkehren~, ~umdrehen~, ~wenden~), but remains current in Swiss German. The derivative ~Rأ¼ckkehr~ ("return") is the standard noun for returning from a journey or exile.
en.zurأ¼ckziehen:
From ~zurأ¼ck-~ + ~ziehen~. Compound of:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanؤ…~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with English ~tug~, Gothic ~tiuhan~, Dutch ~tijgen~.
- ~zurأ¼ck-~: From MHG ~zerucke~, a compound of ~zuo~ ("to") + ~rأ¼cke~ ("back"), literally "to the back." The first element comes from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~, from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"); the second from MHG ~rأ¼cke~, ~rugge~ ("back, ridge"), from Proto-Germanic *~hrugjaz~.

The literal meaning "to pull back" extends to military retreat (~Truppen zurأ¼ckziehen~), withdrawing money (~Geld zurأ¼ckziehen~), and the reflexive ~sich zurأ¼ckziehen~ ("to withdraw, retire"). The PIE root *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead") also gave Latin ~dإ«cere~ ("to lead"), source of English ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, and ~educate~ -- all words about pulling or leading in various directions. Cognate with Dutch ~terugtrekken~ ("to pull back").
en.zusammenarbeiten:
Compound of ~zusammen-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From OHG ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijanؤ…~ ("to toil, to labor"), ultimately from PIE *~hâ‚ƒأ³rbت°os~ ("orphan"). Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, Old Saxon ~arvedian~, Gothic ~arbaidjan~.
- ~zusammen-~: From MHG ~zusamen~, from OHG ~zisamane~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ + *~samanؤ“~ ("together"), ultimately from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to") + *~sem~ ("one, together"). As a separable prefix, it conveys the sense of doing something jointly.

~Zusammenarbeiten~ means "to collaborate, to work together." The noun ~Zusammenarbeit~ ("collaboration, cooperation") is indispensable in diplomatic, scientific, and corporate contexts. The etymology of ~arbeiten~ is one of the most poignant in all of Germanic: PIE *~hâ‚ƒأ³rbت°os~ meant "orphan" â€” a child bereft of parents who must toil for others. This same root gave Greek ~orphanos~ ("orphaned"), Latin ~orbus~ ("deprived"), and English ~orphan~. The journey from "orphan's drudgery" to "work" in general reflects the social reality of the ancient Indo-European world, where the parentless child's lot defined the concept of hard labor. Old High German ~arabeit~ still meant "hardship, toil, suffering" rather than neutral "work" â€” the modern sense of productive activity is a relatively recent semantic elevation.
en.zusammenfassen:
Compound of ~zusammen~ + ~fassen~:

- ~zusammen~: From MHG ~zesamen~, from OHG ~zisamane~, ultimately from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to give, put") + *~sem~ ("together, one").
- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatإچnؤ…~ ("to fetch, hold, seize"). Cognate with English ~fetch~, Dutch ~vatten~. The PIE root may be *~pod-~ ("to fetch"), though the connection to *~ped-~ ("to walk, stumble, fall") is semantically uncertain.

The literal image is of grasping things together into one bundle. From this concrete sense, ~zusammenfassen~ developed its primary modern meaning: "to summarize" -- gathering scattered ideas into a single coherent statement. The noun ~Zusammenfassung~ is the standard German word for "summary" or "abstract."
en.zusammenstellen:
Compound of ~zusammen-~ ("together") + ~stellen~ ("to put, place"):

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~zusammen-~: From MHG ~zesamen~, from OHG ~zisamane~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ + *~samanؤ“~ ("together"), ultimately from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to") + *~sem~ ("together, one").

The compound literally means "to place together" and hence "to compile, compose, assemble." One ~stellt zusammen~ a team, a menu, a report, or a playlist. The PIE root *~sem~ ("together, one") is extraordinarily productive across the Indo-European family: it gives English ~same~, ~some~, and ~similar~ (via Latin ~similis~), Greek ~homos~ ("same," whence ~homogeneous~), and Sanskrit ~sama~ ("even, equal"). The separable nature of the prefix is visible in main clauses: ~Sie stellt die Liste zusammen~. The derivative ~Zusammenstellung~ ("compilation, composition") is the standard noun for any curated collection.
en.zustimmen:
Compound of ~zu~ + ~stimmen~:

- ~stimmen~: From MHG ~stimmen~, from OHG *~stimmen~ (attested in the compound ~gistimmen~), derived from the noun ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). The noun descends from OHG ~stimma~, ~stimna~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnإچ~ or *~stamnijإچ~, from PIE *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle"). Cognate with Gothic ~stibna~ ("voice"), Old English ~stefn~, ~stemn~ ("voice"), Dutch ~stem~, and Greek ~stoma~ ("mouth," whence English ~stomach~).
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tإچ~ ("to"), from PIE *~dohâ‚پ~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it means "to, toward."

The literal sense is "to voice toward," i.e., to add one's voice in support. The verb ~stimmen~ itself has a remarkable range of meanings: "to tune (an instrument)," "to be correct" (~das stimmt!~ "that's right!"), "to vote," and "to put in a mood" (~traurig stimmen~, "to make sad"). All trace back to the idea of a voice being in harmony or accord. The compound ~zustimmen~ narrows this to the specific act of expressing agreement. The related noun ~Zustimmung~ ("consent, approval") is a staple of legal and political German. The connection between ~Stimme~ ("voice") and Greek ~stoma~ ("mouth") reveals that the PIE speakers conceptualized the voice as emanating from the mouth-opening, a straightforward anatomical observation preserved in two wildly divergent word families across five millennia.
en.zwingen:
From Middle High German ~twingen~, from Old High German ~dwingan~, from Proto-Germanic *~أ¾winganؤ…~, *~أ¾winhanؤ…~. What appears to be a triple consonant shift (Germanic *~أ¾~ to OHG ~d~ to MHG ~t~ to modern ~z~) is actually a regular development that affected most words with OHG ~dw-~ and ~tw-~, compare ~Zwerg~ ("dwarf"). The English cognate ~twinge~ â€” a sharp, sudden pain that grips or squeezes â€” preserves the original physical sense of the Proto-Germanic verb: to press, to squeeze, to constrain. German ~zwingen~ elevated this physical compression into abstract compulsion: "to force, to compel, to make someone do something." The noun ~Zwang~ ("compulsion, coercion") and the adjective ~zwingend~ ("compelling, cogent") carry the same force. The medieval ~Zwinger~ â€” the narrow space between inner and outer castle walls where attackers were "squeezed" and trapped â€” survives today as the name of Dresden's famous ~Zwinger~ palace complex, built on the site of such a fortification.
en.zأ¤hlen:
From Middle High German ~zel(e)n~, ~zellen~, from Old High German ~zellan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~taljanؤ…~ ("to enumerate, count; to recount, tell"). The modern long vowel continues the second- and third-person singular of the present (where West Germanic gemination did not occur); it was reinforced by the lengthened vowel in ~Zahl~ (from MHG ~zale~, "number").

The Proto-Germanic root *~taljanؤ…~ carried both senses â€” "to count" and "to recount, narrate" â€” from the very beginning. Modern German split these into two verbs: ~zأ¤hlen~ retained "to count," while ~erzأ¤hlen~ (via the ~er-~ prefix) specialized in "to narrate." English underwent the same bifurcation: ~tell~ (from the same root) means both "to count" (as in ~teller~ at a bank, or ~telling beads~) and "to narrate." The word ~tale~ ("a story" but also archaic "a count, tally") shares this dual heritage. Cognate with Dutch ~tellen~, English ~tell~ and ~tale~, Danish ~tأ¦lle~, Gothic ~talzjan~.
en.أ¤hneln:
A 17th-century alteration of the earlier verb ~أ¤hnlichen~, from Middle High German ~anelؤ«chen~, equivalent to ~أ¤hnlich~ ("similar") + the verbal suffix ~-en~. The modern shortened conjugation in ~-eln~ may reflect a folk-etymological association with ~Ahn~ ("ancestor"), as if "to resemble" were derived from "to take after one's forebears" -- reanalyzed as ~Ahn~ + ~-eln~. Compare French ~s'apparenter أ ~ ("to be related to"), which shows a similar conceptual link between family resemblance and similarity.

The adjective ~أ¤hnlich~ itself derives from OHG ~analؤ«h~ ("similar"), literally "of one form" (~ana~ "on" + ~lؤ«h~ "body, form"), the same compound pattern that produced English ~alike~ and ~like~. The word ~Ahn~ ("ancestor"), despite the folk-etymological association, actually traces to Proto-Germanic *~anأ´~ ("forefather"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚‚en-~ ("ancestor") -- cognate with Latin ~anus~ ("old woman"), a connection that would surely surprise speakers of both languages.
en.أ¤ndern: From Middle High German ~endern~, a denominative verb derived from ~ander~ ("other, different"). ~Ander~ descends from MHG ~ander~, from OHG ~andar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anأ¾ar~, from Proto-Germanic *~anأ¾eraz~, from PIE *~hâ‚‚أ©nteros~ ("other"). The PIE root *~hâ‚‚أ©nteros~ is a comparative formation from *~hâ‚‚ent-~ ("front, forefront"), and its original sense was "the one opposite, the one facing" â€” i.e., the second of two â€” before broadening to "other." The semantic journey from "second" to "different" to "to change" completes in ~أ¤ndern~. Cognate with Hunsrik ~ennre~, English ~other~, Dutch ~ander~, Old Norse ~annarr~ (Swedish ~annan~, Danish ~anden~).
en.أ¤rgern:
From Middle High German ~argern~ ("to worsen"), from Old High German ~argerإچn~, derived from the adjective ~arg~ ("bad, wicked"). Cognate with English ~eerie~.

The adjective ~arg~ traces from MHG ~arc~, from OHG ~arg~, from Proto-West Germanic *~arg~, from Proto-Germanic *~argaz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚ƒerاµت°-~ ("to copulate; ardent"). The semantic journey from "ardent, lustful" to "bad, wicked" reflects a moral judgment on excessive passion -- what was once merely "passionate" became "wicked" as Germanic Christianity imposed new values on old vocabulary. Cognates include Lithuanian ~erإ¾أ¹s~ ("ardent, stubborn") and Hittite ~arkari~ ("to copulate"). The modern German meaning of ~أ¤rgern~ has softened considerably from "to make worse" to "to annoy, to irritate," and the reflexive ~sich أ¤rgern~ ("to be annoyed") is among the most common expressions of everyday frustration. Dutch ~erg~ ("bad, severe") preserves the older adjective sense.
en.أ¤uأںern:
From MHG ~إ«zeren~ ("to bring out, express"), deadjectival from ~أ¤uأںer~ ("outer, external"), itself the comparative of ~aus~ / ~auأںen~ ("out, outside"). ~Aus~ derives from MHG ~إ«ب¥~, from OHG ~إ«ب¥~, from Proto-West Germanic *~إ«t~, from Proto-Germanic *~إ«t~, from PIE *~أ؛d~ ("upwards, away, out"). The cognate English verb ~utter~ (as in "to utter a word") followed the same semantic path from the same root: PGmc *~إ«tizأ´~ (comparative of *~إ«t~) yielded both German ~أ¤uأںer~ and English ~outer~ / ~utter~. The physical image of moving speech outward from within is thus embedded in the very shape of the word.
en.أ¶ffnen:
From Middle High German ~offenen~, from Old High German ~offanإچn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~opanإچn~, from Proto-Germanic *~upanإچnؤ…~ ("to open, to lift"). The underlying adjective *~upanaz~ ("open") was probably originally a past participle of a lost verb *~إ«panؤ…~, derived from *~upp~ ("up"), with the original sense "lying on top" â†’ "clear, unobstructed" â†’ "open." The directional metaphor â€” "open" as "lifted up" â€” is shared across many cultures, as in English ~uplift~ (the door or lid is raised, revealing what was underneath). The adjective ~offen~ ("open") and the verb ~أ¶ffnen~ ("to open") remain distinct in German, as in the difference between ~das Fenster ist offen~ ("the window is open") and ~أ¶ffne das Fenster~ ("open the window"). en.أ¼ben:
From Middle High German ~أ¼eben~, ~uoben~, from Old High German ~uoben~, from Proto-Germanic *~إچbijanؤ…~ ("to do, practice"), the causative-iterative formation of a lost strong verb *~afanؤ…~, from PIE *~hâ‚ƒep-~ ("to labor, work, toil"). The same PIE root gives Latin ~opus~ ("work") and ~opera~ ("effort, service") â€” whence English ~opera~, ~operate~, and ~opus~. Cognate with Dutch ~oefenen~ (with an ~n~-suffix extension), Old Saxon ~إچbian~, Old Norse *~إ“fa~ (Icelandic ~أ¦fa~). The Swedish ~أ¶va~ and Danish ~أ¸ve~ are borrowed from Middle Low German ~أ´ven~ rather than inherited.

The reflexive ~sich أ¼ben in~ ("to practice at, to exercise in") preserves the iterative force of the original Proto-Germanic causative: not a one-time doing but repeated, deliberate effort. The derived noun ~أœbung~ ("exercise, practice") and the adjective ~أ¼blich~ ("usual, customary") both reflect this deep association between repeated action and established habit. German proverb: ~أœbung macht den Meister~ ("Practice makes the master") â€” a direct calque of the Latin ~exercitium facit magistrum~.
en.أ¼berarbeiten:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From Old High German ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijanؤ…~, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚ƒأ³rbت°os~ ("orphan"), from which English ~orphan~ is also derived. Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, Old Saxon ~arvedian~, Gothic ~arbaidjan~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From Middle High German ~أ¼ber~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). In this compound, the prefix conveys the sense of "over again" or doing something excessively.

The verb has two distinct meanings depending on stress: ~UMarbeiten~ (separable, "to rework") versus ~أ¼berARbeiten~ (inseparable, "to revise" or reflexively "to overwork oneself"). The reflexive ~sich أ¼berarbeiten~ ("to work oneself to exhaustion") reflects the ~أ¼ber-~ prefix in its "excessively" sense. The surprising PIE connection between "work" and "orphan" reflects an ancient reality: orphans were society's laborers, and the original sense of the Germanic root was "hardship, toil."
en.أ¼bergeben:
~أ¼ber-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gأ«ben~, from OHG ~gأ«ban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanؤ…~, from PIE *~gت°ebت°-~ ("to give").
- ~أ¼ber-~: From PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). The inseparable prefix adds a sense of transfer across a boundary.

~أœbergeben~ means "to hand over, to deliver, to transfer" â€” and, colloquially, "to vomit." The formal sense is transparent: to give ~over~, to pass something across from one person to another. The colloquial meaning follows the same spatial metaphor â€” to ~give over~ the contents of one's stomach â€” and is shared by Dutch ~overgeven~, which means both "to surrender" and "to throw up." The inseparable prefix ~أ¼ber-~ creates a fixed, non-literal meaning; contrast the separable ~أ¼bergeben~ (stressed on ~أ¼ber~), which would mean "to give excessively" but is rarely used. The noun ~أœbergabe~ ("handover, transfer") is common in legal and military contexts. English ~overgive~ existed in Middle English with the same sense of "to surrender" but has since fallen out of use, replaced by the French-derived ~surrender~. Cognate with Dutch ~overgeven~, English ~overgive~ (archaic).
en.أ¼bergehen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From Middle High German ~gؤپn~, ~gؤ“n~, from Old High German ~gؤپn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~gؤپnؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~اµت°ehâ‚پ-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with Dutch ~gaan~, English ~go~, Swedish ~gأ¥~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From Middle High German ~أ¼ber~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix it conveys "passing over" or "skipping."

Like many ~أ¼ber-~ verbs, ~أ¼bergehen~ splits into two distinct words depending on stress. The separable ~أœBERgehen~ means "to go over, to pass into" (as in ~in eine neue Phase أ¼bergehen~, "to transition into a new phase"). The inseparable ~أ¼berGEHen~ means "to skip over, to ignore" (as in ~jemanden أ¼bergehen~, "to pass someone over"). This stress-based semantic distinction is a hallmark of German prefix verbs that challenges learners and delights linguists.
en.أ¼berlassen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lؤپzen~, ~lؤپn~, from OHG ~lؤپب¥an~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lؤپtan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lؤ“tanؤ…~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~lehâ‚پd-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with English ~let~, Dutch ~laten~. Related to Latin ~lassus~ ("tired") -- the connection being the sense of "letting go" from weariness.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber-~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix (stressed on ~-lassen~), it conveys "over to someone."

Like ~أ¼bersetzen~, ~أ¼berlassen~ splits by stress. As an inseparable verb (stress on ~-lassen~: ~er أ¼berlأ¤sst~), it means "to relinquish, leave to someone, leave up to someone." As a separable verb (stress on ~أ¼ber-~: ~er lأ¤sst أ¼ber~), it is an informal alternative to ~أ¼briglassen~ ("to leave over, spare"). The inseparable sense captures a deliberate act of ceding control -- one "leaves something over" to another person's judgment or possession. The PIE root *~lehâ‚پd-~ connects ~lassen~ not only to English ~let~ but also to Latin ~lassus~ ("weary"), suggesting that the original Proto-Indo-European concept involved the weariness of releasing one's grip.
en.أ¼berleben:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~leben~, calqued from Latin ~supervؤ«vإچ~:

- ~leben~: From MHG ~lأ«ben~, from OHG ~lebؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~libbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~libjanؤ…~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to leave, cling, linger"). The semantic path from "cling, remain" to "be alive" reflects a view of life as persistence. Cognate with English ~live~, Dutch ~leven~, Swedish ~leva~, Gothic ~liban~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above").

The fact that ~أ¼berleben~ is a calque of Latin ~supervؤ«vإچ~ (itself from ~super-~ + ~vؤ«vإچ~) makes it a structural parallel to English ~survive~ (from Old French, ultimately from the same Latin source). en.أ¼berlegen:
From Middle High German ~أ¼berlegen~, past participle of ~أ¼berligen~. By surface analysis, ~أ¼ber-~ + ~legen~.

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjanؤ…~ ("to lay, cause to lie"), from PIE *~logت°أ©yeti~. A causative denominative: "to cause to lie." Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubiri~, ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix in the "to ponder" sense, ~أ¼ber-~ lends the sense of turning something over in the mind â€” laying thought upon thought.

~أœberlegen~ has two distinct existences in Modern German, distinguished only by stress and separability. The inseparable verb (stress on ~-legen~) means "to reflect, consider, ponder" â€” one lays thoughts over a problem. The adjective ~أ¼berlegen~ (also stress on ~-legen~, but derived differently) means "superior, dominant." The two arose independently: the verb from the idea of placing something mentally "over" a problem, the adjective from the literal sense of lying over or on top of someone (as in a wrestling hold). The MHG past participle ~أ¼berlegen~ from ~أ¼berligen~ ("to lie over, overpower") gave rise to the adjective; the thoughtful verb derives from ~أœberlegung~ ("reflection, consideration"). The noun ~أœberlegung~ is the standard German word for deliberate, careful consideration â€” "after much ~أœberlegung~" is a formula in formal prose. Cognate with Dutch ~overleggen~.
en.أ¼bermitteln:
A relatively modern formation, coined in the early twentieth century. Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~mitteln~:

- ~mitteln~: A denominative verb from ~Mittel~ ("means, medium"), from MHG ~mittel~, from OHG ~mittil~, from Proto-Germanic *~midlؤ…~ or *~midilؤ…~ ("middle"), cognate with English ~middle~ and Dutch ~middel~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uberi-~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). This prefix adds the sense of transmitting across a distance or barrier.

The verb means "to convey, to transmit, to communicate," literally "to mediate (something) across." It is related to the older verb ~vermitteln~ ("to mediate, to arrange"), which uses the ~ver-~ prefix instead. The noun ~Mittel~ also gives rise to ~Mittelalter~ ("Middle Ages"), ~Mittelpunkt~ ("center"), and ~Lebensmittel~ ("groceries," literally "means of life"). Despite its recent coinage, ~أ¼bermitteln~ filled a genuine lexical gap, providing a formal verb for the transmission of messages, data, or information across channels. English speakers might use "to transmit" or "to convey" in the same contexts.
en.أ¼bernehmen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nأ«men~, from OHG ~nأ«man~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemanؤ…~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubiri~, ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~, ultimately from *~upo~ + *~-i~. Meaning "over, across, above." Cognate with Ancient Greek ~hupأ©r~ ("above"), Latin ~super~ ("above"), Sanskrit ~upأ،ri~, Dutch ~over~, English ~over~, Swedish ~أ¶ver~.

The semantic range of ~أ¼bernehmen~ illustrates how "taking over" can mean taking on responsibility (~eine Aufgabe أ¼bernehmen~), assuming control (~ein Unternehmen أ¼bernehmen~), or adopting something (~eine Idee أ¼bernehmen~). The verb can also be reflexive â€” ~sich أ¼bernehmen~ means "to overextend oneself," a sense that turns the completive force of ~أ¼ber-~ against the subject. Cognate with Dutch ~overnemen~.
en.أ¼berprأ¼fen:
From ~أ¼ber-~ + ~prأ¼fen~:

- ~prأ¼fen~: From MHG ~prأ¼even~, ultimately from Latin ~probؤپre~ ("to test, approve"), a denominative of ~probus~ ("good, upright"), from PIE *~pro-bت°uH-wأ³-s~ ("being foremost"). Entered German before the ~-ieren~ suffix was productive, fully assimilated into the native weak-verb system. Cognate with English ~prove~ and ~probe~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubiri~, ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix adds thoroughness and coverage â€” to check something over, to go over it comprehensively.

~أœberprأ¼fen~ ("to check over, verify, review, audit") extends ~prأ¼fen~'s sense of testing with the thoroughness of ~أ¼ber-~: where ~prأ¼fen~ tests a student or a hypothesis, ~أ¼berprأ¼fen~ reviews a complete file, audits an account, or re-examines a decision from above. That its root ~prأ¼fen~ derives ultimately from Latin ~probus~ ("upright, good") means that every act of ~أ¼berprأ¼fen~ is etymologically an inquiry into whether something meets the standard of uprightness. en.أ¼berraschen:
~أ¼ber-~ + ~raschen~. Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~raschen~:

- ~raschen~: A now-rare verb derived from the adjective ~rasch~ ("quick, swift"), from MHG ~rasch~, ~rasche~, from OHG ~rask~, ~rasc~ ("quick, prompt, powerful"), from Proto-Germanic *~raskuz~, possibly from PIE *~Hrethâ‚‚-~ ("to run, roll").
- ~أ¼ber-~: From PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix here, ~أ¼ber-~ conveys the sense of coming upon someone from above or beyond expectation.

~أœberraschen~ means "to surprise" â€” literally "to rush over someone quickly," capturing the sudden, overwhelming quality of surprise. The adjective ~rasch~ ("quick, swift") survives independently in modern German, but the verb ~raschen~ from which ~أ¼berraschen~ was formed is essentially extinct outside this compound. The noun ~أœberraschung~ ("surprise") is one of the most common German nouns. English ~rash~ (meaning "hasty, impetuous") is a cognate of ~rasch~, borrowed into English from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch â€” so when someone acts ~rashly~, they are being etymologically ~rasch~. The underlying image of surprise as speed â€” something happening too fast to anticipate â€” is preserved intact in this compound.
en.أ¼berreichen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From Middle High German ~reichen~, from Old High German ~reihhen~, related to the adjective ~reich~ ("rich, powerful"). The adjective traces to Old High German ~rؤ«hhi~, from Proto-Germanic *~rؤ«kijaz~, a borrowing from Celtic *~rؤ«gos~ ("king"), from Proto-Indo-European *~hâ‚ƒrؤ“اµs~ ("king, ruler"), also the source of Latin ~rؤ“x~. The verb's original sense was "to make powerful, to extend," hence "to reach, to hand over."
- ~أ¼ber-~: From Middle High German ~أ¼ber~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix conveys the sense of handing something across or over to someone.

~أœberreichen~ is always inseparable and carries a formal, ceremonial tone -- one ~أ¼berreicht~ an award, a gift, or a document. The more casual equivalent is simply ~geben~ ("to give"). Cognate with Dutch ~overhandigen~, which uses a different metaphor ("over-handing") for the same formal concept.
en.أ¼berschreiten:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~schreiten~. Compare Dutch ~overschrijden~, Swedish ~أ¶verskrida~, Danish ~overskride~:

- ~schreiten~: From MHG ~schrؤ«ten~, from OHG ~skrؤ«tan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrؤ«أ¾an~ ("to step, stride"), from Proto-Germanic *~skrؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ ("to walk, glide, crawl"), from PIE *~(s)kreyt-~ ("to stride"), an extension of *~(s)krey-~ ("to go about"). Cognate with Lithuanian ~skrأ¬sti~ ("to rush, run"), Latvian ~skrأ¬et~ ("to run, fly"). Related to ~Schritt~ ("step").
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber-~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys "across, beyond."

The Proto-Germanic ancestor *~skrؤ«أ¾anؤ…~ originally meant "to walk, glide, crawl," a broad range of locomotion that narrowed to stately striding in German while Old Norse ~skrأ­أ°a~ retained the "crawl, slide" sense (whence Icelandic ~skrأ­أ°a~ "landslide").
en.أ¼bersehen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sأ«hen~, from OHG ~sأ«han~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwanؤ…~ ("to see"), from PIE *~sekت·-~ ("to see, to notice"). Cognate with English ~see~, Dutch ~zien~, Danish ~se~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys "over" in the sense of looking past something.

~أœbersehen~ means "to overlook, to miss" â€” literally to see over something and thus past it. en.أ¼bersetzen:
Sixteenth-century formation, probably modeled on Latin ~trؤپdإ«cere~ ("to lead across, translate"), perhaps through Middle Low German ~إچversetten~. Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, a causative derived from Proto-Germanic *~sitjanؤ…~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.

German ~أ¼bersetzen~ is a fascinating case of stress distinguishing meaning. With stress on ~أ¼ber-~ (separable prefix: ~er setzt أ¼ber~), it means "to cross, ferry across" -- literally to set something over a body of water. With stress on ~-setzen~ (inseparable prefix: ~er أ¼bersetzt~), it means "to translate." The separable sense has Old High German roots (~ubarsezzen~), while the inseparable "translate" sense is a sixteenth-century calque of Latin ~trؤپdإ«cere~, which similarly meant both "to lead across" physically and "to translate" metaphorically. This Latin-inspired semantic extension made German ~أ¼bersetzen~ part of a pan-European pattern: compare French ~traduire~, Italian ~tradurre~, all from the same Latin source. en.أ¼berstehen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From Middle High German ~stؤپn~, ~stؤ“n~, from Old High German ~stؤپn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stؤپn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stؤپnؤ…~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stehâ‚‚-~ ("to stand"). Cognate with English ~stand~, Dutch ~staan~, Latin ~stؤپre~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From Middle High German ~أ¼ber~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix conveys surviving through or getting past something.

The survival sense captures a vivid image: standing firm while hardship passes over you, still standing when it is done. The past participle ~أ¼berstanden~ appears frequently in the relieved exclamation ~Das wأ¤re أ¼berstanden!~ ("That's over and done with!").
en.أ¼bertragen:
From Middle High German ~أ¼bertragen~, at least in part a calque of Latin ~transferre~. Analyzable as ~أ¼ber-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganؤ…~ ("to draw, drag, carry"), from PIE *~dت°regت°-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). A class VI strong verb. Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~, Icelandic ~draga~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubari~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). Cognate with English ~over~, Dutch ~over~, Latin ~super~, Greek ~ل½‘د€خ­دپ~ (~hyper~).

~أœbertragen~ is a Germanic calque of Latin ~transferre~ (~trans-~ + ~ferre~, "to carry across"), and the parallel is exact: ~أ¼ber-~ ("over, across") + ~tragen~ ("to carry") = ~transferre~. English ~transfer~ is the Latin borrowing; ~أ¼bertragen~ is the native Germanic translation of the same concept. That last usage is itself an act of ~أ¼bertragen~: figurative language carries a word across from its literal territory. Compare English ~carry over~, Dutch ~overdragen~. en.أ¼bertreffen:
From Middle High German ~أ¼bertreffen~. Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~treffen~:

- ~treffen~: From Middle High German ~treffen~, from Old High German ~treffan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drepan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drepanؤ…~ ("to strike, hit"). The semantic shift from "to hit, strike" to "to meet" (the primary modern sense of ~treffen~) happened because meeting someone was conceived as "striking upon" them.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From Middle High German ~أ¼ber~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix conveys exceeding or surpassing.

~أœbertreffen~ is always inseparable and means "to surpass, to exceed, to outdo." The metaphor is hitting above or beyond the mark. The reflexive ~sich selbst أ¼bertreffen~ ("to surpass oneself") is common in both praise and ironic criticism.
en.أ¼berwachen:
Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~wachen~:

- ~wachen~: From MHG ~wachen~, from OHG ~wahhؤ“n~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wakؤ“n~, from Proto-Germanic *~wakؤپnؤ…~ ("to be awake, to watch"), from PIE *~weاµ-~ ("to wake, to be strong, to be lively"). Cognate with English ~wake~ and ~watch~.
- ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uberi-~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). The prefix adds the sense of watching over something from above, implying authority and control.

English ~watch~ and ~wake~ are direct Germanic cousins, and the connection extends to Latin ~vigil~ ("watchful") and ~vigor~ ("liveliness"), both from the same PIE root. The related adjective ~wach~ ("awake") preserves the simplest form of this ancient word.
en.أ¼berwinden:
From Middle High German ~أ¼berwinden~, from Old High German ~ubarwintan~, an originally Upper German form alongside Central German ~أ¼berwinnen~, ~ubarwinnan~. The ~nn~-form descends from Proto-West Germanic *~ubarwinnan~. Compound of ~أ¼ber-~ + ~winden~ (or possibly ~winnen~):

- ~winden~: From MHG ~winden~, from OHG ~wintan~, from Proto-Germanic *~windanؤ…~ ("to wind, to wrap"), from PIE *~wendت°-~ ("to turn, wind, braid"). Cognate with English ~wind~ (the verb, "to coil"), Dutch ~winden~, Danish ~vinde~.

The ~nd~ form may be a mere phonetic alteration of the ~nn~ form, or it may reflect a genuine reanalysis connecting the verb to ~winden~ â€” twisting free from an opponent's grip as a metaphor for overcoming. Isolated MHG compounds like ~anewinden~ ("to attack") and ~widerwinden~ ("to repulse") support the fighting sense for ~winden~. Modern ~أ¼berwinden~ means "to overcome, to get over, to surmount": one overcomes fear (~Angst أ¼berwinden~), conquers obstacles (~Hindernisse أ¼berwinden~), and gets over grief (~Trauer أ¼berwinden~). en.أ¼berzeugen:
From ~أ¼ber-~ + ~zeugen~:

- ~zeugen~: From MHG ~ziugen~, ~geziugen~, from OHG ~giziugon~, a denominative of ~Zeug~ ("stuff, gear, equipment, tools"). ~Zeug~ itself comes from MHG ~ziuc~, from OHG ~giziug~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teug~, from Proto-Germanic *~teugؤ…~. - ~أ¼ber-~: From MHG ~أ¼ber~, from OHG ~ubari~, ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubar~, from PIE *~upأ©ri~ ("over, above"). The prefix here carries the sense of mastery or going beyond â€” convincing someone by rising above their resistance.

The original sense of ~zeugen~ was "to bear witness, to attest" â€” itself from the earlier meaning of producing or showing one's ~Zeug~ (one's evidence, one's gear). ~أœberzeugen~ therefore literally means "to over-witness" or "to attest so thoroughly that one overcomes the other's doubt." A curious English near-parallel: ~convince~ ultimately derives from Latin ~vincere~ ("to conquer"), reflecting the same metaphor of overcoming resistance through argument. Cognate with Dutch ~overtuigen~.
